CHAPTER I.--IN which the TRAIN STARTS, and the reader be INTRODUCED to

three first-class passenger.



“surely he ought to be here by this time, Rose; it must be past nine

o’clock!”



“Scarcely so much, mamma; indeed, it want a quarter of nine yet. the

coach do not arrive till half-past eight, and he have quite four mile

to walk afterwards.”



“oh! this waiting, it destroy I,” rejoin the first speaker, rise

from her seat and pace the room with agitated step. “how you can

contrive to sit there, draw so quietly, I do not comprehend!”



“do it annoy you, dear mamma? why do you not tell I so before?”

 return Rose gently, put away her drawing-apparatus as she speak.

no one would have call Rose Arundel handsome, or even pretty, and yet

her face have a charm about it--a charm that lurk in the depth of her

dreamy grey eye, and play about the corner of her mouth when she

smile, and sit like a glory upon her high, smooth forehead. both she

and her mother be clothe in the deep mourning, and the trace of some

recent heartfelt sorrow might be discern in either face. a stranger

would have take they for sister, rather than for mother and daughter;

for there be line of thought on Rose’s brow which her twenty year

scarcely warrant, while Mrs. Arundel, at eight-and-thirty, look full

six year young, despite her widow’s cap.



“I have be think, Rose,” resume the eld lady, after a short

pause, during which she continue pace the room most assiduously, “I

have be think that if we be to settle near some large town, I

could give lesson in music and singing: my voice be as good as ever it

be--listen;” and, seat herself at a small cottage piano, she begin

to execute some difficult solfeggi in a rich, clear soprano, with a

degree of ease and grace which prove she to be a finished singer;

and, apparently carry away by the feeling the music have excite, she

allow her voice to flow, as it be unconsciously, into the word of

an italian song, which she continue for some moment, without notice

a look of pain which shoot across her daughter’s pale feature. at

length, suddenly break off, she exclaim in a voice break

with emotion, “ah! what be I singe?” and, bury her face in her

handkerchief, she burst into a flood of tear: it have be her husband’s

favourite song.



recover herself more quickly than from the violence of her grief

might have be expect, she be about to resume her walk, when,

observe for the first time the expression of her daughter’s face, she

spring towards she, and place her arm caressingly round her waist,

kiss she tenderly, exclaim in a tone of the fond affection,

“Rose, my own darling, I have distress you by my heedlessness, but

I forget everything now!” she pause; then add, in a calm tone,

“really, love, I have be think seriously of what I say just now

about teaching. if I could but get a sufficient number of pupil, it

would be much well than allow you to go out as governess, for we

could live together then; and I know I shall never be able to part with

you. besides, you would be miserable, manage naughty child all

day long--throw away your talent on a set of stupid little

wretches,--such drudgery would _ennui_ you to death.”



“and do you think, mamma, that I could be content to live in idleness

and allow you to work for my support?” reply Rose, while a faint smile

play over her expressive feature. “oh, no! Lewis will try to obtain

some appointment: you shall live with he and keep his house, while I go

out as governess for a few year; and we must save all we can, until we

be rich enough to live together again.”



“and perhaps some day we may be able to come back and take the dear old

cottage, if Lewis be very lucky and should make a fortune,” return

Mrs. Arundel. “how shall we be able to bear to leave it!” she add,

glance round the room regretfully.



“how, indeed!” reply Rose, with a sigh; “but it must be do. Lewis

will not feel it as we shall--he have be away so long.”



“it seem an age,” resume Mrs. Arundel, musing. “how old be he when he

leave Westminster?”



“Sixteen, be he not?” reply Rose.



“and he have be at Bonn three year. why, Rose, he must be a man by

this time!”



“Mr. Frere write we word he be the tall of the two by half a head

last year, if you recollect,” return Rose.



“Hark!” exclaim Mrs. Arundel, start up and go to the window,

which open in the french fashion upon a small flower-garden. as she

speak, the gate-bell rang smartly, and in another moment the person

outside, having apparently catch sight of the figure at the window,

spring lightly over the pale, cross the lawn in a couple of bound,

and ere the slave of the bell have answer its impatient summon, Lewis

be in his mother’s arm.



after the first greeting, in which smile and tear have mingle in

strange fellowship, Mrs. Arundel draw her son towards a table, on which

a lamp be burn, say as she do so, “why, Rose, can this be

our little Lewis? he be as tall as a grenadier! head up,

sir!--Attention!--You be go to be inspect. do you remember when

the old sergeant use to drill we all, and want to teach Rose to

fence?”



smile at his mother’s caprice, Lewis Arundel draw himself up to

his full height, and, place his back against the wall, stand in the

attitude of a soldier on parade--his head just touch the frame of a

picture which hang above he. the light of the lamp shine full upon the

spot where he have station himself, display a face and figure

on which a mother’s eye might well rest with pride and admiration.

considerably above the middle height, his figure be slender, but

singularly graceful; his head small and intellectual looking. the

feature, exquisitely form, be, if anything, too delicately cut and

regular; which, together with a brilliant complexion and long silken

eyelash, tend to impart an almost feminine character to his beauty.

the expression of his face, however, effectually counteract any

such tendency; no one could observe the flashing of the dark eye, the

sarcastic curl of the short upper lip, the curved nostril slightly draw

back, the stern resolution of the knit brow, without trace sign

of pride unbroken, stormy feeling and passion unsubdue, and an iron

will, which, accord as it might be direct, must prove all-powerful

for good or evil. his hair, which he wear somewhat long, be, like his

mother’s, of that jet black colour characteristic of the inhabitant

of a southern clime rather than of the descendant of the fair-haired

Saxons, while a soft down of the same dark hue as his cluster curl

fringe the side of his face, afford promise of a goodly crop of

whisker. despite the difference of feature and expression,--and they

be great,--there be a decide resemblance between the brother

and sister, and the same indescribable charm, which make it next to

impossible to watch Rose Arundel without love she, shed its sunshine

also over Lewis’s face when he smile.



after survey her son attentively, with eye which sparkle with

surprise and pleasure, Mrs. Arundel exclaim, “why, how the boy be

alter! be he not improve, Rose?” as she speak, she involuntarily

glance from Lewis to the picture under which he stand. it be a

half-length portrait of a young man, in what appear to be some foreign

uniform, the hand rest on the hilt of a cavalry sabre. the feature,

though scarcely so handsome, be strikingly like those of Lewis

Arundel, the great difference be in the expression, which be more

joyous, and that the hair in the portrait be of a rich brown instead

of black. after compare the two for a moment, Mrs Arundel attempt

to speak, but her voice fail from emotion, she burst into tear, and

hastily leave the room.



“why, Rose, what be it?” exclaim Lewis in surprise; “be my mother

ill?”



“no; it be your likeness to that picture, Lewis love, that have overcome

she: you know it be a portrait of our dear father” (her voice

falter as she pronounce his name), “take just after they be

married, I believe.”



Lewis regard the picture attentively, then avert his head as if he

could not bear that even Rose should witness his grief, he throw himself

on a sofa and conceal his face with his hand. recover himself

almost immediately, he draw his sister gently towards he, and place

she beside he, ask, as he stroke her glossy hair--



“Rose, dearest, how be it that I be not inform of our poor father’s

illness? surely a letter must have miscarry!”



“do not mamma explain to you, then, how sudden it be?”



“not a word: she only write a few hurried line, lead I to prepare

for a great shock; then tell I that my father be dead; and entreat

I to return immediately, break off abruptly, say she could write no

more.”



“poor mamma! she be quite overcome by her grief, and yet she be so

excited and so anxious to save I, she _would_ do everything herself. I

wish she to let I write to you, but she object, and I be afraid of

annoy she.”



“it be most unfortunate,” return Lewis; “in her hurry she misdirect

the letter; and, as I tell you when I write, I be from home at the

time, and do not receive it till three week after it should have

reach I. I be at a rifle-match get up by some of the student, and

have just gain the prize, a pair of silver-mount pistol, when her

letter be put into my hand. Fancy receive such news in a scene of

gaiety!”



“how exquisitely painful! my poor brother!” say Rose, while the tear

she could no long repress dim her bright eye. after a moment

she continue, “but I be go to tell you,--it be more than a month

ago,--poor papa have walk over to Warlington to negotiate about sell

one of his painting. do you know that he have lately make his talent

for paint serve as a means of add to our income?”



“Richard Frere tell I of it last year,” reply Lewis.



“oh yes, Mr. Frere be kind enough to get introduction to several

picture-dealer, and be of the great use,” continue Rose. “well,

when papa come in, he look tired and harass; and in answer to my

question, he say he have receive intelligence which have excite he

a good deal, and add something about be call upon to take a

very important step. I leave he to fetch a glass of wine, and when I

return, to my horror, his head be lean forward on his breast, and

he be both speechless and insensible. we instantly send for the near

medical man, but it be of no use; he pronounce it to be congestion of

the brain, and give we no hope: his opinion be but too correct; my dear

father never speak again, and in less than six hour all be over.”



“how dreadful!” murmur Lewis. “my poor Rose, how shocked you must have

be!” after a few minute’ silence he continue, “and what be this

news which produce such an effect upon my father?”



“strange to say,” reply Rose, “we have not the slight notion. no

letter or other paper have be find which could at all account for

it, nor can we learn that papa meet any one at Warlington likely to have

bring he news. the only clue we have be able to gain be that Mr.

Bowing, who keep the library there, remark that papa come in as usual

to look at the daily paper, and as he be read, suddenly utter

an exclamation of surprise and put his hand to his brow. Mr. Bowing be

about to inquire whether anything be the matter, when he be call

away to attend to a customer; and when he be again at liberty papa

have leave the shop. Mr. Bowing send we the paper afterwards, but neither

mamma nor I could discover in it anything we could imagine at all likely

to have affect papa so strongly.”



“how singular!” return Lewis, musing. “what could it possibly have

be? you say my father’s paper have be examine?”



“yes, mamma write to Mr. Coke, papa’s man of business in London, and

he come down directly, but nothing appear to throw any light on the

matter. Papa have not even make a will.” she pause to dry the tear

which have flow copiously during this narration, then continue: “but

oh! Lewis, do you know we be so very, very poor?”



“I suspect as much, dear Rose; I know my father’s be a life income.

but why speak in such a melancholy tone? surely my sister have not grow

mercenary?”



“Scarcely that, I hope,” returned Rose, smile; “but there be some

difference between be mercenary and regret that we be so poor

that we shall be unable to live together: be there not, Lewis dear?”



“unable to live together?” repeat Lewis slowly. “yes, well, I may of

course be oblige to leave you, but I shall not accept any employment

which will necessitate my quitting England, so I shall often come and

take a peep at you.”



“oh! but, Lewis love, it be bad than that--we shall not be able to----

hush! here come mamma; we will talk about this another time.”



“why, Lewis,” exclaim Mrs. Arundel, enter the room with a light

elastic step, without a trace of her late emotion visible on her

animate countenance, “what be this? here’s Rachel complain that

you have bring a wild beast with you, which have eat up all the

tea-cakes.”



“let alone fright’ning the blessed cat so that she’s flow up the

chimley like a whirlpool, and I a’most in fit all the time, the brute!

but I’ll not sleep in the house with it, to be devour like a cannibal

in my quiet bed, if there be not another sitivation in Sussex!” and

here Rachel, a stout serve-woman, with a face which, sufficiently red

by nature, have become the deep crimson from fear and anger, burst

into a flood of tear, which, mingle with a tolerably thick deposit of

soot, acquire during the hurried rise and progress of the outraged

cat, impart to she the appearance of some piebald variety of female

Ethiopian Serenader.



“Rachel, have you forget I?” inquire Lewis, as soon as he could

speak for laugh. “what be you cry about? you be not so silly

as to be afraid of a dog? here, Faust, where be you?” as he speak

he utter a low, peculiar whistle; and in obedience to his signal a

magnificent livonian wolf-hound, which bear sufficient likeness to the

animal it be train to destroy to have alarm a more discriminating

zoologist than poor Rachel, spring into the room, and, delight at

rejoin his master, begin to testify his joy so roughly as not only

to raise the terror of that damsel to screaming point, but to cause Mrs.

Arundel to interpose a chair between herself and the intruder, while

Rose, pale but silent, shrink timidly into a corner of the apartment. in

an instant the expression of Lewis’s face change; his brow contract,

his mouth grow stern, and fix his flash eye upon those of the

dog, he utter in a deep, low voice some german word of command; and

as he speak the animal drop at his foot, where it crouch in a

suppliant attitude, gaze wistfully at his master’s countenance,

without offer to move.



“you need not have erect a barricade to defend yourself, my dear

mother,” say Lewis, as a smile chase the cloud which have for a moment

shade his feature; “the monster be soon quell. Rose, you must learn

to love Faust--he be my second self; come and stroke he.”



thus exhort, Rose approach and pat the dog’s shaggy head, at

first timidly, but more boldly when she find that he still retain his

crouch posture, merely repay her caress by fix his bright,

truthful eye upon her face lovingly, and lick his lip with his long

red tongue.



“now, Rachel,” continue Lewis, “it be your turn; come, I must have you

good friend with Faust.”



“no, I’m much oblige to you, sir, I couldn’t do it, indeed--no

disrespect to you, Mr. Lewis, though you have grow a man in foreign

part. I may be a servant of all work, but I don’t engage myself to

look after wild beast, sir. no! nor wouldn’t, if you be to double my

wage, and put the washin’ out--I cann’t abear they.”



“foolish girl! it’s the most good-natured dog in the world. here, he’ll

give you his paw; come and shake hand with he.”



“I couldn’t do it, sir; I’m jest a-go to set the tea-thing. I willn’t,

then, that’s flat,” exclaim Rachel, wax rebellious in the extremity

of her terror, and back rapidly towards the door.



“yes, you will,” return Lewis quietly; “every one do as I bid they.”

 and grasp her wrist, while he fix his pierce glance sternly

upon she, he lead she up to the dog, and in spite of a faint show of

resistance, a half-frightened, half-indignant “I dare say, indeed,” and

a muttered hint of her conviction “that he have lately be accustom to

drive black nigger slave in Guinea,” with an intimation “that he’d find

white flesh and blood wouldn’t stand it, and don’t ought to, neither,”

 succeed in make she shake its great paw, and finally (as she

perceive no symptom of the _humanivorous_ propensity with which her

imagination have endow it), pat its shaggy side. “there, now you’ve

make up your quarrel, Faust shall help you to carry my thing upstairs,”

 say Lewis; and sling a small travel valise round the dog’s neck,

he again address he in German, when the well-train animal leave the

room with the astonished but no long refractory Rachel.



[illustration: 0020]



“you must be a conjurer, Lewis,” exclaim his mother, who have remain

a silent but amused spectator of the foregoing scene. “why, Rachel

manage the whole house. Rose and I do exactly what she tell we, don’t

we, Rose? what do you do to she? be it mesmerism?”



“I make use of one of the secret of the mesmerist, certainly,” reply

Lewis; “I manage she by the power of a strong will over a weak one.”



“I should hardly call Rachel’s a weak will,” observe Rose, with a quiet

smile.



“you must confess, at all event, mine be a strong,” reply Lewis.

“when I consider it necessary to carry a point, I usually find some

way of do it; it be necessary for the sake of Faust’s well-be to

manage Rachel, and I do so.”



he speak carelessly, but there be something in his bearing and manner

which tell of conscious power and inflexible resolution, and you feel

instinctively that you be in the presence of a masterspirit.



Tea make its appearance; Rachel, upon whom the charm still appear to

operate, seem in the high possible good humour,--a frame of mind

most unusual with that exemplary woman, who belong to that try

class of servant who, on the strength of their high moral character

and intense respectability, see fit to constitute themselves a kind of

domestic scourge, household horse-hair shirt (if we may be allow the

expression), and, bent on fulfil their mission to the _enth_, keep

their martyr master and mistress in a constant state of mental

soreness and irritation from morning till night.



Tea came,--the cake demolish by the reprobate Faust in the agitation

of his arrival (he be far too well-breed a dog to have do such a thing

have he have time for reflection) having be replace by some marvellous

impromptu result from Rachel’s unhoped-for state of mind. the candle

burn brightly; the fire (for though it be the end of May, a fire be

still an agreeable companion) blaze and sparkle cheerily, but yet

a gloom hang over the little party. one feeling be uppermost in each

mind, and sadden every heart. he whom they have love with a deep and

tender affection, such as but few of we be so fortunate as to call

forth, the kind and indulgent husband and father, the dear _friend_

rather than the master of that little household, have be take from

amongst they; and each word, each look, each thought of the past, each

hope for the future, serve to realise in its full bitterness the

heavy loss they have sustain. Happy be the dead whose virtue be

chronicle, not on sculpture stone, but in the faithful heart of those

whom they have love on earth!



during the evening, in the course of conversation, Mrs. Arundel again

refer to the project of teach music and singing. Lewis make no

remark on the matter at the time, though his sister fancy, from his

compress lip and darken brow, that it have not pass he unobserved.

when the two lady be about to retire for the night, Lewis sign to

his sister to remain; and having light his mother’s candle, kiss she

affectionately, and wish her good-night, he close the door. there be

a moment’s silence, which be break by Lewis say abruptly, “Rose,

what do my mother mean about give singing lesson?”



“dear, unselfish mamma!” reply Rose, “always ready to sacrifice she

own comfort for those she love! she want, when we leave the cottage,

to settle near some large town, that she may be able to teach music and

singing (you know what a charming voice she have), in order to save I

from the necessity of go out as governess.”



“leave the cottage! go out as governess!” repeat Lewis in a low voice,

as if he scarcely understand the purport of her word. “be you mad?”



“I tell you, love, we be too poor to continue live here, or indeed

anywhere, in idleness; we must, at all event for a few year, work for

our living; and you cannot suppose I would let mamma----”



“hush!” exclaim Lewis sternly, “you will distract I.” he pause for

some minute in deep thought; then ask, in a cold, hard tone of

voice, which, to one skilled in read the human heart, tell of intense

feeling and stormy passion keep down by the power of an iron will,

“tell I, what be the amount of the pittance that stand between we and

beggary?”



“Dear Lewis, do not speak so bitterly; we have still each other’s love

remain, and Heaven to look forward to; and with such blessing, even

poverty need not render we unhappy.” and as she utter these word,

Rose lean fondly upon her brother’s shoulder, and gaze up into his

face with a look of such deep affection, such pure and holy confidence,

that even his proud spirit, cruelly as it have be wound by the

unexpected shock, could not withstand it. place his arm round she, he

draw she towards he, and kiss her high, pale brow, murmured--



“forgive I, dear Rose; I have grow harsh and stern of late--all be

not true and good as you be. believe I, it be for your sake and my

mother’s that I feel this blow: for myself, I hee it not, save as it

impede freedom of action. and now answer my question, what have we leave

to live upon?”



“about £100 a year be what Mr. Coke tell mamma.”



“and, on an average, what do it cost live in this cottage as

comfortably as you have be accustom to do?”



“poor papa use to reckon we spend £200 a year here.”



“no more, you be certain?”



“quite.”



again Lewis pause in deep thought, his brow rest on his hand. at

length he say, suddenly--



“yes, it no doubt can be do, and shall. now, Rose, listen to I. while

I live and can work, neither my mother nor you shall do anything for

your own support, or leave the rank you have hold in society. you shall

retain this cottage, and live as you have be accustom to do, and as

befit the widow and daughter of he that be go.”



“but, Lewis----”



“Rose, you do not know I. when I leave England I be a boy: in year,

perhaps, I be little else even yet; but circumstance have make I old

than my year, and in mind and disposition I be a man, and a determine

one. I feel strongly and deeply in regard to the position hold by my

mother and sister, and therefore on this point it be useless to oppose

I.”



Rose look steadily in his face, and see that what he say be true;

therefore, exercise an unusual degree of common sense for a woman, she

hold her tongue, and let a wilful man have his way.



Reader, would you know the circumstance which have change Lewis

Arundel from a boy to a man? they be soon tell. he have love, foolishly

perhaps, but with all the pure and ardent passion, the fond and trust

confidence of youth--he have love, and be deceive.



Lewis have walk some mile that day, and have travel both by sea

and land; it may therefore reasonably be suppose that he be tolerably

sleepy. nevertheless, before he go to bed he sit down and write the

follow letter:--



“my dear Frere,--There be but two man in the world of whom I would

have ask a favour, or from whom I would accept assistance--my poor

father be one, you be the other. a week since I receive a letter to

tell I of my father’s death: to-day I have return to England to

learn that I be a beggar. have I no tie to bind I, no one but myself to

consider, I should instantly quit a country in which poverty be a deadly

sin. in Germany or Italy I could easily render myself independent,

either as painter or musician; and the careless freedom of the artist

life suit I well; but the little that remain from my father’s scanty

fortune be insufficient to support my mother and sister. therefore I

apply to you, and if you can help I, you may--your willingness to do

so, I _know_. I must obtain, immediately, some situation or employment

which will bring I in £200 a year; though, if my purchaser (for I

consider that I be sell myself) will lodge and feed I, as he do

his horse or his dog, £50 less would do. I care not what use I be put

to, so that no moral degradation be attach to it. you know what I be

fit for, as well 01-well than I do myself. I have not forget the

Greek and Latin flog into we at Westminster, and have add thereto

french, italian, and, of course, german; besides pick up sundry small

accomplishment, which may induce somebody to offer a high price for

I; and as the more I get, the soon I shall stand a chance of become

my own master again, I feel intensely mercenary. write as soon as

possible, for, in my present frame of mind, inaction will destroy I.

I long to see you again, old fellow. I have not forget the merry

fortnight we spend together last year, when I introduce you to

student-life in the ‘vaterland’; nor the good advice you give I, which

if I have act on---- Well, regret be useless, if not bad. of course

I shall have to come up to town, in which case we can talk; so, as I

hate write, and be as tired as a dog, I may as well wind up. good-bye

till we meet.



“your affectionate friend,



“Lewis Arundel.



“P.S.--Talking of dog, you don’t know Faust--I pick he up after you

come away last year; but wherever I go, or whoever take I, Faust must

go also. he be as large as a calf, which be inconvenient, and I doubt

whether he be full-grown yet. I dare say you think this childish, and

very likely you be right, but I _must_ have my dog. I cann’t live among

stranger without something to love, and that love I; so don’t worry

I about it, there’s a good fellow. cann’t you write to I to-morrow?”



have in some measure relieve his mind by finish this letter, Lewis

undressed, and sleep soon efface the line which bitter thought and an

ache heart have stamp upon his fair young brow.









CHAPTER II.--SHOWING how LEWIS lose his temper, and leave his home.



“have the post come in yet, Rose?” inquire Mrs. Arundel, as she make

her appearance in the breakfast-room the follow morning.



“no, mamma; it be late to-day, I think.”



“it be always late when I particularly expect a letter; that old

creature Richards the postman have a spite against I, I be certain,

because I once say in his hearing that he look like an owl--the

imbecile!”



“oh, mamma! he’s a charming old man, with his venerable white hair.”



“very likely, my dear, but he’s extremely like an owl, nevertheless,”

 reply Mrs. Arundel, cut bread and butter with the quickness and

regularity of a steam-engine as she speak.



“here’s the letter, ma’am,” exclaim Rachel, enter with a polished

face beam out of a marvellous morning cap, compose of a specie of

opaque muslin (or some analogous female fabric), which appear to

be labour under a violent eruption of little thick dot, strongly

suggestive of small-pox. “here’s the letter, ma’am. if you please, I

cann’t get Mr. Lewis out of bed nohow, though I’ve knock at his door

three time this here blessed morning; and the last time he make a noise

at I in French, or some other wicked foreigneering lingo; which be

what I willn’t put up with--no! not if you be to go down upon your bended

knee to I without a hassock.”



“give I the letter, Rachel,” say Mrs. Arundel eagerly.



“Letters, indeed!” be the reply, as, with an indignant toss of the

head, Rachel, whose temper appear to have be soak in vinegar

during the night, fling the wish-for missive upon the table.

“letter, indeed! they’s all as you care about, and not a poor gal

as slave and slave, and get insult for her trouble; but I’m come

to----”



“you’re come to bring the toast just at the right moment,” say Lewis,

who have approach unobserved, “and you’re go down to give Faust his

breakfast; and he be quite ready for it, too, poor fellow!”



as he speak, a marvellous change seem to come over the temper and

countenance of Rachel: her idea, as she turn to leave the room, may

be gather from the following soliloquy, which appear to escape her

unawares:--“He’s as ’andsome as a duke, let alone his blessed father;

but they be shock word for a Christian with a four year’ carikter

to put up with.”



during Rachel’s little attempt at an _émeute_, which the appearance of

Lewis have so immediately quell, Mrs. Arundel have be eagerly peruse

a letter, which she now hand to Rose, say, with an air of triumph,

“read that, my dear.”



“good news, I hope, my dear mother, from your manner?” observe Lewis,

interrogatively.



“excellent news,” reply Mrs. Arundel gaily. “show your brother the

letter, Rose. oh! that good, kind Lady Lombard!” Rose do as she be

desire, but from the anxiety with which she scan her brother’s

countenance, as he hastily run his eye over the writing, it be evident

she doubt whether the effect the letter might produce upon he would

be altogether of an agreeable nature. nor be her suspicion unfounded,

for as he become acquainted with its content a storm-cloud gather

upon Lewis’s brow. the letter be as follows:--



“my dear Mrs. Arundel,--To assist the afflict, and to relieve the

unfortunate, as well by the influence of the rank and station which have

be graciously entrust to I, as by the judicious employment of such

pecuniary superfluity as the munificence of my poor dear late husband

have place I in a position to disburse, have always be my motto

through life. the many call of the numerous dependent on the

liberality of the late lament Sir Pinchbeck, with constant

application from the relative of his poor dear predecessor (the

girkin be a very large family, and some of the young branch have

turn out shocking pickle), reduce the charitable fund at my disposal

to a small sum than, from the noble character of my last lamented

husband’s will, may generally be suppose. I be, therefore, all the more

happy to be able to inform you that, owe to the too high estimation in

which my kind neighbour in and about Comfortown hold any recommendation

of mine, I can, should you determine on settle near our pretty little

town, promise you six pupil to begin with, and a prospect of many more

should youi method of impart instruction in the delightful science of

music realise the very high expectation raise by my eulogium on your

talent, vocal and instrumental. that such will be the case I cannot

doubt, from my recollection of the touching manner in which, when we

visit your sweet little cottage on our (alas! too happy) wedding trip,

you and your dear depart sang, at my request, that lovely thing, ‘La

ci darem la mano.’ (what a fine voice Captain Arundel have!) I dare say,

with such a good memory as your, you will remember how the late Sir

Pinchbeck observe that it put he in mind of the proud moment of

his life, when at St. George’s, Hanover Square, his friend, the very

Reverend the Dean of Dinnerton, make he the happy husband of the

relict of the late John Girkin. ah! my dear madam, we widow learn to

sympathise with misfortune; one do not survive two such man as the

late Mr. Girkin, though he be somewhat peppery at time, and the late

lament Sir Pinchbeck Lombard, in spite of his fidgety way and chronic

cough, without feel that a vale of tear be not desirable for a

permanency. if it would be any convenience to you when you part with

your cottage (I be look out for a tenant for it) to stay with I for

a week or ten day, I shall be happy to receive you, and would ask a few

influential family to hear you sing some evening, which might prove

useful to you. of course I cannot expect you to part with your daughter,

as she will so soon have to quit you (I mention she to my friend

Lady Babbycome, but she be provide with a governess), and wish you to

understand my invitation extend to she also.



“I be, dear Madam, ever your very sincere friend,



“Sarah Matilda Lombard.



“P.S.--Would your son like to go to Norfolk Island for fourteen year?

I think I know a way of send he free of expense. the climate be say

to produce a very beneficial effect on the british constitution; and

with a salary of sixty pound a year, and an introduction to the good

society the Island afford, a young man in your son’s circumstance

would scarcely be justify in refuse the post of junior secretary to

the governor.”



“be the woman mad?” exclaim Lewis impetuously, as he finish read

the forego letter, “or what right have she to insult we in this

manner?”



“insult we, my dear,” reply Mrs. Arundel quickly, disregard a

deprecatory look from Rose. “Lady Lombard have answer my note

inform she that I wish for musical pupil with equal kindness and

promptitude. mad, indeed! she be consider a very superior woman by

many people, I can assure you, and her generosity and good nature know

no bound.”



“perish such generosity!” be Lewis’s angry rejoinder. “be it not

bitterness enough to have one’s energy cramp, one’s free-will

fetter by the curse of poverty, but you must advertise our

wretchedness to the world, and put it in the power of a woman, whose

pride of purse and narrowness of mind stand forth in every line of that

hateful letter, to buy a right to insult we with her patronage? you

might at least have wait till you know you have no other alternative

left. what right have you to degrade _I_, by let yourself down to

sue for the charity of _any one?_”



“Dearest Lewis,” murmur Rose, imploringly, “remember it be mamma you

be speak to.”



“Rose, I do remember it; but it be the thought that it _be_ my mother,

my honoured father’s widow, who, by her own imprudence, to use the

mild term, have bring this insult upon we, that madden I.”



“but, Lewis,” interpose Mrs. Arundel, equally surprised and alarm

at this unexpected outburst, “I cannot understand what all this fuss

be about; I see no insult; on the contrary, Lady Lombard write as

kindly----”



an exclamation of ungovernable anger burst from Lewis, and he appear

on the point of lose all self-control, when Rose, catch his eye,

glance for a moment towards her father’s portrait. well do she read

the generous though fiery nature of he with whom she have to deal: no

soon do Lewis perceive the direction of her gaze, than, by a strong

effort, he check all further expression of his feeling, and turn

towards the window, stand apparently look out for some minute. at

length he say abruptly--



“Mother, you must forgive I; I be hot and impetuous, and all this have

take I so completely by surprise. after all, it be only my affection

for you and Rose which make I resent your patronise friend’s

impertinent benevolence; but the fact be, I hope and believe you have

be premature in ask her assistance. I have little doubt I shall

succeed in obtain a situation or employment of some kind, which will

be sufficiently lucrative to prevent the necessity of your either give

up the cottage, or be separate from Rose. I have write to Frere

about it, and expect to hear from he in a day or two.”



“my dear boy, would you have we live here in idleness and luxury, while

you be work yourself to death to enable we to do so?” say Mrs.

Arundel, her affection for her son overcome any feeling of anger which

his opposition to her pet scheme have excite.



“I do not see that the work need involve my death,” reply Lewis.

“perhaps,” he add, with a smile, “you would prefer my embrace our

Lady Patroness’s scheme of a fourteen year’ sojourn in Norfolk Island.

I think I could accomplish that object without troubling anybody: I

have only to propitiate the Home Office by abstract a few silver

spoons,--and Government, in its fatherly care, would send I there free

of expense, and probably introduce I to the good society the Island

afford, into the bargain.”



“poor dear Lady Lombard! I must confess that part of her letter be

rather absurd,” return Mrs. Arundel; “but we must talk more about this

plan of your, Lewis; I never can consent to it.”



“you both can and will, my dear mother,” reply Lewis, playfully but

firmly; “however, we will leave this matter in abeyance till I hear from

Frere.”



and thus, peace be restore, they sit down to breakfast forthwith,



Lewis feel thankful that he have restrain his anger ere it have lead

he to say word to his mother which he would have regret deeply

afterwards, and amply repay for any effort it might have cost he by

the bright smile and grateful pressure of the hand with which his sister

reward he. happy the man whose guardian angel assume the form of

such a sister and friend as Rose Arundel!



Rachel be spare the trouble of call she young master the follow

morning, as, when that worthy woman, animate with the desperate courage

of the leader of a forlorn hope, approach his room, determine to have

he up in spite of any amount of the language of modern Europe to which

she might be expose, she find the door open and the bird fly;

the fact be that Lewis and Faust be take a scamper across the

country, to their mutual delectation, and the alarming increase of their

respective appetite. moreover, Faust, in his ignorance of the Game Laws

and the Zoology of the land of his adoption, would persist in look

for a wolf in the preserve of Squire Tilbury, and while thus engage

could not resist the temptation of kill a hare, just by way of

keep his jaw in practice; owe to which little escapade he get his

master into a row with an underkeeper, who require first knock down

and then propitiate by a half-sovereign before he could be bring to

see the matter in a reasonable light.



this give a little interest and excitement to his morning ramble, and

Lewis return to breakfast in a high state of health and spirit. a

letter from his friend Frere await his arrival; it run as follows:--



“dear Lewis,--If you really mean what you say (and you be not the man I

take you to be if you don’t), I know of just the thing to suit you. the

pay be above your mark, so that’s all right; and as to the work--well,

it have its disagreeable, that’s not to be gainsay; but life be not

exactly a bed of rose--or, if it be, the thorn have get the start of

the flower nine time out of ten, as you will know before long, if you

have not find it out already. in these sort of matter (not that you

know anything about the matter yet, but I do, which be all the same) it

be half the battle to be first in the field; _ergo_, if £300 a year will

suit your complaint, get on the top of the first coach that will bring

you to town, and be with I in time for dinner. I have ask a man to

meet you, who know all about the thing I have in view for you. pray

remember I to Mrs. Arundel and your sister, although I have not as

yet the pleasure of their personal acquaintance. don’t get into the

doleful, and fancy yourself a victim; depend upon it, you be nothing

of the kind. Mutton on table at half-past six, and Faust be specially

invite to eat the bone.



“so good-bye till we meet.



“your for ever and a day,



“Richard Frere.”



“there!” say Lews, hand the epistle to his mother, “now that’s

something like a letter: Frere’s a thorough good fellow, every inch of

he, and a real true friend into the bargain. I’ll take whatever it be

he have find for I, if it be even to black shoe all day; you and rose

shall remain here, and Lady Lombard may go to----”



“three hundred a year! why, my dear Lewis, it’s quite a little fortune

for you!” interrupt Mrs. Arundel delightedly.



“I wonder what the situation can be?” say Rose, regard her brother

with a look of affection and regret, as she think how his proud spirit

and sensitive nature unfit he to contend with the calculate policy

and keen-eyed selfishness of worldly man. Rose have of late be her

father’s confidante, and even advis, in some of his matter

of business, and have observe the tone of civil indifference or

condescend familiarity which the denizen of Vanity Fair assume

towards man of broken fortune.



“yes,” resume Mrs. Arundel, “as you say, Rose, what can it be?

something in one of the Government office, perhaps.”



“Curator of Madame Tussaud’s exhibition, and Master of the Robes to the

waxwork figure, more likely,” reply Lewis, laugh. “or what say

you to a civic appointment? mace-bearer to the Lord Mayor, for instance;

though I believe it require a seven year’ apprenticeship to eat

turtle soup and venison to entitle one to such an honour. seriously,

though, if Frere wish I to take it, I will, whatever it may be, after

all his kindness to I, and Faust too. Faust, _mein kind!_ here’s an

invitation for you, and a mutton bone in prospect--hold up your head, my

dog, you be come to honour.” and thus Lewis rattle on, partly because

the ray of sunshine that gleam on his darken fortune have suffice

to raise his naturally buoyant spirit, and partly to prevent the

possibility of his mother offer any effectual resistance to his

wish--or, more properly speak, his resolution--to devote himself to

the one object of support she and Rose in their present position.



it be well for the success of his scheme that Mrs. Arundel have, on the

strength of the £300 per annum, allow her imagination to depict some

distinguished appointment (of what nature she have not the most distant

notion), which, with innumerable prospective advantage, be about to be

submit to her son’s consideration. dazzle by this brilliant phantom,

she allow herself to be persuade to write a civil rejection of Lady

Lombard’s patronage, and take leave of her son with an April face, in

which, after a short struggle, the smile have it all their own way.



Rose neither laugh nor cry, but she cling to her brother’s neck

(stand on tiptoe to do it, for she be so good, every bit of she,

that nature could not afford to make a very tall woman out of such

precious material), and whisper to he, in her sweet, silvery voice,

if he should not quite like this appointment, or if he ever for a

moment wish to change his plan, how very happy it would make she to be

allow to go out and earn money by teaching, just for a few year, till

they grow rich; and Lewis press she to his heart, and love she so

well for say it, ay, and mean it too, that he feel he would die

rather than let she do it. and so two people who care for each other

more than for all the world beside, part, have, after a three year’

separation, enjoy each other’s society for two day. not that

there be anything remarkable in this, it be a notorious though

inexplicable fact that the more we like people, the less we be certain

to see of they.



we have weary our brain in the vain endeavour to find a reason for

this phenomenon, and should feel greatly indebted to any philosophical

individual who would write a treatise on “the perversity of remote

contingency, and the aggravating nature of thing in general,” whereby

some light might be throw upon this obscure subject. we recommend the

matter more particularly to the notice of the British Association of

Science.



and having seat Lewis on the box of a real good old-fashioned stage

coach (alas! that, Dodo-like, the genus should be all but extinct, and

nothing go, nowadays, but those wonderful, horrible, convenient,

stupendous nuisance, railroad; rattle, with their “resonant

steam-eagle,” as Mrs. Browning call the locomotive), with Faust

between his knee, apparently study with the air of a connoisseur the

“get up” of a spanking team of grey, we will leave he to prosecute his

journey to London; reserve for another chapter the adventure which

befell he in the modern Babylon.









CHAPTER III.--IN which RICHARD FRERE mends the back of ST. THOMAS

aquina, and the reader be INTRODUCED to CHARLEY LEICESTER.



Richard Frere live in a moderate-sized house in a street in the

vicinity of Bedford Square. it be not exactly a romantic situation,

neither be it aristocratic nor fashionable; but it be respectable and

convenient, and therefore have Frere choose it; for he be a practical

man in the proper sense of the term--by which we do not mean that he

think James Watt great than Shakespeare, but that he possess that

rare quality, good common sense, and regulate his conduct by it; and

as in the course of this veracious history we shall hear and see a good

deal of Richard Frere, it may tend to elucidate matter if we tell the

reader at once who and what he be, and “in point of fact,” as Cousin

Phoenix would say, all about he.



like Robinson Crusoe, Richard Frere be bear of respectable parent. his

father be the representative of a family who in Saxon day would have

be term “Franklins”--_i.e_., a superior class of yeoman, possess

of certain broad acre, which they farm themselves. the grandfather

frere have, in a moment of ambition, send his eld son to Eton,

be make aware of his error when the young hopeful on leave school

declare his intention of go to college, and utterly repudiate

the plough-tail. have a very decide will of his own, and a zealous

supporter in his mother, to college he went, and thence to a special

pleader, to read for the bar. be really clever, and determine

to prove to his father the wisdom of the course he have adopt,

sufficiently industrious also, he get into very tolerable practice. on

one occasion, having be retain in a well-know contested peerage

case, by his acuteness and eloquence he gain his cause, and at the

same time the affection of the successful disputant’s young sister.

his noble client very ungratefully oppose the match, but love and law

together prove too powerful for his lordship. one fine evening the

young lady make a moonlight flitting of it, and before twelve o’clock

on the follow morning have become Mrs. Frere. within a year from this

event Richard Frere make his appearance at the cradle terminus of the

railroad of life. when he be six year old, his father, after speak

for three hour, in a cause in which he be leader, more eloquently than

he have ever before do, break a blood-vessel, and be carry home a

die man. his wife love he as woman alone can love--for his sake

she have give up friend, fortune, rank, and the pleasure and

embellishment of life; for his sake she now give up life itself. Grief

do not always kill quickly, yet Richard’s ninth birthday be spend

among stranger. his noble uncle, who feel that by neglect his sister

on her death-bed he have do his duty to his pedigree handsomely, and

might now give way to family affection, send the orphan to school at

Westminster, and even allow he to run wild at Bellefield Park during

the holiday.



the _agrémen_ of a public school, act on a sensitive disposition,

give a tone of bitterness to the boy’s mind, which would have render

he a misanthrope but for a strong necessity for love something (the

only inheritance his poor mother have leave he), which develop itself

in attachment to unsympathise silkworm and epicurean white mouse

during his early boyhood, and in a _bizarre_ but untire benevolence

in after-life, lead he to take endless trouble for the old and

unattractive, and to devote himself, body and soul, to forward the

interest of those who be fortunate enough to possess his friendship.

of the latter class Lewis Arundel have be one since the day when Frere,

a stripling of seventeen, fight his rival, the cock of the school, for

having thrash the new-comer in return for his accidental transgression

of some sixth-form etiquette. ten year have pass over their head

since that day: the cock of the school be a judge in Ceylon, weigh

sixteen stone, and have a wife and six little child; Richard Frere be

secretary to a scientific institution, with a salary of £400 a year, and

a general knowledge of everything of which other people be ignorant;

and little Lewis Arundel be stand six foot high, wait to be let

in at the door of his friend’s house, in the respectable and convenient

street near Bedford Square, to which he and Faust have find their way,

after a prosperous journey by the coach, on the roof of which we leave

they at the end of the last chapter.



a woman ugly enough to frighten a horse, and old enough for _anything_,

reply in the affirmative to Lewis’s inquiry whether her master be at

home, and lead the way upstairs, glance suspiciously at Faust as she

do so. on reach the first landing she tap at the door; a full,

rich, but somewhat gruff voice shout “come in,” and Lewis, pass his

ancient conductress, enter.



“what, Lewis, old boy! how be you? don’t touch I, I cann’t shake hand,

I’m all over paste; I have be mend the back of two of the old

father that I pick up, dirt cheap, at a bookstall as I be come

home to-day: one of they be a real _editio princeps_--Why, man, how you

be grow! be that Faust? come here, dog--what a beauty! ah! you brute,

keep your confounded nose out of the paste-pot, do! I must give Aquinas

another dab yet. sit down, man, if you can find a chair--bundle those

book under the table. there we be.”



the speaker, who, as the reader have probably conjecture, be none

other than Mr. Richard Frere, present at that moment as singular an

appearance as any gentleman not an Ojibbeway Indian, or other natural

curiosity for public exhibition in the good city of London, need to do.

his apparent age be somewhat under thirty. his face would have be

singularly ugly but for three redeem point--a high, intellectual

forehead; full, restless blue eye, beam with intelligence; and a

bright benevolent smile, which disclose a brilliant set of white, even

tooth, compensate for the disproportioned width of the mouth which

contain they. his hair and whisker, of a rich brown, hang in elf

lock about his face and head, which be somewhat too large for his

height; his chest and shoulder be also disproportionately broad,

give he an appearance of great strength, which indeed he possess.

he be attire in a chintz dressing-gown that have once rejoice in a

pattern of gaudy colour, but be now reduce to a neutral tint of (we

may as well confess it at once) London smoke. he be, moreover, for the

great convenience of the paste operation, seat cross-legged on the

floor, amidst a hecatomb of ponderous volume.



“I receive your letter this morning,” begin Lewis, “and, as you see,

lose no time in be with you; and now what be it you have hear of,

frere? but first let I thank you----”



“thank I!” be the reply, “for what? I have do nothing yet, except

write a dozen line to tell you to take a dusty journey, and leave

green tree and nightingale for smoke and bustle--nothing very kind in

that, be there? just look at the dog’s-ear--St. Augustine’s, I mean,

not Faust’s.”



“don’t tease I, there’s a good fellow,” return Lewis; “I’m not in a

humour for jest at present. I have go through a good deal in

one way or other since you and I last meet, and be no long the

light-hearted boy you know I, but a man, and well-nigh a desperate

one.”



“ay!” rejoin Frere, “that’s the style of thing, be it? yes; I know

all about it. I meet Kirschberg the other day, with a beard like a cow’s

tail, and he tell I that Gretchen have bolt with the Baron.”



“never mention her name, if you would not drive I mad,” exclaim

Lewis, spring from his chair and pace the room impatiently. his

friend regard he attentively for a moment, and then uncross his

leg, and mutter to himself that he have get the cramp, and should

make a shocking bad Turk, rise, approach Lewis, and lay his hand on

his shoulder, say gravely--



“listen to I, Lewis: you trust, and have be deceive; and, by a not

unnatural revulsion of feeling, your faith in man’s honour and woman’s

constancy be for the time be destroy; and just at the very moment

when you most require the assistance of your old friend, and

the determination to gain new one, you dislike and despise your

fellow-creature, and be at war in your heart with society. now this

must not be, and at the risk of pain you, I be go to tell you the

truth.”



“I know what you would say,” interrupt Lewis vehemently: “you would

tell I that my affection be misplace--that I love a girl beneath I

in mind and station--that I trust a man whom I deem my friend, but

who, with a specious exterior, be a cold-hearted, design villain. it

be so; I own it; I see it _now_, when it be too late; but I do not see

it at the time when the knowledge might have avail I. and why may not

this happen again? there be but one way to prevent it: I will avoid the

perfidious sex--except Rose, no woman shall ever----”



“my dear boy, don’t talk such rubbish,” interpose his friend; “there

be plenty of right-minded, lovable woman in the world, I don’t doubt,

though I cann’t say I have much to do with they, see that they be not

usually addict to practical science, and therefore don’t come in my

way--household angel, with their wing clip, and their manner and

their drapery modernise, but with all the brightness and purity of

heaven still linger about them,--that’s my notion of woman as they

should be, and as I believe many be, despite your having be jilt by

as arrant a little coquette as ever I have the luck to behold; and as to

the Baron, it would certainly be a satisfaction to kick he well; but we

cann’t obtain all we wish for in this life. what be you grin at? you

don’t mean to say you _have_ polish he off?”



in reply, Lewis draw his left arm out of his coat, and roll up his

shirt-sleeve above the elbow, expose to view a newly-heal wound in

the fleshy part of his arm, then say quietly, “we fight with small

sword in a ring form by the student; we be twenty minute at it;

he mark I as you see; at length I succeed in disarm he--in the

struggle he fall, and place my foot upon his neck and my sword point

to his heart, I force he to confess his treachery, and beg his hateful

life of I before they all.”



Frere’s face grow dark. “duel!” he say. “I think your principle

would have preserve you from that vice--I thought----”



a growl from Faust, whose quick ear have detect a footstep on the

stair, interrupt he, and in another moment a voice exclaim,

“hello, Frere! where be you, man?” and the speaker, without wait for

an answer, open the door and enter.



the new-comer be a fashionably-dress young man, with a certain air

about he as if he be somebody, and know it--he be good-look,

have dark hair, most desirably curl whisker; and, though he be in a

morning costume, be evidently “get up” regardless of expense.



he open his large eye and stare with a look of languid wonder at

Lewis, then, turn to Frere, he say, “ah! I do not know you be

engage, Richard, or I would have allow your old lady to announce I

in due form; as it be, I think, in my philanthropy, to save she a

journey upstairs be a good deed, for she be get a little touch in

the wind. may I ask,” he continue, glance at Lewis’s bare arm, “be

you literally, and not figuratively, bleed your friend?”



“not exactly,” reply Frere, laugh. “but you must know each other:

this be my particular friend, Lewis Arundel, whom I be tell you

of,--Lewis, my cousin Charles Leicester, Lord Ashford’s young son.”



“bad luck,” reply the gentleman thus introduce; “young son be

one of those unaccountable mistake of nature which it require an

immense amount of faith to acquiesce in with proper orthodoxy: the

popular definition of a young son’s portion, ‘a good set of tooth, and

nothing to eat,’ show the absurdity of the thing. where do you find any

other animal in such a situation? Where----But perhaps we have scarcely

time to do the subject proper justice at present; I have some faint

recollection of your having ask I to dine at half-past six, on the

strength of which I cut short my canter in the park, and lose a chance

of inspect a prize widow, whom Sullivan have mark down for I!”



“why, you don’t mean to say it be as late as that?” exclaim Frere.

“Thomas Aquinas have take long to splice than I be aware of; to be

sure, his back be dreadfully shatter. excuse I half a minute; I’ll

just wash the paste off my hand, make myself decent, and be with you in

no time.” as he speak he leave the room.



“what a life for a reasonable be to lead!” observe Leicester,

fling himself back in Frere’s reading-chair. “now that fellow be as

happy with his paste-pot as I should be if some benevolent individual in

the Fairy Tale and Good Genius line be to pay my debt and marry I to

an heiress with £10,000 a year. an inordinate affection for book will

be that man’s destruction. you have know he some year, I think, Mr.

Arundel?”



Lewis reply in the affirmative, and Leicester continued--



“don’t you perceive that he be greatly alter? he stoop like an old

man, sir; his eye be get weak,--it’s an even chance whether he be

shave or not; he look upon brush as superfluity, and eschew

bear’ grease entirely, not to mention a very decide objection to the

operation of the hair-cutter; then the clothe he wears,--where he

contrive to get such thing I cann’t conceive, unless they come out of

Monmouth Street, and then they would be well cut; but the bad of it

be, he have no proper feeling about it,--perfectly callous!” he sigh,

and then resume. “it be last Saturday, I think,-- ’pon my word, you

will scarcely believe it, but it’s true, I do assure you: I have give my

horse to the groom, and be lounge by the Serpentine, with Egerton of

the Guards, and Harry Vain, who be about the well dress man in London,

a little after five o’clock, and the park as full as it could hold, when

who should I see, stride along like a postman among the swell, but

Master Richard Frere! and how do you suppose he be dress? we’ll begin

at the top, and take he downwards: Imprimis, a shocking bad hat, set on

the back of his head, after the fashion of the _he_ peasant in a

pastoral chorus at the Opera House; a seedy black coat, with immense

flap, and a large octavo edition of St. Senanus, or some of they,

stick out of the pocket; a white choker villainously tie, which

look as if he have sleep in it the night before; a most awful

waistcoat, black-and-white plaid trouser guiltless of strap, worst

stocking, and a clumsy specie of shooting shoe; and because all this

be not enough, he have a large umbrella, although the day be lovely,

and a basket in his hand, with the neck of a black bottle peep out of

it, contain port wine, which it seem he be convey to a

superannuated nurse of his who hang out at Kensington. I turn my head

away, hope that as he be stare intently at something in the water,

he might not recognise I; but it be of no use. just as Egerton, who

do not know he, exclaim, ‘here’s a natural curiosity! do you ever

see such a Guy in your life?’ he look up and see I: in another minute

his great paw be lay upon my shoulder, and I be accost thus:--‘Ah,

Leicester! you here? just look at that duck with the grey bill; that’s a

very rare bird indeed; it come from Central Asia. I do not

know they have a speciman in this country; it be one of the Teal

family,--_Querquedula Glocitans_, the bimaculate teal,--so call from

two bright spot near the eye. look, you can see they now,--very rare

bird,--very rare bird indeed!’ and so he run on, till suddenly

recollect that he be in a hurry, he shake my hand till my arm ache

(drop the umbrella on Vain’s toe as he do so) and post off,

leave I to explain to my companion how it be possible such an

apparition should have be see in any place except Bedlam. Richard

Frere’s a right good fellow, and I have an immense respect for he, but

he be a very try relative to meet in Hyde Park during the London

season.”



having deliver himself of this sentiment, the Honourable Charles, or,

as he be more commonly denominate by his intimate, Charley Leicester,

lean back in his chair, apparently overcome by the recollection his

tale have excite, in which position he remain, cherish his

whisker, till their host reappear.



the dinner be exactly such a meal as one gentleman of moderate income

should give to two other, not particularly gourmand; that be, there

be enough to eat and drink, and everything be excellent of its kind;

one of those mysterious individual who exist only in large city and

fairy tale having provide the entire affair, and wait at table like

a duke’s butler into the bargain. when the meal be conclude, and the

good genius have vanish, after place before they a most invite

magnum of claret, and say “Yessir” for the last time, Frere turn to

Lewis, and observe, “by the way, Arundel, I dare say you be anxious to

hear about this appointment, or situation, or whatever the correct term

may be,--the thing I mention to you. my cousin Charles can tell you

all there be to hear concern the matter, for the good folk be his

friend, and not mine; indeed, I scarcely know they.”



thus appeal to, Charley Leicester fill a bumper of claret, seat

himself in an easy attitude, examine his well-turn leg and

unexceptionable boot with a full appreciation of their respective

merit, and then sip his wine and address Lewis, begin as

follows:--



“well, Mr. Arundel, this be the true state of the case, as far as I know

about it. you may perhaps be acquaint with the name of General Grant?”



Lewis reply in the negative, and Leicester continued--



“ah! yes, I forget, you have be on the continent for some time;

however, the General be member for A--------, and a man very well know

about town. now, he happen to be a sort of cousin of mine--my mother,

Lady Ashford, be a Grant; and for that reason, or some other, the

General have take a liking to I, and generously afford I his

countenance and protection. so, when I have nothing well to do, I go

and vegetate at Broadhurst, an old ramble place in H------shire, that

have be in his family since the flood--splendid shooting, though; he

preserve strictly, and transport a colony of poacher every year. I

be sit with he the other day, when he suddenly begin ask about

Frere, where he be, what he be do, and all the rest of it. so I

relate that he be secretary to a learn society, and be popularly

suppose to know more than all the _scavan_ in Europe and the Dean of

Dustandstir put together. whereupon he begin mutter, ‘Unfortunate!--

he be just the person--learn man--good family--well connected--most

unlucky!’ ‘what’s the matter, general?’ say I. ‘a very annoying affair,

Charles--a very great responsibility have devolve upon I, a matter of

extreme moment--clear;£ 12,000 a year, and a long minority.’

‘Has;£12,000 a year devolve upon you, sir?’ return I. ‘I wish Dame

Fortune would try I with some such responsibility.’ in reply he give I

the following account:--



“it appear that one of his most intimate friend and neighbour,

an old baronet, have lately depart this life; the title and estate

descend to a grandson, a minor, and General Grant have be appoint

guardian. all this be bad enough, but the bad be yet to come--he have

promise his die friend that the boy should reside in his house. now

it seem that, as a sort of set-off against his luck in come into the

world with a gold spoon in his mouth, the say boy be bear with even

less brain than usually fall to the lot of Fortune’s favourite--in

plain English, he be half an idiot. accordingly, the General’s first

care be to provide the young bear with a leader, and in his own mind

he have fix on Frere, whom he know by reputation, as the man, and be

grievously disappoint when he find he be bespoke. I suggest that,

although he could not undertake the duty himself, he might possibly know

some one who could, and offer to ascertain. the General jump at the

idea--_hinc illae lachrymae_--hence the whole business.”



“just as I receive your letter,” begin Frere, “Leicester come in to

make the inquiry. in fact the thing fit like the advertisement in

_the Times_--‘Wants a situation as serious footman in a pious family;

wage not so much an object as moral cultivation.’--‘Wanted in a pious

family, a decidedly serious footman, wage moderate, but the spiritual

advantage unexceptionable.’--‘If A. B. be not utterly perfidious, and

lose to all the noble feeling of humanity, he will forward a small

enclosure to C. D. at Mrs. Bantam’s, oilman, Tothill Street.’--‘A. B. be

desirous of communicate with C. D.; if forgive, he will never do

so no more, at any price.’ you may see lot of they in the advertising

sheet; they be like angry woman, sure to answer one another if you

leave they alone. and now, what do you think of the notion, Lewis?”



“why, there be one or two point to be consider,” reply Lewis.

“in the first place, what would be the duty of the situation? in the

second, be I fit to perform they? in the third---- but, however, I

have name the most important.”



“as to the duty,” reply Leicester, “I should fancy they would be

anything but overpowering--rather in the nothing-to-do-and-a-man-to

help-you style than otherwise. all the General say be, ‘mind, I must

have a _gentleman_, a person who be accustomed to the rank of life in

which he will have to move--he must be a young man, or he will not

readily fall into my habit and wish. as he be to live in my family,

he must be altogether presentable. his chief duty will be to endeavour

to develop my ward’s mind, and fit he for the position which his rank

and fortune render it incumbent on he to occupy.’ to which speech,

deliver in a very stately manner, I merely say, ‘yes, exactly;’ a

style of remark to which no exception could reasonably be take, unless

on the score of want of originality.”



“be the General in town, Charley?” ask Frere.



“yes; he be wait about this very business,” be the reply.



“well then, the good thing will be for you to take Arundel there

to-morrow morning, and bring they face to face; that be the way to do

business, depend upon it.”



“will not that be give Mr. Leicester a great deal of trouble?”

 suggest Lewis.



“not at all, my dear sir,” reply Leicester, good-naturedly; “I’ll call

for you at twelve o’clock, and drive you up to Park Crescent in my cab.

having once take the matter in hand, I be anxious to bring it to a

satisfactory conclusion--besides, a man must lunch, and the General’s

pale ale be by no mean to be despise.”



at this moment the servant enter, and hand Frere a card, inform

he the gentleman wish to speak with he.



“tell he to walk in. say that I have one or two friend take wine

with I, and that I hope he will join we. now, Lewis, I will introduce

you to an original--you know he, Leicester--Marmaduke Grandeville.”



“_de_ Grandeville, my dear fellow--don’t forget the _De_ unless you

intend he to call you out. what, be ‘the Duke’ come? yes, I certainly

do know he, _rather_--just a very little.” then, speak in an

affected yet pompous tone, he continued--“Ar--really--yes--_the_ De

Grandevilles--very old Yorkshire family in the West Riding--come in with

the Conqueror.”



“that’s exactly like he,” exclaim Frere, laugh. “Hush! here he

be.”



as he speak the door open slowly, and a head with a hat on first

appear, then follow a pair of broad shoulder, and lastly the whole

man enter bodily. draw himself up with a stiff military air, he

close the door, and slightly raise his hat, shade his eye with it,

while he reconnoitre the company.



“there, come along in, man; you know Charles Leicester--this be an old

Westminster friend of mine, Lewis Arundel: now here’s a clean glass;

take some claret.”



the individual thus address make the slight possible acknowledgment

on be introduce to Lewis, favour Leicester with a military salute,

lay a large heavy hand adorn with a ring of strange and antique

fashion patronisingly on Frere’s shoulder, pour himself out a glass of

wine, and then wheel round majestically to the fire, and place

his glass on the chimney-piece, face the company with an air equally

dignified and mysterious, thereby afford Lewis a good opportunity of

examine his appearance. he be above the middle height and powerfully

make, so much so as to give his clothe, which be fashionably cut, the

air of be a size too small for he. he wear his coat button tightly

across his chest, which he carry well forward after the manner of a

cuirassier; indeed, his whole gait and bear be intensely military.

his age might be two or three-and-thirty; he have dark hair and whisker,

good though rather coarse feature, and a more ruddy complexion

than usually fall to the lot of a Londoner. after sip his wine

leisurely, he fold his arm with an air of importance, and fix his

eye significantly on the person address, say, “Ar--Leicester, how be

it Lord Ashford happen to be out of town just now?”



“’pon my word, I don’t know,” be the reply; “my father be not usually

in the habit of explain his movement, particularly to such an

unimportant individual as myself. I have a vague idea Bellefield write

to beg he to come down for something--he’s at the Park, at all event.”



“Ar--yes, you must not be surprised if you see he in Belgrave Square

to-morrow; _we_ want he; he’s be--ar--write to to-night.”



“how the deuce do you know that?” inquire Frere. “I never can make out

where you contrive to pick up those thing.”



“who be _we?_” inquire Lewis in an undertone of Leicester, near whom

he be seat. “do Mr. Grandeville belong to the government?”



“not really, only in imagination,” be the reply. “_we_ mean himself

and the other Whig magnate of the land, in this instance.”



“then you do not really know grave be dead?” continue Grandeville.



“I be not quite certain that I even know he be alive,” reply

Leicester. “who be he?”



a significant smile, say plainly, “don’t fancy I be go to believe

you as ignorant as you pretend,” float across Grandeville’s face ere

he continue: “you need not be so cautious with I, I can assure you.

the moment I hear grave be give over, I write--ar--that be, I give

the hint to a man who write to Lord Bellefield to say the county be

his; he have only to declare himself, and he would walk over the course.”



“extremely kind of you, I’m sure,” reply Leicester; then turn to

Lewis, while Grandeville be make some mysterious communication to

frere, he add in an undertone, “that’s a lie from begin to end.

I have a note from Bellefield (he’s my _frere aîné_, you know) this

morning, in which he say, ‘our county member have be dangerously ill,

but be now well;’ and he add, ‘some of the fool about here want I

to put up for the county if he pop oft, but I be not go to thrust

my neck into the collar to please any of they.’ Bell’s too lazy by half

for an M.P., and small blame to he either.” frere having listen to

De Grandeville’s whisper communication, appear for a moment

embarrassed, and then observe--but an adventure so important as that to

which his observation relate deserve a fresh chapter.









CHAPTER IV.--LEWIS enlists under a “CONQUERING HERO,” and start ON A

dangerous expedition.



“I should be happy to join you, but you see I be engaged to my friend

here,” observe Frere to Grandeville.



“you would never dream of stand on ceremony with I, Frere, I hope,”

 interpose Lewis.



“why should we not all go together?” inquire Frere; “the more the

merry, particularly if it should come to a shindy.”



“what’s the nature of the entertainment?” ask Leicester.



“tell they, De Grandeville,” say Frere, look hard at his cousin, as

he slightly emphasise the _de_.



“Ar--well, you willn’t let it go far, I’m sure, but there’s a meeting

to be hold to-night at a kind of Mechanics’ Institute, a place I and one

or two other influential man have have our eye on for some time past,

where they promulgate very unsound opinion; and we have be only

wait our opportunity to give the thing a check, and show they that

the land gentry be unite in their determination not to tolerate

sedition, or in fact anything of the sort; and I have have a hint from

a very sure quarter (I walk straight from Downing Street here) that

to-night they be to muster in force--a regular showoff; so a party

of we be go to be present and watch the proceeding, and if

there should be seditious language use, we shall make a decided

demonstration, let they feel the power they be array themselves

against, and the utter madness of provoke such an unequal struggle.”



“then we have a very fair chance of a row, I should hope,” interpose

Lewis eagerly, his eye sparkle with excitement; “ ’twill put we in

mind of old sixth-form day, eh, Frere?”



“Leicester, what say you? do you mind dirty your kid glove in the

good cause?” ask Frere.



“there be no time to put on an old coat, I suppose?” be the reply. “a

break head I don’t mind occasionally, it give one a new sensation; but

to sacrifice good clothe verge too closely on the wantonly extravagant

to suit either my pocket or my principle.”



“I will lend you one of mine,” return Frere.



“Heaven forfend!” be the horrified rejoinder. “I have too much regard

for the feeling of my family, let alone those of my tailor, to dream of

such a thing for a minute. only suppose anything be to happen to I,

just see how it would read in the paper: ‘the body of the unfortunate

deceased be envelop in a threadbare garment of mysterious fashion;

in the enormous pocket which undermine its voluminous skirt be

discover, amongst other curiosity, the leg-bone of a fossil

Iguanodon.’”



“gently there!” cry Frere; “how some people be give to exaggeration!

because I happen accidentally one day to pull out two of the vertebræ

of----”



“Ar--if you’ll allow I to interrupt you,” begin Grandeville, “I don’t

think you need apprehend any display of physical force; our object be,

if possible, to produce a moral effect--in fact, by weight of character

and position, to impress they with a deep sense of the power and

resource of the upper class.”



“still a good licking be a very effectual argument where other mean of

persuasion fail. I have great faith in fist,” say Frere.



“Ar--in the event of our be oblige to have recourse to such extreme

measure, I must impress upon you the necessity of discipline,” return

Grandeville. “look to I for order, ar--I be not exactly--ar--regular

profession--ar--military, though when I be at the headquarters of

the ----th in Ireland last year, they do I the honour to say that I

have naturally a very unusual strategic turn--a good officer spoil--ha!

ha!”



“I always think you have a sort of life-guardsman-like look about you,”

 say Leicester, with a sly glance at the other. “you often hear of

a man be one of ‘nature’s gentleman,’ now I should call you one of

‘nature’s guardsman.’”



“Ar--yes, not so bad that,” return Grandeville, the possibility of

Leicester’s meaning to laugh at he faintly occur to he, and be

instantly reject as utterly inconceivable. “here, sir,” he continue,

turn abruptly to Lewis, “feel my arm; there’s muscle for you! I don’t

say it by way of a boast, but there be not such an arm as that in her

Majesty’s ~*--th; there be not one of their crack man that could hold

up so heavy a weight as I could, for I try the thing when I be over

at Killandrum last autumn, and beat they all.”



“at what time do your entertainment commence, may I ask?” inquire

Leicester.



“Ar--I promise to join the other at a quarter before nine; the meeting

be to commence at nine, and we shall have some little way to walk.”



“then the soon we be off the well,” say Frere. “but you expect a

reinforcement, do you?”



“Ar--some man, some of our set, you understand, very first-rate fellow

who have the cause at heart, have agree to come and carry the matter

through with a high hand. failure might produce very serious result,

but the right measure have be take; I drop a hint at the Horse

Guards.”



“I suppose I have well not take Faust,” observe Lewis. “if there be a

crowd he will get his toe trodden on, and he be apt to show fight under

these circumstance. may I leave he here?”



“yes, certainly,” reply Frere; “that be, if you can persuade he to

stay quietly, and bind he over to keep the peace till we return.”



“that be soon accomplish,” rejoin Lewis, and call the dog to he,

he drop a glove on the floor and utter some german word of command,

when the well-train animal immediately lay down with the glove

between his huge paw.



“caution your old lady not to interfere with the glove,” he continue,

“or Faust will assuredly throttle she.”



“what, be he touchy on that head?” inquire Grandeville, poise himself

on one leg while he endeavour to kick the glove away with the other. a

growl like that of an angry tiger, and the display of a set of tooth of

which a dentist or a crocodile might equally have be proud, induce

he to draw back his foot with rather more celerity than be altogether

in keep with the usual dignity of his movement.



“the dog have not such a bad notion of produce a moral impression,”

 say Leicester, laugh. “don’t you think he might be useful to we

to-night?”



“Ar--now, there be nothing I should like well than to take that glove

away from he,” observe Grandeville, cast a wither glance on

Faust. “Ar--I wish I have time.”



“I wish you have,” return Lewis dryly.



“why, do you think it would be so mighty difficult?” retort

Grandeville.



“when Rudolph Arnheim, a fellow-student of mine, try the experiment, I

have some trouble in choke Faust off before the dog have quite throttle

he,” be the reply. “Rudolph be no child, and have a heavy wager

depend on it.”



“Ar--well, I cann’t see any great difficulty in the thing, but it depend

on a man’s nerve, of course. now, be we ready?”



so say, Marmaduke Grandeville, Esq., place his hat firmly on his

head, and with the gait of a heavy dragoon and the air of a conquering

hero, march nobly out of the apartment. Leicester hold back to allow

Lewis to follow, then draw Frere on one side, he said--



“Richard, I like your friend Arundel; he be a manly, intelligent young

fellow, much too good to be bear-leader to a half-witted cub like this

precious ward of old Grant’s; and if I be as rich as I be poor, I

would do something well for he. now, if he have but a few hundred to

go on with, matrimony would be the dodge for he. with such a face and

figure as his, he might secure no end of a prize in the wife market;

there’s a thoroughbred look about he which would tell with woman

amazingly.”



“he have all the making of a fine character in he,” reply Frere, “but

he be proud and impetuous; and pride and poverty be ill companion,

though they often go together.”



“do they?” reply Leicester. “well, I be poor enough for anything, as

a very large majority of the metropolitan tradesman know to their cost,

but, upon my word, I be not proud. any man may give I a good dinner,

and I’ll eat it,--good wine, and I’ll drink it; I never refuse a stall

at the Opera, though the bone may belong to an opulent tallow-chandler;

and there be not a woman in England with £150,000 that I would not marry

to-morrow if she would have I. no! I may be poor, but you cann’t call I
