proud.” and place his arm through that of his cousin, they descend

to the street together, and rejoin Lewis and his companion.









CHAPTER V.--IS of a DECIDEDLY WARLIKE CHARACTER.



the place of rendezvous for the “gallant defender of the british

constitution,” as Leicester have designate the little party, be a cigar

shop in the immediate vicinity of the building in which the meeting be

to be hold. on their arrival they perceive that the shop be already

occupy by several young man, who be lounge over the counter,

bandy jest and compliment with a ringlete young lady, who appear

thoroughly self-possess and quite equal to the part she have to

perform, have through all her pretty coquetry a shrewd eye to

business, and reserve she most fascinating smile for the most

inveterate smoker.



as Grandeville enter the shop, which he do with a most lordly and

dignified air, he be welcome with general acclamation.



“all hail, Macbeth!” exclaim a thin young man, with a white greatcoat

and a face to match, throw himself into a tragedy attitude.



“most noble commander!” begin another of the group. “most illustrious De

Grandeville! how is----”



“your anxious mother?” interrupt a short, muscular little fellow, with

as rich a brogue as ever claim Cork for its county.



“Hush! be quiet, Pat; we have no time for nonsense now, man,” cry a

tall youth with a profusion of light curl hair, a prominent hooked

nose, a merry smile, and a pair of wicked grey eye, which appear

to possess the faculty of look in every direction at once. “you be

late, De Grandeville,” he add, come forward.



“Ar--no, sir; five minute good by the Horse Guards. Ar--I should have

be here soon, but I have be--ar--recruit, you see. Mr. Bracy,

Mr. Frere, Mr. Arundel--you know Leicester?”



“delighted to see such an addition to our force,” reply Bracy,

bow; then shake hand with Leicester, he add in an undertone,

“walk with I when we start; I have a word to say to you.” Leicester

nod in assent, and then proceed to accost other of the party with

whom he be acquaint.



“Ar--now, gentleman, will you please to attend to order?” begin

Grandeville, raise his voice.



“hear, hear!” cry the pale young man, faintly.



“we’ll do it betther if you’d be houldin’ yer tongue, maybe,” interpose

the hero from Cork, who, be interpret, be none other

than Lieutenant McDermott of the Artillery, believe by the

Commander-in-chief to be at that very moment on duty at Woolwich.



“Ar--you be to divide yourself into three or four body.”



“faith, we must get blind drunk, and see double twice over then, before

we can do that,” remark the son of Erin argumentatively.



“now, Paddy, be quiet,” say Bracy, soothingly; “you know you never get

so far in your arithmetic as vulgar fraction, so you cann’t be suppose

to understand the matter.”



a somewhat forcible rejoinder be drown by Grandeville, who continue,

in his most sonorous tone: “Ar--you will then proceed to the hall

of meeting, and make your way quietly to the right side, as near the

platform as possible. there--keep together, and attract as little

attention as you can, and Mr. Bracy will transmit such direction to you

as circumstance may render advisable. do you all clearly understand?”



a general shout of assent, vary by a muttered “not in the slight

degree,” from McDermott, be follow by the order, “then march!” and in

another moment the party be _en route_. the pale young man, who be

in his secret soul rather alarmed than otherwise, have attach himself

firmly to Frere, with whom he be slightly acquaint, and who he

think would take care of he, so Lewis be leave to pair off with

Leicester.



as they proceed, the latter begin: “depend upon it, there’s some trick

in all this, probably intend for Grandeville’s benefit; that fellow

Bracy be one of the most inveterate practical joker extant, and he

seem particularly busy to-night; he’s a clerk in the Home Office, and

Grandeville believe in he to an immense extent; but here he come.

well, Bracy, what be it, man?”



“be your friend safe?” inquire Bracy aside, glance at Lewis as he

speak.



“the most cautious man in London,” be the reply, “and one who

appreciate our noble commander thoroughly; so now allow we a peep

behind the scene.”



“well, the matter stand thus,” return Bracy. “I be walk with

Duke Grandeville one night about three week ago, when we chance to

encounter the good folk come away from one of these meeting; they

be nothing very formidable--a fair sample of young Newgate Street,

youthful patriot from Snow Hill, embryo republicans of St. Paul’s

Churchyard, Barbican, and other purlieus of Cockaignia, lead by a few

choice spirit--copy clerk, who hide their heroism from the light of

day in lawyer’ office, bookseller’ shopman from the row, who regard

themselves as distinguish literary character, and prate of the

sovereignty of the press, and the like. well, as might be expect, they

discourse most ferociously, and the Duke, overhear some of their

conversation, be deeply scandalise, and fancy he have discover a

second Cato Street conspiracy. the thing appear to promise fun, so I

encourage he in the idea, and we attend the next meeting, when

they talk the usual style of radical clap-trap. everything be an

abuse--the rich be tyrant, the poor slave, and property require

transfer (_i.e_., from its present possessor to themselves); they

know they never should be king, so they cry down monarchy; but they

trust that, with strong lung and good-luck, they might become pay

delegate, therefore they clamour for a republic. there be much

noise, but no talent; sanguinary theory be discuss, which they have

neither mind nor mean to enable they to carry out; in short, the place

be one of those innocent sedition shop which act as safety valve to

carry off popular discontent, and ensure the health and vigour of the

british constitution. of course, however, Grandeville do not see it in

that point of view, and from that night forth he become positively rabid

on the subject; so it enter the head of some of we that we might

improve the occasion by persuade he that he might, through I,

communicate information to the Home Office (I need scarcely tell you

that it never reach the authority there), and we have lead he on

sweetly and easily, till he positively believe that he be to be at the

Hall to-night as an accredited government agent, with full power to

suppress the meeting, and I know not what else.”



“but surely you’ll get into a fearful row,” urge Leicester.



“we be safe for a bit of a shindy, no doubt,” be the cool reply; “in

fact I do not consider that the thing would go off properly without it,

so I bring an Irishman with I to render it inevitable; but I have

bribe a doorkeeper, and let the bad come to the bad, we can easily

fight our way out.”



“to be sure we can,” exclaim Lewis, “lick a hundred such fellow as

you have describe. this be glorious fun; I would not have miss it for

the world.”



Bracy glance at he for a moment with a look of intense approval, then

shake he warmly by the hand, he say, “Sir, I’m delighted to make

your acquaintance; your sentiment do you honour, sir. be you much

accustomed to row of this nature, may I ask?”



“I have be resident in Germany for the last three year,” be the

reply; “and although they have a very fair notion of an _émeute_ after

their own fashion, they don’t understand the use of the fist as we do.”



“there be two grand rule for crowd-fighting,” return Bracy. “first,

make play with your elbow, Cockneys’ rib be as sensitive as nigger’

shin; secondly, if it come to blow, strike at their face, and never

waste your strength; but when you _do_ make a hit, drop your man if

possible; it settle he, and frighten the rest. here we be!” so

say, he turn into a kind of passage which lead to an open door,

through which they pass into the body of the hall.



it be a large room with a vaulted ceiling, and appear capable of

hold from five to six hundred person. at the farth end of it be

a platform, raise some foot, and divide from the rest of the hall by

a stout wooden railing. the room be light with gas, and considerably

more than half fill. although the majority of the audience appear to

answer the description Bracy have give of they, yet along the side

of the apartment be range number of sturdy artisan and craftsman,

amongst whom many a stalwart form and stern determine visage might be

detect.



“there be some rather awkward customer here to-night,” whisper

Leicester. “if we chance to get black eye, Arundel, we must postpone

our visit to the General to-morrow.”



“the man that give I a black eye shall have something to remember it

by, at all event,” return Lewis quickly.



“Hush! that fellow hear you,” say Leicester.



Lewis glance in the direction indicate, and meet the sinister gaze,

of a tall, heavy-build mechanic, in a rough greatcoat, who frown

menacingly when he find that he be observe. Lewis smile carelessly

in reply, and proceed after Bracy up the room. when he have pass, the

man, still keep his eye upon he, quit his seat and follow

at some little distance. on reach the upper end of the room they

perceive Grandeville and two or three other, among whom be McDermott,

on the platform, while Frere and the rest of their party have congregate

on and near a flight of five or six step lead to it from the body of

the hall.



“Bravo, Grandeville!” observe Bracy, in an undertone, to Leicester. “do

you mark that! he have secure a retreat--good generalship, very. I shall

have to believe in he if he go on as well as he have commence. Hark!

they be begin to give tongue.”



as he conclude, a little fat man come forward and say a good deal

about the honour which have be do he in be allow the privilege

of open the evening’s proceeding, to which he append a long and

utterly incomprehensible account of the object of the meeting. his zeal

be evident, but nature have never intend he for an orator, and the

chance of life have fit he with a short husky cough, so that nobody

be very sorry when he cede the rostrum to his “esteemed friend, if he

might be allow to say so (which he be), Jabez Broadcom.” this Jabez

Broadcom be evidently a great gun, and his come forward create no

small sensation. he be a tall, gaunt-look man, with straight weak

hair and an unhealthy complexion; but his great feature, in every sense

of the word, be his mouth.



it _be_ a mouth, not only for mutton, but for every other purpose to

which that useful aperture could be apply; at present it be to

be devoted to the task of convey its owner’s mighty thought, in

appropriate language, to the eager listener who surround he.



this gentleman then, having, by dint of draw in his lip and

thrust they out again, and roll his eye so fearfully as to

suggest a sudden attack of english cholera, get up his steam to the

require height, proceed to inform the assembly that they be,

individually and collectively, free and enlighten citizen of the

great metropolis of Europe, prepare to recognise their sacred right,

and resolve to go forth as one man to assert and maintain they. having

impart this information (through his nose, for the great effect),

he begin to ask himself a species of Pinnock’s catechism, so to speak,

which run somewhat after the follow fashion:--



“and why be I here to-night? because I love profit? no. because I love

personal distinction? no. because I love my country? yes. because I

would not see her child slave? yes. because purse-proud oppressor,

revel in their wealth, trample on the honest poor man? yes.”



having say by heart several page of this, in which he be exceedingly

well up, and which he rattle off most fluently, he continued--



“but such tyranny shall not always be tolerate. british freeman, whose

proud boast it be that they have never bear a foreign yoke, shall no

long crouch beneath a despotic rule at home. the atrocious barbarity

of a brutal poor-law, which tax honest householder to furnish

salaried ruffian with power to drag the half-eat crust from the

famished jaw of helpless poverty----”



(a slight sensation be here occasion by McDermott mention for the

benefit of the meeting in general, and the orator himself in particular,

his conviction that the last sentence be “very pretty indeed,” together

with a polite inquiry as to whether he could not be so kind as to say it

again. peace be restore after sundry shout of “turn he out!”)



“Shame!” etc., the orator resumed--



“let they build their bastile, let they tear the wife from her husband,

the mother from her child; let they crowd their prison-house with the

honest son of labour whom their brutality have force into crime--the

poor man need never dread starvation while the hulk hunger and the

gallow gape for he--but a day of retribution be at hand; let the

tyrant tremble beneath their gild roof--those unjust usurper of

the soil--the poor man’s bitter foe, the land gentry, as they

arrogantly style themselves, must be cut off and root out.”



“pretty strong, that!” observe Bracy, in a whisper.



“Ar--this willn’t do, you know!” return Grandeville, in an equally low

voice. “I must, really--ar--interfere.”



“well hear he out,” rejoin Bracy, “and then get up and address they

yourself.” to which suggestion, after a slight remonstrance, the former

agree; but such a shine light as Mr. Jabez Broadcom be not to be put

out as quickly as they desire; he be the great card of the evening,

and know it, and prolong his speech for a good three-quarter of an

hour, during which time he theoretically dethrone the Queen, abolish

the Lords and Commons, seat a National Convention in St. Stephen’s,

and make all the rich poor, and the poor both rich and happy, whilst he

practically render himself so hoarse as to be nearly inaudible;

for which gallant exertion in the cause of liberty he receive the

tumultuous applause of the meeting, together with Lieut. McDermott’s

express conviction that he be “a broth of a boy entirely,” together

with an anxious inquiry, “whether his mother have many more like he.”



when Broadcom retire from the rostrum there appear some

misunderstanding and confusion as to his successor; take advantage

of which, Grandeville look at Bracy, who nod, add, “now’s your

time! go in, and win;” then, catch a cadaverous-look individual

who be about to advance by the shoulder, and twist he round, he

exclaim, “now, my man, stand out of the way, will you? this gentleman

be go to address the company.” he next thrust Grandeville forward,

and pat he encouragingly on the back, leave he to his own device.

that heroic gentleman, having bow to his audience with much grace and

dignity, wave his hand to command attention, and begin as follows:--



“Ar--listen to I, my friend! Ar--hem--I be prepared to admit--that be,

it be impossible to deny--that many great and serious evil exist in the

complicated social fabric of this glorious country. the vast increase of

population----”



“owe to the introduction of chloroform,” suggest Bracy.



“though slightly check by----”



“the alarming consumption of Morrison’s Pills,” interpose the

Irishman----



“the wise facility afford for emigration,” continue Grandeville,

not heed these interruption, “be one chief cause of the poverty and

distress which, though greatly exaggerate by the false statement of

evil-dispose and design person (groan and cry of ‘hear!’), be

to be find even in this metropolis, beneath the foster care of

an enlightened and paternal government (increase murmur of

dissatisfaction). but if you believe that these evil be likely to be

redress by such measure as have be point out to you this evening,

or that anarchy and rebellion can lead to any other result than misery

and ruin--ar--I tell you, that you be fearfully mistaken! Ar--as a

man, possess of--ar--no inconsiderable influence--and ar--intimately

connect with those power against which you be madly array

yourself, I warn you!”



here the excitement and dissatisfaction, which have be rapidly

increase, reach a pitch which threaten to render the speaker

inaudible; and amid cry of “who be he?”--“an informer!”--“government

spy!”--“turn he out!”--“throw he over!” several person rise from

their seat and attempt to force their way on to the platform, but

be keep back by Lewis and other of Grandeville’s party, who, as have

be already mention, have take possession of the flight of step,

which afford the only legitimate mean of access from the body of the

hall.



undisturbe by these hostile demonstration, Grandeville continue, at

the top of his voice,--“I warn you that you be provoke an unequal

struggle,--that you be bring upon yourselves a fearful retribution.

even now I be arm with authority to disperse this meeting--to----”



what more he would have add the reader be not fate to learn, for at

this moment the man in the rough greatcoat, who have follow Lewis from

the entrance of the room, exclaim, “come on, we be not go to

stand this, you know; never mind the step,” seize the railing of

the platform, and draw himself up, spring over, follow by several

other. in an instant all be confusion. Grandeville, take in some

degree by surprise, after knock down a couple of his assailant, be

overpower, and, amid cry of “throw he over,” hurry to the edge

of the platform; here, grasp the rail with both hand, he struggle

violently to prevent the accomplishment of their purpose.



“come along, boy! we must rescue he,” exclaim Bracy; and suit

the action to the word, he bound forward, and hit right and left,

reach the scene of conflict. Lewis and the other, abandon the

step, follow his example, and the row become general. for some

minute the uproar be terrific; blow be give and receive; blood

begin to flow from sundry nose; and certain eye that have begin the

evening blue, brown, or grey, as the case might be, assume a hue dark

as Erebus. as for Lewis, he knock down one of the fellow who have

hold of Grandeville; then he pick up the Irishman, who of course have

single out and attack the big man in the crowd (none other indeed

than the rough-coated patriot, who appear a sort of leader among

they), and be immediately fell by he to the ground; then

he assist Frere in extricate the pale-face youth from three

individual of questionable honesty, who be avail themselves of the

confusion to empty his pocket; as he do so he feel himself seize

with a grasp of iron, and turn his head, find he be collar by the

gigantic leader. a violent but ineffectual effort to free himself only

serve to convince he that in point of strength he be no match for

his antagonist, who, regard he with a smile of gratified malice,

exclaim, “now then, young feller, I’ve be a-wait to get hold of

you. how about a black eye now?” as he speak he draw he forward with

one hand and strike at he savagely with the other. avoid the blow by

suddenly dodge aside, Lewis close with his adversary, and insert

his knuckle within the fold of his neckcloth, tighten it, until in

self-defence, and in order to avoid strangulation, the fellow be force

to loosen his grasp of Lewis’s collar. the instant he feel himself free,

Lewis, give the neckcloth a final twist, and at the same time press

his knuckle into the man’s throat, so as for the moment almost to

throttle he, step back a couple of pace, and spring forward

again before the other have time to recover himself, hit up under his

guard and succeed in plant a stinging and well-direct blow

exactly between his eye; this, follow by a similar application rather

low on the face, settle the matter. reel backwards, his antagonist

lose his footing and fall heavily to the ground, drag one of his

companion down with he in a futile attempt to save himself. the fall

of their leader throw a damp on the spirit of the other; and although

those in the rear be still clamorous with threat and vociferation,

the member of the crowd in more immediate proximity to the little party

show small inclination to renew the attack.



[illustration: 0050]



“now’s our time for get away,” say Bracy. “make a bold push for the

door.”



“Ar--I should say,” rejoin Grandeville, one of whose eye be

completely close from the effect of a blow, and whose coat be hang

about he in riband, “let we despatch one of our party for the police

and military, and stand firm and maintain our ground till they come up,

then capture the ringleader and clear the room.”



“nonsense,” say Leicester, who, despite his regard for his wardrobe,

have behave most spiritedly during the skirmish. “we shall all be

murder before they appear; besides” (he add aside to Bracy), “it

will be make much too serious a business of it; we should get into

some tremendous scrape.”



“yes, that’s true,” say Bracy; then turn to Grandeville, he add,

“I don’t think my instruction would bear we out if we be to go any

far. remember, we be only to make a pacific demonstration.”



“and faith, if break head, and get a return in kind, come under

that same denomination, it’s a pretty decent one we’ve make already,

’pon I conscience,” put in McDermott, wipe away the blood that be

still trickle from a cut in his forehead.



while these remark be bandy from one to another, the party have

contrive to make their way from the platform, and be now in the body

of the room, strive to push through the crowd towards the side door.

this at every step become more and more difficult, till at length they

be so completely hem in that further progression become impossible,

and it be evident that a fresh attack upon they be meditate.

fortunately, however, they be not far from the point of egress, and

Bracy, having catch the eye of his ally the doorkeeper, who be on the

alert, exclaim, “now, Grandeville, we must fight our way through these

fellow and gain the door; there’s nothing for it but a spirited charge.

you and I, Frere and his friend, and Paddy have well go first as a sort

of wedge.”



“Ar--head the column and break the enemy’s rank, ar--yes, be you all

ready? charge!”



as he give the word they rush forward in a compact body, and knock

down and push aside all who oppose they, succeed in reach

the door. here a short delay occur while Bracy and his friend be

open it, and several of their late antagonist, irritate at the

prospect of their escape, incite the other to attack they, so that

before their egress be secure even the irish lieutenant have have

fight enough to satisfy he, and the pale young man, having long

since give himself up as a lost mutton, actually faint with fear and

over-exertion, and be drag from under the foot of the combatant

and carry out by Frere and Lewis, but for whom his mortal career would

then and there have end.



how, as they emerge into the street, a party of the police arrive and

cause more confusion and more broken head; and how Grande-ville and

the Irishman on the one hand, and sundry Chartists, with Lewis’s late

antagonist among they, on the other, be jointly and severally take

into custody and march to the station-house, where they spend the

night; and how Leicester contrive just in the nick of time to catch an

intelligent cab, into which he, Lewis, Frere, and the faint victim

with the pallid physiognomy compress themselves, and be convey

rapidly from the scene of action, it boot not to relate: suffice it to

say that a certain barrel of oyster, flank by a detachment of

pint bottle of stout, which have take up their position on Frere’s

dining-table during the absence of its master, sustain an attack about

half-past eleven o’clock that night which prove that the mode in which

their assailant have pass the evening have in no way impair their

respective appetite.









CHAPTER VI.--IN which LEWIS ARUNDEL SKETCHES a COW, and the AUTHOR draw

a young lady.



it be about noon on the day follow the event narrate in the last

chapter. Frere have depart to his office at the scientific institution

some two hour since, and Lewis and Faust be look out of the

window, when a well-appoint cab dash round the corner of a cross

street, and a pair of lavender-colour kid-glove draw up a splendid

bay horse, who arch his proud neck and champ the bit, impatient of

delay, till a young male child in livery coat and top-boot roll off

the back of the vehicle and station itself before the animal’s nose,

which act of self-devotion appear to mesmerise he into tranquillity,

and afford the occupant of the cab time to spring out and knock at

Frere’s door. five minute more see Leicester and Lewis seat side by

side and drive rapidly in the direction of Park Crescent.



“I don’t know how you feel this morning, Arundel,” begin Leicester; “but

positively when I first wake I could scarcely move. I’m black and blue

all over, I believe.”



“I must confess to be rather stiff,” be the reply, “and my left hand

be unproducible. I cut my knuckle against the nose of that tall fellow

when I knock he down, and shall be force to wear a glove till it

heal.”



“you do that uncommonly well,” return Leicester; “the man be as

strong as Hercules, and vicious into the bargain. he evidently have hear

what you say about a black eye, and mean mischief. I be come to

help you when you finish he off.”



“it would have be most provoking to have be disfigure just at this

time,” rejoin Lewis. “one could not very well go to propose oneself as

a mentor for youth with a black eye obtain in something nearly akin to

a street row.”



“no,” say Leicester; “the General would consider our last night’s

exploit as dreadfully _infra dig._ he be quite one of the old school,

and reckon Sir Charles Grandison a model for gentleman. you must be

careful to avoid the free-and-easy style of the present day with he;

but I think you’ll suit he exactly; there’s naturally something of the

_preux chevalier, héro de roman_ cut about you that will go down with

he amazingly.”



“in plain English, you consider I stiff and affected,” return Lewis.

“do not scruple to tell I if it be so.”



“Stiff, yes; affected, no,” be the rejoinder. “indeed, your manner be

unusually simple and natural when you thaw a little, but at first you

be--well, I hardly know how to describe it; but there be something

about you unlike the man one usually meet. you have a sort of

half-defiant way of look at people, a sort of ‘you’d well not insult

I, sir’ expression. I don’t know that I should have observe it towards

myself, but it be your manner to Grandeville that particularly strike

I. I have not annoy you by my frankness?” he add interrogatively,

find that Lewis do not reply. regardless of this question, Lewis

remain silent for a minute or two, then suddenly turn to his

companion, and speak in a low, hurried voice, he said--



“can you conceive no reason for such a manner? be there not enough in

my position to account for that, ay, and more? by birth I be any

man’s equal. my father be of an old family, a captain in the Austrian

service, and in the high sense of the word a gentleman. I have

receive a gentleman’s education. up to the present time I have

associate with gentleman on term of equality, and now suddenly,

through no fault of my own, I be in effect a beggar. the very errand we

be upon prove it. through the kindness of Frere and of yourself,--a

stranger,--I be about to receive a favourable recommendation to some

proud old man as a hire servant; for though in name it may not be so,

in fact I shall be nought but a hireling! be it strange then that I view

man with suspicion? that I be watchful lest they attempt to refuse I

the amount of courtesy due to those who, having never forfeit their

own self-respect, be entitle to the respect of other?”



he pause, and remove his hat, allow the cold breeze to blow

freely around his heated brow. Leicester, who, despite his foppery, be

thoroughly kind-hearted, be equally surprised and distressed at the

burst of feel his word have call forth, hasten to reply.



“my dear fellow, I really be--that be, ’pon my word, I have no idea

you look upon the affair in this light. I can assure you, I think you

quite mistake the matter; a tutorship be consider a very gentlemanly

occupation. if I have any work in I, I’m not at all sure I might

not--that is, it would be a very sensible thing of I to look out for

something of the kind myself. Stanhope Jones, who be up at Trinity with

I, and about the fast man of his year, run through his fortune, get

a tutorship in Lord Puzzletête’s family, go abroad with the eld

cub, and pick up a prize widow at Pisa, with tin enough to set the

lean tower straight again, if she’d have a fancy to do so.”



during this well-mean attempt at consolation Lewis have have time to come

to the conclusion that he be in the position of that unwise individual

who wear “his heart upon his sleeve for daw to peck at;” or, in plain

English, that he have be betray into a display of feeling before a

man incapable of appreciate or understand it; and a less agreeable

conviction at which to arrive we scarcely know. nothing, however,

remain but to make the good of it, which he accordingly do, by

admit the possibility that there might be much truth in Leicester’s

view of the case, and change the subject by say, “now I want you to

give I a peep at the _carte du pay_ of the unknown region I be about

to explore. I think I pretty well comprehend the General from your

description. of what other member do the family consist?”



“ah! yes, of course you must be curious to know. well, the _dramatis

persono_ be somewhat limited. first and foremost, the General,--you

comprehend he, you say?” Lewis make a sign in the affirmative, and

Leicester continue: “then we have an awful personage, who I expect will

be a severe trial to you--Miss Livingstone; she be a relation, an aunt

I think, of the General’s late wife, who live with he and keep his

house, and be the terror of my boyhood whenever I be stay down

at Broadhurst. she never be over young, I believe; at least I cann’t

imagine her anything but middle-aged, and she must now be sixty or

thereabout. for the rest, she look as if she have swallow a poker,

and, by some mysterious process of assimilation, become imbued with its

distinguish characteristic; for she be very stiff, very cold, and

as far as I know utterly impenetrable, but of a stirring disposition

withal, which lead she to interfere with everybody and everything.

lastly, there be my cousin Annie, the General’s only daughter; she

inherit her mother’s beauty, her father’s pride, her great-aunt’s

determination to have her own way, and the devil’s own love of teasing.

to set against all this, I believe she to be thoroughly good and

amiable, and everything of that kind; at all event she be a most

bewitch girl, and bid fair, under judicious management, to become

a very charming woman! fancy her mission be to reform my brother

Bellefield and render he a steady married man, and I wish her joy of

it. she come into her mother’s fortune when she be of age, and the

respective governor have set their heart upon the match.”



“and what say Lord Bellefield?” inquire Lewis listlessly.



“oh, Bell reckon she willn’t be of age, and that the match cann’t come off

these four year, by which time he expect to be so hard up that he must

marry somebody; and as there will be plenty of the needful, she will

suit his book as well as any other.”



“the young lady, of course, approve?” continue Lewis dreamily, untie

a knot in the thong of Leicester’s whip.



“catch a woman refuse a coronet,” return Leicester, as he pull up

at a house in Park Crescent so suddenly as almost to throw the bay on

his haunch.



“General Grant beg you will walk upstairs, Mr. Leicester. he be engage

at present, but desire I to say he particularly wish to see you,”

 be the reply make by a most aristocratic butler to Leicester’s inquiry

whether his master be at home. “keep the bay move, Tim. now, Arundel,

turn to the right--that’s it,” and suit the action to the word,

Charley the indolent leisurely descend from the cab, and cross the

“marble hall,” lounge up a wide staircase follow by Lewis.



“silence and solitude,” he continue, open the door of a large

drawing-room handsomely furnish. “I hope they willn’t be long before

they introduce we to the luncheon-table. oyster be popularly suppose

to give one an appetite; but the native we demolish at Frere’s last

night must have be sadly degenerate, for I declare to you I could

scarcely get through my breakfast this morning. ah! what have we

here?--a water-colour landscape in a semi-chaotic condition. Annie have

be sketch, as sure as fate. I’ll introduce a few masterly touch

and surprise she.” so say he seat himself at the table and begin

dabble with a brush.



“by Jove, I’ve do it now!” he exclaim in a tone of consternation,

after a minute’s pause. “just look here; I think I would insert the

trunk of a tree in the foreground, and the confound brush have get red

in it, so I have make a thing like a lobster and spoil the drawing.”



“I think, if you wish, I could turn it into a cow, and so get you out

of the scrape,” suggest Lewis, smile at his companion’s guilty

countenance.



“my dear fellow, the very thing,” exclaim Leicester, hastily rise

and thrust Lewis into his seat; “let’s have a cow, by all mean.

that’s famous,” he continue, as with a few graphic stroke Lewis

convert the red daub into the semblance of an animal. “Bravo! make she

an eye--now the horn--what a fascinating quadruped! where’s the tail to

come?”



“you would not see the tail in the position in which the cow be suppose

to be lie,” remonstrate Lewis.



“still, it would make it more natural,” urge Leicester. “as a personal

favour, just to oblige I, stretch a point and give she a tail.”



“there, then, I’ve twist it under her leg,” say Lewis, make the

desire addition; “but depend upon it, there never be a cow’s tail so

situate.”



“all the great proof of your talent,” be the reply. “the ideal be

what you artist (for I see you be one) be always rave about, and

this be a speciman of it.”



so engross have the two young man be with their occupation that they

have not observe the entrance of a third person. the newcomer be that

most charming of all create being, a very lovely girl of seventeen.



as every poet since Homer have do his utmost to clothe in fitting

language a description of the good speciman of the class which it

may have be his hap to meet with, and as no man in his sense would

exchange half-an-hour of the society of one of the original for all the

fanciful description of woman that ever be write, we would fain be

excuse from add one more to the number; and be all our reader of

what grammarian most ungallantly term “the worthy gender,” we should

cut the matter short by beg each man to imagine the damsel in

question exactly like the “unexpressive she” who be, for the time being,

queen of his soul. but as we flatter ourselves certain bright eye will

sparkle and coral lip smile over this “o’er true tale,” and as we have

already be ask by “ocean” of young lady, “what be the heroine to

be like?” we will e’en make a virtue of necessity and give a _catalogue

raisonné_ of her many perfection.



Annie Grant, then (for we’ll have no disguise about the matter, but own

at once that she it _be_ who enter the drawing-room unperceive, and

that she it _be_ who be destine to play the heroine in this our drama

of the Railroad of Life; and be it observe interparenthetically that we

use the theatrical metaphor advisedly, for Shakespeare have tell we that

“all the world’s a stage,” and it be a matter of common notoriety that

in the present day all stage have become railroads)--Annie Grant, then,

we say, be rather above the middle height, though no one would

have think of pronounce she tall; her gown of _mousseline--poil

de_--psha! what be we think of?--she have not a gown on at all; how

should she, when she be go to ride directly after luncheon? no, her

habit, which fit to perfection, be well calculate to set off her

slight but singularly graceful figure to the good advantage. her hair,

which be braid in broad plait for the great convenience (see

that ringlet under a riding-hat be an anomaly, not to say an

abomination), be _really_ auburn,--by which definition we intend to

guard against the pale red, or warm, sand-colour lock which usually

pass current for the very rare but very beautiful tint we would

particularise,--and if a poet have speculate as to the probability

of some wandering sunbeam be imprison in its golden mesh, the

metaphor, though fanciful, would not have be unapt. delicate, regular

feature, large blue eye, now dance and sparkle with mischievous

glee, now flash with pride, a mouth like an expressive rosebud, a

clear skin, with a warm glow of health painting each velvet cheek, but

retreat from the snowy forehead, combine to form a whole on which to

gaze be to admire.



this young lady, be such as we have describe she, trip lightly

across the apartment till she have station herself behind her cousin

Charles, and perceive that both gentleman be so preoccupied as not

to have observe her approach, contrive, by stand on tiptoe and

peep over Leicester’s shoulder, to witness the introduction of the

cow of which we have already make honourable mention.



during the animate discussion on the tail question she nearly betray

her presence by laugh outright; repress the inclination, however,

she retrace her step, and have nearly succeed in reach the side

door by which she have enter, when her habit, catch against a table,

cause the overthrow of a piece of ornamental china and reveal she

presence.



on hear the sound, Lewis, recall to a sense of his situation, and

for the first time strike by the idea that, in touch the drawing, he

have be guilty of an unwarrantable liberty, rise hastily from his seat,

colour crimson as he do so, from an agreeable mixture of shyness,

mortification, and proud self-reproach. Leicester, on the other hand,

with the _à-plomb_ and presence of mind of a man of the world, turn

leisurely, and whispering, “keep your own counsel, there’s no harm

do,” he advance towards his cousin, say with a nonchalant air,

“you have steal a march upon we, Annie. this gentleman and I call to

see the General upon business, and as he seems resolve to afford we a

practical lesson on the virtue of patience. I venture to while away the

time by show my friend some of your sketch. by the way, let I

introduce you. my cousin, Miss Grant--Mr. Arundel.” thus invoke, Lewis,

who in order to atone to his wound self-respect, have wrap himself

in his very cold and haughty manner, and resemble a banished

prince rather than an every-day Christian, advance a few step and

acknowledge the introduction by a most grandisonian inclination of the

head.



the lady perform her part of the ceremony with an easy courtesy, into

which perhaps an equal degree of hauteur be infuse, although not the

slight effort be visible.



“Mr. Arundel be doubtless a judge of painting, and my poor sketch be

by no mean calculate to bear severe criticism,” remark Miss Grant

demurely.



as Lewis remain silent, Leicester hasten to reply: “a judge! of

course he be; he’s just return from Germany, the happy land where

smoking, singing, and paint all come by nature.”



“indeed!” return Miss Grant. “then, if it be not too troublesome,

perhaps I might ask Mr. Arundel’s advice as to a sketch of Broadhurst

I be attempt before your arrival; I leave off in despair, because I

could not manage anything for the foreground.”



“try an elephant,” suggest Leicester; “it would have a grand effect,

besides possess the advantage of novelty, and fill up lot of

space.”



“would you bring I the drawing, Charles?” return his cousin.



“I know too well the style of assistance I may expect from you in

such matter. who embellish my poor head of Minerva with a pair of

_moustache?_”



“I do,” rejoin Leicester complacently, “and I be proud of it. Minerva

be the goddess of war, and sport _moustache_ in virtue of her

profession.”



“be you never go to give I the drawing, Charles?” ask Annie

impatiently. “positively, cousin be most uncourteous being. Mr.

Arundel, might I trouble you to hand I that sketch?”



thus appeal to, Lewis have nothing for it but to comply, which he do

accordingly, bite his lip with vexation at the _dénouement_ which now

appear inevitable. but Leicester’s resource be not yet exhaust;

stretch out his hand before his cousin have receive the drawing, he

coolly take possession of it, say, “I know you mean this drawing

as a little surprise for I. you have hear I say how much I covet a

sketch of dear old Broadhurst, and so you have kindly make one for I.

you have really do it extremely well! who be it--field--you have

be learn of? positively, you have catch his style!”



“don’t flatter yourself that I do you the honour of recollect any

such wish, even suppose you really utter it in my hearing, of which

I entertain grave doubt,” return Annie; “but if you particularly

desire it I will make you a present of it when it be finish--if I

could only manage that tiresome foreground!”



“I like it well without,” be the reply. “there’s nothing to

interfere with the outline of the building, which stand forth in bold

relief--and--eh!--well, what’s the matter?”



during his speech his cousin have rise from her seat, and approach

he, catch sight of the drawing, which she have no soon do than,

raise a little white hand, she point to the intrusive cow and ask

quietly, “where do that come from?”



the comic perplexity of Leicester’s face be irresistible to behold, as,

with a glance at Lewis to secure his sympathy and co-operation, he be

evidently about to adopt the cow at all hazard, when the door open,

and a tall, stately old man, with a military port and erect bearing,

enter, and survey the group with evident surprise, draw himself

up still more stiffly, ere, with slow and measured step, he advance

towards they.



_it be General Grant!_









CHAPTER VII.--WHEREIN the reader be INTRODUCED to MISS LIVINGSTONE, and

informed who be the GREATEST MAN of the AGE.



“ah! General,” exclaim Leicester, as he rise leisurely from the

arm-chair in which he have be recline, “I hope they have not

disturb you on our account. I be criticise one of Annie’s sketch

_pour passer le temps_--really she draw very nicely. let I introduce

Mr. Arundel, Mr. Frere’s friend, about whom I write to you yesterday.”



a stiff bow, acknowledge on Lewis’s part by an equally haughty

inclination of the head, be the result of this introduction, when

General Grant observed--



“Mr. Frere be a man of whom I have a very high opinion, both on

account of his unusual intellectual attainment, and his manly, upright

character. have you be long acquaint with he, sir, may I ask?”



“he be my guide and protector when I first go to Westminster,”

 reply Lewis, “and we have be close friend ever since.”



“a most fortunate circumstance,” remark the General sententiously.

“the mind of youth be easily impressible for good or evil, and unless

such establishment be greatly alter for the well since my time,

Satan have no lack of emissary at a public school. will you allow I a

few minute’ private conversation with you, Mr. Arundel? the library be

in this direction.” so say, General Grant open the door with

frigid courtesy, and sign to Lewis to precede he, follow with a

stateliness of demeanour admirable to behold.



Scarcely have they leave the room, when Annie, clap her hand

joyfully, exclaim, “what a creature! why, he’s as stiff and dignified

as papa himself. now then, Charley, tell I who he be, and all about

he: we shall have Aunt Martha or somebody come, and then I shall

never know, and be oblige to die of curiosity. you be asleep, I

believe.”



“there you go--that’s always the way with woman,” return Leicester,

speak very slowly and with an exaggeration of his usual mode of

pronunciation, which be something between a lisp and a drawl; “ask

half-a-dozen question in a breath, and resolve to get up a suicidal

amount of curiosity if they be not as speedily answer. why, my

dear child, I would not speak as quickly as you do for any amount of

money--at least any amount of money I should be at all likely to get for

do so.”



“now, Charley, don’t be tiresome. who be the man?” rejoin Annie,

half pettishly. then, see that her imperious manner only induce

her cousin still far to tease she, she add, in an implore tone,

which no heart of any material soft than granite could resist, “you

will tell I--won’t you? I want to know so much, and I have have nothing

to amuse I all day.”



“there, do you hear that?” soliloquised Leicester, appeal to society

in general. “trust a woman to get her own way. if she cann’t scold you

into give it to she, she’ll coax you. well, you little torment, I

suppose you must know all about it. the man, as you please to call he,

be seek the honourable post of bear-leader to the cub your father have

the felicity of be guardian unto.”



“what, a tutor for poor Walter!” rejoin Annie meditatively. “but

surely he’s a gentleman, be he not?”



“very particularly and decidedly so, as far as I be a judge,” return

Leicester, hook a footstool towards he with his cane, and deposit

his foot thereupon. “at least I dine and spend last evening in his

company, and never wish to meet a well fellow.”



“but,” continue Annie, pursue her train of reasoning, “if he be a

gentleman, why do he want to go out as a tutor?”



“because, unfortunately, there be a vulgar prejudice extant in this

feeble-minded country that the necessary of life, such as bread

and cheese, cigar, kid glove, and the like, must be pay for--this

require money, whereof Arundel have little or none. moreover, Richard

Frere hint at a mother and sister in the case, who likewise have to be

support.”



as he speak a shade of deep thought flit across Annie’s expressive

feature, and after a moment’s pause she resume.



“now I understand his strange manner: he be mentally contrast

himself (he be evidently a proud man) and his position; it must indeed

have be a struggle--and he do this for the sake of his mother and

sister. Charley, do you know, I rather admire he.”



“yes, I dare say you do; he’s a decidedly good-look fellow for the

style of man; there’s a thoroughbred air about he, and he carry

himself well.”



“Psha! I be not talk of his appearance: except that he be tall and

dark, I scarcely know what he be like,” return Annie quickly. “no! I

mean that there be something fine in the idea of a proud mind submit

to degradation and indignity for the sake of those it love; bear

with a martyr-spirit the thousand hourly annoyances-----” check

herself suddenly, as she perceive upon her cousin’s face something

nearly akin to a contemptuous smile, Annie continue, “Charles, how

stupid you be! I hate you!”



“not possible,” be the cool reply. “moreover, you have really no cause

to do so. I assure you I be not exactly laugh at your sudden plunge

into the sentimental; it be merely a notion which cross my mind, that

out of the thousand hourly annoyance by which poor Arundel be to be

martyrise, some nine hundred and fifty would originate in the caprice

of a certain young lady who shall be nameless. in the monotony of life

amid the leafy shade of Broadhurst, even tease a tutor may be deem

a new and interesting variety, as the botanist have it. seriously,

though, you can coax the General to let he teach you German.”



“and embellish my water-colour sketch by the insertion of occasional

cow, with impossible tail make to order--eh, cousin Charley?” return

Annie with an arch smile. “give I my drawing, sir, and let I look at

the creature. how well he have do it! I know a cow at Broadhurst with

just such a face!”



“there’s a world of speculation in the eye,” rejoin Leicester

carelessly, though he be slightly surprised at the extent of her

information respect the “tail” debate; “the animal appear to be

ruminate on the advisability of petition Parliament against the

veal trade, or some other question of equal interest to the ‘milky

mother of the herd.’”



while Annie and her cousin thus gaily converse, a very different scene

be enact in the library. during a short delay, occasion by General

Grant’s be oblige to answer a note, Lewis have time to recollect

himself, and to school the rebellious feeling which his conversation

with Leicester and the other event of the morning have call into

action. he think of Rose and his mother, and of his determination

that they at least should be spare all knowledge of the real evil of

poverty; and this reflection be for the time sufficient to efface

every selfish consideration. bring his strength of will into play, he

regain the most complete self-control, and even experience a sort of

morbid pleasure in the idea of voluntarily humiliate himself before

the proud old man, whose clear, cold eye be occasionally raise from

the note he be employ in writing to fix its scrutinise glance on

Lewis’s feature.



having seal the missive and give it to a servant, he slowly

approach the spot where Lewis be stand, and after a word or two of

apology for having keep he wait, began--



“I presume my nephew, Mr. Leicester, have make you in some degree

acquaint with the nature of the circumstance in which I be at present

place, and of the necessity which render I anxious to secure the

service of some gentleman as tutor to my ward, Sir Walter Desborough?”



“Mr. Leicester inform I that the young gentleman’s education have be

neglect, and that his mind be singularly undeveloped,” reply

Lewis, choose the least offensive term in which he might express his

conviction that the youth in question be rather a fool than otherwise.



“yes, sir, though it be even bad than you describe,” return the

General. “in fact it depend upon the degree of success which may attend

the effort which must now be make whether Sir Walter Desborough can

ever be consider capable of manage his own affair, or able to take

that place in society to which his rank and fortune would naturally

entitle he. you perceive, therefore, that the post of tutor will be one

of much trust and importance, and the duty attend it most onerous.

Mr. Frere have write so high a character of your various attainment

that I cannot but feel perfectly satisfied of your competency; but you

be very young, and as I should, in the event of your undertaking the

charge, expect a strict performance of your duty, it be only fair to

inform you that I conceive they may be irksome in the extreme. what be

your feeling on the subject?”



Lewis pause for a moment in thought, and then replied--



“I will be frank with you, sir. be I free to act as I choose, such an

office as you describe would be one of the last I should select; but the

welfare of other depend upon my exertion, and I have determine to

refuse no occupation not unworthy a gentleman which will enable I

to render the necessary assistance to my family. if, therefore, you

consider I fit to undertake the charge of your ward, I be willing

to do so, and to fulfil the duty of such a situation to the good of my

ability, on one condition.”



“what be that?” inquire General Grant quickly.



“that I may be allow to pursue whatever system I may deem well fit

to attain the desire end, without the interference of any one, and may

be accountable for my conduct to you alone.”



“rather a singular request, young gentleman,” return the General,

knit his brow.



“my reason for make it be easily explain, sir,” reply Lewis,

firmly but respectfully. “unless such permission be accord I, I feel

certain all my effort would prove unavaile: I must have full power

to do what I think right, or I could not act at all, and should have

undertake a duty which I should be incompetent to perform.”



“well, sir, there be truth in what you say,” reply General Grant,

after a moment’s consideration. “I like you none the bad for speak

in a manly, straightforward manner. it be my intention to go down to

Broadhurst in a day or two: you shall accompany I; and if, after see

my ward, you be still willing to undertake the task of conduct

his education, I shall be happy to entrust he to your care, upon the

condition you have propose. your salary will be £300 a year. this, you

be aware, be unusually high, but the case be a peculiar one, and money,

fortunately, a very secondary consideration. an entire suite of room

will be devoted to the use of yourself and your pupil, and a horse keep

for you, that you may accompany he in his ride. do these arrangement

meet your wish?”



Lewis bow his head in token of acknowledgment, and say, “I have one

other request to make. I bring a Livonian wolf-hound with I from

Germany; he be much attach to I, and I should be unwilling to part

from he.”



“bring he with you, sir,” return the General, his lip slightly

curl with a sarcastic smile; “a dog more or less will make little

difference in such an establishment as that at Broadhurst. and now, if

you will give I the pleasure of your company at luncheon, I shall be

happy to introduce you to my relative, Miss Livingstone, who do I the

honour to preside over my household. my daughter, I believe, you have

already see;” and as he speak he lead the way to the dining-room, where

the rest of the party be already assemble.



Miss Livingstone, who scrutinise Lewis as if she suspect he of

belong to that ingenious fraternity clepe the swell mob, be, in

appearance, a very awful old lady indeed. the near approach we can

make to a description of her feature be to say that they bear a marked

(with the small-pox) resemblance to those of Minerva _and_ her owl;

the sternness of that utilitarian goddess--the Miss Martineau of

Olympus--and the sapient stupidity of the so-call bird of wisdom,

find their exact counterpart in Miss Livingstone’s time-honour

physiognomy. this lady be apparel after a strange and impose mode,

as behove a spinster of such orthodox station and ferociously virtuous

propriety as the General’s female commander-in-chief. Minerva’s helmet

be modernise into a stupendous fabric, wherein starch, muslin, and

ribbon of an unnatural harshness struggle upwards in a pyramid, whence

pointing with stiffened end innumerable, suggestive of any amount of

porcupine, they appear ready and anxious to repel or impale society

at large. a triangle of spotless lawn supply the place of the

breastplate beneath which Jove’s daughter be accustom to conceal she

want of heart; and a silk gown of an uncomfortable shade of grey, make

so scanty as to render at first sight the hypothesis of a mermaid_ic_

termination conceivable, complete the costume of this immaculate old

lady.



having apparently satisfy herself that Lewis have no immediate

design upon the spoon and fork, she condescend to afford he the

meteorological information that although the sunshine might delude the

unwary into believe it to be a fine day, she have receive private

information that the weather be not to be rely upon: after

promulgating which opinion she place herself at the head, and assume

the direction of, the luncheon-table.



Charley Leicester appear to be the only individual of the party

insensible to a certain freezing influence, which might be specify as

one of Miss Livingstone’s most characteristic attribute. having exert

himself to supply that lady with every possible adjunct she could

require, and seduce she into an amount of Cayenne pepper which

afterwards subject she to considerable physical suffering, he began--



“I be present, a day or two ago, Miss Livingstone, when a question be

start as to what man of modern time have be the great benefactor

to his race. it open a mine of very curious speculation, I can assure

you.”



“I do not doubt it, Charles,” return Miss Livingstone; “and I be glad

to learn that the young man of the present day employ their time in such

profitable discussion. what decision do you arrive at?”



“well, ma’am,” resume Leicester gravely, “there be of course much

difference of opinion. James Watt have rather a strong party in his

favour, but an ex-railway director be present who have lose £10,000 on

the do-em-and-foot-in-it line, and he blackball he. Lord John

be propose; but some of the man who take in _Punch_ laugh so

immoderately when his name be mention that it be immediately

withdraw. one youth, who be know to be a little bit flighty, not quite

accountable, poor fellow! declare for Lord Brougham, but we soothe

he, and he have sense enough leave to see his error almost immediately.

at length it come to my turn----”



“and whom do you mention?” inquire Miss Livingstone, with a degree of

interest most unusual in she.



“I have be ponder the matter deeply,” continue Leicester, “to try

and hit on some worthy against whom no valid objection could be raise.

at one moment I think of Moses----”



“I fancy it be restrict to man of modern time,” interpose Miss

Livingstone.



“he to whom I refer, ma’am,” return Leicester, “be not the

Israelitish lawgiver, but the man of the City Mart, that benevolent

individual who clothe poverty in ‘a light paletot at ten-and-six,’ and

enable the honest heart of free-bear Britons to palpitate beneath a

‘gent’s superior vest’ for the trifling remuneration of five shilling.”

 this speech be algebra, or thereabout, to the lady to whom it be

address, but she have a sort of instinctive apprehension that

Leicester be talk nonsense, and accordingly draw herself up stiffly,

complete her resemblance to Minerva by compose her feature into

a very satisfactory likeness of the Gorgon. no way affect by this

transformation, Leicester continued--



“on mature reflection, however, I discard Moses & Son, and be go

to give it up as hopeless, when, all of a sudden, a bright thought

flash across I, and spring to my foot, I exclaim in a voice of

thunder, ‘gentleman, I have it; the difficulty be one no long: the

great modern benefactor to the human race is--_Bass!_’”



“_who?_” exclaim Miss Livingstone, entirely mystified and a good deal

flurry by the narrator’s unusual energy.



“Bass,” resume Leicester; “that remarkable man whose gigantic intellect

first conceive the project of regenerate society through the medium

of _pale ale!_ the idea be hail with enthusiasm; we immediately send

for a dozen; and ere the liquor be dispose of, there be not a man

present but would have stake hundred on the soundness of my opinion.”



utterly disgusted and confuse by this unexpected termination to the

anecdote, Miss Livingstone rise from her chair, sail out of the room,

and thus the visit conclude.



Lewis, after a solitary walk, during which he be revolve in his

mind the step he have just take, and strive to discern in the dull

lead-coloured horizon of his future one ray of light which might yield

promise of bright time to come, be ascend Frere’s staircase,

when the door of the room above open suddenly, and a voice, which he

think he recognise, exclaimed--



“then I may depend upon you; you’ll be with I by eight at the late,

and bring your friend, if possible. ah! here he be! Mr. Arundel,

delight to see you--none the bad for last night, I hope--wasn’t it

glorious? Grandeville have get such a face on he, he willn’t be able to

show for a week to come; and Meeking of the pallid feature be so seedy

this morning that I be force to burthen my conscience by invent

a fictitious fall from his horse, on the strength of which I send his

mamma to nurse he. we must book that to the pious fraud account, and

let the charity absolve the lie. rather shaky divinity, eh, Frere? well,

_au revoir_; I’m off.”



so say, Mr. Tom Bracy--for he it be, and none other--dash down

the stair, and having deeply scandalise Frere’s ancient domestic by

an anxious inquiry how it be she do not get a husband, take his

departure.



“frere!” exclaim Lewis, throw himself into a chair and coldly

repulse Faust, who never could imagine himself otherwise than welcome,

“I’ve do it!”



“so have I, man,” be the reply; “and pretty considerably brown, too,

as that nice youth who have just leave I would call it. but what have you

do to make you so doleful?”



“sell myself,” return Lewis bitterly.



“not to the old gentleman, I hope,” rejoin Frere, “though your black

look would almost lead one to imagine so.”



“what weak, inconsistent fool we be!” pursue Lewis.



“speak for yourself, young man,” observe frere parenthetically.



“how vacillating and impotent,” continue Lewis, not heed the

interruption, “be even the strong will! I have do this morning the

thing I believe I most anxiously desire to do--the thing I come here

hope to accomplish--I have secure a competence for my mother and

sister. I have do so on well term than I have deem possible. I have

meet with consideration, if not kindness, from--from my employer.” he

pronounce the word firmly, though his temple throb and his lip

quiver with suppress emotion as he do so. “all this should make

I content, if not happy. happy!” he repeat mockingly. “frere,” he

continue, with a sudden burst of impetuosity, “it have not do so--I be

miserable!”



he rise from his seat and begin pace the room with impatient stride.

Faust follow he for one or two turn, wag his tail and gaze

up into his face with love eye; but find his effort to attract

attention unavaile, he utter a piteous whine, and, retreat to a

corner, crouch down, as perfectly aware that his master be unhappy as

if he have be a human creature and could have “tell his love” in

word. Frere would have speak, but Lewis check he by a gesture,

and continue his rapid walk for some minute in silence. at length he

spoke--



“you think I selfish and ungrateful, and you be right; I be so. I

have school myself to bear all this, and I _will_ bear it; but bitter

thought arise and at time overpower I. I be very young” (“true for

you,” mutter Frere, _sotto voce_), “and I be so unfit for such a life

as lie before I, a life of tame and ceaseless drudgery, in which

to indulge the high aspiration and noble daring that win man honour

become misplace folly; to live with people whose equal, if not

superior, I feel myself, in a semi-menial capacity; to obey when I

would command; to forfeit all that be bright and fair in

existence--intercourse with the high order of mind, the society

of pure and refined spirit; and, above all, to lose the only thing I

really prize on earth--my independence.



“well,” he continue, after a pause, “the die be cast, and repine be

bad than useless. I will give this experiment a fair trial; it may

be the harness will set more easily on I than I imagine; and should it

become unbearable, I can but cast it off and start afresh: there be such

a thing as to compel one’s destiny!”









CHAPTER VIII.--LEWIS RECEIVES a LECTURE and a cold BATH.



Richard Frere listen to the somewhat grandiloquent remark with which

the last chapter conclude, mutter to himself, “‘compel destiny,’

indeed; it strike I you’ll find ‘destiny,’ as you call it, will have

the good of it at that game;” then turn to his companion, he observe

more gravely, “now, listen to I, Lewis. what you have just say be no

doubt true enough; you _be_ about as unfit in taste and habit for the

life that be before you as a man well can be, but for that reason it be

exactly the very good thing for you. for what purpose do you suppose we

be send into this world? most assuredly not only to please ourselves,

and by follow out our own desire and caprice, create a sphere for

the exercise and increase of our natural fault. no; the only true view

of life be as a school, wherein our character be to be discipline,

and all the change and chance, sorrow, trial, and temptation we

meet with be the agent by which the education of the soul be carry

on.”



“and a low, wretched view of life it be,” reply Lewis bitterly; “a

seventy year’ pupilage under the rod of destiny. the heathen sage be

right who say that those whom the god love die early. if it be not

for Rose and my mother, I would join some regiment bind for India,

volunteer into every forlorn hope, and trust that some Sikh bullet

would rid I of the burthen of life without my incur the guilt of

suicide.”



“in fact, you would die like an idiot, because you lack moral courage

to face the evil of life like a man,” return Frere. “but wait a bit:

your argument, such as it be, be found on a fallacy, or on that still

more dangerous thing, a half-truth. grant that life be one scene of

bitter experiences,--which would be grant a very large lie,--for what

be this discipline intend to fit we? that be the question. you be

ambitious--how would you regard obstacle in your path to greatness? you

would rejoice in they, would you not, as opportunity for bring

out the high quality you fancy you possess? fortitude, courage,

indomitable perseverance, ready wit, aptitude to lead and govern

your fellow-man, and fifty other magnanimous attribute; and deem the

greatness unworthy your notice could it be obtain without a struggle.

but what be human greatness? a triumph for the hour, bring its

attendant care and evil with it--mark that,--a bauble, which some

other ambitious genius may possibly wrest from your grasp, which old

age would unfit you to retain, of which death must deprive you in a

few year more or less. now take the true, the Christian’s view of

life--obstacle to overcome, demand all our strength of mind,

and then prove too mighty for we without the assistance of a power

superior to that of man, but which will be give we if we seek it

properly. and the victory win, what be the prize we shall obtain? a

position, accord to our advance in righteousness, among the spirit

of just man _make perfect_ intercourse (with reverence be it speak)

with the Source of all good, Omniscience our teacher, Omnipotence our

only ruler, Perfect Justice our lawgiver, Perfect Wisdom our director,

the Powers of Heaven for our associate, and our own soul, free

from the trammel of mortality, fit to appreciate and enjoy these

inestimable blessing; and all this, not for time, but for eternity.

Lewis, you be a reasonable being, and to your own reason I will leave

the question.”



there be silence for some minute. at length Lewis raise his head,

reveal feature on which the trace of deep emotion be visible, and

stretch out his hand to his friend, say in a voice which tremble

from excess of feeling, “God bless you, frere; you be indeed a

true friend!” he pause; then add suddenly, “frere, promise I one

thing,--promise I that whatever I may do, whatever rash act or evil

deed my feeling may hurry I into, you will not give I up; that while

we both live you will act by I as you have do to-day--that you will

preserve I from myself, stand between I and my fiery nature; then

shall I feel that I be not utterly deserted--you will be the link that

shall still bind I to virtue.”



“well, if you fancy it will make you any happy, or well, or more

reasonable, I will promise it,” return frere; “more particularly as I

should most probably do it whether I promise it or not.”



“you promise, then?” ask Lewis eagerly.



“I do,” reply Frere.



Lewis once more wring his friend’s hand with such eagerness as to elicit

a grimace of pain from that excellent individual, and then continued--



“a conversation of this nature regularly upset I; I must go out and

walk off the excitement before I shall be fit for anything. come, Faust,

good dog! I speak up for Faust to-day, Frere, and the General accord

a dignified assent: ‘a dog more or less will make little difference in

such an establishment as Broadhurst.’”



“do he say that?” inquire Frere.



“word for word,” return Lewis.



“well, I think well thing of he! ‘folk be sich fool!’ as my old

lady downstair say. be you off? mind you be at home in good time

for dinner, for I have be seduce into accept another evening

engagement for we.”



“any more fighting?” ask Lewis anxiously.



“no; thank goodness for that same!” return Frere.



“I wish I could meet that long Chartist,” continue Lewis, shake his

fist; “not that I bear he any ill-will, but it would be such a

relief to I just now to knock somebody down. mayn’t I set Faust at a

policeman?”



“not unless you prefer Brixton to Broadhurst, and the treadmill to the

tutorship,” return Frere.



“well, good-bye till dinner-time,” respond Lewis, leave the room. “I

willn’t punish your carpet any long. come, Faust!”



“that be a most singular young man,” soliloquise Frere as he take down

and unroll a persian manuscript; “very like an excitable steam-engine

with an ill-regulate safety-valve in disposition; I only hope he willn’t

blow up bodily while I have the care of he. he be a fine fellow,

too, and it’s impossible not to be very fond of he; but he’s an awful

responsibility for a quiet man to have thrust upon he.”



Meanwhile Lewis, walk hurriedly up one street and down another, with

the design of allay the fever of his mind by bodily exercise, find

himself at length in the neighbourhood of Hyde Park, and, tempt by

the beauty of the afternoon, he continue his stroll till he reach

Kensington Gardens. here, stretch himself on one of the bench,

he watch the group of gaily-dress lounger and listen to the

military band, till he begin to fear he might be late for Frere’s

dinner; and retrace his step, he proceed along the bank of the

Serpentine towards Hyde Park Corner. as he arrive nearly opposite the

receiving-house of the Humane Society, his attention be attract

by the lamentation of a small child, whom all the endearment of a

sympathise nursery-maid be powerless to console. the child, be

a fine sturdy boy, and the maid remarkably pretty, Lewis be move by a

sudden impulse of compassion to stop and inquire the cause of the grief

he beheld. it be soon explain. Master Tom have come to sail a little

boat which his grandpapa have give he; the string by which the length

of its voyage be to have be regulate have break, and the boat have

drift far and far from its hapless owner, until at last it

have reach a specie of buoy, to which the park-keeper’s punt be

occasionally moor, and there it have choose to stick hard and fast. in

this rebellious little craft be embark, so to speak, all Master

Tom’s present stock of earthly happiness; thence the sorrow which Mary’s

caress be unable to assuage, and thence the lamentation which have

attract Lewis’s attention.



“don’t cry so, my little man, and we’ll see if we cann’t find a way

of get it for you,” observe Lewis encouragingly, raise the

distressed shipowner in his arm to afford he a well view of his

strand property. “we must ask my dog to go and fetch it for we. come

here, Mr. Faust. you be not afraid of he? he willn’t hurt you--that’s

right, pat he; there’s a brave boy; now ask he to fetch your boat for

you. say, ‘please, Mr. Faust, go and get I my boat!’ say so.” and the

child, half-please, half-frightened, but with implicit faith in the

dog’s intellectual power, and the advisability of conciliate its good

will and implore its assistance, repeat the desire formula with

great unction.



“that’s well! now, nurse, take care of Master--what do you say?--ay,

Master Tom, while I show Faust where the boat be.” as he speak he take

up a stone, and attract Faust’s attention to his proceeding, jerk

it into the water just beyond the spot where the boat lay, at the same

time direct he to fetch it.



with a bound like the spring of a lion the noble dog dash into the

water and swam vigorously towards the object of his quest, reach it,

seize it in his powerful jaw, and turn his head towards the bank in

preparation for his homeward voyage, while the delighted child laugh

and shout with joy at the prospect of regain his lose treasure.

instead, however, of proceed at once towards the shore, the dog

remain stationary, beat the water with his forepaw to keep himself

afloat, and occasionally utter an uneasy whine.



“here, Faust! faust! what in the world’s the matter with he?” exclaim

Lewis, call the dog and incite he by gesture to return, but in

vain; his struggle only become more violent, without his make the

slight progress through the water.



attract by the sight, a knot of lounger gather round the spot,

and various suggestion be hazard as to the dog’s unaccountable

behaviour. “I think he must be seize with cramp,” observe a

good-nature, round-face man in a velveteen jacket, who look like one

of the park-keeper. “the animal be suicidally dispose, apparently,”

 remark a tall, aristocratic-looking young man, with a sinister

expression of countenance, to which a pair of thick moustache impart

a character of fierceness. “anxious to submit to the cold-water cure,

more probably,” remark his companion. “it will be kill rather than

cure with he before long,” return the former speaker with a half

laugh; “he’s get low in the water every minute.”



“he be catch by the string of the boat which be twist round the

buoy!” exclaim Lewis, who during the above conversation have seize

the branch of a tree, and raise himself by his hand, have reach a

position from which he be able to perceive the cause of his favourite’s

disaster; “he’ll be drown if he be not unfastened. who know where the

key of the boat-house be keep?”



“I’ll run and fetch it,” cry the good-natured man; “it’s at the

receiving-house, I believe.”



“quick! or it will be of no use!” say Lewis in the great excitement.



the man hurry off, but the crowd round the spot have now become so

dense--even carriage fill with fashionably-dress lady having

stop to learn the catastrophe--that it be no easy matter for he to

make his way through it, and several minute elapse without witness

his return. in the meantime the poor dog’s struggle be become

fainter and fainter; his whining have change to something between a

hoarse bark and a howl, a sound so clearly indicative of suffer as to

be most distressing to the bystander; and it be evident that if some

effort be not speedily make for his relief he must sink.



“he shall not perish unassisted!” exclaim Lewis impetuously; “who will

lend I a knife?”



Several be immediately offer he, from which he select one with a

broad blade.



“may I inquire how you propose to prevent the impending catastrophe?”

 ask superciliously the moustache gentleman to whom we have before

allude.



“you shall see directly,” return Lewis, divest himself of his coat,

waistcoat, and neckcloth.



“I presume you be aware there be not one man in a hundred who could

swim that distance in his clothe,” resume the speaker in the

same sneer tone. “do you actually--I merely ask as a matter of

curiosity--do you really consider it worth while to peril your life for

that of a dog?”



“for such a noble dog as that, yes!” reply Lewis sternly. “I might

not take the trouble for a _mere puppy_;” and he pronounce the last two

word with a marked emphasis, which render his meaning unmistakable.

the person he address colour with anger and slightly raise his

cane, but he read that in Lewis’s face which cause he to relinquish

his intention, and smile scornfully he fold his arm and remain to

observe the event.



this be Lewis’s introduction to Charles Leicester’s eld brother, Lord

Bellefield, the affiance of Annie Grant.



having complete his preparation, Lewis place the knife between his

tooth, and motion to the crowd to stand on one side, give a short

run, dash through the shallow water, and then, breast the stream

gallantly, swain with powerful stroke towards the still struggle

animal. as he perceive his master approach, the poor dog cease

howling, and seemingly re-animate by the prospect of assistance,

redouble his effort to keep himself afloat.



in order to avoid the stroke of his paw, Lewis swam round he, and

support himself by rest one hand upon the buoy, he grasp the

knife with the other, and at one stroke sever the string. the effect

be instantly perceptible: free from the restraint which have till now

paralyse his effort, the dog at once rise higher in the water; and

even in that extremity his affection for his master overpower his

instinct of self-preservation, he swam towards he with the child’s boat

(of which, throughout the whole scene, he have never loosen his hold)

in his mouth.



merely wait to assure himself that the animal have yet strength enough

remain to enable he to regain the shore, Lewis set he the example

by quit the buoy and strike out lustily for the bank; but now the

weight of his clothe, thoroughly saturate as they have become, begin to

tell upon he, and his stroke grow perceptibly weak, while his breath

come short and thick.



Faust, on the contrary, free from the string which have entangle he,

proceed merrily, and reach the shore ere Lewis have perform half

the distance. deposit the boat in triumph at the foot of one of the

bystander, the generous animal only stop to shake the wet from his

ear, and then plunge in again swam to meet his master. it be perhaps

fortunate that he do so, for Lewis’s strength be rapidly desert

he, his clothe appear to drag he down like leaden weight.

availing himself, of the dog’s assistance, he place one arm across

its back, and still paddle with the other, he be partly drag and

partly himself swam forward, till his foot touch ground, when, let

the animal go free, he wade through the shallow water and reach the

bank, exhaust indeed, but in safety.



reject the many friendly offer of assistance with which he be

instantly overwhelmed, he wrang the water from his dripping hair,

stamp it out of his boot, and hastily resume his coat and

waistcoat, be about to quit a spot where he be the observed of all

observer, when Lord Bellefield, after exchange a few word with

his companion, make a sign to attract Lewis’s attention, and having

succeed in so do, say, “that be a fine dog of your, sir; will you

take a twenty pound note for he?”



Lewis’s countenance, pale from exhaustion, flush with anger at these

word; pause a moment, however, ere he reply, he answer coldly,

“have he be for sale, sir, I should scarcely have risk drown in

order to save he; I value my life at more than twenty pound.” then

turn on his heel, he whistle Faust to follow he, and walk away at

a rapid pace in the direction of Hyde Park Corner.



amongst the carriage that immediately drive off be one contain two

lady who have witness the whole proceeding; and as it dash by he,

Lewis, accidentally look up, catch a glimpse of the bright eager

face of Annie Grant!









CHAPTER IX.--WHEREIN RICHARD FRERE and LEWIS TURN mahometan.



Lewis rather expect a lecture from Richard Frere on account of

his aquatic exploit; but he need not have make himself uneasy on the

subject, for the only remark his friend volunteer be: “well, you

know, if the dog could not be save without, of course you be oblige

to go in and fetch he. I should have do the same myself, though I

hate cold water as I hate the old gentleman, and never could swim in my

life.”



when they have conclude dinner, Frere inquire suddenly: “by the way, do

you mean to come with I to-night?”
