“before I can answer that question,” return Lewis, “you must

condescend to inform I where you be go, and what you mean to do

when you get there.”



“to be sure; I think I have tell you; but the fact be, I have be

work rather hard lately (I read for three hour after you be go

to bed last night), and my head be not over clear to-day. the case be

this, sir: Tom Bracy, who, as I before tell you, be lamentably addict

to practical joke, happen to be acquaint with a certain elderly lady

who devote her life to lion-hunting.”



“to _what?_” inquire Lewis.



“to catch celebrity, otherwise term lion,” reply Frere,

“and parade they at her party for the benefit of her friend and

acquaintance. on the last occasion of this kind she confide to Bracy

her longing desire to obtain an introduction to a certain persian

prince, or thereabout, who have lately come over to this country to

avoid the somewhat troublesome attention of his family, his young

brother be most anxious to put out his eye, and his grandfather only

wait a favourable opportunity for bow-string he.”



“‘a little more than kin, and less than kind,’” quote Lewis.



“I know you would say that,” return frere; “in fact, I should have

feel quite surprised if you have not. but to proceed with my account.

Bracy soon find out that his hostess have never see the aforesaid

asiatic magnate, and know next to nothing about he; whereupon he

determine ‘to get a little fun,’ as he call it, out of the affair, and

accordingly inform she, very gravely, that from his acquaintance with

the persian language, he be in the habit of accompany the prince to

evening party in the character of interpreter, and that if she would

entrust he with an invitation, he should be happy to convey it to his

Highness, and try to induce he to accept it. she joyfully accede to

the proposal, and this very evening the party be to take place. and now

can you guess the purport of Bracy’s visit to I?”



“he want you to act as interpreter in his stead, I suppose; his

knowledge of Persian be probably confine to the word ‘bosh.’”



“Wrong!” rejoin frere, laugh. “a high destiny await I. I be for

the nonce to be elevate to the proud position of one of the Blood Royal

of Persia. in plain English, Bracy know as much of the Prince as I do

of the Pope; the whole thing be a hoax from begin to end, and he

want I to personate his Highness, which I have promise to do, while

you be to represent an attendant satrap, a sort of Mussulman gold

stick-in-waiting, always suppose you have no objection so to employ

yourself.”



“to tell you the truth, I be scarcely in the vein for such fooling,”

 return Lewis moodily. “I hate practical joke to begin with, nor can I

see much fun in take advantage of the absurdity of some weak-minded

old lady. at the same time I be tolerably indifferent about the matter,

and if you have pledge yourself to go, rely upon my accompany

you, I will put my own taste out of the question, and do as you wish.”



“equally sententious and amiable,” return frere; “but the truth be, I

have promise Bracy (partly fancy you would like the fun), and go I

must.”



“I’ll accompany you then,” rejoin Lewis. “I’d make a great sacrifice

than that for you any day, old fellow. and now may I ask who be the lady

to be victimise?”



“an opulent widow, one Lady Lombard, ‘the interesting relict of a

be-knight pawnbroker,’ as Bracy call she,” reply Frere.



“_who_ inquire Lewis, become suddenly interested.



“why, how now?” return Frere, astonish at his friend’s impetuosity.

then repeat the name, he continue, “do you know the lady?”



“yes, I do,” rejoin Lewis; “know she for a coarse-minded, purse-proud,

wretched old woman!”



“Phew!” whistle Frere. “may I ask how the good lady have be so

fortunate as thus to have excite your bitter indignation against she?”



“never mind,” return Lewis, rise hastily and walk to the window;

“it be enough that I know she to be the character I have describe.”



“that’s odd now,” mutter Frere, soliloquising. “if I have not be

acquaint with his ‘_antécêden_’ as the french term it, nearly as well

as I know my own, I should have fancy the late lament Lombard have,

in bygone hour, refuse to negotiate some small loan for he, on the

perishable security of personal clothing. he cann’t have pop the

question to the widow at one of the german watering-place, and

encounter a negative?”



“frere, don’t mention my dislike of Lady Lombard to your facetious

acquaintance,” observe Lewis, turn round. “I have no ambition to

become a butt for his bad pun.”



“never fear, man, I’ll not betray your confidence,” return frere;

“more particularly when, as in the present instance, I don’t happen to

share it.”



“do you care to know?” ask Lewis.



“not by no manner of mean, as the young lady say, when the parson

ask she whether she be prepared to give up all the pomp and vanity

of this wicked world,” return Frere. “and now, as we have to be

convert into Pagans before ten o’clock, suppose we start.”



a quarter of an hour’s brisk walking bring they to Bracy’s lodging,

where they find that gentleman deeply immerse in study, with the fez

which be to assist in change Frere into a prince stick rakishly on

one side of his head. on perceive his visitor he spring from his

seat, and make a low salaam, in the course of which performance the

fez tumble off and knock down a candle, he exclaimed--



“most illustrious brother of the Sun, and first-cousin once remove

of the Moon and all the star, may your shadow never be less! you do I

proud by honour my poor dwelling with your seraphic presence!”



“I see you have get the wherewithal to make Heathens of we,” return

Frere, point to the couple of persian dress which hang against the

wall like a brace of Bluebeard’s headless wife.



“Bude Light of the Universe, yes!” reply Bracy. “your slave have

procure the ‘_wear_ with all’ necessary to complete your transformation

from infidel Feringhees to true son of Islam. would I have have my

prince appear without a khelaut--a dress of honour? be Cheshm! upon my

eye be it;--by the way, it’s a remarkable fact that the expression ‘my

eye’ should be Court lingo in Persia, and border upon billingsgate

in English.”



“you seem particularly well up in the pseudo-oriental metaphor to-night,

Bracy,” observe Frere; “have the fez inspire you?”



“no, there’s nothing miraculous in the affair,” return Bracy; “it

be very easily explain. I have be read up for the

occasion--cramming, sir; a process successfully practise upon heavy

Johnians at Cambridge and corpulent turkey poult in Norfolk.”



“indeed! I be not aware that you be a persian scholar. may I inquire

what line of study you have adopt?”



“one that I have myself strike out,” respond Bracy, “and which have

be attend, I flatter myself, with the most successful result. I

first subject myself to a strict course of Hajji Baba, after which I

undergo a very search self-examination in Morie’s ‘zohrab’, or the

‘Hostage.’ I next thoroughly confuse my mind with ‘Thalaba,’ but bring

myself round again upon ‘Bayley Frazer’s travel’; after which I make

myself master of ‘Ayesha, or the Maid of Khars.’ and by way of lay

in a fitting stock of the sentimental, finish off with Byron’s

‘Giaour’;--stop, let I give you a speciman.” and replace the unruly

fez, he spring upon a chair, and throw himself into a mock-tragedy

attitude, begin bombastically to recite--



               “ ’twas sweet, where cloudless star be bright,



               to view the wave of watery light,



               and hear its melody by night;



               and oft have Hassan’s childhood play’d



               around the verge of that cascade:



               and oft upon his mother’s breast



               that sound have harmonise his rest;



               and oft have Hassan’s youth along



               its bank be sooth’d by Beauty’s song,



               and soft seem’d each melt tone



               of music mingle with its own.’



“there now, I call that pretty well for a young beginner; a little of

that will go a great way with my Lady Lombard; it be like a penny bun,

cheap to begin with, and very fill at the price.”



“Turks and Persians be not exactly alike, though you seem to think they

be,” observe Frere dryly. “have you lay down any plan of operation,

may I ask? you must give I very full and clear direction how to

behave, for, to tell you the truth, my acquaintance with the manner and

custom of the high rank of Persia be infinitesimally select.”



“oh! it’s all plain sailing enough,” return Bracy; “you have only

to look wise, roll your eye about, and occasionally jabber a little

Persian, or any other unknown tongue you may prefer, which I, not

understanding, shall translate _ad libitum_ as the occasion may

require.”



“and sweetly you will do it too, or I be much mistaken,” mutter Frere,

divest himself of his greatcoat.



“pray inform I, as I be unfortunately ignorant of all the oriental

language, how do you propose to supply my deficiency?” inquire

Lewis. “be my part, like Bottom the weaver’s, to be nothing but

roar?”



“why, as you be about to enact a lion, it would appear not

inappropriate,” return Bracy. “yes, it never strike I; there seem

a slight difficulty there--you never get up any _Memoria Technica_, do

you?”



Lewis shake his head.



“that’s unlucky,” continue Bracy; “a page or two of that would have

serve the purpose beautifully. I meet a man the other night who have

strike out a new system for himself, and be perfectly rabid about

it. he have bottle, accord to his own account, the whole history of

England into an insinuating little word that sound to I something

like ‘Humguffinhoggogrificicuana,’ and bag all Hansard’s report,

from Pitt to Peel, in half-a-dozen line of impossible doggerel. oh! he

be a wonderful fellow--clearly mad, but intensely funny. I keep he

in tow two good hour, and make he explain his system twice over to

everybody, till the people be ready to cry, he bore they so. I be

nearly be punish for it though, as he be actually weak enough to

believe in I, and call the next day to fraternise.”



“and how do you escape?” ask Lewis.



“why, I have a sort of tiger (the imp that let you in, in fact), who be

a first-rate liar--most excellent, useful boy, I do assure you, sir; I

send he down with a message that I have an attack of asiatic cholera,

but if he would take a glass of wine, and look at the paper till the

crisis should be over, I would come to he if it terminate favourably.

that settle the business; he do not wait the event, but be off like a

shot, think the Infection might disagree with his ‘system,’ perhaps.”



“then he have not repeat his visit?” inquire Frere.



“no; and I hope he will not,” return Bracy, “for there will be nothing

leave for I to have but Elephantiasis or the Plague, and he must be very

far go in innocence if he can swallow either of they.”



“be I expect to put on these thing?” ask Frere, hold up a most

voluminous pair of persian trouser, make of a species of silk gauze

enrich with glitter spangle.



“yea, verily, most emphatically and decidedly yes,” reply Bracy.



“well, what must be must be, I suppose,” rejoin frere, with a sigh of

resignation; “but I never think to see myself in such a garment. ‘sure

such a pair be never see!’ one thing be clear, I must stand all the

evening, for there’s no man live could sit down in they.”



“never fear,” return Bracy encouragingly; “only do you go into my

bedroom and put on your robe, and I’ll ensure your ‘take your seat

on your return.’ never make mountain of molehill, man; there be

bad dress than that in the world; for instance, it might have be a

kilt.”



“that’s true,” say Frere reflectively, and unhook the rich Mrs.

Bluebeard, he proceed after sundry ejaculation of disgust to carry it

into the other room, whither after a minute or two Bracy follow he,

to perform, as he say, the part of lady’s-maid. after a lapse of about

a quarter of an hour the door be again unclose, and Bracy, exclaim,

“now, Mr. Arundel, allow I to have the honour of introduce you to his

Sublime Highness Ree Chard el Freer,” usher in the person name.



never be so complete a transformation see. the persian dress, round

off and conceal the angularity of his figure, give a sort of

dignity to Frere, quite in keep with the character he be about

to assume; while moustache and a flow beard impart a degree

of picturesqueness to his countenance which accord well with his

irregular but expressive feature and bright animate eye. a shawl

of rich pattern confine his waist, while a girdle, stud with

(apparently) precious stone, sustain a sword and dagger, the jewel

hilt and brilliantly ornament sheath of which add not a little to

the magnificence of his appearance.



“_Voilà!_” exclaim Bracy, pat he on the back. “what do you think

of that by way of a get-up? there’s a ready-make prince for you. asylum

of the Universe, how do you find yourself? do your new garment sit

easily?”



“none of your nonsense, sir,” reply Frere. “if I be a prince, behave

to I as _sich_, if you please. I tell you what, I shall be tear

some of this drapery before the evening be over. ah! well, it be not for

life, that be one comfort; but I never be properly thankful before for

not having be bear a woman. think of sink into the vale of year in

a muslin skirt--what a prospect for an intellectual being!”



“now, Mr. Arundel, your dress await you,” say Bracy, “and ‘time be on

the wing.’ we shall have her ladyship in hysteric if she fancy her

prince mean to disappoint she.”



Lewis’s toilet be soon complete, and prove eminently successful,

the flow robe set off his tall, graceful figure to the utmost

advantage, and the scarlet fez, with its drooping tassel, contrast

well with his dark curl and enhance the effect of his delicately cut

and strike feature. Bracy make his appearance at the same

moment, most elaborately get up for the occasion, with a blue satin

underwaistcoat and what he be pleased to denominate the Order of the

Holy Poker suspend by a red ribbon from his button-hole, the tiger of

lie celebrity be despatch for a vehicle, and the trio start.



“to a reflective mind,” begin Bracy, when an interval of wood-pavement

allow conversation to become audible,--“to a reflective mind, there

be no section of the zoology of the London street more interesting

than that which treat of the habit and general economy of the genus

cabman.”



“as to their general economy,” return frere, “as far as I be

acquaint with it, it appear to consist in do you out of more than

their fare, and expend the capital thus acquire at a gin-palace.”



“Sir, you misapply term, treat an important subject with unbecoming

levity, and libel an interesting race of man,” return Bracy, with a

countenance of the most immovable gravity.



“interest, you mean,” rejoin Frere.



“one very striking peculiarity of the specie,” continue Bracy, not

heed the interruption, “be their talent for subtle analysis of

character, and power of discriminate it by the application of unusual

test.”



“what’s come now?” inquire Frere. “keep your ear open, Lewis, my

son, and acquire wisdom from the lip of the descendant of many Bracys.”



“I be aware an assertion of this nature should not be lightly hazard,”

 resume Bracy, “as it carry little conviction to the ill-regulate

mind of the sceptical, unless it be verify by some illustrative

example draw from the actual.”



“you have not get such a thing as a Johnson’s Dictionary about you, I

suppose?” interrupt frere. “I want to look out a few of those long

word.”



“with this view,” resume Bracy, “I will relate a little anecdote, which

will at the same time prove my position and display the capacity of the

London cabman for terse and epigrammatic definition. I have be engage

on committee business at the House of Commons a short time since, and

be return to my lodging when, as I emerge into Palace Yard, it

begin to rain. see I without an umbrella, a cabman on the stand

hail I with a view of ascertain whether I require his service.

while I be debate with myself whether the rain be likely to

increase or not, I be hail by the cad of an omnibus just turn into

Parliament Street.”



“I never do make pun,” begin Frere, “or else I should be incline to

ask whether be expose to so much _hail_ and rain at the same time

do not give you cold?”



“it happen that I have just bet a new hat with a man,” continue

Bracy, still preserve the most perfect gravity, “as to how many time

the chairman of the committee would take snuff, and have lose my wager;

this make I feel awfully stingy, and accordingly avail myself of

the low of the two estimate, I fraternise with the ’bus fellow, and

metaphorically throw over the cabman. as I be ascend the step of

the vehicle I have resolve to patronise, the following remark from

the injure Jehu reach my ear; it be address to an amphibious

individual, ‘_en sabot et bandeaux de foin_’ (as the _Morning Post_

would have it), clepe the waterman; and if you don’t think it fully

bear out my previous assertion, I can only say that you be an

incompetent judge of evidence. he first attract his friend’s attention

by point to I over his shoulder with his thumb, and wink

significantly; then add in a tone of intense disgust, ‘see that cove;

I thort he worn’t no good.’Stead o’ takin’ a cab to his self, like a

gent, he’s a goin’ to have _threepen’orth of all sort_’” as Bracy, amid

the laughter of his companion, conclude his recital, the vehicle which

convey they draw up at the door of Lady Lombard’s mansion.









chapter X.--CONTAINS a PRACTICAL commentary on the proverb, “all be not

GOLD WHICH GLITTERS.”



Lady Lombard, be in many sense of the word a great lady, live in

a great house, which look out upon that gloomy sight, a London garden,

and have its front door at the back for the sake of appearance. at this

pervert entrance do Bracy’s mendacious tiger, stand on tip-toe

the well to reach the knocker, fulminate like a duodecimo edition

of Olympian Jove, until two colossal footman, in a great state of

excitement and scarlet plush, open the door so suddenly as nearly to

cause the prostration of the booted boy, who only save himself from

fall by stumble, boot and all, against the tall shin of the

high footman, thereby elicit from that noble creature an

ejaculation suggestive of his intense appreciation of the injury do

he, and hinting, not obscurely, at his wish in regard to the future

destiny of his juvenile assailant. that youth, however, who, we be

force to confess, be not only as “impudent as he be high,” but,

reckon by the peculiar standard which the expression aforesaid

indicate, at the very least three foot more so, hasten thus to rebuke

his adversary: “hit’s lucky for you, Maypole, as I hain’t hon the bench

of majorstrait yet, hor ther’d a be five bob hout o’ your red plush

pocket for swearin’, as sure have heggs be heggs! Hif that’s hall yer

gratitude for I a-bringin’ of ye my honourable master and two noble

Purshun prince, hi’d well horder the carridge to turn round and

take’em back agen.”



having astonish the disgusted giant by this speech, the imp bound

down the step and hold open the cab-door with an air of dignified

condescension.



“be not that boy a treasure?” whisper Bracy to Frere as they alight.

“how neatly he take the shine out of that thick-witted pyramid of fool’s

flesh! I could not have do the thing well myself.”



“I don’t pretend to any very unusual power of foresight,” mutter

Frere under his beard, “but I think I could point out that brat’s

residuary legatee.”



“ah, indeed!” return Bracy; “and who do you fix upon? the archbishop

of Canterbury?”



“no, the hangman,” be the gruff reply.



“well, I’d myself venture to insure he against drown for a very

moderate premium,” rejoin his master, laugh; “but now I really must

beg you to bear in mind that you be utterly ignorant of the English

language.”



“Inshallah! I’d forget my illustrious descent most completely,”

 answer Frere, “but I’ll be careful; so, for the next three hour, ‘my

native’ tongue, ‘good-night.’”



while this conversation have be carry on in an undertone, the

party have be usher upstairs amidst the wonder gaze of servant

innumerable, of all sort and size, from the little foot-page

staggering under a galaxy of button to the mighty butler barely able to

walk beneath the weight of his own dignity.



“what name shall I say, gentleman?” ask the last-name official in his

most insinuating tone; for a persian prince be a rarity sufficient to

impress even his imperturbable spirit with a sense of respect.



“his Highness Prince Mustapha Ali Khan and suite,” return Bracy

authoritatively.



immediately the door of a well-light saloon be fling back on its

hinge, and in a stentorian voice the major domo announce, “his

Highness Prince Mystify-all-I-can and see-it.”



“by Jove! he’s hit it,” whisper Bracy to Lewis, as, follow Frere,

they enter the room. “he willn’t beat that if he try till he’s black

in the face.”



as he finish speak, the guest, who have crowd as near the door as

good breeding would allow to witness the Prince’s _entrée_, draw back as

a rustling of silk and satin announce the approach of their hostess.



Lady Lombard, who, to judge by appearance, would never again celebrate

her forty-fifth birthday, have be a handsome, and still be a

fine-look woman. she be tall and portly; in fact portly be rather

a mild term to use in speaking of her ladyship, but we don’t like to

stigmatise she as stout, and beyond that we could not go in speak

of a lady. she have a very bright colour and a very fair skin, in the

display of which she be by no mean niggardly, her gown have short

sleeve (so short, indeed, as scarcely to be worth mention), and

being----well, we know a french word which would express our meaning,

but we prefer our own language, and must therefore say, be rather too

much off where it would have be well a little more on. she wear a

profusion of light ringlet, which we feel justified in state, upon

our personal responsibility, to have be her own, for Lady Lombard be

an honourable woman, and pay her bill most punctually. these flaxen

lock rejoice in one peculiarity--they be not divide in the centre,

after the usual method, but the _in medio tutissimn ibis_ principle have

be abandon in favour of a new and striking coiffure, which, until we

be introduce to her ladyship, we have believe to be restrict to the

blue-and-silver epicene page who worship the prima donna and poke fun

at the soubrette on the opera stage. the page-like parting, then, be

on one side of her head, and across her ample forehead lie a festoon

of hair, arrange so as to suggest, to a speculative mind, a fanciful

resemblance to the drapery at the top of a window curtain. her feature

be by no mean without expression; on the contrary, meek pomposity

and innocent self-satisfaction be write in legible character on her

good-natured countenance.



the most carefully write description usually prove inadequate to

convey to the reader’s mind a just idea of the object they would fain

depict; but as we be especially anxious that other should see Lady

Lombard with our eye, we must beg their attention to the follow

simple process, by which we trust to enable they to realise she.



let each reader, then, call to mind the last average speciman of fat and

fair babyhood which may have come under his notice; let he imagine it

clothe in the rich sky-blue satin; let he deprive it of its coral,

and substitute in its place a gold watch and appendage; round its fat

little excuse for a neck let he clasp a diamond necklace; let he dress

its hair, or provide it a flaxen wig--if its hair should be as yet a

pleasure to come--make after the fashion we have above describe; and

let he, lastly, by a powerful effort of imagination, inflate this baby

until, still preserve its infantine proportion, it shall stand five

foot nine in its satin shoes,--and he will then have arrive at a very

correct idea of Lady Lombard as she appear when, rustle forward in a

tremor of delight, she advance to perform the part of gracious hostess

to the Prince of Persia.



“really, Mr. Bracy,” she begin, as that gentleman, with a countenance of

solemn satisfaction, step forward to meet she, “really, this be _too_

kind of you; how do you do? so you have positively bring I the _dear_

prince? _will_ you introduce I to he, and explain to he how _very_

much honoured I be by his condescension in come this evening?”



be it observe, by the way, that her ladyship speak with the great

_empressement_, and have a habit of utter many of her word in

italic, not to say small capital.



“it will give I much satisfaction to do so,” return Bracy, with grave

courtesy; “but I can assure you the prince come quite of his own accord.

the moment I have explain your invitation to he he catch the note out

of my hand, press it three time to his forehead, and exclaim in the

court dialect of Iraun, ‘_Hahazyr imeyur manzur_, he do, indeed.”



“no-o-o, _really!_” ejaculate Lady Lombard, more emphatically than she

have ever yet speak in her life; then, as a faint glimmering come across

she that there be a slight anomaly in appear so deeply interested in

a remark which she could by no possibility understand, she add: “but

you should recollect, Mr. Bracy, that _every_ one do not possess your

remarkable acquaintance with the eastern language.”



“Psha! how forgetful I be!” return Bracy. “your ladyship must excuse

I; the prince have be so short a time in this country that I be

scarcely yet accustomed to my new duty. the few word I have the honour

to repeat to you merely signify--you know the eastern metaphor be

very peculiar--‘I will kiss’--it’s the usual form of accept any

distinguished invitation--‘I will kiss her ladyship’s door-mat!’

curious, be it not?”



“yes, _indeed_,” be the sympathetic reply. at the same moment Bracy,

turn to Frere, present he to their hostess, say “Prince, this

be Lady Lombard--_Twygt-hur rhumauld gâl!_”



the first sound that escape his Highness be a hysterical grunt which,

in an Englishman, might have be deem indicative of suppress

laughter, but proceed from the bearded lip of a persian potentate,

assume the character of an eastern ejaculation. after mutter a few

_real_ persian word with an appearance of deep respect, Frere take she

ladyship’s plump white hand between both his own and raise it to his

lip; then, relinquish it, he speak again, make a low salaam, and

draw himself up to his full height, cross his arm on his breast

and stand motionless before she. the appeal look which she cast upon

Bracy when the prince speak be a severe trial to his gravity; but by

long experience in practical joking he have acquire wonderful command

of countenance, which stand he now in good stead, and he proceed

to translate Frere’s sentence into certain flowery and unmeaning

compliment, which be about as unlike their real signification as need

be.



after Lewis have go through the same ceremony without the speech, for

which omission Bracy account by explain that it be not etiquette

for the persian noble to speak when in attendance on their prince,

they be lead to the upper end of the apartment, where Frere seat

himself cross-legged on a sofa and make himself very much at home,

keep Bracy fully employ in invent translation to speech, not

one word of which he, or any one else present, comprehend. Lewis, in

the meantime, who be become dreadfully tired of the whole affair,

stand near the end of the sofa, with his arm fold across his breast,

look especially scornful and very particularly bored.



“ah!” exclaim Lady Lombard, as a pretty, graceful girl, very simply

dress, make her way up the room, “there’s that _dear_ Laura Peyton

arrive. I _must_ go and speak to she, and bring she to be introduce

to the Prince.” she then add, aside to Bracy, “she’s _immensely_ rich;

clear six thousand a year, and do not spend two.”



“a very charming trait in her character,” return Bracy. “I’ll mention

it to the Prince. I don’t know that there ever _be_ an Englishwoman

queen of Persia; but that’s no reason there never should be one.”



Bracy be accordingly introduce to the young lady, and lead she, smile

and blushing, up to Frere, by whom he seat she, and pave the way for

conversation by the follow remark:--



“_tharmy buoi aintsheaz tunnar?_” which for the damsel’s edification he

translated--“Asylum of the Universe! the maiden, the daughter of rose,

salute thee!”



after a short interval Lady Lombard again bear down upon they in full

sail, tow in her wake a small, hirsute, baboon-like individual,

evidently one of her menagerie.



“there’s a chimpanzee!” whisper Bracy to Frere. “now, if that picture

of ugliness turn out an eastern traveller we’re go ’coon.”



“all right,” return frere in the same tone, “he’s only an exile

something. he come to our shop with a recommendation from some of the

parisian _savan_ the other day.”



“I must trouble you _once_ again, Mr. Bracy,” insinuate Lady Lombard.

“Professor Malchapeau be _die_ to be introduce.”



“no trouble, but a pleasure,” return Bracy. “I shall have the great

satisfaction in make two such illustrious individual know to each

other. do the Professor speak English?”



“Yas; I vas spik Angleesh von pritté veil,” reply the person allude

to, strut forward on tiptoe. “I ave zie honaire to vish you how you

do, my prince?”



Frere make some reply, which Bracy paraphrase into “the descendant of

many shah kiss the hem of the mantle of the Father of science.”



the Professor’s “Angleesh” not provide he with a suitable reply

ready make, he be oblige to resort to that refuge for destitute

foreigner--a shrug and a grimace.



Lady Lombard come to his assistance.



“now, Professor, suppose you be to tell his Highness your affect

history;” add in a whisper, “Mr. Bracy, the interpreter, be connect

with government, and might be of the great use to you.”



“Ohf, miladi, if all zie body have your big heart in dem, zie vicke

vorld should be von eaven,” reply the Professor, gratefully, through

his talented nose. “my littel storie! ohf, zie Prince should not vant to

ear he?”



his Highness, however, be graciously pleased to signify his anxiety

so to do, the small man resumed--



“ah, _ma Patri!_ vhat I ave come thro’ for he, ven I vill _raconte_

nobody shall not belief.”



“to enable the Prince to understand your account more clearly,”

 interrupt Bracy, “may I ask to what country it relate?”



“vidout von doubt, saire! you shall tell zie Prince dat my littel tale

be Swish. my fadaire vas vot you call von mayor of zie canton of Zurich.

my brodaire and myselfs vas his only schild; since a long time ve va

live very appy, _mais enfin_--but on his end--zie _sacré Autriche_--von

bad oystrish government, do vot you call oppress _ma pauvre patrie_,

and my fadaire, _toujour brave_, get himself into von littel

conspiration, vaire he do commit vat you call zie offence politique;

vas trown to prison, and in his confinement he do die. ah! ‘_mourir

pour la patrie, c’est doux_,’ to die for zie country be zie--vat you

call _doux_ in Angleesh?”



“you will find the same word in both language, Professor, only we

pronounce it deuce,” reply Bracy politely.



“ah! c’est bon, to die for zie country be zie deuce! _eh bien_, after

my poor fadaire be entomb, my brodaire do run himselfs avay, and vas

convert to _un berger_, a little shepherd of cow, and I, _hélas! pour

moi, fêtais désolé_--for myself, I be dissolute, leave alone in zie

vide vorld, visout von friend to turn against. _Mais le ciel embrace

les orphelins_--eaven embarrasse zie orphan; I march on my foot to

Paris; I find an unexpected uncle, who have suppose himself dead for

some year; I undervent all zie sciences, and _enfin I voici_--on my

end here I be.”



“a most affect history indeed,” return Bracy, cover his mouth

with his hand to conceal a smile. as for Frere, he have for some time

past be nearly suffocate by suppress laughter, which at length

make itself so apparent that nothing but his beard and an assumed fit of

coughing could have save he from discovery.



while this conversation have be go on, Miss Peyton call Lady

Lombard’s attention to Lewis by observe: “the interpreter, in

entertain the Prince, seem entirely to have forget that very

handsome young attendant who stand there, look so haughty and

disconsolate.”



“dear I! so he do,” exclaim Lady Lombard anxiously. “how _very_

handsome he be! such a thoroughly eastern countenance! _he’s_ a man of

very high rank, too, over there. what _could_ we do to amuse he?”



“perhaps we might show he some print,” suggest Laura; “at all event

the attention might please he.”



“oh, _yes!_ how _clever_ of you! I should never have think of that

now. I’ve a table cover with they in the boudoir,” exclaim Lady

Lombard delightedly; “but do you think you could turn they over for he?

I’m so foolish, I should be quite _nervous_; you see it’s so awkward his

not understand English, poor fellow! I know I’m _very_ foolish.”



“I shall be most happy to do anything I can to lessen your

difficulty,” reply the young lady good-naturedly. “shall I look out

a book of print?”



“if you _would_ be so kind, my dear, you’ll find _plenty_ in the

boudoir; and I’ll go to Mr. Bracy and get he to speak to he for I.”



the result of this application be the capture of Lewis, who, inwardly

rage, be carry off to the boudoir and seat at a table, while Miss

Peyton, half frightened, half amuse, turn over a volume of print for

his edification. Lady Lombard and sundry of the guest stand round for

some minute watch the smile and pantomimic gesture with which

Lewis, or rather Hassan Bey, as Bracy have name he, feel bound to

acknowledge the young lady’s attention.



amongst the guest who be thus amuse themselves lounge a young

dandy, who, on the strength of a Mediterranean yacht voyage, set up for

a distinguished traveller. to Lady Lombard’s inquiry whether he speak

persian he simper, “Re’ely--no, not exactly so as to talk to he; but

he’ll do vastly well. they prefer silence, re’ely, those fellow do. you

know I’ve see so much of ’em.”



“you be in Persia, be you not?” ask one of the company.



“Re’ely--not exactly in his part of Persia. Stamboul, the city of

palace, be my headquarters: but it’s much the same; indolence, beard,

and tobacco be the characteristic of both race.”



“don’t you think he be charmingly handsome?” ask an old young lady,

shake her ringlet after a fashion which five year before have be a

very “tell” manoeuvre.



“Re’ely, I should scarcely have say so,” be the reply; “the boy be

well enough for an Asiatic, I like a more--ahem!--manly style of thing.”

 and as he speak he pass his hand caressingly over a violent pair of

red whisker which garnish his own hard-feature physiognomy.



the cool impudence of this remark inspire Lewis with so intense a

sentiment of disgust that his lip curl involuntarily, and he turn

over the print before he with a gesture of impatience. on look up

he be rather disconcert to find Laura Peyton’s pierce black eye

watch he curiously.



“you’ve give we nothing new in the musical way lately, Lady Lombard,”

 observe the “sere and yellow leaf” damsel before allude to.



“I expect a lady to stay with I soon,” be the reply, “whom I _think_

you’ll be pleased with; she sing and play in very_ first_-rate style.”



“indeed! be she an amateur or professional, may I inquire?”



“why, _really_, my dear Miss Sparkless, you’ve ask a difficult

question. the fact be,” continue Lady Lombard, sink her voice, “it’s

one of those _very_ sad case, reduce fortune--you understand. I mean

to have she here _merely_ out of charity.” sink her voice still

low, the follow word only become audible: “wife of a Captain

Arundel--foreign extraction originally--quite a _mésalliance_, I

believe.”



as she speak some new arrival attract her attention, and she and her

confidante leave the boudoir together.



it may easily be conceive with what feeling of burn indignation

Lewis have listen to the forego remark; but Frere’s lecture of the

morning have not be without its fruit. with his anger the necessity

for self-control present itself, and he be congratulate himself at

having check all outward sign of annoyance when he be startle by

a silvery voice whispering in his ear: “persian or no Persian, sir,

you understand English as well as I do;” and slightly turn, his eye

encounter those of Laura Peyton fix on he with a roguish glance.

his resolution be instantly take, and he reply in the same tone:

“having discover my secret, you must promise to keep it.”



“agree, on one condition,” be the rejoinder.



“and that is------?” ask Lewis.



“that you immediately make a full confession and tell I all about it.”



“it be a compact,” be the reply.



“that be good,” rejoin the young lady. “now move the portfolio, so

that your back will be towards those people. that will do. hold down

your head as if you be examine the print, and then answer my

question truly and concisely. first, you be an english gentleman?”



“yes, I hope so.”



“who be the prince?”



“my friend, Richard Frere.”



“and why have you both come here dress like Persians?”



“to mystify our foolish hostess.”



“for shame, sir! I’m very fond of Lady Lombard.”



“but you know she be a silly woman.”



“well, never mind. who plan this hoax?”



“Bracy, the so-call interpreter.”



“do Prince Frere talk real Persian?”



“yes.”



“and do the other man understand he?”



“not a bit.”



“then he invent all the answer? that’s rather clever of he. I shall

go and listen presently. and you cann’t talk either persian or gibberish,

so you hold your tongue and look sulky. well, I think it’s all very

wrong; but it’s rather droll. poor, dear Lady Lombard! she’d never

survive it if she do but know! and now, tell I, lastly, what put you

in a rage just this minute and enable I to find you out?”



“you would not care to know.”



“but I do care to know, sir, and you have promise to answer all my

question.”



“you hear the speech that woman make about a Mrs. Arundel?”



“yes, surely.”



“Learn, then, that my name be Lewis Arundel, and the lady refer to

be my mother. now do you understand?”



as Lewis utter these word, in atone of suppress bitterness, his

companion hastily turn her head and say, in a low, hurry voice--“I

beg your pardon! I fear I have pain you; but I do not know--I could

not guess----”



“pray do not distress yourself,” return Lewis kindly, Rose’s smile for

a moment smooth his haughty brow and play round his proud mouth.

“I be sure you would not hurt any one’s feeling knowingly; and since

you observe my annoyance, I be glad to have be able to explain its

cause.”



so engrossed have they be by this conversation that they have not

observe Miss Sparkless enter the boudoir by another door; and they be

first make aware of her presence by see her standing, breathless with

astonishment, at discover Miss Peyton in familiar colloquy with a

persian nobleman utterly ignorant of the English language.



“do you speak German?” ask Lewis quickly.



“yes, a little,” return Miss Peyton.



“she have not catch a word yet,” continue Lewis. “tell she you find

out by accident that I have pick up a few german sentence when

the Prince be at the court of Prussia. White lie, unhappily, be

inevitable on these occasion,” he continue, see his companion

hesitate. “it’s the only way to prevent an _éclaircissementt_; and then,

think of poor Lady Lombard’s feeling!”



“as I seem fairly embark in the conspiracy, I suppose I must do your

bidding,” be the reply, and Miss Sparkless, the middle-aged young lady,

be accordingly inform of Lewis’s german proficiency, whereat, fall

into an ecstasy, she replied--



“how charming! what a dear creature he be!” on which the dear creature

himself, catch Miss Peyton’s eye, be very near laugh outright.



“Laura, my _love_,” exclaim Lady Lombard, enter hastily, “the

Prince be go down to supper; will you come?” then take her hand

caressingly, she add, “have you be _very_ much bore by he, poor

fellow?”



“I find he could speak a few word of German, and that help we on,”

 be the reply.



“yes, _really_--ah; we might have think of _that_ before,” return

Lady Lombard, by no mean certain the german language might not form an

important and customary branch of persian education.



during supper Laura Peyton contrive to be seat between Frere and

Bracy, the latter of whom she keep so constantly engage in interpreting

for she that he scarcely get anything to eat, and come to the conclusion

that in the whole course of his experience he have never before

encounter such a talk woman. nor be his annoyance diminish by

observe that Lewis, who be seat opposite, appear to be derive

the utmost amusement from his discomfiture. having exhaust every

possible pretext for break off the conversation, and be each time

foil by the young lady’s quiet tact, he be about to resign himself to

his fate and relinquish all idea of supper, when a project occur to

he which he immediately hasten to put into execution. wait till

Frere have speak a persian sentence, he suddenly draw himself up,

look deeply scandalise, frown at the speaker, shake his head and

mutter something unintelligible in a tone of grave remonstrance, then

pause for a reply, which Frere, intensely perplexed, and by no mean

clear that he have not do something un-persian and wrong, be force to

utter. this only seem to make matter bad: Bracy again remonstrate

in gibberish, then appear to have determine on his course, and

muttering, “well, there’s no help for it, I suppose,” he turn to Lady

Lombard, and begin in a tone of deep concern--



“I have a most disagreeable duty to perform, and must beg you to believe

that nothing but absolute necessity could have induce I to mention the

matter; but I have remonstrate with his Highness without effect, and I

dare go no far--he be subject to most violent burst of passion, and

become dangerous when oppose. he draw his dagger and attempt to stab

I only yesterday, because I interfere to prevent his have one of the

waiter of the hotel strangle with a bow-string.”



Lady Lombard turn pale on receive this information, while Bracy

continued--



“it be most unfortunate, but the Prince have be so much delighted with

this young lady’s charming flow of conversation that, in his ignorance

of the custom of this country, he have actually commission I to offer

you £500 for she, and declare his determination of take her home with

he.”



the effect of this communication may be “well imagine than

describe.” Miss Peyton, aware of the true state of affair, hide she

face in her handkerchief in an uncontrollable fit of laughter; Lewis,

sorely tempt to follow her example, bent over his plate till the

flow tassel of the fez conceal his feature; frere, excessively

annoyed at the false imputation, all but begin a flat denial of the

charge in somewhat forcible English, but remember his assume

character just in time, clench his fist and grind his tooth with

impatience, while Lady Lombard, observe these gesture, and construe

they into indication of an approach burst of fury, be nearly

swoon with terror, when a note be put into her hand by a servant;

hastily cast her eye over it, she hand it to Bracy, saying--



“this be most fortunate; it may serve to divert his attention.”



as he become aware of its content his countenance fall, and hold it

so that Frere might read it, he whispered--



“here’s a treat! we _be_ in for it now, and no mistake!”



the note run as follows:--



“Dr. --------, Persian Professor at Addiscombe, present his compliment

to Lady Lombard, and beg to inform she that be only in town for a

few hour, and learn accidentally that his Highness Prince Mustapha

Ali be spend the evening at her house, he have venture to request

her permission to intrude upon her uninvited, as he be most anxious to

renew his acquaintance with his Highness, whom he have the honour to know

in Persia.”









CHAPTER XI.--TOM BRACY MEETS his MATCH.



the position in which we leave Lewis and his friend at the conclusion

of the precede chapter be decidedly more peculiar than agreeable, and

afford no bad illustration of the american expression, “a pretty

tall fix.” Bracy, the fertile in expedient, be the first to hazard

a suggestion, which he do by whisper to Frere, “you have well be

take suddenly ill; I shall say you have have too much tongue (if you

have not, I have), and that it have disagree with you.”



“wait a bit,” return frere; “you have see the real Prince, haven’t

you?”



Bracy nod in assent, and Frere continue, “he’s something like I, be

he not?”



“well looking,” be the uncomplimentary rejoinder.



“well, never mind that,” resume Frere. “I don’t set up for a beauty,

but if I be sufficiently like to pass for he I might contrive to humbug

the fellow for a few minute, and then we could manage to slip away

quietly without any shindy at all.”



“you can try it on if you choose, but he be safe to find you out,

unless he be a perfect fool, and that be too great a mercy to hope for,”

 return Bracy dejectedly. “if the bad come to the bad, pretend to

pick a quarrel with he, draw your carving-knife and make a poke at he;

then Arundel and I will bundle he out of the room bodily, and swear we

be do it to save his life. I can see nothing else for it, for there

go the woman, and, by Jove, here’s the learn Pundit himself! oh! ben’t

he pretty to look at? why, he be a fac-simile of the picture in the old

edition of Gay’s Fables, of ‘the Monkey who have see the World.’”



while this dialogue be proceed, Lady Lombard, having gather the

lady under her wing, have march they off to the drawing-room, Miss

Peyton find an opportunity as she pass Lewis to say, in German,

“tell your Prince that when I sell myself I shall want a great deal more

than £500.”



“in fact, that your value be quite inestimable,” return Lewis.



“exactly so,” be the reply. “I be glad you have sufficient penetration

to have find it out already.”



the description give by Bracy of the Doctor’s outward man be by no

mean inapt. his hair and whisker be grey, and, still adhere to the

fashion of his young day, he wear powder and a pig-tail. his dress

consist of a black single-breasted coat with a stand-up collar,

knee breech, and silk stocking; a profusion of shirt-frill rush

impetuously out of the front of his waistcoat, a stiff white neckcloth

appear thoroughly to deserve the appellation of “choker” which Bracy

apply to it, while a shirt-collar starch to a pitch of savage

harshness invade the region of his cheek to an extent which render

the tract of country lie between the ear and the corner of the

mouth a complete _terra incognita_. constant study of the eastern

hieroglyphic have probably render his wear spectacle a matter of

necessity; at all event a huge pair in a broad tortoiseshell setting

garnish his nose, which, truth compel we to confess, be more than

slightly red, in which particular it afford a decide contrast to

his general complexion, which be, we say it distinctly and without

compromise, yellow.



to this gentleman, who enter with a hasty step and glance round he

with a quick, abrupt, and rather startling manner, do Bracy address

himself with much _empressement_.



“my dear sir, this be most fortunate; the Prince be quite delighted at

the rencontre, but you must expect to find his Highness greatly alter.

the care of life, my dear sir, the anxiety attend--ah! I see you

be impatient; I willn’t detain you, but I wish to warn you that if you

should perceive any great change in his appearance, you must not be

surprised, and above all be careful not to show it by your manner. you

have no idea how sensitive he be on the point; quite morbidly so,

really. don’t let I detain you--how well you be look!”



a good deal of pantomimic action have accompany the delivery of this

speech, the Doctor be engage in make vain and futile attempt to

get past his persecutor, who on his part continue, with an affectation

of the deep respect, constantly, and with the utmost perseverance, to

frustrate they. the conclude word of his address, however, elicit

the follow rejoinder, speak in a quick, cross manner:--



“you have the advantage of I, sir, for I do not remember ever set

eye on you before in my life. I never forget a face I have once see.”



“confound his memory!” think Bracy, “Frere willn’t have a chance with

he;” he only say, however, “you be right, Doctor; the fact of your

look well be so self-evident that I venture to remark it, without

have any previous datum to go upon--but here be his Highness,” and as

he speak, he at length move on one side and allow the man of learn

to pass.



frere come forward at the same minute, Bracy whisper, while the

Doctor bend in a low salaam:



“I have bother his brain sweetly for he, he hardly know whether

he’s stand on his head or his heel; so now you must take care of

yourself, and joy go with you.”



Frere, thus apostrophise, return the Doctor’s salute with much

cordiality, and Bracy, feign some excuse, leave they to entertain

each other, have before his eye a wholesome dread of the newcomer’s

address he in Persian, and thereby discover his deplorable

ignorance of that interesting language.



Time, which do not stand still for prince any more than for private

individual, pass on with its usual rapidity. Most of the gentleman

having eat as much, and drink probably more (look at it in a

medical point of view) than be good for they, have rejoin the lady,

and it become evident to Bracy that a crisis in his evening’s amusement

be approach. on his return to the drawing-room he must of course

resume his duty as interpreter, and this inconvenient Persian

professor would inevitably discover the imposture. this be the more

provoking, as Frere’s likeness to the Prince must evidently have be

much strong than he have imagine, and his acquaintance with the rule

of persian etiquette more extensive than he have believe possible, for

the Doctor continue to converse with the utmost gravity, and appear

to believe in he implicitly. while he be still ponder the matter

in his anxious mind, the few last remain guest convey themselves

away, and the Prince and his party be leave to dispute possession of

the supper-room with empty champagne bottle and half-tipsy waiter.

Frere, when he perceive this to be the case, beckon Bracy to

approach, and as soon as he be within earshot, whispered--



“I have humbug the old fellow beautifully on the score of our Persian

recollection, but he have just be question I about you,--where you

acquire your knowledge of the language, whether you have be much in

the East, how I become acquainted with you, and all the rest of it. I

put he off with lie as long as I could, but it would not do, and as a

last resource, I have be oblige to refer he to you.”



“the deuce you have!” be the reply; “that be pleasant. he’ll be

jabber his confounded lingo, and I shall not understand a word he

say to I; besides, my jargon willn’t go down with he, you know. I tell

you what, I shall be off, and you must say upstairs (he can interpret

for you) that I have be send for by the prime minister at a minute’s

notice, _à la_ De Grandeville.”



“’tis too late,” reply Frere; and at the same instant the doctor

seize Bracy by the button, and in a stern and impressive manner ask

some apparently search question in Persian. few man have enjoy the

delight of see Tom Bracy in the unenviable frame of mind express

by the nautical term “take aback,” but of that favour few be the

bystander on the present occasion. never be an unhappy individual

more thoroughly and completely at a loss; and it must be confess

the situation be an embarrassing one. to be address by an elderly

stranger in an unintelligible language, in which you be expect to

reply, while at the same time you be painfully conscious that your

incapacity to do so, or even (not understand the question) to give an

appropriate answer in your native tongue, will lead to a discovery you

be most anxious to avert, be an undeniably awkward position in which

to be place. that Bracy find it so be most evident, for he fidget,

stammer, glance appealingly towards Frere for aid, and at last be

oblige, between annoyance and an intense appreciation of the absurdity

of his situation, to get up a fictitious cough, which, irritate the

membrane of the nose, produce a most violent genuine sneeze. from the

effect of this convulsion of nature he be relieve by a hearty slap

on the back, while at the same moment the tone of a familiar voice

exclaim in his ear--



“Sold, by all that’s glorious! Bracy, my boy, how do you find yourself?”

 and on look up he recognise in the laugh face of the Addiscombe

doctor, now divest of its spectacle, the well-know feature of

Charley Leicester.









CHAPTER XII.--LEWIS forfeit the respect of all POOR-LAW guardian.



equally surprised and mystify at the complete manner in which the

table have be turn upon he, Bracy stand listen with a

disgusted expression of countenance to the peal of laughter which his

discomfiture elicit from his companion.



“yes, laugh away,” growl the victimise practical joker; “it’s

all very funny, I dare say, but one thing I’ll swear in any court of

justice, which be, that you have be talk real Persian, at least if

what Frere jabber be real Persian.”



“of course I have,” return Leicester, still in convulsion. “when

Frere and I plan this dodge we know what a wide-awake gentleman we

have to deal with, and take our measure accordingly. I learn four

persian sentence by heart from his dictation, and pretty good use I

have make of they too, I think.”



“it be not a bad idea, really,” observe Bracy, who, having get over

his annoyance at the first sense of defeat, instantly recover his

good-humour. “how well you be get up! I do not recognise you one bit

till you pull off the barnacle.”



“yes, I get little Stevens, who do the light comic business at one

of the minor, to provide the apparel and come and dress I. I hope you

admire my complexion; he lay on the red and yellow most unsparingly.”



“he have do it vastly well,” return Bracy. “I shall cultivate that

small man; he may be extremely useful to I on an occasion.”



“now we ought to be go upstairs,” interrupt frere; “these waiter

fellow be begin to stare at we suspiciously too. I say, Bracy, cut

it short, man; we have have all the fun now, and I’m get tired of the

thing.”



“Ya, Meinheer,” rejoin Bracy aloud, add in a low tone, “the

slavey will swallow that or anything else for Persian. they be all

more or less drunk, by the fishy expression of their optic.”



Laura Peyton be astonish somewhat later in the evening by the

Addiscombe professor lean over the back of the sofa on which she be

seat and ask whether she have enjoy her last valse at Almack’s the

evening before last.



“surely you can feel no particular interest about such a frivolous and

unintellectual matter, sir,” be the reply.



“I be about to follow up the inquiry by ask whether your partner

make himself agreeable.”



“to which I shall reply, after the irish fashion, by ask how it can

possibly concern you to know, sir?”



“merely because I have the honour of the gentleman’s acquaintance.”



“that, in fact, you be one of those uncommon character who know

themselves,” return Laura with an arch smile. “be not that what you

wish to impress upon I, Mr. Leicester?”



Charley laugh, then continue in a low tone, “I see you know I. do

your own acuteness lead to the discovery, or be there traitor among

we?”



“your friend Mr. Arundel’s expressive feature let I into the secret

of his acquaintance with the english language before we go down to

supper; but I enter into a contract, not to betray the plot if he

would tell I all I might wish to know about it, so the moment he come

up I make he inform I who you be. what a gentlemanly, agreeable

person he be!”



as she say this a slight shade pass across Leicester’s good-natured

countenance, and he reply, more quickly than be his wont--



“I have fancy Miss Peyton superior to the common feminine weakness of

be catch by the last handsome face.”



“what a thoroughly _man_-like speech!” return the young lady. “do I

say anything about his appearance, sir? do you suppose we poor woman

be so utterly silly that we can appreciate nothing but a handsome face?

your professor’s disguise have imbue you with the turkish belief that

woman have no soul.”



“no one fortunate enough to be acquaint with Miss Peyton would

continue long in such a heresy,” reply Leicester, with the air of a

man who think he be say a good thing.



“yes, I know you would make some such reply,” return Laura. “you first

show your real opinion of woman by libel the whole sex, and then try

to get out of the scrape by insult my understanding with a personal

compliment. wait,” she continue, see he be about to defend himself,

“you must not talk to I any more now, or you will excite Lady Lombard’s

suspicion and betray the whole conspiracy. go away, and send my new

friend Mr. Arundel Hassan Bey here; Lady Lombard commit he to my

charge, and I want to cultivate he.”



Leicester try to assume a languish look, which he be in the

habit of practise upon young lady with great success, but become

suddenly conscious of the wig and spectacle, and gather from

Laura’s silvery laugh that such adjunct to an interesting expression

of countenance be incongruous, not to say absurd, he join in her

merriment, then add, “you be in a very wicked mood to-night, Miss

Peyton; but I suppose I must e’en do as you bid I, and reserve my

revenge till some more fitting opportunity;” then, mix with the

crowd, he seek out Lewis and deliver the young lady’s message

to he, add in his usual drawling tone, “you have make a

what-do-ye-call-it--an impression in that quarter. woman always run

after the last new face.”



“you be right,” return Lewis, with a degree of energy which startle

his listless companion; “and those man be wisest who know they for the

toy they be, and avoid they.”



Leicester gaze after his retreating figure in astonishment, then

murmur to himself, “what’s in the wind now, I wonder; be the good

youth try to keep up the asiatic character, or suddenly turn

woman-hater? confound that little Peyton girl, how sharp she be

to-night!”



“how very well Mr. Leicester be disguise!” observe Laura Peyton to

Lewis, after they have converse in German for some minute on general

topic.



“yes,” reply Lewis; “though I cann’t say his appearance be improve by

the alteration.”



“a fact of which he be fully aware,” return Laura, smile.



a pause ensue, which be terminate by Laura’s ask abruptly, “do

gentleman like Mr. Leicester?”



“really I have not sufficient knowledge of fact to inform you, but I

should say he be a very popular man.”



“popular man! I hate that phrase,” return his companion pettishly. “it

be almost as bad as describe any one as a man about town, which always

give I the idea of a creature that wear a pea-jacket, live at a

club, board on cigar, talk slang, carry a bet-book, and never

have its hair cut. cann’t you tell I what you think of Mr. Leicester

yourself?”



“well, I think he gentlemanly, good-natured, agreeable up to a certain

point, cleverish---”



^ “yes, that will do; I quite understand. I don’t think you do he

justice--he have a kind heart, and more good sense than you be dispose

to give he credit for. you should not form such hasty judgment of

people; a want of charity I perceive be one of your fault. and now

I must wish you good-night; I hear my kind old chaperone anxiously

bleat after I in the distance.”



so say she arise and hasten to put herself under the protection of

“a fine old English gentlewoman,” who, with a hooked nose, red gown,

and green scarf, look like some new and fearful variety of the genus

Parroquet. at the same time, Bracy summon Lewis to join the Prince,

who be about to depart, which, after Lady Lombard have in an enthusiasm

of gratitude utter a whole sentence in the large capital, he be

allow to do.



Leicester accompany they, tear himself away from Professor

Malchapeau, who have single he out as a brother savan, and commence

_raconte_-ing to he his affect history, thereby leave that shaggy

little child of misfortune to lament to his sympathise hostess the

melancholy fact that “Zie Professor Addiscombe have cut his little tale

off short, and transport himselfs avay in von great despatch.”



’twere long to tell the joke that be make, the new and additional

matter bring to light, as each of the quartette, assemble round a

second edition of supper in Bracy’s room, detail in turn his own

personal experience of the evening’s comicality--the cigar that be

smoke, or the amount of sherry cobbler that be imbibe: suffice it

to say, that a certain lyrical declaration that they would not “go home

till morning,” to which, during their symposium, they have commit

themselves, be verify when, on issue out into the street, the cold

grey light of early dawn throw its pale hue over their tired face and

struggle with sickly-look gas lamp for the honour of illuminate

the thoroughfare of the sleep city.



Leicester’s cab, with his night-horse--a useful animal, which, without

a leg to stand upon, possess the speed of the wind, and have every

defect horseflesh be heir to, enjoy a constitution which throve on

exposure and want of sleep, as other organisation usually do on the

exact opposite--be in wait. into this vehicle Charley (who bear

some token of sherry cobbler in the unsteadiness of his gait), having

make two bad shot at the step, rush headlong and drive off at an

insane pace, and in a succession of zigzag.



Frere and Lewis watch the cab till, having slightly assault an

unoffending lamp-post, it fly round a corner and disappear; then,

having exchange a significant glance suggestive of sympathetic

anticipation of a sombre character in regard to the safety of their

friend, they start at a brisk pace, which soon bring they to Frere’s

respectable dwelling. while the proprietor be search in every pocket

but the right one for that terror of all feeble-minded elder, that pet

abomination of all father of family, that late invention of the

enemy of mankind--a latch-key--they be accost by a lad of about

fifteen, whose ragged clothe, bronze feature, and murillo-like

appearance accord well with his supplication, “_per pietà Signor,

denaro per un pover’ italiano_.”



[illustration: 0094]



Frere look at he attentively, then exclaim, “I tell you what, boy,

it willn’t do; you’re no more an italian than I be. you should not try to

impose upon people.”



the boy hang down his head, and then reply doggedly, “it’s your own

fault; you’ll let an english boy starve in the street before you’ll

give he a bit of bread, but you be charitable enough to they foreign

blackguard.”



“that’s not true,” reply Frere. “however, liar or not, you must be

feed, I suppose; so if you choose to take a soup-ticket, here’s one for

you.”



“no,” return the boy proudly, “you have call I liar, and I willn’t

accept your miserable bounty. I’d soon starve first.”



“as you please,” return frere, coolly pocket the reject ticket.

“now have the goodness to take yourself off. Come, Lewis.”



“I’ll join you immediately,” reply Lewis.



“mind you shut the door after you, then,” continue Frere, “or we shall

have that nice lad walk off with the silver spoon.” so say, he

enter the house.



Lewis wait till his retreating footstep be no long audible, then

fix his pierce glance upon the boy, he say in an impressive voice,

“answer I truly, and I will give you assistance. where do you learn to

speak italian with so good an accent?”



“in Naples, sir!”



“how do you get there?”



“I serve on board a man-of-war.”



“and how have you fall into this state of beggary?”



the boy hesitate for a moment, but something lead he instinctively to

feel that his confidence would not be abuse, and he answer: “when we

get back to England and the crew be pay off I receive £15.

I get into bad company; they tempt I to everything that be wrong.

my money be soon go; I have no friend in London, and I wouldn’t have

apply to they after go on so bad if I’d have any. I sell my clothe

to buy bread; and when I have nothing leave I beg, and lately I’ve

pass myself off as an italian boy, because I find people more willing

to give to I.”



“and do you like your present life?”



“no, I have to bear cold and hunger; and when people speak to I as _he_

do just now it make I feel wicked. some day it will drive I mad, and

I shall go and murder somebody.”



“what do you wish to do, then?”



“if I could buy some decent clothe, I’d walk down to Portsmouth and try

and get afloat again.”



“and what would it cost to provide they?”



“I could rig myself out for a pound.”



Lewis pause for a moment, then add quickly: “boy, I be poor and

proud, as you be, therefore I can feel for you. have I be expose to

temptation, friendless and untaught, I might have fall as you have

do. you have learn a bitter lesson and may profit by it; it be in my

power to afford you a chance of do so.”



he draw a card from his pocket and write upon it a few word in pencil,

then hand it to the boy, continue: “there be the direction to a

friend of mine, the captain of a ship about to sail in a few day; show

he my card, and tell he what you have tell I. there be a sovereign

to provide your dress, and five shilling to save you from beg or

stealing till you get to Portsmouth; and when next you be tempt to

sin remember its bitter fruit.”



as he speak he give he the money. the boy receive it mechanically,

fix his bright eye for a moment on the face of his benefactor, and

then, utterly overcome by such unexpected kindness, burst into a flood

of tear. as Lewis turn to depart the first ray of the rise sun

fall upon the tall, graceful figure of the young man and the tattere

garment and emaciated form of the boy.



far different be the scene when Lewis Arundel and the creature he be

thus rescue from infamy meet again upon the _RAILROAD of life!_









CHAPTER XIII.--IS CHIEFLY HORTICULTURAL, show the effect PRODUCED by

training upon a sweet and delicate ROSE.



Rose Arundel sit at the open window of her little bedroom and gaze out

into the night. the scent of many flower hang upon the loaded air,

and the calm star look down from Heaven, contrast their impassive

grandeur with the unrest of this weary world. the evening have be

lovely; not a breath of wind be stir; the long shadow that sleep

upon the green sward, and afford a dark background on which the

brilliant glow-worm shone like diamond on a funeral pall, be

motionless; the silence, unbroken save when some heavy beetle or other

strange insect of the night wing its drowsy way across the casement,

be almost oppressive in its depth of stillness; it be a time and

place for grave and earnest thought, a scene in which the full heart be

conscious of its own sorrow. and Rose, although she have too much good

sense and right principle to allow herself to feel miserable, be far

from happy. the key to the inner life of every true-hearted woman must

be seek in the affection. the only two people whom Rose have love,

as she be capable of love, be her father and brother; for Mrs.

Arundel, though all her impulse be kind and amiable, do not possess

sufficient depth of character to inspire any very strong attachment.

between Captain Arundel and his daughter have exist one of those

rare affection which appear so nearly to satisfy the craving of our

spiritual nature, that lest this world should become too dear to we they

be blessing we be seldom permit long to enjoy. Rose and her father

be by nature much alike in disposition, and in form her character,

and educate and develop her mind, he have for some year find

his chief interest, while in her affection lie his only solace for the

blighted hope and ruin prospect of a lifetime.



originally highly connected, Captain Arundel have incur the

displeasure of his family by form in the heat of youthful passion,

and under peculiar circumstance, a marriage with the daughter of an

english resident at Marseilles by a foreign mother. too proud to seek to

conciliate his relation, Mr. Arundel become a voluntary exile, enter

into the austrian army, where he speedily rise to the rank of captain

and serve with much distinction, till fail health induce he to

resign his commission and return to England for the sake of educate

his child. his heart be set on one object--namely, to bestow upon

his son the education of an english gentleman, and for this purpose he

have avail himself of a very unusual talent for painting as a mean

by which he might increase his slender income sufficiently to meet the

expense of send Lewis to Westminster and afterwards to a german

university. the constant application thus render inevitable foster

the seed of that most insidious of all ailment, a heart-disease, and

while still form plan for the welfare of his family, an unwonted

agitation induce a paroxysm of his complaint, and ere Rose could

realise the misfortune that threaten she she be fatherless.



although stunned at first by the unexpected shock, her be not a mind

to give way at such a moment, and to those who judge by the outward

expression only Mrs. Arundel’s grief appear much more intense than

that of her daughter. but Rose’s sorrow be not a mere transitory

feeling, which a few week more or less might serve to dissipate; it have

become part of her very nature, a thing too sacred to be lightly bring

to view, but enshrine in the sanctuary of her pure heart it remain

a cherished yet solemn recollection, which would shed its hallowing

influence over the future of her young life. and now, as she sit with

her calm, earnest eye upturned to the tranquil heaven above she, her

thought wander back to he she have so dearly love, and she ponder

the solemn question which have ere now present themselves to many a

mourning spirit, and long to penetrate the secret of the grave

and learn thing which death alone can teach we. then she recall

conversation she have hold with he that be go on these very

subject, and remember how he have say that the thing which God have

not see fit to reveal, could neither be needful nor expedient for we to

know; that such speculation be in themselves dangerous, inasmuch

as they tend to lead we to form theory which, have no warrant in

scripture, might be at variance with truth; and that it be well to

wait patiently in humble faith--that a time would come when we should no

long see through a glass darkly, and the hide thing of God should

be make known unto we. then her thought, still pursue the same

train, lead she to reflect how all her father’s aspiration, crush and

disappoint in the wreck of his own fortune, have centre in his son,

and the bitter tear which no personal privation or misfortune could

have force from she, flow down her cheek as she reflect how these

bright anticipation seem doomed never to be realise.



Unselfish by nature, and train to habit of thoughtfulness by

witness her father’s life of daily self-sacrifice, Rose have never

be accustom to indulge on her own account in those day-dream so

common to the sanguine mind of youth. but the germ of that pride and

ambition which be Lewis’s besetting sin exist in a minor degree

in Rose’s disposition also, and find vent in a visionary career of

greatness she have mark out for her brother, and for which his unusual

mental power and striking appearance seem eminently to qualify he.

in nourish these vision her father have unconsciously assist, when

in moment of confidence he have impart to she his hope that Lewis

would distinguish himself in whatever career of life he might select,

and by his success restore they all to that position in society which

by his own imprudence he have forfeit. what a bitter contrast do the

reality now present! Rose have receive that morning a letter from her

brother detail his interview with General Grant and its result; and

though, from a wish to spare her feeling, he have be more guard in

his expression than on the occasion of his conversation with Frere

the precede day, yet he do not attempt to disguise from her his

repugnance to the arrangement, or the degradation to which his haughty

spirit lead he to consider he be submit.



“Poor Lewis!” murmur Rose, “I know so well what misery it will be to

he; the slight, the hourly petty annoyance which his proud, sensitive

nature will feel so keenly; and then, to waste his high talent, his

energy of character and strength of will on the drudgery of teaching,

when they be certain to have lead he to distinction if he have only

have a fair field for their exercise--it would have break dearest papa’s

heart, when he have hope so differently for he. but if _he_ have live

this never would have be so. he often tell I he have influential

friend, and though he never would apply to they on his own account,

he declare he would do so when Lewis should become old enough to enter

into life. I wonder who they be. he never like to talk on those

subject, and I be afraid of pain he by inquire. I be glad there

be a Miss Grant: I hope she may prove a nice girl and will like Lewis;

but of course she will--every one must do that. oh! how I hope they

will treat he kindly and generously--it will all depend upon that. poor

fellow! with his impulsive disposition and quick sense of wrong--his

fiery temper too, how will he get on? and it be for our sake he do

all this, sacrifice his freedom and his hope of win himself a

name. how good and noble it be of he!”



she pause, and lean her brow upon her little white hand, sit bury

in deep thought. at length she speak again.



“if I could do anything to earn money and help I should be so much

happy. poor papa get a good deal lately for his picture; but they

be so clever. Lewis can paint beautifully, but my drawing be so

tame. I wonder whether people would buy poetry. I wish I know whether my

verse be good enough to induce any one to purchase they. dearest papa

praise those line of mine which he accidentally find one day. of

course he be a good judge, only perhaps he like they because they

be mine.” and the tear roll silently down her pale cheek as memory

bring before she the glance of bright and surprised approval, the warm

yet judicious praise, the tender criticism--word, look, and tone of

love now lose to she for ever, which the accidental discovery of she

verse have draw forth. with an ache heart she close the casement,

and light a candle, proceed to unlock a small writing-desk, from

whence she draw some manuscript verse, which run as follows:--



               the PREACHER’S ADDRESS to the SOUL.



                        Weary soul,



                   why dost thou still disquiet



                   Thyself with senseless riot,



                   take thy fill and measure



                   of earthly pleasure?



                   the thing which thou dost prize



                        be not reality;



                        all be but seem.



                   Waking, thou still liest dreaming.



                   that which before thine eye



                   now passeth, or hath past,



                   be nought but vanity--



                        it cannot last.



                   this evil world, be sure,



                        shall not endure.



                   art thou a-weary, Soul, and dost thou cry



                   for rest? wait, and thou soon shalt have



                        that thou dost crave,



                        for death _be real_--the Grave _no mockery_.



                   the SOUL’S REPLY.



                   preacher, too dark thy mood;



                        God make this earth--



                        at its primeval birth



                   “God see that it be good.”



                   and if through Adam’s sin



                        death enter’d in,



                   hath not Christ die to save



                        I from the grave?



                   repent sin for his sake be forgiven--



                        there be a heaven.



                   for that this earth be no abide-place,



                        shall we displace



                   the flower that God hath scatter’d on our path--



                        the kindly hearth;



                   the smile of love still brighten as we come,



                        make the desert, home;



                   the seventh day of rest, the poor man’s treasure



                        of holy leisure;



                   bright sunshine, happy bird, the joy of flower?



                        ah, no! this earth of ours



                   be “very good,” and hath its blessing still;



                        and if we will,



                   we may be happy. say, stern preacher, why



                   should we then hate to live, or fear to die,



                   with Love for Time, Heaven for Eternity?



Rose peruse they attentively, sigh deeply, and then resumed--



“yes, _he_ like they, and say (I remember his very word) there be

more vigour and purpose about they than in the general run of girlish

verse. how could I find out whether they be worth anything?” she

pause in reflection, then clasp her hand together suddenly, she

exclaimed--



“yes, of course, Mr. Frere; he be so good and kind about the picture,

and Lewis say he be so very clever, he will tell I. but may not he

think it strange and odd in I to write to he? have I well consult

mamma?”



but with the question come an instinctive consciousness that she be

about the last person whom it would be agreeable to consult on such an

occasion. Rose, like every other woman possess the slight approach

to the artist mind, feel a shrink delicacy in regard to what the

Browning school would term her “utterance,” which render the idea of

show they where they would not be appreciate exquisitely painful to

she. now, Mrs. Arundel have a disagreeable knack of occasionally brush

against a feeling so rudely as to cause the unlucky originator thereof

to experience a mental twinge closely akin to the bodily sensation

yclept toothache.



it will therefore be no matter of surprise to the reader to learn that

Rose, after mature deliberation, resolve to keep the fact of she having

apply to Mr. Frere a secret, at all event till such time as the

result should become known to she.



she accordingly select such of her poetical effusion as she deem

most worthy, in the course of which process she stumble upon a short

prose sketch, the only thing of the sort she have ever attempt,

it be, in fact, a lively account of her first appearance at a
