dinner-party, write for the benefit of a young lady friend, but for

some reason never send. this, after look at a page or two, she be

about to condemn as nonsense, when an idea come across she that if Mr.

Frere be to form a just estimate of her power, it be scarcely fair

to select only the good thing; so she pop in the sketch of the

dinner-party as a kind of destitution test, to show how badly she

_could_ write.



then come the most difficult part of the business--the letter to Frere.

true, she have write to he before, act as her father’s amanuensis,

but that be a different sort of thing altogether. still, it must be

do, and Rose be not a person to be deter by difficulty; so she

take a sheet of paper and write “Sir” at the top of it, and having

do so, sit and look at it till she become intensely dissatisfied.

“Sir”--it seem so cold and uncomfortable; so she take a second sheet

and write, “Dear Sir.” yes! that be well, decidedly. she only hope

it be not too familiar in writing to a young man; but then, Mr. Frere

be not exactly a young man; he be a great deal old than Lewis; above

thirty most likely; and three or four-and-thirty be quite middle-aged;

so the “Dear Sir” be allow to remain.



“_Ce n’est que le premier pas qui coute?_ and having once start, it

be not long before Rose’s nimble pen have cover two side of the sheet

of paper, and the following letter be the result:--



“Dear Sir,--I know not how to offer any excuse for the trouble I be

about to give you, otherwise than by explain the reason which have

induce I to apply to you; and, as I know your time be valuable, I will

do so as briefly as I can. do not think I forgetful of, or ungrateful

for, your great kindness to Lewis, when I tell you that ever since I

receive my brother’s letter inform I of his engagement as tutor to

General Grant’s ward, I have feel miserable at the idea of his working

hard at an occupation which I fear must be distasteful to he, in order

to provide for Mamma and myself the comfort we have hitherto enjoy.

it be impossible to prevent this in any way, for we try to shake his

determination, but in vain. now I feel that I should be so much happy

if I could assist, in ever so small a degree, in relieve he from his

burthen; and the only possible idea that occur to I (for he will

not hear of my go out as governess) be that I might be able to earn

something by my pen. with this view I have venture to enclose for

your perusal a few verse which I have write at odd time for my own

amusement; and I trust to your kindness to tell I honestly whether

they possess any merit or not. I dare not hope your opinion will be

favourable; but if by possibility it should prove so, will you do I the

additional kindness of advise I what step to take in order to get

they publish. I have never be in London, but I have hear there be

a good many bookseller who live there; and as I dare say you know

they all, perhaps you would kindly tell I to which of they you would

recommend I to apply. I have not tell Mamma that I be write, for, as

I feel a presentiment that your answer will only prove to I the

folly of the hope I be so silly as to indulge, it be not worth while

disturb she about the matter. once again thank you for your

extreme kindness to Lewis, and hope that you will not consider I too

troublesome in thus apply to you, believe I to remain your sincerely

oblige,



“Rose Arundel.



“P.S.--I have enclose a little prose sketch with the verse, but I be

_quite sure_ you will not like that. perhaps, if Lewis have not leave you

when this arrive, you will be so very kind as not to say any thing to

he about it, as he would be sure to laugh at I.”



when Rose have finish this epistle she feel that she have do something

towards attain the object she have at heart, and go to bed feel

more happy than she have do since the receipt of Lewis’s letter.

Straightway fall asleep, she dream that she be introduce to Mr.

Murray, who offer she £100 to write a short biographical memoir of

General Grant for the “Quarterly Review.”









chapter XIV.--PRESENTS TOM BRACY in a new and interesting ASPECT.



three day pass by, and still poor Rose receive no answer to her

letter, but remain a prey to alternate hope and fear and all “the

gnawing torture of an anxious mind.” on the fourth arrive the following

characteristic note:--



“my dear Miss Arundel,--I dare say you’ve be abuse I like a

pick-pocket; at least I must have appear to you deserve of such

abuse, for treat your request so cavalierly; but the fact be, I have

be down in a cornish tin mine for the last two day, and only receive

your packet on my arrival in town, an hour ago. and now to business. I

don’t set up for a judge of poetry, though I know what please I

and what don’t (I should be a donkey if I do not, you’ll say); for

instance, the present school of ‘suggestive’ poetry don’t suit I

at all. but then I have an old-fashioned prejudice in favour of

understand what I read, and call a railway locomotive a ‘_resonant

steam eagle_,’ for instance, do not tend to simplify literature; the

only thing such phrase ‘suggest’ to I be that it would be a great deal

well if the author be content to stick to plain English, and when

they have such inexpressibly grand idea, not to trouble themselves

to express they at all. your verse have at least one good point in

they--they be so word that a plain man may understand they; in fact,

all that I have yet read I like--the feeling be invariably pure, true,

and beautiful (your heart’s in the right place, and no mistake); the

language be well choose, and sometimes eloquent; there be, of course,

plenty of place where it become weak and young lady-like, but that

be only to be expect. we cann’t all be man, unfortunately. I could

not help laugh when you ‘suppose I know’ all the bookseller and

publisher in London, Heaven forbid! for in that case I should have a

very miscellaneous acquaintance. however, I do know several, and I

will go the first thing to-morrow morning and consult one of they--a

gentleman on whose judgment I can rely as to what will be the most

advisable course for we to pursue. I say _us_, because, as I don’t

mean to let the matter rest till I have succeed, I consider myself

a partner in the concern. Lewis part from I in high health and very

tolerable spirit. he leave town, with General Grant, the same morning

on which I start for Cornwall. you shall hear from I again when I can

report progress. don’t write any more nonsense about give I trouble:

in the first place, the thing be no trouble; in the second, I should not

mind it one bit if it be.



“I be yours very truly,



“Richard Frere.”



the first thing next morning Frere call upon his friend the publisher,

who, as soon as he understand that nothing beyond advice be require of

he, become very communicative and agreeable; glance his eye over the

verse and approve of they, though he add, with a Burleigh-like

shake of the head, that he wish they be anything but poetry. frere

wonder why, and ask he. in reply he learn that the public mind

have acquire a sadly practical bias, which lead he to suggest that

poetry be the very thing of all other to bring it right again, he be

far inform that the evil be much too deeply seat to be affect

by so weak an application as the poetry of the present day; and the

truth of this assertion appear undeniable, the subject be drop.



“the good thing for you to do with these MSS., Mr. Frere,” continue his

adviser, “would be to get they insert in some popular periodical.”



“well, I don’t object,” return Frere. “which have I well send

they to? there’s ‘gently’s miscellany,’ and the ‘New Weekly,’ and

‘Gainsworth’s magazine,’ and half-a-dozen more of’em.”



“what do you suppose would be the result of adopt such a line of

conduct?” inquire his friend.



“why, as the thing be in themselves good, they’d probably put ’em in

next month, and send a cheque for the amount, enclose in a polite note

ask for more.”



“I fear not,” be the answer. “a very promising young friend of mine

send a nicely write paper to the least exclusive of the periodical

you have just mention; hear nothing of it, he venture at the end

of six month to write and inquire its fate. in reply he receive a note

from the editor, which appear to he more explicit than satisfactory.

it be couch in the follow laconic terms:--‘Declined with thank.’”



“Phewl that’s pleasant,” rejoin Frere. “what would you advise, then,

under the circumstance? I place myself quite in your hand.”



his friend lean back in his chair and consider the matter deeply. at

length he seem to have hit upon some expedient, for he mutter

with great emphasis, “yes, that might do. he could if he would.

yes--certainly!” then turn suddenly to Frere, he exclaim, “mind,

you’ll never breathe a word of it to any living being!”



“not for the world,” return Frere. “and now, what be it?”



“you’ve hear of ‘Blunt’s magazine’?”



“yes; I’ve see it in several place lately.”



“no doubt; it’s a most admirably conduct publication, and one which be

certain to become a great favourite with the public. now I happen to be

acquaint with one of the gentleman who edit it, and shall be happy to

give you a note of introduction to he. but you must promise I to be

most careful never to reveal his name.”



“certainly,” rejoin Frere, “if you wish it. but may I venture to ask

what it would signify if all London know it?”



his companion turn upon he a look of indignant surprise; but

perceive that he make the inquiry in honest simplicity of heart, his

face assume an expression of contemptuous pity as he reply, in such a

tone of voice as one would use to a little child who have inquire why it

might not set light to a barrel of gunpowder, “my dear sir, you do

not know--you cannot conceive the consequence. such a thing would be

utterly impossible.”



he then write a few line, which he hand to Frere, say, “you will

find he at home till eleven.”



“and this mysterious name,” observe Frere, glance at the address,

“is!--eh! nonsense!--Thomas Bracy, Esq. why, he be an intimate friend

of my own! that’s famous. oh! I’ll have some fun with he. I’m sure I’m

extremely obliged to you; good morning.” so say Frere seize his hat,

shoulder his umbrella, and hurry off, overjoy at his discovery.



the mendacious tiger, of whom we have already make honourable mention,

answer Frere’s inquiry as to whether his master be at home with a

most decide and unequivocal negative, add the gratuitous information

that, “he have go down to dine with his uncle at Hampstead the previous

day and be not expect home till four o’clock that afternoon.”



“well, that’s a nuisance,” return Frere. “I tell you what, boy, I’ll

step in and write your master a note.”



“yes, sir, certainly, if you please, sir; only we’ve be a have the

sweep hin, and the place be hall in a huproar, so as it’s unpossibul to

touch nothink.”



at this moment a bell ring violently, and the boy, beg Frere to

wait, bound up the stair with a cat-like rapidity, return almost

immediately with the information that “he be wery sorry, but he’d just

be to the greengrocer’s, and while he be hout master have come home

quite promiscuous.”



“and how about the soot?” ask Frere, a light break in upon he.



“please, sir, cook’s be and clean it hup while I be go.”



“I think so,” return frere; “you’re a nice boy!” then catch he

by the collar of his jacket, he continue, “tell I, you young scamp,

how often do you speak the truth?”



the urchin, thus detect, glance at Frere’s face, and read there

that any attempt to keep up appearance must prove a dead failure,

reply with the utmost _sing froid,_ “please, sir, whenever I cann’t

think of nothink well.”



“there’s an answer,” return frere meditatively. “well, you need never

learn swimming--water willn’t harm you; but mark my word, and beware of

hemp.” so say he loosen his hold on the boy’s collar and follow

he upstairs.



the tiger, not having recognise Frere in his european habiliment, have

merely tell his master that a gentleman wish to see he on business;

and Bracy, who have reason to expect a visit from a certain literary Don,

have rush into his dressing-room to exchange a very decidedly “fast”

 smoking-jacket for the black frock-coat of editorial propriety; for

which reason Frere be leave to entertain himself for a few minute with

his own society. after examine sundry clever caricature sketch of

Bracy’s, which evince a decide talent for that branch of art, Frere

seat himself in an easy-chair in front of a writing-table on which

lie a mysterious document, write in a bold, dash hand, which

involuntarily attract his attention. perceive at a glance that it

contain no private matter, he amuse himself by peruse it. for the

reader’s edification we will transcribe it:--



_Blunt’s Magazine, June. sheet 3 and 4



question on Quicksilver...4



the Homeless Heart (Stanzas by L. O. V. E.) . . .1



Hist. Parallels, No. 3 (Cromwell and Cour-de-Lion) . . 7



L’Incomprise (by the Authoress of Inconnue). . .6



Hard Work and Hard Food; or, how would you like it yourself?



a Plea for the Industrial Classes .. . 5



Dog-cart drive (by the Editor), Chap, 10 the Spicey



Screw;” Chap. II, do the governor” . .. 7



want something light, abt...2_



the last item in this singular catalogue be write in pencil. “now I

should like to know what all that mean,” soliloquise Frere.

“something light about two? a luncheon would come under that definition

exactly--two _whats?_ that’s the question! two pound? it would not be

particularly light if it weigh as much as that. perhaps the figure

stand for money--the price they pay for the magazine article, I dare

say; 4--6--7. now, if they happen to be sovereign, that will suit my

young lady’s case very nicely. ah! here he come.”









CHAPTER XV.--CONTAINS a disquisition on modern poetry, and AFFORDS the

reader a peep behind the EDITORIAL CURTAIN.



the position in which Frere have place himself prevent Bracy from

discern his feature as he enter, and he accordingly accost his

visitor as follows:--



“my dear sir, I be really distressed to have keep you wait, but as

you arrive I be just jot down the result of a little flirtation

with the Muse.”



“and that be it, I suppose?” observe Frere, turn his face towards

the speaker and point to the document before allude to.



“why, Frere, be it you, man?” exclaim Bracy in surprise. “as I’m a

sinner, I take you for that learn elder, Dr.-------. my young imp tell

I you be a gentleman who wish to see I on particular business. if

that juvenile devil take to tell lie _to_ instead of _for_ I,

I shall have to give he his due for once, in the shape of a sound

cane.”



“you may spare yourself the trouble,” return frere, “as by some

accident he have only speak the truth this time; for I hope you don’t

mean to insinuate that I be anything but a gentleman, and I have most

assuredly come to you on business--that be, always suppose Mr. --------

of -------- Street have inform I correctly in regard to your

editorial function.”



“what! have the _cacoethes scribendi_ seize you also, and tempt you

into the commission of some little act of light literature?” add

Bracy.



“thank goodness, no,” answer Frere. “I’m happy to say I’m not so far

go as all that come to yet. no, this be a different case altogether,”

 and he then proceed to inform his companion of Rose’s, application,

and the necessity which exist to make her talent available for

practical purpose.



“magazine write afford rather a shady prospect for realise capital

in these day,” observe Bracy, shake his head discouragingly. “let’s

look at the young lady’s interesting effort. have you ever see she?

Arundel’s sister ought to be pretty. what’s this? ‘the Preacher’s

address to the Soul.’ why, it’s a sermon in rhyme. Heaven help the girl!

what’s she think of?”



“read it and you’ll see. I like it very much,” return frere, slightly

nettled at the reception his _protégées_ production appear likely to

meet with.



“oh! it’s a sermon clearly,” continue Bracy; “here’s something about

vanity and the grave. I hear it all last Sunday at St. Chrysostom’s,

only the fellow call it gwave and gwace. he’d pick up some

conscientious scruple against the use of the letter r, I suppose.

it’s quite wonderful, the new-fangle doctrine they develop nowadays.

Hum--ha--‘Making the desert home,’--rather a young idea, eh? ‘happy

birds,’--don’t like that, it put one too much in mind of ‘jolly dog’

or ‘odd fish.’ I should have say dicky bird, if it have be I; that’s

a very safe expression, and one that people be accustom to. ‘the joy

of flowers,’--what on earth do she mean by that, now? I should say

nobody could understand that; for which reason, by the way, it’s the

good expression I’ve see yet. Poetry, to be admire in the present day,

must be utterly incomprehensible. we insert very little, but that’s the

rule I go by. if I cann’t understand one word of a thing, I make a point

of accept it; it’s safe to become popular. ‘love for time, Heaven for

eternity,’--well, that’s all very, nice and pretty, but I’m sorry to say

it willn’t do; it’s not suit to the tone of the Magazine, you see.”



“I cann’t say I _do_ see very clearly at present,” return frere; “what

kind of poetry be it that you accept?”



“oh, there be different style. now here’s a little thing I’ve get in

the June part, ‘the Homeless Heart, by L. O. V. E.’ her real name be

Mary Dobbs, but she couldn’t very well sign herself M.D.; people would

think she be a physician. she’s a very respectable young woman (such a

girl to laugh), and engage to an opulent stockbroker. now listen:



                        “‘homeless, forsaken,



                        deeply oppress’d,



                        Raving, yet crave



                        Agony’s rest;



                        Bitterly hating,



                        fondly relent,



                        sinning, yet win



                        soul to repent;



                        when for her sorrow



                        come a to-morrow,



                        shall she be bless’d?’”



“that’s a question I cann’t take upon myself to answer,” interrupt

frere. “but if those be in the style you consider suit to the tone of

your magazine, it must be a very wonderful publication.”



“I flatter myself it be, rather,” reply Bracy complacently. “but

that’s by no mean the only style; here’s a thing that will go down with

the million sweetly. listen to this,” and as he speak he extract

from a drawer a mighty bundle of paper label “Accepted Poetry,” and

select one or two specimen from the mass, read as follows:--



                   “the COUNTESS EMMELINE’S DISDAINMENT.”



          “bitter-black the winter’s whirlwind wail’d around the haunted hall,



          where the sheeted snow that fleet fester’d on the mouldering wall.



          “but his blacker soul within he childish calm appear’d to view,



          and when gaze, ’twa amazing whence the sceptic terror grow.



          “then her voice, so silver-blend, to a trumpet-blast do swell,



          as she task’d he when she ask he, ‘Mr. Johnson, be it well?’



          “Ashen-white the curdle traitor pale before her eagle eye,



          whilst deny, in replying, deeply grow his perjury.”



“there! I cann’t stand any more of that, at any price!” exclaim Frere,

put his hand to his ear. “unless you wish to make I seriously

ill, spare I the infliction of those detestable compound adjective.”



“my dear fellow, you’ve no taste,” return Bracy. “why, that’s write

by one of our good contributor; an individual that will make Tennyson

look to his laurel, and do the Brownings brown, one of these day. but

if that’s too grand for you, here’s a little bit of pastoral simplicity

may suit you better:--



               “‘to a herblet, name UNKNOWN.



                   ‘once upon a holiday,



                        sing heigho;



                   still with sportive fancy playing



                   while all nature be a-maye,



                        on a sunny bank I lay;



                   where the happy grass do grow,



                   ‘Neath the fragrant lime-tree row,



                        sing heigho!



                    ‘there a little fairy flower,



                        sing heigho!



                   glance from its baby eye



                   with a look of sweet surprise,



                        grow beneath a bower,



                   bring unto my soul the dawning



                   of a mystic spirit warning,



                        sing heigho!



                   ‘then I weep, and say, despairing,



                        sing heigho!



                   Fate be dark, and earth be lonely,



                   and the heart’s young blossom only



                        render life worth bearing----”



“now then, what’s the matter with you?” inquire Bracy, interrupt

himself on see Frere snatch up his hat and umbrella.



“if you’re go to read any more of that, I’m off; that’s all,”

 return frere. “my power of endurance be limited.”



“oh, if you be positively such a Hottentot as to dislike it,” rejoin

Bracy, “I’ll not waste any more of its sweet simplicity upon you; but,

you’ll see, the gentle public will rave about it to an immense extent.”



“now tell I honestly, Bracy--you don’t really admire that childish

rubbish?”



thus appeal to, Bracy’s face assume an expression of most comical

significance; and after pause for a moment in indecision, he replied--



“well, I’ve a sort of respect for your good opinion, Frere, and I don’t

exactly like to send you away fancy I a great ass than I be; so

I’ll honestly confess that, what between affected germanism on the

one hand and the puerility of the Wordsworth-and-water school on the

other, the poetry of the present day have sink to a very low ebb indeed.”



“then don’t you consider it the duty of every honest critic to point

this out, and so guide and reform the public taste as to evoke from the

‘well of English undefile’ a truer and purer style?” return Frere

earnestly.



“my dear fellow, that all sound very well in theory, but in practice,

I’m afraid (to use a metaphor derive from one of the humane and

intellectual amusement of our venerate forefather), that cock willn’t

fight. it may be all very well for some literary Don Quixote, with

a pure Saxon taste and a long purse, to tilt at the public’s pet

windmill, because he conceive they to be giant abuse. if he meet

with a fall, he need only put his hand in his pocket and purchase a

plaster, get a triple shield of experience in for the money. but it

be far otherwise with a magazine. if that be to continue in existence it

must pay; in order to pay it must be render popular; to make a thing

popular you must go with the stream of public opinion, and not against

it. the only chance be to head the tide and turn it in the direction you

desire. but to attempt that a man ought to possess first-rate talent,

and I’m free to confess that I, for one, do not; and therefore, you see,

as people must be amuse, I’m very willing to amuse they in their own

way, as long as I find it pleasant and profitable to do so. _Voila!_ do

you comprehend?”



“I comprehend this much,” return frere gruffly, “that the ground of

your argument be expediency and not principle; and I tell you plainly

that do not suit I, and I’m afraid Miss Arundel be too much of

my mind in that particular for her writing to suit your wonderful

magazine; so the soon I take my departure the well for your

morning’s work.”



“stay a moment, don’t get on stilt, man,” return Bracy, resume his

examination of Rose’s paper. “be there nothing but verse? what have we

here? ‘my First Dinner-Party’--this seem more likely.”



he pause, and run his eye over several of the page, mutter from

time to time as he go along, “yes, good lively style--quick power of

observation--a very graphic touch--bravo! ha! ha! here, listen to this--



“‘immediately before I stand a dish which even my inexperience believe

itself able to recognise; it be jelly of some kind, with certain dark

object encase in it, as fly occasionally be in amber. these

opaque portion I settle, in my own mind, must be preserve fruit,

and accordingly (fearful lest, in my ignorance of fashionable dish, I

should say “yes” to some tremendous delicacy which might prove utterly

impracticable), when invite to partake of it, I graciously signify

my assent. imagine my horror when, on put the first mouthful to my

lip, I discover the jelly be savoury--_i.e_. all pepper and salt,

and the creature embed in it a fragment of some dreadful fish! eat

the thing be out of the question; the mere taste I have take of it make

I feel uncomfortable: an attempt to conceal it beneath the knife and

fork prove utterly futile. I glance at the butler, but he be too much

absorb in his own dignity and the dispensation of champagne to observe

I; I gaze appealingly at a good-look young footman, but he

merely pull up his shirt-collar foppishly, think he have make an

impression; I even venture to call, in a low voice, to the sprightly

waiter who have elope with my untouched plate of lamb five minute

before, but he do not hear I; and there I sit with a huge plateful of

horrible food before I, which I could neither eat nor get rid of, “a

cynosure for neighbour eye,” force, as my fear suggest, to run

the gauntlet of all the mocking glance of the assemble company.’”



“there,” continue Bracy, “I call that a stunning description; I could

not have do it well myself. the girl write so easily! let I see,

18--25--28 line in a page of manuscript; there’s not much of it, I

think I can get it in. I want two page of amusing matter in the fourth

sheet.”



“ahl something light, about two. now I understand,” exclaim frere,

point to the mysterious document on the table; “that be not a

memorandum in regard to luncheon, then.”



“a what?” return Bracy, shout with laughter. “no,” he continue,

as soon as he have in some measure recover his composure, “that be

the ‘make-up,’ as we call it, of the third and fourth sheet of the

Magazine.”



“indeed!” return Frere. “I should think it must require a great deal

of careful reflection to select suitable article and arrange they

properly.”



“eh! no, not a bit; the thing’s simple enough when you once get in the

way of it. have plenty of variety, that’s the grand point--what one

don’t like, another will. take large shot for big bird, and small

shot for little one, and then you’ll bag the whole covey; that’s

my maxim. now, look here: first we begin with a scientific article,

‘question on Quicksilver.’ there’s not one reader in a hundred that can

understand that paper when they’ve read it; and very few even of those

who can take it in care two straw about quicksilver--why should

they? but they all read it, because it’s a cheap way of get up the

necessary amount of scientific jargon to hash into small talk. I never

look at that man’s paper myself; I know they’re safe, though I cann’t

understand a word of ’em--but they’re a great help to the Magazine.

then come our friend, the ‘Homeless heart.’ I put that in as a drop of

romantic barley-sugar to soften the woman’s throat after swallow the

science. next we have ‘an Historical Parallel.’ famous fellow they be;

the principal dodge in write they be to take an ‘entirely new reading

of the character,’ as the actor say. in the present article, if I

recollect right, they prove cour-de-Lion to have be a hypocritical

fanatic, and Cromwell a chivalric, magnanimous enthusiast. it’s safe to

take, depend upon it. ‘L’Incomprise’ tell its own tale--it’s as close

an imitation of Eugene Sue and George Sand as english morality will

tolerate, though the invention of guttapercha, or some other elastic

agent, enable even that stiff material nowadays to stretch to length

which would astonish our grandmother. then come the ‘Plea for the

Industrial Classes’--a regular savage poke at the present Poor Law

(we’re oblige to do a little bit of political economy as well as our

neighbour, you know); it’s awfully heavy, but it will neutralise any

ill effect ‘L’Incomprise’ may have have on father of family all the

well. lastly, there’s my own little thing, ‘dog-cart drive.’ Ahem!

have you see that?”



“not i,” reply Frere; “I’ve no time for read tra---- I mean novel

and that sort of thing.”



“I believe it’s like; I hear it’s a good deal talk about,” continue

Bracy with an air of bashful self-complacency. “‘Bell’s life’ speak very

handsomely of it last week; there be six whole line devote to it, I

think. upon my word I should like you to read it.”



at this moment Frere suddenly discover that he have remain over his

time, and should be too late for some deeply interesting experiment

that be to come off that morning at what his companion term his

science shop; so receive an assurance from Bracy that Rose’s sketch

should be insert in the Magazine, and that he would consider what

would be her good mode of proceed in regard to the poetry, the

friend shake hand and part, frere promise to make himself

acquaint with the subject-matter of “Dog-cart drive” at an early

opportunity.









CHAPTER XVI.--MISS LIVINGSTONE SPEAKS a bit of her MIND.



it be a lovely morning in early summer, when the sun, shine into

Lewis’s bedroom at Broadhurst, arouse he from a heavy dreamless

   sleep, the result of his previous night’s dissipation at Lady

Lombard’s. the sensation of wake for the first time in a strange place

be usually a disagreeable one; there be an unfamiliar newness in the

aspect of everything around we, an absence of old association, which

to an impressible disposition be singularly disheartening. this be

peculiarly the case with Lewis; the costly furniture of the room,

arrange with a stiff propriety, the spotless carpet, the chair-cover

too clean and slippery to be sit upon, the bright cold mirror, the

polished grate, in which a fire would have be high treason, each and

all suggestive of the chilling influence of that rigid disciplinarian

Miss Livingstone, serve painfully to realise his new position.

Splendour without comfort be an anomaly he have never before

encounter, and in his then frame of mind it arouse all the bitter

feeling which even his strength of will be unable to subdue, and

he mentally compare himself to a slave work in gild chain, and

long for independence, no matter through what hardship, struggle,

and danger it must be attain. but there be a healthy energy about

his mind which prevent his yielding to these morbid feeling; hastily

dress himself, he find his way into the pleasure garden, and as it

be yet early, stroll onward through the park.



after wander about for nearly an hour, the calm beauty of the scenery

and the exhilarate freshness of the morning air produce their

natural effect upon his spirit, it occur to he that his absence

might be comment upon, and possibly give offence; accordingly, he

retrace his step towards the house. Ignorant of the _locale_, however,

he be unable to discover the door by which he have go out, and after

make one or two attempt in a wrong direction, be compel to effect

his entrance through a french window opening into a conservatory. Lewis

possess a great taste for, and some knowledge of botany, and his

attention be at once attract by the rare and beautiful plant around

he. so completely be he engross by his admiration, that not until

he hear his own name pronounce do he become aware that he be not

the sole tenant of the conservatory. turn at the sound, he perceive

Annie Grant, in a very become costume, busily employ in alter the

arrangement of certain flower-pot.



before we proceed far, it may be as well to afford the reader an

insight into Lewis’s feeling towards this young lady, as they be by

no mean of such a nature as might be expect from a young man towards

a pretty and agreeable girl, with whom he be about to be domesticate.

in order to account for his peculiar state of mind on this subject we

must take a retrospective glance at an episode in Lewis’s student life,

which have be already allude to in a conversation between Frere and

his friend. about a year before the period at which our story open

Lewis have encounter, at a festive meeting of the worthy citizen of

Bonn, the very pretty daughter of a wealthy shopkeeper, and strike by

her bright eye and a certain naïve simplicity of manner, have dance

with she the great part of the evening. flatter by the attention

of the handsome young Englishman, the damsel, who (her simplicity being

confine entirely to manner) be as arrant a little flirt as ever cause

a heartache, take care that the acquaintance should continue; and while

she be merely bent on add to her train of admirer, Lewis fall

in love with she as deeply as a man can do with a girl completely his

inferior in mind as well as in station. Imagination, however, which

at eighteen be alarmingly active, supply all deficiency, and Lewis

continue to dream his lady-love be an angel, till one fine morning

the fact of her elopement with a young german baron, who look upon

matrimony as a superfluous ordinance, induce he to alter his opinion.

with the termination of the adventure the reader be already acquaint,

but the effect upon Lewis’s disposition be one which time might weaken

but could never efface. the fatal lesson that one who seem true and

pure be not so, once learn could never be forget; the seed of

mistrust be sown, and strive as he might, the perfect faith, the

bright, eager confidence of youth, be lose to he for ever.



Ànnie, as the reader be aware, be unusually lovely, and Lewis

accordingly regard she in the light of a dangerous man-trap;

besides this, oddly enough, she be by no mean unlike an ethereal and

spiritualise representation of “Gretchen”; the feature and colour

be similar, and the arch simplicity of the _Fraulein’s_ manner be

part and parcel of Annie’s very nature. the painful recollection which

this resemblance excite add unconsciously to the prejudice (for

it amount to that) which Lewis have conceive against the General’s

daughter; but the true source of the feeling lie deeply. however

circumstance may cause he to affect, or even to believe the contrary,

there be in every man’s heart a latent desire to render himself

agreeable to any young and pretty woman into whose society he may be

throw, more especially where the individual be conscious of possess

power of pleasing if he choose to exert they; and even Lewis’s slight

experience of society have suffice to enlighten he in regard to this

point, on which the dull be usually clear-sighted. but couple with

this feeling come the humiliating consciousness that although by birth

and education Miss Grant’s equal, the position he hold in the family

render he her inferior; and this idea be gall in the extreme to

Lewis’s haughty nature. Annie, on the other hand, profoundly ignorant

of all these wheel within wheel, entertain the most amiable and

benevolent intention towards her new associate. she know he be

unfortunate, she see he be a gentleman, and she have hear that he be

undertake a duty he dislike, for the sake of his mother and sister;

and for all these reason her woman’s heart warm towards he, and

she determine to do what she be able to render his position as little

painful as might be; moreover, she be sufficiently acquaint with

the idiosyncrasy of her father and great-aunt to be aware that any

particular kindness the young tutor would be likely to meet with in

the family must emanate from herself. accordingly, when Lewis, having

reply to her cordial “good morning, Mr. Arundel,” by slightly raise

his hat, and make a formal bow, be about to pass on, she renew the

attack by adding--



“may I trouble you to move this flower-pot for I? it be so heavy.”



thus appeal to, Lewis stop short, and for a moment debate

with himself the possibility of refuse; but without be actually

ill-breed, such a possibility do not exist; so, resign himself to

his fate with a very ill grace, he deposit his hat on a vacant

flower-stand, and toss back his dark curl with the air of a sulky

lion shake his mane, he take the garden-pot, which indeed seem too

heavy for Annie’s little hand, ask, with a stately coldness by no

mean in character with the mild nature of the inquiry--



“where would you wish to have it place, Miss Grant?”



“here, if you will be so kind,” return the young lady, indicate the

spot by point with the end of a pert little parasol.



Lewis, having instal the plant in its appoint place, be again

about to take his departure, but ere he do so, glance involuntarily

at the effect of his labour, his quick eye at once discern the object

of the change Annie be strive to effect, and perceive that, in’

order to carry out her design, several heavy flower yet require

move. nothing, however, be far from his thought than the idea of

volunteer his assistance, when Annie, catch the direction of his

eye, continued--



“yes, the White Camellia be too low.”



“while the rhododendron be as much too high,” return Lewis eagerly,

and forget his proud scruple in the impulse of the moment, he set

to work with the great energy to complete the arrangement which his

correct taste acknowledge to be an improvement.



the Camellia have be exalt and the Rhododendron abase, and many

other “pet of the parterre” have experience sudden change of position,

and still Lewis work with unabated zeal, and still his fair companion

direct and approve, when just as, poise like a fly Mercury on one

foot half-way up a high flower-stand, he be stretch to his utmost

to install a gaudy Cactus, all red and green like a paroquet, on the

topmost pinnacle, a stately tread be hear approach, and General

Grant enter the conservatory. Lewis colour with mingled anger

and annoyance at be detect in such a situation, but Annie

good-naturedly come to his assistance. trip up to her father and

take both his hand, she exclaimed--



“good morning, papa. welcome to dear old Broadhurst once again. how

pretty it all look! but they have place my flower so stupidly, I must

have every one of they alter. I’ve be work away for half-an-hour

at least, and as Mr. Arundel happen to be pass, I press he into

the service, for some of the pot be so heavy.”



“much too heavy for you to attempt to move, my dear,” return the

General in a tone of marked disapproval; “but why do you not summon one

of the gardener to make the alteration you wish, without trouble

Mr. Arundel, who must have have other duty to perform?”



“as it be your desire, sir, to be present at my introduction to my

future pupil,” reply Lewis, who have by this time reach _terra firma_

and recover his self-possession, “I have refrain from make any

attempt to see he till I should have learn your further wish on

the subject. my time be therefore quite at Miss Grant’s disposal, if I

could be in any way useful to she.”



“my daughter be oblige by your politeness, sir, but will not trespass

upon it far,” reply the General coldly. “my dear Annie,” he

continue, “it only want ten minute to nine; you will oblige I by

prepare for breakfast. punctuality be a quality by the neglect of

which all order be subvert, propriety set at nought, much valuable

time waste which can never be recall, and the comfort of a family

totally destroy. your excellent aunt be aware of my opinion on this

subject, and during the twelve year she have do I the favour to

preside over my household she have never keep I wait one minute.”



“‘well, dear papa, I’ll do my good to please you,” return Annie;

“but,” she add, lay her hand on-his shoulder caressingly,

and look up in his face with a glance half mischievous and half

imploring, “you willn’t expect I to be so terribly perfect as Aunt

Martha? Recollect, she be three time as old as I be, and ought

therefore to be three time as wise.”



the General try to look displeased, but he could not resist Annie for

he be human after all; so, stroke her glossy curl, he tell she

that Mrs. Botherfille (a serious schoolmistress, who, for the trifling

consideration of £300 per annum, condescend to allow the youthful

female aristocracy of the land to sit at her foot and learn from

her lip how to regenerate society through the medium of frivolous

accomplishment) have fail in cure she of talk nonsense, at which

Annie laugh merrily and then trip off, turn as she pass Lewis

to take a last glance at the newly-arrange flower, and say, “now,

don’t they look pretty, Mr. Arundel?”



as the direction in regard to Lewis and his pupil’s separate

establishment (for such the isolated suite of room they be to occupy

might be consider) have not as yet be communicate to the servant,

General Grant request the favour of Lewis’s company at breakfast with

as much ceremony as he could have use if he have be invite a royal

duke to a banquet; and as a request from such a quarter be equivalent

to a command, Lewis could only comply. half a minute before the clock

strike nine, Miss Livingstone, that human hedge-hog, rustle into the

breakfast-room, more stiff and starch in mind and body than any other

living creature. as for her cap, a railway train might have pass over

it without injure that rigid mystery, while her gown be at the least

sabre, not to say bullet-proof. if ever there be a wife fit for

our Iron Duke, that adamantine spinster be the woman--only that to have

marry she would have require more courage than twenty waterloo!



as the clock strike nine the household servant make their appearance,

and all the family kneel down (with the exception of Miss Livingstone,

who, be evidently fashion as the ancient believe elephant to be,

without knee-joint, merely rear up against the breakfast-table, as

the next good thing she could do), while the General read they a short,

sharp, but polite prayer, after which he bless they very much as if he

be do the reverse, and suffer they to depart. the breakfast be

excellent as far as the commissariat department be concern, and the

tea be not so cold as might have be expect consider that Miss

Livingstone pour it out.



even Lewis’s short acquaintance with that austere virgin’s usual

expression of countenance lead he to believe that a dark shade

than ordinary lower upon her brow; nor be he mistaken, for after

despatch a piece of dry toast with the air of an acidulated martyr,

the spirit (we fear it be not an amiable one) move she, and she speak.



“I must say, General, your benevolence have rather overpower your

judgment, to my poor thinking, in this singular addition to the

establishment at Broadhurst. I really consider that I ought to have be

a little more clearly inform as to the fact of the case before these

new arrangement be actually decide on.”



“if you refer to Sir Walter Desborough, madam,” return the general

sternly, “I must recall to your memory the fact of my having mention

to you, this day week, my intention that my ward should reside at

Broadhurst.”



“I be not in the habit of forget any communication you do I

the honour of make to I, General Grant, nor have I forget the

conversation to which you refer; but if you mention that your ward be

a dangerous idiot, and that you expect I to preside over a private

lunatic asylum, that circumstance certainly have escape I.” the

wrinkle on the General’s forehead deepen as he reply with a

glance towards Lewis, “you forget, Miss Livingstone, that we be not in

private.”



“really,” rejoin the lady, “if, as I believe, that _young_” (and she

lay an ill-natured emphasis on the word) “gentleman have undertake the

duty of keeper----”



“tutor,” interpose the General sharply.



“well, tutor, then, if you like to call it so,” continue Miss

Livingstone, “the name do not much signify. but if Mr. Arundel be to

have the care of this dreadful boy, the soon he know what his duty

will be, and set about they, the well; for I tell you plainly,

General Grant, that unless there’s a man about the creature who can

manage he, I willn’t sleep another night in the house with he. there’s

no trust those idiot; we may all be murder in our bed.”



as the good lady, who have by this time get the steam up to a very high

degree of pressure, hazard the above uncomfortable suggestion, Annie,

who have be listen with an expression of painful annoyance to

her aunt’s harangue, suddenly turn pale and glance with a look of

appeal inquiry towards her father, who reply to she rather than to

Miss Livingstone in the follow terms:--



“really, my dear Annie, I be compel to say that the fear with which

your excellent relative” (and he look bayonet at Minerva, who shake

her head till her terrific cap rustle like an angry hailstorm) “would

seek to inspire you be utterly without foundation.” he pause, take a

pinch of snuff viciously, as though it be gunpowder and he be prime

himself for a fresh discharge; and thus prepare he turn to Lewis,

say--but we will reserve the volley for another chapter.









chapter XVII.--CONTAINS much FOLLY and a little common sense.



“the truth of the matter be this, Mr. Arundel,” begin General Grant.

“from a mistaken policy your future pupil have be indulge in every

caprice of his weak intellect, till the slight opposition to his

wish irritate he beyond all control that have yet be exercise over

he; but as his only attendant be an old female domestic who be his

nurse, and her son, a lad young than Sir Walter himself, whom he have

be foolishly permit to look upon in the light of a companion, this

be not so much to be wonder at.”



“it will be a difficult task to eradicate fault of temper which

have be allow to become habitual, especially where the reasoning

faculty be defective,” observe Lewis thoughtfully.



“you may well say that, sir,” chime in Miss Livingstone. “his reasoning

faculty (as you please to term they) be so _defective_, that in my

humble opinion the boy be neither more nor less than a fool; and you may

as well try to drive a pig straight as to talk sense to a fool. but how

a man so particular as General Grant can have bring such an inmate

into his family, and then expect that thing be to go on with the order

and precision that----”



“Madam!” begin the General in a voice of thunder, his stock of patience

utterly exhaust by this indirect mode of attack. but Annie, with a

degree of tact and moral courage for which Lewis have by no mean be

disposed to give her credit, lay her hand imploringly on her father’s

arm, and whisper a few magical word which serve to avert the storm

that have appear inevitable. an awkward pause ensue, which be break

by the General, who, rise majestically from his chair, inform Lewis

that he should request his attendance in half-an-hour; then cast

a wither glance at Miss Livingstone, which cause that respectable

porcupine of private life to bristle up if possible more fiercely

than before, he quit the room. no soon have her nephew-in-law’s

retreating footstep cease to echo through the long corridor than the

good lady, free from the restraint of his presence, do then and there

openly, avowedly, and with much vehemence, utter a declaration of war

to the knife with that gallant officer, issue a protest against the

introduction of “rampant idiot” into that heretofore peaceful family,

and finally assert her own liberty of action by promulgate she

determination to depart forthwith, leave her companion to contemplate

the agreeable contingency of “be frighten out of their wit every

day, and murder in their bed all night.”



have in some degree relieve her mind by this explosion, she apply

the superfluous steam still remain to the purpose of locomotion, she

crisp schako rend the air, and her high-heeled shoe knock sharp

little double knock, as of an angry postman, against the polished oak

floor as she sweep along.



and these “pleasant passage” be the first votive offering which Lewis

see present to the Lares and Penates of Broadhurst.



General Grant sit bolt upright in his easy-chair, as if he be on his

charger, and his face wear an expression of scrutinise authority, as

of a commander about to review his troop, when Lewis, in obedience to

his summon, enter the library.



“take a chair, Mr. Arundel. I have desire the attendance of Sir Walter

Desborough, and expect he will be here immediately.”



in compliance with this request, Lewis seat himself to await the

arrival of his future pupil; but the minute glide by, and still no

pupil appear. at length, just as the General’s small stock of patience

become exhausted and he have beg Lewis to ring the bell, the butler

return, say that it be impossible to induce Sir Walter to leave

his room unless his female attendant might come with he. General Grant

frown portentously, glance expressively towards Lewis, muttering,

“some of the evil effect of a grievous system of neglect,” then add,

to the servant, “you may desire Mrs. Peters to accompany Sir Walter

Desborough.”



“one of the first point to which you will have to direct your

attention, Mr. Arundel,” continue the General as the domestic quit

the apartment, “be to induce my ward to dispense with the society of

this person and her son. he may retain their service as attendant, but

must be teach no long to regard they as companion.”



as he speak the door open and admit three individual. of these,

the first who claim our notice be the unfortunate young baronet who

be to be Lewis’s future charge. he appear about fourteen, but be

tall for that age; his figure be slight and not ungraceful, and his

feature be handsome; his forehead be high, but narrow and recede;

his eye be bright and clear, though totally devoid of expression, and

there be an appearance of weakness and irresolution about the mouth,

which too clearly indicate his want of intellect. Mrs. Peters be a

very stout old lady, on whom the care of life and a rare speciman

of the female costume of some bygone age appear to sit easily; she

outline might have suggest to an imaginative beholder the idea of a

huge pillow which have “come alive” and make itself a gown out of one

of the chintz bed-curtain, forget the waist. her conversation

be embellish by a redundancy of mild ejaculation, amongst which a

benediction on her own “heart alive,” and an apostrophe to a solitary

possessive pronoun which have lose its noun, and agree with nothing in

particular, stand pre-eminent. her stock of idea, which be by no

mean inconveniently large, have be present to she in her youth, and

require alter to suit the present fashion. still she be a good old

woman in her way; her “heart alive” be a very kind one; and she doate

on poor Walter, spoil and indulge he till she have make even a

great fool of he than nature have intend. the trio be complete

by her hopeful son Robert, or, as he be more familiarly term,

Bob Peters, who, one year young than Sir Walter, be as clever and

mischievous an imp as ever indue a page’s livery and bear a splendid

crop of button to fascinate society. press close to his nurse’s side

and drag the pretty page after he by the wrist, Walter enter the

alarming presence of his guardian and his tutor, hang back like a

startled colt the moment he perceive a stranger.



“Walter, come here; I want to introduce you to this gentleman,”

 exclaim General Grant in the blandest tone he could command; but in

vain--Walter only hang his head and shrink close to his protectress.



“oh, my! Walter dearie, go to the General. bless my heart alive,

you ain’t so silly as to be afraid of _he_,” exclaim Mrs. Peters,

emphasise the _him_. as though it refer to a pet lamb or a tame

rabbit.



“go in and win, Master Walter; the gentleman willn’t bite yer,” suggest

Bob in an audible whisper.



but their remonstrance produce no effect upon Walter, and serve only

to increase General Grant’s irritation.



“he must be teach obedience, sir,” he remark quickly, appeal to

Lewis. “nothing can be do until he become obedient;” then turn to

the old nurse, he continue, “Mrs. Peters, Sir Walter will not require

your attendance at present; you may leave the room, and take your son

with you.”



“I’m afeard, sir, you willn’t be able to do nothing with Master Walter

without one of we stop with he. you see, he’s kind of use to we,”

 urge Mrs. Peters.



“I shall feel oblige by your leave the room, Mrs. Peters. when I

require your advice! will inform you of the fact,” return the General,

walk with stately step towards the door, which he hold partially

open to permit the egress of the servant, while he prevent Walter

from follow they.



as he see his friend depart the boy raise his eye, which gleam with

mingle fear and anger, to General Grant’s face, but cold inflexibility

be write there so unmistakably that even the darken perception

of the idiot could not fail to perceive it; and apparently feel

instinctively that resistance would be unavaile, his countenance

assume a sulky, dogged expression, and he suffer himself to be lead to

a seat without opposition. but, despite this success, the General seem

as far from gain his point as ever; neither kindness nor coercion

could induce Walter to pay the slight attention to the remark

address to he, or to utter a single word. any one, to have see he

at that moment, would have imagine he to be hopelessly imbecile. that

such be not the case, however, Lewis, who without interfere openly

have be closely observe he from the moment of his entrance, feel

convinced. he have particularly watch the play of his feature, and

have remark when he first come in that they be characterise by an

expression of fear and shyness rather than of stupidity, and that it be

not until his guardian have banish those whom he know well, and in whom

he have confidence, that they assume the look of stolid sulkiness which

they now wear. after make several unsuccessful attempt to elicit from

his ward some proof of intelligence, General Grant at length quit

the room in search of his daughter, actuate thereunto by a vague

consciousness that his own manner might possibly be deficient in

conciliatory power, and that Annie, from the fact of she belong

to the soft sex, possess a decide advantage over he in this

particular. avail himself of this opportunity, Lewis catch up a

young kitten which be play about the room, towards which he have

observe Walter cast several furtive glance; and caress the little

animal as he hold it in his arm, he approach his pupil, say

quietly--



“I’m sure you like the kitten, Walter, she be so playful and pretty?”



the boy make no answer, but the sullen look in his face gradually give

place to a milder expression, and he glance from Lewis to the kitten

with an appearance of intelligence, for which any one who have see he

a minute before would not have give he credit. Lewis see that he have

touch the right string, and continue in the same kind and gentle

manner--



“we must make a great pet of the kitten. she will play with we and amuse

we nicely.”



as he say this Walter draw close to he, and seeming, in his interest

about the kitten, to forget his fear of the stranger, hold out his hand

for the little creature to be give to he.



“will you be kind to she if I let you have she?” continue Lewis.



Walter nod in token of assent, and Lewis hand he the kitten, which

he immediately begin to fondle and play with, laugh with childish

glee at its gambol. after amuse himself in this manner for several

minute he suddenly turn to Lewis and ask in a half-whisper--



“do you like pony, too?”



delight at this proof of the success of his attempt to win his pupil’s

confidence, Lewis signify his intense affection for pony in general,

and inquire whether Walter possess one. on receive an affirmative

nod he continued--



“and be you very fond of ride it?”



this question seem to perplex the boy, for he make no reply, and a

half-puzzle, vacant expression banish the gleam of intelligence which

have light up his feature. Lewis repeat the inquiry in two or three

different form, but with no well success. a pause ensue, during

which the young tutor ponder with himself the good mean of call

forth and strengthen the faint germ of intellect which evidently

exist in the clouded mind of the poor idiot, when Walter again look

up and exclaim abruptly--



“Bob say I’m to ride the pony when somebody come to take care of I.”



“and I be that somebody,” return Lewis, smile good-naturedly. “you

shall ride the pony to-day if you like.”



this seem to please he, for he nod and laugh, and resume his

gambol with the kitten. suddenly a new idea appear to strike he,

for his face become clouded, and draw close to Lewis, he whisper,

point to the door by which General Grant have leave the apartment--



“don’t tell he, or he willn’t let I go.”



“why should you think so, Walter? that gentleman be your guardian, and

mean to be very kind to you,” return Lewis; but Walter shake his head

and repeated--



“don’t tell he; he willn’t let I go.”



at this moment the General return, accompany by Annie, whose

feeling of sympathy and pity be slightly temper by the fear which

Miss Livingstone have labour industriously to instil into her mind.

Lewis draw the General on one side and give he an outline of all that

have pass during his absence, add, that although it be of course

too soon for he to judge with any degree of accuracy to what extent

they might proceed, it be evident his pupil possess some reasoning

power which cultivation might develop. and he be go on to add that

harshness appear to he likely rather to increase than diminish the

evil, when his attention be attract by an exclamation of anger from

Walter.



the moment General Grant return his ward have relapse into his former

state of sullen apathy, and all Annie’s attempt to induce he to notice

she only appear to increase his obstinacy, till at length she begin

to stroke the kitten, which he still hold in his arm. this, for some

unexplained cause (probably because he fancy she might be about to

injure his favourite, or to deprive he of it), irritate he

beyond control, and forget his fear in his anger, he utter

the exclamation above allude to, and strike at she fiercely with

a riding-whip which he have bring in with he. spring forward,

however, before the blow could descend, Lewis catch his uplifted arm

and hold it in an iron grasp, while in a grave but stern voice he said--



“Walter, I be surprised at you. attempt to strike a lady! you must never

do such a thing again.”



the calm, impressive manner in which he utter these word appear to

produce a beneficial effect in subdue the boy’s irritation; for

after make one furious but unavaile attempt to free himself, he sit

perfectly still and unresisting. nothing, however, could induce he to

make friend with Annie, or to allow she to touch his beloved kitten,

though when Lewis caress it, and even take it in his arm, he appear

well content.



a fortnight’s careful study of the young baronet’s character only

serve to confirm the impression Lewis have receive during this first

interview. that he possess some power of reasoning and reflection be

evident; but the great difficulty lie in find a key to the working

of his mind by aid of which these power might be strengthen and

develop. any direct question seem to puzzle and confuse he, and the

only plan which appear to offer any hope of success be, if possible,

to discover some train of thought (if the vague and desultory fancy

which flit across his feeble brain deserve to be so call), and

then to lead he gently on by suggest new idea, some of which he

might adopt and retain. but it be an up-hill task; and often when

Lewis, with a degree of calm perseverance which in one of his eager and

impetuous disposition could scarcely have be look for, have succeed

in make he acquire, as he believe, a lead idea on which he hope

to base some superstructure of elementary knowledge, a look of hopeless

vacuity would show that no progress have be make, and that the labour

must all be go through again. at other time some shrewd remark or

pertinent question would take Lewis as it be by surprise, and induce

he to imagine that he have underrate his pupil’s mental capacity, and

that the fault must lie in his own inexperience of such case. but there

be much to be unlearn as well as to be teach. as be often the case

in person of weak intellect, the mere animal tendency be unusually

strong. he be subject to violent burst of passion, if his will be

in the slight degree thwart, which it require all Lewis’s firmness

and strength of character to contend against successfully. occasionally

fit of melancholy would seize he, during which he would sit for hour

without speak, his head rest dejectedly on his hand, and nothing

appear able to interest or amuse he. if not prevent, he would eat

so voraciously as to injure his health. he be also indolent and averse

to active exertion of any kind. but Lewis take much pain to teach he

to ride, and the exercise thus obtain tend greatly to strengthen

his constitution. his fondness for animal be one of the most amiable

point in his disposition. he and Faust ere long become inseparable, and

Lewis find the dog a most useful auxiliary in inculcating--by example,

not precept, for Faust could not _quite_ talk--the necessity of implicit

obedience.



a month soon glide by, and at its expiration Lewis inform General

Grant that if he still wish he to undertake the care of his ward he

be willing to do so; an offer of which that noble commander joyfully

avail himself, be in his secret soul equally surprised and please

at the degree of success which have already attend Lewis’s effort, and

only too glad to secure the service of one who could and would save

he all further difficulty in regard to the onerous and troublesome

responsibility which he have take upon himself. for the next six month

of his residence at Broadhurst Lewis see but little of the family.

during the great part of that time the General be absent on a visit

to some relation in Scotland, whither his daughter accompany he.

Miss Livingstone, having supply herself with a resident victim in the

person of Miss Susan Pinner, an unhappy little fourteen-year-old cousin

once remove (the further the well from such a relative, we should

imagine), spend her time very much to her own satisfaction in daily

offer up the helpless sacrifice thus acquire at the altar of her

evil temper, and in tyrannise over the poor of the neighbourhood with

most excruciating benevolence. a sick family be a rare treat to this

venerable scourge. Nauseous be the medicine she force down the

throat of the destitute, aggravate the direction with which she

torture the suffering, hateful the dietary on which she nourish all

sick person and young child! truly an irritate poor man’s plaster

be that sphinx of modern society, Minerva Livingstone, and Odipus

himself would never have guess at one-half her mode of ingeniously

torment indigent merit. fortunately, work out the detail of this

ferocious philanthropy occupy so much of the good lady’s time that

Lewis enjoy a happy immunity from her attention, and be allow to

put in practice his theory for the improvement of his pupil without

let or hindrance; and it be with a degree of pleasure, which be in

itself sufficient reward for his trouble, that he perceive his plan

likely to succeed beyond his most sanguine expectation. affair be in

this position when--but such an interesting disclosure require a fresh

chapter.









CHAPTER XVIII.--LEWIS RECEIVES a mysterious COMMUNICATION, and be RUN

away with BY two YOUTHFUL BEAUTIES.



the arrival of the post-bag be an interesting event to Lewis, as

almost the only pleasure he allow himself be a rapid interchange of

letter with his sister; and to this correspondence be he indebted for

an amount of warm sympathy, judicious advice, and affectionate interest

in his pursuit which tend greatly to relieve the monotony and

diminish the irksomeness of his situation; but with the exception of

Rose and (occasionally) frere, his correspondent be exceedingly

limited in number, and their epistle few and far between. it be then

a matter of no small surprise to he to receive a businesslike-look

letter in an unknown handwriting. to break the seal (which bear the

impression of the letter J. and L. unite in a flourish cipher that

at first sight look like a bad attempt to delineate a true lover’s

knot) be the work of a moment. the content be as follows:--



“Sir,--My partner and myself, have some connection with Warlington,

be cognisant of the death of your late lamented father, which sad

event be report to have be cause by the sudden discovery of some

important information contain in a public journal. it be in our power

to impart to you the nature of that information; but as we have every

reason to believe its importance have not be overrate, we be only

prepared to do so on the following terms:--viz., the present receipt of

ten guineas, and a bond pledge yourself to pay to we the sum of

£200 should the information prove as valuable as we conceive it to be.

await the favour of a speedy answer,



“we have the honour to remain, Sir,



“your obediently,



“Jones & Levi, Attorneys-at-law.



“----Street, Old Bailey.”



“what a strange letter!” soliloquised Lewis, after peruse it carefully

for the second time. “the writer be evidently acquaint with the

circumstance of my poor father’s death, but that prove nothing;

the newspaper story rest on the evidence of the library-keeper at

Warlington, and he probably tell it to every one who come into his shop

for the next week; and this tale may have be invent to suit the

circumstance, with a view to extort money. one have hear of such

roguery; still, in that case, why insist on the £200 bond? that seem

as if Messrs. Jones & Levi themselves have faith in the value of their

information; or it may only be do in order to give I that impression.

I’ll send the letter up to Richard Frere and ask he to ferret out these

gent--I dare say they _be_ thorough gent. Walter, I will not allow

you to give Faust all your glove to play with; that be the third pair

he have bite to piece this week. faust! drop it, sir! do you hear I?

that’s right--good, obedient dog! now for Master Richard.”



so say he take a pen and write, in a delicately-form yet free and

bold hand, the follow note:--



“dear old Frere,--Certain individual, sign themselves ‘Jones

& Levi,’ have see fit to favour I with the enclose mysterious

communication, which on the face of the thing look very like an

attempt to swindle. as there be, however, just a remote possibility

that something may come of it (for their account of the circumstance

precede my poor father’s death tally exactly with the recital my

sister give I on my return), you will, I be sure, add one more to your

many kindness by investigate this matter for I. you must bear in

mind that £10 note be by no mean too plentiful with I, and that,

under present circumstance, my bond for £200 would scarcely be worth

as many penny. my poor charge progress slowly; he have become much

more docile and tractable, and be considerably improve in manner and

general amiability, but his mental capacity be lamentably deficient;

his reasoning power and usual habit of thought be about on a par with

those of an average child of six or seven year old; many intelligent

child of that age be greatly his superior in intellect: still,

he make visible progress, and that be recompense sufficient for any

expenditure of time and trouble. he appear much attach to I, and

(perhaps for that very reason--perhaps from the necessity to love

something, which exist in the nature of every man worthy of the name)

I have become so deeply interested in he, that duty which six month

ago I should have reckon irksome in the extreme, I now find a real

pleasure in perform. I bear you with these detail because... because

you be so old a friend that I have acquire a prescriptive right to

bore you when I like. as Walter and Faust (who clearly know that I

be write to you, and send you an affectionate wag of the tail) be

become impatient at the length of my epistle, there be a walk in

prospect dependent on my arriving at a satisfactory conclusion, the

soon I do so the well.



“your ever, L. A.”



as Lewis fold and seal this missive a servant enter with a note on

a silver waiter, say, as he present it, “for you, sir. I be desire

to wait while you read it.”



it be write in a stiff, formal hand, and run as follows:--



“Miss Livingstone present her compliment to Mr. Arundel, and request

the favour of an interview with he.”



“what be in the wind now, I wonder?” think Lewis, but he only say,

“tell Miss Livingstone I will do myself the pleasure of wait on she

immediately,” and the servant retire.



Minerva be enthrone in state in the small drawing-room, the large

one be an awful apartment, dedicate to high and solemn social

convocation, and by no mean lightly to be enter. care sit upon she

wrinkle brow, and look as uncomfortable there as in such a situation

might reasonably have be expect. as Lewis enter, this remarkable

woman rise and perform as near an approach to a curtsey as her

elephantine conformation would permit; then, graciously motion her

visitor to a seat, she growl an inquiry after the well-being of his

pupil, promulgate a decidedly scandalous account of the state of

the weather, with a disheartening prophecy append relative to

meteorological misery yet to come; and having thus break her own ice,

dive into the chilly recess of her cold water system, and fish up

from its stony depth the weighty grievance that oppress she.



it appear that the same post which have convey the mysterious

document from Messrs. Jones & Levi have also bring a letter from

General Grant contain the intelligence that he be about to return

home forthwith, that the house be to be prepare for the reception of

a large Christmas party, and that he wish Miss Livingstone to pay

a round of visit preparatory to the issue of innumerable note of

invitation, by which the neighbourhood be to be induce to attend

sundry festive meeting at Broadhurst; and all this be to be do more

thoroughly and on a large scale than usual, for some mysterious reason

in regard to which the General be equally urgent and enigmatical. but

Minerva shall speak for herself.



“have thus, Mr. Arundel, make myself acquaint with General Grant’s

wish (fourteen bed to be ready this day week, and not even the

hanging put up on one of they--but man be so inconsiderate nowadays),

I proceed to give Reynolds (the housekeeper) full and clear

instruction (to not one of which do she pay proper attention--but

servant be so careless and self-conceite nowadays) as to all the

necessary domestic arrangement. I then desire the coachman might be

inform that I should require the carriage to be ready for use at

two o’clock to-morrow (as you be perhaps aware, sir, that since the

General’s departure for Scotland I have restrict myself to a simple

pony-chaise). Judge of my amazement when I be tell there be no horse

fit for use! I beg to see the coachman instantly, but learn that

he be confine to his bed with influenza. the second coachman be in

Scotland with the General, so that in fact there be not a creature of

sufficient respectability for I to converse with to whom I could

give direction about the matter. under these circumstance, which

be equally unexpected and annoying, I consider myself justify in

apply to you, Mr. Arundel. would you oblige I by go through the

stable and ascertain whether anything can be devise to meet the

present emergency? I be aware that the service I require of you be

beyond the strict routine of your duty; but you must yourself perceive

the impossibility of a lady venture among stableman and helper

without show a disregard to that strict rule of propriety by which it

have be the study of my life to regulate my conduct.”



having reach this climax, Minerva glance with an air of dignified

self-approval towards Lewis and begin a very unnecessary process of

refrigeration with the aid of a fan apparently compose, like its

mistress, of equal part of cast-iron and buckram. Lewis immediately

signify his readiness to undertake the commission, and promise to

return and report progress, bow and leave the room.



on reach the stable a groom attend his summon, and, after the

fashion of his race, enter into a long explanation of the series of

untoward circumstance to which the present state of equine destitution

might be attribute, in the course of which harangue he perform, so

to speak, a fantasia on the theme, “and then do you see, sir, coachman

be hill,” to which sentence, after each variation, he constantly

return. the substance of his communication be as follows:--Shortly

before the General’s departure one of the carriage horse have fall and

break his knee, and its companion have an unamiable predilection

for kicking, the pair be sell and a couple of young unbroken animal

purchase, which, after a summer’s run, be destine to replace the

delinquent. shortly after this the General fall in love with and buy

a pair of iron-grey four-year-old, also untraine. all these young

horse be now take up from grass and about to be break in, but the

coachman’s illness have interrupt their education.



“well, but be neither pair of the young stock available?” inquire

Lewis.



“I’m afeare not, sir,” be the reply. “the bay ain’t never be in

harness, and the iron-grey only three time.”



“oh, the grey _have_ be in harness three time, have they?” resume

Lewis. “let we take they out to-day and see how they perform.”



“if you please, sir, I be only pad groom, and I cann’t say as I should

feel myself disactly compertent to drive they wild young devil.”



“bring out that mail phaeton; put on the break harness, and I’ll drive

they myself,” return Lewis.



“but, if you please, sir,” begin the groom in a tone of remonstrance.



“my good fellow, you waste time in talk. of course if anything go

wrong in consequence of your obey my direction, I alone shall

be answerable; but nothing will go wrong if your harness be sound,”

 return Lewis quickly.



the man, see the young tutor be determine, summon one of his

fellow, and in a short time the phaeton be make ready and the horse

harness and lead out. they be a splendid pair of dark iron-grey,

with silver mane and tail; their head, small and well set on, their

sloping shoulder, and fine graceful leg, speak well for their descent;

but they snort with fear and impatience as they be lead up to their

place, and their bright full eye gaze wildly and restlessly around.



“be quiet with they!” exclaim Lewis as one of the man lay a rude

grasp on the rein to back the near-side horse into his place. “you never

can be too quiet and steady with a young horse. Soho, boy! what be it

then? nobody be go to hurt you,” he continue, pat the startle

animal, and at the same time back he gently into the require

position.



the operation of putting-to be soon complete; and Lewis say, “you

have well lead they off if there be any difficulty in get they to

start,” take the rein in his hand and spring up lightly but quietly.

seat himself firmly, he ask, “now, be you all ready?” and

receive an answer in the affirmative, continue, “give they their

head;” then make a mysterious sound which may be faintly portray by

the letter “tchick,” he endeavour to start his horse. but this be

no such easy matter. the near-side horse the moment he feel the collar

run back, pull against his companion, who return the compliment by

rear and strike with his fore-foot at the groom who attempt to

hold he.



“Steady there!” cry Lewis. “Pat his neck; that’s right. quiet, horse!

stand, sir! one of you call those man here,” he add, point to a

couple of labourer who be dig in a slip of ground near. “now, my

man,” he resume as they come up, “take hold of the spoke of the hind

wheel and move the carriage on when I give you the signal. be you

ready? stand clear; all right.” as he speak he again attempt to start

the horse, and this time more successfully.



the animal which have rear at the first attempt spring forward, and

find the weight which he have probably fancy be immovable yield to

his effort, appear anxious to proceed, but the other still hang back,

and be partly drag forward by his yoke-fellow, partly push on

by the man who be propel the carriage. Lewis again try mild

measure, but without effect; and at length, consider that the

soothe system have be carry far enough, he draw the point of the

whip smartly across the animal’s shoulder. in reply to this the recusant

fling up his heel as high as the kicking-strap would permit; but on

a second and rather sharp application of the thong he plunge forward

and throw himself into the collar with a bind that try the strength

of the trace; then pull like a steam-engine, appeared resolve to

revenge himself on his driver by strain every sinew of his arm to

the utmost pitch of tension. but row, fencing, and other athletic

exercise have render those arm as hard as iron; and though the

swollen muscle round and stand out till his coat-sleeve be stretch

almost to burst, Lewis continue to hold the rein in a vice-like

grasp, and the fiery horse, arch their proud neck and toss the

foam-flake from their champing jaw, be compel to proceed at a

moderate pace. the groom run by their side for a short distance,

then, at a sign from Lewis, one of the man watch his opportunity and

scramble up while the phaeton be still go on; the other, having

open a gate lead down a road through the park, remain gaze

after they with look of the deep interest.



[illustration: 0128]



“well, sir, you’ve manage to start ’em easy than I expect,” observe

the groom, as, in compliance with Lewis’s desire, he seat himself at

his side. “Coachman be a good half-hour a get ’em hout of the

yard last time as they be put-to; that near-sider wouldn’t take the

collar no how.”



“and yet he’ll turn out the well horse of the two if he’s judiciously

manage,” return Lewis. “he have high courage than his companion,

though they’re both splendid animal. they only require careful

driving and work moderately every day to make as good a pair of

carriage-horse as a man need wish to sit behind.”



“it ain’t the first time as you’ve handle the ribbon by a good many,

I should say, sir,” continue Bob Richards (for that be the man’s name,

dear reader, although I’ve never have an opportunity of tell you so

before). “I see’d as you know’d what you be about afore ever you get on

the box.”



“_before_ I get up!” return Lewis. “how do you manage that, my

friend?”



“why, sir, the furst thing as you do be to cast your eye over the

harness to see as all be right; then, afore ever you put your foot on

the step you take the rein into your hand, so that the minute you

be up you be ready for a bolt, hif so be it have please Providence to
