start the ’orse off suddenly. now, anybody as ben’t use to the way

of four-footed quadrupal wouldn’t never have think of that.”



“your power of observation do you credit,” return Lewis, with

difficulty repress a smile. “you be right, I have be accustom to

drive, as you imagine.” and as he speak the remembrance of scene

and person now far away come across he, and he think with regret

of pleasant hour pass with his young associate in Germany, when the

mere fact of his be an Englishman cause he to be regard as an

oracle on all matter connect with horseflesh.



while this conversation be take place the iron-grey have proceed

about a mile through the park, dancing, curvetting, and stare on all

side, as though they would fain shy at every object they discern.



“they be gradually drop into a steady pace, you see,” observe

Lewis; “they’ll be tired of jump about, and glad to trot without

break into a canter, when they get a little warm to their work.

quiet, boy, quiet!” he continue, as the horse suddenly prick up

their ear and stare wildly about they; “gently there, gently! what in

the world be they frightened at now?”



the question do not long remain a doubtful one, for in another minute

a hollow, rush sound become audible, and a herd of deer, startle by

the rattling of the carriage, break from a thicket hard by, and bound

over the tall fern and stunt brushwood, dart across the road, their

long thin leg and branching antler, indistinctly see in the

grey light of an autumn day, give they a strange and spectre-like

appearance. but Lewis have no time to trace fanciful resemblance, for

the horse demand all his attention. as the sound of pattering foot

approach they begin to plunge violently; at the sight of the deer they

stop short, snort and tremble with fright; and when the herd

cross the road before they, perfectly madden with terror they rear

till they almost stand upright; then, turn short round, they dash

off the road at right angle, nearly overturn the phaeton as they do

so, and break into a mad gallop, despite all their driver’s effort

to restrain they, tear away with the speed of lightning. for a few

second the sound of the wind whistle past his ear, and oppress

his breathing to a painful degree, confused Lewis and deprive he of

the power of speech; but the imminence of the danger, and the necessity

for calmness and decision, serve to restore his self-possession, and

turn towards his companion, who, pale with terror, sit convulsively

grasp the rail of the seat, he inquired--



“can you recollect whether there be any ditch across the park in this

direction?”



“there ain’t no ditch, as I recollect,” be the reply, “but there’s

something a precious sight worser. if these devil go straight ahead for

five minute long at this pace, we shall be dash over the bank of

the lake into ten foot water.”



“yes, I remember; I see where we be now. the ground rise to the left,

and be clear of tree and ditch, be it not?” ask Lewis.



the groom reply in the affirmative, and Lewis continue: “then we must

endeavour to turn they; do you take the whip, stand up, and be ready to

assist I at the right moment. what be you think of?” he continue,

see that the man hesitate and be apparently measure with his eye

the distance from the step to the ground. “it would be madness to jump

out while we be go at this rate. be cool, and we shall do very well

yet.”



“I’m agreeable to do whatever you tell I, only be quick about it,

sir,” rejoin the groom. “for if it come to jump hout, or sit

still to be drownde, hout I go, that’s flat, for I never could abear

cold water.”



“I suppose the rein be strong, and to be depend on?” inquire Lewis.



“nearly new, sir,” be the reply.



“then be ready; and when I tell you, exert yourself,” continue Lewis.



while these remark pass between the two occupant of the phaeton,

the horse still continue their mad career, resist successfully all

attempt to check the frightful speed at which they be hurry on

towards certain destruction. as they dash past a clump of shrub,

which have hitherto conceal from view the danger to which they be

expose, the full peril of their situation become evident to the eye of

Lewis and his companion. with steep and broken bank, on which american

shrub, mix with flag and bulrush, grow in unbounded luxuriance,

the lake lie stretch before they, its clear depth reflect the

leaden hue of the wintry sky, and a slight breeze from the north

ripple its polished surface. Less than a quarter of a mile of smooth

greensward separate they from their dangerous neighbour. an artist

would have long to seize this moment for transfer to canvas or

marble the expression of Lewis’s feature. as he perceive the nearness

and reality of the danger that threaten he, his spirit rise with the

occasion, and calm self-reliance, dauntless courage, and an energetic

determination to subdue the infuriated animal before he, at whatever

risk, lend a brilliancy to his flash eye, and impart a look of

stern resolve to his finely cut mouth, which invest his unusual beauty

with a character of superhuman power such as the sculptor of antiquity

seek to immortalise in their statue of hero and demigod. select

an open space of turf unencumbered with tree or other obstacle, Lewis

once more address his companion, saying--



“now be ready. I be go to endeavour to turn they to the left, in

order to get their head away from the lake and uphill; but as I shall

require both hand and all my strength for the rein, I want you to

stand up and touch they smartly with the whip on the off-side of the

neck. if you do this at the right moment, it will help to bring they

round. do you understand I?”



Richards reply in the affirmative, and Lewis, lean forward and

shorten his grasp on the rein, work the mouth of the horse till

he get their head well up; then assure himself by a glance that

his companion be ready, he check their speed by a great exertion of

strength; and tighten the left rein suddenly, the groom at the same

moment apply the whip as he have be desire, the fiery steed,

spring from the lash and yield to the pressure of the bit, alter

their course, and go round so sharply that the phaeton be again

within an ace of be overturn, dash forward in an opposite

direction.



“you do that uncommon well, to be sure, sir,” exclaim Richards,

draw a long breath like one relieve from the pressure of a painful

weight. “I think we be over once, though; it be a precious near go.”



“a miss be as good as a mile,” return Lewis, smile. “do you see,”

 he continue, “they be slacken their pace; the hill be begin to

tell upon they already. hand I the whip; I shall give the gentleman a

bit of a lesson before I allow they to stop, just to convince they

that run away be not such a pleasant amusement as they appear to

imagine.”



so say, he wait till the horse begin sensibly to relax their

speed; then hold they tightly in hand, he punish they with the whip

pretty severely, and give they a good deal more run than they like

before he permit they to stop, the nature of the ground (a gentle

ascent of perfectly smooth turf) allow he to inflict this discipline

with impunity.



after proceed two or three mile at the same speed he perceive

another cross-road run through the park. gradually pull up as he

approach it, he get his horse into a walk, and as soon as they have

once again exchange grass for gravel he stop they to recover wind.

the groom get down, and gather a handful of fern, wipe the foam from

their mouth and the perspiration from their reeking flank.



“you’ve give ’em a pretty tidy warming, though, sir,” he observe. “if I

be you I would not keep ’em stand too long.”



“how far be we from the house, do you imagine?” inquire Lewis.



“about three mile, I should say,” return Richards. “it will take you

nigh upon half-an-hour, if you drive ’em easy.”



Lewis look at his watch, muttering, “More than an hour to Walter’s

dinner-time.” he then continue, “get up, Richards; I have not quite

do with these horse yet;” add, in reply to the man’s questioning

glance as he reseat himself, “I’m only go to teach they that a herd

of deer be not such a frightful object as they seem to imagine it.”



“Sure_ly_ you’re never agoin’ to take’em near the deer again, Mr.

Arundel; they’ll never stand it, sir,” expostulate Richards.



“you can get down if you like,” observe Lewis, with the slight

possible shade of contempt in his tone. “I will pick you up here as I

return.”



Richards be a thorough John Bull, and it be a well-know fact that to

hint to one of that enlightened race that he be afraid to do the most

insane deed imaginable be quite sufficient to determine he to go

through with it at all hazard. accordingly, the individual in question

press his hat on his brow to be prepare for the bad, and fold

his arm with an air of injure dignity, sit sullenly hope for an

overturn, which might prove he right, even at the risk of a break

neck.



Lewis’s quick eye have discern the herd of deer against a dark

background of tree which have serve to screen they from the less

acute perception of the servant, and he now contrive, by skirt the

aforesaid belt of Scotch fir, to bring the phaeton near the place where

the deer be station without disturb they, so that the horse be

able clearly to see the creature which have before so greatly alarmed

they. it have be often remark that horse be greatly terrify by an

object see but indistinctly, at which, when they be able to observe it

more closely, they will show no sign of fear. whether for this reason,

or that the discipline they have undergone have cool their courage and

teach they the necessity of obedience, the iron-grey approach the

herd of deer without attempt to repeat the manoeuvre which have be

so nearly prove fatal to their driver and his companion. Lewis drive

they up and down once or twice, each time decrease the distance

between the horse and the animal, to the sight of which he wish to

accustom they, without any attempt at rebellion on their part beyond a

slight preference for use their hind leg only in progression, and

a very become determination to arch their neck and point their ear

after the fashion of those high-spirited impossibility which do duty

for horse in greek frieze and in the heated imagination of young lady

artist, who possess a wonderful (a very wonderful) talent for sketch

animal. having continue this amusement till the deer once again

convey themselves away, Lewis, delight at having carry his point

and overcome the difficulty which have oppose he, drive gently back

to Broadhurst; and having commit the reek horse to the care of

a couple of groom, who begin hiss at they like a whole brood of

serpent, return to make his report and soothe the tribulation of that

anxious hyæna in petticoat, Miss Martha Livingstone.









CHAPTER XIX.--CHARLEY LEICESTER BEWAILS his cruel misfortune.



Frere’s answer to Lewis’s note make its appearance at Broadhurst on the

morning of the second day after that on which the event narrate in the

previous chapter take place. it run as follows:--



“Dear Lewis,--I think I’ve tell you before--(if it ben’t you it be

your sister, which be much the same thing)--not to write such a pack of

nonsense as ‘add to my many kindness,’ and all that sort of stuff,

because it’s just so much time and trouble waste. I see no particular

kindness in it, that’s the fact. you and she live in the country, and I

in town; and if there be anything that either of you want here, why of

course it’s natural to tell I to get or to do it for you; and as to

apologise, or make pretty speech every time you require anything,

it’s sheer folly; besides, I _like_ do the thing for you. if I

don’t I wouldn’t do they, you may depend upon _that_; so no more of

such rubbish ‘an you love I.’ and now, touch those interesting, or

rather interest_ed_, individual, Messrs. Jones & Levi. I think when

I read their letter they be rascal or thereabout, but a personal

interview place the matter beyond doubt; and if you take my advice,

you’ll see they--well, never mind where--but keep your £10 in your

pocket, that’s all. depend upon it, they be more used to make rich

man poor than poor one rich. however, I’ll tell you all their saying

and doing, as far as I be acquaint therewith, and then you can judge

for yourself. as soon as I receive your letter I trudge off into the

city, find the den of thief--I mean the lawyer’s office--of which

I be in search, send in my card by an unwashed Israelite with a pen

behind each ear and ink all over he, whom I take to be a clerk, and by

the same unsavoury individual be usher into the presence of Messrs.

Jones & Levi. Jones be a long cadaverous-look animal, with a clever,

bad face, and the eye of a hawk; Levi, a fat Jew, and apparently a

German into the bargain, with a cunning expression of countenance and

a cringe manner, who give one the idea of having be feed on oil-cake

till he have become something of the sort himself; a kind of man who, if

you have put a wick into he, wouldn’t have make a bad candle, only one

should so have long to snuff he out. well, I soon tell these worthy

what I be come about, and then wait to hear all they have to say for

themselves. the Gentile, be most richly gift with speech, take upon

he to reply--



“‘let I offer you a chair, Mr. Frere, sir. delighted to have the honour

of make your acquaintance. I speak for my partner and myself--eh, Mr.

Levi?’



“‘in courshe, shir. Moosh playsure, Misthur Vreer, shir,’ mutter Levi,

who speak through his nose, after the manner of modern Israelites, as if

that organ be afflict with a permanent cold.



“when I have seat myself Jones return to the attack by observe:

‘our letter contain a certain definite and specific offer. do Mr.

Arundel agree to that, Mr. Frere, sir?’



“‘Mr. Arundel have place the matter entirely in my hand, Mr. Jones,’

reply I; ‘and before I can agree to anything I must understand clearly

what benefit my friend be likely to derive from the information hint

at in your letter.’



“‘may I inquire, Mr. Frere, sir, whether you be a professional man?’

ask Jones.



“‘if you mean a lawyer, Mr. Jones,’ reply I, ‘I be thankful to say I

be not.’



“I suppose he do not exactly relish my remark, for he resume, in a

less amicable tone than he have use before--



“‘I believe the letter to which I have already refer contain a

clear statement of the _only_’ (he emphasise the word strongly) ‘term

upon which we should be dispose to communicate the information,’ and he

glance towards his partner, who echoed--



“‘de _only_ turmsh.”



“‘then, gentleman,’ say I (gentleman, indeed!), ‘I beg most distinctly

to inform you that my friend shall never, with my consent, pay £10

down and become liable for £200 more, this liability depend on a

contingency which you have no doubt provide against, on the mere chance

that some information in your possession may refer to the exciting cause

of his father’s death and prove valuable to he.’



“‘De informationsh ish mosht faluaple,’ break in Levi.



“‘I beg pardon, Mr. Levi,’ exclaim Jones quickly, ‘but I believe we

agree this matter be to be leave to my management?’



“Levi nod his large head and look contrite, while Jones continue:

‘in that case, Mr. Frere, sir, I have only to add that if Mr. Arundel

refuse to comply with our term we shall not part with the information

on any other. at the same time, I should advise he to reconsider the

matter, for I do not hesitate to say that I quite coincide with Mr. Levi

in his opinion concern the importance of the information which be in

our possession.’



“as he say this an idea occur to I, and I replied--



“‘suppose, instead of the bond for £200 in the event of some contingency

which may never occur, Mr. Arundel be willing to pay £20 down for the

information, would you agree to that?’



“‘say vive and dirtysh,’ put in the Jew, his dull eye brighten

at the prospect of money. ‘say vive and dirtysh, and it shall pe von

pargainsh.’



“‘would you agree to take that sum, Mr. Jones?’ ask I.



“he glance at his partner with a slight contraction of the brow and

shake his head; but the spirit of avarice arouse in the Jew be not so

easily to be put down, and he continue, in a more positive tone than he

have yet venture to use--



“‘yesh, he dosh agree. I and my bardner ve vill take the vive and

dirtysh poundsh, ready monish, Mr. Vreer.’



“‘not quite so fast, my good sir,’ return I. ‘if you be so very ready

to give up the bond for £200, to be pay in case the information should

prove as valuable as you assert it to be, the natural inference be that

you yourself have mighty little faith in the truth of your assertion;

and as I happen to be pretty much of that way of thinking also, I shall

wish you both good morning.’



“so say, I put on my hat and walk out of the room, leave the Jew

and the Gentile to fight it out to their own satisfaction.



“I have not a very strong affection for lawyer before, and I cann’t say

this visit have serve to endear the profession to I particularly. you

know the old story of the man who define the difference between

an attorney and a solicitor to be much the same as that between an

alligator and a crocodile. well, Messrs. Jones & Levi realise such a

definition to the life, for a more detestable brace of rascal I never

encounter; and depend upon it, the less you have to do with they

the well; at least, such be the opinion of yours for ever and a day

(always suppose such an epoch of time may exist),



“Richard Frere.”



“so,” exclaim Lewis, refolde the letter, “that chance have fail

I. well, I never expect anything would come of it; and yet--heigho!

I certainly be bear under an unlucky star. I think Frere be rather

precipitate. accord to his account of his proceeding, he seem to

have feel such an intense conviction that the man be rascal that

he call on they rather for the purpose of expose they than to

investigate the matter. he prejudge the question. however, I have no

doubt the result would have be the same in any case. what a bore it

be that man will be rogue! I shall have out those horse again after

Walter have get through his lesson. if they go quietly I shall take he

with I for a drive to-morrow.” and thus commune with himself, he

summon Walter and commence the usual morning routine.



Miss Livingstone have, by Lewis’s advice, order post-horse to the

carriage, and be in that way enable to accomplish her round of visit.

Lewis carry out his intention of drive the iron-grey, who conduct

themselves with so much propriety that on the follow day he take his

pupil with he, and find the drive pleased and amuse the poor boy he

repeat it every fine day. thus a week slip away, and the time for

the General’s return arrive. it be late on the afternoon of the day

on which he be expect, and Lewis be wearily assist poor Walter to

spell through a page of dissyllable, when that peculiar gravel-grind

sound become audible which, in a country house, necessarily precede

an arrival. then there be a great bustle as of excited servant, a

babel-like confusion of tongue, bump and thump of heavy luggage,

much trampling of foot, ring of bell and slamming of door; then

the sound grow fainter, be remit at interval, and at last cease

altogether. the house be no long masterless--General Grant have

return. Walter’s attention, by no mean easy to command for five

minute together at the good of time, become so entirely estrange by

the commotion above allude to, that Lewis close the book in despair

and tell he to go and play with Faust, who, sit upright on a rug in

front of the fire, be listen with the deep interest to all that

pass in the hall, and be only restrain from bark by a strict

sense of propriety operate on a well-discipline mind. the boy gladly

obey, and Lewis, rest his ache head on his hand, fall into deep

thought--he think of old time, when, head of his class at a public

school, alike leader and idol of the little world in which he move, his

young ambition have shape out for itself a career in which the bar, the

bench, the senate, be to be but step-stone to the high honour

to which energy and talent might attain; and he contrast his present

position with the ideal future his boyish fancy have depict. then he

bethink he of the tyrant who command that a live man should be

chain to a corpse, and consider how the cold and numbing influence

of the dead, gradually paralyse the vital energy of the living, be,

as it be, typical of his own fate. he could not but be conscious

of unusual power of mind, for he have test they in the struggle for

honour with the deep and subtile thinker of Germany, and have come off

victorious; and to reflect that these talent, which might have ensure

he success in the game of life, be condemn to be waste in the

wearying attempt to call forth the faint germ of reason in the mind

of an almost childish idiot! the thought be a bitter one! and yet for

month past he have felt resign to his fate; and the deep interest he

take in his pupil’s improvement, together with the time such a quiet

life afford for reflection and self-knowledge, have render he

content, if not what be conventionally term happy. to what then

should he attribute his present frame of mind? at this moment a tap at

the study door interrupt his meditation, and he be unable to pursue

his self-analysis far. have he do so, he might possibly have

discover that pride, his besetting sin, lie at the root of the evil.

as long as he live in comparative seclusion his duty sit easily upon

he; but now that he be again about to mix in society, his position as

tutor became gall in the extreme to his haughty nature. as he hear

the summon above mention he start from his reverie, and sweep

his hair from his forehead by a motion of his hand, exclaim, “come

in.” as he speak the door open, and our old acquaintance, Charley

Leicester, lounge into the room.



“ah! how do you do, Arundel?” he begin in his usual languid tone. “I

know all the in and out of this place, and I think I should find you

here--this use to be _my_ den once upon a time; many a holiday’s task

have I groan over in this venerable apartment. be that your incubus?”

 he continue in a low tone, glance towards Walter. “handsome

feature, poor fellow! do he understand what one say?”



“Scarcely, unless you speak to he individually,” return Lewis. “you

may talk as you please before he, the chance be he will not attend;

but if he do, he will only understand a bit here and there, and even

that he will forget the next moment, when some trifle occur to put it

out of his head. Walter, come and shake hand with this gentleman!”



thus speak to, Walter turn sheepishly away, and stoop down, hide

his face behind Faust. Lewis’s mouth grow stern. “Faust, come here,

sir!” the dog arise, look wistfully at his playfellow, lick his hand

lovingly, then walk across the room, crouch down at his master’s

foot.



“now, Walter, look at I.” at this second appeal the boy raise his eye

to Lewis’s face. “go and shake hand with Mr. Leicester.”



“don’t worry he on my account, pray, my dear Arundel,” interpose

Leicester good-naturedly.



“the General make a great point of his be introduce to every one;

and I make a great point of his doing as I bid he,” return Lewis with

mark emphasis.



but it be unnecessary, if mean as a hint to Walter, for his tutor’s

eye appear to possess a power of fascination over he. no soon do

he meet his glance than he arise from his kneeling position, and go

up to Leicester hold out his hand say, “how do you do?”



Charley shake hand with he kindly, ask he one or two simple

question, to which he reply with tolerable readiness; then observe

that his eye be fix on a silver-mount cane he hold in his hand,

he inquire whether he think it pretty, and receive an answer in the

affirmative, add, “then you may take it to amuse yourself with, if you

like.”



a smile of childish delight prove that the offer be an acceptable one;

and carry off his treasure with he and call Faust, who on a sign

from his master gladly obey the summon, he betake himself to the

farther end of the room, which be a very large one, and begin play

with his canine associate. Leicester gaze at he for a moment or two,

and then observed--



“what a sad pity! such a fine-grow, handsome lad, too! why, in a year

or two he will be a man in appearance, with the mind of a child. do he

improve much?”



“yes, he improve steadily, but very slowly,” return Lewis.



Leicester wander dreamily up to a chimney-glass, arrange his hair

with an air of deep abstraction, pull up his shirt-collar, caress

his whisker, then separate the tail of a nondescript garment,

which give one the idea of a cut-away coat try to look like a

shooting-jacket, he extend his leg so as to form two side of a

triangle, and subject his frigid zone to the genial influence of

the fire, he enjoy for some minute in silence the mysterious delight

afford to all true-bear englishman by the peculiar position above

indicate. at length he sigh deeply and mutter, “Heigho! it’s no use

think about it.”



“that depend on what it be, and how you set to work to think,” return

Lewis.



“that may do as a general rule,” continue Leicester, “but it willn’t

apply to the case in point. the thing I be try to cipher out, as

the Yankees call it, be the incomprehensible distribution of property in

this sublunary life. now look at that poor boy--a stick for a plaything

and a dog for a companion make he perfectly happy. those be his

only superfluous requirement, which together with eating, drinking,

clothing, and lodging might be provide for £300 a year.

instead of that, when he be twenty-one he will come into from £8000 to

£10,000 per annum, besides no end of saving during his minority. well,

to say nothing of your own case” (Lewis’s cheek kindle and his eye

flash, but Leicester, absorb in his own thought, never notice it,

and continue), “though with your talent a little loose cash to give

you a fair start might be the making of you---just look at my wretched

position,--the son of a peer, bring up in all kind of expensive

habit, mix in the good set at Eton and at Oxford, the choose

associate of man of large property, introduce into the high society

in London--of course I must do as other do, I cann’t help myself.

there be certain thing necessary to a young man about town just

as indispensable as smock-frock and bacon be to a ploughman. for

instance, to live one must dine--to dine one must belong to a club. then

London be a good large place, even if one ignore everything east

of Temple Bar; one must keep a cab if but to save boot-leather--that

entail a horse and a tiger. again, for four month in the year people

talk about nothing but the opera--one cann’t hold one’s tongue for four

month, you know--that render a stall indispensable. it’s the fashion

to wear white kid glove, and the whole of London come off black on

everything, so there’s a fine of 3s. 6d. a night only for have hand

at the end of one’s arm. the atmosphere of the metropolis be compose

chiefly of smoke--the only kind of smoke one can inhale without be

choke be tobacco smoke; besides, life without cigar would be a desert

without an oasis--but unfortunately havannah don’t hang on every

hedge. I might multiply instance _ad infinitum_, but the thing be

self-evident--to provide all these necessary a man must possess money

or credit, and I unfortunately have more of the latter than the former

article. it be, as I have explain to you, utterly impossible for I to

exist on less than--say £1500 a year; and even with my share of my poor

mother’s fortune and the Governor’s allowance, my net income don’t

amount to £800; _ergo_, half the London and all the Oxford tradesman

possess little manuscript volume contain interesting reminiscence

of my private life. it’s no laugh matter, I can assure you,” he

continue, see Lewis smile; “there’s nothing cramp a man’s”--here

he release a coat-tail in order to raise his hand to conceal a

yawn--“augh! what do you call ’em?--energies--so much as have a load

of debt hang round his neck. if it haven’t be for those confound

Oxford bill check I at first start, ’pon my word I don’t know

that I might not have do something. I have idea about a parliamentary

career at one time, I can assure you, or diplomacy--any fool’s good

enough for an _attaché_. now, if I have that poor boy’s fortune, and he

have my _mis_fortune, what an advantage it would be to both of we; he’ll

never know what to do with his money, and I should--rather! just fancy

I with £10,000 a year, and a coat on my back that be pay for. by

Jove, I should not know myself. ah, well! it’s no use talk about it;

all the same, I be an unlucky beggar.”



“but,” interpose Lewis eagerly, “if you really dislike the life

you lead so much, why don’t you break through all these trammel of

conventionality and strike out some course for yourself? with £800 a

year to ward off poverty, and the interest you might command, what a

splendid career lie before you! be I in your position, instead of

despond I should deem myself singularly fortunate.”



“so you might, my dear fellow,” return Leicester, after pause for

a minute to regard Lewis with a smile of languid wonder. “so you might

with your talent, and--and wonderful power of get up the steam

and keep it at high pressure. I dare say we should see you a Field

Marshal if you take to the red cloth and pipe-clay trade; or on the

woolsack if you prefer join the long-robe gentleman. now,

I haven’t get that sort of thing in I: I be bear to be a man of

property, and nothing else; and the absurdity of the thing be the

bring a man into the world fit only for one purpose, and then place

he in a position in which, to use the canot of the day, he cann’t

‘fulfil his mission’ at any price. it’s just as if nature be to form

a carnivorous animal, and then turn it out to grass.” having deliver

himself of this opinion with the air of a deeply injure man, the

Honourable Charles Leicester consult a minute Geneva watch with an

enamel back, and replace it in his waistcoat pocket, continue,

“five o’clock; I shall just have time to smoke a cigar before it be

necessary to dress for dinner. I presume tobacco be a contraband article

in the interior of this respectable dwelling-house?”



“a salutary dread of Miss Livingstone’s indignation have prevent I

from ever try such an experiment,” return Lewis.



“well, I willn’t run the risk of offend the good lady,” reply

Leicester. “Aunt Martha have a wonderful knack of blight the whole

family for the rest of the day if one happen to run against one of her

pet prejudice. by the way, you must have find she a most interesting

companion?”



“we be great friend, I can assure you,” rejoin Lewis. “she

condescend to patronise I most benignantly; but I have not speak

half-a-dozen time with she in as many month.”



“I suppose she have enlighten you as to the event about to come off

during the next three week.”



“by no mean. beyond the fact of the General’s return, and the

information that the house be to be fill with people, Miss

Livingstone have allow I to remain in a state of the most lamentable

ignorance.”



“what! have not you hear that the county be vacant, and the General have

be persuade to allow himself to be nominate as a candidate on the

conservative interest?”



“but I think he be already member for the borough of A--------?”



“yes; he will resign that if he succeed for the county. oh, you’re

quite in the dark, I see; we mean to stir heaven and earth to get he

in. my father give he all his interest--Bellefield be come down

to look up the tenantry. you know we (that be, Belle and the Governor,

bad luck) have large estate in the county. Belle can do a little bit

of love-making in between while, and so kill two bird with one stone.

and who else do you think be coming?--a very great man, I can assure

you; no less a personage, in fact, than--ar--_the_ De Grandeville!

he have be induce to--ar--” (and here he mimic De Grandeville’s

pompous manner inimitably) “throw his little influence--ar--into the

scale, and--ar--show himself on the husting, and--ar--arrange one or

two matter which will in fact--ar--render the thing secure! the plain

truth be that he really be a good man of business, and the General

have engage he as an electioneering agent. well, then, there be a lot

of people come besides; and ball and dinner will be give to half

the county. in short, the General mean to do the thing in style, and

spend as much money as would keep I out of debt for the next three

year. several party be to arrive to-morrow, so the General bring

Annie and I down with he as a sort of advanced guard. there will be

some fun, I dare say; but an awful deal of trouble to counterbalance it.

I shall lose my cigar, though, if I stand gossip here any long.

let I see, the near way to the stable will be to jump out of

that window; deduct the distance save from the amount of exertion in

leaping, and the remainder will be the gain of a minute and a half.

well, time be precious, so off we go. I suppose you appear in the course

of the evening? take care, Walter; that be right.”



thus say, he fling open the window, spring out with more agility than

from his usual listless movement might have be expect, pull

the sash down again, and having nod good-naturedly to Walter,

disappear.



General Grant feel and express himself greatly delighted at the marked

improvement which have take place in his ward’s manner and appearance,

and attribute it with justice to Lewis’s judicious management, that

young gentleman rise many degree in his employer’s favour. the General

be essentially a practical man--he be endow with a clear head, and

(save where prejudice interfere) a sound judgment, and be happily

devoid of that inconvenient organ, a heart (whence proceed, amongst

other reprehensible emigrant, the whole host of amiable weakness,

which merely gain for their proprietor that most useless, because

unsaleable, article--affection), he look upon his fellow-creature as

machine, and weigh they in the balance, patronise those only

who be not find want. Lewis have prove himself a good teaching

machine, and the General value he accordingly.



“the great point now, Mr. Arundel,” he say, “be to endeavour to expand

your pupil’s mind. you have develop in he (and I give you great

credit for the degree of success you have attain) power of acquire

knowledge,--those power must be cultivate; he must have opportunity

afford he of see people and amass fact for himself; and

to this end it be my wish that he should mix as much as possible in

society. I be about to entertain a large party at broad-hurst, and I

conceive that it will be a desirable opportunity to accustom Sir Walter

to the presence of stranger, and to enable he, by the force of example

act on his imitative power, to acquire the manner and habit of

those of his own rank. I therefore propose that after two o’clock on

each day your pupil and yourself should join the family circle and enter

into any scheme for amusement or exercise which may be arrange. I

consider myself most fortunate,” continue the General, with a little

patronising inclination of the head towards Lewis, “in having secure

the service of a gentleman whom I can with such entire satisfaction

present to my friend.”



in compliance with this injunction Lewis be force, much against his

will, to withdraw from the retirement under the shadow of which he have

hitherto contrive to screen himself from those annoyance to which his

dependent situation expose he, and which his sensitive nature lead he

especially to dread. on the follow day arrival succeed one another

with great rapidity, and when Lewis join the party after luncheon

there be several face with which he be unacquainted. one, however,

immediately arrest his attention, and turn to Leicester, he

inquire the name of the person in question.



“eh! who be the man with moustache, do you say? what! don’t you know

he?” exclaim Leicester, if, indeed, the slow, languid manner in which

that young gentleman be accustom to promulgate his sentiment can

be properly so term. “how very odd! I think everybody know _he_;

that’s my _frère aîné_ Bellefield; come with I, and I’ll introduce

you.”



“excuse I,” return Lewis, draw back with a flushed cheek as the

recollection of the scene on the bank of the Serpentine come vividly

before he. “I have no idea it be your brother; I never imagine for a

moment----”



“my dear Arundel, don’t excite yourself; as a general rule, there’s

nothing in this life worth get up the steam about,” return

Leicester, draw on a kid glove. “Bellefield will be extremely happy

to make your acquaintance--in fact, he be always extremely happy. if

you be to cut your throat before his very eye he would be extremely

happy, and if he think you do it well, probably fold his arm, ask

what you would take for the razor, and be extremely happy to buy it of

you. but as he’ll be constantly here, there exist a positive necessity

for you to know he--so come along.”



thus say, Charley Leicester link his arm in that of Lewis and

carry he off, _nolen volen_, to be introduce to his brother.



Lord Bellefield having see Lewis only once before, and under very

peculiar circumstance, do not immediately recognise he; and having

make up his mind that for electioneer purpose it be necessary to

bear all specie of social martyrdom amiably, undergo his introduction

to Lewis with great resignation, curl up his moustache and show

his white tooth in a ready-make smile--of which article he have always a

stock on hand--most condescendingly.



Lewis’s be, however, a face that once see it be not easy to forget.

moreover, there be at that moment an expression gleam in his dark

eye not altogether consistent with the conventional indifference

befit a mere social introduction, and Lord Bellefield be too close

an observer not to notice it.



“I’ve a strange idea I’ve see you somewhere before, Mr. Arundel,” he

remark.



“if I be not much mistaken,” return Lewis, “your lordship once do I

the honour,” and he lay a slightly sarcastic emphasis on the word, “to

offer I a sum of money for a favourite dog.”



there be something in Lewis’s manner as he utter these word which

show that he have neither forget nor forgive the insult that have

be offer he. Lord Bellefield perceive it, and reply, with a

half-sneer--



“ay, I recollect now--you jump into the water to fish he out; and I

naturally imagine that, as you appear to set such store by he, you

must expect to make money of he. have you get he still?”



Lewis reply in the affirmative, and his lordship continued--



“well, I’ll give you your own price for he any day you like to name the

sum.”



without wait for an answer he turn away and begin converse in an

undertone with his cousin Annie.









CHAPTER XX.--SOME of the character FALL out and other fall in.



“so! you’re old acquaintance, it seem!” observe Leicester, who have

overheard the conversation follow upon Lewis’s introduction to Lord

Bellefield. “Frere tell I about the dog business, but I never know till

now that it have be Bellefield who offer you money for he. I can see

you be annoy about it. Belle fancy money can buy everything (which

be pretty true in the long run), and a dog be a dog to he and nothing

more. he’d never dream of make a friend of one; in fact, he vote

friendship a bore altogether; so you must not heed his insult to Herr

Faust. what be people go to do this afternoon? I wish somebody would

settle something. Annie, just attend to I a minute, will you--what be

we go to do?”



“Papa talk of a skating party on the lake,” return Annie, “but

I’ve have no definite order. where can papa be? do go and look for he,

Charles.”



“be he in the house, think you?” inquire Charles, rise languidly and

gaze round with a look of dreamy helplessness.



“I see General Grant cross the lawn with a gentleman--Mr. De

Grandeville, I believe--not five minute since,” observe Lewis.



“exactly; then as you know where to find he, Arundel, and I don’t, I

dare say you’ll be kind enough to tell he that--what be it, Annie?”

 say Leicester, reseat himself in an easy-chair with an expression of

intense relief.



“Charley, how idle you be! I be quite ashamed of you,” exclaim Annie

vehemently; then, turn to Lewis, she continue, “if you would be so

kind, Mr. Arundel, as to ask papa whether the lake scheme hold good,

and if we be to walk or drive there, I should be so much oblige to

you.”



Lewis signify his willingness to execute her wish, and call to

Walter to accompany he, leave the room.



“well, Annie, how do you like Lewis Arundel by this time?” inquire she

cousin. “ben’t I right in tell you he be quite a catch?”



“yes, indeed,” return Annie warmly; “and he be so kind and clever

about that poor Walter, I don’t know what we should do without he. I

think it be quite delightful to see his manner towards he, poor boy! it

combine all the tenderness of a woman with the firmness of a man, he be

so patient and forbearing; but it must in some degree repay he for his

trouble to see the improvement he have effect, and the strong affection

he have inspire. Walter absolutely seem to dote upon he.”



“a most desirable acquisition, certainly, the affection of an idiot,”

 observe Lord Bellefield with a satirical curl of the lip.



“I never despise real affection of any kind,” return Annie quickly.



“I be delighted to hear you say so, _belle cousine_,” reply Lord

Bellefield, fix his bold, rove eye on she with an expression

intend to be fascinating, but which be simply disagreeable.



Annie look annoyed, and say she must warn Miss Livingstone of the

intend expedition, rise and quit the apartment.



when the brother be leave together, Charles, after a minute’s pause,

began--“I say, Bellefield, I wish you’d try and be a little more civil

to young Arundel. you annoy he by the way in which you offer money

for his dog, just after he have risk his life to save it, and I don’t

think you mend matter by what you add to-day. recollect he’s a

gentleman by birth, and have the feeling of one.”



“curse his feeling!” be the unamiable rejoinder; “he’s a proud,

insolent young puppy. if he’s a gentleman by birth, he’s a beggar by

position, and require pull down to his proper level. I’ve no notion

of dependent give themselves such air, and shall let he know my

opinion some of these day.”



Charley Leicester regard his eld brother with a half-sleepy look of

serio-comic disgust, then slightly shrug his shoulder, he draw

on his glove, place his hat on his head, arrange his curl to his

satisfaction at a mirror, and lounge gracefully out of the room.



Scarcely have he do so when the late subject of their conversation

enter by another door which open into the conservatory, and glanced

round the apartment as if in quest of some one. apparently the object of

his search be not to be discern, for turn to Lord Bellefield, he

inquire “whether he could direct he where to find Miss Grant?”



the person address favour he for some second with a supercilious

stare ere he answer, “and what might you want with that young lady,

pray?”



Lewis pause for a moment before he dared trust himself to reply, for

the tone in which the question have be ask be most insolent. at

length he say, “I can have no objection to gratify your lordship’s

curiosity. the General wish I to inform Miss Grant that he have

arrange a skating party on the lake for this afternoon, and that

carriage would be at the door in ten minute to transport those of the

company thither who might prefer drive to walk.”



“really, you must possess a wonderful memory, Mr. Arundel; I dare swear

those be the General’s very word. as, however, I can scarcely imagine

it consistent with your onerous duty to play the part of squire to

dame, I’ll save you the trouble for once, by deliver your message

myself.” and with an irritate smile, as he remark the anger his

word have produce, Lord Bellefield turn and quit the apartment.



Lewis stand for a moment gaze after the retreating figure, his chest

heaving and his nostril expand, like those of some hunted animal;

then pace the room (his invariable custom when labour under strong

excitement), he give vent to the follow break sentences:--



“he mean to insult I--his word, his look, everything prove it--and

I do not resent it. perhaps he think I fear he--if I believe so,

I’d follow he, and before they all fix on he the blow of shame that he

must avenge, or own himself a coward.” as he speak he take two or three

Mother--that poor boy--I dare not sacrifice they.” he fling himself into

a chair, and press his hand against his burn brow, resume,

“oh, why be I call upon to bear this?--how have I sin, that this

degradation should be force upon me?--the coward! he know I be bind

hand and foot, or he dare not thus insult I; it be like strike a

fetter man--” he pause, then add, “well, a time may come when I may

meet he more as an equal; at all event, now it be my duty to bear as

much as human nature can, and I’ll do it.” he remain silent for a few

minute, with his hand over his eye, wait till the excitement should

pass away. from this state he be arouse by feel something touch

he, and look up, he perceive the idiot, half kneeling, half sit

by his side, gaze up into his face with look of wonder and sympathy.

this mute evidence of affection act as a balm to his wounded spirit,

and lay his hand kindly on the boy’s shoulder, he say, “Walter,

my poor fellow, have I frightened you? I be not angry with _you_, you

know. come, we will walk down to the lake and see the skating. what have

become of Faust, I wonder? we must take he with we, of course.”



“who be that who go away just now?” return Walter. “he with the

hair over his mouth, I mean?”



“that be Lord Bellefield, your friend Mr. Leicester’s brother.”



“he’s a bad man, ben’t he?”



“why should you think so, Walter?”



the boy pause for a few moment in reflection, then answer, “his eye

look wicked and frighten I; besides, he make you angry--I hate he.”



“you should not say that, Walter; you know it be not right to hate any

one,” return Lewis, feel dreadfully hypocritical; then link his

arm in that of his pupil, they pass out through the conservatory.



as the sound of their retreating footstep die away a figure peep

timidly into the apartment, and see it be untenante, enter and

gaze after they long and fixedly. it be Annie Grant, who, return

to learn the result of Lewis’s embassy to her father, have involuntarily

overhear both the insult and the burst of wound feeling which it have

call forth.



in that short five minute be sow seed that, as they grow to

maturity, bear sleepless night and weary day, and the tearless sorrow

of a break heart, as a portion of their bitter fruit.



the lake in Broadhurst Park present a gay scene on the afternoon

in question. the General, anxious to propitiate the good-will of the

voter, have order the park to be throw open to all who might choose

to witness or join in the amusement of skating. a sharp frost, which have

continue without intermission for several day, have cover the water

with a firm coating of ice, which afford a surface as smooth as

glass for the evolution of the skater. the sun be shine brightly,

bring out beautiful effect of light and shade on the steep and

rugged bank, and cause the hoar-frost on the feathery branch of a

young birch plantation to glitter like spray of diamond. on the side

approach by the drive from the house a tent have be pitch, in such

a direction that any of the party who fear to expose themselves to the

cold might witness the performance of the skater and yet be shelter

from the troublesome intrusion of the north wind.



as Lewis and Walter come in sight of the spot (on which several group

of well-dress people, together with a considerable number of a low

class, be already assemble) the latter utter an exclamation of

delight, and rouse out of his usual state of apathy by the novel

Leicester, to whom he have take an extreme fancy.



“Mr. Arundel be go to teach I how to skate, Mr. Leicester, and you

be to help,” he exclaim, as soon as he have recover breath after his

run.



“be I?” return Leicester with a good-natured smile “how do you know

that I will help you?”



“because Mr. Arundel say so; and everybody mind he--Faust and all.”



“be that true, Arundel? be I to do just as you tell I?” inquire

Leicester, as the individual allude to joined they.



“it be quite right that Walter should think so, at all event,” return

Lewis; “but I tell he to ask you, as a favour, whether you would lend

we your assistance. Walter be anxious to learn to skate, and to save his

cranium from get a few artificial bump suddenly develop upon it,

I propose that you and I should each take one of his arm and keep he

from fall, till he learn to stand safely upon his skate without

assistance.”



Leicester give vent to a deep sigh of resignation, then mutter,



“well, I should certainly never have dream of undergo such an

amount of exertion on my own account; but I suppose Walter fancy

it will be very charming, and he have not a great many pleasure, poor

fellow!” he continue aside. and so, like a good-natured, kind-hearte

creature, as, despite his affectation, he really be, he perform

the service require of he, and actually exert himself till his

complexion become, as he express it, “red than that of some

awful ploughboy.” after a time Walter grow tired with the unaccustomed

exercise, and take off his skate, the trio proceed to join the

party at the tent. as they approach, Annie trip up to Leicester,

and seize his arm, say, “where have you be all this time? I want

you particularly.” she then add something in a low voice which have

great degree, and elicit from he the incredulous ejaculation,

“nonsense!”



“I know you’d be surprised,” return Annie, laugh. “she be go to

remain here till the party break up, so you’ll have plenty of time

to make yourself agreeable, if it’s not ‘too much trouble,’ or ‘such a

bore,’” she continue, mimic Charles’s languid drawl.



“how be this matter bring about, pray?” inquire her cousin; “and why

on earth do you fancy it concern I in any way?”



“it be all my doing,” return Annie. “I be not blind when we be in

Scotland; and after you leave we I make a point of cultivate the young

lady, and fortunately for you, approve of she, I ask papa to let I

invite she to Broadhurst.”



“of course, with that discretion which be such a striking characteristic

of your amiable sex, impart to he all your view in do so.”



“now, Charley, you be very cross and unkind and disagreeable. I ask

she merely because I think it would give you pleasure; and though I

like sometimes to tease you a little myself, of course I never dream

of say anything to my father which could annoy you.”



“well, you be a dear, good little cousin, I know, so I willn’t scold

you,” be the reply, and they enter the tent together.



a few minute afterwards Lewis be engage in point out to Walter one

of the skater who be perform some very intricate figure with

great success, when he hear a female voice exclaim, “surely I be

not mistaken--that be Mr. Arundel!” and turn at the sound, beheld,

lean on the arm of Charles Leicester, Miss Laura Peyton, the young

lady who have penetrate his disguise at Lady Lombard’s party. not to

return her bow be impossible; but at the recollection of all that have

pass on that evening his cheek flush and his feature assume a

cold, haughty expression, the result of mingled pride and vexation,

under which he strove to conceal his annoyance. Annie, who be not aware

that Lewis and her friend have ever meet before, glance from one to the

other with look of the great astonishment, which be by no mean

diminish when Miss Peyton continue, “now let I inquire after the

Prince of Persia. I hope you leave his Highness in the enjoyment of good

health.”



while Lewis be strive to frame a suitable reply, Annie, who

could restrain herself no long, exclaim, in a tone of the utmost

bewilderment, “the Prince of Persia! my dear Laura, be you out of your

sense?”



the only reply her friend be able for some minute to return be

render inaudible by a fit of laugh, in which Leicester, and at last

even Lewis himself, could not resist join.



“now I call that abominable,” continue Annie; “you be all enjoy

some excellent joke, and I be leave to pine in ignorance. Laura, what

_be_ you laugh at?”



“ask Mr. Leicester,” return Miss Peyton, breathless with laughter.



“Charles, what be it all about?”



“ask Arundel,” be the reply; “he be the proper person to explain.”



“Mr. Arundel, you _must_ tell I!”



“really, I must beg you to excuse I,” begin Lewis. “Miss Peyton--that

be--Mr. Leicester--in fact, it be utterly impossible for I to tell you.

come, Walter, you’ve rest quite long enough, you’ll catch cold sitting

still, after make yourself so hot;” and as he speak he take his

pupil’s arm and hastily quit the tent.



of course as soon as he be out of earshot, Annie reiterate her demand

that the mystery should be explain, and of course Laura beg Charles

to relate the affair, and then, woman-like, interrupt he before he

have utter half-a-dozen word, and be once fairly off, do not stop

with much spirit and drollery; then, in her turn, she have to be inform

of the position Lewis hold in the General’s family; how wonderfully

Walter have improve under his care, and how much everybody like he.

when they have fully discuss these matter, they be join by Lord

Bellefield, who escort they across the ice to witness more closely the

proceeding of the skater.



later in the afternoon a party of young man have undertake to skate a

quadrille. this be something new, people hurry from all side to

witness the performance, and a crowd speedily collect. Walter have

express a wish to see it, and Lewis, please at the unusual interest

he take in all that be go forward, which he rightly regard as

a proof of the decide progress his intellect be make, willingly

comply.



the crowd still continue to thicken as the quadrille proceed, and it

have just occur to Lewis that the weight of so many people collect

in one spot would try the strength of the ice pretty severely, when

a slight crack sound confirm his suspicion, and induce he to

withdraw Walter from the group. it be fortunate that he do so, for

scarcely be they clear of the crowd when a sharp crack, like the

report of a pistol, ring in his ear, follow in rapid succession

by one or two similar explosion. then come a rush of many foot,

accompany by the shrill scream of woman, and on look round Lewis

perceive that a portion of the ice have give way, and that several

person be struggle in the water.









CHAPTER XXI.--FAUST get ON SWIMMINGLY, and the reader be INTRODUCED to

a diving BELLE “wringing” WET.



the shriek allude to in the last chapter still continue, and Lewis,

consign Walter to the care of a servant, hasten to the spot to

render any assistance which it might be in his power to afford. as he

reach the scene of action the panic and confusion be so great that

it be no easy matter to ascertain the extent of the mischief, or to

perceive how good it might be remedie. Lord Bellefield, who seem

the only person at all collect, be issue direction in a loud,

authoritative voice, to which the majority of the bystander appear

too much alarmed and excited to pay attention. the number of person who

be actually immerse have be increase by the injudicious attempt of

those who have first endeavour to assist they by rush to the edge of

the broken ice, which, give way under their weight, have plunge

they also into the water. as Lewis come up a rope be fling across the

opening, and hold tightly by man on either side. grasp this firmly

with one hand, the young tutor assist to extricate several person who

be cling to the edge of the ice. he be just spring back as the

portion on which he be stand break away beneath his foot, when a

cry be raise, “there’s a lady in the water!” and immediately some one

add, “it’s the General’s daughter--it’s Miss Grant.” before the word

be well speak Lewis have fling off his greatcoat and be about

to plunge into the water, when his eye suddenly catch that of Lord

Bellefield, who, have in the confusion accidentally station himself

by his side, be point with vehement gesture to the spot where,

partly sustain by the buoyant nature of her dress, partly support by

a mass of float ice, the form of Annie Grant be to be discern. at

the sight of the eager face of the man who have insult he some evil

spirit seem to take possession of Lewis’s breast; check himself

suddenly, he step back a pace, and fix his eye with a pierce

glance on Lord Bellefield’s feature, say coldly, “I beg pardon, your

lordship will, of course, rescue Miss Grant.”



for a moment anger and surprise deprived Lord Bellefield of the power of

speak, but as soon as he could find word he reply, “go on, sir; as

you could risk your life for a dog, you will surely take a cold bath to

save your master’s daughter.”



the speech be an ill-choose one, for it excite a degree of irritation

which outweigh all other consideration, and fold his arm across

his chest, Lewis reply in a tone of the bitter irony, “your

lordship must excuse I. _I be no squire of dame_.”



Lord Bellefield’s only rejoinder be an oath, and fling off his

wrapper, he appear about to spring into the water. suddenly change

his intention, he turn to Lewis and exclaim, his face livid with

rage and vexation, “ten thousand curse on you! you know I cannot swim.”



it be at such moment as these, when by our own wilful act we have lay

ourselves open to his attack, that the tempter urge we on to crime

which in our calm moment we should shudder to contemplate. a glance

of triumph shoot from Lewis’s dark eye; the fearful thought flash

across he, “she be to be his bride--her fortune be to repair his

extravagance--perhaps he love she--let he save she himself, I will not

rescue her _for he_.” and the fiend prompt the idea, worthy of its

originator, that he might revenge himself on Lord Bellefield by leave

Annie to perish. but, like many other clever people, for once the demon

outwit himself, the very magnitude of the offence serve to awaken

Lewis to the sinfulness of the line of conduct he have meditate. almost

in the same moment in which the idea occur to he a mist seem to

clear itself away from his mental vision, and he perceive the abyss of

guilt on the brink of which he be stand. and now the agonising doubt

suggest itself to he whether his repentance might not have come too

late--whether Annie might not sink before he could reach she; and as

Lord Bellefield run off impetuously to hasten the movement of a party

who be bring a small flat-bottom boat towards the spot, Lewis

spring into the water, clear a quarter of the distance in his leap,

and swam with vigorous stroke in the direction of the still float

figure.



his fear be not unfounded. Annie’s dress, which have hitherto serve

in great measure to sustain she, be rapidly becoming saturate with

water; every instant she sink lower, and while he be still some yard

from the spot, to his horror he perceive the fragment of ice on

which she rest roll round and slip from her grasp. the effect be

instantaneous. utter a pierce shriek, which ring through his ear

like a death-knell, she throw out her arm in a vain attempt to save

herself, and disappear beneath the water. at the same moment there be

a rush, a bound, a plunge--some large animal dash past Lewis, and ere

the last fragment of Annie’s dress disappear Faust have seize it in

his mouth and prevent its wearer from sink. the bystander now draw

the rope which have be fling across the opening in the ice in such a

direction that Lewis could grasp it, and thus support, he contrive to

raise Annie’s head above the water, and with some assistance from Faust,

to keep both she and himself afloat till such time as the punt should

arrive. this, fortunately, be not long. the instant it be launch,

Lord Bellefield and one or two other jump into it, and in another

moment Annie Grant be rescue from her perilous situation, to the

horror of which she be, however, by this time happily insensible. as

they be lift she into the boat, poor Faust, who probably do not

understand that his service be no long need, still retain his

hold on her dress, and Lord Bellefield strike he so fiercely with the

handle of a boathook that he fall back stunned, and would have sink

have not Lewis, who be still in the water, throw his arm round he and

support he.



“the punt can hold no more,” exclaim Lord Bellefield. “Miss Grant’s

safety must not be endanger for _any_ consideration and as he speak he

push the boat from the spot, leave Lewis still cling to the rope

and support the weight of the dog, which do not as yet begin to show

any sign of life.



“we will bring the boat back for you, sir, directly” cry one of the

man who be assist Lord Bellefield in punt.



“you must be quick about it, if you care to be of any use,” return

Lewis in a faint voice, “for I cann’t hold on much long; my limb be

becoming numb with the cold.”



“well let go the dog if you’re in any difficulty,” suggest Lord

Bellefield with a malicious laugh as the boat move rapidly away.



“that be the way they would repay your faithful service, eh! my poor

Faust,” murmur Lewis. “never fear, we’ll sink or swim together, my

dog. if any one deserve to drown for this day’s work ’tis I, not

you.” at the sound of his master’s voice the poor animal open his eye

and begin to show sign of return animation. Fortunate be it for

they both that Lewis have contrive to place the rope under his arm in

such a position as almost entirely to support, not only his own weight,

but that of the dog also; for long before the boat return his strength

be entirely exhaust, and his limb, from the length of time he have

be immerse in the icy water, have completely lose all sensation, and

be powerless as those of a child.



Lord Bellefield contrive to detain the boat on various pretext, till

at last the man who have promise to return lose all patience, and push

off without wait for permission; in another moment it be by Lewis’s

side.



“take the dog first,” exclaim Lewis in a voice scarcely audible from

exhaustion. “now, you must lift I in, for I cann’t help myself.” with

some difficulty (for even with the assistance of the rope Lewis have be

barely able to keep his own head and that of Faust above water) the

nearly recover from the effect of the blow, and be able to stand

up and lick his master’s face and hand as he lie at the bottom of the

punt. Lewis, however, by no mean appear in such good case; his cheek

and even lip be deadly pale, his breathing be hard and laborious,

and he lie with his eye close and his limb stretch out with
