a landing be practicable, Charles Leicester, who have assist his

brother in convey Annie to the carriage, which be fortunately in

wait, come run back, and as his eye fall upon the prostrate form

of Lewis, he exclaimed--



“why, Arundel! good heaven, I believe he’s insensible.”



nor be he wrong. the instant the necessity for exertion be over

the reaction have be too much for Lewis, and he have faint. he be

instantly lift from the boat and carry to the tent, where such

restorative as could be at the moment procure be apply, at first

without success, but after a short time the colour begin to return to

his lip, and in a few minute more he be restore to consciousness.



“Bravo, that’s all right,” begin Charley Leicester, as Lewis, with a

faint smile, sit upright and return his hearty shake of the hand with

a feeble pressure. “you begin to look a little less like a candidate for

a coffin than you do five minute ago. I declare, when I see you in the

boat, think it be a case of ‘found drown.’ faust! good dog, how

unpleasantly wet you be--what a bump he’s get on the top of his head,

just where the organ of combativeness--no, veneration, ben’t it? ought

to be. how do that happen? in fact I’m quite in the dark as to the

whole affair, for I have go to fetch shawl for some of the lady, and

when I reach the scene of action Bellefield be fish his intended,

half-drown, out of a moist punt, and enlist I to assist in

convey the dripping damsel to the carriage. do you fall in

together?”



“you will hear enough about it soon, I dare say,” return Lewis,

speak feebly and with apparent difficulty. “I be afraid I have

scarcely sufficient life leave in I just now to tell you.”



“don’t attempt it,” return Leicester good-naturedly. “and the soon

you get those soak clothe off, the well. of course they will send

back the trap for you.”



“my carriage be on the spot,” interrupt a tall, aristocratic-look

man who have assist in convey Lewis to the tent. “my carriage be on

the spot, and be very much at this gentleman’s service. we must all

feel anxious to prevent his suffering from the effect of his gallant

conduct. the preserver of Miss Grant’s life must be consider as a

public benefactor.”



at this praise a slight colour rise to Lewis’s pale cheek, and a

look of pain pass across his feature. _he_ to be style Annie’s

preserver!--_he_ who have all but sacrifice her life to his feeling of

revenge! and as the recollection occur to he a slight shudder run

through his frame.



“there, you be actually shiver,” exclaim Leicester. “I shall not

let you stay here any long. since Sir Ralph Strickland be so kind as

to offer his carriage, there be nothing to delay we. can you walk? take

my arm.”



Lewis, with an inclination of the head to Sir Ralph, take Leicester’s

proffer arm, and have with difficulty rise from his seat,

attempt to walk, but at the first step he stumble, and would have

fall have not his friend support he.



“Steady, there,” continue Leicester; “you’re hardly in march order

yet. would you like to wait another minute or two?”



“I think I have well try to proceed,” reply Lewis; “exercise may

serve to restore the circulation.”



“allow I to take your other arm,” say Sir Ralph Strickland kindly;

“then I think you will be able to reach the carriage--it be close at

hand. the length of time you be in the water have cramp your limb. I

see the whole affair, and never witness anything more interesting than

the conduct of your noble dog.”



and as he speak he stoop and pat Faust, then force Lewis to

accept his offer of assistance, they leave the tent together. as his

blood begin once again to circulate the cramp and stiffness gradually

disappear, and ere the trio reach the carriage Lewis scarcely

require assistance. on reach Broadhurst he find the General wait

to receive he, and the instant he alight he have to undergo a long,

prosy, and pompous harangue, embody that noble commander’s gratitude,

during the delivery of which oration the subject of it be keep stand

in his wet clothe, a compulsory act of homage to the cold-water system,

by no mean congenial to his feeling, mental or bodily. however, it

come to an end at last, and Lewis be permit to retire to his own

room. moreover, Charles Leicester (instigate thereunto by a hint from

Miss Peyton) waylay the apothecary who have be summon on Annie’s

account, and cause he to inspect Lewis’s condition, which measure

result in a command to have his bed warm, and instantly to deposit

himself therein; with which medical ordinance Lewis be fain to comply.



there he lie until, from be much too cold, he become a great deal

too hot, for before night he be in a high state of feverish excitement,

accompany by violent pain in the head and limb. his medical adviser

be, however, fortunately really skilful, and by vigorous and timely

measure he contrive to avert the rheumatic fever with which his

patient be threaten; and after spend three day in bed Lewis

arise, feel indeed especially weak, but otherwise little the bad

in body for his aquatic exploit. we say in body, for mentally he have

suffer, and be still suffer bitterly. as he lie on the couch of

sickness in the silent hour of the night, face to face with conscience,

the recollection of the sin he have commit (for a sin it be, and he

be too honest-hearted in his self-scrutiny not to recognise it as such)

haunt he. the fact that he have be unable by his own act to repair

the consequence of the evil he have meditate impress he deeply--but

for Faust Annie would have sink ere he could have reach the spot,

probably to rise no more. it appear a special interference of

providence to convince he of the folly of self-reliance, and to impress

upon his mind a sense of the mercy of God, in save he from the

consequence of his revengeful feeling. true, he have repent of his

fault almost in the moment of committal; true, he have risk his life in

proof of the sincerity of his repentance; true, the provocation he have

receive might, in the eye of man, serve in great measure to justify

he; still, the knowledge that but for the interposition of Providence

he might now have feel himself a murderer fill he with emotion of

the deep penitence, and at the same time of the lively gratitude.



in this frame of mind the encomium pass upon his gallant conduct be

most distressing to he, and a short note from Annie, thank he in

a few simple word for having save her life, add fuel to the fire of

his self-condemnation. amongst other good resolution for the future he

determine to bear any insult Lord Bellefield might offer with as

much patient endurance as could by any possibility be consistent with

self-respect in one in his dependent situation; and the reader may judge

of the sincerity of his repentance if he reflect what such a resolution

must have cost his haughty nature. he also determine to seek an

opportunity of confess to Annie how little he deserve her gratitude,

and to implore her forgiveness for the wrong he have intend she. the

dipping that young lady have undergone do not appear to have affect

either her health or her spirit. by the doctor’s order she also have

be send to bed immediately on her return home, where, fall asleep,

she escape a lecture from Minerva and all other evil consequence

of her immersion, and wake the next morning none the bad for the

accident.



it be about a week after the day on which these event have take place,

when, the afternoon be fine, Lewis and Walter propose to take a

ride together. Walter have mount his pony, and Lewis be strap a

greatcoat in front of his horse’s saddle, when Richards, the groom, who

have be elevate to the rank of second coachman (as the illness of the

head coachman have render his resignation an act of necessity, and

the next in command have succeed to his vacant box), come forward, and

touch his hat, ask if he could speak to Lewis a minute.



“certainly; what be it?” return Lewis, step aside a few pace.

“why, sir, p’r’aps you know as the General’s go out a-driving?”



“I be not aware of the fact,” return Lewis; “but what then?”



“he’s a-driving of hisself, sir,--_our_ iron-grey, Mr. Arundel. Master

ain’t so young as he use to be, and it’s my belief if any-think

startle ’em he willn’t be able to hold ’em--they go sweetly now, but

they do pull most amazing. I drive ’em yesterday, and afore I get home

my arm ache properly.”



“do you mention this to General Grant?” inquire Lewis.



“well, I tell he I be afeard he’d find ’em pull rather stiff; but he

only give I one of his dark look, as much as to say, ‘keep youi

advice to yourself, and mind your own business.’ Master’s rather a hard

gentleman to talk to, you see; he’s always be use to shoot and

flog the black, out in the Ingies, till it’s kind a-become natural

to he; and _as_ he cann’t act the same here with we white, why it put

he out like.”



“I do not see that anything can be do now,” observe Lewis, after

a moment’s reflection. “if I have be here when the General start I

would have tell he the trick the iron-grey play we, and advise he

not to drive they just yet; but I dare say it would have do no good;

for, as you say, your master be not over fond of advice gratis. I

suppose he have one of the groom with he?”



“only a mere boy, sir, and Miss Annie,” be the reply.



“what!” exclaim Lewis in a quick, excited tone of voice; “be Miss

Grant with he? why do you not say so before? which road have they

take? how long have they be go?”



“about twenty minute, or p’r’aps not so long,” return Richards. “I

think they’re go to Camfield--leastway, I hear master tell Miss

Annie to bring her card-case, ’cos he be go to call on Colonel

Norton.”



“that must be eight mile by the road, but not much above five across

the field by Churton Wood,” rejoin Lewis.



“that _be_ right, Mr. Arundel,” be the reply; “and the gate be

unlocked, for I ride that way with a note for Colonel Norton the day

afore yesterday.”



Ere Richards have finish speak Lewis be on horseback; and as soon

as they reach the park he turn to his pupil, say, “now, Walter,

sit firmly, guide the pony on to the turf, tighten your rein, and then

for a good canter; touch he with the whip--not too hard--that’s it.”

 put his own horse in motion at the same time, they ride forward at

a brisk canter, which, as the horse grow excited by the rapid motion,

become almost a gallop. cross the park at this pace, they turn down

a bridle-path which lead through a wood and across several grass field,

beyond the last of which lie a wide common. as they approach this

Lewis take out his watch. “above four mile in twenty mintues,--I call

that good work for a pony. you ride very well, Walter,--you’ve a capital

seat on horseback now.”



“I can leap too,” rejoin Walter. “richard teach I the day when you

be ill in bed.”



“I’m glad to hear it,” return Lewis, who, while his pupil be

speak, have be endeavour, unsuccessfully, to open a gate, “for

they have fasten this gate with a padlock, and we must find our way

over the hedge.”



“oh! but I can’t----” begin Walter.



“yes you can,” interpose Lewis, “when I have clear the road for

you, and show you how to do it. sit still and watch I.” so say,

he select a place where the hedge be thin and the ditch and bank

practicable, and put his horse into an easy canter, ride at it.

be particularly anxious that nothing should go wrong, and that Walter

should be convince of the feasibility of the attempt, Lewis be not

well pleased when his horse, instead of rise to the leap, refuse it,

and reply to a tolerably sharp application of the spur by plunge

violently and turn short round. his rider, however, sit as firmly

as if he be part of the animal, and canter round two side of the

field, get he well in hand and again ride he at the hedge, work his

mouth with the bit and give he the spur. this discipline produce the

desire effect; for instead of refuse the leap this time, the horse

spring forward with a bind which would have clear an obstacle of

twice the size, and alight on the other side several foot beyond the

ditch. Lewis ride on a few yard, and then turn, leap back into the

field and rejoin his pupil. “now, Walter, you must do as I have do.

Canter up to that gap, give the pony his head, touch he on the flank as

he approach the hedge, sit firmly and press in your knee, and you’ll

go over as nicely as possible.”



but poor Walter’s courage fail he; the conflict between Lewis and

his horse have destroy his confidence, and he be afraid to make the

attempt. his tutor read it in his blanched cheek and quail glance,

and be as kind and judicious as he be firm, forbore to press the

point, and dismounting, lead the pony through the gap, and assist

Walter to scramble over on foot; then remount his steed, he test

his obedience by once more leap he over; and having thus achieve

the adventure of the locked gate, tutor and pupil canter off across

the common. but this little episode have cause some loss of time, and

when Lewis reach the lane lead to the village, near which Colonel

Norton’s house be situate, he learn from a man who be mend

the road that a phaeton, answer the description of General Grant’s

equipage, have pass a few minute before.



“my friend Richards’ fear be needlessly excited then, it seem, and

the old gentleman be a well whip than he give he credit for be,”

 think Lewis. “Come, Walter,” he add aloud, “we will go back by the

road. don’t trot just yet; the horse be warm, we must allow they to

get a little cool.”



after proceed about half a mile along the lane, which be only just

wide enough to allow vehicle to pass each other, they overtake an

elderly woman in a red cloak most picturesquely perch between two

pannier on a donkey’s back. such an arrangement be a novelty to

Walter, he be proceed to inquire of what use the pannier be, when

Lewis’s quick sense of hearing catch a sound which cause he to rein

in his horse and, enjoin silence, pause to listen. his ear have not

deceive he. owe to the frosty weather the road be particularly

hard, the rut also have be lately mend with coarse gravel, and as

he listen the sound of horse’ foot gallop, and the rattle of a

carriage proceeding at unusual speed, become distinctly audible in the

lane behind they. the vehicle be evidently rapidly approach. the

lane be in this part extremely narrow, Lewis’s first thought be for

Walter’s safety. seize the pony’s rein, he set spur to his horse, and

they canter on a short distance till they reach a gateway lead

into a field. the gate be fortunately open, and desire Walter to ride

into the field and wait till he join he, he turn his horse’s

head and begin, to retrace his step. as soon as he have pass an old

oak-tree which stand at a corner of the road and prevent any one from

see beyond it, he perceive the cause of the sound which have reach

he, and which he have already but too correctly divine.



at about a hundred yard from the spot where he be station appear a

phaeton draw by a pair of magnificent iron-grey horse, which Lewis have

no difficulty in recognise. from the furious pace at which they be

advance, it be evident that their driver have lose all control over

they; while about half-way between Lewis and the equipage in question

be the donkey and pannier, with the old woman in the red cloak before

allude to. the gentleman drive the phaeton shout to she to get out

of the way, and Lewis make sign as to which side of the road she have

well take; but she appear either paralyse with fear or unable to

guide her donkey; and ere she be able to comply with, or probably to

comprehend these direction, the infuriate horse have overtake she,

and dash against she, fling she, donkey, pannier and all, to the

ground with a shock like that of a battering-ram. at the same instant

lewis, avail himself of the temporary check, ride forward, and

spring from his saddle, seize the head of the phaeton horse, and

with much difficulty, and no inconsiderable personal risk, succeed in

stop they.









CHAPTER XXII.--THE TRAIN arrive AT an IMPORTANT station.



the catastrophe relate at the end of the last chapter attract the

attention of a couple of labourer who have be engage in mend the

road, and they immediately hasten to the spot to render any assistance

which might be require. by their aid the poor woman be extricate from

her perilous situation, and fortunately prove to be less injure than

could have be expect, a dislocated shoulder be the most serious

hurt she have sustain. commit the phaeton and horse to the care

of one of the work man, Lewis and the other labourer carry the poor

woman to a cottage by the roadside, and deposit she on a bed till

such time as the surgeon (for whom General Grant have, by his daughter’s

suggestion, despatch the groom on the horse which Lewis have ride)

should arrive. luckily, they have not long to wait, as the boy meet the

person he be in search of return from his round of professional

visit. the dislocated shoulder be soon set to right and bandage up,

and the sight of Annie’s well-fill purse render easy an arrangement

with the tenant of the cottage to allow the invalid to continue their

inmate till the next day, when she could be remove without detriment.



in the meantime the General have draw Lewis on one side, and be

expatiate to he upon the cause of the accident. “you perceive, Mr.

Arundel, that my wrist be slightly swollen? well, sir, that be from an

old strain receive in the little affair at Sticumlÿkphun. I be only

a captain then. the company to which I belonged get separate from the

regiment in cross a jungle, and a party of the Rajah’s irregular

horse try to cut we off; they be upon we so suddenly, we haven’t

time to form a hollow square, and for a minute our fate appear

sealed;--they ride the man down like sheep. in the _mêlée_ a gigantic

trooper cut down the colour-sergeant and be about to possess himself

of the flag, when I seize the staff with my left hand and strike at he

with my sabre, but, unfortunately, it break on his cuirass; his sword

have also snap with the blow which have cause the sergeant’s death,

and a struggle ensue between we for the possession of the colour.

his strength be in proportion to his height, but although I feel as if

every muscle in my arm be about to snap, I hold on till one of my

man shoot he through the head. at the same moment a troop of the 14th

Lancers ride up and rescue we--but my wrist-have never recover the

strain. however, I find little difficulty in hold in these horse,

till, just now, when we have turn to come home, some boy overthrow a

barrow full of stone by the roadside, which startle the animal;

they break into a gallop, and despite all my effort to prevent it, the

accident to which you be witness occur.”



“have I know of your intention, sir, I should have caution you not to

trust they too implicitly,” reply Lewis. “before your return,--by Miss

Livingstone’s wish,--I go over the stable to ascertain whether there

be any carriage horse she could use. I drive these grey the second

or third time they have ever be in harness, and they run away with I

in Broadhurst Park; but I have take they out several time since, when

Walter wish for an airing, and I believe they have become quiet.”



“indeed,” return the General, more graciously than be his willnot, “I

be not aware you be so good a whip; that relieve I from a great

difficulty; you will be so obliging as to drive the phaeton home, and I

can ride your horse. with my wrist in their present condition it would

be a great risk for I to attempt to hold in those animal, and the

groom be a mere boy. Annie, my dear,” he continue, as his daughter

approach they from the cottage, “our difficulty be at an end.

Mr. Arundel, it appear, have be in the habit of drive these horse

lately, and will be so good as to take my place and see you safely

home.”



“but, papa----” begin Annie in a tone of remonstrance, while a slight

accession of colour replace the rose which fear have banish from her

cheek.



“my dear, the arrangement be the only one which appear feasible under

present circumstance. I shall ride Mr. Arundel’s horse and will keep

near, so you need be under no alarm,” return her father majestically.



Annie by no mean approve of the plan. in the first place, she be

a good deal afraid of the horse, and have no experience of Lewis’s

skill as a driver, be naturally alarmed at trust herself again

behind they. in the second place, she have a vague idea that it be

scarcely etiquette to take a _tête-à-tête_ drive with the handsome young

tutor. but she see that her father be quite determined, so, like a

sensible girl, she refrain from offer opposition which she foresee

would be useless.



Lewis, however, read in that “book of beauty,” her expressive face,

the secret of her fear, take an opportunity, while the General be

alter the stirrup to suit himself, to reassure she by say, “you

need not be in the least afraid, Miss Grant. believe I, I would

not undertake so great a trust as that of your safety do I not feel

perfectly sure that I could drive you home without the slight

danger.”



as Lewis speak Annie raise her eye and glance at he for a moment. it

have be already remark, in the course of this veracious history, that

when Lewis smile, the nameless charm which in Rose Arundel’s face win

the love of all who know she shed its lustre over his handsome feature.

to analyse such an expression of countenance be scarcely possible, but

perhaps the near approach to a correct description of it would be

to say that it be a bright, sunshiny look which inspire other with a

conviction of its wearer’s kindliness of heart and honest truthfulness

of purpose. such be its effect in the present instance, and when her

father hand she to her seat in the phaeton the uneasiness which have

arise from a want of confidence in her driver have in great measure

disappear. Lewis wait, with the rein in his hand, till the General

have mount and ride off with Walter, who acquiesce silently in the

change of companion, then spring lightly to his place, he desire the

man at the horse’ head to stand aside, and drive off. the iron-grey

soon find out the difference between their late conductor and their

present one, and after one or two slight attempt to gain their own way

give up the point and settle down into a quiet, steady trot. Annie,

whose alarm have quicken her perception on the subject, be not long

in remark the change, and turn to her companion, observe, “how

do you contrive to make the horse go so quietly, Mr. Arundel? when papa

be drive they they do nothing but dance and caper the whole way, and

at last, as you be aware, run away with we.”



Lewis, who consider that the present be a favourable opportunity,

which might never occur again, to unburden his mind in regard to

the skating affair, and be debate with himself how he might well

introduce the subject, hear her question mechanically, as it be,

without its reach the ear of his understanding, and it be not until

he observe her look of surprise at receive no answer to her query

that he hasten to reply, “I beg your pardon, Miss Grant, I be

think on quite a different subject. I have live such a hermit’s life

of late with poor Walter that I fear I have become dreadfully absent.”



“I merely ask by what charm you have contrive to tame these fiery

steed,” return Annie, smile at his evident bewilderment.



“the charm of a steady hand and a strong arm,” reply Lewis. “but

these horse and I be old acquaintance; we have a struggle once for the

mastery, and I conquer, which they have not forget.” he then give

she a short account of the runaway scene in Broad-hurst Park, to which

she listen with much interest. when he have conclude, Annie remark,

“how dreadful it must have be when they be rush towards the lake,

and you feel uncertain whether you might be able to check their wild

career! that lake seems destine to become the scene of dangerous

adventure. I must take this opportunity,” she continue with a faint

blush, “of thank you for save my life. in the few hurried line

I write you, I be afraid I scarcely make you understand how much I--in

fact, that I be not ungrateful.”



it be now Lewis’s turn to feel embarrassed. the moment he have seek

for be arrive. he must confess that which would turn his companion’s

gratitude into aversion; he must forfeit her good opinion irretrievably,

and probably for this very reason (so perverse be human nature), he, for

the first time, discover that he value it highly. Annie be the only

member of the family (with the exception, perhaps, of Charles Leicester)

who have never cause he to feel painfully his dependent situation;

and it have not escape his notice how on several occasion she have

interfere to save he from some trifling annoyance, which her woman’s

tact lead she to feel would be doubly mortify to his proud and

sensitive nature. still he have resolve to make the confession, and with

he to resolve and to do be one and the same thing. another difficulty

which render his task more embarrassing be that, in order to make his

explanation intelligible, he must revert to Lord Bellefield’s insult,

and though at that moment nothing would have give he great

satisfaction than to bestow on that unworthy scion of nobility a sound

horse-whipping, he shrink from the idea of be suppose capable of the

littleness of revenge himself by injure his enemy in the affection

of his betrothed. think, however, be useless; the more he reflect

the more embarrassed do he become, so he plunge at once _in medium re_

by exclaim, “you cannot be aware, Miss Grant, of the pain your word

give I. far from deserve your gratitude, I must implore your pardon

for having nearly sacrifice your life to my unfortunately warm temper

and revengeful feeling; nor shall I again enjoy peace of mind till I

have obtain your forgiveness, should I indeed be fortunate enough to

succeed in do so.”



at this singular address Annie open her large eye and regard she

companion with unmixed astonishment, feel by no mean satisfied that

he have not suddenly take leave of his sense; not heed her surprise,

however, Lewis continue: “in order to make my tale intelligible, I must

revert to an occurrence which I would rather, for many reason, have

leave unmentione; but you will, I hope, do I the justice to believe

that I be actuate by no unworthy motive in allude to it. about a year

ago my favourite dog become entangle whilst swim in the Serpentine

river, and would have be drown if I have not jump in and save

he.”



“I know, I see it all; we be drive in the park at the time,”

 interrupt Annie eagerly.



“as I regain the bank,” resume Lewis, “a gentleman, whom I have since

learn to be your cousin, Lord Bellefield, come up and offer I a sum

of money for the dog. I have not accomplish Faust’s rescue without some

risk, for though I be a good swimmer, my wet clothe keep drag

I down, and I confess the offer of money for an animal I have just

imperille my life to save irritated I, and I return Lord Bellefield

an answer which perhaps he be justify in consider impertinent.

when Mr. Leicester introduce I to his brother, on the day of the

skating-party, it be evident he have not forget this transaction, and

he soon find an opportunity to address I in a style which could only

have be apply to a dependent with safety.”



as he speak these word in a tone of bitter contempt, his eye flash

and his cheek burn, his companion murmur as though she be

think aloud, “it be ungenerous of he, in the extreme.” Lewis

remain silent for a moment, and then continue in a calm voice: “I

be by nature of a lamentably hasty temper, and my impulse would have

lead I to resent Lord Bellefield’s insult on the spot; but many

consideration withhold I, and still possess by angry feeling, I

join the party on the lake. after the ice have give way, while I be

assist those who cling to the edge to scramble out, I first become

aware that you be in the water, and I be about to jump in and swim

to your assistance when, by some ill luck, your cousin approach in

a state of great excitement and order I authoritatively to ‘save my

master’s daughter.’”



“oh, how could he say such a thing!” exclaim Annie indignantly.



“as he speak,” resume Lewis, “some evil spirit seem to take

possession of I, and, to annoy he, I bow and draw back, say,

‘your lordship must excuse I--I be no squire of dame;’ add that of

course he would rescue you himself. from the irritation produce by

my reply I discover that his lordship be unable to swim, and have

reason to suppose your safety be especially important to he, the

fiendish idea cross my mind that by leave you to perish I could

revenge myself on he more effectually than by any other mean.”



“how could you be so unjust, so cruel, even in idea?” interrupt Annie

reproachfully,--“I, who have never injure you in thought, word, or

deed; but you be madden at the time, and know not what you do.”



“I must indeed have be mad,” exclaim Lewis, completely overcome by

the kindness of these last word, “when I could even for a moment

forget the gentle courtesy with which you have always treat I--the

consideration--the----” he pause abruptly and press his hand to

his forehead as if to shut out some hateful vision, a relaxation of

vigilance of which the near-side horse take advantage to shy at its

own shadow and break into a canter, which manouvre restore Lewis’s

self-possession in an instant, the rein be again tighten, and the

culprit admonish, by a sharp stroke of the whip, that he be not to

indulge in such caprice for the future, ere his driver resume: “I have

scarcely form the idea you so justly stigmatise as cruel, when the

atrocity of the act flash across I, and as Lord Bellefield run off

to procure a boat, I spring into the water and swam towards you. imagine

then the agony of mind with which I perceive that you would sink before

I could reach you! at that moment I feel what it be to be a murderer!

the rest of the tale you have no doubt hear from other--how it please

the Almighty to permit the instinct of my noble dog to become the

instrument by which you be save from death, and I from a life of

remorse, to which death itself would have be preferable. of this you

be already aware; it only remain for I to add that if the deep

self-abhorrence, the most sincere repentance for the past may weigh

with you, you will forgive I the wrong I meditate.” at this moment the

sound of horse’ foot cantering give notice that General Grant be about

to effect a junction with the main body, and Annie reply hastily, “as

far as I have anything to forgive, Mr. Arundel, I do so most heartily.

if for a moment you think of allow my life to be sacrifice, you

risk your own to save it immediately afterwards, so that I remain your

debtor, even put to-day’s adventure out of the account--for I fully

believe papa and I be in a fair way to break our neck, though he

would not allow it.”



“well, Annie,” remark the General, ride up to his daughter’s side,

“you don’t appear to be frighten now.”



“no, papa,” be the reply, “there be nothing to be alarm at; the

horse go as quietly as possible.”



“ah! I think I have pretty well tame they,” return the General

triumphantly. “you scarcely find they at all difficult to restrain now,

Mr. Arundel, I presume.”



“they do pull a little strongly even yet, sir,” return Lewis quietly;

“that glove be whole when I take the rein.” as he speak he hold up his

leave hand and disclose two large rent cause by the friction.



“Hum!” reply the General, slightly disconcert. “well, you have

drive they very steadily; don’t hurry they, take they in cool. Walter

and I will precede you and explain how this adventure come about.” so

say he give his horse the rein, and he and Walter canter on.



“Lord Bellefield have behave abominably,” observe Annie abruptly, after

they have proceed for some distance in silence; “he ought to apologise

to you, and I have a great mind to make he do so.”



“do not think of such a thing,” return Lewis hastily. “if I can read

his character, Lord Bellefield be a very proud man, and to one whom he

consider his inferior he could not bring himself to apologise; nor, on

calmly review my own conduct, can I entirely acquit myself of having

give he cause of offence. in my manner towards he I have show too

plainly my forgetfulness of our difference of station. feel that the

son of one who be a soldier, a man of old family, and a gentleman in

the high sense of the word, be any man’s equal, I overlook the

distinction between the heir to a peerage and a poor tutor, and

I treat Lord Bellefield, as I would any other man whose manner

displease I, cavalierly, without consider, or indeed care, in

what light my conduct might appear to he. this error I be resolve to

avoid for the future, and if he will on his part forbear further insult,

it be all I desire. believe I,” add Lewis in a tone which carry

conviction with it, “I do not undervalue your kindness in advocate my

cause, but I would not have you suffer further annoyance on my account;

so if you have really forgive I, you will well show it by forget

the whole matter as speedily as possible.”



Annie shake her head as though she consider such a termination to the

affair highly improbable, merely reply, “perhaps you be right in

think I should do more harm than good by my interference; at all

event, I will be guide in the matter by your wish. and now, Mr.

Arundel,” she continue, “let I say what I have often wish, but have

never be able to find an opportunity to tell you before, and that be,

that as long as you be with we--not that I mean to limit it only to

that time--I hope you will regard I as a friend. I have hear from my

cousin Charles an outline of the circumstance through which my father

be fortunate enough to secure your valuable assistance for poor Walter,

and I can well conceive how greatly you must feel the loss of the

society of your mother and sister.”



“I know not how to thank you for such unexampled kindness; you be

indeed return good for evil,” reply Lewis warmly. he pause for a

moment, as if he be consider how good he might express his meaning,

then add, “as far as may be, I shall most gladly avail myself of the

privilege of your friendship. I cannot tell you the weight you have

take off my mind by this convincing proof of your forgiveness. you may

imagine how exquisitely painful, know how little I deserve they,

be all the civil speech people consider it necessary to make I

on my ‘gallant conduct,’ as they term it; as if there be anything

wonderful in swim a few yard to save a life!--the wonder would be

for any man who could swim _not_ to do so.”



“and yet, think thus lightly of the peril, you tell I you be so

carry away by your angry feeling as to hesitate whether or not to

leave I to perish,” return Annie reflectively. “how strange that

the mind can be engross by passion so completely as to banish all its

natural impulse!”



“you will laugh at I, and think my german education have fill my brain

with strange, wild fancy,” reply Lewis; “but I believe that we

be under a species of demoniacal possession at such moment--that by

indulge our evil feeling instead of resist they we have give

Satan additional power over we. you know the legend of the Wild

huntsman: I cannot but look upon the description of the spirit-rider

who accompany the baron, one on a white, the other on a black steed,

and alternately ply he with good and evil counsel, less as an

allegory than a reality.”



“you believe, then, that we be constantly surround by spiritual

being imperceptible to our bodily sense?” ask Annie. “it be rather a

fearful idea.”



“believe,” return Lewis, “be perhaps too strong a term to apply to

any theory not distinctly bear out by Holy Writ, but as far as I

have study the subject, I think the existence of spiritual being

of opposite nature, some good, some evil, be clearly indicate by

Scripture; and there be many passage which would lead one to suppose

that they be permit, under certain restriction, to interest

themselves in mundane affair, and influence the thought which be the

spring of human action--immaterial agent, in fact, for work out

the will of God. nor do I see anything fearful in the idea; on the

contrary, as we cannot doubt that it be our own fault if the evil

spirit ever prevail against we, and that good angel witness our

struggle to do right, and be at hand to assist we, I consider the

theory a most consolatory one.”



“I never look at the subject in this light before,” observe Annie

thoughtfully. “of course, like most other people, I have a vague,

visionary kind of belief in the existence of good angel and evil

spirit, but I never apply the belief practically, never imagine they

have anything to do with I; and yet it seem reasonable that what

you have suggest should be the case. oh! if we could but have our

spiritual eye open so that we could see they, we then should love the

good angel so much, and hate and fear the evil one to such a degree,

that it would be quite easy to act rightly, and impossible to do wrong.”



“I suppose, if our faith be as strong as it should be,” return

Lewis, “we ought so to realise the truth of Christianity that we should

feel as you describe.”



his companion make no reply, but sit for some minute apparently

pursue the train of thought to which his word have give rise. at

length rouse herself, she turn to Lewis, say, with a _naïve_

smile, “we shall be capital friend, I see. I do not know you could

talk so nicely about thing of this kind. I delight in people who give

I new idea--you must teach I German, too, when all this bustle be

over. I shall ask papa to let you do so. I want to learn german above

everything, and to read Schiller, and Goethe, and La Motte Fouqué,

and all sort of people. will you take compassion on my ignorance, and

accept I as a pupil? I shall not be quite as dull as poor Walter, I

hope.”



“I shall be delighted to play Master of the Ceremonies to introduce

you to those of the german author who be well worth know, always

provide that the General approve of my so do,” return Lewis.



“oh! papa will approve,” reply Annie. “he can care nothing about it

one way or another, and whenever that be the case he always let I do

as I like; and as to Aunt Martha--well, there may be some difficulty

with she, I confess, but the most ferocious animal be tame by

kindness, and it’s hard if I cann’t coax she into submission to my will

and pleasure.”



“I flatter myself I have become rather a favourite with Miss Livingstone

since the affair of the horse,” observe Lewis. “I have hear she

describe I as ‘a young man of unusual ability and irreproachable

moral character’ to three distinct set of visitor during the last

week.”



“you’ve catch her tone exactly,” return Annie, laugh; “but it’s

very abominable of you to deride my venerable aunt.”



and so they chat on, Lewis forget alike his proud reserve and

his dependent position in his pleasure in once again meet with the

kindness and sympathy to which he have be so long a stranger, and Annie

engross by the joy with which she perceive the ice that care and

sorrow have freeze round the heart of her young companion melt before the

fascination of her look and manner; and when the phaeton draw up before

the ample portal of Broadhurst, it would have be hard to decide which

of the two feel most sorry that pleasant drive have come so quickly to an

end.



our train still run along the _Railroad of Life_, but a most important

station have be pass when Lewis first arrive at the conclusion that

he have cease to dislike Annie Grant.









CHAPTER XXIII.--DE GRANDEVILLE THREATENS a confidence and elicit

CHARLEY LEICESTER’S idea on MATRIMONY.



it be the morning of Twelfth-day, and in Broadhurst’s ancient mansion

confusion reign supreme; for twelfth-night to be celebrate with

high festivity. a grand ball be about to be give to the county,

and legion of upholsterer’ man have take the house by storm, and be

zealously employ in turn it out of the window. Minerva be

great upon the occasion; starch to the nth, she rustle through the

apartment like an austere whirlwind, strike an icy terror into

the heart of the stout workman, and lead the chief upholsterer

himself the life of a convict felon on the treadmill--solitary

confinement, imply separation from Minerva, would have be a boon

to that harass tradesman. whatever he put up she instantly have take

down; all his suggestion she violently oppose; he never give an order

that she do not contradict; when he be downstairs she require he at

the top of the house; if he appear without his hat, she take he

out of door. Foxe’s Martyrs would seem a mere book of sport beside a

faithful chronicle of all that upholsterer suffer on the occasion

at the hand of Minerva Livingstone. have he not be endow with

remarkable tenacity of life, ere he have set that house in order he would

have die.



amongst other of the dispossess, Charley Leicester, having retreat

from room to room before the invader, at last, fairly drive out, be

fain to seek refuge in the garden. in this extremity he betake himself

to a certain terrace-walk, where he trust to find sunshine and quiet.

have, as he fondly imagine, secure these necessary ingredient to

his happiness, he be proceed to recruit exhausted nature with a mild

cigar, when a footstep be hear approach, and immediately afterwards

the erect and portly form of _the_ De Grandeville hove in sight and bore

down upon he. now it must be know that these two gentleman regard

each other with very different feeling--Leicester, albeit by no mean

give to discover fault of character in his acquaintance, could not

but perceive the absurd self-consequence and pompous pride which be so

palpably display in De Grandeville’s every look and action, and while

this revolt his good taste and produce in he a passive feeling of

dislike, the style of conversation usually adopt by the redoubtable

Marmaduke, which, however it might begin, invariably end in some form

of self-glorification, actively bore he. accordingly, it be with

anything but a feeling of satisfaction that he now witness his

approach. De Grandeville, on the other hand, look up to Leicester on

account of his connection with the peerage, and know his popularity

among the good set of man about town, regard he as an oracle on all

point of _etiquette_ and _bienséance_. be, therefore, at that moment

in the act of revolve in his anxious mind a most weighty matter on

which he require good advice, Charley be the man of all other he most

wish to meet with. Marching vigorously onward he soon reach the

spot where, half-sit, half-lie, on the broad top of a low stone

balustrade, Leicester be ruminate over his cigar. having halt

immediately in front of his victim, De Grandeville raise his hand to

his forehead in a military salute, which manouvre, acquire partly in

jest, partly in earnest, have now become habitual to he.



“Ar--enjoy a weed? eh! Mr. Leicester?” he begin. “ ’pon my word,

you’ve select a most picturesque spot for your bivouac. if it’s not

against the standing order to smoke here, I’ll join you in a cigar,

for--ar--to tell you the truth, I rather want five minute’ conversation

with you.”



“I’m in for it,” think Leicester. “well, what must be, must; the sun

will be off here in about half-an-hour, and I suppose I can endure

he for that space of time.” he only say, however, hold out his

cigar-case languidly, “can I offer you one?”



“Ar--many thank, you’re one of the few man whose taste I can, rely on;

but--ar--really, the thing they sell now, and pretend to call genuine,

be such trash, that--ar--I be force to import my own.

I send out an agent to Cuba express--ar--at least, Robinson, who

supply my club--ar--the Caryatides, you know--send he on a hint from

I, and I cann’t match the cigar he bring I anywhere; I’ve never meet

with anything like they. ask your brother; he know they--ar--I let he

have half a box as the great favour.”



“bell live on cigar and gin-and-water when he’s in his native state,”

 return Leicester, slightly alter his position so that he could rest

his back more conveniently against a statue. “if he’s be go too

fast, and get out of condition, he take a course of that sort of

thing, and it always bring he right again; it’s like turn a screwy

horse out to grass.”



De Grandeville, who have appear somewhat abstract during this

interesting record of the domestic habit of Lord Bellefield,

change the conversation by observe, “Ar--you see, when a man of a

certain--ar--position in society get--ar--towards middle life--ar--say,

three or four-and-thirty, it appear to I that it add very much to his

weight to--ar--to----”



“to drink brown stout instead of pale ale,” exclaim Leicester more

eagerly than his willnot. “I observe you do so at----, when we be

treat the incorruptible elector, and it strike I as a decide

mistake.”



“Ar--yes, I believe--that be, of course--you be right; but that be not

exactly what I be go to observe,” return De Grandeville, slightly

embarrassed. “in fact, I be go to say that it add to a man’s weight

in society, increase his influence, and improve his general position

to be--ar--well marry!”



“about that I scarcely know; it’s not a matter to decide on hastily,”

 return Leicester, coolly light a fresh cigar, which, be of

an obstinate disposition, require much scientific management and

considerable hard puffing to induce it to perform properly. “in

regard to (puff) marriage, Mr. De Grandeville, look at it

philosophically--and I can assure you it’s a subject on which I’ve

expend much (puff, puff) serious thought,--looking at it in a

reasonable businesslike point of view, it become a mere (puff) affair

of debtor and creditor,--a question of what you lose and what you gain.

let we try the matter by various test and see how the account stand.

we’ll begin with the watchword of the day, for instance: ‘liberty,

Fraternity, and equality.’ Liberty,--a single man can do as he like

without consult anybody; a married man can do as he like only when

his wife share the inclination, which, as no two people ever look at

anything in exactly the same point of view, appear a somewhat stringent

restriction. Fraternity,--a single man may choose his friend where he

feel inclined, male or female, as it may have please Providence to

create they; a married man dare not, unless he have a taste for domestic

misery, and possess eye which be nail-proof, cultivate a female

friendship, and somehow one feel if one be married one should not

exactly wish to have a set of man always dangle about one’s house.

Equality,--a single man, if he have receive a gentleman’s education,

wear a good coat, and have wit enough to keep himself warm, be anybody’s

equal; a married man must bear all his wife’s burden as well as his

own, and don’t get ask by the Browns because the Smiths have tell

they her great-grandfather be transport for steal a pewter pot.

now, let we look at the per contra side. a single man soon get tired of

his unlimited Liberty; there’s no fun in have your own way if you’ve

no one to contradict you. a little opposition become a positive luxury,

and this you’re sure to obtain _by_ matrimony. then, as to Fraternity,

friend be well than acquaintance, certainly, just as a mule be

preferable to a jackass, but they’re not much comfort to one after all.

my most intimate friend live in Ceylon and write to I once in five

year about hunt elephant. now, your wife be part of your good and

chattel, belong to you as completely as your bootjack, and when in

hour of indolence you wish to sit with your soul in slipper, she, if

she be worth her salt, be ready to pull off the psychological boot that

be pinch your mind, and prevent the _dolce far niente_ from become

meaningless and insipid. lastly, there’s no such equality in the world

as between husband and wife when they be really suited to each other,

appreciate their relative position justly, and endeavour to make

practice and principle coincide. these be my idea regard the

marriage state, Mr. De Grandeville; but ’tis no use discuss

the matter. Society have long since decide the question in favour of

wedlock, and there be only enough exception to prove the rule. Byron

enunciate a great truth when he declare:



               “‘man be not form to live alone;’



the animal’s gregarious, sir, and the solitary system be totally opposed

to all its taste and habit.”



so say, Charley emit a long puff of smoke, and caress his

whisker, calmly await his companion’s reply; but this demand a fresh

chapter.









CHAPTER XXIV.--RELATES how CHARLEY LEICESTER be first “sprighte by A

FOOL,” then BESET by AN AMAZON.



“Ar--really--‘pon my word, you seem to have study the subject deeply,

Mr. Leicester,” return De Grandeville, who be somewhat astonish

at the length and volubility of Charley’s notable “Essay on Matrimony,”

 with which the last chapter be conclude, and too completely blind by

self-importance to perceive that the other be more or less laugh at

he. “however, the drift of your argument appear in favour of marriage,

and--ar--in fact--ar--I quite think as you do on the matter. now, in my

position, I consider such an arrangement would be most desirable, always

suppose one can meet with--ar--a suitable partner.”



“ay, there’s the rub,” rejoin Leicester, leisurely flip the ashe

from the end of his cigar.



“I consider that I have a right to look--ar--high,” continue De

Grandeville, fold his arm with dignity. “our family date from

the Conquest; our original ancestor come over as equerry to William of

Normandy. I suppose you be aware how the name arise from an incident in

that invasion?”



Leicester profess his ignorance of the anecdote, and De Grandeville

proceed: “my ancestor, who, like most of his descendant, be a

remarkably long-sight individual, be ride near the person of his

liege lord some few day after the victory of Hastings, when at the

extreme verge of the horizon he descry the city of Canterbury, and

in the excitement of the moment he exclaim, point with his mail

hand, ‘_Voila! une grande ville._’ William overhear the remark, and

fix his pierce glance upon he, observe sarcastically, ‘ha! say

thou so? he who hath be the first to discern yon great city should be

the first to enter it.’ ‘by the grace of God, and with your permission,

sire, so I will,’ exclaim my ancestor. William nod assent, my

ancestor clap spur to his horse, and never draw bridle till the

standard of Normandy float on the high tower of Canterbury. for

this gallant exploit he be make governor of the city, and receive the

name and title of De Grandeville. It’s--ar--a creditable story.”



“extremely,” return Leicester, yawning. “I’ve a vague idea the man we

all come from be hang for horse-stealing.”



“Ar--yes--very good,” rejoin De Grandeville, recognise an excellent

jest in his companion’s assertion; “but, as I be about to observe, in

my position a man owe as it be a duty to his family; he ought not to

marry a nobody.”



“decidedly, such a connection should be avoid,” return Charley

sententiously, present the hot end of his cigar to an inquisitive

snail which appear inclined to join the party.



“Ar--the De Grandevilles have be from time immemorial large landed

proprietor,” resume their grandiloquent descendant; “half the county

of---- belong to they; the estate hold by my branch of the family

be immense, and though--ar--just at present they be not exactly in my

possession, yet if anything be to happen to my cousin Hildebrand and

his seven boy, I might be place in--ar--a very different position;

therefore, in look out for a wife, I hold it incumbent on I to

select a lady who would not disgrace a prominent situation, be she

call upon to fill one.”



Leicester (whose attention have be thoroughly engross by the snail,

which, after having make sundry futile attempt to avoid the cigar and

continue its onward course, have at length yield the point, and having

turn round, be now crawl off in an opposite direction) somewhat

astonish his companion by quote with great _empressement_ the word

of the old nursery ballad--



                        “off he set



                        with his opera hat.”



as, however, he immediately afterwards assume a look of the deep

attention, De Grandeville set it down as an instance of the eccentricity

of genius, and continued--“Ar--this, as you must perceive, render

certain qualification essential in the object of my choice. I

could select no one who by birth and position be not perfectly

unexceptionable. I should also require she to possess, in an

eminent degree, the manner of society; another great point would

be--ar----------”



“plenty of tin,” suggest Charley, make a face at the retreating

snail.



“Ar--yes--in my position it would of course be a matter of prudence,

before bring upon myself the expense of a family, to ascertain that

I can command an income sufficient to enable I to mix in the set to

which--ar--in point of fact, I belong.”



“nothing under £3000 a year would suit my book,” reply Leicester,

“£3000 per annum and perfection I might put up with, but £4000 would

be well without an actual angel, and beyond that mark I’d bate an

attach quality in the damsel for every additional £500 in the fund.”



“Ar--I have reason to believe that the income of the lady in regard to

whom I be about to ask your advice exceed the sum you first mention,”

 reply De Grandeville.



“oh, there be then a real _bona fide_ lady in the case--you’ve

positively mark down your bird?” exclaim Leicester. “pray, have I

the honour of her acquaintance?”



“Ar--yes--I have often meet she in your society--in fact, she form one

of the party now domesticate at Broadhurst.”



“stay in the house, eh?” return Charley, feel slightly curious.

“by Jove! who can it be? you’re not go to try and cut out Bellefield

by propose for my cousin Annie, be you? I wish you would, it would

sell Bell so beautifully.”



“of course--ar--you be joke,” return De Grandeville proudly.

“I would not do such a shabby thing by his lordship upon any

consideration.”



Leicester be amuse at the cool way in which his companion seem to

take it for grant that he have only to enter the list against his

brother in order to secure the prize. he keep his entertainment to

himself, however, merely reply, “well, if it ben’t Annie, who be

it? I can scarcely imagine you have set your affection on Miss

Livingstone.”



“the Livingstones be a good old family,” return De Grandeville, “but

the representative of the name to whom you allude would have be a more

suitable match for my late excellent father than for myself. no, sir,

the lady to whom I may probably offer the opportunity of ally herself

to the house of De Grandeville be as suitable in age as in all other

qualification--Miss Peyton be in her two-and-twentieth year.”



“miss how much!” exclaim Leicester impetuously, sit bolt upright

and fling the remnant of his cigar after the snail, which be yet

strive to make good its retreat.



“Miss Laura Peyton,” return De Grandeville; “I don’t wonder you be

surprised. I be aware, as well as yourself, that her grandfather be in

trade. I can assure you that stand in my way for a long time, and it be

not till I have go through the pedigree carefully, with a friend in

the Herald’s College, and clearly trace back the family to the time of

Richard Cour de Lion, that I ever think seriously of the thing.”



“and how do you mean to carry on the campaign?” ask Leicester, who

have by this time recover his composure. “do you intend to lay regular

siege to the young lady’s affection, or be it to be a look-and-die,

‘_veni vidi, vici_’ affair?”



“Ar--really--I be scarcely sanguine enough to hope to carry the citadel

by a _coup-de-maim_,” return De Grandeville; “but my tactic will be

very much regulate by those of my fair enemy at present. if I might

judge by one or two slight skirmish we have have together, the garrison

will not hold out to extremity when once the breastwork be take, and

the place properly invest.” at this moment a servant approach De

Grandeville with a message from General Grant request his presence.

“Ar--yes--say I’ll attend the General immediately,” be the reply; then,

as the servant depart, De Grandville continue, “Ar--the course of

true love never do run smooth, you see, Mr. Leicester. Ar--I shall have

an opportunity of speak to you again on this matter, and hear

your opinion more in full; at present I must wish you good morning.” so

say, he slightly raise his hat in salutation, and march off in a

great state of dignified self-complacency.



Leicester watch he till he be out of sight; then, spring from

his seat, he begin pace up and down the terrace with hasty

stride, mutter from time to time such uncomplimentary remark as,

“insufferable puppy!”



“Conceited ass!” all of which evidently bear reference to his late

companion. having let off a little of his extra steam by this mean, he

give vent to the following soliloquy: “well, I’m nicely in for it this

time! because a love affair, with the chance of possible consequence,

ben’t trouble enough, I must have a rival step in--and such a

rival--why, the very sight of that man disagree with I; and then

to hear he talk, it’s positively sicken! I’ll be off to London

to-morrow morning; and yet I do like the girl,--I know I do, because it

be continually occur to I that I be not half good enough for she.

I suppose she look upon I as a mere fortune-hunter--think I only care

about she for the sake of her money. I wish she haven’t a farthing!

I wish--eh! what be I talk about? Heigho! that’s another curse

of poverty: a poor devil like I cann’t even afford the luxury of a

disintereste attachment. then that man--that De Grandeville--to hear an

animal like that debate whether she be good enough for he! I declare

he’s make I feel quite feverish! I’d no idea it be possible for

_anything_ to excite I to such a degree. if the notion be not too

preposterous, I should really begin to fancy I must be fall in love!

she never can have the bad taste to like he--in fact, there’s nothing

to like in he--and yet the fellow seem confident; but that be

the nature of the brute. though I don’t know, woman be such fool

sometimes, she might take he at his own price--that military swagger of

his might go down with some of the sex. once let a woman fancy a man

to be a hero, or a martyr, or a patriot, or any other uncomfortable

celebrity certain to make a bad husband, and she will be ready to throw

herself at his head,--just as if such a fellow be not the very last

man in the world whom she ought to select! I suppose it’s the

additional odd in favour of widowhood that constitute the great

attraction--female be naturally capricious. well, I shall try and take

the matter easily, at all event. I dare say it willn’t break my heart

whichever way it go. I shall make observation, and if she really have

the bad taste to prefer this man, he’s welcome to she--a woman who could

love he would never do for my wife; that one fact would argue an amount

of incompatibility of temper which would be furnish work for doctor’

Commons before the first year’s connubial infelicity be over. I

wonder whether there’s any lunch go on; it’s astonish how thirsty

anything of this kind make I! pale ale I must have, or _mit colum!_”

 and having arrive at this conclusion, he thrust his hand--of whose

delicate appearance he be especially careful--into his pocket to

preserve they from the cold, and stroll off to put his resolution into

practice.



in the meantime, Marmaduke De Grandeville, while listen with his

outward ear to General Grant’s dull electioneering detail, be

inwardly congratulate himself on the favourable impression he have make

on that very sensible young man, the Honourable Charles Leicester, and

think what a useful ally he have secure to assist he in carry out

his matrimonial project.



verily, there be as many comedy perform off the stage as upon it!



the ball at Broadhurst take place on the evening of the day on which the

above conversation have pass, and be a wonderful affair indeed. it

be give for a special purpose, and that purpose be to conciliate

everybody, and induce everybody to promise General Grant their vote and

interest at the ensue election. accordingly, everybody be invite--at

least everybody who have the slight pretension to be anybody--and

everybody come; and as almost everybody bring somebody else with they,

a wife, or a daughter, or the young lady from London who be spend

Christmas with they, there be no lack of guest. the object of the

entertainment be no secret; and the king of the county, the Marquis

of C----------, be in the conservative interest, and consequently

anxious to secure the General’s return, not only come himself, but

actually bring a real live duke with he to exhibit to the company.

this be a great stroke of policy, and tell immensely, particularly

with the small anybodie who be almost nobody, but who, having

associate with a duke, straightway become somebody, and remain so

ever after. moreover, in all case of incipient radicalism, chartist

tendency, or socialist symptom, his grace be an infallible specific.

depend on it, there be no well remedy for a certain sort of democracy

than a decoction of strawberry-leave; apply that to the sore place and

the patient instantly become sound in his opinion, and continue a

healthy member of the body politic. the particular duke on the occasion

in question be a very young one, little more than a boy in fact (if

a duke can ever be consider in the light of a boy). this youthful

nobleman have a lead idea--though you would hardly have suppose it,

to look at he--he believe that he be the good match in England; and

so, in the conventional sense of the term, he undoubtedly be, although

he would have be very dear at the price to any woman with a head and

a heart. his pastor and master, back by the maternal anxiety of

a duchess unambitious of the dignity of dowagership, have sedulously

cultivate this one idea till it have assume the character of

a monomania, under the influence of which this unhappy scion of

aristocracy look upon life as a state of perpetual warfare against the

whole race of woman, and be haunt by a frightful vision of himself

carry off and forcibly married to the chief of a horde of female

pirate, with long tongue, long nail, and an utter absence of

creditable ancestry. his outward duke (if we may be allow the

expression) be decidedly prepossess. he be tall and not ungraceful

in figure, and have a bright, round, innocent face, as of a good child.

his hair be nicely brush and part; whisker he have none; indeed,

the stinginess of nature to he in this particular be so remarkable,

that, as the eld Miss Simpkins afterwards observe to an eager

audience of uninvited young sister, “so far from whisker, my dear,

now I come to think of it, his grace have _rather the reverse!_” however,

take he “for all in all,” he be a very creditable young duke, and a

perfect godsend on the occasion in question. then there be a descending

scale from his Grace downward, lead through the aristocracy of birth

to the aristocracy of rich, till it reach the _élite_ of the country

town, and the more presentable specimen of yeomen farmer. but let

we join a group of people that we know, and hear what they think of the

guest who be so rapidly assemble.



in a snug corner of the reception-room, not far from a door lead into

the large drawing-room, stand one of those mysterious innovation of

modern upholstery, a species of the genus ottoman, which resemble a

Brobdignagian mushroom, with a thimble make to match stick in the middle

of it. seat at her ease upon this nondescript, half-bury by the

yield cushion, appear the pretty figure of Laura Peyton; by

her side, attire in much white muslin, crinoline to a balloon-like

rotundity, but which apparently have shrink abominably at the wash in the

region round about its wearer’s neck and shoulder, sit another--well,

from the juvenility of her dress and manner we suppose we must say

_young_ lady, though it be a historical fact that she have be at

school with Annie Grant’s mother; but then poor Mrs. Grant marry when

she be quite a child, and die before she be thirty, and of course

Miss Singleton must know her own age well, and she have declare herself

eight-and-twenty for the last five year. this lady possess one

peculiarity--she always have a passion for somebody; whether the _object_

be of the gentler or the sterner sex be all a matter of chance; but

as she be in the habit of observe, “there exist in her nature a

necessity for passionately loving.” and it have become proverbial that

necessity have no law. the object of her adoration just at present be

“that darling girl,” Laura Peyton; and really that young lady be in

herself so lovable, that to endeavour to account for Miss Singleton’s

devotion by insinuate that the heiress be usually surround by

all the most desirable young man in the room would be the height of

ill-nature.



“dear I!” exclaim Miss Singleton, whose troublesome nature have

another necessity for like to hear its own voice as often as possible.

“dear I! I wish I know who all the people be! dearest Miss Peyton,

do not you sympathise? ah, that tell-tale smile! we girl certainly be

sadly curious, though I believe the man be just as bad, only they’re

too proud to own it. but really, we must contrive to catch somebody who

will tell we who everybody be. there’s that handsome, grave, clever

Mr. Arundel: I shall make he a sign to come here--ah! he see I

directly--he _be_ so clever. Mr. Arundel, do tell I, who _be_ all

these people?”



“rather a comprehensive question,” return Lewis, smile; “moreover,

you could scarcely have apply to any one less able to answer it, for

beyond our immediate neighbour I really do not know a dozen people in

the room.”



“Mr. Arundel’s acquaintance lie rather among illustrious foreigner,”

 observe Miss Peyton demurely. “be any member of the royal family of

Persia present, for instance, his intimate knowledge of the language,

manner, and habit of that interesting nation would be invaluable to

we.”



“as you be strong, be merciful,” return Lewis, in a tone of voice

only to be hear by the young lady to whom he speak.



“dear I! how very delightful! what a thing it be to be so clever!”

 exclaim Miss Singleton, arrange her bracelet and round her arm

(which be now one of her good point) with an action that express,

as plainly as word could have do, “there, look at that--there’s

grace for you!”



“here come some one who can tell we everything,” she continue; “that

good-natured, fascinating Mr. Leicester, with his love of whisker all

in dear little curl. tiresome man! he willn’t look this way. would you be

so very good, Mr. Arundel, as to follow he and bring he here? say that

Miss Peyton and I want he particularly.”



“I beg you’ll say nothing of the kind, Mr. Arundel,” interpose Laura

quickly, with a very become blush. “really, Miss Singleton, you run on

so that----”



“I will deliver your message verbatim, Miss Singleton,” return Lewis

with the same demure tone and manner in which Miss Peyton have refer

to the persian prince; and without wait to mark the effect of his

word, he mingle with the crowd, and almost immediately return

with the gentleman in pursuit of whom he have be despatch. Charles

Leicester, who be most elaborately get up for the occasion, though his

good taste prevent he from run into any absurd extreme in dress,

look remarkably handsome, and be flatter by the summon he

have just receive, particularly happy. both these fact Miss Peyton

discover at a glance, but whether urge by some secret consciousness,

or annoy by an indescribable look of intelligence which lurk in the

corner of Lewis’s dark eye and reveal itself through the sternness

of his compress lip, she receive he with marked coldness, and

observe, in reply to his offer to play showman to the collection of

strange animal there assemble, that she have no taste for zoology, and

that it be Miss Singleton’s curiosity he have be summon to satisfy.



“yes, indeed, Mr. Leicester,” exclaim that mature damsel, in no way

daunt by a shade of discontent which, despite his endeavour to

the contrary, overspread the countenance of the gentleman she be

address; “yes, indeed, I’m die to know all sort of thing. in

the first place, who’s that tall, stout gentleman in the wonderful

waistcoat?”



“that,” reply Leicester, coolly examine the person indicate, “that

be--no, it ben’t! yes, surely!--I think I be right--that be the

Marquis of Carabbas.” then see from her manner she do not recognise

the name, he continue, “he have enormous estate situate in----”



“where?” ask Miss Singleton earnestly, think she have lose the name.



“that interesting tract of country clepe, by John Parry, the Realms of

Infantine Romance,” continue Leicester.



“oh, Mr. Leicester, you’re laugh at I. how wicked of you--the

Marquis of Carabbas! let I see: haven’t he something to do with

Whittington and his Cat?”



“with the cat, possibly,” reply Leicester; “for if my memory fail not,

the fortune of the noble Marquis, like those of the ever-to-be-lament

Lord Mayor of London town, be the result of feline sagacity, and it’s

not likely there exist two such talented cat--even Puss in Boots may

only be another episode in the career of the same gifted individual.”



“another of its nine life, in fact,” suggest Lewis.



“yes, of course,” rejoin Leicester. “I dare say it be the original

‘cat of nine tale,’ only, like the sibylline leave, several of the

manuscript have be lose to posterity through the carelessness of some

elfin Master of the Rolls.”



“I beg your pardon, but I really must interrupt you,” exclaim Miss

Singleton. “can you tell I, soberly and seriously, who that very

strange-look person may be who have just seize the General’s hand and

nearly shake his arm out of the socket?”



see that Laura Peyton’s eye ask the same question, though her

lip be silent, Leicester glance in the direction indicate, and

immediately reply, “that energetic female rejoice in the name of

Lady Mary--but be more commonly know among her intimate as

_Jack_--Goodwood. in person she be what you behold she; in character,

she present a most unmitigated speciman of the _genus_ Amazon; for the

rest, she be a very good woman at heart, but my especial torment; she

always call I Charley, and her usual salutation be a slap on the back.

she hunt, shoot, break in her own horse, have ride a hurdle race,

in which she come in a good second, and be report to have dragoon

her husband into pop the question by the threat of a sound

horse-whipping. and now, Miss Singleton, you’ll have an opportunity of

judge for yourself, for she have catch sight of I, and be bear

down upon we in full sail.”



“well, but be she really a lady?” inquire the astonish Miss

Singleton, who, in her philosophy, have most assuredly never dream of

such a possibility as Jack Goodwood.



“she be second daughter of Lord Oaks,” be the reply, “and Goodwood be

one of _the_ goodwood, and be worth some £8000 a year; but here she

be.”



as he speak the lady in question join the group. her age might be

eight or nine-and-thirty; she be tall and decidedly handsome, though

her feature be too large; she have magnificent black eye and very

white tooth, which prevent the width of her mouth from interfere

with her pretension to beauty; her complexion be brilliant in the

extreme, nature having bestow on she a clear brown skin, which

withstand the combined effect of exposure to sun and wind, and soften

the high colour induce by the boisterous character of her ladyship’s

favourite pursuit. but if her personal gift be strike, the style

or costume she see fit to adopt render she still more remarkable. as

it will be necessary to describe her dress minutely in order to convey

any idea of her appearance, we throw ourselves on the mercy of our lady

reader, and beg they to pardon all error of description, see that

mantua-making be a science in which we have never graduate, and of

which our knowledge be derive solely from oral tradition pick up

during desultory conversation among our female friend, usually hold

(if our memory fail we not) on their way home from church.



her dress consist, then, of a gown of exceedingly rich white silk,

make half-high in the body and remarkably full in the skirt, over which

she wear a polka of bright scarlet Cashmere line and trim with white

silk, and adorn with a double row of the hunt button. her head be

attire in a spanish hat of black velvet, while a single white feather,

secure by a valuable diamond clasp, be allow to droop over the

brim and mingle with the rich masse of her raven hair, which be

picturesquely arrange in a complication of braid and ringlet. she

lean on the arm of a gentleman double her age, whose good-humour

heavy face afford a marked contrast to the ever-vary expression

that light the animate feature of she who be, in every sense of the

word, his well half. Leicester’s description have but slightly enhance

the vigour of her mode of salutation, for as she reach the spot where

he stand she clap he on the shoulder with a small, white-gloved

hand, exclaim in a deep but not unmusical voice--



“Bravo, Charley! run you to earth at last, you see. where have you

hide yourself all this age? now, Goody,” she continue, turn to her

husband, “you may go. Charley Leicester will take care of I--don’t lose

your temper at whist, don’t drink too much champagne, and mind you’re

forthcoming when I want you.”



“there’s a life to lead,” return her spouse, appeal to Leicester.

“do you ever see such a tyrant?”



“be off, Goody, and don’t talk nonsense,” be his lady-wife’s rejoinder.



[illustration: 0178]



“how be it we never see you at the Manor-House now?” begin the master of

that establishment in a hospitable tone of voice, but his lady cut he

short in his speech by exclaiming--



“why? because he find you such a bore he could not stand you any

long; nobody can except I, and even my power of endurance be

limited, so,” she continue, take he by the shoulder and turn he

round, “right about face--head up--march. _Voilà_,” she add, turn

to Leicester, “he’s famously under command, ben’t he, Charley? all my

good breaking in--he be as obstinate as a mule before I marry he,

nobody could do anything with he. he’s in splendid condition, too, for

a man of sixty. I’ll back he to walk, ride, hunt, shoot, or play at

billiard with any man of his age and weight in the three kingdom. I’ve

be oblige to dock his corn, though; there be seldom a day that he

don’t finish his second bottle of port. he only drink one now. but I

say, Charley, about this election of Governor Grant’s, how be he go

the pace? you must tell I all about it; I’ve be in Paris for the last

two month, and I’m quite in the dark.”



“’pon my word, I take so little interest in the matter that I can

scarcely enlighten you, Lady Mary,” return Leicester, glance

uneasily at Miss Peyton, who be talk with much apparent

_empressement_ to Miss Singleton, though her quick ear drink in every

word speak by the other.



“who’s that girl?” resume Lady Mary, lower her voice a little

(_very_ little) as she perceive the direction of Leicester’s glance.

“Miss Peyton, eh?” she continue, “you shall introduce I; but first

tell I who’s that man by her side, like an old picture.”



“Mr. Arundel,” be the reply; “tutor to poor young Desborough.”



“he’s too good for the work,” return Jack; “he’s too near thoroughbred

to take to collar and keep his trace tight with such an uphill pull as

that must be. I say, Charley,” she continue in a half whisper, “he’s

handsomer than you be. if you don’t mind your play, he’ll bowl you out

and win with the favourite--there, it’s no use get up the steam or

look sulky with I,” she add, as Leicester utter an exclamation

of annoyance. “I can see it all with half an eye; you’re as thoroughly

what Goody call ‘spoony’ as a man need to be; but now, Charley, don’t

go put your foot in it, you know: be it all right with the tin?

that’s the main question.”



“ask I to dance, for pity’s sake, and let I get out of that creature’s

way,” murmur Laura Peyton to Lewis; “I never have a taste for see

monster.”



Lewis smile and offer she his arm. at the same moment De Grandcville,

gaudily ornate, march up and request the honour of Miss Peyton’s

hand for the set then form.



“I be engage to Mr. Arundel for the next quadrille,” return Miss

Peyton.



“for the follow one, then--ar?”



“I shall have much pleasure,” be the reply. “in the meantime allow I

to introduce you to my friend Miss Singleton, who be at present without

a partner.”



De Grandeville, charm to have the opportunity of oblige Miss Peyton,

act on the hint, and the two couple hasten to take their place
