continued chatter, heedless of his evident annoyance, until she have

worry he into a state of mind border on distraction, when some

fresh fancy seize she, she fasten herself on to a new victim and

leave he to his meditation. these be by no mean of an agreeable

character; and after wander listlessly through the suite of room and

watch Laura Peyton, as during the interval of the dance she talk

and laugh gaily with De Grandeville (an occupation which do not tend

greatly to raise Leicester’s spirit or soothe his ruffled temper),

he stroll into a card-room tenant only by four elderly gentleman

immerse in a rubber of whist, and fling himself on a vacant sofa

in a remote corner of the apartment, give himself up to gloomy

retrospection.



he have not remain there long when Lewis enter and glance round as

if in search of some one; then approach Leicester, he began--



“you’ve not see Walter lately, have you? your amusing friend, Lady

Mary Goodwood” (“confound the jade,” mutter Leicester, _sotto voce_)

“introduce herself to I just now, and having captivate Walter by

her bright smile and scarlet jacket, carry he off, to tease I,

I believe, and I cann’t tell what she have do with he. but,” he

continue, for the first time observe his companion’s dejected manner

and appearance, “be anything the matter; you’re not ill, I hope?”



“I wish I be,” be the unexpected reply; “ill--dead--anything rather

than the miserable fool I am----”



“why, what have occur?” ask Lewis anxiously. “can I be of any use?”



“no, it’s past mending,” return Leicester in an accent of deep

dejection. he pause, then turn to Lewis he resume almost fiercely:

“the tale be soon tell, if you want to hear it. I meet that girl--Laura

Peyton, I mean--in town about a year ago; in fact--for my affair be

no secret--every fool know that I be a beggar, or thereabout. I be

introduce to she because she be a great heiress, and dangle after

she through the whole of a London season for the sake of her three

per cent. well, last autumn I meet she again down in Scotland; we be

stay together for three week in the same house. of course we see a

good deal of each other, and I soon find I like she well for herself

than I have ever do for her money; but somehow, as soon as this feeling

arose, I lose all nerve, and could not get on a bit. the idea of the

meanness of marry a woman for the sake of her fortune haunt I day

and night, and the more I care for she the less be I able to show

it. my cousin Annie perceive what be go on, it seem, and without

say a word to I of her intention, strike up a friendship with Laura,

and invite she here; and somehow--the thing’s very absurd in a man like

myself, who have see everything and do everything, and find out

what humbug it all be--but the fact of the matter be, that I’m just as

foolishly and romantically and deeply in love with that girl as any

raw boy of seventeen could be; and I don’t believe she care one _sous_

about I in return. she think, as she have a good right to do, that I be

hunt she for her money, like the rest of they, I dare say; and--stop

a minute,” he continue, see Lewis be about to speak--“you have

not hear the bad yet: because all I’ve tell you be not enough,

that conceite ass, De Grandeville, must need come and consult I this

morning as to whether Miss Peyton be worthy of be honour with his

hand, hint pretty plainly that he do not anticipate much difficulty

on the lady’s part; and by Jove, from the way in which she be go on

with he this evening, I believe that for once he ben’t lie: then

that mad-head Mary Goodwood come and bother with she confound

‘Charley’ this and ‘Charley’ that, and her absurd plan of monopolise

one--of course she mean no harm; she have know I from a boy, and it’s

her way; besides, she really be attach to old Goodwood. but how be

Laura Peyton to know all that?”



“why, rouse up, and go and tell she yourself, to be sure,” reply

Lewis.



“no, not I!” return Leicester moodily. “I’ll have no more trouble

about it. I’ll leave this house to-morrow morning, and be off to Baden,

or Naples, or timbuctoo, or some place where there be no woman, if

such a Paradise exist--and she may marry De Grandeville, or whom she

please, for I. you see, it would be different if she care at all for

I, but to worry one’s heart out about a girl who do not even like

one----”



“_Halte là!_” interrupt Lewis; “looker-on see most of the game; and

if I know anything of woman’s nature”--he pause and bite his lip as the

recollection of Gretchen cross his mind--“depend upon it, Miss Peyton

be not as indifferent to you as you imagine.”



“do you see how coldly she receive I to-night?” urge Leicester.



“yes; and her so do only confirm my previous opinion. that

chatter Miss Singleton have annoy she by bid I summon you

in Miss Peyton’s name; but the very fact of her annoyance show

consciousness; have she be indifferent to you she would not have care.

then her irritation at Lady Mary’s familiarity prove the same thing.”



“you really think so?” return Leicester, brighten up. “my dear

fellow, you’ve quite put new life into I. it’s very odd now, I never

see it in that light before. what would you have I do, then?”



“if, as you say, you really and truly love she,” return Lewis gravely,

“lie aside--excuse my plain speaking--lie aside your fashionable air,

which disguise your true nature, and tell she of your affection in a

simple, manly way, and if she be the girl I take she to be, your trouble

will not be waste.” so say, he rise and quit the room, leave

Leicester to reflect on his advice.









chapter XXV.--CONTAINS a MYSTERIOUS INCIDENT, and show how the course

of true love never do RUN SMOOTH.



as Lewis, after the conversation detail in the last chapter, be

Livingstone, be enjoy, in company with a young lady friend, the

dangerous luxury of stand by an open window. the moment she perceive

Lewis she advance towards he and began--



“may I detain you one moment, Mr. Arundel? can you tell I anything of

my cousin Charles? I’m afraid he must be ill, and I wish he to exert

himself so particularly to-night.”



“he be not ill,” return Lewis. “I leave he not two minute since in

the card-room.”



“in the card-room?” repeat Annie in a tone of annoyance; “what can he

be do there? be he play whist?”



“no,” be the reply; “he do not appear in a humour to enjoy the

dancing, and have go there for the sake of quiet.”



“a fit of his incorrigible idleness, I suppose,” remark Annie

pettishly; “really it be too provoking; it must seem so odd his

absent himself on such an occasion as this. would you mind

the trouble of return and tell he I want to speak to he

particularly, and that he will find I here?”



“I shall be most happy; it be no trouble,” begin Lewis. he pause,

and then add in a low tone, “perhaps you scarcely do Mr. Leicester

justice in attribute his absence to a fit of indolence; I fancy,

from his manner, something have occur to annoy he.”



“something to annoy he!” exclaim Annie, start and turn pale

as a disagreeable possibility suddenly occur to she. “surely he have

not?--she never can have----!” then see Lewis’s glance fix on she

with a look of peculiar intelligence, she pause abruptly, and a most

become blush overspread her feature. Lewis pity her confusion, and

hasten to relieve it by observing--



“if I have venture to guess the direction of your thought somewhat too

boldly, Miss Grant, you must pardon I, and believe that do I not think

I might thereby in some slight degree repay the kindness Mr. Leicester

have invariably show I, I would not have allow you to perceive it.

if,” he add in a low tone, “you will permit I to advise you, I

believe you could most effectually serve your cousin’s interest by

explain to Miss Peyton, at your first opportunity, the nature of the

friendship which exist between Lady Mary Goodwood and Mr. Leicester,

mention at the same time the fact that they have know each other

from childhood.”



“that’s the difficulty, be it?” rejoin Annie. “oh! I can set that

right in five minute. thank you very much, Mr. Arundel--how extremely

kind you be; but,” she add with an arch look, “you be most

alarmingly clever; I shall become quite afraid of you.” then turn to

her companion, she add, “now, Lucy dear, you will catch your death of

cold standing at that window. you will send Charles Leicester, then, Mr.

Arundel.” so say, she link her arm in that of her friend, and the

two girl leave the room.



“Leicester’s a lucky dog to have such a zealous advocate in that sweet

cousin of his,” think Lewis as he retrace his step towards

the card-room. “she be a great deal too good for that brute, Lord

Bellefield; she have well have choose Charles, if she must marry either

brother, though he be scarcely she equal in mind or depth of character,

and without that I don’t believe married life can ever progress as it

should do.” on reach the card-room he find it only tenant by the

whist player; and rightly imagine that his advice have so far restore

Leicester’s spirit as to induce he again to return to the ball-room,

he resume his search for Walter, and at length discover he in the

ice-room, where, under the auspex of a pretty, interesting look

girl, the daughter of one of the tenantry, call in on the occasion

to assist the female servant, he be regale himself with unlimited

cake.



while Lewis be gently insinuate the possibility of his having have

enough, two or three man, amongst whom be Lord Bellefield, lounge into

the room and begin eat ice at a table opposite that at which Lewis

and Walter be station. one of the party, who be unacquainted with

Lewis, apparently strike by his appearance, address Lord Bellefield

in an undertone, evidently inquire who the young tutor might be; the

answer, though speak in a low voice, be (whether designedly or not we

will not say) perfectly audible to the person to whom it relate.



“that? oh, some poor devil old Grant have pick up cheap as dry-nurse to

his pet idiot; a kind of male _bonne_, as the french term it; a sort of

upper servant, half valet, half tutor. you need not notice he.”



there be a degree of littleness in this speech which completely rob

it of its sting. it be such a mean attempt at an insult that Lewis see

it would be let himself down even to feel angry about it; and merely

allow his lip to curl slightly with a contemptuous smile, he fold

his arm and patiently await the conclusion of Walter’s repast. after

Lord Bellefield and his friend have devour as many ice as seem good

the spot where Lewis stand, Lord Bellefield, in draw out his

be still walk on, when Lewis, after a moment’s hesitation, resolve

to adhere to his determination of treat Lord Bellefield as he would

any other man his superior in rank, and perhaps inwardly rejoice at the

opportunity of return good for evil, or at least civility for insult,

stoop and pick up the glove, then advance a step or two, he

present it to its owner, saying--



“excuse my interrupt your lordship, but you have drop your glove.”



now it so happen that the moment before Lewis have remove his own

glove to render some assistance to Walter, and have not replace it when

he extend his hand to Lord Bellefield, who, without make any reply,

sign to his french valet, then assist in the champagne department,

and when he approach, said--



“_Tenez, Antoine!_ take the glove from this gentleman, and bring I a

clean pair.”



the insolence of his look and the affected drawl in which he spoke

render his meaning so unmistakable, that, after a slight attempt to

repress the inclination, one of his companion burst into a laugh, while

the other, who have sufficient good feeling to be disgust at such an

unprovoked insult, turn on his heel and walk away. Lewis stand for a

suppress anger; still it be evident that he be strive to master

his passion, and apparently he be in great measure successful, for when

he speak it be in a low, calm voice.



“be I to understand,” he say, “that your lordship, consider this

glove pollute by the accident of my having touch it, will never wear

it again?”



“ya--as,” be the reply; “you may very safely come to that conclusion

without any fear of misinterpret my intention.”



“in that case,” continue Lewis, in the same low, clear voice, though

his eye, which be fix on Lord Bellefield’s, actually glow with

the intensity of his emotion, “I will crave your permission to retain it

as a memorial of this evening. your lordship will observe it be a

_right hand_ glove. I may, on some future occasion, have the pleasure

of call your attention to the care with which I have preserve the

relic.”



so say, he bow coldly, and still hold the glove with a vice-like

grasp, as though he fear to have it wrest from he, he turn away

without wait a reply.



“what on earth do the fellow want with that glove?” inquire Lord

Bellefield’s companion, who, not be a particularly intellectual young

gentleman, have be greatly mystify by the whole proceeding. “and what

in the world be the matter with you?” he add, observe for the first

time that his friend be look strangely pale and shudder slightly.



“eh--come along--we’re stand in a confounded draught, and I’ve never

rightly recover that ague I pick up at Ancona,” be the reply; and

take his companion’s arm, Lord Bellefield hastily leave the room.



so engross have Lewis be with his own share of the transaction that

he have not observe the breathless interest with which the whole scene

have be watch by the girl before allude to. she now approach he

under the excuse of offer some cake, and, as he somewhat impatiently

“I beg your pardon, sir, but what be it you intend to do with that

glove?”



surprise alike at the question and the quarter from whence it

“and what be it you fear?” ask Lewis.



“that you be go to challenge he to fight a duel to-morrow

morning--and--and perhaps mean to wear that glove on the hand you shoot

he with.”



as she utter these last word a strange expression flit across

Lewis’s face; it have pass, however, ere he replied--



“you be mistaken. as long as I remain under this roof I shall avoid any

collision with that gentleman. Nay, more; should he repeat his insult

(though I scarcely think he will), I shall not attempt to resent it.

so,” he continue with a smile, “as I be live here, I think he be

his feature, as though she strove to read his very soul, she be about

to turn away, satisfied that he be not attempt to deceive she,

here be something to buy you some new ribbon for your cap.”



“I will not accept your money, sir,” she reply somewhat haughtily;

“but your secret be safe with I as in the grave.” then take Walter’s

plate, which be by this time empty, she cross the room and mingle

with the other servant.



it be later in the evening; much dancing have be accomplish, many

civil speech and some rude one make, mild flirtation begin to assume

a serious character, and one or two aggravate case appear likely to

end in business. the heart of match-make mamma beat high with

hope, marriageable daughter be look up, and eligible young man,

and secure Miss Peyton for a waltz; but his success do not tend

silent, or only open her mouth to say cut thing. the last polka

she enter the room in which the repast be lay out, and he it be

Leicester would wish his hope and fear confide.



see that thing thus continue steadily to “improve for the bad,”

 and that the tide which Shakespeare discover in the affair of man

appear to have set dead against he, the unfortunate “Charley” have,

in a spirit of self-mortification, repudiate supper and reject offer

“I say, Charley, cast your eye up the course a minute; the heavyweight’s

make play with the favourite at a killing pace. I’d bet long odd he

pop and she say ‘do’ before the meeting’s over; so if that don’t

suit your book, Charley, my boy, the soon you hedge on the double

event the well.”



the music-room at Broadhurst be a spacious apartment, with a coved

ceiling and deep bay windows hang with rich crimson damask curtain,

and contain othman of the same material in the recess. on one of

these Leicester fling himself, and half hide by the voluminous fold

of the drapery, sketch out a gloomy future, in which he depict

himself quarrel with De Grandeville, shoot he in a consequent

duel, and reside ever after in the least desirable part of the

backwood of America, a prey to remorse, without cigar, and cut off

from kid glove and pale ale in the flower of his youth. occupy with

these dreary thought, he scarcely notice the entrance of various

seceder from the supper-table; nor be it until the sound of the

pianoforte arouse his attention that he perceive the room to be

tenant by some twenty or thirty people scatter in small coterie

throughout the apartment. at the moment when he become alive to external

impression Miss Singleton be about to favour the company with a song,

having secure a mild young man to turn over the music, who know

not life and believe in she to the full extent with a touching

simplicity. before this interesting performance could commence, however,

sundry preliminary arrangement analogous to the nautical ceremony of

“clear for action” appear indispensable. first, a necessity exist

for take off her glove, which be not accomplish without much

rounding of arm, display of ring, and rattle of bracelet, one of

which, in particular, would catch in everything, and be so incorrigible

that it be force to be unclasp in disgrace and commit to the

custody of the mild young man, who blush at it and hold it as if it

be alive. then Miss Singleton draw up her head, elongate her neck to

a giraffe-like extent, raise her eye, simper, cast they down again,

glance out of their corner at the “mild one” till he tremble in his

polished boot and jingle the wicked bracelet like a baby’s rattle

in the excess of his agitation, and finally commence her song by an

energetic appeal to her mother (who have be dead and bury for the

last fifteen year) to “wake she early” on the ensue first of May.

just as she be assure the company that “she have be wild and

wayward, but she be not wayward now,” a couple enter the room, and

apparently wish not to disturb the melody, seat themselves on a

sofa in a retire corner which chance to be nearly opposite to the

recess of which Leicester have take possession; thus, although the whole

length of the music-room intervene, he could (himself unseen) catch

occasional glimpse of this sofa as the ever-change group of lounger

form and disperse themselves.



the occupant of the seat be Miss Peyton and De Grandeville; and could

Charles Leicester have overhear the follow conversation the passive

annoyance with which he observe the colloquy might have give place to

a more active sentiment.



“Ar--really,” remark De Grandeville, “that be a very--ar--touching,

pathetic song----”



“murder,” observe Miss Peyton, quietly finish his sentence for

he.



“Ar--eh--yes, of course, I be go to--ar--that be, your exquisite

taste have--ar--in fact--ar--beyond a doubt the woman be commit

murder.”



“Recollect, the ‘woman,’ as you be pleased to call she, be my

particular friend, Mr. De Grandeville,” return his companion with a

slight degree of hauteur in her tone.



“Ar--yes, of course, that speak volume in her favour,” be the

rejoinder; “and although it be not every one who be gift with

the--ar--talent of vocalisation, yet the estimable quality which

one seek in the--ar--endear relation of friendship may be

find--ar--that be, may exist--ar----”



“what do you think of the champagne at supper?” interrupt Miss Peyton

abruptly.



“really--ar--’pon my word I do not particularly notice it!

be--ar--so agreeably situate that I could not devote much attention to

the--ar--commissariat department.”



“surely it be unusually strong,” persist Laura.



“Ar--yes, of course you be right, it be no doubt owe to its agreeably

exhilarate quality that it be so universally popular with the fair

sex. be I--ar--so fortunate as to be--ar--a married man, I should

always have champagne at my table.”



“what a temptation!” return Miss Peyton, smile ironically. “your

wife will be an enviable woman, if you mean to indulge she in such

luxury.”



“it delight I to hear you say so,” exclaim De Grandeville eagerly.

“if such be your opinion, I be indeed a fortunate man. I have not

intend,” he continue in a low tone, “to speak to you at this early

period of our acquaintance on the subject near to my heart, but

the--ar--very flattering encouragement----”



“Sir!” exclaim Miss Peyton in a tone of indignant surprise.



“which you have deign to bestow upon I,” continue De Grandeville,

not heed the interruption, “lead I to unfold my intention without

further delay. I be now arrive at an age when, in the prime of life,

and with judgment so mature that I consider I may safely act in

obedience to its dictate without the risk of make any great mistake,

it appear to I, and to those of my highly bear and influential friend

whom I have consult on the subject, that I might greatly improve my

general position in society by a judicious matrimonial alliance.

now, without be in the slight degree actuate by--ar--anything

approach to a spirit of boasting, I may venture to say that in the

selection of a partner for life I have a right to look--ar--high. my

family may be trace back beyond the Norman conquest, and the immense

estate in our possession--ar--my cousin Hildebrand hold they

at present--but in the event of anything happen to his

seven--ar--however, I need not now trouble you with such family detail,

suffice it to say that we be of ancient descent, enormous land

proprietor, and that my own position in society be by no mean

an unimportant one. now, although I be aware that by birth you be

scarcely--ar--that be--that the Peyton family cannot trace back their

origin--ar--I have make up my mind to waive that point in consideration

of----”



“excuse I, sir,” interrupt Miss Peyton. “doubtless your mature

judgment have lead you to discover many, in fact so? _some thousand_ of

good and weighty reason why you should overlook the humble origin of

the poor peyton; but there be one point which appear to have escape

even your sagacity, namely, whether this unworthy descendant of an

ignoble family desire the honour of such an alliance as you propose.

that you may no long be in doubt on the subject, allow I to thank

you for the sacrifice you propose to make in my favour, and most

unequivocally to decline it.”



no one could be in De Grandeville’s company for ten minute without

perceive that on the one subject of his own importance he be more or

less mad; but with this exception he be a clear-head, quick-sight

man, use to society and accustom to deal with the world. Laura

Peyton, in her indignation at the inflated style of the preamble of his

discourse, have commit the indiscretion of refuse his hand before

he have distinctly offer it. De Grande-ville perceive the mistake, and

hasten to avail himself of it by replying--



“excuse I, Miss Peyton, but you jump rather hastily to conclusion. have

you hear I to the end you might have learn that there be equally

strong reason why in my present position I dare not yield to the

impulse of my feeling--for that I greatly admire and respect you

I frankly own. should these reason disappear under a change of

circumstance, I shall hope to have the honour of again address

you on this subject with a more favourable result. in the meantime,

to assure you that I entertain no unfriendly recollection of this

interview, permit I the honour----”



so say, ere she be aware of his intention, he raise her hand to

his lip, bow respectfully, and rise, quit the apartment. Miss

Peyton, equally surprised and provoke at the turn De Grande-ville have

give to the conversation, remain for a minute or so ponder the

matter, with her eye fix on the ground; as she raise they they

encounter those of a gentleman who be pass down the room at the

time. Charles Leicester (for he it be) return her gaze haughtily,

and as their eye meet a contemptuous smile curl his lip, and bow

coldly, he pass on without a word. well might he despise she, for he

have witness the parting salute, and not unnaturally deem she the

affiance bride of Marmaduke De Grandeville. Ere he retire for the

night his servant have receive order to pack up his clothe and to

procure post-horse by eight o’clock on the following morning. Annie

Grant, who, when the late guest have depart, seek her friend

Laura’s dressing-room to explain to she the old friendship which have

exist between her cousin Charles and Lady Mary Goodwood be equally

surprised and distressed to find her communication receive with a

hysterical burst of tear.









chapter XXVI.--SUNSHINE after SHOWERS.



Annie Grant find her friend strangely uncommunicative on the subject

of her fit of weeping; she declare that it be nothing--that she feel

nervous and overtired, but that a good night’s rest be all she require

to set she to right again; then kiss she affectionately, Laura,

with much caressing, turn she out of the room. as sound sleep be the

specific to which Miss Peyton trust for the restoration of her health

and spirit, it can scarcely be imagine that, after pass four

restless hour in a vain attempt to obtain the desire boon, she should

have feel particularly refresh. weary both in mind and body, she

be arouse from a dreamy, half-sleep, half-wake, but wholly

uncomfortable state into which she have fall by the sun shine

brightly into her room. the beauty of the morning, though a thick hoar

frost lie upon the ground, banish all further desire for sleep, and

commence her toilet, she resolve on a scheme which her

acquaintance with the usual habit of the family lead she to conceive

feasible--namely, to possess herself of the third volume of a new novel

in which she be considerably interested, and with that for a companion,

to take a brisk walk in the clear morning air and return ere any of

the party have make their appearance at the breakfast-table. dress

hastily, she wrap herself in a thick shawl and trip lightly down

the staircase, only encounter in her progress a drowsy housemaid, who

stare at she with lack-lustre eye, as though she take she for a ghost.

before she could carry her whole plan into execution, however, it be

necessary that she should visit the library in order to procure the

volume she wish to take with she. open the door quickly, she

have proceed half-way across the room ere she perceive it be not

untenanted. as she pause, uncertain whether or not to proceed, Charles

Leicester--for he it be who, act on his resolution of the previous

night, be write a few line to account for his abrupt departure--rise

from the table at which he have be sit and advanced towards she.

he be attire for a journey, and his pale feature and the dark circle

under his eye give token of a sleepless night. there be a restless

energy in his tone and manner, as he address she, totally oppose to

his usual listless indifference; and no one could be in his company a

moment without perceive that (to use a common but forcible expression)

something have come over he--that he be (at all event, for the

present) a change man.



“you be an early riser, Miss Peyton,” he say. “I do not expect to

have an opportunity of wish you good-bye in person.”



“I be not aware you intend leave Broadhurst so soon,” return

Laura, feel, she scarcely know why, exceedingly uncomfortable. “shall

you return before the party break up?”



“no. I shall go abroad directly, and endeavour to procure an attachéship

to one of the embassy; the turkish, I think: I’ve never see

Constantinople.”



“surely you’ve form this resolution somewhat abruptly,” observe Miss

Peyton. “it be only yesterday you agree to escort your cousin Annie

and myself to ride over and sketch the ruin of Monkton Priory. I be

think this morning, as soon as I see the sunshine, what a charming

canter we should have.”



“I should be more sorry, Miss Peyton, to be force to break so agreeable

an engagement, do I not feel certain _you_ will have no difficulty in

supply my place on the occasion,” return Leicester, lay a

mark emphasis on the pronoun. “I must now wish you good morning,” he

continue; then bow coldly, he take up his hat and turn to leave

the room.



Miss Peyton allow he to reach the door ere she could make up her mind

what course to pursue; then colour brightly, she exclaim, “stay one

moment, Mr. Leicester.” as he pause, and close the door, which he have

partially open, turn towards she, she continue, “I will not affect

to misunderstand your allusion, and although the subject be one on which

I should not willingly have enter, I consider it due to myself not to

suffer you to depart under a mistake, into which I should have think

you know I too well to have fall.”



“Mistake!” repeat Leicester eagerly. “be it possible that I can be

mistaken? be you not then engage to Mr. De Grandeville?”



“Most assuredly be I not,” return Miss Peyton, “nor, unless I very

greatly alter my opinion of that gentleman, shall I ever be so. I do

think Mr. Leicester would have give I credit for well taste than to

have suppose such a thing possible, but I see I be mistaken; and now,”

 she add, “having find the book I come to seek, I must wish you good

morning, and--a pleasant journey to Constantinople.”



“stay, Miss Peyton,” exclaim Leicester, for once really excited; “you

have say too much or too little. pardon I,” he continue, “I will not

detain you five minute, but speak I must.” take her hand, he lead she

to a seat, and resumed--



“I be place in a position equally painful and difficult, but the good

and most straightforward course I can pursue will be to tell you in as

few word as possible the simple truth, and then leave you to decide

upon my fate. the difficulty! have to encounter be this:--You be an

heiress; I, a portionless young brother, without a profession, and

bring up in expensive and indolent habit. be I then to tell you

that I love you, and that the dear wish of my heart be to call you

mine, how can I expect you to think that I be not actuate by mercenary

motive? to believe that I do indeed, deeply, truly love you, with an

intensity of which I scarcely could have believe my nature capable?

when first I seek your society, I frankly own (and if the admission

ruin my cause I cannot help it, for I will not attempt to deceive you)

it be the report of your rich which attract I. I consider you

lady-like and agreeable, and this be the case, I would willingly have

do as I see man of my acquaintance do everyday--marry for money; but

as I become intimate with you, and discover the priceless treasure

of your heart and mind, my view and feeling alter. I soon learn

to love you for yourself alone, and then for the first time, when I

perceive that in marry you I have everything to gain and nothing

to offer in return, I become fully aware of the meanness of the act I

contemplate--in fact I see the matter in its true light, and feel

that to ask you to become my wife would be an insult rather than a

compliment. thus, the more I grow to love you, the less I venture to

show it, till at last, pride come to my assistance, I resolve to tear

myself away, and quit Scotland abruptly, intend never to renew our

intimacy, unless some unexpected stroke of fortune should enable I

to do so on more equal term. my cousin Annie, however, have it seem

guess my secret, and invite you here without mention her intention

to I till you have actually arrive. have I act consistently, I should

have leave this place a fortnight ago; but I have suffer so much

during my absence, and the delight of again associate with you be so

overpowering, that I have not sufficient strength of will to carry out

my determination; thus I continue day by day yield myself to the

fascination of your society, learn to love you more and more, and

yet not dare to tell you so, because I feel the impossibility of

prove--even now it seem absurd to say--my disinterestedness; but that

I love you for yourself alone. such have be for some day my state of

feeling, when yesterday I be nearly drive distract by that man, De

Grandeville, actually select I as his confidant, and consult I

of all people in the world as to the advisability of make you an offer

of marriage, hint that he have reason to believe such a proposal would

be favourably receive by you.”



“Insolent!” exclaim Miss Peyton, raise her eye for the first time

during Leicester’s address, and look he full in the face. “so

far from encourage he, I have never speak to he save to turn his

pompous speech into ridicule since I be first introduce to he.”



“so I would fain have teach myself to believe yesterday,” resume

Leicester; “but the coldness of your manner towards I, and the marked

attention you allow he to pay you during the evening, torture I

with doubt, and when, after an animate conversation in the music-room,

I see he raise your hand to his lip, I imagine he have put his design

into execution, and be an accepted suitor.”



“a reject one would have be near the mark,” murmur Miss Peyton.



“utterly miserable,” continue Leicester, “at the idea of having

irrevocably lose you--provoke that you should have accept a man so

completely your inferior in mind, and, indeed, in every particular,

I order post-horse before I retire for the night, and but for this

accidental meeting should have be already on my road to London. and

now,” he continue with passionate eagerness, “it be for you to decide

whether my future life be to be happy or miserable. if truth have any

power of reveal itself, you will believe that I love you deeply,

tenderly, for yourself alone; and you will decide whether such an

affection be calculate to ensure your happiness; but if you be unable

to credit my sincerity, only say the word, and I leave you for ever.”



he cease, and clench his hand in the excess of his emotion till

the nail appear to grow into the flesh, stand before she, pale and

agitated, like a criminal await the sentence which shall send he

forth a free man or consign he to a felon’s grave. after watch she

anxiously for a few moment, during which she remain without speak,

her head avert and her feature conceal by her close straw bonnet,

he resume: “I see it be in vain to wait; your silence tell I that

I have nothing to hope--fool that I be ever to deem it could be

otherwise! Farewell, Laura; may you be as happy as I would have strive

to render you.”



he turn, and his hand be again on the lock of the door, when a low,

sweet voice, every accent of which thrill through his very soul,

murmured--



“Mr. Leicester--Charles--do not go--you will not leave I?”



and accordingly he do _not_ go, but come back instantly like an

amiable, obedient young man as he be, and receive the reward of merit

by learn from the lip of she he love that she be not only convince

of the sincerity of the affection he have bestow on she, but prize

the gift so highly that she feel oblige to return it, which statement

sound very like a contradiction, but be nothing of the kind.

then follow a bright, happy half-hour, one of those little bit of

unmitigated sunshine which gleam once or twice in a lifetime to thaw

the ice that tear which have never find vent form more or less thickly

around the heart of each of we; and ere it be over, Laura Peyton stand

pledge to become the wife of Charley Leicester, who dis-order the

post-horse and postpone his journey to Constantinople _ad infinitum_.



several droll little scene occur later on that morning between

various member of the party assemble at Broadhurst. in the first

place, Annie Grant, who, completely tired out, and greatly concern

at the mysterious impediment which obstruct the course of her cousin

Charles’s love affair, have seek her pillow with a firm conviction she

should never close her eye all night, fall asleep immediately, and wake

soon after nine o’clock on the follow morning under the impression

that she have just go to bed. while she be dress she resolve in

her anxious mind her cousin’s difficulty, and come to the follow

conclusion: first, that for sundry reason connect with his natural

indolence and a painful sense of his dependent position, Charley would

never “tell his love;” secondly, that Laura, not divine these reason,

be pique and hurt at his prolonged silence; and thirdly, that it

behove she (Annie) to remove these stumbling-block by a little

judicious interference. accordingly, when she have finish her toilet,

and, give a last parting glance at her pretty face and graceful figure

in the cheval glass in her dressing-room, have think--well, I don’t

know that we’ve any business to pry into her thought, but by the bright

half-smile, half-blush which result from the inspection it may be

conclude they be of an agreeable nature. when she have perform this

little unconscious act of homage to her own beauty she trip off to

her friend’s room, and find that young lady fasten a very dangerous

little bow of ribbon around her neck, with a small turquoise brooch

make in the shape of a true lover’s knot. I wonder why she should have

select _it_ from some twenty other on that morning in particular?



“idle girl!” exclaim Annie, kiss she affectionately, as if idleness

be a highly commendable quality, “idle girl! not dress at ten

o’clock, and I’ve be ready for the last five minute.”



“I’m very sorry, dear; but if you know what pleasant dream I’ve

enjoy, you would not wish to have dispel they,” return Laura

demurely, though there be a fund of merriment gleam in her dark eye

which Annie in her innocence do not perceive. feel, however, that

under the circumstance her friend have no business to have be so very

happy, even in her dream, she answer somewhat pettishly--



“you have be more favour than I have be. I go to bed cross and

worried, and fret over all my trouble again in my dream. Laura

dear,” she continue, “I want to say something to you, if I think you

would not be angry with I: I wish you--but cann’t you guess what I’m

go to say?”



Miss Peyton shake her pretty head, and confirm the conviction

express by De Grandeville, that her family be of modern date, by

repudiate any connection with the race of Odipus. so poor, sensitive

Annie be force to clothe her meaning in plain and unmistakable word,

which she endeavour to do by resuming--



“my cousin Charles, dear Laura--you know we be bring up together as

child, and I love he as a brother; he be so kind-hearted and such

a sweet temper; and of course I be aware he make himself rather

ridiculous sometimes with his indolence and affectation, but he have

be so spoiled and flatter by the set he live in--it be only

manner--whenever he be really call upon to act, you have no notion

what good sense and right feeling he display. dear Laura, I cann’t bear

to see he so unhappy!”



at the beginning of this speech Miss Peyton colour slightly; as it

proceed her eye sparkle, and any one less occupy with their own

feeling than be Annie Grant might have observe that tear glisten

in they; but at its conclusion she observe in her usual quiet tone--



“I don’t believe Mr. Leicester be unhappy.”



“ah! you don’t know he as well as I do,” return Annie, her cheek

glowing and her eye beam with the interest she take in the subject;

“he be so wretched all yesterday evening; he eat no supper, and sit

moping in corner, as unlike his natural, happy self as possible.”



“do you hear that he have order post-horse at eight o’clock this

morning?” inquire Laura.



“no! you don’t mean it!” exclaim Annie, clasp her hand in dismay.

“oh, I hope he be not go!”



“you may depend upon it he be,” rejoin Miss Peyton, turn to the

glass avowedly to smooth her glossy hair, which do not in the slight

degree require that process, but in reality to hide a smile. “he must

be on his way to town by this time, _unless_ anything have occur this

morning to cause he to alter his determination.”



“that be impossible,” return Annie quickly; then add in a tone of

the deep reproach, “oh, Laura! how could you be so cruel as to let

he go?” she burst into a flood of tear. and Laura, that heartless

young hyæna of fashionable life, that savage speciman of the perfidious

sex of whom a poet sings--



               Woman, though so mild she seem,



                   will take your heart and tantalise it;



               be it make of Portland stone,



                   she’d manage to Macadamise it”--



what do you suppose she do on the occasion? nothing wonderful, and yet

the good thing she could, for she wreathe her soft arm round Annie’s

neck, and kiss away her tear, whisper in a few simple touching

word the secret of her happy love.









CHAPTER XXVII.--BROTHERLY LOVE “À LA MODE.”



now let we shake the kaleidoscope and take a peep at another

combination of our _dramatis personæ_ at this particular phase of their

destiny. Lord Bellefield be breakfast in his private sitting-room;

a bright fire blaze on the hearth; close to it have be draw a sofa,

upon which, wrap in a dressing-gown of rich brocade silk, lounge

the tenant of the apartment; a breakfast-table stand by the sofa, on

which be place an empty coffee cup, a small flask of french brandy,

and a liqueur glass, together with a plate of toast apparently scarcely

touch, a cut-glass saucer contain marmalade, and a cigar-case. his

lordship appear to be by no mean in an amiable frame of mind. he

have sit up the previous night some two hour after the ball be over,

play _ecarté_ with certain intimate of his own, whom he have cause

to be invite to Broadhurst, during which time he have contrive to

lose between £200 and,£300. early in the past day he have form a

canvassing engagement with General Grant for eleven o’clock on the

follow morning, which have oblige he to rise soon than be by any

mean agreeable to his taste, or consonant with his usual habit; and

lastly, he expect an important letter, and the post be late. while

he be ponder this agglomerate (to choose a euphonious word) of

small evil, the door open noiselessly, and Antoine, the french valet,

carry a well-brush coat as tenderly as if it have be a baby, stole

on tiptoe across the room. Lord Bellefield, whose head be turn away

from the door, stretch out his hand, exclaim impatiently, “well,

where be they?”



“_Milor!_” return the astonished Frenchman, who in his interest about

the coat have forget the letter.



“the letter, fool, where be they?” reiterate his lordship angrily.



“_Mille pardon, Milor_; but ven I do valk myself up zie stair, I

be not avare dat zie lettair have make zemselve to arrive,” rejoin

Antoine with a self-satisfied smile, as if he have say something clever.



“do you ask?” return his master with a frown.



“_Non pas précisément_--I do not exactly demand,” stammer Antoine

with (this time) a deprecatory smile.



Lord Bellefield’s only reply be an oath; then, see the man remain,

uncertain what to do, he added--



“go down again directly, idiot, and don’t return without my letter,

unless----” a menacing gesture of his clench fist supply the blank,

and the valet quit the room, mutter with a shrug as he close the

door, “_Qu’ils sont barbare, ce Anglais_; but, _parbleu_, like all zie

savage, dey be make of gold--_eh! bien, c’est égal_,--he shall pay I

veil for he.”



Lord Bellefield be not fate to enjoy the blessing of peace that

morning, for scarcely have his servant close the door ere some one else

tap at it. “come in,” shout the victimise peer, append a wish

concern his visitor, of which the most charitable view we can take be

that he be desirous of offer he a warm reception. however this

may be, Charles Leicester (for he it be to whose lot his brother’s

left-handed benediction have fall) enter the room, his face

reflect the joy of his heart, and draw a chair to the opposite

side of the fire-place, seat himself thereupon, and begin rub

his hand with a degree of energy totally oppose to his usual listless

indifference.



“be there no other fire in the house that you be necessitate to come

and warm your hand here, Mr. Leicester? I fancy you be aware that

if there be one thing in the world which annoy I more than another,

it be to be intrude on in a morning,” observe his lordship pettishly.

then, for the first time catch sight of his brother’s face, he

continue, “what on earth be you look so absurdly happy about?”



“now, don’t growl this morning, Belle; be a little bit like a brother

for once in your life. I’m come to receive your congratulation,”

 return Leicester.



“have your jewish money-lender turn Christian and burn his book,

like the magician of old?” inquire Bellefield sarcastically.



“something almost as wonderful,” reply his brother, “for I live in

good hope of pay he.”



“why, you don’t mean to say my father be go to be such a confound

fool as to pay your debt?” continue Bellefield, spring up in the

excitement of the moment. “I swear I’ll not allow it; he’ll burden the

estate so that when I come into the title I shall be a beggar.”



“keep yourself cool, my good brother; you might be sure I should never

in my wildest moment dream of ask you to congratulate I on any good

fortune which could by the most remote contingency either affect your

interest or interfere with your ease and comfort,” reply Leicester,

for once provoke to say a cutting thing by his brother’s intense

selfishness.



“really, Charles, I’m in no humour for foolery or impertinence,” say

Lord Bellefield snappishly. “if there’s anything you wish I to know,

tell it at once; if not, I be expect important letter, and should be

glad to be alone.”



“what should you say if you hear I be go to be hang, Belle?”

 ask Charley.



“wish you joy of your exalt destiny, and think thing might have be

bad,” be the answer.



“apply both the wish and reflection to the present emergency,” return

Leicester, “for I’m in nearly as sad a case--I’m go to be marry.”



“on the principle that what be not enough to keep one can support two,

I suppose!” rejoin Lord Bellefield in a tone of the most bitter

contempt. “well, I do _not_ think--but I wash my hand of the affair

entirely. only mind this--the property be strictly entail, my father

can do nothing without my consent, and if you expect that you’re to be

support in idleness at our expense----”



“my dear fellow, I expect nothing of the kind,” return Charley,

caress his whisker. “my wife and I mean to set up a cigar divan, and

all we shall look for from you be your custom; we certainly do hope to

make a decent live out of that.”



Lord Bellefield utter an exclamation expressive of disgust, and then

inquire abruptly--



“well, who be the woman?”



“she ben’t exactly a woman,” return Charley meekly; “that be, of

course, speak literally and in a physiological point of view she be

a woman, but in the language of civilised society she be something more

than a mere woman--for instance, by birth she be a lady. nature have

bestow on she that somewhat unusual feminine attribute, a mind, to

which art, through the medium of the various educational science,

have add cultivation; then she have the sweetest, most lovable

disposition----



“there! spare I your lover’ rapture,” return Lord Bellefield; “of

all stale trash they be the most sickening; and tell I plainly, in

five word, who she _be_, and what she _have_.”



“Laura Peyton--heiress--value unknown,” return Leicester emphatically

and concisely.



“Miss Peyton!” exclaim Lord Bellefield in surprise. “my dear Charles,”

 he continue in a more cordial tone than he have yet use, “do you really

mean that you’re engage to Laura Peyton? why, she be say to have

between four and five thousand a year in the fund, besides a princely

estate in------shire. be you in earnest?”



“never be so much so about anything before in my life,” return

Leicester. “if I don’t marry Laura Peyton, and that very soon too, I

shall do something so desperate that society have well shut up shop at

once, for it’s safe to be ‘uproot from its very foundation,’ as the

conservative paper say if a poor devil of a chartist happen to strop

his razor before commit the ‘overt act’ by which he cut his own

throat.”



“’pon my word,” exclaim Lord Bellefield, as he become convinced that

his brother be really in earnest, “ ’pon my word, you’ve play your

card deucedly well. I declare, if I haven’t be book for little Annie

here, I wouldn’t have mind marry the girl myself. why, Charley,

you’ll actually become a creditable member of society.”



as he speak a tap be hear at the door, and Antoine make his

appearance, breathless with the haste in which he have run upstairs.



“_Enfin elle sont arrivées_,” he exclaim, hand the letter on a

silver waiter; “vhy for _zey vos si tard_, zie postman, he do slip up

on von vot you call--(_ah! q té ils sont difficile, ce sacrés mot

Anglais_)--slid? _oui! oui!_ he do slip himself up on von slid, and

tumble into two ditch.”



Lord Bellefield seize the letter eagerly. sign to the valet to

leave the room, without heed his lucid explanation of the delay, he

select one in a particular handwriting, and tear it open, hastily

peruse the first few line; then rub his hand, he exclaim with

an oath, “by ------! Beppo’s won, and I’m a clear, £12,000 in pocket.

Charley, boy,” he continue, with a sudden impulse of generosity (for no

one be all bad), “how much be your debt?”



“I believe about £2000 would cover they,” return Leicester.



“then I’ll clear you, old fellow,” reply Lord Bellefield, clap he

on the shoulder, “and you shall marry your rich bride a free man.”



“my dear Bellefield, I cann’t allow it--you be too kind--I--I really

don’t know how to thank you--I cann’t think what’s come to everybody

this morning,” cry poor Charley, as, fairly overpower by his good

fortune, he seize Lord Bellefield’s hand and wrung it warmly. at that

moment those two man, each warp and harden differently, as their

disposition differ, by the world’s evil influence, feel more as

brother should feel towards each other than they have do since they

play together year ago as little child at their mother’s knee.

with one the kindly feeling thus revive be never again entirely

forget; with the other--but we will not anticipate.









CHAPTER XXVIII.--BEGINS ABRUPTLY and ENDS UNCOMFORTABLY.



“well, what be it? for I can see by your eye that you have something

you wish to ask I, Walter,” observe Lewis, as his pupil stand before

he nervously move his foot and twist the lash of a dog-whip round

his hand.



“only Millar want--that is, he don’t want, but he say he would take

I out with he to see he shoot those great pretty bird.”



“Pheasants,” suggest Lewis.



“yes, to see he shoot pheasant,” continue Walter, “if you would let

I go. Millar say,” he add, see that Lewis appear doubtful,

“Millar say all real gentleman like shooting, and that I’m quite old

enough to learn.”



one great change work in Walter since he have be under Lewis’s

direction--a change from which his tutor augur the most favourable

result--be the almost total disappearance of those fit of morbid

despondency and indifference to external object, at time almost

amount to unconscious imbecility, to which he have formerly be

subject; it be therefore a part of Lewis’s system to encourage he

to follow up vigorously any pursuit for which he evince the slight

predilection; indeed, so effectual a mean do he consider this of

arouse his faculty, that he often sacrifice to it the daily routine

of mechanical teaching. have, therefore, run over in his mind the pro

and con, and decide that if he accompany his pupil no danger could

accrue, he graciously give his consent, and having encase his foot in

a stout pair of boot, and see that Walter follow his example,

both master and pupil hasten to the stable-yard to join the worthy

individual with whom the expedition have originate.



Millar, who, as the reader have probably ere this divine, be none other

than General Grant’s head gamekeeper, appear anxious to be off without

delay, as he have receive order to kill a certain amount of game which

be require for a forthcoming dinner-party. the morning be, as we have

already say, lovely, and Lewis enjoy the brisk walk through some of

the most wild and picturesque scenery the country afford with a degree

of z at which he be himself surprised. the pheasant, however,

not be endow with such superornithological resignation as certain

water-fowl, who, when require for culinary purpose be invite, as

the nursery rhyme relate, to their own execution by the unalluring

couplet,



               “dilly dilly dilly duck, come and be kill!”



appear singularly unwilling to face death at that particular epoch,

and contrive accordingly by some mean or other to render themselves

invisible. in vain do Millar try the choice spinnie, in vain do

he scramble through impassable hedge, where gap there be none,

render himself a very pin-cushion for thorn; in vain do he creep

along what he be pleased to term dry ditch, till from the

waist downward he look more like a geological speciman than a

leather-gaitere and corduroyed Christian; still the obdurate pheasant

refuse to stand fire, either present or prospective (gun or kitchen),

and at the end of three hour’ hard walk through the good preserve

the disconsolate gamekeeper have only succeed in bag a brace.

at length completely dishearten, he come to anchor on a stile,

and produce a flask of spirit, with the content of which (after

fruitlessly press Lewis and Walter to partake thereof) he proceed

to regale himself. find himself the well for this prescription,

he shout to a dishevel individual clepe the beater, who for the

trifle consideration of eighteenpence per diem and a meal of broken

victual, deliver himself over to the agreeable certainty of be wet

_to_ the skin, and scratch and tear _through_ it, with the by no mean

remote contingency of getting accidentally shoot into the bargain. the

creature who appear in answer to this summon, and who in spite of

the uncomfortable description we have give of his occupation, seem to

enjoy his day’s sport excessively, be too old for a boy and too young

for a man. his face be, of course, scratch and bleed, and his

elf lock, drench with the hoar frost, now melt into a specie of

half-frozen gelatine, give he a strange, unearthly appearance. his

clothing, if rag which look like the cast-off garment of an indigent

scarecrow deserve the name, be so tattered and tear, that the fact of

their hang upon he at all be calculate to shake one’s faith in the

newtonian theory of gravitation, till one gain a clue to the mystery

by recollect the antagonistic principle “attraction of cohesion;”

 the only personal attraction, by the way (save a pair of clear grey eye

give a shrewd expression to his face), that our friend possess.



“Villiam,” begin his superior--and here let it be remark

parenthetically that it be the custom of this excellent gamekeeper

invariably to address his satellite for the time be as “Villiam,”

 utterly disregard the occasional fact that the sponsor of the youth

have see fit to call he otherwise--“Villiam,” observe Mr. Millar,

“you’re vet.” this be an incontrovertible certainty, evident to the

mean capacity, “Villiam” do not feel call upon to reply in word,

merely shake himself like a Newfoundland dog for the benefit of the

bystander, and glance wistfully at the flask. “yer vet right thro’

yer, Villiam,” resume his employer dogmatically; “so shove a drop o’

this here down yer throat, and make spurrit and vater of yerself.”



to this proposition “Villiam” reply by stretch out his hand,

grasp the flask eagerly, then tug at a tangled lock of hair on

his forehead as a salutation to the assemble company, and growl

out in a hoarse, damp voice, “here’s wushin’ hall yer ’ealth,” he

proceed to “do his spiriting,” though by no mean as “gently” as the

delicate Ariel be accustom to perform that operation. having thus

qualify his cold-water system by the introduction of alcohol, the

spirit move he and he spake.



“yer ain’t bag much game, Master, this mornin’, I reckon?”



“not I,” be the reply; “no man cann’t shoot thing as ain’t wisibul, yer

know, Villiam. I cann’t think vot’s get all the game.”



“they do tell I as pheasand as look wery like ourn go to Lunnun in

t’carrier’s cart twice a veek,” observe “Villiam” in a dreamy, absent

kind of manner, as if the remark be totally foreign to the subject

under discussion.



“ah! that’s vot yer hear, be it, Villiam?” return Millar carelessly.

“Hif that’s the case, I suppose (for ’tain’t likely they valk there

of themselves) somebody must take ’em?”



“that be right, Master,” be the rejoinder.



“have it hever cum across yer--take another drop of spurrit, Villiam;

yer vet--have it hever cum across yer who that somebody his?” demand

Millar in an easy, careless tone of voice.



“his it true as ther General think o’ puttin’ hon a second

hunder-keeper?” rejoin “Villiam,” reply, like an irish echo, by

another question.



“Hi’m avake, Villiam,” return his patron with an encouraging wink, “it

certingly his possibul hif I vas to tell ther General that I know

a quick, hintelligent lad have might be wery useful in _catchin

poarchers_--yer understand, Villiam--sich a thing might cum about.”



“in that case hi’m free to mention that hi see three cove a cummin’

hout o’ Todshole Spinney vith a sack as vosn’t haltogither hempty,

atween three and four o’clock this here bless mornin’.”



“and vot might yer be a’ doin’ yerself, hout o’ bed at that time o’

night, Villiam?” inquire Millar suspiciously.



“a lying in a dry ditch vith my heye open,” return the imp

significantly.



“I see!” rejoin the keeper reflectively. “Yer don’t happen

haccidentally to know any o’ they three cove, Villiam, I suppose?”



“Ther von have carry the sack worn’t haltogither unlike long Hardy, the

blacksmith,” be the reply.



the worthy Mr. Millar meditate for some minute in silence on the

information thus acquire; then rouse himself with a sudden start, he

observe, “now, Villiam, hif you’ll be so hoblige have to beat along

that ere ’edgerow to the right, ve’ll see hif ve can knock hover

another brace o’ longtail, and ve can talk about Mr. Hardy ven ve have

finish our day’s vork. there’s a precious young limb o’ vickedness,”

 he add, turn to Lewis as the boy get out of earshot, “he’s von hof

’em, bless yer, only he’s turn again ’em with a mercenary view hof

get a hunder-keeper’s sitivation.”



“in which rascality do you mean to allow he to succeed?” ask Lewis.



“not by no manner o’ mean--halway suppose I can pump he dry

without,” be the prudent reply; and shoulder his doublebarrel the

gamekeeper quit his perch on the stile and resume his shooting.



whether the intelligence he have receive have affect his nervous system

(reserve for future discussion the more doubtful question of his

possess such an aristocratic organisation), or whether in the

excitement of the moment he have allow himself to imbibe an unusually

liberal allowance of the content of the spirit-flask, we do not pretend

to decide; but certain it be that he miss consecutively two as fair

shot as ever present themselves to the gun of a sportsman, and end

by wound, without bring down, a young hen pheasant, despite the

warning cry of “‘ware hen” from the perfidious “Villiam,” then locate

in a quagmire.



“Veil, I never do!” exclaim the unfortunate perpetrator of this the

great crime which in a gamekeeper’s opinion a sportsman can commit;

“I ’aven’t do sich a think have that since I wo a boy o’ thirteen

year old, and father quilt I with the dog-whip for it, and sarve

I right, too. this here’s a werry snipey bit, too,” he continue

dejectedly; “but hif I cann’t ’it apheasand, hit’s useless to ’old up

my gun hat a snipe.”



“your ill luck in the morning have make you impatient and spoil your

shooting,” observe Lewis, wish good-naturedly to propitiate his

companion.



this speech, however, seem to produce just a contrary effect, for

Millar answer gruffly, “perhaps, Mister, you fancy as you can do

well yourself; hif so, you’re velcome to take the gun and try.”



“I’ve no objection,” reply Lewis, smile at the very evident contempt

in which, as a “lunnuner,” his companion hold he; “I’ll try a shot or

two, if you like.”



“here you be, then, sir,” be the reply, as the keeper hand he the

gun; “the right barrel’s shot for pheasand, and the left for snipe;

so look hout, and if yer don’t bag Villiam, or Master Valter here,

hit’ll be a mercy, I expect.”



if the unfortunate Millar hope to console himself for his own failure

by witness a similar _mis_hap on the part of the young tutor, he

be once more doomed to be disappoint; for scarcely have Lewis

take possession of the gun when a splendid cock-pheasant rise within

distance, though far off than either of the shot the keeper have

just miss, and, ere its gaudy plumage have well catch the ray of

the sun above the top of the young plantation, fall to the ground,

quiver in the agony of death. as the smoke from the discharge

clear away, a snipe, scare alike by the report of the gun and the

approach of the beater, spring from a thick clump of alder bush and

dart away, utter its peculiar cry.



“no use--hit’s clean out o’ shot,” exclaim Millar, as Lewis, swift as

thought, again raise the gun to his shoulder. slightly pique by the

keeper’s contemptuous manner, he determine not to throw away a chance

of vindicate his skill as a marksman, and though he feel by no mean

sure of success, on the “nothing venture nothing have” principle, the

instant he get a clear sight of the bird he blaze away at it. great

then be his delight to perceive the snipe suddenly tower upwards and

then drop to the ground, as if strike by lightning.



“Vel, if that hain’t a clever shot!” ejaculate Millar, surprised into

admiration in spite of himself; “bless’d if yer ’aven’t tuk the shine

hout of I properly. I thort yer vos a reg’lar green un, but I’m free to

confess I couldn’t ’ave kill that ere bird at that distance ther good

o’ time.”



“nor have I, it seem,” exclaim Lewis, as the snipe, which be only

wound, rise, fly a short distance, and drop again.



“hit’s dead this time. I’ll bet a quart,” observe Millar; “hit’ll never

git hup no more, hif ve can honly find it.”



“I think I can,” say Lewis; “I mark the exact spot where it fall.

Walter, do you stay with Millar till I come back. I should not like to

lose it.”



so say, Lewis, completely carry away by the excitement of the

sport, return the gun to its owner, and dash the branch aside,

bound forward, and be soon hide amongst the tree, as he force

his way through the dense underwood towards the spot where he trust

to find the snipe. with some difficulty, and after much energetic

scrambling, Lewis reach the place where he have see the bird fall, but

even then it be no such easy matter to find it, nor be it till he have

nearly decide that he must relinquish the search that he discover his

victim catch in a forked branch, and perfectly dead. having secure

his prize, the next object be to rejoin his companion, and this

accordingly he endeavour to accomplish without delay; but since the

day of pious Æneas the task of retrace our step, the _revocare

gradus_, have be a work of difficulty, more especially if we have

begin by take a few in a wrong direction, and Lewis’s case prove no

exception to the rule. after one or two wrong turn he become completely

bewilder, and feel sure that he should never discover his right

course while surround by the thick underwood, he strike into the first

path which present itself, and follow its winding, find himself,

almost immediately, close to the hedge which separate that side of the

plantation from a grass-field beyond. as he make his way towards a gap

in this hedge his attention be attract by the sound of voice, and

on approach the spot he perceive two person engage in earnest

conversation.



they be a man and a girl, the former, who wear the dress of a

gentleman, have his arm round his companion’s waist. the interview

seem, however, about to terminate, for as Lewis pause, uncertain

whether or not to make himself know to the lover (for such he

conjecture they to be), the gentleman stoop, imprint a kiss on the

damsel’s brow, then say, “remember, you have promise!” loose the

bridle of a horse which be fasten to the branch of a tree, spring

into the saddle, and ride hastily away. not, however, before Lewis have

recognise the feature of Lord Bellefield.



surprise at this discovery be the first feeling of which Lewis be

conscious, then a sudden desire seize he to ascertain who the girl

could be, and without wait to reflect on what far course it might

be advisable for he to pursue, he cross the gap, spring over the

ditch beyond, and present himself before she. with a violent start

and a slight scream at this sudden apparition, the girl raise her head,

disclose to Lewis the intelligent face and earnest eye of the young

female who have accost he on the previous evening immediately after

the affair of the glove have take place. Lewis be the first to speak.



“I have startle you, I fear,” he begin. “I quit my companion to

go in search of a snipe I have just shoot, and become bewilder in the

wood, have contrive to miss they. hear voice in this direction, I

jump over the hedge, hope I should find some one who could tell I

how to retrace my step.”



“be you in the hazel walk when you leave your party, sir?” inquire the

girl in a voice which falter from various conflict emotion.



Lewis answer in the affirmative, and she continued--



“then, if you go straight on till you come to the corner of the field

you will see a gate on your left hand; get over that and follow the road

which lead into the wood, and it will bring you to your friend.”



Lewis thank she, and then stand a moment, irresolute whether or not

to allude to the parting he have just witness. it be no affair of his,

and yet could he answer it to his conscience not to warn she against

the design which, he do not doubt, Lord Bellefield entertain against

she?



“do not think I interfere without reason,” he observe, “but I be an

involuntary witness to your parting with that gentleman, and I wish to

ask you if you be acquaint with his name and position?”



the girl cast down her eye, and after a pause, murmur that she know

he be very rich.



“and his name?” urge Lewis.



“Mr. Leicester, brother to the young Lord,” she believe.



“he have tell you that, have he?” return Lewis sternly; “and do it not

occur to you to inquire of the servant last night whether your wealthy

admirer have reveal to you his real name?”



“no; she have never doubt that he have do so,” make game of a

fellow.



“and perhaps be unwilling to call attention to your connection with

he by make the inquiry?” resume Lewis.



a bright blush prove that he have hit upon the truth; but the probe

nature of his question rouse the girl’s spirit, and raise her eye,

she look he full in the face as she in her turn inquired--“And pray,

sir, who be you? and what right have you to question I in this way?”



“my name be Lewis Arundel; I reside at Broadhurst, as tutor to Sir

Walter Desborough,” be the reply; “and my right to ask you these

question be the right every man possess to do his good to counteract

the design of a heartless libertine; for such I take your friend to

be, and now I will give you my reason for think he so. in the first

place, he have not tell you his true name: he be not Lord Bellefield’s

brother, as he pretend, but Lord Bellefield himself; and in the second

place, at the very moment when he be make profession of affection

here to you, he be engage to be married to his cousin, the daughter of

General Grant.”



“it be not true, you hate he,” exclaim the girl with flash

eye. “you quarrel with he last night, and now you seek to revenge

yourself by sow dissension between he and I, but you shall not

succeed. I see through your meanness, and despise you for it.”



“girl, you be infatuate,” return Lewis angrily, “and must reap the

fruit of your obstinate folly. I speak only for your good, and tell you

the simple truth. if you choose to disbelieve I, the sin will lie at

your door, and not mine.”



as he speak he turn and leave she. by the time he reach the gate into

the wood his conscience begin to reproach he for having be too hasty.

he look back to see if the girl be still there; she have not move

from the spot where he have quit she, but stand motionless, apparently

bury in the deep thought. suddenly observe that his eye be

direct towards she, she start, and draw her shawl close around

she, hurry away in an opposite direction. Lewis watch her retreating

figure till it become no long visible; then get over the gate, he

walk leisurely along the turfed road to rejoin his companion. he

be no coward, far from it; but have he know that at that moment a

gun-barrel cover he, level by the stalwart arm and keen eye of one

before whose unerring aim by the broad light of day, or beneath the

cold ray of the moon, hare, pheasant, or partridge fall like leave in

autumn--one who, hide from the gaze of man, have witness his parting

from the girl not five minute since,--had he know the deep interest

feel for she by this person, and how, his suspicion be arouse, he

have watch day after day to discover the feature of her clandestine

suitor, but have never succeed, till, creep through the bush, he

have accidentally come up at the moment when Lewis, having speak eagerly

to she, turn and leave the spot,--had he know the struggle between the

good and evil principle in that man’s heart, a struggle on the result

of which depend life or death,--had he know all this, Lewis Arundel,

though a brave man, would scarcely have pace that greenwood alley with

a pulse so calm, a brow so unruffled and serene.



[illustration: 0204]









chapter XXIX.--DE GRANDEVILLE meet his MATCH.



unpleasant as be the situation in which Lewis be leave at the end

of the last chapter, we can scarcely imagine that any of our reader,

however they may be accustom to look on the “night side of nature,”

 can have coolly make up their mind to the bad, and settle to their

own dissatisfaction that he fall a victim to the poacher’s gun. we say

we cannot imagine such a possibility; not because we have any very deep

reliance on the tender-heartedness of all our fellow-creature, see

that this tale may fall into the hand of a poor-law guardian or a

political economist; that a butcher may read it fresh from the shamble,

or a barrister after defend some confess murderer. but we feel

certain, butcher or barrister, law-giver or guardian must alike perceive

that, as we be write the life and adventure of Lewis Arundel, we

cannot commit manslaughter without add thereunto suicide; or, to

speak plainly, we cannot kill Lewis without dock our own tale;

therefore, the utmost extent for which our most truculent reader can

possibly hope must be a severe gun-shot wound, entail a linger

illness and a shattered constitution. but even these pleasant and

reasonable expectation be doom to meet with disappointment, the fact

be that almost at the moment in which “long Hardy” (for he it be)

level his gun at Lewis’s retreating figure, his quick ear have catch

a sound betoken the advance of some person through the bush in

his immediate vicinity; and neither wish to encounter any of the

gamekeeper’s satellite, nor consider the deed he have meditate

exactly calculate to be perform before any, even the most select

audience, the poacher slowly recover his gun and proceed to convey

himself away, after a singular snake-like fashion of his own, reserve

to himself the right of shoot his suppose enemy at some more

convenient season. in the meantime Lewis walk quietly on, unconscious

of the danger he have escape, until a turn in the road bring he in

sight of his companion. during the course of their homeward walk Lewis

question the gamekeeper as to his intention concern the poacher

to whose proceeding he have that morning gain a clue.



“Veil, yer see, Mr. Arundel,” return Millar, in whose estimation Lewis

have rise fifty per cent, since his clever shot at the snipe, “yer see,

it ain’t ther fust time as this chap hardy have give we a good deal o’

trouble: we catch he a poarchin’ about three year ago, and he wor

in ---------- gaol for six month at a stretch. Veil, ven he cum out,

he tuk to bad course altogether--jine ther chartists, they chap as

preache equalerty, ’cos, be at the wery bottom of ther ladder

therselve, equalerty would pull they hup and their better down;

vunce let ’em get to ther middle round, and they’d soon give

up equalerty--hit would be the ‘haristogracy of talent,’ or ther

‘shu-premacy of physic-all force’ (vich mean, adwisability of pitch

into somebody else) vith ’em then. I hate such canot as I hate

varmint, so I do.”



having deliver himself of this opinion with much emphasis, the keeper

proceed to relieve his mind by flog an inoffensive dog for an

imaginary offence ere he continued--



“Veil, arter he jine the chartist he vent to Lunnun as a Delicate, as

they call ’em; and have they find he in wittle and drink, lodgin’

and hother parquisite, in course he worn’t in no hurry to cum back;

howsomdever, I suppose at last they diskivere what I could a tell ’em

at furst--that he ben’t worth his keep; and so they pack he off home

agen. I ’specte vhen I hear he vas arrive vot he’d be hup to. he

call hisself a blacksmith, but he drive more shot into ’are and

pheasand than nail into ’orse’ ’oof, _you_ may depend.”



“and how do you propose to put a stop to his depredation?” inquire

Lewis.



“Vy, I should like to catch he in the wery act--nab he vith the game

upon he,” return the keeper meditatively; “then ve could get he

another six month. but he’s precious sly, and uncommon swift of

foot too, though he ain’t fur hoff my age, vich shall never see

five-and-forty no more.”



“I wish, Millar,” say Lewis, after a moment’s consideration, “I wish

that whenever you receive information which you think likely to lead to

this man’s capture, you’d send I word; there’s nothing I should like

well than to lend you a hand in take he. I might be useful to you,

for I be consider a fast runner.”



“and suppose it come to blow? they poarche chap be rough customer

to handle sometimes,” rejoin Millar, with a cunning twinkle in his

eye, as if he expect this information would alter his companion’s

intention.



“so much the more exciting,” return Lewis eagerly; “an affray with

poacher would be a real treat after such a life of inaction as I’ve

be lead lately.”



as he speak--throw off for a moment the cold reserve which have now

become habitual to he--his eye flash, he draw himself up to his full

height, and fling back his graceful head with an air of proud defiance.

the gamekeeper regard he fixedly, and mentally compare he with,

not the fighting gladiator, for Millar’s unclassical education have never

render he acquaint with that illustrious statue, but he have

once be present at a prize-fight, in which a tall, athletic youth,

rejoice in the ornithological sobriquet of “the spicy Dabchick,”

 prove victor, and to that dabchick do he assimilate Lewis. at length

his thought find vent in the following ejaculation--



“Veil, Mr. Arundel, hif ther’s many more like you hup there, that

bless Lunnun cann’t be as bad a place as I think it.”



Lewis smile. perhaps (for, after all, he be human and under

twenty-one) the evident admiration which have replace the no less

evident contempt with which the sturdy gamekeeper have regard he

early in their acquaintance be not without its charm; at all event,

when, after another hour’s shooting, Millar go home to dinner, and

Lewis and Walter return to Broadhurst, the young tutor diminish his

income to the extent of half-a-crown, and the keeper, as he pocket

the “tip,” renew his assurance that he would send Mr. Arundel timely

notice “vhenever there vas a chance of be down upon that poarche

willain, Hardy.”



Charley Leicester, as he do not start for Constantinople, find himself

at liberty to escort Laura Peyton and his cousin Annie to view the ruin

of Monkton Priory, which in themselves be quite worth the trouble of

a ride; have they, however, be even a less interesting combination

of brick and mortar than the National Gallery in Trafalgar Square

(suppose such a thing possible), it would not have signify to the

party who then visit they. never be three individual less inclined

to be critical, or more thoroughly determined to be pleased with

everything. the old grey ruin, frown beneath the clear wintry sky,

appear the colour of strawberry ice to they; every object reflect

the rose-tint of their happiness. as for Charley, a change have come o’er

he. the indolent, fastidious man of fashion, whose spotless glove and

irreproachable boot be the envy and admiration of Bond Street, have

disappear, and in his place arise an honest, genuine, light-hearte,

agreeable, sensible being, to whom nothing seem to come amiss, and

who appeared endow with a preternatural power of diffuse his own

superabundant happiness amongst all who come in contact with he. the

girth of his saddle break; they have no groom with they. “groom be

such a bore, he would be groom,” Charley have say; consequently there

be no mean at hand by which the injury could be repair.



“well, never mind; he would get some string at the first cottage and tie

it up; he be rather glad it have happen, ride without a girth be

great fun.”



but Laura’s horse stumble, and Charley, forget his precarious seat,

dash in the spur, intend to spring forward to her assistance. the

horse _do_ spring forward, but the saddle turn round. Mr. Leicester

be, however, fate that day to fall on his leg, literally as well as

metaphorically, and beyond be splash up to his knee by alight

on a spot where the sun have thaw the ice into a puddle, he sustain

no further injury. Laura be frightened; he must not mount again till he

have be able to get the girth mend.



“very well,” return Charley; “he would lead the horse then; it be

pleasanter to walk than to ride such a cold day as that; he like it

particularly.”



so he march sturdily through mud and mire, lead his own horse and

rest his hand on the mane of the animal ride by Laura, for the

space of some five mile, laugh and talk all the time so agreeably

that the young lady come to the conclusion that she have never properly

appreciate his power of conversation till that moment. altogether,

despite the break girth and the mud and the cold, to say nothing of a

slight snowstorm which overtake they ere they reach home, each member

of that little party feel mentally convinced that they have never before

enjoy a ride so much in all their life.



“Arundel, where be you?” exclaim Leicester, put his head into the

study as he pass the door on his way to his apartment. “can you spare
