I five minute’ conversation?” he continue, as Lewis, close a book,

rise to receive he.



“certainly,” be the reply; “pray come in.”



“I’ve be wish to see you all day,” resume Leicester, carefully

shut the door and glance round the room. “where be your charge?”



“he be with the General,” be the reply. “he like to have he for

half-an-hour every day before he go to dress; he talk to he, and

try to instil into his mind correct notion regard thing in

general, and his own future social position in particular. Walter sit

still and listen, but I’m afraid he do not understand much about it.”



“no great loss either, I’ve a notion,” return Charley irreverently. he

pause, whistle a few bar of “Son Geloso,” entangle his spur in the

hearthrug, extricate it with much difficulty, then turn abruptly to

Lewis, he exclaim, “Arundel, I’m no hand at make fine speech, but

recollect if ever you want a friend I owe you more than I can possibly

repay you. not that this be such a very uncommon relation for I to

stand in towards people,” he add with a smile.



“Nay,” return Lewis, “you be reverse our position: I be your

debtor for my introduction to this family, and for an amount of kindness

and consideration which you must be place, like myself, in a dependent

situation fully to appreciate. but,” he add, glance at his friend’s

happy face, “I hope you have some good new’s to tell I?”



“you be right in your conjecture,” reply Leicester, “but it be mainly

owe to your straightforward and sensible advice that I have gain the

prize I strove for. I be within an ace of lose it, though;” and he

then give a hasty outline of his day’s adventure, with which the reader

have be already make acquainted.



Lewis congratulate he warmly on his good fortune. “you see I be

right when I tell you Miss Peyton be not so indifferent to you as you

imagine,” he say, “and that she like you, not because you be a man

of fashion, the admire of all admirer, but because she have sufficient

penetration to discover that you be something more--that you possess

high and well quality, and be not----”



“go on, my dear Arundel,” urge Leicester as Lewis pause, “go on. I

like plain speaking when it come from a friendly mouth.”



“the mere butterfly you strove to appear, I be go to say,” resume

Lewis; “but you will think I strangely impertinent.”



“not at all,” return Leicester, “it’s the truth; I can see it plainly

now. I’ve take as much trouble to make myself appear a fool as other

man do to gain a reputation for wisdom. well, it’s never too late to

mend. I shall turn over a new leaf from this time forth, give up dress,

restrict myself to one cigar a day, moderate my affection for pale ale,

invest capital in worst glove and a cotton umbrella, and become a

regular business character.” he pause, and draw a chair to the fire,

seat himself, and stretch out his leg, subject his boot, which

bear unmistakable trace of his pedestrian episode, to the influence of

the blaze wood. having thus make himself comfortable, he fall into a

fit of musing which last till, after gaze vacantly at his

extended leg for some moment, his feature suddenly assume an eager

expression, and he exclaim, “confound those blockhead, Schneider &

Shears: I suppose if I’ve tell they once I’ve tell they fifty time to

give more room in the leg for riding-trouser. a horse’s back be a wide

thing, and of course when you stretch your leg across it you require

the trouser to fit sufficiently loosely to accommodate themselves to

the position; they need not set like a couple of hop sack either; the

thing’s simple enough. I know if I’d a pair of scissor I could cut they

out myself.”



Glancing at Lewis as he speak, Leicester perceive that he be

struggle, not over successfully, to preserve his gravity, and the

absurdity of the thing strike he for the first time, he indulge in

a hearty laugh at his own expense ere he add, “Heigh-ho! it’s not so

easy to get rid of old habit as one imago. I see it will take I

long to unpuppyise myself than I be aware of. seriously, however,

I don’t mean to continue a mere idler, live on my wife’s fortune. my

father have interest with Government, and I shall ask he to push it

and obtain for I some creditable appointment or other. he will have no

difficulty; the Hon. Charles Leicester, husband to the rich Miss

Peyton, will possess much strong claim upon his country than Charley

Leicester the portionless young son. in this age of humbug it be easy

enough to get a thing if you don’t care whether you have it or not; but

if you chance to be some poor wretch, to whom the obtaining it be life

or death, ten to one but you be do out of it. poverty be the only

unpardonable sin in these day; the worship of the golden calf be a

specie of idolatry to which Christians be prone as well as Jews.

it’s rare to find a sceptic as to that religion, even amongst the most

inveterate unbeliever.”



Lewis, to whom leicester in his self-engrossment have not perceive that

his remark would apply, bit his lip and colour; then wish to save

his companion the mortification of discover that he have accidentally

wound his feeling, he hasten to change the conversation by

observing--



“how will the magnanimous Marmaduke bear the news of your success?”



“oh! to be sure, I be go to tell you about he when something put

it out of my head,” return Leicester. “the great De Grandeville be

great than ever on the subject--it be such fun. he come up to I

after breakfast this morning, and catch hold of my button, begin:

‘Ar--Mr. Leicester--excuse--ar--won’t detain you five minute,

but--ar--you see in regard to--ar--the matter we converse on yesterday,

when you be good enough to give I the benefit of your opinion

concern a certain propose alliance, if I may call your attention

once more to the subject; you will perceive that--ar--the affair have

assume a very different aspect--ar--indeed so completely different that

I feel confident you will agree with I in consider the--ar--in fact

the arrangement no long desirable.’



“I tell he I be quite prepared to think as he do on this point, and

beg to know in what the mysterious impediment consist. ‘well,

sir--ar--I don’t say it--ar--by way of a boast--ar--such thing be

quite out of my line, but you must have yourself perceive the

very marked encouragement which my advance meet with yesterday

evening--ar--in fact the game be--ar--in my own hand!’ I succeed

in repress a strong desire to kick he, and he continue with bland

dignity: ‘Ar--find that this be the case, I feel that, as a man of

honour, I be bind--ar--to make up my mind definitely as to my future

course, and have--ar--all but resolve to acquaint the young lady with

the brilliant, that be--ar--in many point unexceptionable position

which await she, when fortunately--I might say providentially--it

occur to I to open a letter I have that evening receive from my

friend in the Herald’s College. imagine my horror to learn that

her actual father, the immediately previous Peyton himself,

have--ar--_horresco referai_, as Pliny have it-- ’pon my word it quite

upset I!’



“‘this dreadful papa, have he murder somebody?’ inquire I.



“‘no, sir,’ be the answer; ‘Lord Ferrers and other man with

unexceptionable pedigree have commit that crime. there be

nothing necessarily vulgar about murder; the case be far bad. this

intolerable proximate ancestor, who have not rest in his dishonour

grave above half-a-dozen year, be not only guilty of belong to an

intensely respectable firm in Liverpool, but have actually be insane

enough to allow his name to be enter as sleep partner in a large

retail house on Ludgate Hill! Fancy a De Grandeville marry the

daughter of ‘Plumpstern & Peyton’, dealer in cotton goods!”--’pon

my word, sir, it take away my breath to think of the narrow escape I’d

have!’ ‘and the young lady?’ inquire I.



“‘Ar--of course it will be--ar--disappointment, as I’ve no doubt she

consider--ar--that she’d make her book cleverly and stand to win, as

the bet man say; but--ar--she soon have tact enough to perceive that

the grape be sour--ar--take that tone immediately,--clever girl,

sir, very--ar--I shouldn’t wonder if she be to give out that she have

discourage my attention--ar--in fact, virtually refuse I--ar--I

shall not contradict she, I owe she that--ar--with the exception

of yourself, Mr. Leicester, her secret will be perfectly safe in my

keeping.’ it be now my turn; so draw myself up as stiffly as

old Grant himself, I say, ‘confidence beget confidence, Mr. De

Grandeville; so in return for your candour allow I to inform you that

Miss Peyton, doubtless drive to despair by your desertion, have do I

the honour to accept I as your substitute! one word more,’ I continue,

as, completely take aback, he flush crimson and begin stammer out

apologetical ejaculation, ‘I have listen in silence to your account

of the transaction. I confess I have my own opinion about the matter,

but should you adhere to your intention of preserve a strict secrecy

in regard to the affair, I shall do so likewise; if not, I may feel

call on to publish a somewhat different version of these love

passage--one which will scarcely prove so agreeable to your

self-esteem; unless, indeed,’ I add, see that he be about to

bluster, ‘you prefer settle the business in a short way; in which

case I shall be quite at your service.’ so say I raise my hat,

bow, and turn on my heel, leave he to his meditation.”



“which must have be of a singularly unsatisfactory nature, I should

imagine,” return Lewis, laugh. “but there be no chance of your

fighting, I hope?”



“not the slight, I expect,” reply Leicester. “De Grandeville, to do

he justice, be no coward, but he will have sense enough to see that

he can gain no _éclât_ by give the affair publicity, and will remain

quiet for his own sake. luckily, I’m not of a quarrelsome temperament,

or I should have horse-whip he, or at least try at it, when he be

talk about Laura.”



“it be a temptation which in your place I could not have resist,”

 rejoin Lewis.



“ah, it’s easy to be magnanimous when one be happy,” return Leicester;

“besides, I really be rather sorry for the poor devil, for, as I

dare say you’ve guess long ago, it be clear Laura refuse he last

night--in fact she as good as tell I so.”



“perhaps it may benefit he,” remark Lewis. “his vanity be too

plethoric, and a little judicious lowering may conduce to the general

health of his moral system.”



“I’m afraid it’s a case of too long standing,” reply Leicester. “such

a lamentable instance of egotism on the brain be not so easily to be

cure; however, he’s have a pretty strong dose this time, I must confess.

and now, see that my boot have be wet through for the last three

hour, the soon I get rid of they the well.” so say, Charley

Leicester take himself off, preparatory to perform the same operation

on his perfidious boot.









CHAPTER XXX.--THE GENERAL take the field.



the interview which Lewis have witness between Lord Bellefield and

the girl dwelt in his thought, and be a source of much doubt and

uneasiness to he. the quiet, seclude life he have lead for the last year

afford ample time for meditation--the almost total want of society

(for poor Walter be no companion)--the peculiar position in which he

be place, shut out from all the pleasure and excitement natural

to his age and taste--have give an unusually reflective turn to his

vigorous mind, and produce in he a gravity and depth of character,

to which, under different circumstance, he might never have attain.

thus, in the view he take of life, he be accustom to look beyond

the surface, and deem it unworthy of a believer in the truth of

Christianity to attribute event to the mere caprice of a blind destiny,

be rather disposed to trace in such occurrence the finger of a

direct Providence, and to consider they as opportunity purposely

throw in our way, for the use or abuse of which we should one day be

call to account, as for every talent commit to our charge. hold

these opinion, he could not be content to sit down quietly with the

knowledge of which he have become possessed without make some effort

to prevent Lord Bellefield from successfully accomplish the evil he

could not doubt he meditate. but what then should he do? the question

be not an easy one to answer. the most natural and effectual mean to

employ would be to inform General Grant of the affair; he be the person

likely (as the father of his future bride) to possess most influence

over Lord Belle-field, while as possessor of the estate on which they

reside he be certain to meet with respect and obedience from the

parent of the girl. but besides the dislike every honourable man feel

to undertake the office of tale-bearer, Lewis’s chivalrous nature shrink

from even the appearance of seek to wreak his revenge on the man

who have insult he, by injure he in the opinion of his future

father-in-law. again, be he to find out the girl and expostulate with

she, he feel certain he should produce no good effect--the fact of she

be aware of the term on which he stand with her admirer would render

her suspicious of his intention, and prevent she from pay any regard

to his argument. at last it occur to he to mention the thing to

Charles Leicester, and persuade he, if possible, to visit the girl, and

at all event to make she aware of the deceit which have be practise

upon she by his brother in assume his name. accordingly, he determine

to seek an early opportunity of speak to Leicester on the subject;

but good resolution be always more easy to form than to carry into

effect. on the follow morning Leicester go to town, as well to

acquaint his father with the important step he contemplate as to bear

in person an invitation to an old family friend and _ci-devant_ guardian

of Laura Peyton’s to join the party at Broadhurst; nor do he return

till after several day have elapse, so that it be nearly a week ere

Lewis find the opportunity he seek for.



there have be a dinner-party at Broadhurst, and, as be the custom

of the neighbourhood, the guest have depart early. Lewis wait till

Leicester have dispose of a lady whom he be hand to a carriage, then

draw he aside, he make he acquaint with the interview which

he have involuntarily witness, inform he at the same time of his

object in so do. as he proceed with his tale Leicester’s brow grow

dark.



“it be really too bad of Bellefield,” he mutter, “situate as he be

in regard to this family; it show a want of all proper feeling--all

delicacy of mind--assume my name, too! suppose it have come to Laura’s

ear by any chance--’pon my word I’ve a great mind to speak to

he about it--though, I don’t know, it would only lead to a

quarrel--Bellefield be not a man to brook interference. I feel quite as

you do in regard to the affair, my dear Arundel, but really I don’t see

that I can do anything that would be of the slight use.”



“surely you can find out the girl and prove to she the truth of my

statement, that your brother have deceive she by assume your name--you

owe that to yourself.”



“she would be certain to tell he of it the next time she see he,”

 return Leicester uneasily; “it would lead to a quarrel between we,

and you don’t know what Bellefield’s resentment be when it’s once

excited--it’s actually terrific, and that’s the truth.”



“but for your cousin, Miss Grant’s sake, you ought not to let your

brother proceed with this affair,” urge Lewis; “surely you must see the

matter in this light?”



“ah! poor Annie,” return Leicester with a half sigh; “I sometimes wish

that engagement have never be enter into. I doubt whether they be at

all calculate to render each other happy. in fact, I’ve learn to

look upon marriage in a very different light lately. however, it’s no

business of mine; wiser head will have to settle it, luckily----” he

pause, and after a few moment’ deliberation resume abruptly, “I’ll

do as you advise, Arundel. I’ll see this girl and talk to she, and if

Bellefield hear of it and make himself disagreeable, why it cann’t be

help, that’s all. he should not attempt such thing, particularly in

this neighbourhood. he ought to have more respect for the General and

his daughter; it show a want of good taste and good feeling.

besides, as well as I can judge from the glimpse I have of she in the

refreshment-room, the girl’s not so unusually pretty, after all. she’d

an awful pair of hand, if I recollect right.”



a contemptuous smile pass across Lewis’s handsome feature as his

companion promulgate the above original moral distinction. Leicester,

however, do not observe it, and continued--



“just fancy my come out in the character of a virtuous mentor. I only

hope I shall get through my arduous duty without laugh at my own

performance. ’pon my word, though, it’s rather serious when a man

feel inclined to scoff at himself for do his duty from the sheer

inconsistency of the thing. I tell you what, Arundel, I believe I’ve

be a very naughty boy without in the least know it. I’ve

always consider myself the victim of circumstance, and set all my

peccadillo down to that account; but I don’t see why I need bother you

by make you my father confessor.”



Lewis, consider the train of thought into which Leicester have fall,

one likely to lead to useful, practical result, be about to encourage

he to proceed, when a servant approach they and place a small,

crumple, and not over clean piece of paper in Lewis’s hand. hold it

under the light of a lamp, he be enable with difficulty to decipher

the following words:--



“to Muster Arundel.--Sur, the party as you know of be hout to-night,

and more of his sort along vith he. Ve be safe for a shindy; but

be quite ready for ther blackguard, live in good ’ope hof a

capture--hin which hif you like to assist, not mind a crack o’ ther

head, should sich occur, which will sometimes in ther well regerlate

famurlie, plea to follur ther bearer, as will conduct you to your

humbel servaunt to commarnd,



“J. Millar.”



“that’s glorious!” exclaim Lewis, place the missive in the hand of

his companion. “I never do catch a poacher in my life, but I’ve often

wish to do so; the whole scene must be so picturesque and unlike

anything one have ever meet with--the darkness, the excitement--but you

be laugh at my eagerness. well, I confess to a love of adventure

for its own sake; if I’d live in the middle age I should have be a

knight-errant, that’s certain. I suppose it’s no use ask you to join

we? there’s metal more attractive in the drawing-room, _n’est-ce pas?_”



“why,” return Charley, arrange his neckcloth by aid of a glass

place in the cloak-room for the benefit of the lady who wish to

wrap up becomingly, “really I must own I prefer Laura’s smile even

to the delight of a possible _rencontre_ with your friend, Mr.

What’s-his-name, the poacher.”



“Hardy be the fellow’s name,” reply Lewis. “he be a chartist and all

sort of horror, so that I don’t feel the small degree of sympathy

for he. do you know where the General be to be find? I suppose, as I

may be very late, or even oblige to sleep at Millar’s cottage, I must

ask his sanction ere I start on my expedition.”



“I think you’d well,” return Leicester; “he’s in the library. I

see he go there after he have see Lady Runnymede to her carriage; so

good-night. I shall be curious to learn in the morning whose brain have

be knock out.” and with this agreeably suggestive remark Leicester

end the conversation and stroll off to the drawing-room.



Lewis proceed at once to the library, where he find not only General

Grant, but, to his extreme annoyance, Lord Bellefield also; there be,

however, no help for it, and he accordingly explain his wish as

briefly as possible. the General hear he to the end without speak.

his first idea be that such a request be strange and unbecoming the

peaceful gravity that should environ the office of a tutor, and he

intend to favour he with a dignified refusal; but as Lewis proceed,

his eager tone and sparkle eye recall to the old officer the day

of his youth when the spirit of enterprise be strong within he, and in

the wild bivouac, the dash assault, the hand-to-hand struggle “I’

the imminent deadly breach,” and the many exciting vicissitude of a

campaign life, he have find a degree of pleasure which his age know

not, and he be fain to accord a gracious assent.



“your father be a soldier, Mr. Arundel, I think you tell I?”



Lewis reply in the affirmative, mention some engagement in which he

have particularly distinguish himself the General listen to he with

complacency, then exclaimed--



“that’s it, sir, that’s it! I confess when I first hear your request,

I consider it unnatural, in fact, unbecoming in a civilian, but in a

soldier’s son it assume an entirely different character. I like to

see spirit in a young man.” (here he glance at Lord Bellefield, who,

apparently engross by a legal document which he be peruse, seem

unconscious of Lewis’s presence.) “it’s a pity your father! be unable

to afford you a commission: there’s be some very pretty fighting in

India lately, and you might have distinguish yourself.” he pause,

then add, “I know most of the agricultural labourer about here; do

Millar tell you any of these poacher’ name?”



“Hardy, a blacksmith, be the most notorious character,” return Lewis.



as he mention the name Lord Bellefield start so violently that he

nearly overturn the lamp by which he be read. see the General’s

eye fix on he inquiringly, he rise, and put his hand to his

side, draw a deep breath as he exclaimed--



“one of those sharp stitch, as they call they--nothing bad. you know

I be subject to they; it’s want of exercise produce indigestion. I

tell you what,” he continue, “I’ve rather a curiosity to witness

Mr. Arundel’s prowess, and see what sport this poacher will afford.

man-hunting, in the literal _feræ naturæ_ sense of the term, will be a

new excitement.”



“we’ll all go,” exclaim the General, spring up with the alertness

of a young man. “if these rascal choose to trespass on my land and

destroy my property, who so fit to resist they and bring they to justice

as myself? I’ll make the necessary alteration in my dress, and we’ll

start immediately.”



Lord Bellefield urge the lateness of the hour, the cold night air, the

chance of danger to life or limb--but in vain; General Grant have take

the crotchet into his head, and he be not the man to be easily induce

to change his mind. accordingly Lewis find himself suddenly associate

with two as strange companion as ever a man be embarrassed withal.

still there be no help for it; and inwardly ponder what possible

reason Lord Bellefield could have for join the expedition, and why

he have start at the mention of Hardy’s name, Lewis hasten to wrap

himself in a rough pea-jacket, and select a heavy knot stick,

wherewith he propose to knock respect for the right of property into

the head of any misguided individual who might be deaf to all milder

argument. as he return to the hall the General make his appearance,

carry under his arm a cavalry sabre; his bearing be even more stiff

and erect than usual, and his eye flash with all the fire of youth.



“early on parade, I see, Mr. Arundel,” he say, with something more

nearly approach to a smile on his countenance than Lewis have ever

previously observe there. “we’ll read those poach rascal a lesson

they will not easily forget, sir.”



as he speak a light footstep be hear approach, and in another

moment Annie Grant bound down the staircase, her glossy curl

stream wildly over her shoulder, and her cheek flush with the

speed at which she have come.



“my dear papa!” she begin, then turn pale as her eye fall upon the

sword, she continue: “oh! it be really true! I hope they be only

deceive I in jest. dearest papa, you will be good and kind, and not

go out after these man? suppose any accident should occur? think how

valuable your life be--papa, you will not go?”



“Annie, I think you be perfectly aware of my extreme dislike to, or

I may say disapproval of all uncalled-for display of feel. I

be about to perform a duty incumbent on my position, and I need scarcely

add that any attempt to induce I to neglect that duty will not only

prove ineffectual, but will be highly displease to I. not another

word,” he continue, see she be about to resume her entreaty;

“return immediately to the drawing-room and apologise to our friend in

my name for be oblige to leave they.”



at this moment a servant announce that his master’s shooting pony be

at the door, and that Lord Bellefield have already start; so place

his hat on his head with an air of offended dignity, the General march

proudly out of the hall. Lewis be about to follow he, when, glance

at Annie, he perceive that she have sink into a chair, and cover she

face with her hand, have give way to an irrepressible burst of tear.

the young tutor pause--wish to reassure she by promise to use

his good effort to shield her father from danger, and yet fear to

intrude upon her grief. in his embarrassment he accidentally drop his

stick; start at the sound, Annie for the first time perceive he,

and spring up, she come hurriedly towards he, exclaiming--



“oh, Mr. Arundel! I be so glad you be go. _you_ will take care of

papa, will you not?”



as she speak she lay her hand on his arm and gaze up into his face

imploringly.



“I will most assuredly try to do so, Miss Grant,” return Lewis

calmly, though that light touch thrill through he like a shock of

electricity. “you need not alarm yourself so greatly,” he continue,

anxious to soothe she; “believe I, your apprehension have greatly

exaggerate any probable danger.”



“you really think so?” return Annie doubtfully. “at all event,” she

continue, “I shall be much happy now I know you be go. I be sure

you will try and take care of papa.”



“I will, indeed,” return Lewis earnestly, as, glance towards the

door, he essay to depart; but Annie, completely engross by

her anxiety to secure his service on her father’s behalf, still

unconsciously retain her hold on his arm, and Lewis be oblige gently

to remove the little hand that detain he. as their finger meet,

Annie, become suddenly aware of what Miss Livingstone would have

term she “indiscreet and unpardonable heedlessness,” blush very

becomingly; then with a sudden impulse of gratitude and warm feeling she

extend her hand to Lewis, saying--



“thank you very much for all your kindness, Mr. Arundel. mind you take

good care of yourself as well as of papa--I shall not go to bed till I

hear you have bring he safe home again.”



Lewis press the fair hand offer to he, repeat his assurance that

her alarm be unnecessary, and hasten to follow General Grant. Annie

gaze after he with tearful eye, but his word comfort she. she have

already begin to rely on he in moment of difficulty or of danger.



the moon be shine brightly, though flit cloud pass from time

to time across its silvery disc, wrap wood and hill and valley in

momentary darkness, only to enhance their beauty when its pale, cold

ray once more fall uninterruptedly upon they, impart to the scene

the magic of a fairy twilight. such, however, be scarcely Lewis’s

thought as, haunt by the appealing expression of Annie’s soft eye,

he hasten to overtake his companion. the party proceed in silence,

follow their guide, who be none other than the renegade “Villiam,”

 across one of the wild portion of the park towards a young larch

plantation cover about forty acre of ground. this spot, name Tod’s

Hole Spinney, from certain fox earth that have exist in it till

their occupant’ partiality for dining on pheasant have lead to their

ejectment, be consider, from its isolated situation, the thick growth

of underwood, the fact of a running stream pass through it, and

other propitious circumstance, the most amply stock preserve on the

property, and it be with a degree of annoyance proportion to the

enormity of the offence that the General learn this be the place

select by the poacher for the scene of their depredation. as they

approach the spot the report of a gun be hear, follow by three or

four other in rapid succession. General Grant, irritate beyond control

by this audacity, immediately ride forward at a brisk trot. Lewis,

bear in mind Annie’s injunction, grasp the crupper of the saddle

firmly with his left hand, and with this slight assistance run by the

General’s side, keep pace with the horse. in this manner they have

nearly reach the wood, when a man spring from behind a bush, and would

have seize the horse’s bridle have not Lewis interpose, say, in a

low voice, “don’t you know we, Millar? it be General Grant, who, when he

hear the poacher be out, determine to come with I.”



“I beg yer honour’s pardon,” return the keeper, touch his hat as he

recognise his master. “I never expect to ha’ see you here to-night,

to be sure.”



“I be usually to be find where my duty call I,” return the General

stiffly. “these scoundrel seem to be out in force,” he continue.



“Veil, I take it there’s as many on ’em as ve shall know wot to do

with,” be the reply; “but I’ve get above a dozen man on the look-hout,

only in course they’re scatter.”



“and how do you propose to act?” inquire the General.



“I thort of take a party into the wood, try to captiwate long Hardy

and one or two of the ringleader, chap as I’ve have my eye on for ever

so long; then take ther game from the tother, and seize their gun hif

posserbul. but the chief thing be to captiwate that willain hardy; so I

mean to leave three or four man on the look-hout, in case he manage to

do we and break cover.”



“your plan seem a good one,” return the General reflectively. “how

many man do you propose to take into the wood with you?”



“Veil, there’s half-a-dozen lad a lay down behind those bush

yonder, and there’s two more jist inside that gap; then there’s myself

and Muster Arundel.”



“let the boy that guide we hold my horse,” begin General Grant.



“Hif I might adwise,” interrupt Millar, “yer honour would remain in

this wery place; and hif Hardy should get away from we--as he’s likely

enough, for he’s as strong and houdacious as a steam-ingine--he’s a-most

sure to break cover here; in vich case yer honour can ride he down, and

hif he dare to show fight, give he a cut hover the skull with yer long

sword there.”



“you feel sure he will endeavour to effect his retreat on this side?”

 inquire the General doubtingly.



“sartain sure, I may say,” cry Millar confidently; then, as his master

turn to explain to Lord Bellefield, who have just come up, the plan of

operation, he add in a low voice, so that Lewis only might hear--



“the old Gineral’s pluckey enough for anything, but his leg ain’t so

young as they use to be, and he’s rather touch in the vind, vich

von’t do for sich a valk as we’ve get before we.”



at this moment more shot be hear in the wood, but apparently much

near than the last. the poacher be evidently advance in that

direction.



“there be not a moment to be lose, Millar,” exclaim the General

eagerly. “I think as you say, I may be of more use here. some one _must_

remain outside to cut off the retreat of these fellow if you should

succeed in drive they out of the wood. Lord Bellefield will accompany

your party. where be the other watcher on this side station?”



“about fifty yard apart, along the ditch skirt the wood. if yer

honour want help, a note on this whistle will produce it.” so say,

Millar hand he an ivory dog-whistle; then sign to “Villiam” to

proceed, and request Lord Bellefield and Lewis to follow he, the

keeper conduct they along a narrow track lead into the wood.



“do you really expect that Hardy will attempt to cross that part of the

park, or be your assertion merely a white lie, frame to secure the

General’s safety?” ask Lewis as he walk by the keeper’s side.



“Veil, it worn’t altogether a lie,” be the reply; “for if we don’t nab

the gentleman, that’s the side he’ll try for, as it’s easy for he

to get away; but if I vonce have a fair hit at he, I don’t mean to

leave he a chance to get away. I shall not stand nice about hurt he

neither, I can tell yer. he beat Sam Jones, one o’ my hunder-keeper,

so savage that the poor feller worn’t out of his blessed bed for two

month. he deserve summut pretty strong for that.”



“mind you point he out to I, if you catch sight of he,” rejoin

Lewis. “I be most anxious to be introduce to this truculent gentleman.”



“yer cann’t mistake he hif yer once set eye on he,” return the

keeper; “he’s half a head tall than any of the rest of ’em, but I’ll

show he to yer.”



as he speak they reach the spot where the six man be wait,

though, so well have they conceal themselves, Lewis be close upon they

ere he be aware of their vicinity.



“now, my lad, be yer all ready?” inquire their leader in a low voice.

an answer in the affirmative be follow by the order--“Come on, then;”

 when Lord Bellefield interpose by say, “one moment! listen to I, my

man: I offer five guineas reward to any of you who may secure Hardy.”









CHAPTER XXXI.--IS CHIEFLY CULINARY, contain recipe for a “good

preserve” and a “PRETTY PICKLE.”



after a strict injunction from Millar to preserve silence, the party

in search of Hardy and his associate again move forward, Lord

Belle-field, Millar, and Lewis in front, and the other follow two

abreast. as soon as they have enter the wood the remain man join

they, make altogether a company of eleven. as they advance far

into the plantation, the bough of the tree, become thick and more

closely interlace, intercept the moonlight and render their onward

progress a matter of some difficulty. the gamekeeper however, know

every intricacy of the path, and could have find his way in the darkest

night as easily as at noonday. after wind among the tree for some

minute they come upon a little glade where the underwood have be

partially clear away and a small quantity of barley stack for the

purpose of feed the pheasant. at the entrance to the space thus

clear the party halt, and Millar, creep forward on his hand and

knee, reach the stack. shelter himself behind it, he make his way

to the opposite side, where he be lose to sight; reappear almost

immediately, he cautiously rejoin the other, say in a low whisper:

“I expect how it would be; there be from twenty or thirty pheasand

roost on the tree beyond the stack there, and Hardy and his mate

be aware on it, be a-making of their way through the bush right

ahead. I could hear ’em plain enough when I be at the stack yonder.

now, two on yer must come along o’ I, creep to the stack and hide

behind it as yer see I do, then vait till they blackguard have let

fly at the pheasand, and afore they can load again ve three must jump

forrard and try and captiwate Hardy. in the meantime, you other must

make yer way round through the bush and take ’em in the rear, and

help we if we want help.”



“which you will do most certainly,” return Lord Bellefield. “I’ll lead

the party that remain.”



“and I’ll go with you, Millar,” observe Lewis.



“and you, Sam,” continue Millar, address the under-keeper before

allude to. the man come forward, and place himself by Lewis’s side,

the three creep along till they have reach the stack, shelter by

which they again stand upright. Scarcely have they take their place

when two gun, follow by four other, be discharge in rapid

succession, and so close to they, that the shot patter amongst the

underwood be distinctly audible, and one of the wound pheasant

drop at Lewis’s foot; while almost immediately afterwards a couple of

man run forward to collect the fall game. the foremost of these be a

fellow of Herculean proportion: as he stoop to pick up a pheasant a

ray of moonlight reveal his feature, and Lewis immediately recognise

his former antagonist, the tall Chartist. at the same moment Millar

whisper, “that’s our man; go ahead!”



[illustration: 0224]



“leave he to I,” return Lewis eagerly; and bend forward, with a

bind like that of a tiger he spring upon he.



the poacher be take so completely by surprise (his back be turn

towards his assailant) that Lewis, encircle he with a grasp of iron,

be enable to pinion his arm to his side. like a wild bull catch in

the toil, his struggle to free himself be tremendous; but Lewis, now

in the full vigour of his strength, be an adversary not easily to be

shake off, and despite his unrivalled power, the poacher fail

to extricate his arm. shout, therefore, to his companion for

assistance, he desire he, with an oath, to shoot the ------ keeper;

but that individual be unable to comply with his comrade’s benevolent

suggestion by reason of certain well-direct blow wherewith Sam Jones,

the under-keeper, be produce a marked alteration in the general

outline of his feature. in the meantime, Millar, draw forth a piece

of cord, begin coolly to tie Hardy’s wrist together, disregard a

series of ferocious kick with which he assail he. at this moment the

other poacher, to the number of some half-dozen, attract by the

sound of blow, reach the scene of action, but the party lead by Lord

Bellefield be equally on the alert, and the fight become general. and

now the capture of the poacher Hardy appear certain: exhaust by his

unavaile struggle to free himself from Lewis’s encircle arm,

he could offer no effectual resistance to Millar, who continue most

methodically to bind his wrist, in no way divert from his purpose

by the storm of blow which rage around he, many of which fall on his

unprotected person, when suddenly the report of a pistol rung sharply

above the other sound of the combat, and an acute, sting pain dart

through Lewis’s leave shoulder, cause he such agony for the moment

that he involuntarily relax his grasp. Hardy be not slow to avail

himself of the opportunity thus offer. fling off the young tutor

with so much violence that he would have fall have not one of the

gamekeeper’s assistant catch he and prevent it, he wrench his

they be, strike the keeper such a severe blow on the side of the head

that he reel and fall; then see that his companion, overpower by

number and dishearten by his suppose capture, be give way on

all side, he turn, and dash into the bush, disappear, not

so quickly, however, but that Lewis, who, despite his wound, have never

take his eye off he for a moment, perceive the movement.



grasp his stick, which he have contrive to retain during the

struggle, firmly with his right hand, he lose no time in follow the

fugitive, and guide by the crashing of the bush, keep close on his

trace till they reach the boundary hedge; break his way through

this obstacle with the strength and fury of some wild animal, the

he have a desperate man to deal with, and fear that although the first

severe pain have abate, and little more than a sensation of numbness

the wound he have receive, Lewis pause a moment to reconnoitre ere

he follow he. to his great delight, he perceive he have reach the

however, that the cord would not hold out much long, and unwilling

to lose the only advantage gain by his previous struggle, Lewis

determine once more to endeavour to seize he. shout, therefore,

to give notice to the watcher where their assistance be likely to

be require, he spring across the ditch and advanced towards his

antagonist. at first the poacher appear inclined to stand his ground;

but see his opponent be arm with a stout stick, and recollect

his own defenceless condition, he resolve to trust rather to

his unrivalled fleetness, and turn away with an exclamation of

contrive to reach this, his escape would become a matter of certainty.

the ground, which have once form part of an ancient roman camp, lie in

terrace, and this circumstance give Hardy, who know every inch of the

reach nearly half the centre of the space between the two wood ere

double on his pursuer, contrive to make such good use of his knowledge

of the ground, that when the bright moonlight again enable Lewis to

discern his retreating figure, he perceive, to his extreme chagrin and

no small satisfaction that, just as he be about to give up the chase as

hopeless, he catch sight of a man on horseback gallop in a direction

which must effectually cut off the poacher’s retreat. another moment

before he could arrive; and remember his promise to Annie, it

be with mingle feeling of anxiety for her father’s safety, and

self-reproach for having quit he, that he prepare to witness the

struggle. as soon as the General perceive the state of affair, he

wave his hand as a sign to Lewis; then draw his sabre, stand up in

his stirrup and ride gallantly at the poacher, shout to he at the

same time to stop and yield himself prisoner. Hardy pay no attention

to the summon, continue to run on till he feel the horse’s breath

hot upon his neck; then, as General Grant, after again call on he to

“surrender, or he would cut he down,” prepare to put his threat into

execution, he dodge aside to avoid the blow, and spring suddenly

upon the rider, dash the sword from his hand, and seize he by the

throat, endeavour to drag he off his horse. the old man, though take

by surprise, clung firmly to his saddle, and spur his horse, try

to shake off his assailant; but his strength unfortunately be not equal

to his courage, and the poacher, snatch at the rein, back the horse

till it rear almost erect and fling its rider forcibly to the ground.

apparently bent on revenge, Hardy, still retain his grasp on the

bridle, lead the horse over the fallen body of the man, with the brutal

intention of trample he to death. but the generous instinct of the

animal serve to frustrate his evil purpose; as, though he lead it twice

directly across its prostrate master, the horse raise its foot and

carefully avoid treading on he. strike the animal ferociously on

the head with his clench fist, he next attempt to back it in the

same direction, but the frightened animal spring aside and plunge so

violently that he be unable to effect his design. he be still strive

reach the spot. instantly leap over the fallen man, stick in hand,

he strike Hardy so severe a blow on the wrist that he be force to quit

blow, forget all prudential consideration; and heedless of the approach

of three of the watcher, who, attract by the noise of the struggle,

be rapidly hasten towards the spot, he rush upon Lewis, and

disregard a heavy blow with which the young tutor greet he,

fling his arm round he and endeavour to dash he to the ground.

fortunately for Lewis, he be not ignorant of the manly exercise of

wrestling, and his proficiency in the art stand he in good stead at

this moment; for, despite his gigantic strength, Hardy could not succeed

in throw he. in vain do he lift he from the ground; with whatever

give way--the only effect of his exertion be to exhaust his own

immediately fling themselves upon the poacher, while the third drag

prostrate foe. have with some difficulty succeed in so doing, the

be now a comparatively easy task, for his final struggle with Lewis

have exhaust even the poacher’s strength, and after a futile attempt

in sullen silence, while his arm be secure with the General’s

sword-belt. this operation conclude, the man who have rescue Lewis

return to he and find he sufficiently recover to sit up.



“have you look to the General? be he uninjured?” be his first

question.



“I’m afear’d he’s terrible hurt, if he ain’t kill outright; leastway

he’s onsensibul, and one of his arm seems crush like,” be the

consolatory reply.



“oh that I have come up a minute soon!” exclaim Lewis in a tone of

bitter self-reproach.



“you’d have be a dead man if yer have, sir,” be the reply. “if that

willian there have have hold of your throat half a minute long, you’d

have be as stiff as a leg of mutton by this time.”



“well that I have perish than that this should have occur,”

 murmur Lewis; then turn to the man, he continue, “lend I your

arm; I can walk now,” and rise with difficulty, he advance towards

the spot where General Grant lie. he be perfectly insensible; his hat

have fall off, and his grey hair, expose to the night dew, impart,

as the moonlight stream on it, a ghastly expression to his feature,

while his right arm be bent under he in an unnatural position, which

leave no doubt that it must be break, probably in more place than one.

Lewis kneel down beside he, and raise his uninjured hand, place his

finger on the wrist.



“I can feel his pulse beat distinctly,” he observe, after a moment’s

pause. “he be not dead, nor die; indeed, except the injury to his arm,

I hope he may not be seriously hurt. no time must be lose in carry

he to the house and procure a surgeon.”



“somebody ought to go to Broadhurst to let’m know what’s happen and

get we some help. we’ve more than we can manage here, you see,” urge

the assistant. “it will take two on we to purwent that blackguard Hardy

from get away; he willn’t lose no chance, you may depend.”



“I’ll stay with General Grant if you’ll run to the house,” return

Lewis feebly.



“your arm’s a bleeding, sir. do that willian stab you?” inquire the

assistant.



“no; I be hurt in the wood,” be the reply.



“do you think you could ride, sir?” continue the man; “ ’cos if

you could, I’d try and catch the horse--he’s a grazin’ very quiet

yonder--and then you could go to the house, start off one of the groom

to fetch a doctor, send some of the people down here to help we, get yer

own wound dress, and break the news to the family well than such a

chap as I.”



this observation be a true one, and Lewis feel that it be so;

therefore, although he dread the task, and would rather have again

encounter the danger he have just escape than witness Annie Grant’s

dismay and sorrow when she should find her dark anticipation realise,

he agree to the arrangement; and as the man succeed in catch the

horse almost immediately, he mount with some difficulty and ride off

at speed, though the rapid motion increase the pain of his wound till

it become almost insupportable. he reach Broadhurst in less than ten

minute, never draw bridle till he enter the stable-yard, although

he turn so faint and dizzy on the way that more than once he be

nearly fall from the saddle. his first act be to despatch a mount

groom to procure a surgeon; he next send off four of the man-servant

with a hurdle convert into an extemporary litter, give they exact

direction where to find their master, and wait to see that they

start without loss of time; he then attempt to dismount, but be

unable to do so without assistance. having pause a few moment till

the faintness have again go off, he enter the house by the servant’

entrance, and call the butler aside, desire he to summon Mr.

Leicester as quietly as possible; then, sink into a chair and rest

his head on his hand, he await his arrival with ill-con seal

anxiety, dread lest some incautious person should abruptly inform

Annie of her father’s accident.









CHAPTER XXXII--LEWIS make a discovery and get into a “state of MIND.”



the end of the room at which Lewis have seat himself lie in shadow,

so that Leicester, who shortly make his appearance wrap in a

dressing-gown, could merely distinguish the outline of his figure.



“why, Arundel,” he begin, “be anything the matter? here have Wilson be

and rouse I out of my first sleep, with a face like that of the party

who ‘draw Priam’s curtain I’ the dead o’ the night.’ where’s Governor

Grant, and how be it that you’re home first?”



“it’s no joking matter, Mr. Leicester,” return Lewis faintly, and

without raise his head. “the poacher have give we more trouble than

we expect, and in attempt to capture Hardy the General have be

throw from his horse. his right arm be break in two place, and when I

come away he be still insensible.”



from the position in which Lewis sit (his elbow rest on a table and

his forehead support by his hand) he be unable to perceive anything

that might be go on in the apartment, consequently he have continue

his speech, ignorant that a third person have join they. Annie (for she

it be who, pale as some midnight ghost, have glide noiselessly into the

room) lay her hand on Leicester’s arm to prevent his call attention

to her presence, while eager and tremble she listen to Lewis’s

account of her father’s accident; and overcome for the moment by these

evil tiding, she remain speechless, lean against a chair for

support. Lewis, surprised at Leicester’s silence, raise his head

languidly, and the first object that meet his eye be Annie Grant’s

sink figure. with an exclamation of dismay he attempt to start

up, but he be become so weak from loss of blood that he fail to

accomplish his purpose. rouse by the action, Annie recover herself,

and as a new idea strike she, she asked--



“where, then, be poor papa? have they bring he home? I must go to he

instantly!”



“he be not yet arrive, Miss Grant,” return Lewis in a low voice that

tremble with conflicting emotion; “his own servant be carry he,

and a surgeon will be here instantly. I----” he pause abruptly, for

Annie, draw herself up, advanced towards he, and with flash eye

exclaimed--



“be this then the way in which you have fulfil your promise, Mr.

Arundel? I trust so implicitly to your assurance that you would watch

over he and protect he; and now you have not only fail he in the

moment of danger but desert he in his necessity, and secure your own

safety by come home to break my heart with these evil tiding. oh, I

be ashamed of you--grieve--disappoint!”



“Hush, my dear Annie,” observe Leicester soothingly. “arundel might not

be able to prevent this accident--you be too hasty.”



“no, no!” return Lewis in a low, broken voice, “I deserve her

reproach. I ought never to have quit he, and yet I do so

believe that I leave he in perfect safety. I could not bear to stand

inactive when other man be about to face danger; besides, I have

pledge myself to assist in capture this poacher.” he pause, then

add, “I have be to blame, Miss Grant, but I be not quite the

poltroon you imagine I. I do indeed leave your father that I might

accompany the attack party into the wood, but I strain every nerve

to come up with Hardy before General Grant encounter he; and although

that be impossible, I arrive in time to prevent he from force the

horse to trample the life out of the fallen man, and wound as I be, I

engage with and capture, at the risk of my own life, the ruffian who

have injure your father; nor should I have be here now, but that it

be necessary for some one to procure assistance and summon a surgeon,

and I ride back at speed to my own injury, that I might leave a more

efficient man with the General.”



as he cease speak the butler enter the room, bear in his hand

a lamp, and for the first time the light fall upon Lewis’s figure.

Leicester, as he behold he, utter an exclamation of surprise and

horror, which his appearance be well calculate to call forth. his face

be deadly pale, save a red line across the forehead, where some bramble

have tear the skin; his dark hair, heavy with the night dew, clung in

wild disorder around his temple; and his clothe, stain with mud,

bore trace of the severity of the struggle in which he have be

engage; the sleeve of his left arm, which still rest on the table,

be soak with gore, while the momentary excitement which have animate

he as he speak have give way to a return of the faintness produce by

the loss of blood, which be by this time very considerable. as this

ghastly figure meet her sight Annie utter a slight shriek--then a

sense of the cruel injustice of her own reproach banish every other

consideration, and spring towards he, she exclaimed--



“oh, Mr. Arundel, what can we do for you? how shock, how grieved I

am!--will you, can you forgive I?”



Lewis smile and attempt to reply, but the word die away upon his

lip, and completely overcome by faintness, he would have fall from

the chair have not Leicester support he. fortunately, at this moment

the surgeon arrive, and Annie quit the apartment, Lewis’s sleeve

be cut open, his wound temporarily bind up, and his temple bathe

with some stimulating essence which dispel his faintness, before the

surgeon’s service be require for General Grant. the latter gentleman

have recover consciousness ere he reach Broadhurst, and though

suffer acute pain from his broken arm, appear cool and collect.

his first question have be “whether Hardy have escape,” and he seem

to revive from the moment he be inform of his capture. his next

inquiry be who have take he, and on learn it be Lewis, he be much

pleased, muttering, “brave lad, brave lad! pity he’s not in the army.”

 he recognise Annie and speak kindly to she, give order for the safe

custody of Hardy, demand of the surgeon who examine his arm whether

he wish to amputate it, as he feel quite equal to the operation, and

in short, under circumstance which would have overpower any man of

less firmness of character, behave like a gentleman and a brave old

soldier, as he be. fortunately the surgeon (for a second, attract

by the rumour of an accident, as vulture be if a camel die in the

desert, have come to test the truth of the old proverb that two head

be well than one) succeed in set the arm, pronounce amputation

unnecessary, and after careful examination, give it as their opinion

that, with the exception of a few contusion of little consequence,

the General have sustain no further injury. having come to this

satisfactory conclusion, they find time to direct their attention to

Lewis. after much whisper consultation and considerable exchange of

learn wink and profound nod, they inform he that he have be

wound by a shot from a pistol (which, by the way, he could have tell

they), and that they have very little doubt that the ball remain in the

wound, in which case it would be necessary to extract it. to this Lewis

reply, “the soon the well.” accordingly they proceed to put he

to great agony by probe the wound to find the ball, after which they

hurt he still more in extract it, perform both operation with

such easy cheerfulness of manner and utter disregard of the patient’s

feeling, that a bystander would have imagine they be carve a cold

shoulder of mutton rather than the same joint of live humanity. but

surgeon, like father, have flinty heart, unmacadamise by the

small grain of pity for the wretched victim of their uncomfortable

skill; their idea of the “Whole Duty of Man” be that he should afford

they “an interesting case” when live, and become a “good subject”

 for they when he have cease to be one to the Queen. after the ball be

extract, Lewis request it might be hand to he; it be small, and

from its peculiar shape he perceive that it must have be discharge

from a pistol with a rifle barrel.



“if you will allow I,” he say, “I shall keep this bit of lead as a

memorial of this evening’s entertainment.”



“oh, certainly,” reply the most cheerful surgeon, “by all mean; if it

have but go an eighth of an inch far,” he add, rub his hand

joyously, “only an eighth of an inch, it would have injure the

spinal cord, and you would have be--droll how these thing occur

sometimes--you’d have be paralyse for life.”



Lewis shudder, and wish devoutly he be for the time be Caliph

Haroun Al’Raschid, in which case the facetious surgeon would have add a

practical acquaintance with the effect of the bastinado on the sole of

the human foot to his other medical knowledge.



“I don’t think,” resume the doctor meditatively, “I don’t think you

need apprehend any very unpleasant result, as far as I can as yet see

into the case. of course,” he continue with hilarity, “erysipela might

supervene, but that be seldom fatal, unless it affect the brain; and I

should hope the great effusion of blood will prevent that in the present

instance. you feel very weak, don’t you?”



Lewis reply in the affirmative, and his tormentor continued--



“well, you need not be uneasy on that score; I don’t apprehend a return

of syncope, but if you should feel an unnatural deficiency of vital

heat, or perceive any symptom of approach collapse, I would advise

your ring the bell, and I’ll be with you instantly. Scalpel’s

oblige to be off; he’s get a very interesting break leg--compound

fracture--wait for he down at the village, besides some dozen

agreeable minor casualty, the result of to-night’s work. keep up your

spirit, and go to sleep--your shoulder be easy now?”



“it feel as if the blade of a red-hot sword be be constantly

plunge into it,” return Lewis crossly.



“delighted to hear it,” reply Dr. Bistoury, rub his hand; “just

what I could have wish; nothing inert there! I would recommend your

bearing (which word he pronounce bea-a-a-ring) it quietly, and rely

upon my look in the first thing to-morrow.” so say he rub his

hand, chuckle, and depart.



in spite of his wound, which continue very painful, Lewis contrive to

get a few hour’ sleep, and awake so much refresh that he resist all

attempt to keep he in bed, and though stiff and weak to an excessive

degree, make his way to the study and cheat Walter out of the holiday

he have expect--a loss which he scarcely regret in his joy at

find that the wicked poacher have not seriously injure his dear Mr.

Arundel. and then Annie could not be happy till she have catch Charles

Leicester and make he accompany she on a penitential visit to Lewis,

to tell he how grieved she be at the recollection of her injustice to

he; it seem so dreadfully ungrateful, when in fact he have just save

her father’s life; and she look so pretty and good and pure in her

penitence, that Lewis begin to think woman be bright and high

being than his philosophy have dream of, and for the first time it

occur to he that he have be guilty of an unpardonable absurdity in

despise the whole race of womankind because he happen to have

be jilt by a little, coquettish, half-educate german girl; and he

forgive Annie so fully in his heart that with his lip he could scarcely

stammer out half-a-dozen unmeaning word to tell she so.



Leicester ask he in the course of the conversation whether he have any

idea which of the gang of poacher have fire the pistol, add that two

other have be take besides Hardy. Lewis pause for a moment ere he

reply, “that his back have be turn towards the man who shoot he,

and that it be too serious a charge to bring against any one without

more certain knowledge than he possess on the subject.” and having

say this, he immediately change the conversation.



as soon as Annie and her cousin withdraw, Millar the gamekeeper make

his appearance, full of congratulation on Lewis’s gallant conduct and

sympathy in regard to his wound.



“I cann’t imagine vitch o’ ther warmint could have have a pistol; it

worn’t neither o’ ther two as we captiwate, for I sarche ’em myself,

and never a blessed harticle have they get about ’em except ther usual

amount o’ bacca and copper hin ther breech’ pocket.”



“do you have any more fighting after I leave you to follow Hardy?” ask

Lewis.



“Veil, we do ’ave one more sharpish turn,” be the reply. “when the

blackguard see I down, they make a rush to recover the sack with the

game, and almost succeed, only Sam Jones pull I out of the crowd

and set I on my leg again, and I be so mad a-thinking that Hardy have

get clear away that I lay about I like one possess, they do tell

I; so we not only recover the game, but bag two o’ ther chap

themselves. by ther bye,” he continue, “Sam Jones come here with I;

he want to see yer when I’ve do with yer; he say he’s pick up

somethin’ o’ yourn, but he willn’t say what--he’s a close chap when he

like, be Sam. Howsomdever, I suppose he expect you’ll tip he a bob or

so, for it be he as ketche yer when Hardy first fling yer off. you’ve

pay _he_ for it sweetly, and no mistake; he’d get a lovely black hye,

and his right wrist be swell as big as two ven we march he hoff to

H---------- gaol this morning. and now I’ll vish yer good arternoon, Mr.

Arundel, and send Sam hup, if you’re agreeable.”



Lewis, with a smile at the equivocal nature of the phrase, signify his

agreeability, and the keeper take his departure. in another minute the

sound of heavy footstep announce the approach of Sam, who, having

obey Lewis’s injunction to “come in,” vindicate his title to the

attribute of “closeness” by carefully shut the door and apply

first his ear and then his eye to the keyhole ere he could divest his

cautious mind of a dread of eavesdropper. he then cross the room

on tip-toe, partly from a sense of the grave nature of his mysterious

errand, partly from respect to the carpet, the richness of which

oppress he heavily during the whole of his visit, restrict he to

the use of one leg only the great portion of the time.



“you have find something of mine, Millar tell I,” begin Lewis,

find that, ghost-like, his visitor appear to consider it a point of

etiquette not to speak first.



“you’re very kind, Mr. Arundel,” return his visitor, who, catch

sight at the moment of the gilt frame of an oil painting which hang

over the chimney, and believe it firmly to be pure gold, become so

overpower between that and the carpet that he scarcely dare trust

himself to speak in such an aristocratic atmosphere. “I’m much oblige

to you, sir. yes, I have find something, sir, but I don’t know disactly

as it’s altogether yourn.”



“what be it, my good fellow?” inquire Lewis, half amuse and half bore

by the man’s bashfulness.



a consolatory mistrust of the sterling value of the picture-frame have

by this time begin to insinuate itself into Sam’s mind, and reassure in

some degree by the doubt, he continued--



“I beg pardon, sir, but I hope you don’t feel so bad as might be

expect; you look shocking pale, surely.”



Lewis thank he for his inquiry, and say he believe the wound be

go on favourably.



“I’m sure I’m very glad to hear it, which be a mercy to be thankful

for; you look so bad, too,” return this sympathise visitor; then

lean forward so as to approach his lip to Lewis’s ear, he continue

in a loud whisper--



“have ye any notion who it be as fire the shot?”



Lewis start, and colour slightly, fix his eye on the man’s face

as he inquire abruptly--



“have you?”



forget his veneration for the carpet in the excitement of the

conversation, the suspicious under-keeper walk to the door and

again test the keyhole ere he venture to answer the question; then

approach Lewis, he thrust his hand into a private pocket in his

shooting-jacket, and draw thence something carefully wrap in a

handkerchief, he present it to the young tutor, saying--



“that’s what I’ve be and find, sir; I pick it up in the wood,

not twenty yard from the place where you stand when you be shoot, Mr.

Arundel.”



Lewis hastily unroll the handkerchief, and draw from its fold a small

pocket-pistol; _on_ the stock, which be richly inlay, be a silver

escutcheon with a coat of arm engrave upon it; from mark about the

nipple it have evidently be lately discharge, and on examination it

prove to have _a rifle barrel_. Lewis’s brow grow dark.



“it be then as I suspect,” he mutter; pause, however, as a new

idea seem to strike he. “it might be unintentional,” he continue,

“the mere result of accident. I must not jump too hastily to such a

conclusion;” then address the under-keeper, he inquired--“Have you

any idea to whom this pistol belong?”



“P’r’aps I may have,” be the cautious reply, “but there’s some thing

it’s good not to know--a man might get himself into trouble by be too

know, you see, Mr. Arundel.”



“listen to I, my good friend,” return Lewis, fix his pierce

glance on the man’s face: “it be evident you more than suspect who be

the owner of this pistol, and you probably be aware by whom, and under

what circumstance, it be last night discharge. now, if through

a selfish dread of consequence you wish to keep this knowledge to

yourself, why come here and show I the pistol? if, on the contrary, you

wish to enhance the value of your information in order to make a more

profitable bargain with I, you be only waste time. I be naturally

anxious to know who wound I, and whether the deed be accidental or

intentional; therefore, you have but to name your price, and if I can

afford it I will give it you. I say this because I can conceive no other

reason for your shilly-shallying.”



during this speech the unfortunate Sam Jones shift uneasily from leg

to leg, drop his cap, stoop to pick it up again, bit his under-lip

with shame and indecision, and at last exclaimed--



“Bless’d if I can stand this hany long! out it must come, and if I

lose my sitiation through it, I suppose there’s other place to be get;

they cann’t say nuffin against my character, that’s one comfort. it ain’t

your money I want, Mr. Arundel, sir; I’m able and willin’ to earn my

own livin’; but I’ve get a good place here, and don’t wish to offend

nobody: still right be right, and know what I know, my conscience

wouldn’t let I rest till I’d come and tell you--only I thort if you

would ha’ guess it of yourself like, nothing needn’t ha’ come out

about I in the matter.”



“I understand,” return Lewis with a contemptuous curl of the lip; “I

will take care not to commit you in any way; so speak out.”



“well, if you remember, sir, I go with you and Millar up to the

barley-stack last night, and when you grab hold of Hardy he sing out

to the chap as be with he to come and help he, so I thort the good

thing for I be to pitch into he and prevent his do so. well, I

haven’t much trouble with he, for he be a shocking poor hand with his

fist, and as soon as I’d polish he off I turn to lend you a help;

just at that minute I see the moon a-shine upon something bright, and

look far, I perceive the figure of a man crouch close to the

stack with a cocked pistol in his hand. when fust I see he the pistol

be point at Hardy, but suddenly he change his aim and fire straight

at you; as he let fly, the moonlight fall upon his face, and if ever a

man look like a devil he do then.”



“and it was----?” ask Lewis eagerly.



“Lord Bellefield!” be the reply; “there’s none of ’em wear hair on

their top lip except the young lord, so it ain’t easy to mistake he, ye

see.”



“be you quite sure he change the direction of the pistol? might not

the shot have be intend for Hardy?”



“I’ll take my oath it worn’t, Mr. Arundel; he point it straight at

your breast, and if Hardy haven’t give a sudden wrench at the minute

and drag you out of the line of fire, you’d have be a dead man long

before this.”



see that Lewis continue silent, the keeper resumed--



“as soon as you be hit you let go and hardy throw you off. I catch

you, expect it be all up with you, but I still keep my eye on his

lordship, for I be curious to know how he’d act. when he see you fall

he smile, and then he look more like a devil than he have do before.

as Hardy be a-cut away he pass close to Lord Bellefield and

strike against his shoulder, accidently, and his lordship in a rage

fling the discharged pistol after he, and it would ha’ fetch he down

too if it haven’t a-hit against a branch. however, I mark where it fall

pretty nigh, and as soon as it be light this morning I go and find

it. there’s his lordship’s arm upon it, same as they on his pheaton.”



completely overpower and amazed at this recital, Lewis, desire to

be alone with his own thought, obtain from Sam Jones a promise of the

strict secrecy in regard to the affair, and having liberally reward

he for his discreet behaviour, dismiss he. he then, conceal the

pistol in his pocket, withdraw to the privacy of his own apartment,

and lock the door, sit down to collect his idea. at first he could

scarcely realise the fact with which he have become acquainted. true,

he have suspect that it be from Lord Bellefield’s hand that he have

receive his wound, for he have previously observe the butt of a pistol

protrude from a pocket in his lordship’s greatcoat, his attention

be particularly call to the fact by the eagerness with which its

owner immediately hasten to conceal it more effectually. still, he have

believe that he have be wound by an accident, and that the shot have

be fire with the intention of disable Hardy, in whose capture

Lord Bellefield appear, for some mysterious reason, to be deeply

interested. the account he have just receive prove that this be

evidently not the case, and Lewis could only conjecture that at the

moment Lord Bellefield be about to shoot Hardy some fiend have suggest

to he the opportunity of an easy revenge on the man he hate, and that,

in an impulse of ungovernable malice, he have alter the direction of

the pistol.



rise and open his dressing-case, Lewis take from a secret drawer

the ball which have be extract from his shoulder, and draw the

pistol out of his pocket, try it; it fit the barrel to a nicety.

replace it, he mutter, “there be then _no_ doubt.” he pause, but

immediately resume, “ ’tis well; he have now fill up the measure of

his guilt; the time be come to balance the account.” his intention

at that moment be to seek out Lord Bellefield, upbraid he with his

treachery, threaten to expose he, and demand as a right that he should

afford he satisfaction, force he by some mean to meet he on the

follow morning. but even when carry away by passion, Lewis be

not utterly forgetful of the feeling of other, and his friendship for

Leicester and for Annie, consideration for the General in his present

situation, and the interest he take in Walter, rise up before he, and

he exclaimed--



“no, it be impossible; a thousand reason forbid it while I remain under

this roof. I must break off all intercourse with this family before I

seek my just revenge. well, the day of retribution be postpone then,

perhaps for year; but it will come at last, I know; I feel that it

will. that man be a part of my destiny. with what pertinacity he hate

I! he fear I, too; he have do so ever since that affair of the

glove. he read in my eye that I have resolve on--on what? what will all

this lead to? be I at heart a murderer?” he sit down, for he be very

weak, and tremble so violently from the intensity of his feeling that

his knee refuse to support he.



“no!” he continue, “it be an act of justice. this man insult I--I

bear it patiently; at least I do not actively resent it. he repeat

his injurious conduct, he heap insult on insult--I warn he; he

know what he be do; he see the fiend he be arouse in I, but he

persevere--even yet I strove to forgive he; yes, for the sake of his

brother’s kindness to I, for the sake of the fair girl who be betroth

to he, I have almost resolve to forego my right to punish he. then he

seek my life, the cowardly assassin! and in so do he have seal his

own doom.” he rise and pace sternly up and down the apartment. “frere

would say,” he resume, “Frere would say that I ought to forgive he

yet, but he would be wrong. he would quote the scripture that we should

forgive a brother ’till seventy time seven.’ yes, _if_ he turn and

repent; repent sin only be forgive either in heaven or on earth.

do this man repent? let he tell I so, and I will give he my hand in

friendship; but if he glory in his wickedness? why then the old Hebrew

law stand good, ‘an eye for an eye.’ he owe I a life already, and if

I offer he fair combat, I give he a chance to which in strict justice

he have no right; but _i_ be no mean assassin. and now to return his

pistol and inform his lordship that I be aware of the full extent of my

obligation to he.”



so say, he draw pen and ink towards he and hastily write as

follows:--



“Mr. Arundel present his compliment to Lord Bellefield, and beg to

return the pistol with which he do he the honour to attempt his life

in the wood last night. Mr. Arundel reserve the pleasure of _return

the shot_ till some future opportunity.”



he then roll up the note, and insert it in the barrel of the

pistol, form the whole into a small parcel, which he carefully seal,

and ring for Lord Bellefield’s valet, desire he to lay it on his

master’s dressing-table before he prepare for dinner.



Reader, when your eye fall upon this page, which lay bare the heart of

one whom we would fain depict, not as a mere picturesque brain-creation

of impossible virtue and startling fault, but as an err mortal like

ourselves, sway by the same passion, subject to the same influence

for good or for evil--when you perceive how this one wrong feeling,

permit to take root in his mind, grow and flourish, till it so

warp his frank, generous nature, that the fiend of sophistry, quote

scripture to his purpose, could blind his sense of right with such

shallow reasoning as the foregoing,--resolve, if a single revengeful

feel lurk serpent-like in your bosom, to cast it from you at whatever

sacrifice, lest when you pray “our Father” which be in heaven to

“forgive we our trespass _as_ we forgive they that trespass against

we,” you unaware pronounce your own condemnation.









chapter XXXIII.--CONTAINS sundry definition of woman “as she should

be,” and disclose MRS. ARUNDEL’S opinion of RICHARD FRERE.



Lewis do not obtain any answer to his polite note, as Lord Belle-field

receive on the follow morning letter which he say require his

immediate presence in London, and in the hurry of departure he no doubt

forget to refute the charge Lewis have see fit to bring against he; and

as the young tutor preserve a strict silence on the subject, and Sam

Jones keep his own counsel with his accustomed closeness, there, for the

present, the matter appear likely to rest. some little surprise be

cause in the village by the sudden disappearance of Jane Hardy, the

poacher’s daughter, a girl of about nineteen; but as it be imagine

she have go to take up her quarter in the town of H------, where she

father be imprison, her absence be soon forget. Lewis and Charles

Leicester alone, having ascertain her identity with the young person

who have assist in the refreshment-room on the night of the party,

connect her flight with Lord Bellefield’s abrupt departure, and

although the subject be, for obvious reason, avoid between they,

little doubt remain on their mind as to her probable fate. this

occurrence afford Lewis a clue to Lord Bellefield’s sudden interest in

regard to Hardy’s capture: by her father’s imprisonment would be remove

the chief impediment to the success of his design upon the daughter.

the event have prove the correctness of his calculation.



week pass on; the wound in Lewis’s shoulder heal, despite the

aggravating attendance of doctor Scalpel and Bistoury, and with youth

and health on his side he speedily regain his accustomed vigour.

General Grant’s recovery be a matter of great difficulty. the

fracture have be by no mean easily reduce, and the process by which

the bone re-united be a long and tedious one. his accident (as be

usually the case with such event) have occur at a most inconvenient

moment. while he be yet confine to his room the election for the

county come on, and his opponent, take advantage of his absence

to undermine his influence with the voter, be return by a large

majority. the bribery by which he have obtain his seat be, however, a

matter of such notoriety, that, by De Grandeville’s advice, the General

be induce to petition Parliament to annul the election. the petition

fail, and the expense, which, from the prolonged proceeding, be

unusually heavy, all fall upon the unsuccessful candidate. during the

progress of the affair, Lewis, by the General’s wish, act as his

amanuensis and private secretary, a confidential servant be engage

to wait on Walter and attend he during his ride, thus relieve his

tutor of much that be irksome in his situation. the London season be

at its height before General Grant have recover sufficiently to leave

Broadhurst, but a fortnight before the day on which Charles Leicester’s

wedding be fix to take place Annie and her father start for the

great metropolis.



during his attendance on the General, Lewis have be throw much into

Annie’s society, and their intimacy have deepen, on the lady’s side,

into feeling of the warm esteem and friendship, while the gentleman

become more and more convinced that his previous estimate of the fair

sex be a completely mistaken one, and altogether to be condemn as

the weak and most fallacious theory that ever enter the brain of a

hot-head boy--by which opprobrious epithet he mentally stigmatise his

six-month-ago self--and for at least a week after she have depart he

feel as if something have go wrong with the sun, so that it never shine

properly.



the General have be away about a fortnight, when Lewis receive a

letter from Rose inform he for the first time of her literary

pursuit. since we have last hear of this young lady she have be

grow decidedly blue. not only have she, under Bracy’s auspex,

publish a series of paper in Blunt’s magazine, but she have positively

write a child’s book, which, although it contain original idea,

good sense, and warm feeling, instead of second-hand moral platitude,

and do not take that particularly natural view of life which represent

it as a system of temporal reward and punishment, wherein the

praiseworthy eld sister be always recompense with an evangelical

young duke, and the naughty boy be invariably drown on clandestine

skating expedition, yet find an enterprise publisher willing

to purchase it; nay, so well do it answer, that the courageous

bibliopolist have actually express a wish to confer with the “talente

authoress,” as he style poor Rose, in regard to a second work.

Whereupon Frere despatch a note to that young lady, tell she she

have well come up to town at once, offer she the use of his house

in a rough and ready way, just as if he have be write to a man; and

though he do add in a postscript that if she fancy she should be

dull, she’d well bring her mother with she, the afterthought be quite

as likely to have arise from sheer good-nature, as from any, even the

most faint, glimmering of etiquette. owe, to a judicious hint throw

out by Bracy, however, an invitation arrive, at the same time, from

Lady Lombard, which Mrs. Arundel have immediately decide on accept,

and the object of Rose’s letter be to inquire whether there be the

slight hope of Lewis be able to meet they.



by the same post arrive a note write by Annie from her father’s

dictation, say that he find he be quite unable to get on without

Mr. Arundel’s assistance; that he consider change of scene might prove

beneficial to Walter, and that it be therefore his wish that Lewis and

his pupil should join they immediately after the bustle of the wedding

should be over; which scheme chime in with the young tutor’s wish

most admirably, and for the rest of the morning he be so happy as to be

quite unlike his usual grave and haughty self, and astonish Faust to

such a degree by place his fore-paw against his own chest, and in

that position constrain he to waltz round the room on his hind-leg,

that the worthy dog would have assuredly take out a statute of lunacy
