against his master have he be aware of the existence of such a process.



those who witness the marriage of the Hon. Charles Leicester to the

lovely and accomplished daughter of the late Peregrine Peyton, Esq.,

of Stockington Manor, in the county of Lancashire (they say nothing of

Ludgate Hill and ignore Plumpstern totally), describe it to have be a

truly edify ceremony. the fatal knot be tie, and the wretched pair

launch into a married state by the Bishop of L--------, the unhappy

victim submit to their fate with unexampled fortitude and

resignation, and the female spectator evince by their tear that the

lesson to be derive from the awful tragedy enact before they would

not be throw away upon they. nor be the good intention thus form

allow to swell the list of “unredeemed pledge” whence that prince

of pawnbroker, Satan, be popularly suppose to select his paving

material, as, during the ball which conclude the evening, two fine

young man of property fall victim to premature declaration, and after

a rapid decline from the way of good fellowship be carry off by

matrimony, and depart this (_i.e._, fashionable) life in less than two

month after their first seizure.



on Lewis’s arrival in town he find a small packet direct to he in

Leicester’s handwriting, contain, besides the glaze card lovingly

couple by silver twist, a remarkably elegant gold watch and chain for

the waistcoat pocket, together with a few line from Charley himself,

say that to Lewis’s good advice and plain speak he feel he in a

great measure owe his present happiness, and that he hope Lewis would

wear the enclose trifle, the joint gift of himself and Laura, to remind

he of their mutual friendship and regard. have he know that Annie Grant

have notice the fact of his not possess a watch, and suggest the

nature of the gift to her cousin, he would have value it even more

highly than he do.



the happy pair have determine to test the endurance of their felicity by

start for the Rhine, which popular river it be their intention to

go up as far as it be go-up-able, then proceed to Switzerland, do that

land of musical cow and icy mountain thoroughly, and finally take up

their quarter at Florence, where Leicester have succeed in obtain

a diplomatic appointment. a letter have be receive from they date

coblentz, wherein it appear their newfound happiness have stand the

voyage well than might have be expect; a fact mainly attributable

to their having have an unusually calm passage. Laura consider the

Rhine scenery exquisite; Charley think it all very well for a change;

but for a constancy, he must confess he prefer the Serpentine. he

be disgust with the german student, whom he stigmatise as “awful

tiger,” wonder why the woman wear short petticoat if they haven’t

well ankle to show, complain bitterly of the intense stupidity of

the native for not understand either French or English, and wound

up by a long, violent sentence quite unconnected with all that have go

before it, set forth his unalterable conviction that Laura be an

angel, which unscriptural assertion he reiterate four time in as many

line.



a change have take place in Rose Arundel, and Lewis, as he gaze with

affection on her calm, pensive brow, and mark the earnest, thoughtful

expression of her soft, grey eye, feel that she be indeed alter: he

have leave she little more than a child, he find she a woman in the good

and full sense of the expression. reader, do you know all that phrase

imply? do you understand what be mean by a woman in the true and

full sense of the term?



“eh? I should rather think I do, too, just a _very_ little,” reply

Ensign Downylip, wink at society at large; “know what a woman be?

yes, I consider that good, rather.”



“and what, oh! most exquisite juvenile, may be your definition of woman

as she should be?”



the Ensign stroke his upper lip where that confound moustache be so

very “lang a comin’,” rub his nose to arouse his intellect, which

he fail to do because that faculty be not asleep but want, and

replies--



“Ar--well, to begin with: woman be of course a decidedly inferiar

animal, but--ar--take the good speciman of the class, and you’ll find it

vewy pwitty, picquante, devote to polke, light in hand, clean about

the pastern, something like Fanny Elsler, with a dash of Lady--to

give it style (I cann’t stand vulgawity), decidedly fast (I hate your

cart-horsey gal)! plenty of bustle to make it look spicy, ready to

go the pace no end, and able properly to--ahem! appweciate ‘your

truly’--ar--that’s about the time of day, eh, Mr. Author!”



“no such thing, sir,” reply Corulea Scribbler, who be so very superior

that she be momentarily expect to regenerate society singlehande, “no

such thing, sir! I know what the author mean: he justly consider woman

as a--that is, as _the_ concentrated essence of mind; nothing low, base,

earthy--but--in fact--definition should be terse--you’ll excuse my

mathematical taste, but--ahem!--three term at Queen’s College, and

that dear Professor Baa-lamb! naturally produce a logical habit of

thought--you require a perfect woman.”



“no, madam, I be not so unreasonable.”



“I mean, you require a definition of a perfect woman; here you have it

then--the maximum of mind unite to the minimum of matter; or, to speak

poetically, a ‘thing all soul.’” and having thus give her opinion, Miss

Corulea, who measure barely five foot, and be as thin as a lath, shake

her straw-colour ringlet and subside into the Sixth Book of Euclid.



but neither the red-jacket nor the blue-stocking, albeit each the type

of a not unnumerous class, have exactly answer our question as we

would wish it reply to. we do not agree with Charley Leicester in

consider woman an angel; first, because our idea with regard to

angel be excessively vague and undefined, wing and white drapery

be the only marked feature which we have as yet succeed in

realise; and secondly, because, to verify the resemblance, woman

should be faultless, and we have never yet meet with one who have not some

fascinating little sin leave to show that she be not too good for this

world. our notion of a woman, in the good sense of the word, be a being

fit to be a helpmeet for man; and this would lead we into another

disquisition, which we will dismiss summarily by state that we mean a

man worthy of the name, not an ape in a red-coat like Ensign Downylip,

or an owl in a sad-coloured one like Professor Baalamb, but a man whom

it would not be mere satire to call a lord of the creation. a helpmeet

for such an one as this should possess a clear, acute intellect, or she

would be unable to comprehend his aspiration after the good, and true,

and beautiful--the effort of his fall nature to regain somewhat of

its original rank in the scale of create being. she should have a

faithful, love heart, that when, foil in his worldly career,

his spirit be dark within he, and in the bitterness of his soul he

confess that “the good that he would he do not, but the evil he

would not, that he do,” her affection may prove to he that in her

love he have one inestimable blessing yet remain, of which death alone

can deprive he, and then only for a season; for, avail herself of

the fitting moment with the delicate tact which be one of the bright

instinct of a love woman’s heart, she can offer he the only true

consolation, by urge he to renew his christian warfare in the hope

that _together_ they may attain the reward of their high calling, a

reward so glorious that the mind of man be impotent to conceive its

nature. but to be able to do this she must herself have realise, by

the power of faith, the blessedness of thing unseen, and with this

requisite, without which all other excellency be valueless, we

conclude our definition of “woman as she should be.”



such an one be Rose Arundel, and countless other be there who, if not

sinless as the radiant messenger of heaven, be yet do angel’ work

by many a fireside which their presence cheer and blesse. happy be

the man who possess in a wife or sister such a household fairy; and if

some there be who bear alone the burden of life--whose joy be few, for

we rejoice not in solitude--let those whose lot be bright forgive the

clouded brow or the cynical word that at time attest the weariness of

a soul on which the sunlight of affection seldom beam.



no particular alteration be observable in Mrs. Arundel, who seem to

possess the enviable faculty of never grow old, and who remain

just as gay and sparkling as when at sixteen she have enslave the fancy

rather than the heart of Captain Arundel.



“my dear Lewis,” she exclaim, after having ask a hundred question

in a breath regard the internal economy of General Grant’s family,

the affray with the poacher, Charles Leicester’s wedding, and every

other event, grave or otherwise, which occur to her active and

versatile mind, “my dear Lewis, what an original your friend Frere be!

excessively kind and good-natured, but so very odd. he volunteer to

come and meet we at the coach-office, which I consider quite a work of

supererogation; but Rose have imbibe such a mistrust of London and its

inhabitant, whom she expect to eat she up bodily, I believe, that she

persuade I to accept his offer. well, when the coach arrive I look

about, but nobody do I see who at all coincide with my preconceive

idea of Mr. Frere, and I begin to think he would prove faithless,

when I descry an individual in a vile hat and an old, rough greatcoat

perch on a pile of luggage, with a cotton umbrella between his

knee, read some dirty little book, in which he appear completely

immerse. he take not the slight notice of the bustle and confusion

go on around he, and would, I believe, have sit there until now, if

a porter, carry a heavy trunk, have not all but fall over he;

upon which he start up, and for the first time perceive the coach,

exclaim, ‘by Jove, there’s the very thing I be wait for!’ then

shoulder his umbrella, he advance to the window, and thrust

in his great head, growl out, ‘be any of you Miss Arundel?’ rose

answer the question, for I be so take by surprise that I be die

with laughter. as soon as he have ascertain our identity, he continue,

‘well, then I should say the soon you’re out of this the well.

I’ll call a cab.’ the moment it draw up he fling open the door, and

exclaim, ‘now, come along,’ he catch hold of Rose as if she’d be a

carpetbag, drag she out, and push she by main force into the cab.”



“oh, mamma,” interrupt Rose apologetically, “you really colour the

matter too highly. Mr. Frere be as kind as possible. he be a little

rough, certainly, and seem to think I must be as helpless as a child;

but I dare say he’s not accustom to act as squire to dame.”



“indeed he’s not,” resume Mrs. Arundel. “but I be determined he

shouldn’t paw I about like a bale of good, so I rest my hand on a

porter’s shoulder and spring from the coach into the cab while he be

stoop to pick up his wretched umbrella; and finely astonish he

look, too, when he discover what I have do. then he drag down

all the luggage, just as he have do Rose, and try to put two trunk

that do not belong to we on the cab, only I rave at he till I oblige

he to relinquish they. of course I be force to offer he a seat

in the cab, but he coolly reply, ‘no, thank ye; there be too many

bandbox--the square of their basis occupy the entire area. I’ll sit

beside cabby.’ and to my horror he scramble up to the driving-seat, and

take the dirty book out of his pocket, be speedily absorb in its

content; and in this state we actually drive up to Lady Lombard’s door.

I could have beat the man, I be so angry with he. and yet, with it

all, the creature be a gentleman.”



“indeed he be,” return Lewis, “a thorough gentleman in mind, though

from the extent to which he be engross by his literary and scientific

pursuit, and from the fact of live so much alone, he have not the

manner of society. but Frere be a very first-rate man; his be no

ordinary intellect.”



“it be impossible to watch the play of his feature and doubt that for

a moment,” return Rose eagerly. “look at his speak eye--his noble

forehead.”



“oh! Rose be quite _emprise_ with the monster,” remark Mrs. Arundel,

laugh. “it’s a decided case of love at first sight. be it the old

greatcoat, or the dreadful hat, which first touch your heart, _ma

chere_?”



“I’m not bind to criminate myself,” be the reply, “so I shall decline

to answer that question.”



while she speak a short, sharp, double knock, as of an agitated postman,

awake the echo and the porter in Lady Lombard’s “Marble Hall.” in

another minute the brobdignagian footman, with prize calf to his leg,

fling open the drawing-room door and announce, in a stentorian voice,

“Mr. Frere.”



“_quand on parle du diable on en voit la queue?_” whisper Mrs.

Arundel, rise quickly. “positively, Rose, my nerve willn’t stand the

antic of your pet bear this morning. let I see you again before you

go, _Louis, mon cher_--you’ll find I in the boudoir.”



so say, she glide noiselessly out of one door a moment before Frere

enter at the other. Lewis follow her retreating figure with a glance

half-painful, half-amuse. “my mother grow young and more gay every

time I see she,” he observe to Rose. a speak glance be her only

answer, for at the moment Frere make his appearance--and a somewhat

singular one it be. the day be fine, he have discard the obnoxious

greatcoat, and--thank to his old female domestic, who have catch he

go out with a large hole in his sleeve and send he back to put on

another garment, which she herself select--the coat he wear be in

unusually good preservation, and not so very much too large for he; but

the heavy shoe, the worsted stocking, the shepherd’s plaid trouser,

and the cotton umbrella be all _in statu quo_; while his bright

eye, sparkle out of a great bush than ever of untrimmed hair and

whisker, give he a striking resemblance to some honest Scotch terrier,

worthy to be immortalise by Landseer’s pencil. catch sight of

Lewis, he rush towards he, and seize both his hand (in order to

accomplish which act of friendship he allow the umbrella to fall on

Rose’s toe), he shake they heartily, exclaim, “why, Lewis, old boy!

this be a pleasure! I haven’t a notion you would be here so soon. how’s

General Grant? and how’s Walter? and how’s Faust? and how’s everybody?

well, I _be_ glad to see you!”



all this time Frere have take not the slight notice of Rose, who,

having advance a step or two to greet he, have resume her seat, more

pleased to witness his delight in welcome Lewis than any attention

to herself could have render she. having seat himself on a sofa and

pull Lewis clown by his side, he for the first time appear aware of

Rose’s presence, which he hasten to acknowledge by a nod, add, “ah!

how d’ye do? I’ve get something to tell you presently, as soon as I’ve

do with your brother.”



then, turn to Lewis, he recommence his string of question, without

regard Rose’s presence otherwise than by occasionally include

she in the conversation with such interjectional remark as “_You_ can

understand that”--“I explain that to _you_ the other day,” until at

length he abruptly exclaim, “now I must go and talk to _her_--she and

I have get a little business together.”



“perhaps I be _de trop_,” observe Lewis with a meaning smile.



in reply to this Frere merely clench his fist, and having shake it

within an inch of Lewis’s face, march deliberately across the room,

and draw a chair close to Rose, seat himself in it; then lay

hold of one corner of her worsted work, he say in a gruff voice, “put

away this rubbish.”



“I can listen to you, Mr. Frere, and go on with my slipper at the same

time,” return Rose, quietly release her work.



“you cann’t do two thing properly together,” be the reply; “nobody can;

for it’s all fudge about Cæsar’s reading and dictate at the same time.

what I’ve get to tell you be more important than a carpet shoe.”



smile at his pertinacity, Rose, not have a particle of obstinacy

in her disposition, put away her work, and demurely cross her hand

before she, like a good child say its lesson, await her tyrant’s

order. that her attitude be not lose upon Frere that gentleman make

evident by catch Lewis’s eye and point backwards with his thumb,

as much as to say, “there! do you see that?” then produce a note from

his pocket, he coolly break the seal, open it, and hand it to Rose,

mutter, “read that.”



the note run as follows:--



“Mr. T. Bracy present his compliment to Miss Arundel, and beg to

enclose a note of introduction to Mr. Nonpareil, the publisher, as

Mr. Frere agree in think that the offer make by Mr. A--------, of

B------ Street, for the copyright of her interesting tale be quite

inadequate to its merit.”



“how very kind of Mr. Bracy!” exclaim Rose, hand the note to her

brother, re re having quietly read it over her shoulder. “Lewis, I must

ask you to be good enough to go with I to Mr. Nonpareil’s whenever you

can spare the time.”



“you needn’t trouble he,” return frere gruffly; “I mean to take you

there myself; and as there’s never any good in put thing off, I

vote we go this morning. what do you say?”



“you be very kind,” reply Rose, smile; “but really, now my brother

be in town I need not encroach on your valuable time.”



“valuable fiddlestick!” be the courteous reply; “though, of course,

everybody’s time be valuable, if people do but know how to employ it

properly--which they never do. but you don’t suppose if I’d anything

very particular in hand I should be dawdle here, do you? I’ve get

to be at the Ornithological at four, and to call at Moore’s,

the bird-stuffer’s, first; but I can look in there on our way to

Nonpareil’s.”



“yes; but I’m sure Lewis----” begin Rose in a deprecatory tone of voice.



“Nonsense about Lewis!” be the surly rejoinder. “what do you imagine

he know about deal with publishers?--they’re ‘kittle cattle to shoe

behint,’ as a Scotchman would say. I’ve have dealing enough with ’em

to find out that, I can tell you. as for Lewis, if he be to walk into

one of their den with his head up in the air, they’d take he for Lord

Octavo Shallowpate, come to negotiate for another new novel, write

with a paste-pot and scissor, and when they find he have not a handle

to his name with which to shove his rubbish down the public throat,

they’d kick he out of the shop again.”



“then you really think I look as stupid as a literary lord, eh, Frere?”

 inquire Lewis.



“well, that’s too strong a term, perhaps,” answer frere reflectively;

“but you don’t look like a man of business, at all event.”



“where do this sagacious publisher reside?” ask Lewis; and when

Frere have give he the require information, he continue: “then we’ll

settle the matter thus:--My tailor, with whom I be anxious to gain an

interview, live in the adjoining street; accordingly, I’ll walk down

with Rose and you, and while you negotiate with the autocrat of folio,

I’ll take ‘fitting measure’ for get myself ‘neatly bind in

cloth.’”



“so be it then, most facetious youth,” return frere, laugh; “and

the fast you can get ready, you know,” he continue, turn to Rose,

“the well.”



“I’m all obedience,” reply Rose, smile; “but I think you’re rather

fond of tyrannising, Mr. Frere.”



“who, I?” return Frere in astonishment. “not a bit of it; I’m the most

easily manage fellow in London--I be, upon my word.”



“you should see what perfect command his old housekeeper have he in,”

 observe Lewis, with an arch glance at his sister; “the bear dare not

growl at she--she’s a perfect Van Amburgh to he.”



now there be so much truth in this charge that it be rather a sore

subject with Frere. the old woman in question have live with his mother

and have nurse he when a child; and for these reason, as well as from

good nature and a certain easiness of disposition which lie beneath his

rough manner, Frere have allow she gradually to usurp control over he,

till, in all the minutia of his domestic life, she rule he with a

rod of iron. although her admiration of and respect for her master’s

learning be fully equal to her total ignorance of the art and

science; and although her affection for he be boundless, nature

have gift she with a crusty temper, which an interval of poverty and

hardship (extend from the death of Frere’s mother till the time when

his first act on obtain a competence have be to seek she out and

take she into his service) have not tend to sweeten. the dialogue

which occasionally take place between the master and servant be most

amusing, and her power over he be exercise so openly, that his fear

of Jemima have become a stand joke among his intimate. accordingly,

on hear Lewis’s observation, Frere hastily jump up and strode to

the fireplace, mutter, “Nonsense! psha! rubbish! don’t you believe a

word of it, Miss Arundel; but go and dress, there’s a good----” he

be go to add “fellow”; for be it know, the clue to his gruff,

unpolishe behaviour towards the young lady in question be to be

discover in the fact, that from her quiet composure, freedom from

affectation, clear, good sense, and the interest she take in subject

usually consider too abstruse for female investigation, Frere look

upon she as a kindre soul, and as all his other choose intimate be

of the worthy gender, he be continually forget that she be not a

man. check himself, however, just in time, he substitute “creature”

 for “fellow”; and as Rose leave the room, he continue, “ ’pon my word,

Lewis, your sister’s such a nice, sensible, well-inform, reasonable

being, that I be constantly forget she’s a woman.”



“which speech show that amongst your numerous study that of the

female character have be neglect,” reply Lewis; “or that you have

take your impression from very bad specimen of the sex.”



Frere, who during the above remark have draw from his pocket a lump of

crumble sandstone, which, in order to examine more closely, he coolly

deposit on a small satin-wood work-table, look up in surprise as he

rejoin, “your opinion, touch the merit of womankind, seem to

have suffer a recovery, young man, see that the last time I have

the honour of discuss the matter with you, woman be all

perfidious hyaena, or thereabout. what have work so remarkable a

transformation?”



something appear to have suddenly go wrong with Lewis’s boot, for it

be not until he have thoroughly investigate the matter that he reply,

his face be still bent over the offend article, “the simple fact

that as one grow old one grow wise, I suppose. no doubt Gretchen

behave abominably, and render I for the time intensely wretche; but

it be folly in I ever to have place my happiness in the power of such

a little, romantic, flirt, half-educate thing as she be; I should

not do so now, and to argue from such an individual instance, to the

disparagement of the whole sex, be one of the mad notion that ever

enter the brain of a hot-head boy.”



“Phew!” whistle Frere in astonishment, “you be not over civil to your

former self, I must say. if anybody else have speak so disrespectfully

of you you’d have be for punch his head for he; however, I believe

your present frame of mind be the more sane of the two, though sweeping

assertion be always more or less untenable. the truth be, you can lay

down no general rule about it--woman be human as well as man; there be

a few very good, a few very bad, and an immense number who be nothing

particular, in both sex. there be no authority which would lead we to

suppose Adam’s rib be make of ivory more than any of his other bone.

there’s one vice belong to the fair sex, though--they’re always

an unmerciful time put on their bonnet; your sister’s be five

minute already, and I’d lie a bet we don’t see she for five more.” as

he utter the last word, Rose, fully equipped and look the picture

of neatness, trip into the room, to Frere’s intense discomfiture, who

scramble his relic of the Era of the Old Red Sandstone into his pocket

with the air of some culprit schoolboy detect in his malpractice by

the vigilant eye of his pedagogue.









CHAPTER XXXIV.--ROSE and FRERE go to VISIT MR. NONPAREIL the PUBLISHER.



Lewis, having slip away for a moment to take leave of Mrs. Arundel,

who dismiss he with a parting injunction to take care Ursa Major

do not devour Rose, the trio descend the stair, Frere take an

opportunity to whisper to Lewis, “she be down upon I then in every

sense of the word; don’t believe a woman could get ready in five

minute on any consideration; but your sister have more sense than I ever

expect to see under a bonnet, that’s a fact.”



“don’t you think for once you could dispense with that dreadful

umbrella?” inquire Lewis, who have imbibe a few leicesterian prejudice

from his residence at Broadhurst.



“dreadful umbrella! why what’s the matter with it?” exclaim Frere,

half unfurl his favourite; “it’s-water-tight, and have a famous strong

stick to it; what more do you want in an umbrella, eh?”



“it might have be make of silk,” suggest Lewis mildly.



“yes, and be steal and bring back again regularly three time a

week,” return Frere. “I have a silk one once, and the expense that

umbrella be to I, to say nothing of the wear and tear of mind it

occasion, be perfectly terrific. I shudder when I think of it; there

be not a dozen cabman in London who have not receive half-a-crown for

bring home that umbrella. it be a regular bottle-imp to I, always

be lose and always come back again. the ’bus conductor know it

by sight as well as they know the Bank; they be for ever lay trap

to get it into their possession, with a view to obtain the reward of

honesty by bring it home again. I get rid of it at last, though; I

lend it to a fellow who owe I five pound, and I’ve never see man,

money, or umbrella since. now this dear old cotton thing, not be

worth finding, have never be lose; however, if you’ll promise to take

care I have it to-morrow when I call, I’ll leave it here, and if your

sister get wet don’t blame I.”



“Rose, will you undertake the heavy responsibility?” ask Lewis. “I

think I may safely promise so to do,” be the reply. “there be a little

foot page in this establishment in whom I have the great confidence,

and to his custody will! commit it.”



and Frere’s anxious mind be soothe by this assurance, they start

on their expedition. twenty minute’ brisk walking--which would have

be brisker still if Rose have not gently hint that lady be not

usually accustom to stride along like postman; to which suggestion

Frere respond with something very like a growl,--twenty minute’

walk bring they to the very elegant front of Mr. Nonpareil’s shop,

where Lewis leave the two other. a nice young man, with Hyperion curl

outside his head, and nothing save much too high an opinion of himself

within, who lounge gracefully behind the counter, reply to Frere’s

inquiry “whether Mr. Nonpareil be at home,” after the fashion of the

famous Irish echo, i.e., by another question. elevate his eyebrow

till they almost disappear in his forest of hair, he drawl out--



“Wh-a-y? do you w-a-ant he?”



“of course I do, or else I should not have ask for he,” return

frere sharply; then hand his own card and Bracy’s note of

introduction across the counter, he continue, “take those to your

_master_, and tell he that a lady and gentleman be wait to see

he.”



at the word “master” Hyperion colour and appear about to become

impertinent, but something in Frere’s look induce he to alter his

intention, and turn on his heel, he stride into the back shop with an

_air martyre_, which be deeply affect to the risible muscle of the

pair he leave behind he.



“there’s an animal!” exclaim Frere, as the subject of his remark

disappear behind a tall column of account book. “now that ape look

upon himself as a sort of Admirable Crichton, and I’ll be bind have a

high opinion of his own mental endowment than ever Shakespeare or

Milton have of theirs. I dare say the creature have his admirer, too:

some subordinate shop boy, or the urchin who run of errand, take he

at his own price and believe in he implicitly. ye god, what a ‘ship

of fool’ be this goodly vessel of society!”



“I hope he do not rest his claim on the ground of his personal

attraction,” return Rose with a quiet smile.



“his strength must lie in his hair, if he do,” reply Frere, “like

that of his Israelitish Hercules of old. but see, here he come, shake

his ambrosial lock; and behold, he smile graciously upon we. Bracy’s

note have work miracle.”



approach with a smirk and a bow, Hyperion politely signify that Mr.

Nonpareil be disengage, then again retreat, lead the way through a

sort of defile of unsold literature to the sanctum of the enterprise

publisher. this remarkable apartment be of the most minute dimension,

a very duodecimo edition of a room, embellish with a miniature

fireplace, an infinitesimal writing-table, and a mere peep-hole of a

window look across many chimney-pot into space. in the middle of

this retreat of learn, like an oyster in its shell, repose that

Rhadamanthus of literature--the heroic Nonpareil. his outer man

be encase in black, as become the severity of his office; a white

neck-cloth encircle his august throat, while a heavy gold watch-chain

and seal attest his awful respectability. he be of a most

respectable age, neither incautiously young nor unadvisedly old; he be

of a most respectable height, neither absurdly short nor inconveniently

tall; his weight, 12 stone 6 lbs., be most respectable--it have not

vary a pound for the last ten year, nor could one look at he without

feeling that it would remain exactly the same for the next ten year; he

have a most respectable complexion, red enough to indicate that he live

well and that it agree with he, but nothing more. nobody could suspect

that man of an apoplectic tendency; he be much too respectable to

think of die suddenly; the very expression of his face be a sort of

perpetual life assurance; _he_ go out of the world without advertise

the day on which he might be expect to appear most respectably bind

in board! the idea be preposterous. his manner naturally express his

conviction of his own intense respectability, and be impressive, not

to say pompous; while from a sense of the comparative want of

respectability in everybody else it be also familiar, or as his enemy

(all great man have enemy) declare, presume.



as Rose and Frere enter he stand up to receive they, favour frere

with a salutation half-way between a bow and a nod, partially extend

his hand to Rose, and as she hesitate in surprise, hastily draw it

back again, then motion they to the only two chair, save his own

judgment-seat, the apartment contain, resume his throne, and smile

graciously at Rose, leant back, wait apparently until that young lady

should humbly prefer her suit to he.



perceive his design, Rose glance appealingly at Frere, who come to

her assistance by plunge at once in _médias re_ with his accustom

bluntness.



“well, Mr. Nonpareil,” he begin, “touch the object of our visit to

you, I suppose Bracy have tell you in his note what we’ve come about?”



“yes--that be, so far; Mr. Bracy signify that your visit have a

business tendency,” be the cautious reply.



“why, we certainly should not have come here for pleasure,” return

frere shortly; then catch Rose’s look of dismay he continue, “I mean

to say we should not have think of take up your valuable time” (here

he give Rose a confidential nudge with his elbow to indicate that he

speak ironically), “unless we have a legitimate object in do so.”



in answer to this the Autocrat merely incline his head, and reveal a

highly respectable set of tooth; so Frere resumed--



“this young lady, Miss Arundel, have determine, by the advice of Mr.

Bracy and--ahem!--myself, to make you the first offer of a very valuable

work which she have write--a tale of a very unusual description,

peculiarly suit, as I consider, to the present state of society,

point out certain social evil, and show how a more consistent

adherence to the precept of Christianity would prove the only effectual

remedy.”



at these last word Mr. Nonpareil, who, having apparently lapse into a

state of torpor, have listen with a face as immovable as if it have be

cast in bronze, suddenly prick up his ear and condescend to exist

again.



“if I understand you, Mr.----Frere,” suggest that gentleman--“Mr.

frere,” continue Nonpareil, “if I comprehend your meaning, sir, this

lady wish to dispose of the copyright of a religious novel?”



“that’s it,” reply Frere.



“then my answer must mainly depend on the exact height of the

principle.”



“on the how much?” inquire Frere, considerably mystify.



“on the exact height of the principle, sir,” return Mr. Nonpareil

with dignity. “I possess a regular scale, sir, which I have have work

out minutely, proceed from the broad outline of Christianity to the

most delicate shade of doctrine, and descend even to the small

point of the canon law. such an ecclesiological table be most important

in our line. public opinion, sir, fluctuate in such matter, just like

the fund, up one week, down the next, up again the next. now I’ll just

give you an instance. there be a little work we publish, I dare say

you’ve see it, ‘Ambrosius; or, the Curate confess.’ it be think

rather a heavy book when it first come out. the public would not read

it; the trade do not like it; it hang on hand, and I expect to lose

from £200 to £300 upon it. well, sir, the surplice question begin to be

agitated. fortunately, the author have make Ambrosius preach in a white

gown. I immediately advertise it freely, the thing take, we sell 3000

copy in a fortnight, and instead of lose £300 I make £600. but

that’s not all, sir. shortly after that the Rev. Clerestory Lectern, one

of the very tip-top one, go to Rome, and take his three curate, a

serious butler, and the family apothecary with he. this make a great

sensation, convulse the public mind fearfully, and bring on a general

attack of the ultra-protestant epidemic. accordingly, I send for the

author of ‘Ambrosius,’ offer he term he be only too glad to jump

at, shut he up in the back-shop with half a ream of foolscap and a

bottle of sherry, and in little more than a week we print off 5000

copy of ‘Loyoliana; or, the Jesuit in the Chimney Corner.’ the book

sell like wild-fire, sir. a second edition be call for and go off

in no time, and I believe I might have get through a third, only

Lord Dunderhead Downhill join the Plymouth Brethren and marry his

kitchen-maid, which bring public opinion up again several degree and

spoil the sale; but I make a very nice thing of it, altogether.”



so say, Mr. Nonpareil rub his hand gleefully, push his hair off

his forehead, and look at Rose as if he long to coin she into money

on the spot. after a pause he inquire abruptly, “what’s the name,

ma’am?”



“the name of my tale?” begin Rose, slightly flurry at the conversation

so suddenly take a personal turn. “I think of call it ‘Helen

Tremorne.’”



“very gogd, ma’am--very good,” return Mr. Nonpareil approvingly;

“euphonious, aristocratic, and vague. just at this time, a title that

do not pledge a book to anything particular of any kind be most

desirable. about how long do you suppose it will be?”



“Mr. Frere think it would make two small volume about the size of a

work call ‘Amy Herbert,’ I believe,” reply Rose.



“quite right, ma’am, quite right, a very selling size, indeed,” be the

answer; “clever book, ‘Amy Herbert,’ very. so much tenderness in it,

ma’am; nothing pay well than judicious tenderness; the mother of

England like it to read about--the daughter of England like it--the

little girl of England like it--and so the husband of England be

force to pay for it. if you recollect, ma’am, there’s a pathetic

governess in ‘Amy Herbert,’ who call the child ‘dearest’--well

imagine character, that. she’s sell many a copy have that governess. May

I ask do ‘Helen Tremorne’ call anybody ‘dearest’?”



“I really scarcely remember,” say Rose, hide a smile behind her muff.



“it would be most desirable that she should, ma’am,” return Mr.

Nonpareil solemnly. “some vindictive pupil, if possible, ma’am--the

more repulsive the child, the great the self-sacrifice--people like

self-sacrifice to read about--they call such incident touch; and

just at the present moment pathos sell immensely. pray, ma’am, may I

ask be you high or low?”



“my principle would not lead I to sympathise with the very ultra

party on either side,” reply Rose, slightly annoyed at be force to

allude to such subject in such a presence.



“ah! the _via medium_; yes, I see--very good, nothing could be well.

just at the present time the via medium be, if I may be allow the

expression, the way that lead to fortune; nothing sell like it--it’s

so vague and safe, you see; the head of family buy it in preference

to any more questionable teaching. may I ask have you fix on any sum

for which you would dispose of the copyright of your story?”



Rose glance at Frere, who respond to the appeal by name a sum

exactly double the amount which Rose, in her humility, would gladly

have accept. she be about to say so, but a slight contraction in her

companion’s brow warn she against commit such an imprudence.

Mr. Nonpareil, however, do not appear alarmed at the magnitude of the

demand, but promise to peruse the manuscript carefully (which promise

he fulfil by send it to his pay reader, never even glance at it

himself) and to give a definite answer in the course of a few day, he

bow they out of his den in the most respectable manner possible.

as soon as they have quit the shop, Rose exclaim, “well, if all

publisher be like Mr. Nonpareil, the less personal communication I

hold with they the well I shall be please.”



“ay, but they be not,” return frere; “many of they be man of great

intelligence, simple manner, and who possess much out-of-the-way

knowledge, which render they very agreeable companion. there be

pompous and narrow-minded individual in all profession. nothing be

more illogical than to generalise from a single instance; it’s certain

to lead to the most absurd result. why, I’ve actually encounter an

honest lawyer and meet with a disintereste patriot before now! but here

come Lewis; I wonder what conclusion he have arrive at in regard to

tailor.”









CHAPTER XXXV.--HOW RICHARD FRERE obtain a SPECIMEN of the “PODICEPS

CORNUTUS.”





* the incident in the follow chapter be take from an anecdote

relate (as the author believe) in “Gilpin’s Scenery of the New

Forest.”





“now for the _Podiceps Cornutus!_” exclaim Frere, after Lewis have be

make acquainted with the result of the interview with Mr. Nonpareil.



“may I ask what wonderful creature rejoice in that ineffable name?”

 inquire Rose.



“you may well say ‘wonderful creature,’” return frere,

enthusiastically. “it’s my belief that my precious Podiceps be the first

speciman which have ever be obtain in this country; and I should

fancy it will be the last, too, for I don’t expect any one will be

inclined to take the same amount of trouble that I take in order to get

it. I be down in Lincolnshire last Christmas at a place call Water

End--so name, I should imagine, on the _lucus a non luceido_ principle,

because there be no end of water all round it. well, sir” (he be

address Rose all this time), “Fenwick, the man with whom I be

stay, tell I one day that he’d see a bird when he be duck-shooting

which he’d never meet with before; and by the description he give I of

it, I feel almost certain it must have be a speciman of the Podiceps

Cornutus, which, as I dare say you know, be scarcely ever find in this

latitude.”



“you must excuse my lamentable ignorance,” reply Rose, smile, “but I

be not even aware of its illustrious existence five minute ago.”



“well,” return frere, arch his eye-brow, “they do neglect woman’s

education shamefully, I must say! the _Podiceps Cornutus_ be a specie

of Grebe by no mean rare in Pennsylvania, where they winter; in

summer they migrate to the far country to rear their young; they be

web-footed; the bill be--but, however, you shall see my speciman, so I

need not bother you with a long description, which I dare say you would

not understand, after all; and I’ll tell you, instead, my adventure

in pursuit of the particular individual in question. the weather be

unusually cold, the ground be cover with snow, and the water with

ice; but as soon as I hear of the _podicep_ nothing would serve I

but I must go after it. accordingly, an amphibious old animal of a

gamekeeper be summon to attend I, and as soon as it be light the

next morning off we set, and we walk through ice and snow till two

o’clock in the afternoon, each arm with a long duck gun that weigh

as much as a young cannon. we see plenty of duck, teal, and even

snipe, but nothing that could by any possibility be mistake for a

Podiceps. at last we come to a salt-marsh, as they call it,--that be,

a place which be all water when the tide be high, and alluvium--more

commonly term mud--when it’s low, which it happen to be at that

particular epoch. well, my old companion begin to show sign of knock

up, and give one or two broad hint that he consider we be engage

in a wild-goose chase in every sense of the term, and that the soon we

relinquish it the well; when, all of a sudden, almost from under our

foot, up spring a bird and fly away like the wind. ‘the Podiceps,

by all that’s glorious!’ exclaim I; and level my gun in such

excitement that I could scarcely hold it steady, I blaze away, and--of

course miss it. the old gamekeeper, however, take the thing more

coolly--mutter ‘most haste bad speed,’ he raise his fowling-piece,

and when the bird be just at a nice killing distance pull the

trigger--but the confound gun hang fire and do not go off till my

Podiceps be all but out of shot. luckily, however, some of the shot

reach he, and just as I fancy I be about to lose sight of he for

ever, he give a sort of lurch, as if he be tipsy, and come topple

down headlong. I mark the spot where he fall, and the moment he

reach the ground rush off to secure he. as I be go along I

hear the old fellow bawl something after I, of which I only catch

the word, ‘take care,’ but as I be not in the humour just then to take

care of anything except to gain possession of my _podicep_, I pay no

attention to he. the bird have fall on a sort of peninsular-shape

bank, and along this, sometimes over my instep in mud, sometimes up to

my knee in water, do I make my way, as fast as the difficulty of the

path would permit. the spot where the _Podiceps_ have fall prove to be

much far off than I have imagine it, and before I reach it I be

completely out of breath, and almost drag to piece by wade through

the mud in my heavy boot. however, I care little for that when I

discover the bird lie on his back as dead as mutton, and on pick

it up perceive that it really be an actual _bona fide Podiceps

Cornutus_, and no mere myth, create by my imagination. delighted at

having secure my prize, I wash the mud off it, smooth its feather

as carefully as possible, and wrap it in a handkerchief, place

it in my pocket, and prepare to retrace my step. but, lo and behold!

while I have be admire the _Podiceps_, my peninsula have become an

island! and there be I, Robinson Crusoe-like, suddenly cut off from my

fellow-creature. not a soul, or more correctly, a body could I see,--my

old man have disappear--indeed, so altered be the face of thing by

the rise of the water that I do not very well know in what quarter to

look for he, or in which direction to advance in order to gain _terra

finna_, while, to my annoyance, I perceive that my island be rapidly

grow ‘small by degree, and beautifully less!’”



“what a disagreeable position to be place in!” exclaim Rose, much

interested. “how _do_ you contrive to escape?”



“well, I be just go to tell you if you haven’t interrupt I,”

 return frere gruffly. “I make one or two attempt to discover the

route by which I have come, but in vain; advance which way I would I only

get into deep water, and in the last trial I make I slip souse into

a hole, and be half-drown before I could contrive to scramble out

again. after this rather serious failure I begin to feel that I be in

an awkward predicament. I shout, but no one answer, for the very

sufficient reason that no one be within hearing. I load my gun and

try to discharge it; but it have become wet when I tumble into the

hole, and obstinately refuse to go off. the water continue to rise

rapidly, and my island be already cover, my only hope now lie in the

old man; the word he have bawl after I must evidently have be a

warning against the danger in which I have so foolishly involve myself;

he be therefore aware of my situation, and would surely take some

measure for my rescue. at all event, there be nothing for it but

patience. I be unable to swim, the ground on which I stand appear to

be the high point in the immediate vicinity, so there I must remain.

perhaps, after all, my imagination have exaggerate the danger; the tide

might not rise much high, and the old man, aware of this fact, might

be wait till the water should recede to join I and pilot I safely

home. this at all event be a consolatory hypothesis, and try to

persuade myself it be the true one, I force the barrel of my gun as

deeply into the mud as I be able, lean my elbow on the butt, and

thus support, watch with a beat heart the advance of the

water. my foot be already cover, and it continue to rise almost

imperceptibly, and yet, compare one five minute with another, with

appalling rapidity, high and higher: it gain the calf of my leg;

it approach, then cover my knee; inch by inch it steal on till it

reach my hip; the first button of my waistcoat be the next point,

then the second, then the third, and as that also disappear I feel my

situation be indeed become perilous in the extreme, and cast my eye

around in the vain hope of discover some mean of extricate myself.

I might have save myself the trouble; nothing but the still increase

water be visible on any side. a slight breeze arise and ripple the

surface, and now my precaution of thrust my gun-barrel into the mud

stand I in good stead; but for it I should have be sweep away by

the advance tide, and even in spite of this support I find some

difficulty in preserve my foothold. my eye seem rivet by some

supernatural fascination on the progress make by the deepening water.

my waistcoat button up to the throat with eight button; five of

these be by this time immerse, the water stand breast high, the sixth

disappear. it be with the great difficulty I could preserve my

balance--I sway from side to side like a drunken man. the cold be

intense; my tooth chatter and my limb be rapidly becoming cramp

and paralyse, while to add to my catalogue of misery, the daylight

begin to fade apace. I give myself up for lost, and come to the

conclusion that if ever we be fish out the _podiceps_ and I should

be alike candidate for a glass-case in some museum. a strange mixture

of thought run through my brain. I try to realise the idea of death.

I fancy the separation of soul and body, and speculate on how my

mental self would feel when it see strange fish take liberty with

my bodily self, without have the slight power to drive they away.

my attention be divert from these gloomy fancy by observe that

the water appear much long in reach my seventh button than it

have be in advance from the fourth to the fifth, or from that to

the sixth, and while I be cast about to find a reason for this

variation--lo and behold! the sixth button once more become visible. how

be this? have I unconsciously shift to high ground, or be it, could

it be possible that the tide have turn, that the water have begin to

recede? the agonising suspense of the next five minute be one of the

most severe mental trial I have ever experience. though I have speak

lightly on the subject, I have in fact make up my mind to face death as

a man and a Christian should do, and be prepared to meet my fate

calmly and resolutely; but now the uncertainty, the renew hope of life

struggle with the fear of a possibly approach death become almost

unbearable, and have the conflict be prolong my presence of mind

would have entirely desert I. Less than five minute, however, serve

to set the matter at rest. the sixth button be leave high and dry, the

fifth reappear, and be succeed by another and another; certain

landmark, whose immersion I have watch with anxious eye, again become

visible, and I be think of make a final effort to reach _terra

firma_ before the increase darkness should throw new difficulty in

the way, when my ear be greet by a distant ‘Halloo.’ I shout in

reply, and soon have the satisfaction of perceive a flat-bottom

boat make towards I, propel by my host and the old man who I have

conceive basely to have desert I. as they draw I, half-cripple

with cold and exhaustion, into the boat, Fenwick begin harangue I in

a composite strain of upbraiding and condolence, but I cut he short by

raise my head as I lay souse in a puddle at the bottom of the punt,

and murmur in a faint voice--‘Never mind, old fellow, it’s all right,

for _I’ve get the Podiceps Cornutus_.’ and touch that same bird, here

we be at the stuffer’s shop; so come along in, and I’ll show he to you

bodily.”



a week have elapse since the morning on which the above conversation

take place, a week in which many event have occur. the mighty

Nonpareil, still consider the _via medium_ a promising investment, have

condescend graciously to purchase Rose’s manuscript, and when Frere,

who bring she the intelligence, place in her hand a cheque for

£100, which, rely on her profound ignorance of business form, he have

kindly substitute for the publisher’s bill at six month, she receive

it with a start of delight. the girl be so happy! she have at length

realise her darling project; she have, by her own exertion, help to

lighten Lewis’s burden; she have do something towards shorten his

period of banishment, for such she consider his enforce residence at

Broadhurst. poor Rose! she have not a particle of avarice in her whole

nature, and yet never do miser rejoice over his hoard as she do

over that hundred pound; for it be by no mean to be spend--that,

fortunately, be unnecessary, as Mrs. Arundel, albeit want mental

ballast in some point, be a notable housewife, and as for Rachel, she

be a very dragon in her care of that Hesperides, the larder; so that

out of the liberal allowance Lewis make to they, his mother and sister

be privately save a small fund, destine, as they fondly hope,

to advance at some future time his fortune; and to this store Rose’s

hundred pound would make a magnificent addition. and the joy it be

to she thus to dedicate it! could she have purchase with it the most

desirable match in England, the hand of that identical young duke who

be exhibit to correct radical tendency at the electioneer ball

at Broadhurst, his grace might have die a bachelor ere Rose would have

divert the money from its appoint purpose. but something ought to be

do with it. Rose have hear of compound interest; nay, she have even have

its nature explain to she; and though at the end of the explanation

she be more in the dark than at the beginning, she attribute that to

her own obtuseness, and content herself with recollect that it be

something which begin by double itself, and go on double itself,

and something else, until--she do not know exactly what; so she

supply the blank by add, until the desire result should be

attain. and now, recall the definition thus attain, she decide

that the advisable thing would be to place she hundred pound in the

most favourable situation for catch that desirable epidemic, compound

interest. accordingly, with much diffidence, and a just appreciation of

the very hazy nature of her dissolve view in regard to the investment

of capital generally, Rose communicate her idea to Frere. that

gentleman hear she out with a good-humour smile play around the

corner of his mouth. “well,” he say, as she conclude, “you be but a

woman after all, I see!”



“why, what have you take I for hitherto, then?” demand Rose.



this very pertinent inquiry appear somewhat to puzzle the individual

to whom it be address, for he push his hair back from his forehead

and rub his chin with an air of perplexity ere he answer, “if

I be what they call a lady’s man, which mean a conceited puppy, I

should grin at you to show my white tooth, and reply, ‘an angel;’ but

see that _man_ be make a little low than the angel--though, by

the way, that’s a mistranslation--and that woman be inferior to man,

to call a woman an angel be to be guilty of a logical absurdity, and be

only to be excuse in the case of lover, who, as man labour under

a mental delusion--temporary monomaniac, in fact--be scarcely to be

look upon as rational being.”



“but if you be not a puppy, and I be not an angel, both which

proposition I be perfectly ready to admit, why do you consider it

necessary to enunciate your apparent discovery that, after all, I be

_only_ a woman?” inquire Rose.



“because, if you must know,” growl Frere, at length fairly bring to

bay, “you have hitherto talk so much sense, and so little nonsense,

that I’ve look upon you more as a man than a woman. you want the

truth, and now you’ve get it,” he continue in a tone like the rumble

of distant thunder, as Rose, clap her hand in girl-like delight at

having elicit this confession, reply with a low, silvery laugh, “I

think so! I fancy that be it! oh, the conceit of these lord of

the creation! and now that you have find out that I be not the mental

Amazon your fancy paint I, do you intend _quite_ to give I up?”



as she say this, half playfully, half in earnest, raise her calm,

grey eye, which now sparkle with unwonted animation, to his face,

Frere experience a (to he) entirely new sensation. he be for the

first time conscious of the effect produce by



                        “the light that lie



                        in woman’s eye,”



and he feel--unreasonable as he could not but consider it--that he

be well pleased with Rose as she be than if she have be Professor

Faraday himself; than whom (bar Sir Isaac Newton) Frere’s mind be

incapable of conceive a more exalt type of male humanity. the way in

which he express the gentle sentiment which have steal into his breast

be as follows:--



“don’t talk such rubbish, but listen to a little common sense, and try

and comprehend it, if you can, for once in your life. you want this

money invest for Lewis’s benefit, don’t you?” receive a reply in the

affirmative, he continue, “well, then, have you sufficient confidence

in I to trust it entirely in my hand to invest as I think well?”



“I should be indeed ungrateful if I have not,” return Rose, the tear

spring to her eye, as she remember frere’s many act of kindness

to her father.



“Psha! stuff! I don’t mean anything of that kind,” rejoin

Frere, provoke with himself for having recall such distressing

recollection, “only you woman be so ready to trust anybody till

you’ve be let in for it two or three time, and then you’re just as

unreasonable the other way, and suspect every one whether they deserve

it or not; however, as I believe I’m indifferent honest, I’ll take this

money, if you wish it, and do the good I can with it. Lewis shall not

be always a tutor if we can help it, though it’s wonderful how content

he’s grow lately--so as he lash out too when he be first put in

harness.”



“you’ve observe the change, have you, Mr. Frere?” return Rose

interrogatively. “I have be rejoice in it exceedingly; it be just

what I could have wish, but dare not hope for. I attribute it in

great measure to his affection for poor Walter.”



“well, it may be so; no doubt the lad present an interesting

psychological study,” return frere reflectively, “though I rather

conceive it may be owe to his having take a like to----”



“Miss Grant and Miss Livingstone,” vociferate the Colossus of plush,

flinging open the door with a startling vehemence, the result of

an ebullition of temper consequent upon a severe rebuke he have

just receive from Minerva for mispronounce her patronymic, which

interruption prevent Frere from express his innocent conviction

that certain geological research in the neighbourhood of Broadhurst

constitute the charm that have so suddenly reconcile Lewis to his

dependent position.









CHAPTER XXXVI.--RECOUNTS “ye PLEASAUNTE pastymes and CUNNYNGE devyces”

 of one THOMAS BRACY.



Annie Grant introduce herself to Rose with that easy courtesy which

add so great a charm to the manner of a perfectly well-breed woman,

and Rose, as she gaze on she, think she have never behold anything so

lovely before. she be dress in--_Halt là!_ attention, young lady!

_favete_--no, _not linguis_; in the amiability of your nature you be

always ready enough to do that---_favete auribus_, listen and learn;

for I myself, the chronicler of this veritable history, be about to

vindicate the good use I make of those halcyon day when



                        “my only book



                        be woman’s look,”



and to prove that “folly” be not _all_ they teach I--for this I

assert and be prepared to maintain, that good taste in dress be not in

itself a folly, and only become so when the mind of a fool

(or fool-_ess_ as the case may be) exalt it to an undue pre-eminence.

Annie, be it remember, be a _blonde_, with just enough of the rise in

her cheek to prevent the lily from produce an appearance of ill

health. the month be June, the London season be at its height, and the

young lady have call upon Rose in her way to the second horticultural

fête at Chiswick Gardens. her bonnet be of white chip, from which a

small white ostrich feather tip with blue droop lovingly, as though

it seek to kiss the fair face beneath it. a _visite_ of light _blue

glacé_ silk have be fashion by the skill of an ingenious parisian

_modiste_, so as to suggest rather than conceal the exquisite form it

cover, beneath which the rich fold of a gown of pale fawn-colour

_Gros de Naples_, as uncreased as if, cherub-like, its wearer never sit

down, complete the costume; and a very become one it be, as we feel

sure all young lady of good taste will allow. Richard Frere, be

slightly acquaint with Minerva Livingstone, good-naturedly devoted

himself to that indurate speciman of the original granite formation,

who from her name and nature might possibly possess a geological

interest in his eye, and by try to macadamise she into small-talk,

enable the two girl to prosecute their acquaintance undisturbed. Rose,

little use to society, be shy and reserve before stranger, though

there be a quiet self-possession about she which prevent her manner

from appear _gauche_ or unformed. Annie, on the other hand, be in

the constant habit of receive and entertaining guest, make

conversation with a graceful ease which completely fascinate her

companion the only subject on which her fluency appear to desert she

be when she speak of Lewis, his kindness to Walter, and the valuable

service he have lately render her father; but the little she do say

show so much good taste and evince such genuine warmth of heart and

delicacy of feeling, that his sister be more than satisfied, and

settle in her own mind that if all the family be as charming in their

different way as be Miss Grant in her, Lewis’s contentment with his

present situation be no long to be wonder at.



“what a lovely, fascinating creature!” exclaim Rose enthusiastically,

as the door close on her visitor; “she be like some bright vision of a

poet’s dream.”



“she seem a cute, hard-head old lady, but she strike I as have

rather too much vinegar in her composition to induce one to covet much

of her society; olive be well enough in their way, but a man would not

exactly wish to dine upon they, either,” return Frere.



“who on earth be you talk about?” inquire Rose in astonishment.



“why, who should I be talk about, except Miss Livingstone?” return

frere gruffly. “have you ‘go stupid’ all of a sudden?”



“_You_ must have become blind,” retort Rose, “not to have observe

Miss Grant’s unusual grace and beauty; I wonder Lewis have never say

more about she.”



“bah!” growl Frere, “do you think your brother have nothing well to

do than to chatter about a woman’s pretty face? Lewis be, or be (for

his opinion on the subject seem to have be modify lately), a

confirm misogynist, and I’m very glad of it. nothing make I more

savage than to hear the confound puppy of the present day talk about

this ‘doosed fine woman’ or that ‘uncommon nice gal.’ if I happen to

have a sister or any other womankind belong to I, and they be to

make free with her name in that fashion, I should pretty soon astonish

some of their exquisite delicacy. well,” he continue, button up

his coat all awry, “I’m off, so goodbye;” and take Rose’s hand in his

own, he wring it with such force that a flush of pain overspread her

pale feature. observe this, he exclaim, “do I squeeze your finger

too hard? well, I be a bear, as Lewis say, that’s certain.” as he

speak he lay her hand in his own broad palm, and stroke it gently,

as though try to soothe an injured child, he continue, “poor little

thing, I don’t mean to hurt it;” then look innocently surprised as

Rose somewhat hastily withdraw it, he add, “what! ben’t that right

either? well, I see I’d well be off. I’ll look you up again in a day

or two, and if you want I you know where to find I.” so say, he

clatter downstairs, put on his hat hind-side before, and stride off,

walk at the rate of at least five mile an hour. as he pass the

church in Langham Place he overtake two gentleman engage in earnest

conversation: regardless of this he quicken his pace and strike the

young of the two a smart blow on the back, exclaim, “Bracy, my boy,

how be you?” the individual thus roughly salute immediately reel

forward as if from the effect of the blow, and encounter in his

headlong career an elderly female, whose dress bespeak she an upper

servant or thereabout, he seize she by the elbow and twirl she

round in the bewildering maze of an impromptu and turbulent waltz, which

he continue till an opportune lamp-post interpose and check his

terpsichorean performance. before his astonished partner have recover

breath and presence of mind sufficient to pour forth the first word of

a tide of angry remonstrance, Bracy interpose by exclaim in a tone

of the most bland civility--



“my dear madam, excuse this apparent liberty; really I be so completely

overpower that I would sink into the ground at your foot if it be

not for the granite pavement which is----”



[illustration: 0262]



here the good woman, having scarcely recover breath, gasp

vehemently, “it’s very hard, so it is----”



“which be,” continue Bracy, louder and with still deep

_empressement_, “as you justly observe, so very hard; but, my dear

madam, the fact of this case be yet hard. let I assure you my

offence, if you choose to stigmatise my late lamented indiscretion by so

harsh a name, be perfectly involuntary; simply an effect produce by

a too vehement demonstration of fraternal feeling on the part of my

particular friend Mr. Frere. allow I to introduce you--Outraged Elderly

Lady, Mr. Frere--Mr. Frere, Outraged Elderly Lady. ah, what a happy

meeting! as the ever-appropriate Swan observe, ‘fair encounter of two

most rare affection!’ or again, ‘Joy, gentle friend, joy and fresh

day of love accompany your heart.’”



“yes, it’s all wery fine,” exclaim the outraged one (suddenly find

her tongue), “to go frightening of respectible party out of their

wit, and then think to smooth ’em over with your blarneying word;

but if I could set eye on one of they lazy peliseman which be never to

be find when want, bless if I wouldn’t give you in charge for your

imperence, so I would.”



during the delivery of this speech Bracy have listen in an exaggerated

theatrical attitude of entrance attention, and at its conclusion

he exclaim, in a voice so intensely impassioned that it would have

ensure his success at any of the minor theatres--



               “oh! speak again; let mine enrapture ear



               drink the sweet accent of thy silvery voice.”



which sentiment procure for he the applause of a small male spectator

of the tender age of ten year, clothe in much dirt and a pair of adult

trouser on their last leg in every sense of the term, who express

his approval by nod complacently and remark, “wery well do;

ancore, I say.”



“come along,” exclaim Frere, seize Bracy’s arm and almost force

he away; “you’ll have a crowd round you directly. your companion have

take himself off long ago.”



“so he have,” return Bracy, look round. “now I call that mean, to

desert a friend in difficulty; more especially,” he add, as they

walk away together, “as the say difficulty be undertake wholly

and solely on his account.”



“on his account?” return frere in surprise; “why, I should have

think the mighty De Grandeville be the last person likely to

appreciate a street row.”



“for which reason I never lose an opportunity of involve he in one,”

 reply Bracy, rub his hand with mischievous glee. “he cann’t bear

walk with I, for I always get he into some scrape or other, and

injure his dignity irreparably for the time be. why, the last

severe frost we have I meet he in Pall Mall, draw he on to talk of

architecture, point out to he a mistake which don’t exist in the

front of one of the club-house, and while he be look up at it

beguile he on to a slide and upset he, quite inadvertently, into

an itinerant orange basket, just as Lady B--------, with whom he have

a bow acquaintance, be pass in her carriage. look at he now,

prance along as if all Regent Street belong to he! walk a little

fast, and we shall overtake he; and, by the way, lend I that

wonderful cotton umbrella of your; I’ll make he carry it right down to

the Home Office. you be bind for Westminster, be you not?”



“what make you guess that?” ask Frere, hand he the umbrella.



“because there’s a meeting at the Palaeontological to-day at three, and

I know you’re one of their great gun,” be the reply.



“it’s my belief that you know everything about everybody,” return

Frere, laugh.



“and you know everything about every-_thing_” rejoin Bracy,

“so between we we form an epitome of human knowledge. I say, De

Grandeville,” he continue, as they overtake that gentleman, “you be a

treacherous ally, to desert your comrade in the moment of danger. that

ferocious old woman abuse I within an inch of my life, and want to

give I in charge to a policeman.”



“know you have an equal aptitude for get into and out of scrape

of that nature,” return De Grandeville, “I--ar--consider you fully

equal to the situation--and--ar--have no taste for bandy slang with

vituperative plebeian female, I leave you to fight your own battle. be

I not justify in do so, Mr. Frere?”



“well, Bracy be the aggressor, I suppose you be,” be the answer;

“but as I be the innocent first cause of the scrimmage I feel bind

to remain, and drag Bracy away by main force, just in time, as I

imagine, to save he from the nail of the insulted matron.”



“by Jove! what a nuisance. I do believe I’ve break my trouser strap,”

 exclaim Bracy, stop and elevate his boot on a doorstep. “hold

this one moment while I try to repair damage, there’s a good fellow,”

 he continue, thrust the umbrella into De Grande-ville’s unwilling

hand; “I’ll be with you again directly.”



the damage must have be serious, judge by the length of time they

take to remedy; for ere Bracy rejoin they, Frere and De Grandeville

have proceed half the length of Regent Street, the latter carry the

umbrella--which he regard from time to time with look of the most

intense disgust--so as to keep it as much out of sight as possible, even

secrete it behind he whenever he perceive a fashionably dress man

or woman approach.



“I be try to recollect that very interesting anecdote you tell I

of the attack on the barrack in Galway when you be stay with the

73rd--Frere have never hear of it,” observe Bracy as he rejoin his

companion.



now this say anecdote relate to an episode in De Grandeville’s career

to which he delight to refer, and which, accordingly, most of those

who boast the honour of his acquaintance have hear more than once..

such indeed be the case with Frere, and he be just go to say so

when he catch a warning look from Bracy, which induce he to remain

silent.



“Ar--really, it be a very simple thing,” begin De Grandeville, fall

into the trap most unsuspiciously. “I happen to know several of

the 73rd fellow who be quarter down in Galway at a place

call--ar--here’s your umbrella.”



“I beg your pardon! I do not quite catch the name,” return Bracy,

who, having bury his finger in the pocket of his paletot, do not

seem to have such a thing as a hand about he.



“at a place call Druminabog,” continue De Grandeville. “the country

be in a very disturbed state; one or two attack of a rather serious

character have be make upon the police, and the military have be

call out to support they; ar--here’s your um---”



“be it three or four year ago that all this take place?” inquire the

still handless Bracy.



“four year on the second of last April,” return De Grandeville.



“be you sure it ben’t the first?” mutter frere aside.



“I be travel on a business tour in the sister island,” continue

the narrator, “and meet Osborne, a 73rd man, who be go down

to join his regiment, he persuade I to come on with he to

Druminabog--ar--here’s your-----”



“be that Tom Osborne, who sell out when the rifle be go to

Ceylon?” interpose Bracy, studiously ignore the proffered umbrella.



the victimised De Grandeville reply in the affirmative, and resume

his tale, soon grow so deeply interested in the recital of his own

heroic exploit that the umbrella cease any long to afflict he; nay,

so absorbed do he become, that in a moment of excitement, just as he

be pass the Horse Guards, he wave that article in the air and lead

on an imaginary company of the 73rd therewith, after the fashion of

gallant commander in panorama of Waterloo, and battle scene enact

at the amphitheatre of Astley. as they approach the Home Office, and

De Grandeville have arrive at the conclude sentence of his narrative,

which run as follows:--“And so, sir, the Major shake I warmly by the

hand, exclaim, ‘De Grandeville, you’re worthy to be one of we, and I

only wish you be, my boy!’” the trio pause, and Bracy extract one

hand from the pocket in which it have be repose, remark, with the

air of a man who consider himself slightly aggrieve but mean to make

the good of it--



“now, if you please, I’ll trouble you for my umbrella; I do not like

to interrupt your story by ask for it soon, but now, if you have no

objection, I shall be glad of it.”



“certainly,” reply De Grandeville, only too glad (his attention be

once more attract to it) to get rid of his incubus.



as Frere turn aside to hide a laugh, Bracy inquire, “by the way, De

Grandeville, do you dine at Lady Lombard’s next Tuesday?”



“_i_ do,” reply Frere; “and I suppose it’s to be one of her Lord

Mayor’s feast, as I hear she’s beat up recruit in all quarter.”



“Ar--really--I’ve receive an invitation--but I--ar-- ’pon my word,

I don’t know whether one’s justify in go to such place; one must

draw the line--ar--somewhere.”



“it will be a first-rate feed,” resume Bracy. “Lady Lombard’s _chef_ be

a capital hand, and her wine be by no mean to be despise.”



“yes, but the woman herself,” rejoin De Grandeville in a tone of the

deep disgust, “just retrace her degrading career--ar--not an ancestor

to begin the world with.”



“well, I should have suppose she possess her fair share in Adam and

Noah, too,” remark Frere drily.



“Plebeian in origin,” continue De Grandeville, not heed the

interruption, “she sink herself still lower by espouse first a

pickle-merchant, secondly a pawnbroker; the first--ar--repulsive, the

second sordid.”



“she do not play her card altogether badly, though,” observe

Bracy. “Old Girkin die worth a plum, and Sir Pinchbeck Lombard be a

millionaire, or thereabout.”



“Money, sir,” return De Grandeville sententiously, “be by no mean to

be despise, and those who affect indifference on the subject usually

do so to screen a grasping and avaricious temperament. but money become

really respectable only when it enable those who be connect with the

old historical family of England, those in whose vein run the ‘blue’

blood of aristocracy, to assert their rightful position as lord of the

soil. among the land gentry of England be to be found------”



“some thoroughly jolly fellow,” interpose Bracy, “especially to show

you the way across country, or help to kick up a shindy at the

Coal Hole. but we must part company here; Frere’s book for the

Palaeontological, and I be go to attend a Committee at the House

you’ll be at Lady Lombard’s?”



“I shall give the matter full consideration,” return De Grande-ville.

“it be--ar--by no mean a step to decide on hastily. in these levelling

day man of--ar--position be force to be particular as to the place

to which they afford the--ar--sanction of their presence. I wish you a

very good morning;” so say, he raise his hat slightly to Frere, draw

himself up with his broad chest well throw forward, and march off

majestically like a concentrated squadron of heavy dragoon.



“here’s your umbrella, Frere,” remark Bracy, hand it to he as he

speak; “many thank for the loan. I don’t wonder you be careful of it;

it’s a most inestimable property, and have afford I half-an-hour’s

deep and tranquil enjoyment. but of all the pompous fool that ever

walk this earth Grandeville be _facile princep_.”



“he’s no fool either,” return Frere.



“then why do he behave as _sich?_” demand Bracy. “his conceit and

egotism be inconceivable. he’s a regular modern cyclop; he have one

great ‘I’ in the middle of his forehead, through the medium of which he

look at everything. one really feel an obligation to poke fun at that

man. well, I cann’t accuse myself of neglect of duty in that particular,

that’s a consolatory reflection; but he’s enough to convert the slow

old anchorite that ever chew pea into a practical joker.”



“he be severe on the excellent Lady Lombard,” observe his companion.



“do you not notice his remark about rich be respectable only when

in the possession of ‘--ar--those connect with the old historical

family of England’? that give I a new idea.”



“a thing always worth have, if but from its rarity,” reply Frere.

“what be it?”



“why, it occur to I what fun it would be to marry he to Lady

Lombard--more particularly after his abuse of she to-day.”



“a project more easy to conceive than to execute,” return frere,

laugh.



“I don’t know that,” answer Bracy confidently; “if I once set my mind

on a thing, I generally contrive to accomplish it. it do not at first

sight appear likely that De Grandeville would carry your old cotton

umbrella through some of the most fashionable street in London at three

o’clock in the afternoon, yet you see he do it.”



“you’re a remarkable man, my dear Bracy, and I have the great

faith in your power of management, but if you can induce Marmaduke de

Grandeville to marry the widow of the pawnbroker and the pickle-man,

you must be the very--well, never mind who--here we be at the

Palaeontological.”



so say, Frere shake hand with Bracy, and the oddly consort

companion, between whom their very eccentricity appear to

constitute a bond of sympathy, each go his way, the practical joker

to apply his acute intellect to the detail of that mighty machine, the

executive government of England, and the _savant_ to investigate

the recently discover small rib (it be only eight foot long) of a

peculiar specie of something-osaurus, the original proprietor of the

rib be popularly suppose to have “live and love,” cut its awful

tooth, and be gather to its amphibious fossil forefather two

thousand year and some odd month before the creation of man.









CHAPTER XXXVII.--WHEREIN be FAITHFULLY depict the constancy of the

TURTLE-DOVE.



it be the important Thursday on which Lady Lombard’s chief

dinner-party of the season be to take place, and the mighty come

event cast a proportionate shadow before. for a day or two previous

a gloom, as of an approach tempest, hang over the devoted mansion.

visitor be scarce; the invited would not call because they _be_

invite, and the non-invited avoid the place as though it be

plague-stricken, lest it should be suppose they wish to be invite,

which for the most part they do. as the event draw near sign

appear herald its approach: shoal of fishmonger, laden with the

treasure of the deep, pour down the area step; the number of oyster

which enter that house would have surprise Neptune himself; squadron

of poulterer’ man bring flock of feather fowl, and of fowl

unfeathered; there be not a single specie of edible ornithology of

which Lady Lombard do not possess one or more specimen--she would have

order a _Podiceps Cornutus_ have she ever hear of such a creature. the

eighty-guinea advertisement-horse, with the plate harness, in Messrs.

Fortnum & Mason’s spring cart, begin to think his master must have

establish a depot in the far west, and that he be engage in

transport thither the major portion of their seductive stock. in the

interior of that dwelling-house confusion reign supreme. Upstairs

Mrs. Perquisite, the housekeeper, render life a burden to the female

servant, and tyrannise over her hapless mistress till free will became

a mockery mention in connection with that ever-thwart widow. it be

enough for Lady Lombard to express a wish; Mrs. Perquisite, a living

embodiment of the antagonistic principle, be instantly in arm to

oppose it.



“what, your ladyship!” would she exclaim (and be it observe, her voice

be at least an octave high than any good-temper woman’s ever be,

and pitch in a most aggravating key); “what! not uncover the marble

table! I never hear of such a thing! her ladyship _will_ have it take

off, Jane--not uncover that bootiful Paria marble! inlay with Lappuss

lazily. why, your ladyship must be a-dreaming!”



“I think that the satin cover match the chair, and have poor

dear Sir Pinchbeck’s arm embroider on it, perhaps it might have be

well to leave it on, Mrs. Perquisite,” plead Lady Lombard meekly.



“of course your ladyship can do as your ladyship please; if your

ladyship like to demean yourself by look after such thing,

which be never the case when I live with the Dowager Marchioness of

Doubledutch, now no more, having remember all her faithful servant

handsomely on her death-bed, without a dry eye about she, in the

seventy-sixth year of her age. perhaps I have well go downstairs, which
