be only in the way, and your ladyship can direct Jane to set out the

room accord to your ladyship’s fancy.”



Poor Lady Lombard, when once that defunct Dowager Marchioness be let

loose upon she, feel that her fate be seal. it be not for she, the

widow of a man who have be knight, to fly in the face of the peerage;

so she humbly authorise the removal of the Lombard arm, implore

Perquisite to arrange the room as she have be accustom to set out

those of the poor dear Marchioness, and betook herself to the sanctity

of her own boudoir, leave the field to the virago, to whom she pay

£60 per annum for keep she in a continual state of moral bondage.



but while such scene as the foregoing be enact in the upper

portion of the establishment, the French _chef de cuisine_, Monsieur

Hector Achille Ulisse Abelard d’Haricots, be make a perfect

Pandemonium of the low region. the physical energy display by that

accomplish foreigner be truly admirable, his ubiquity be marvellous;

the tassel at the top of his white night-cap appear to have be

multiply infinitesimally and to pervade space, the sound of his

polyglot exhortation and reflection re-echo through the lofty

servant’ office. wonderful be the strange oath he pour forth,

when Antoine, a long, limp, shamble french lad, “_son élève_, zie son

of--_hélas! baigné des larme_, he even till at present scarcely could

pronounce her name--his angelic sister, since some time entomb,

having espouse _un brave Anglais_, his long-lose Louise Amélie

Marie-Antoinette de Brownsmit, née d’Haricots,”--when this unworthy

offspring of international alliance commit some unpardonable artistic

error, and unlike “Polly” of lyrical celebrity, do _not_ “put the

kettle on,” or “take it off again,” exactly at the critical moment. deep

and nasal be his ejaculation when some obtuse butcher’s boy would

not understand his “Anglishe,” which that somewhat apocryphal personage,

“_ce brave garçon_ Brownsmit” (who be Hector Achille’s Mrs. Harris,

and be consequently bring forward on all occasion), have declare he

speak like a native.



“_Mais, que diable!_ vot be zie?” he would exclaim, raise his

eyeglass to examine with a face of deep disgust a shin of beef; “vot

be zie? do I not ordaire _un gigot_, vot you call a leg of ship, and

’ere you ’ave transport to me--_ah, que c’est dégoutant!_--zie

stump of a cow: _qu’ils sont bête, ce Anglais_--takes ’Im avay.”



but if there be earthquake and tornado in the culinary and

decorative department, difficulty hydra-head have arise in the

boudoir of Lady Lombard, where sit a council of three, Rose merely

act as secretary and write just what she be bid. the third

privy councillor (besides the giver of the feast and Mrs. Arundel) be a

certain Mrs. Colonel Brahmin, relict of the late Colonel Brahmin, which

gallant officer have be cut off in the prime of life, together with 200

tawny private of the----th native infantry, by fall into an ambush

of armed Sikhs, head by Meer Ikan Chopimatoo at Choakumcurree. after

this afflict event Mrs. Colonel Brahmin return to England, in the

thirty-third year of her age, with a small pension, a very become

widow’s cap, and an earnest desire to replace the victim of Ikan

Chopimatoo’s scimitar without loss of time.



now, in bygone hour the lamented Sir Pinchbeck Lombard, in his capacity

of East India director, have know and patronise the lamented brahmin;

what, therefore, could be more natural than that their disconsolate

widow should desire to mingle their tear? and, indeed, Mrs. Colonel

Brahmin be so anxious to ensure the effectual working of this

mutual-misery-mingle Association, that on her return to England she

be good enough to stay six month with Lady Lombard; and although,

during the whole of that period, she tell every one she be anxiously

look out for a house, so few edifice be there in London and its

vicinity, that she be unable to find one till the very week before she

hostess be about to start on a self-defensive tour to the Lakes. since

then she have be vizi-in-chief to her wealthy sister in affliction,

ride in her carriage, eat her dinner, and entertain her guest,

especially such eligible male as appear likely to succeed to the

(nominal) command leave vacant by the cut-off colonel; but up to the

present time these young eligible have remain unattached, and the

appointment be still to be fill up. Mrs. Brahmin be not really

pretty, though, by dint of a pair of fine eye, glossy hair, a telling

smile, and little white hand, she contrive to pass as such. in her

manner she affect the youthful and innocent; and very well she do

it, consider her natural astuteness, and the amount of experience and

_savoir vivre_ she have acquire when follow the world-wide fortune

of the cut-off one. Lady Lombard believe in she to a great extent, and

like she well than she deserve. Perquisite see at a glance, not

only through, but considerably _beyond_ she, and hate she with all

the rancour of a vulgar mind. but Mrs. Brahmin be too strong for

Perquisite, and with her soft voice and imperturbable simplicity put

she down more thoroughly than the veri virago could have do--the

housekeeper’s most bitter speech and cut innuendo produce much

the same effect on the mild Susanna that a blow might have do upon an

air-cushion--viz., exhaust the aggressor’s strength without make

the slight impression on her opponent.



Mrs. Brahmin have be prepare to find in Mrs. Arundel a dangerous

rival, and be ready to defend her position to the death, and to battle

_à l’outrance_ for her portion of the Lombard loaf and fish. but her

courage be not destine to be put to the proof, the present be an

occasion on which an appeal to arm be unnecessary--diplomacy would

suit her purpose well, and on diplomacy, therefore, she fall back. she

have not be ten minute in Mrs. Arundel’s company ere she discover

her weak point--she be unmistakably vain. accordingly, with artless

simplicity, Mrs. Brahmin indirectly praise everything Mrs. Arundel say

or do, and Mrs. Arundel straightway suffer her discrimination to be

tickle to sleep, take Mrs. Brahmin at her own price, and dote on

she from that time forth, until--but we will leave event to develop

themselves in their due course.



Rose and Mrs. Brahmin be mutual enigma--neither could comprehend the

other. Rose have hear the detail of the “Chopimatoo” affair, and all

her sympathy be ready to be enlist in behalf of the interesting

widow; but the “sweet simplicity,” cleverly as it be do, do not

deceive she. with the instinct of a true nature she feel that it be

assume, and that beneath it lie the real character. what that might

be remain to be discover, and she suspend her judgment till

opportunity might afford she a glimpse of that which be so studiously

conceal. on the other hand, the character of Rose be one which Mrs.

Brahmin could by no means comprehend, perhaps because in its entireness

it be beyond and above her comprehension; but part of it she discern

clearly enough, and most particularly do they puzzle she. for instance,

she perceive that Rose have a mind, properly so call--that her idea

and opinion be _bona fide_ the product of her own intellect, and not

like those of too many girl, a dim reflex of somebody else’s; but the

straightforward, earnest truthfulness of her nature she could by no

mean fathom, such a quality be essentially foreign to her own

disposition; accordingly, she deem it put on for a purpose, which

purpose it behove she to find out. but her investigation do not

prosper well, from the simple fact that _ex nihilo nil fit_: rose,

have nothing to conceal, conceal it effectually.



many and important be the consultation hold in the boudoir by this

council of three, as to who should, and who should not, be invite. Lady

Lombard’s smooth brow grew furrow with the unwonted demand upon her

power (?) of mind.



“Sir Benjamin and Lady Boucher regret exceedingly that a previous

engagement prevent their accept Lady Lombard’s kind invitation for

Thursday, the--th.”



“dear I, how _dreadfully_ provoke!” sigh the perplexed

“in-vitress.”



“my dear Susanna” (the Brahmin’s Christian name), “the Bouchers be

engage, and there’ll be nobody fit to meet the General Gudgeons. what

be we to do?”



“would you ask the Dackerels? they’re such very nice people, and live in

such very good style, dear Lady Lombard,” coo Mrs. Brahmin (for, be it

observe, that bereave one’s method of speak, together with the

low, gentle, sleepy, caress tone of her soft voice, involuntarily

remind her hearer of the cooing of a dove or the purring of a cat).



“they’re only lieutenant-colonel, be they, my love?” inquire Lady

Lombard doubtingly.



“oh! my dear Lady Lombard, surely you must recollect he have be a full

colonel, by purchase, these five year, _vice_ Rawbone Featherbed,

who sell out and marry an heiress--at least,” murmur Innocence,

remember herself, or rather her _part_, “she be say to be very

rich; but of course it must have be a love-match. I cannot believe

people be so--so horrid as to marry from any other motive.”



“well, then, we’d well ask the Dackerels. Miss Arundel, my love, will

you request the pleasure of Colonel and Mrs. Dackerel’s company--with

one r, my dear--at seven o’clock. that shy son with the long leg, I

suppose we need not ask he, my dear?”



“he’s lately come into a large yorkshire property from an uncle on the

mother’s side and have take the surname of Dace, and perhaps, as he’s

so shy, he might feel hurt at not be ask. I feel such sympathy with

shyness, you know; besides, somebody say he be an author,”

 rejoin Susanna, drop her eyelid and look as unconscious and

disintereste as if John Dace Dackerel Dace, Esq., barrister-at-law,

still depend upon that ghost of nothing, his professional income,

instead of the rent-roll of the manor of Roachpool, in the West Riding.



“if they come they’ll make--let I see,” muse Lady Lombard; “what do

I say the Fitzsimmons’s be? yes, twelve; well, then, they’ll make

fifteen, and the table only hold three more, and that tiresome Mr. De

Grandeville haven’t send an answer yet, and I shall be so disappointed if

he do not come, for he know everybody and move in such high society,

and be such a tall, noble, military-look creature.”



this eulogium recalling, probably by contrast (see that the lamented

Brahmin have be remarkably small of his age all through his boyhood,

and never outgrown it afterwards), sad recollection of the fair

Susanna’s kill and wound, produce a little embroider handkerchief

which just hold the two tear its owner felt call upon to shed on such

occasion. the memory of the victim have, however, be so often before

embalm by pearly drop in her presence that Lady Lombard have grow

rather callous on the subject, and she abruptly invade the sanctity of

grief by exclaiming--



“it lie between the Lombard Browns and the Horace Hiccirys, my dear.

the Hiccirys live in well style, I know: Mrs. Hicciry be to have

be present at Court last year, only little Curatius choose to be bear

instead--the most lovely child! but the Lombard Browns be godson, at

least _he_ be, of poor dear Sir Pinchbeck’s, and they’ve not dine here

this season.”



“I think, dear Lady Lombard, if I might venture to advise, the Horace

Hiccirys would do well. Mrs. General Gudgeon would get on so well with

Mrs. Hicciry, I’m sure; and I’m afraid Mrs. Dackerel,--you know she’s

very clever, write poetry, those sweet thing in the Bijou--all clever

people be sarcastic, you know,--I’m afraid Mrs. Dackerel might laugh at

poor dear Mr. Lombard Brown’s little eccentricity about his h’s.”



“ah, yes, that’s true,” return Lady Lombard; “yes, I forget his h’s.”



“as he probably do himself,” whisper Mrs. Arundel aside to Rose.



“then, my dear Miss Arundel, may I trouble you to write a note to the

Horace Hicciry’s--with two I’s, my love--15 Bellairs Terrace, Park

Village West. what a pretty hand you write, and so quick I then if Mr.

De Grandeville will only come, the table will be fill properly.”



“and a dear, charming party it will be,” coo the bereaved one, who

have manoeuvre herself into an invitation at an early stage of the

proceeding.



“yes, my love, I hope it will,” reply the giver of the feast

anxiously. “and if I be quite sure that Perquisite and Haricot would

not quarrel, and that General Gudgeon would not take too much port wine

after dinner, and tell his gentleman’s story to the lady up in the

drawing-room, more particularly since I hear Miss MacSalvo have take an

extra serious turn lately, I should feel quite happy about it all.”



“you’d well add a postscript to the great Gudgeon’s note mention

the port wine and its alarming consequence, Rose,” whisper the

incorrigible Mrs. Arundel. her daughter smile reprovingly, and the

sitting conclude.



exactly at the time when Lady Lombard have completely give he up, and

be revolve in her anxious mind how she might well supply his loss, De

Grandeville condescend graciously to vouchsafe a favourable answer.



on the afternoon of the eventful day, as Frere be return from

his place of business, he meet--of course accidentally--Tom Bracy, who

immediately take possession of his vacant arm and engage he in a

disquisition on the use of formic acid as an anaesthetic agent, which

discussion prove so deeply interesting to his companion, that in less

than five minute he be completely lose to all outward object and

reduce (for all practical purpose) to the intellectual level of a

docile child of three year old.



“well,” continue frere eagerly, as Bracy pause before a hairdresser’s

shop, “well, supposing, for the sake of argument, I consent to waive

my objection; suppose I allow that by the process you describe you’ve

produce your acid----”



“excuse my interrupting you one moment, but I be go in here to have

my hair cut. if you’re not in a particular hurry, perhaps you’ll come in

with I, and I think I can show you where you be wrong.”



“yes--no, I’m not in a hurry; come along, I’m convinced there’s a

mistake in your theory which upset your whole argument--merely subject

to the common analysing process----”



“by the way,” observe Bracy carelessly, “you’d be all the well for

a little judicious trim yourself; besides, it’s more sociable. this

gentleman and I each want our hair cut. sit down, Frere.”



“eh? nonsense; I never have my hair cut except when the hot weather set

in,” remonstrate that individual; but he be fairly in the toil. Bracy

set a garrulous hairdresser’s man at he, who deprive he of his

hat, pop he down in the appoint chair, and envelop he in a

blue-stripe wrapper before he very well know where he be, or have

arrive at any kind of decision whatsoever on the subject. no soon be

he seat than Bracy administer a fresh dose of his anaesthetic agent;

Frere resume his argument, and long ere he have exhaust the catalogue

of chemical test to which his opponent’s theory (invent for the

occasion) might be subject, the hair-cutter (previously instruct)

have reduce his hair and whisker to the latitude and longitude usually

assign to such capillary attraction by the “manner and custom of ye

English in ye nineteenth century.” and thus Frere become, for the time

being, a reasonable look mortal, and Bracy win a new hat, which he

have bet that morning with a mutual acquaintance, on the apparently

rash speculation that he would before the day be over administer an

anaesthetic agent to Richard Frere, under the influence of which he

should have his hair cut.









CHAPTER XXXVIII.--DESCRIBES the HUMOURS of a LONDON DINNER-PARTY in THE

NINETEENTH CENTURY.



Dear Rose Arundel (excuse we the adjective, kind reader, for we

frankly own to be very fond of she), having be a perfect godsend to

everybody during the whole morning of the party day, having think

of everything and do everything, and look on the bright side of

everything, and sacrifice herself so pleasantly that an uninitiated

beholder might have imagine her intensely selfish and do it all for

her own personal gratification,--Rose having, amongst other gymnastic

of self-devotion, run up and down stair forty-three time in pursuit of

waif and stray from Lady Lombard’s memory, commit the first bit

of selfishness of which she have be guilty all day, by sit down

to rest for five minute before she begin her toilet; and lean

her forehead on her hand, think over her own chance of pleasure or

amusement during the evening. she have have one disappointment: Lewis

have be invite, and Lewis would not come. he do not say he could not

come, but he put on what Mrs. Arundel call his “iron-face,” and say

shortly, “the thing be impossible;” and no one could have look on his

compress lip and doubt the truth of the assertion. it grieve Rose,

for she read his soul as it be an open book before she, and she see

there pride, that curse of noble mind, still unsubdue. Lady Lombard

patronise they, and Lewis could not submit to witness it. Rose have

hope well thing than this; she have not fail to observe the change

that have take place in her brother during his residence at

Broadhurst; she see that from an ardent, impetuous boy he have become an

earnest-minded, high-souled man, and in the calm dignity of his look and

bear she recognise the evidence of conscious power, chasten by the

discipline of a mind great enough to rule itself. nor be she wrong in

her conjecture; only she mistook a part for the whole, and argue with

the gentle sophistry of a woman’s love heart, conclude that to be

finish which be but in fact begin. Lewis have learn to _control_

(except in rare instance) his haughty nature, but he rely too much on

his own strength, and so he have fail as yet to subdue it. Rose be

too honest to disguise the truth from herself when it be fairly place

before she, and she acknowledge, with an aching heart that the great

fault of her brother’s character yet remain unconquered. poor Rose! as

this conviction force itself upon she, how she sorrow over it. he

be so good, so noble, and she love he so entirely--oh! why be he not

perfect? if Lewis could have read her thought at that moment he would

have assuredly make one of the guest at Lady Lombard’s hospitable

board.



as the clock strike the half-hour, form the _juste milieu_ between

seven and eight, post meridiem, the goodly company assemble in Lady

Lombard’s drawing-room, be warn by the portly butler that dinner

be serve, pair off and betook themselves two by two (like the

animal come out of Noah’s Ark, as represent on the dissect

puzzle of childhood) to the lofty dining-room, where much english good

cheer, disguise under absurd french name, await they. during the

short time that Bracy have be in the house he have not be altogether

idle. he first take an opportunity of inform Lady Lombard that De

Grandeville be directly descend from Charlemagne, and that he be

only wait till the death of an opulent relative should render he

independent of his profession to revive a dormant peerage, when it be

generally suppose his colossal intellect and unparalleled legal

acuman would render he political leader of the House of Lords; he then

congratulate she on her good fortune in having secure the presence of

this illustrious individual, who, he assure she, be in such request

amongst the aristocracy of the kingdom that he be scarcely ever to be

find disengage, and wound up by run glibly through a long list of

noble name with whom he declare the mighty Marmaduke to be hand and

glove. accordingly, good Lady Lombard, believe it all faithfully,

mentally elect De Grandeville to the post of honour at her right hand,

depose for the purpose no less a personage than General Gudgeon.

when we say no less a personage, we speak advisedly, for that gallant

officer, weigh sixteen stone without his snuff-box, and be fully

six foot high, be, if not exactly “a Triton amongst minnow,” at all

event a Goliath amongst gudgeon, which we conceive to be much the same

thing.



having achieve his object of place De Grandeville in exactly the

position he wish he to occupy, Bracy next proceed to frustrate a

scheme which he perceive the fair Susanna (who be his pet antipathy)

to have originate for the amatory subjugation and matrimonial

acquisition of John Dace Dackerel Dace, Esq., of Roachpool, in the West

Riding. the aforesaid John D. D. have a weakness border indeed on a

mental hallucination; he fancy he be bear to be a popular author--“to

go down to posterity upon the tongue of man,” as he himself be willnot

to express it--and the way in which he attempt to fulfil his exalted

destiny and effect the wish-for transit, _viâ_ these unruly member of

his fellow-mortal, be by write mild, dull article, sign J. D. D.,

and send they to the editor of various magazine, by whom they be

always unhesitatingly reject.



the frequent repetition of these most unkind rebuff, and the consequent

delay in the fulfilment of his mission, have tend to depress the spirit

(at no time an intensely ardent one) of John Dace Dackerel, and have

induce a morbid habit of mind, through which, as through a yellow veil,

he take a jaundiced view of society at large; and even the acquisition

of the surname of Dace, and his accession to the glory of Roachpool,

have scarcely suffice to restore cheerfulness to this victim of a

postpone destiny. Bracy, from his connection with Blunt’s magazine,

know he well, and have reject, only a fortnight since, a forlorn

little paper entitle “the Curse of Genius, or the Trammelled Soul’s

Remonstrance;” in which his own cruel position be touchingly shadow

forth in the weak possible English. accost this son of sorrow in a

confidential tone of voice, Bracy began--



“as soon as you can spare a minute to listen to I, I’ve something

rather particular to tell you!”



“to tell I?” return the blighted barrister in a hollow voice,

suggestive of any amount of black crape hatband. “what ill news have

I now to arm, or I may say, to steel my soul against?” and here be it

observe that it be a habit with this pseudo-author to talk, as it

be, a rough copy of conversation, which he from time to time correct

by the substitution of some word or phrase which he conceive to be an

improvement upon the original text.



“perhaps it may be good news instead of bad!” remark Bracy

encouragingly. the blighted one shake his head.



“not for _I_” he murmur; then turn to Susanna, he continue,

“excuse my interrupt our conversation, but this gentleman have some

intelligence to impart--or I may say, to break to I.”



Mrs. Brahmin smile sweetly such a sympathetic smile that it go

straight through a black satin waistcoat, with a cypress wreath

embroider on it in sad-coloured silk, and reach the “crushed and

wither” heart of J. D. D. .



“you know,” continue Bracy, “I be oblige, most unwillingly, to

decline that touch little thing of your. the--what be it? the Cough

of Genius?”



“the Curse,” suggest its author gloomily.



“ah! yes. I read it cough--you don’t write very clearly--yes, * the

Cursing Genius.’ you know, my dear Dace, we editor be place in a

very try position. a great responsibility devolve upon we; we be

scarcely free agent. now your article affect I deeply” (this be

strictly true, for he have laugh over the most tragic touch till the

tear run down his cheek), “but I be force to decline it. I could not

have put it in if my own brother have write it. you will naturally ask,

why? _because it do not suit the tone of Blunt’s Magazine!_” and as

Bracy pronounce these awful and mysterious word he shake his head and

look unutterable thing; while the “child of a postpone destiny,”

 see the shadow of a still further postponement cloud his dark

horizon, shake his head likewise, and relieve his elaborately-work

shirt-front of a sigh.



“but,” resume Bracy, “think the paper much too original to be lose,

I take the liberty of hand it over to Bullbait, the Editor of the

Olla Podrida. well, sir, I see he this morning, and he said----”



“what?” exclaim the fated one eagerly, a hectic tinge colour his

sallow cheek.



“don’t excite yourself, my dear Dace,” rejoin Bracy anxiously; “you’re

look pale: too much brain work, I’m afraid. you must take care of

yourself; so many of our great genius have die young. but I see

you’re impatient. Bullbait say--he’s a very close, cautious character,

never like to commit himself, but he actually say, _he’d think about

it!_”



“be that all?” groan the disappointed Dace, relapse into

despondency.



“All! my dear sir? all! why, what would you have? when a man like

Bullbait say he’ll think about a thing, I consider it a case of _opus

operatum_--reckon the deed do. if he mean to refuse your paper, what

need have he to think about it? no, Mr. Dace, if you’re not correct

a proof of the ‘Cough’--psha, ‘curse,’ I mean--(when one once take a

wrong idea into one’s head how difficult it be to get it out again!)

before the week be over, I’m no prophet. by the way,” he continue,

as Rose, look well than pretty in the whitest of muslin frock,

resign a comfortable seat to a cross old lady in a gaudy turban, which

give she the appearance, from the neck upwards, of a plain male Turk,

liberally endow with the attribute commonly assign to his nation

by writer of fairytale and other light literature for the nursery,

amongst which man-stealing and cannibalism be two of the least

atrocious--“by the way, I must introduce you to this young lady; a

kindre soul, sir, one of the most rise authoress of the day.”



“no, I really----” begin the Dace, flap about in the extremity of

his shyness like one of his fishy namesake abstract from its native

element.



“nonsense,” resume Bracy, enjoy his embarrassment. “Miss Arundel,

let I have the pleasure of make you acquaint with one of our man of

genius, a writer to whom a liberal posterity will no doubt do justice,

however the trammelled sycophant of a clique may combine to delay his

intellectual triumph.” then in an aside to J. D. D. he add, “make

play with she; Bullbait want she particularly to write for the Olla,

and she hang back at present: she would merely have to say a word to

he, and you might obtain the run of the magazine.”



thus urge, John Dace, Dackerel Dace, Esq., call up all the energy

of his nature, and by their assistance overcome his habitual

sheepishness, he cause to descend upon Rose a torrent of pathetic

small-talk which overwhelm that young lady till dinner be announce,

when he claim her arm and float with she down the stream of

descend humanity until he find himself safely moor by her side at

the dinner-table.



Bracy have thus, as he would himself have express it, take the

change out of that odd fish Dace, and frustrate, for the time be,

the matrimonial tactic of the Brahmin’s widow, be make his way

through the various group of people in search of Miss MacSalvo, whose

rampant Protestantism might, he consider, afford he some sport if

judiciously handle, when he be suddenly intercept by the innocent

Susanna with the inquiry, “pray, Mr. Bracy, can you explain this

wonderful metamorphosis in your friend Mr. Frere? he’s grow quite

handsome.”



thus appeal to, Bracy regard attentively the individual in question,

who be good-naturedly turn over a book of print for Lady Gudgeon, a

little shrivel old lady, so deaf as to render conversation with she

a pursuit of politeness under difficulty. having apparently satisfy

himself by this investigation, Bracy reply, “to the good of my belief,

I should say he have only have his hair cut, and be for once dress like

a gentleman.”



“he be wonderfully clever, be he not?” inquire the lady.



“clever!” repeat Bracy. “that’s a mild word to apply to such

acquirement as Frere rejoice in. he know all the language, live or

dead; possess an intimate acquaintance with the art and science, have

all the ‘ology’ at his finger’ end, and be not only well up in the

history of man since the creation, but will tell you to a fraction how

many feed a day keep a Mastodon in good condition two or three thousand

year before we tailless monkey come into possession of our land

property.”



“I suppose, as he dress so strangely in general, that he’s very poor:

all clever people be, I believe,” return Susanna, with an air of the

most artless _naïveté_, the idea have for the first time occur to

she that, _faute de mieux_, the philosopher might do to replace the man

of war.



Bracy read her thought, and kindly invent a few fact and figure

by which he increase Frere’s income about sevenfold, and give he a

magnificent stock of expectation, regard the realisation whereof not

the most forlorn hope ever exist.



having do this small piece of mischief also, he continue his search

after Miss MacSalvo. the result of these machination be that Lady

Lombard signify to De Grandeville that he be to hand she down to

dinner; John Dace Dackerel Dace, Esquire, perform the same office by

Rose, much to the disgust of Richard Frere, who have intend to secure

that pleasure for himself, and who be, at the moment in which he

first become aware of his misfortune, capture by the brahminical widow,

whose silky manner he could not endure, go downstairs in a frame of

mind anything but seraphic. Mrs. Arundel contrive to gain possession

of General Gudgeon, with a view, as she observe to Bracy, to discover,

firstly, his system of feeding, which, from its result, she feel

sure must be an excellent one; and secondly, to ensure his obtaining

a liberal supply of port wine, to the end that she might satisfy a

reprehensible curiosity as to the precise nature of the “gentleman’s

story” Lady Lombard be so anxious to suppress; which act of

un-_English_-woman-like _espièglerie_ must be set down to the score of a

foreign education, than which we know not a well receipt for unsex

the mind of the daughter of Albion. when we add that Bracy, with a

face of prim decorum, escort Miss MacSalvo, a gaunt female, whose

spirit appear to have war with her flesh so effectually that there

be little more than skin and bone leave, we believe we have account

for every member of the party in whom our reader be likely to feel the

slight interest.



during the era of the fish and soup, by which our modern dinner be

invariably commence, little be discuss except the viand; but after

the first glass of sherry mute lip begin to unclose, and conversation

flow more freely. thus it come about that John Dace Dackerel Dace,

Esquire, of the Inner Temple (we admire his name so much that we lose no

opportunity of repeat it), having revolve in his anxious mind some

fitting speech wherewith to accost the talented young authoress, of whom

he feel no inconsiderable degree of dread, fortify himself with an

additional sip of sherry ere he propound the very original inquiry,

“whether Miss Arundel be fond of poetry?” before Rose could answer

this query her neighbour on the other side, one Mr. James Rasper, a

very strong young man with a broad, good-natured, dullish face, demand

abruptly, in a jovial tone of voice, “whether she be fond of ride?”



as soon as she could collect her sense, scatter by the rake fire of

this cross-examination, Rose reply “that she be particularly fond of

some kind of poetry,” which admission she qualify by the apparently

inapposite restriction--“When she be on a very quiet horse.”



J. D. D. be about to follow up his attack by a lead question in

regard to the gush pathos of the bard of Rydal, when Rasper prevent

he by exclaim, “no! do you really?” (which he call “railly”).

“then I know just the animal that would suit you.” and having thus

mount his hobby-horse he dash at everything, as be his willnot when

once fairly off, and rattle away, without stop, till dinner

be finish, and he have talk Rose completely stupid; while

the unfortunate Dace, foil in his weak attempt to captivate the

influential authoress, plunge again into the deep water of affliction,

where, ponder over this further postponement of his destiny, he sink,

and be hear no more.



exactly opposite to Rose and her companion sit Frere and the

simple Susanna, who, labour zealously at her vocation--viz.,

husband-hunting--throw away much flattery and waste an incalculable

amount of “sweetness on the desert air.” to all her pretty speech

Frere return monosyllabic reply in a tone of voice suggestive of

whole forest full of bear with sore head, while a cloud hang heavy

on his brow, and his bright eye flash envy, hatred, malice, and

all uncharitableness at the unconscious James Rasper. at last Susanna

chance to inquire whether he be fond of music; and as, without

falsifying fact, he could not answer this negatively, he be force to

reply, “yes; I like some sort of music well enough.”



“some sort only,” return Susanna in a tone of infantine artlessness.

“oh! you should like every kind, Mr. Frere. I never hear a merry tune

without longing to dance to it, and pathetic music affect I even

to tear. but what class of music be it that you particularly

prefer?--though I need scarcely ask--operatic, of course.”



“not i,” growl Frere; “I hate your opera.”



“oh, Mr. Frere!” exclaim Simplicity, fix its large eye

reproachfully upon he, “you cann’t mean what you say. not like opera!

why, they be perfectly delicious. look at a well-fill house--what a

magnificent _coup d’oil!_”



“a set of pigeon-hole full of fool, and a long row of fiddler,”

 rejoin frere; “I cann’t say I see much to admire in that. I go to one

of your opera last year, and a rare waste of time I think it. it be

one of Walter Scott’s Scotch story bewitch into Italian. there be

poor Lucy of Lammermoor dress out like a fashionable drawing-room

belle, singe duet all about love and murder with a pale-face,

moustachioed puppy, as much like Edgar Ravenswood as I be like the

Belvidere Apollo--a brute engage, on the strength of a tenor voice, to

make love to all and sundry for the space of four calendar month, for

which ‘labour of love’ he be pay to the tune of £500 a month, a salary

on which well man than himself contrive to live for a whole year.

then Lucy’s cruel mamma, who be the great feature in the novel, be

metamorphose into a rascally brother, who growl baritone atrocity

into the ear of a sympathise chorus of indigent needle-woman and

assistant carpenter, who act the nobility and gentry of Scotland

at half-a-crown a head and their beer. the first act be all love and

leave-taking, the second all cursing and confusion, and the third all

madness and misery: and that’s what people call a pleasant evening’s

amusement. the only thing that amuse I be in the last scene, when the

stipendiary lover kill himself first and sing a long scena afterwards.

I think that very praiseworthy and persevering of he, and if I’d be

Lucy such a little attention as that would have touch I particularly,

and I dare say it would have do she, only--see that she have die

rave mad some five minute before, and be then drink bottle

porter in her dressing-room for the good of her voice--she be perhaps

scarcely in a situation to appreciate it.”



“but if you don’t like the singing, I dare say you prefer the ballet?”

 suggest Susanna.



“no, I don’t,” be the short, sharp, and decisive reply.



“not like the ballet? oh! Mr. Frere, what can be your reason?” inquire

the surprised turtle-dove.



“well, I have a reason good and sufficient, but I shalln’t tell it to

you,” growl Frere; then mutter as an aside, which be, however,

sufficiently audible, “a set of jump Jezebels skip about in white

muslin kilt, for they’re nothing well; respectable people ought to be

ashamed of look at ’em.” having enunciate this opinion, Frere cast

a doubly ferocious glance at Mr. Rasper, then eloquently describe

to Rose the point of his favourite hunter, and relapse into surly

monosyllable, beyond which no amount of cooing could again tempt he.



Marmaduke de Grandeville, enthrone in state on the right hand of the

lady of the house, gaze regally around he, and in the plenitude of his

magnificence be wonderful to behold. but, after all, he _be_ human,

and the evident depth and reality of Lady Lombard’s admiration and

respect soften even he, so that ere long he graciously condescend

to eat, drink and talk--not like an ordinary mortal, for that he never

do, but like himself. for instance, the topic under discussion be

the new Houses of Parliament, then in even a more unfinished state than

they be at present, De Grandcville elaborately explain the whole

design, every detail of which he appear to have at his finger’

end--a fact for which he account when he allow it to be understand

that--“Ar--he have--ar--give Barry a hint or two--ar--that Barry be a

very sensible fellow, and not above--ar--act upon an idea when he see

it to be a good one;” and it must be own that as De Grandeville have

only once be in Mr. Barry’s company, on which occasion he have sit

opposite to he at a public dinner, he have make the good use of his

time, and not suffer his power of penetration to rust for want of

use. have in imagination put the finishing-stroke to the Victoria

Tower (one of the furth stretch of fancy on record, we should

conceive), he contrive to work the conversation round to military

matter, set General Gudgeon right on several point refer to

battle in the Peninsula at which the General have himself be present,

and give so graphic an account of Waterloo, that to this day

Lady Lombard believe he act as Amateur Aide-du-Camp and Privy

Counsellor-in-chief to the Duke of Wellington on that memorable

occasion. he then talk about the De Grandeville estate till every one

present believe he to be an immense land proprietor, and wound up

by the anecdote of William of Normandy and the original De Grandeville,

which, with a slight biographical sketch of certain later worthy of

the family (one of whom, Sir Solomon de Grandeville, he declare to have

suggest to King Charles the advisability of hide in the oak), last

till the lady quit the room, when, by Lady Lombard’s request, he

assume her vacant chair, and do the honour with dignified courtesy.



Bracy, who during dinner have appear most devoted to Miss MacSalvo,

now endeavour to render himself universally agreeable. he applaud

General Gudgeon’s story, and ply he vigorously with port wine,

which, as Mrs. Arundel have take care the servant do not neglect to

replenish his wine-glass at dinner, begin to tell upon he visibly.

he elicit the name, pedigree, and performance of all Mr. James

Rasper’s horse, and receive from that fast young man a confidential

statement of his last year’s bet account, together with a minute

detail of how he have execute that singular horticultural operation

clepe “hedge on the oak,” during which dry recital his throat

require constantly moisten with wine, in spite of which precaution

his voice grow exceedingly thick and husky before the sitting conclude.

on two individual of the party, however, all Bracy’s effort be

throw away: Frere continue silent and moody, only open his lip

occasionally, shortly and sternly to contradict some assertion, and

relapse into his former taciturnity; while J. D. D. sit silently

bewail his postpone destiny over a glass of water and two

ratafia cake, which seem to possess the singular property of never

diminish.



at length the gentleman rise to go upstairs, a matter easily

accomplish by every one but General Gudgeon, who make three

unsuccessful attempt to get under weigh, and then look helplessly

round for assistance. Bracy, the ever-ready, be at hand in an instant.



“my dear general, let I lend you an arm. you’re cramp from sit so

long.”



“tha-a-ank you, my dear bo-o-oy,” return the gallant officer, who

appear to have be seize with a sudden, wild determination to alter

the english language by divide monosyllable into three part, and

otherwise fancifully to embellish his mother-tongue. “Tha-a-ank you!

it’s that confou-wow-wow-nde gun-shot wound in my knee-ee. I get it at

Bu-Bu-Bu--no! not bucella. what be it, eh?”



“Busaco,” suggest Bracy, fear he have over-dose his patient.



however, when once the General get upon his leg he use they to

well advantage than might have be expect, and proceed upstairs,

“roll grand,” as that prince of clever-simple biographer (to adopt

one of Mrs. Browning’s double-barrel adjective), Boswell, say of

his ponderous idol. encounter Frere at the foot of the staircase, he

stumble against that gentleman with so much force as nearly to knock

he down. as he recover his footing Frere turn angrily towards his

assailant; but his irritation change to an expression of contemptuous

pity as his eye fall upon the white hair of General Gudgeon, and

step on one side, he allow he to pass. he be quietly follow,

when Mr. James Rasper, who have witness his discomfiture with an

ill-breed laugh, excite by the wine he have drank, attempt, by way of a

stupid practical joke, to repeat General Gudgeon’s involuntary assault,

and reckon Frere a good-natured, quiet sort of person, not likely to

resent such a jest, pretend to stumble against he, and push past

he when about half-way up the first flight of stair. never do a man

(to use a common but forcible expression) “mistake his customer” more

completely. in an instant Frere have collar he, drag he down a

step or two, then retain his grasp of the coat-collar, seize he

by the waistband of his trouser, and by a great exertion of strength,

swing he clear over the banister, lower he till his foot be about

a yard from the floor, and then let he drop. after which performance,

having glance round to see that his victim be not injure by the fall,

he coolly pursue his way upstairs.









CHAPTER XXXIX.--IS in two fytte--VIZ., FYTTE the FIRST, a sulky

FIT--FYTTE the SECOND, a fit of HYSTERICS.



Frere reach the drawing-room in a state of mind which the occurrence

relate in the last chapter have not tend to render more amiable. the

front room be evidently the more popular of the two, a numerous group

be gather round Mrs. Brahmin, who in the sweetest of mild soprano

be daintily coo forth a plaintive love-ditty, which be evidently

tell well with John Dace, D.D. avoid the crowd, Frere make his

way into the back drawing-room, which, bar an ardent flirtation in

a corner between two poor young thing who could not, by the most remote

possibility, marry for the next fifteen year, be unoccupied. here

seat himself astride across a chair as if it have be a horse, and

lean his arm on the back, he fall into a deep fit of musing. from

this he be rouse by the approach of a light footstep, and look up,

perceive Rose Arundel.



“why, Mr. Frere,” she exclaim playfully, “I do believe you be

asleep; will you not come into the other room? Mrs. Brahmin be singe

like a nightingale and charming everybody.”



“nightingale be humbug. I hate singe woman in general, and

abominate Mrs. Brahmin in particular, so I’m well where I be,” be the

grumpy reply.



Rose have often before receive speech from Frere quite as rude as the

present one, but in this instance there be a peculiarity in his method

of deliver it which at once strike her attention. usually his bearish

saying be accompany by a half-smile or merry twinkle of the eye,

which prove that he be more than half in jest, but now there be a

bitter earnestness in his tone which she have never before remark,

and Rose feel at once that something have occur to annoy he; so

she quietly draw a chair to the table near which he be seat, and

carelessly turn over the page of a book of print which lie before

she, observed--



“if you be not to be tempt within the siren’s influence, and

positively refuse to be charm with sweet sound, I suppose I be bind

by all the rule of politeness to remain here and try to talk you into a

more harmonious frame of mind.”



“pray do nothing of the kind,” return frere, “unless you’ve some

well reason than a mere compliance with what you please to term ‘the

rule of politeness,’ for they be thing I trouble my head about mighty

little. besides,” he add sarcastically, “your new friend, Mr. James

Rasper, must have find his way upstairs by this time, I should imagine,

and I should be sorry to deprive you of the pleasure of his intellectual

conversation, more particularly as you seem to appreciate it so

thoroughly.”



“how viciously you say that!” return Rose, smile. “but tell I, be

you really angry? have I do anything to annoy you? I’m sure it’s most

unwittingly on my part, if I have;” and as she speak she look so good,

and so willing to be penitent for any possible offence, that a man

must have have the heart of an ogre to have resist she. such a heart,

however, Frere appear to possess, for he answer shortly--



“no, I’ve no fault to find with you. I dare say it may be quite

accord to the ‘rule of politeness’ to cast off old friend and take

up with new one at a minute’s notice, and be completely engross by

they, though they may contrive to talk about horse till they prove

themselves little well than ass to the mind of an unprejudiced

auditor. there be your friend converse eagerly with Bracy, ask, no

doubt, what have become of you.”



“you be very unjust, Mr. Frere,” return Rose, look hard at her

book and speak eagerly and quickly. “Mr. Rasper be no friend of

mine; I scarcely know his name till you mention it. he sit next I at

dinner, and talk to I about horse and gallop over plough field

after fox, till I become so stupid that I have scarcely two idea leave

in my head, but of course I be bind to answer he civilly. so much for

my new friend, as you call he; what you mean by my cast off old one

I don’t at all know; I have do nothing of the kind that I be aware

of.”



“no, you have not,” return Frere, recall to his well self by

Rose’s harangue; “it be I who be, as you say, unjust and absurd, but

the honest truth be that I want to talk to you myself. all these good

people be bore more or less, none of’em able to converse rationally

for five minute together. I mean to have hand you down to dinner,

but that silky, scheme widow get hold of I instead and irritate I

with her bland platitude; and then I hear that idiot prating to you

about horse’ leg, and you appear so well satisfied with he, when

I know that you be one of the few woman who could understand and

appreciate well thing, that altogether I grow savage, and could

gladly have punch my own head or any one else’s.”



“it be quite as well Mr. Rasper be on the opposite side of the table

to you,” return Rose, “or you might have carry out your theoretical

inclination by practise on he, and then we should have have a scene.”



Frere look a little awkward and conscious as he replied--



“though I be a bear, I be not quite such a savage animal as all that

come to; I do not give the fatal hug unless I be attack first.”



at this moment Bracy and Mr. Rasper, whose back be turn towards

they, approach within earshot. the latter appear much excited, and

Rose hear he say--



“it’s no use talk, I’ve be grossly insult, sir, and if you willn’t

take my message to he, by------ I’ll take it myself, and give he as

good as he give I, or perhaps a little well.”



Frere hear he also, and a flash of anger pass across his feature.



“my dear Rasper, you’re excited,” return Bracy soothingly. “I do not

witness the affair certainly, but I cannot think that any insult be

intend. Frere be rough in his manner, but the well-hearted fellow in

the world.”



“I don’t know what _you_ may consider an insult, Mr. Bracy; but take a

man by the collar and swinge he over the banister like a cat, at the

risk of his neck, be quite insult enough for I, one for which I’ll have

satisfaction, too.”



“Hush, my dear fellow, you’ll attract general attention if you speak so

loud. here, come aside with I, and we’ll talk the matter over quietly.”



so say, he draw Rasper’s arm within his own, and lead he through a

side door which open upon the staircase. involuntarily glance at

his companion, Frere perceive her eye rivet on his feature with an

expression of alarmed inquiry.



“well, what’s the matter?” he demand, answer her speak look.



“what be that man so angry about?” return Rose breathlessly; “what

have you be do?”



“nothing very wonderful,” rejoin Frere coolly. “the young gentleman,

as I suppose one be bind to call he, drink rather more wine than be

prudent, and fancy I look a quiet, easy-temper kind of person,

by way of a dull jest, indulge himself with fall against and rudely

push by I on the staircase; and I, not be at the moment in the

humour for joking, do, as he very truly observe, swing he like a cat

over the banister, where, cat-like, he fall upon his leg.”



“oh, Mr. Frere, how could you do such a thing? and now he be dreadfully

angry, and talk about send you a message, which mean that he want

to fight a duel. Mr. Frere, you will not fight with he?” and as

Rose speak her pale cheek flush with unwonted animation, and tear,

scarcely repressed, glistened in her earnest eye.



“what do you think about it?” return frere, look at she with a kind

smile.



“oh, I think, I hope, you be too good, too wise, to do such a thing.

for Lewis’s sake, for the sake of all your friend, you will refrain.”



“for a well reason still, my dear, warm-hearted little friend,”

 return frere kindly but solemnly; “for God’s sake I will not break his

commandment, or incur the guilt of shed a fellow-creature’s blood.

but,” he add, “all this folly have frighten you;” and as he speak he

take her little trembling hand in his and stroke it caressingly, and

this time it be not withdraw.



“then you will apologise, I suppose,” Rose observe after a short pause.



“well, we’ll hope that may not be necessary,” return her companion,

“see that Rasper the infuriate be more to blame in the affair than

I be; but if the good youth be so obtuse that nothing less will quiet

he, I suppose I must accommodate his stupidity by do so. it be a

less evil to pocket one’s dignity for once in a way than to murder or be

murder in support of it.”



at this moment Bracy enter the room _solo_, with such a vexed and

anxious expression of countenance that Frere, who guess rightly at the

cause, could, though he like he the well for it, scarcely forbear

smile.



“go back to your singing widow,” observe Frere to Rose, “and when I

have administer his sop to Cerberus I will come and tell you what wry

face he have make in swallow it.”



Rose fix her eye on he with a scrutinise glance, and read

in his honest face that he be not deceive she, smile on he

approvingly, and rise, quietly mingle with the company in the front

drawing-room.



“I say, Frere,” begin Bracy as Rose disappear, “I’m sadly afraid

you have get into a tiresome scrape. that young fool, Rasper, declare

you’ve pitch he over the banister.”



“a true bill so far, and richly he deserve it,” return Frere.



“I can well believe that,” be Bracy’s reply, “for he be more than

half screw whence leave the dinner-table; but the shake appear to have

sober he into a state of the most lively vindictiveness. however,

it’s no laugh matter, I can assure you: he have send you a message by

I, and mean fight.”



“_he_ may, but _i_ don’t,” return frere shortly.



“my dear Frere, I wish I could make you understand that the affair be

serious. rasper’s determine to have you out. I can make no impression

upon he, and you cann’t refuse to meet a man after pitch he over the

banister,” rejoin Bracy in a tone of annoyance.



“cann’t I, though?” return frere, smile. “I’m not of such a yielding

disposition as you imagine. where be the sweet youth?”



“I leave he in the cloak-room,” answer Bracy; and as Frere immediately

turn to descend the stair, continue, “ ’pon my word, you’d well

not go near he: he’s especially savage. depend upon it, you will have

something disagreeable occur.”



“do you think I’m go to be force into fight a duel, a sin of the

first magnitude in my eye, because I’m afraid of meet an angry boy?

you don’t know I yet,” return frere sternly; and without wait

further parley he run downstairs, follow by Bracy, with a face of the

most comic perplexity. the door of the cloak-room stand half open, and

at the further end of the apartment might be perceive the outraged

Rasper, pace up and down like a cage lion, “nurse his wrath to keep

it warm.” unintimidate even by this tremendous spectacle, Frere coolly

enter the room, and immediately walk up to his late antagonist,

hold out his hand.



“come, Mr. Rasper,” he say, “this have be a foolish business

altogether, and the soon we mutually forget it the well. here’s my

hand: let’s be friend.”



that this be a mode of procedure on which Mr. Rasper have not calculate

be evident, as well by his extreme embarrassment as by his appear

completely at a loss what course to pursue. for a moment he seem half

inclined to accept Frere’s proffer hand; but his eye fall upon Bracy,

and probably recall the threat he have breathe forth in the hearing

of that worthy individual, he feel that his dignity be at stake; and

give himself a shake to re-arouse his indignation, he reply, “I

shall do no such thing, sir. you have grossly insult I, and I demand

satisfaction.”



“excuse I,” return frere quietly, “I do not insult you: I simply

would not allow you to insult I; no man worthy of the name would.”



“it’s no use jangle about it, like a couple of woman, _i_ consider

that you have insult I: what _you_ may think matter nothing to I. I

have be insult, I require satisfaction, and I mean to have it too,”

 reiterate Mr. Rasper, talk himself into a passion.



“now, listen to I,” return frere impressively. “you be a young man

than I be, and have probably, therefore, more of life before you. you

be of an age and temperament to enjoy life vividly. there be many that

love you; I can answer for three, for I meet your mother and two

sister at Lord Ambergate’s a fortnight since, and the kind creature

entertain I for half-an-hour with your praise. why, then, seek to

throw away your own life and embitter theirs, or bring upon your head

the guilt of homicide, entail banishment from your home and country,

and other evil consequence, merely because, having drink a few extra

glass of wine, you seek to play off a practical joke upon I, and

I, not be at the moment in a jesting humour, retaliate upon you, as

you, or any other man of spirit, would have do in my situation? come,

look at it in a common sense point of view: be this a cause for which to

lose a life or take one?”



after wait a moment for a reply, during which time Rasper

stand gnaw the finger of his white glove in irresolution, frere

resumed--“If you’re sorry for your share in the matter, I’m perfectly

willing to own that I be for mine; and now, once more, here’s my

hand--what do you say?”



“say, that you’re a regular out-and-out good fellow, and that I’m

a------d ass, and beg your pardon heartily,” be the energetic

rejoinder; and bring his hand down upon Frere’s with a smack that

re-echo through the room, Rasper and his late antagonist shake hand

with the strength and energy of a brace of giant; and then, both

talk at once with the great volubility, they ascend the stair

arm in arm, Bracy follow they, with his left eye fix in a

specie of chronic wink, expressive of any amount of the most intense

satisfaction and sagacity. as they re-enter the drawing-room, Rose,

whose power of hearing, always acute, be in the present instance

render still more so by anxiety, catch the follow word; “then you

promise you will dine with I at Lovegrove’s on Thursday, and I’ll

pick up half-a-dozen fellow that I know you’ll like to meet, regular

top-sawyer, that you’re safe to find in the first flight, be it where

it may.”



“only on condition that you come to my room on Friday and bring your

brother, and we’ll show you sport man how we bookworm live--Bracy,

we shall see you?”



“you’ll dine with we, too, at Blackwall, Mr. Bracy,” rejoin the first

speaker, who be none other than the redoubtable rasper. and numerous

other genial sentence of like import reach the ear and comfort the

heart of that little philanthropist, Rose Arundel, who could no more

bear to see her fellow-creature disagree than could Dr. Watts, when in

his benevolence he indict that pretty hymn which begins--



                   “let dog delight to bark and bite,



                   for ’tis their nature to!”



then proceed to state the interesting ornithological fact that



                   “bird in their little nest agree,”



and touchingly appeal to the nobler instinct of childhood in the

pathetic metrical remonstrance--



                   “those little hand be never make



                   to tear each other’s eye.”



oh! excellent and prosy Watts, doer of dull, moral platitude into

duller doggerel, co-tormentor with Pinnock and the Latin grammar of my

early boyhood, would that for thy sake I have the pen of Thomas Carlyle,

for then would I write thee down that which I suspect thou wast, my

Watts, in most resonant un-English, nay, I would make thee the subject

of a “Latter-Day Pamphlet,” and treat of thee in connection with the

_vexata questio_ of prison discipline, would by thy aid invent a new and

horrible punishment for refractory felon, who in lieu of handcuff

and bread and water, hard labour, or solitary confinement, should

straightway be condemn to a severe course of “Watts’ Hymns.”



Thomas Bracy, his mind be relieve from the _onus_ of this rather

serious episode in his evening’s amusement, now cast his eye around to

discover how the various scheme project by his fertile brain might

be progress. the first group that meet his eye afford he unmixed

satisfaction--Lady Lombard, seat on a low fauteuil, be listen

with delighted attention to De Grandeville, who, hang over she,

be talk eagerly (about himself) with an air of the most lover-like

devotion. the next pair that his glance fall upon scarcely pleased he

so well, for Mrs. Brahmin have again hook the Dace, and appear in a

fair way of land he safely. “however,” reflect Bracy, “one comfort

be that he’s such an awful fool he will bore she to death in less than a

week after they’re marry, and she’ll revenge herself by flirt with

every man she meet, which be safe to worry he to distraction, and

they’ll be a wretched, miserable couple; so I really believe there’ll

be more comedy evolve by let they alone than by interfere with

they;” and console himself with this agreeable view of the matter,

he turn his attention to the state, mental and physical, of General

Gudgeon. that gallant son of Mars, as though conscious of the hope and

fear that be abroad concern his possible behaviour, be take

the good method of neutralise the dangerous effect of his devotion

to Bacchus by compose himself to sleep in a mighty arm-chair. next

he be seat Miss MacSalvo, who be engage in a truly edify

conversation with Mrs. Dackerel, mother to the “postpone one,” on the

propriety of establish a female Missionary Society for the prevention

of Polygamy amongst the Aborigines of the North-eastern Districts of

South-West Australia; an evil which both lady agree to be mainly

owe to the fact that the woman do not know how to conduct themselves

like the woman of civilised nation; a fact to which Bracy assent by

observe “that be self-evident, or the man would find one wife quite

enough;” on which Miss MacSalvo turn up the white, or more properly

speak, the yellow of her eye, and ejaculate, “ah, yes indeed!”

 with much unction, though it be to be doubt whether after all she

perceive the full force of the remark.



“why, General,” exclaim Bracy quickly, “_you_ have be in Australia;

you’re the very man we want; rouse up, my dear sir, and enlighten our

darkness.”



“pray, sir,” observe Miss MacSalvo, address General Gudgeon, “pray,

sir, can you give I any insight into the habit and custom of those

interesting, but, alas! misguided individual, the Aborigines of South

Australia; more particularly with reference to the female portion of the

population--any little anecdote which may occur to you now?”



“by Jo-o-ove, ma’am,” return the General, whose English have not yet

‘suffer a recovery’, “you’ve come to--you’ve come to the right, eh--to

the, the right, what-be-it?”



“Shop,” suggest Bracy.



“Ye-es, to the right sho-op, if that’s what you want, ma’am. I should

think there ain’t a man--there ain’t a man--eh? yes, breathing--that can

tell you more--eh? more about larks----”



“it be scarcely with a view to the ornithology of the country that I be

anxious to gain information,” interrupt Miss MacSalvo; “the fact I

require regard the general behaviour and moral conduct of the female

population of the north-eastern district.”



“eh! oh, yes--yes, I under--I understand what you’re up to, eh?” resume

the General, with what he intend for a significant wink at Bracy;

“there be Tom Slasher and I--a rare wild young, eh? yes, a wild young

dog be Tom; well, ma’am, there be a gal over there--she ben’t one of

the native, though--they’re taw-taw--yes, tawny colour--but this

gal be a nigger--reg’lar darkie--Black-_hide_ Susan, Tom use to call

she--witty chap be Tom.”



and the General be fairly start, continue to talk most volubly,

though, from the peculiarity of his diction, he do not get to the

point of his story so quickly as might have be expect. in the

meantime Frere contrive to rejoin Rose, and seat himself almost in

her pocket, observe in a low voice--



“well, I’ve manage to tame the dragon, you see.”



“yes, and persuade he to dine _with_ you instead of _upon_ you,”

 return Rose, smile; “but tell I,” she add, “how do you contrive

to satisfy he. be you force to apologise?”



“oh, I put the thing before he in a common sense point of view,”

 reply frere; “appeal to his good feeling as if I have faith in his

possess such a quality, which be the sure way to call it forth if it

exist, and wind up by tell he that if he be sorry for his share

in the business, I be ditto for mine, which mode of treatment prove

eminently successful. he apply a forcible adjective to the word ass,

and stigmatise himself by the epithet thus compound, he shake I

heartily by the hand, and straightway we become the great friend,

ratify the contract by an exchange of dinner invitation, without

which ceremony no solemn league and covenant be consider bind in

England in these day of enlightened civilisation.”



“well, I think you have behave more bravely and nobly than if you

have fight twenty duel,” exclaim Rose, fairly carry away by her

admiration. “I esteem and respect you, and--and----!” here she stop

short, and a bright blush overspread her pale feature, for she

perceive Frere’s fine eye fix upon she with an expression of

delighted surprise which she have never observe in they before, and

which bring to her recollection the fact that, after all, he be

a live man not many year old than herself, instead of some

magnanimous, philosophical, and heroic character in history do into

modern English and animate by magic for her express delectation. the

light in Frere’s eye have, however, fade, and he have relapse into his

accustomed manner ere he reply: “I cann’t say I see anything to make a

fuss about in it. I ben’t go to allow a half-tipsy boy to insult I

with impunity, so I pitch he over the banister as a trifling hint to

that effect; neither do I feel inclined to shoot he, or let he shoot

I, by way of compensation for his tumble, because it would have be

equally wrong and irrational so to do, and I go and tell he my idea

in plain English, which be the natural course to pursue, and produce

the desire effect. I really cann’t see anything remarkable in it all.”



“I fancy that I do,” reply Rose archly; “but of course we poor woman

cannot pretend to be competent judge in such a case.”



“you know you don’t think anything of the kind,” return frere; “you’ve

get a very good opinion of your own judgment, so don’t tell story.”



“without either admit or deny the truth of your assertion, I

should like to know what ground you have for make it?” ask Rose.



“I can soon tell you, if that’s all you want to know,” return Frere.

“you could not act for yourself with the quiet decision I have before

now see you exercise when occasion require it unless you possess

self-appreciation sufficient to give you the requisite degree of

confidence.”



Ere Rose could reply their conversation be interrupt by a pierce

shriek follow by an extreme bustle and confusion on the other side of

the room. the cause be soon explain. excited with wine, and artfully

draw on by Bracy, General Gudgeon have tell one of his “gentleman’s

story” to Miss MacSalvo, on the strength of which outrageous anecdote

that zealous advocate for establish a Female Missionary Society for

the Prevention of Polygamy amongst the Aborigines of the North-Eastern

District of South-West Australia have see fit to go off into a perfect

tornado of the most alarming hysteric!









CHAPTER XL.--SHOWS, AMONGST other matter, how RICHARD FRERE passed a

RESTLESS NIGHT.



the hysterical affection of the praiseworthy antipolygamist having

take place late in the evening, may be say to have break up the

party. Mrs. General Gudgeon, who, when the catastrophe occur, be

more or less asleep over the same book of print to which she have

devote herself on her first arrival, originate, as she witness the

confusion, a faint idea--(all this lady’s idea, and they be not

many, be of a dim and hazy character, so that a good impression of her

thought--if we may be allow the term--be a rarity hardly to be meet

with)--that her well half be in some way connect with the matter;

and know that dine out usually “produce an effect upon he” (as

she delicately and indefinitely phrase it), she forthwith institute

an inquiry after her carriage, and that “vehicle for the transmission of

heavy body” be report in readiness, she issue marching order,

and as soon as the honourable and gallant officer could be get upon his

leg, take he in tow, and in his company depart.



the Dackerels hasten to follow this example, the maternal Dackerel

having come in for her share of the General’s “good thing,” and

appear much inclined to “trump” Miss MacSalvo’s hysteric with a

fainting fit; J. D. D., with a face even long than usual, support

her retire footstep. he have be warm his chilly spirit in the

sunshine of the widow’s smile, till, in the possibility of some

day call that delicate creature his own, the outline of a new and

fascinating destiny have be trace upon the foolscap paper of his

imagination; but the doom be still upon he, and in the call of filial

piety he recognise a fresh postponement even of this last forlorn hope.

Frere have shake hand with Rose, apologise for not be able to

lunch with they the next day--a thing which nobody have ask he to

do--and, having set the butler and both the tall footman to look for

his cotton umbrella, and put on consecutively two wrong greatcoat, be

about to walk home, when Mr. James Rasper interfere; he would drive his

friend home--anywhere--everywhere--so that he would but accompany he.

he want to show he his cab; he wish to learn his opinion of his

horse--in short, he would not be deny; and Frere, begin to think

his friendship a bad alternative than his animosity, be force to

consent, which he do thus--



“well, yes, if you like. I shall get home soon, that’s one comfort;

and I’ve get three hour’ work to do before I can go to bed. be this

your trap? the brute willn’t kick, will he? ugh! what an awkward thing to

get into. I believe I’ve break my shin. go ahead! mind you steer clear

of the lamp-post. I cann’t think why people ride when they’ve get leg

to walk with.”



Bracy wait patiently to hand Miss MacSalvo downstair, which he do

with much gravity and decorum, lament the disgraceful conduct of

General Gudgeon, of whom he remark, with a portentous shake of the

head, that “he greatly fear he be not a man of a sober or edifying

frame of mind,” which observation be certainly true as far as the

sobriety be concern.



whether Jemima of the sour countenance have, in arrange Frere’s bed,

impart somewhat of the angularity of her own nature to the feather,

or whether the event of the evening have excite that part of his system

in regard to the existence whereof he indulge in a very bigotry of

scepticism, namely, his nerve, certain it be that when (having read

Hindostanee till daylight peep in upon his study) he go to bed he

do _not_ follow his usual system of drop asleep almost as soon

as he have lay his head upon his pillow; neither could he apply his

ordinary remedy for insomnolency, for when he try to concentrate his

attention on some difficult sentence in his Hindostanee, or to solve

mentally an abstruse mathematical problem, a figure in white muslin

obscure the asiatic character or entangle itself inextricably with

rectangular triangle, so that the wish-for Q. E. D. could never

be arrive at. Frere have never think Rose Arundel pretty till that

night--one reason for which might have be that he have never think

about her appearance at all; but now, all of a sudden, the recollection

of her animate face as, carry away by the impulse of the moment, she

have begin to tell he how she admire his noble conduct, occur to

he, and all its good point flash upon he and haunt and oppress

he. the smooth, broad forehead--he have observe that before, and

decide it to be a good forehead in a practical point of view--i.e.> a

capacious knowledge-box; but now he feel that it be something more,

and the mysterious attribute of beauty force itself upon his notice and

fling its charm around he. then her eye--those deep, earnest, truthful

eye--seem yet to gaze at he, with a bright expression of interest

sparkle through their softness. he could not, try as he please,

banish the recollection of those eye; as he lie and think they come

across he, and bewitch he like a spell. and her mouth--what a world

of eloquence be there even in its silence; there might be trace the

same firmness and resolution which mark the haughty curl of Lewis’s

short upper lip; but the pride and sternness be want, and in their

place a chasten, pensive expression seem to afford a guarantee that

the strength of character thus indicate could alone be arouse in a

good cause; but the true expression of that mouth be to be discover

only when a smile, suggestive of every soft, bright trait of woman’s

nature, reveal the little pearl-like tooth. all this seem to have

come upon frere like a sudden inspiration; he could not banish it

from his recollection, and the more he reflect upon it, the less he

understand it. and so he toss and tumble about, restless alike in

mind and body, till at last, just as the clock strike six, he fall into

a doze. but sleep afford he no refuge from his tormentress. Rose,

change and yet the same, haunt his dream; but a halo appear to

surround she--she have acquire a character of sanctity in his eye.

never again could he inadvertently address she as “sir,” and he would as

soon have think of connect the idea of a “good fellow” with one of

Raphael’s Madonnas as with Rose Arundel. at half-past seven Jemima--a

very chronometer for punctuality--knock at his door, and receive no

answer, _sans cérémonie_ walk in, to see what might be the matter; and

find her master rather snore than otherwise, invade his slumber

by exclaim in a shrill voice--



“it ain’t of much use I get out of my blessed bed with the rhumatiz

in the small o’ my back to bring your hot-water by halfpast seven, if

you lay there snore like a hog, Master Richard, and willn’t answer a

body when they call you;” to which appeal she receive the somewhat

inconsequent reply--



“well, suppose I wouldn’t let he shoot I, there’s nothing very fine in

that, Rose.”



“listen to he,” exclaim Jemima, aghast; “lor’ a mussy! I hope he

ain’t a wandering, or take to the drink. Master Richard, will ye please

to wake and talk like a Christian, and not go frightening a body out of

their wit,” she continue in a tone of voice as of an agitated sea-mew.



“eh, what? oh, be that you, Jemima? I be so sound asleep; go away and

I’ll get up directly,” mutter Frere, become conscious of those usual

colloquial antipode, “his room and his company.” but Jemima have be

flurry and render anxious on his account, first by his silence, next

by his incoherent address, and now find her alarm have be without

foundation, her well feeling turn sour, and have her master at

an advantage, see that he could not rise till she should please to

convey herself away, she give vent to her acidulate sentiment in the

follow harangue--



“yes, it’s all very well to say ‘go away,’ as if you be speak to

a dog, after frightening people out of their wit by talk gibberish

about shoot and fine rose; but I see how it be, you’re a taking to

evil course, a stay out here till one o’clock in the morning, for I

hear ye a comin’ in, lie awake with the rhumatiz in the small o’

my back, drinking and smoking cigar, which spile the tooth and

hundermine the hintellect, and account for be non compo Mondays

the next morning; but I’ve live with you and yours thirty year and odd,

and I ain’t a-go to see you rack-and-ruining of your constitution

without a-speak up to tell you of it, for all your look black

at the woman that nurse you when yer be an hinocent babby, all

onconscious of sich going on.”



“my good woman, don’t talk such rubbish, but go away and let I get my

thing on,” return frere in a species of apologetic growl.



“Rubbish indeed!” continue Jemima in a violent falsetto, her temper

be thoroughly arouse by the contemptuous epithet apply to she

unappreciated homily; “that’s all the thank one get for one’s good

advice, be it? but I don’t care. I’ve live with you, man and boy, nigh

half my life, which, like the grass of the field, be three score year

and ten come Michaelmas twelvemonth, and I’m not a-go to see you take

to evil course without lift up my voice as a deacon set on a hill to

warn you against ’em, which be what your blessed mother would have do

only too gladly if she ben’t an angel in the family vault, where we

must all go when our time come; smoke filthy cigar and stop out

till one o’clock in the morning, indeed!” and mutter these word over

and over to herself, as a sort of _refrain_, Jemima hobble out of the

room with more stoutness and alacrity than could have be expect from

her antiquated appearance. relieve from the incubus of her presence,

Frere rise and proceed to dress himself; but the nightmare that have

oppress he, whether sleep or wake, haunt he still.



in vain he try to shave himself; the vision in white muslin come

between his face and the look-glass and occasion he to cut his

chin. at his frugal breakfast it be with he again, and strange to

say, take away his appetite; it go out with he to his scientific

institution, and weaken his perception, and absorb his attention,

and dull his memory, till even the most positive resolve nebulæ swam

in a mist before he, and the mountain of the moon, which have lately

develop a new crater, might have be the _bona fide_ production of

that planet instead of merely her african godchildren, for aught that

he could have state to the contrary. he get through his morning’s work

somehow, and then the vision prompt he to call at Lady Lombard’s,

and give he no peace till he start for the goodly mansion of that

hospitable widow, which he do in such an unusually agitated frame of

mind, that for the first time in the memory of man he forget his cotton

umbrella; he hurry wildly through the street, overthrow little

child and reverse apple-woman, not to mention an insane attempt to

constitute himself a member of the “happy family,” by dash violently

against the wire of their cage, which contain all kind of strange

animal except a Richard Frere, or a _Podiccps Cornutus_, till at last

he reach the locality in which Lady Lombard’s house be situate.



and here a new and unaccountable crotchet take possession of his

brain. frere, who since he could run alone and express his sentiment

intelligibly in his native tongue have never know what bashfulness

mean, be seize with a sudden attack of that uncomfortable sensation,

the extinguisher of so many would-be shine light of humanity, who

but for that “floor” quality would have publish such book and make

such speech that the hair of society at large, upraised with wonder

and admiration, must have stand on end through all time, “like quill

upon the fretful porcupine.” so violent be this attack of shyness that,

after having hurry from his office as though life and death hang upon

his speed, he could not make up his mind whether to pay the project

visit or not, and actually stroll up and down, pass and repasse

the door some half-dozen time before he venture to knock at it; nay,

to such an extent have this mysterious “_timor panico_” seize upon

he, that when the plush-clothe “man mountain” appear in answer to his

summon he merely leave his card, and inquire meekly how the lady

be, post off at, if anything, a more rapid pace than that at which

he have walk on his way thither.



then ere he have proceed the length of a street come the reaction,

under the influence of which he not unjustly stigmatise himself as

an egregious fool, and but for very shame would fain have retrace his

step. he could not, however, make up any credible excuse for face the

noble footman a second time, so as the next good thing to see Rose,

he find his way to Park Crescent and call upon Lewis, to whom he

relate how he feel so restless and fidgety that he be persuade he

must be about to develop a feverish cold, or some analogous abomination.

having engage Lewis to accompany he on the following evening to

a lecture at the Palaeontological on “the Relations of the early

zoophyte,” whoever they might be, he be about to depart, when, as he

reach the hall, a carriage, with a splendid pair of grey, dash up

to the door, and a pretty little brunette with sparkle black eye,

a brilliant complexion, and a bonnet the colour of raspberry

ice, descend, and pass Frere with a glance half saucy, half

contemptuous, run upstairs as if she be an _habituée_ of the house.

this be Emily, Countess Portici, Loid Bellefield’s young sister, who,

have at nineteen run away with an italian nobleman, for love of his

black eye and ivory complexion, have ere she be five-and-twenty grown

heartily sick of they and of Italy, and discover some good reason to

quit that land of uncomfortable splendour to enjoy the gaiety of a

London spring, leave her picturesque husband to console himself

as good he might during her absence. she possess very high spirit

without any vast amount of judgment to counterbalance they, and her

present frame of mind be that of a school-girl rejoice in a holiday,

into which she be determined to cram as much pleasure, fun, and frolic

as an unlimited capacity for enjoyment would enable she to undergo. on

the strength of her position as a married woman, she constitute
