herself Annie’s chaperon on all occasion when the vigilance of Minerva

Livingstone could be elude; and as that Gorgon of the nineteenth

century be not so young as she have be, and find late hour tend

to reduce her stamina and degrade the dignity of ill temper to the

ignominious level of mere peevishness, she unwillingly allow the

Countess Portici to act as her substitute and escort Annie to such

evening entertainment as from their nature threaten to invade the

hour dedicate by Minerva to repose. there be much similarity of

feature and of manner between the Countess and her brother Charles

Leicester, only that Charley’s languid drawl be in Emily replace by a

sparkling vivacity, which, together with a certain selfish good-nature

that lead she to promote the enjoyment of other on every occasion in

which it do not come in contact with her own, be sufficient to render

she a general favourite. Annie be no exception to this rule; and always

delight to escape from the petrify influence of Minerva, eagerly

second all her lively cousin’s scheme for her amusement.



the object of the Countess’s visit on the present occasion be to secure

Annie for the following evening, when they be to dine together, and

be afterwards to be escort to the Opera by Lord Bellefield, where

they be to hear a new soprano with a voice three note high than

that of anybody else, which note might by a mild and easy figure of

speech be not inaptly term bank-note, see that by their exercise

the fair cantatrice have realise the satisfactory sum of thirty thousand

pound.



the Countess’s scheme happen to fit in very nicely with the view of

the elder, as the General dine out, and Minerva be nurse a cold,

which must have reduce the temperature of her blood to some frightful

figure below zero, the project meet with no more opposition than, from

the constitution of Miss Livingstone’s mind, be inevitable. and thus it

come about that on the following day Emily call for Annie, and the two

girl (for the matron be a very _girlish_ speciman of five-and-twenty)

drive round the park together, and then retire to Emily’s boudoir and

“talk confidence” till it be time to dress. Annie’s revelation

do not go much more than skin-deep, and relate chiefly to anxiety

concern papa and difficulty with Aunt Martha, who be “so tiresome

about thing, and never would let anybody love she,” and then branch

off to a retrospective sketch of the preliminary difficulty which have

obstruct Charley Leicester’s wedding, end by a detailed account

of the ceremony itself, and Annie’s hope and fear as to the ultimate

result of the bridegroom’s good resolution.



emily, on the contrary, plunge at once _in médias re_, and relate

how all last winter she have be render wretched by “Alessandro’s”

 attention to the Marchese Giulia di B------ani (she reveal the blank

in an agitated whisper), and what all her particular friend have say

to she on the subject, and how she have jointly and severally reply to

they that the dignity of her sex support she; whence, warm with

her subject, she go on to state how she in her turn have support this

dignity by repulse the advance of Captain Augustus (familiarly and

affectionately reduce, for colloquial purpose, into Gus) Travers,

who, having be her first love, and retire _vice_ Alessandro Conte de

Portici promote to the rank of husband, consider that it be again

his innings, and have diligently seek to become platonically her third

love and dispute the post of _cavalier servente_ with all and sundry,

in spite of which constancy and devotion she have persevere in her

repulsiveness until, between her cruelty and a reckless indifference to

malaria, poor Gus be attack with a brain fever, and then of course

when he grow a little well she could not continue unkind to he,

for she might have have his life to answer for, and that be a serious

consideration; and so by degree he take to come to the Palazzo

Portici constantly and go about to place with she, and somehow she

get accustom to he, and Alessandro do not seem to mind, and poor Gus

always behave very well, and only ask to be allow the privilege of

her friendship, and everybody do the same sort of thing--“It’s their

way over there, you know, Annie dear;” till at last Belle-field come,

and he have never be able to endure Gus because he be so handsome,

poor fellow, so Bellefield make a great fuss and say all sort

of shocking thing, and set Alessandro at she; and bad than all,

quarrel with Gus and want to horsewhip he, and it almost come to a

duel, only she write Gus a little note, implore he not to fight, but

to go away and forget she; and he have do the first directly, and she

dared say he have do the second, for she’d never see he since, which

of course she be very glad of. and here she heave a deep sigh and

caress a comic and unnatural transalpine poodle, which by reason of

its flow lock look like an animate carriage mat, as though it

have be a pet lamb, the sole prop of some heart-break and dishevel

shepherdess, to which picture of pastoral pathos do Emily, Countess di

Portici, then and there mentally assimilate herself.



and to all this history of love, and hate, and platonic friendship,

whatever they might be, simple innocent Annie listen with much

interest and more perplexity. she have a vague notion that Emily have

behave foolishly, if not wrongly; but she be very fond of her cousin,

who, from the difference in their respective age, have acquire a

degree of ascendency over she which their natural character scarcely

warrant. then Annie’s deep ignorance of foreign manner and custom

throw a mist of uncertainty around the whole affair, beneath the shadow

of which she be able to put the most charitable construction on Emily’s

conduct without “stultify her moral sense” (to speak as a logician);

still she felt call upon to give her cousin a little good advice in

regard to strive entirely to forget, and scrupulously to avoid for the

future, the too fascinating Gus, for which Emily kiss she and call

she a dear, silly, little prude; then twine their arm round each

other’s taper waist, the girl descend to the dining-room, unite

for the time being, literally and figuratively, by the close bond

of amity and affection. stand rather in awe of her brother, Emily

conduct herself during the meal with so much gravity and decorum that

she quite throw a shade over Annie’s usual lightheartedness, and by the

time they reach their opera box a more sombre trio (not even except

the soprano, the tenor, and the baritone, of whom the first two be

prepared to be poison, and the third to stab himself on their marble

tomb before the evening should be over) could not have be find

beneath the roof of her Majesty’s theatre.



between the act of the opera a divertissement be introduce, in which

a _danseuse_, who have acquire an italian reputation, but who be as

yet unknown in England, be to make her first appearance. Emily be

converse volubly about her various merit, when a fashionably-dress

young man with delicate feature, a profusion of dark wave curl,

and a pair of the most interesting little black moustachio imaginable,

lounge into one of the stall and begin lazily to scrutinise the

company through a richly-mount opera-glass. he be undeniably

handsome, but the expression of his face be disagreeable, and his

whole demeanour blasé and puppyish in the extreme. as he enter,

Annie perceive her cousin to give a violent start, and, as she meet her

glance, to colour slightly; then, evidently unwilling to attract she

brother’s notice, she make a successful effort to recover herself, and

appeared completely absorb in the terpsi-chorean prodigy of the

new opera-dancer. just at the conclusion of the divertissement some one

knock at the door of the box, and on Lord Bellefield’s open it,

Annie hear a man’s voice say, in a hurried manner, “can your Lordship

allow I two minute’ conversation? my business be of the utmost

importance.” Lord Bellefield reply in the affirmative and quit the

box, close the door behind he. as he do so, Emily, lay her finger

on her cousin’s arm, say in a hurried whisper: “Annie, do you see that

gentleman in the fourth row of stall, the sixth from this end? that’s

Gus; ben’t he handsome, poor fellow? ah!” she continue, as the object

of her scrutiny suddenly bring his opera-glass to bear upon their box,

“he have make I out, and he do not know that Bellefield be here. oh! I

hope he willn’t think of come up!”



as she speak, Gus, having become aware of her presence, make an

almost imperceptible sign of recognition, and in the same quiet manner

telegraph an entreaty to be allow to join she; upon which Emily

frown and shake her head by way of prohibition, favour Gus

afterwards with a pensive smile, to show that her refusal proceed less

from choice than from necessity. almost as she do so Lord Bellefield

return, look annoyed and anxious. “I be oblige to leave you for

half-an-hour,” he say, “but you will be perfectly safe here, and I

shall return in plenty of time to escort you home. you may depend upon

my come to fetch you.” and almost before he finish speak he have

quit the box and be go.



confuse and half-frightened at his sudden departure, Annie remain for

a minute or two with her eye fix on the door through which he have

as it be vanish; when she again glance towards the stage the stall

lately occupy by Augustus Travers be vacant.









chapter XLI.--ANNIE GRANT FALLS into difficulty.



Lewis, accord to agreement, accompany frere to the

Palaeontological, and add to the circle of his acquaintance those

mysterious being, the “relation of the early Zoophytes.” when the

lecture be over, Frere, who have an order to admit two into the House

of Commons, take Lewis with he to hear the speaking. the debate prove

interesting: the Premier address the House at length; a well-know

satirist rise to reply to he, remark on various point in the speech

with much talent and more ill-nature, and the minister be again on

his leg to answer his opponent, when Lewis, glance at his watch,

discover to his annoyance that it be considerably past eleven; and

aware that General Grant have a particular objection to his servant

be keep up late, communicate this fact to his companion, and wish

he goodnight.



“what! cann’t you stay and hear ----------‘s answer?” be the reply, “and

then I’d come away, too.”



Lewis explain that the thing be impossible, and Frere continued--



“well, what must be, must, I suppose; and as my hearing----------‘s

reply be another inevitable necessity, I must e’en say good-night, so

_Schlaffen sie wohl_.”



Lewis grasp his proffered hand, and leave the gallery, start on

his homeward route. as he approach Charing Cross his attention be

attract by the restlessness of a magnificent horse, which, in a

well-appoint cab, be wait at the door of one of the house. as he

slacken his pace for a moment to ascertain whether the effort make

by a diminutive cab-groom to restrain the plunging of the fiery animal

would prove successful, the house door be fling open, and a gentleman,

apparently in headlong haste, spring down the step so recklessly that

he miss his footing, and would have fall have not Lewis catch he by

the arm in time to prevent it. as the person he have thus assist turn

to thank he, the reflection of the gaslight fall upon his face, and

Lewis recognise Lord Bellefield, though his feature be characterise

by a strange expression which Lewis have never observe in they before.

draw back, he bow coldly, and be about to pass on when Lord

Bellefield exclaimed--



“stay one moment, Mr. Arundel. I have be force to leave the

Opera-house suddenly: the Countess Portici and Miss Grant be in Lord

Ashford’s box, and I have promise to return to see they home, but be

quite unable to do so. you would oblige--that be, I be sure General

Grant would wish you----”



“will your lordship favour I with the loan of your pass-ticket?”

 interrupt Lewis shortly.



as Lord Bellefield comply with this request, Lewis remark that his

hand tremble to such a degree that he could scarcely grasp the ivory

ticket.



“you will tell the Countess that it be impossible for I to come to

they,” continue the young nobleman hurriedly; then pass his hand

across his eye, as if he be half bewilder, he spring into the cab,

and seize the rein, drive off at a furious pace in the direction of

Westminster Abbey.



Lewis gaze after he for a moment in surprise, then turn on his

heel, walk rapidly to the end of the haymarket, hope to reach the

theatre before the opera should be conclude. in this expectation he

be however disappointed, for when he gain the Opera Colonnade he

perceive, from the crush of carriage and the bustle and confusion

which be go on, that the opera be over. hastily push through the

crowd, he endeavour to find the box Lord Bellefield have indicate,

but to one as little acquainted as be Lewis with the intricacy of the

Opera-house this be no such easy matter; first, he run up considerably

too high; in his eagerness to retrieve this error he descend as much

too low; and even when he have attain the proper level he more than

once take a wrong turning. at length he catch a box-keeper, who, on

learn his difficulty, volunteer to conduct he to the box he be

in search of. Lo, and behold, when they reach the spot the door stand

open, and the box be tenantless!



in order to explain how this awkward and embarrassing result have be

bring about, we must beseech the reader’s patience while we resume the

break thread of our narrative where we relinquish it at the end of

the last chapter.



Scarcely have Lord Bellefield quit the box five minute when the

attendant open the door and Augustus Travers make his appearance. he

be very humble and courteous, and all he say to Emily with his

tongue might have be print in the “Times” the next morning without

affording matter for the most arrant gossip to prate about; but the

language speak by his eloquent blue eye be of a very different

character. he tell she vocally that he have be travel in the East

since they have last part; that he have be unwell, have feel restless

and unsettle; that he have find it impossible to remain contentedly in

any place, have become a citizen of the world, a wanderer over the face

of the globe; that he have only return to town during the last week,

and have no notion she have leave Italy--dear Italy!--and here his eye

say, “that country which your presence make a paradise to I,” just as

plainly as if his tongue have speak the word (in fact they say it more

plainly, for his tongue appear to consider it fashionable to speak

English with a slight lisp, which occasionally render his meaning

indistinct); “but when he see her”--continued his tongue--“he could not

resist come up to her box to learn whether she have quite forget all

her old friend;” and here his eye resume that his faith in she be

so strong that nothing, neither absence nor aught else, could in the

small degree shake it.



then Emily reply that she be always delighted to see any old friend,

but that she really be quite shocked to find he look so ill; which

observation she utter with particular tenderness, because, not be

aware that he have play French Hazard at a club in St. James’s Street

till five o’clock on the previous morning, she account for his pale

look by the romantic hypothesis that he be die for love of she. and

so they continue to converse in an undertone, apparently much to their

mutual satisfaction, while Annie, having bow coldly when she be

introduce to the fascinating Augustus, of whose presence there she

greatly disapprove, pretend altogether to ignore he, and to turn

her attention solely to the opera. and time run on, till, just as

the baritone singer be approach, with suicidal intention, the

(imitation) marble tomb suppose to contain the corpse of his tenor and

soprano victim, but which really be tenant by a live carpenter,

who, in a paper cap and flannel jacket, be wait till the fall of

the curtain should enable he to carry away the entire mausoleum, Annie,

look at her watch, perceive that it be past eleven, and glance

towards Emily, remind she in dumb show that Lord Bellefield might

be momentarily expect. this intelligence Emily, in a low tone,

communicate to her friend, who smile, to show his white tooth,

and reply that “Bellefield and he have meet at Baden, and have become

wonderful friend again;” despite which assurance Emily still urge

his departure, and he still linger on, till the opera come to an end

before Lord Bellefield make his appearance. be Saturday night, there

be no ballet, and the house begin to empty rapidly.



“what can possibly have become of your brother, Emily?” exclaim Annie,

who, dislike the whole situation most particularly, be fast lapsing

into that uncomfortable state of mind familiarly term “a fuss.”



“if you will allow I, I shall be delighted to see you to your

carriage,” insinuate Gus.



“thank you, but I be sure my brother will be here directly,” return

emily; “he would be extremely annoyed to find that we have go without

wait for he. pray do not let we detain you.”



but of course Gus would not go; “he should be wretched unless he know

they be in safety; he see they be anxious, he would ascertain

whether Lord Bellefield have return; there might perhaps be difficulty

in get up their carriage,” and so he leave the box, promise to

return instantly.



“what _be_ we to do, Emily, if Bellefield do not come?” exclaim

Annie, press her hand together much as the _primaa donna_ have do

when, some quarter of an hour since, she have ejaculate at the very

tip-top of her lofty voice, “_Addesso Morir!_”



“what be we to do, you silly child?” reply emily, laugh, “why,

walk downstairs, to be sure, and allow Gus to take care of we till we

can find the carriage. be not he handsome, poor fellow!”



before Annie could urge her dislike to this scheme, Travers return,

bring with he a tall, good-look boy, embarrass by a perpetual

consciousness of his extreme youth and his first tail-coat.



“I can see nothing of Lord Bellefield,” begin Gus; “it be evident

something must have occur to prevent his return. let I introduce

my brother Alfred,” he continue, address Emily; “he be a naughty

little boy in pinafore when you see he last--and now what will you do?

every one be go or go.”



“oh, wait a minute long; I’m sure he will come,” urge Annie.



“really we cannot,” return Emily. “we shall get shut up in the

opera-house all Sunday, if we don’t take care.”



“which would be indeed dreadfully wicked--a most terrific climax of

depravity,” simper Gus. “seriously,” he continue, “you must accept my

arm, though I be sorry the alternative should be so very disagreeable to

you.” these latter word he speak in such a tone that Emily alone could

hear they, for which he obtain a reproachful, tender, and upbraid

glance, with a view to which reward he have probably utter they.



“Come, Annie, we positively must go,” exclaim her cousin impatiently.



“Alfred, why don’t you offer Miss Grant your arm?” chime in Gus,

draw Emily’s within his own. thus urge, poor Annie, sorely against

her will, accept Alfred’s tremble arm and quit the box; Emily

and Augustus Travers follow. as they descend the stair a slight

confusion occur: an irish gentleman have lose his hat, and want to

return to look for it, a measure against which a stout old lady, to

whom he be act as escort, vehemently protest, while an obsequious

box-keeper be vainly endeavour to understand the locality in which

the embarrassed Hibernian imagine he have leave the miss article.

while Annie and her juvenile protector be manoeuvre to get past this

group, Augustus Travers pause, say in a low tone to his companion,

“let they precede we; I _must_ speak two word to you in private, and if

I lose this opportunity I may never have another. emily, if you value my

peace of mind, I entreat you do not refuse.”



a large party, compose chiefly of young man, be descend at the

moment, so that Emily’s reply be inaudible, but when, having get in

some degree clear of the confusion, Annie look back for her chaperone,

Travers and the Countess be nowhere to be see. horrify at this

discovery, Annie stop abruptly, exclaim, “oh, we have miss Mr.

Travers and my cousin! we have well turn back.”



the boy glance quickly round, and as he perceive the truth of her

assertion a meaning smile pass across his feature. all trace of it

have, however, vanish ere he reply, “they must have turn down the

other staircase, but it will bring they out at the same place as

this would have do; we shall meet they at the bottom.” then, as his

companion still hesitate, he continue, “I can assure you it be so; we

should only lose they if we be to return.”



half convince by this argument, and completely frighten by the party

of young man, who, talk and laughing, be rapidly follow they,

Annie suffer herself to be hurry on by her companion till she

reach the foot of the staircase; here she pause and look anxiously

around for her cousin and Travers--they be nowhere to be see.

annoyed, distressed, and frighten, she turn to her companion,

exclaim, “they be not here, you see. what _be_ we to do?”



“wait, I suppose,” return the boy, who seem puzzled and vex. “this

be a nice trick of Master Gus’s,” he continue in a half soliloquy; “he

ought at least to have give I a hint what to do.”



before Annie could inquire what he imagine his brother’s intention

might be, a fresh incident divert, and, from its disagreeable nature,

soon wholly engross her attention. the crush-room, as it be call,

where she be now stand, be occupy almost entirely by man, who,

break up into party of four or five, be pace up and down, wait

for their friend to join they, or stand in group, canvass the

various merit and demerit of the different performer. to one or two

of these coterie Annie soon become an object of especial notice.



“do you see that girl?” whisper a pert youth with light curl and a

turn-down collar. “ben’t she a regular stunner, eh?”



“Ya’as, dev’lish pwitty, ra-ally,” drawl a moustachiod puppy, stare

through an eyeglass at the object of his admiration. “aw--I wonder who

she possibly ca-an be. I actually don’t know har.”



“I suppose she’s stand there to be look at,” return the first

speaker. “her juvenile gallant cann’t get she along at any price, it

seem.”



“Ra’ally, it be almost worth while to relieve he of his charge,”

 drawl moustachio. “he seem particularly incompetent to fill it,

not--aw--equal to the situation--ha! ha!”



“why don’t you volunteer, Spooner, if you think so?” urge a third

speaker.



“Na-o, I don’t do that sort of thing--I’m--aw--quite a reform

character,” be the reply; “but if you wa-ant a leader for such a

forlorn hope--aw--here come your man.”



as he speak, a tall, distinguished-looking individual, with much

watch-chain and more whisker, who look forty, but might be a year

or two young, lounge up to the group, and show his tooth with

a repulsive smile, inquire, “what be you young reprobate grin

about, eh?”



“we be only say it be a pity that young lady have not a more

efficient protector, and advise Spooner to volunteer, Sir Gilbert,”

 be the reply.



“who be the individual?” inquire the last comer, screw a glass

into the corner of his eye. a moment’s inspection serve to elucidate

the mystery; and remove the glass with a contemptuous smile, he

add, “the boy be little Alfred Travers, who have just leave Eton; he’s

evidently wait for his brother, who, I’ve a notion, have more string

than one to his bow to-night; as for the damsel, _noscitur a sociis_.

we’ll play the fascinating Gus a trick for once in his life. come with

I, Forester; I may want you to bully the boy.” then turn on his

heel, he advance towards Annie, and salute she with a low bow,

began--



“this be a most unexpected pleasure! I have no idea you be here

to-night; where have you hide yourself this age?” then perceive

that, confuse by his address, and uncertain whether he might not be

some acquaintance whose feature she have fail to recognise, the young

lady be completely at a loss how to reply, he continue, “I see that

you have be cruel enough to forget I; while I, on the contrary, have

carry your lovely image in my heart, and time have fail to efface

even the shadow of a charm. but let I be of use to you. have you a

carriage here, or will you allow I to place mine at your disposal?

the house be become desert--let I escort you. stand aside, young

gentleman,” and as he speak he advance towards she, offer his arm.



but Annie, having recover from her first surprise, feel convince

that the person address she be a total stranger, and draw back in

alarm, she say to her companion in a hurried whisper--



“indeed, I do not know that gentleman--there must be some mistake--pray

let we get away.”



thus urge, the boy draw up his slight figure to its full height, and

turn to the individual in question, say haughtily--



“you be mistaken, sir; I must trouble you to allow we to pass.”



“it be you who mistake jest for earnest, my good boy,” be the

contemptuous reply; “the lady and I be old friend; she be merely

try to tease I by pretend to have forget I. this gentleman”

 (and he glance at his companion) “will explain the matter to you.” then

again offer his arm to Annie, he continue, “really, if you persist

in your silly joke we shall have the carriage drive off.” confuse by

his pertinacity, Alfred Travers glance at his trembling companion, and

read the truth in the terrified expression of her face, his boyish

chivalry take fire, and anxious to vindicate his title to be consider

a man, he exclaim, angrily--



“stand back, sir, and let we pass; do you mean to insult the lady?”



the person he address, Sir Gilbert Vivian, be a _roué_ Baronet who,

having be a man about town for the last sixteen year, and have long

since lose all the good character he have ever possess, and acquire a

reputation of a diametrically opposite tendency, be scarcely a person

to stick at trifle, laugh as he replied--



“do you hear that, Forester? this good youth accuse you of insult

the young lady--hadn’t you well give he a lesson in civility?”



as he speak he make a significant gesture, which the other respond to

by exclaiming--



“Insult the lady! what do you mean, you young cub, eh?” and grasp he

by the arm, he twist he roughly round, thereby separate he from

Annie.



“take that, and find out,” be the thoroughly school-boy answer, as,

bound forward, the ex-etonian administer to his antagonist a

ring box on the ear.



this, save that the blow be more skilfully apply and rather

hard than he have calculate upon, be just the result Forester have

anticipate. seize the struggle boy by the collar, he declare he

would give he in custody for an assault, and, despite his resistance,

drag he from the spot in a pretend search after a policeman.

avail himself of the confusion, the Baronet place himself by Annie’s

side, and bend over she, said--



“it’s no use wait for the fascinating Augustus, I can assure you; he

have other game in view to-night, and cann’t come; so for once you must

allow I the honour of act as his deputy--’pon my word, you must,”

 and as he speak he attempt to take her arm and draw it within his own.



Poor Annie! distressed, confused, and frighten, the desertion, or

rather capture, of the boy, her only protector, have increase her alarm

twenty-fold, and now the renew persecution of the Baronet bring she

fear to a climax, and attempt to withdraw her hand from his grasp,

in a very agony of terror she exclaimed--



“oh! where _be_ emily? will nobody help I?” and burst into a flood of

tear.



at this moment a tall figure suddenly interpose between they, and the

Baronet’s wrist be seize with such a vice-like grasp that he utter

an exclamation of mingled rage and pain, and drop the little hand of

which he have unjustly possess himself as though it have be a red-hot

cinder; while Annie, utter a cry of delight, spring forward, and

clasp the arm of the new-comer, clung to it as some drown wretch

cling to the plank which shield he from the rushing water that

threaten his destruction.



Lewis, for he it be (as every reader above the unsuspecting age of four

and a half have of course ere this discover for himself), understand

at a glance the outline of the situation, and intuitively divine much

of what Annie must have go through, pity and sympathise with she so

deeply that the anger he would otherwise have feel against the man who

have insult she be completely conquer by the strong feeling

which absorb he, and his only thought be how good to soothe and

tranquillise the frightened girl who cling to he.



“do not alarm yourself,” he say kindly; “you have nothing more to fear.

I will not leave you for a moment till you be again at home and in

safety. lean on my arm, you tremble so that you can scarcely walk;” and

half lead, half support she, he draw she away from the scene

of her disaster, and pass through the crowd of loiterer whom the

scuffle between Forester and Alfred Travers have attract to the spot,

conduct she towards the near exit.



so quietly and suddenly have all this take place, that ere Sir Gilbert

Vivian have leave off rub his wrist, or thoroughly realise the sudden

frustration of his scheme, the object of his insolent attention be

almost out of sight. irritated at his failure, and urge on by the

scarcely suppress laughter of those who have witness his defeat, he

mutter an oath, and turn on his heel, follow hastily in the track

of Annie and her deliverer. come up with they just as they reach

the entrance lead into the colonnade, he tap Lewis smartly on the

shoulder, say angrily--



“a word with you, sir, if you please. I wish to ask what you mean by

your impertinent interference. who the d-----l be you, I should like to

know?”



a flush of anger pass across Lewis’s brow, and he be about to make

a reply which would scarcely have tend to bring the matter to an

amicable conclusion, when an almost convulsive pressure of the arm on

which Annie hang recall his self-control, and draw himself up with

a stern dignity which bespoke an apt pupil in the school of General

Grant, he fix his pierce eye upon the Baronet as he answer, “you

have already, sir, act probably under some _mistake_” (and he lay a

strong emphasis upon the last word), “subject this lady to an amount

of fright and annoyance which should secure the forbearance of any

one move in the society of gentleman. should you wish to call and

apologise to her father for your share in this unlucky adventure, I

shall be happy to explain to you in his presence the part I have take

in the affair. there be my address,” and without wait further parley

Lewis hand he his card, and draw Annie gently forward, pass on.

as they reach the entrance, a gentleman come hastily the other way,

nearly run against they. look up, Annie perceive it to be Augustus

Travers, who, recognise she, exclaim, “I have leave the Countess

Portici in the carriage, and be return to seek for you, Miss Grant.

she be much alarmed at having miss you.” the only reply Annie make

to this speech be by a slight inclination of the head, and press

hastily forward, she pass on. as Lewis assist she into the carriage,

she, for the first time, speak. “you will come with we,” she say

eagerly; “remember you have promise not to leave I.” then catch

sight of Augustus Travers, who have follow they, a new idea strike

she, and she continue, “tell that gentleman I be afraid his brother have

become involved in some difficulty on my account; he have well go back

and seek for he.” Lewis repeat her message and then spring into the

carriage, which instantly drive off, leave the discomfited dandy to

accomplish his mission as good he might.









CHAPTER XLII.--A TÊTE-À-TÊTE, and a tragedy.



a party more silent than the trio occupy General Grant’s carriage

never drive from the door of her Majesty’s theatre. Annie, delight to

find herself once again in safety, leant back amidst cloak and

cushion to recover as good she might the effect of the terror she

have undergo. somewhat to her surprise and displeasure, Emily, without

utter a word by way either of explanation or condolence, also throw

herself back among the cushion, and arrange a fold of her mantle

so as to conceal her face, appear unconscious of the presence of her

companion. to this silent system they scrupulously adhere till they

reach Conduit Street, when Emily exclaim in a quick, eager tone

of voice, “where be they go? tell he to drive to Berkeley Square

directly.”



Lewis, to whom this speech be address, let down the window and give

the coachman the requisite order, and in less than five minute the

carriage stop at the house occupy for the season by the Countess

Portici. the servant let down the step, and Lewis spring out,

assist the Countess to alight; as she do so she turn her head, and

say hurriedly, “Annie, I shall see you tomorrow,” enter the house,

and the door close after she. Lewis resume his place, and the carriage

drive away.



“I think she be very unkind not to have say she be sorry for having

miss I, and I’ll never go out with she again,” observe Annie

petulantly. “and Lord Bellefield, too,” she continue--for she have by

this time reach that stage of recovery when, trace back her alarm

to its first cause, it become a relief to she to pour forth her wrong,

and in Lewis she feel sure of a prudent and sympathise auditor--“it be

all his fault for desert we in such a shameful way.”



“you be not perhaps aware that, meet I accidentally, his lordship

despatch I to you as his substitute,” return Lewis.



“do he intend then to have come back himself, if you have be unable to

act as his deputy?” inquire Annie quickly.



“he tell I it be impossible for he to do so,” be Lewis’s reply.



“then if he have not happen to meet you by mere chance, he would have

leave we to find our way to the carriage as good we could. how shameful!

just imagine what would have become of I if you have not arrive when

you did?--that dreadful man!--I believe I should have die of fright.”

 she pause, then add, in her usual gentle, win voice, “I must

again plague you with my thank, Mr. Arundel; you be fate always to

render I service for which I be unable to make you any return; except

by my sincere friendship,” she continue timidly.



“and that be a reward for which a man might------” begin Lewis

passionately. he be go to add, “gladly die,” but he check himself

abruptly, and if Annie could at that moment have see his face, she

would have be scare at the expression of despair by which it be

characterise, an expression change instantly to a look of the

stern resolution, as he continue, in a calm, grave voice, “I mean

that your uniform kindness and consideration have overpay any trifling

service I may have be fortunate enough to render you.”



“do Lord Bellefield give any reason for be unable to return to we?”

 inquire Annie after a pause. Lewis reply in the negative, and Annie

resume, “papa will be wait for we--he never go to bed till I come

and pray make he understand clearly how much my cousin be to blame in

the matter.”



“of course, if General Grant question I I must tell he exactly what I

have do and why I do it,” return Lewis gravely; “but--may I indeed

use the privilege of a friend, and venture for once to advise you?”



“oh yes, pray do,” rejoin Annie eagerly; “I shall be so much oblige

to you. I dare say I be go to do something very foolish.”



“from my acquaintance with your father’s high and chivalrous character,”

 continue Lewis, “I feel sure that the fact with which I must make he

acquaint will incense he greatly against Lord Bellefield, and as the

General be, both from temperament and education, a man of action, his

resentment be almost certain to lead to some practical result. now

just at present you be naturally and justly angry with your cousin; but

young lady’ anger be seldom of a very vindictive description, your

least of all so, and when, after frown he into penitence, you have

graciously forgive he, will not a serious rupture with the General be

Bellefield? all that I would recommend,” continue Lewis, see that

Annie bend down her head and make no reply, “would be, not what the

lawyer term _suppressio veri_--I would not for the world have you

conceal anything; but much depend upon the spirit in which a tale be

tell, and I be anxious to save you from the subsequent regret which

yield to a momentary impulse of anger may cost you.”



“tell I plainly what it be you think my father would do?” inquire

Annie abruptly.



“I think--pardon I if I speak too freely--I think the General would

resolve to break off the engagement which Mr. Leicester long since

inform I exist between yourself and Lord Bellefield; and it be to

save you the pain such a resolve might cost you that I venture to offer

you my advice.”



“you be mistaken,” reply his companion hurriedly; “such an

arrangement as that to which you refer may have be, perhaps still

be, contemplate; but the idea have always be distasteful to I, and

anything which would preclude the possibility of further reference to

it would be to I a subject of rejoice rather than of regret you may

think it strange in I to speak thus openly to you; but I be sure my

confidence be not misplace, and--and I be most anxious my father should

understand clearly the insult (for I consider it no less) my cousin have

to-night offer I.”



whether the information thus communicate be a source of pain or

pleasure to her auditor, we must leave the reader to conjecture for

himself, as when Lewis next speak his manner be calm and grave as ever.



“there be one possibility,” he say, “of which you must not entirely

lose sight: there may have be some urgent necessity for Lord

Belle-field’s presence elsewhere--some sufficient reason for his

apparent neglect, which he will only have to mention in order alike to

disarm your indignation and that of General Grant.”



“really, my cousin appear to have secure a most able advocate,”

 return Annie, with the slight possible shade of annoyance

perceivable in her tone. “I be scarcely prepared to find you so zealous

in his cause.”



Lewis’s face grow dark as he reply in a low, earnest voice, “while I

live, Lord Bellefield shall always meet with the strict justice at my

hand! justice!” he continue bitterly, “it be a god-like principle, and

sculptor have symbolise it well--the blinded brow, to show the stern

singleness of heart; the scale, to weigh the merit of the case; and

the keen sword, the agent of a sudden and full retribution.”



he speak in a tone of such deep and concentrated feeling, that Annie, as

she listen to his word, tremble involuntarily. with the keenness of

a woman’s instinct she appreciate the intensity of the feeling and the

power of the will that be, for the time, able to control it. for the

time!--in that phrase lie the secret of her prescient, terror.



Lewis be too much engross by the strength of his own emotion to

perceive the alarm he have excite; nor be it till they reach the

corner of Park Crescent that he again spoke--



“how do you contrive to become separate from the Countess Portici?”

 he inquire. “you be absolutely alone amongst those people--be you

not?--when I come up.”



Scarcely have Annie inform he of the circumstance which lead to her

desertion when the carriage stop.



“the General wish to see you before you retire for the night, Miss

Grant,” insinuate the aristocratic butler, as, lean on Lewis’s arm,

Annie enter the paternal mansion.



“where be my father?” she inquire hastily--“in the library?” receive

an affirmative answer, she continue, turn to Lewis: “you must come

with I; remember your promise!--I by no mean consider myself safe till

this interview be over.”



Lewis smile assent; his unnatural stiffness of manner seem to have

disappear like magic the moment their _tête-à-tête_ be over, and

Annie again restore to the protection of her own home.



the General appear in high good humour. “you be late, you dissipate

puss!” he say as Annie enter. “ah! Mr. Arundel,” he continue, “I do

not know you have be of the party. what have you do with Emily and

Bellefield, Annie?”



“Emily be safely at home,” be the reply; “she would not come far

than Berkeley Square. as to my cousin Bellefield, he must answer for

himself, if he be not irrecoverably lose; he choose to leave we to take

care of ourselves. we have have an adventure, and I should have die of

fright if Mr. Arundel have not come to my assistance like one of the good

genii in the ‘Arabian Nights’ entertainment.’ but I must go to bed, or

Aunt Martha will be implacable; she always examine Lisette on oath as

to the precise moment at which she finally leave my room. Mr. Arundel

will tell you the whole history much well than I can--so good-night!”

 and cast a glance, half arch, half implore, but wholly

irresistible, at Lewis, she glide out of the apartment, and be go

ere the General have sufficiently “come at” the meaning of her speech to

attempt to detain she.



fix his eye on Lewis with a look of sublime perplexity, which

border closely on the ludicrous, he exclaim, “pray, what be the

meaning of all this, Mr. Arundel? can you explain to what my daughter

allude?”



thus call upon, Lewis be force to narrate the adventure of the

evening, with the detail of which the reader have be already make

acquaint.



the General hear he attentively, though his brow grow dark as he

proceed. he listen in silence, however, till Lewis begin to describe

the scene in the crush-room at the Opera-house, when he become so much

excited that he spring from his seat and begin pace the apartment with

impatient stride. at the mention of Sir Gilbert Vivian’s impertinent

behaviour he exclaimed--



“a scoundrel! I remember when he be break upon parade for insolence to

his command officer. I hope you knock he down, sir!”



“I felt strangely tempt to do so,” reply Lewis, “but he have several

of his friend with he, so that I should have be certain to get into

a disagreeable squabble; and in that case what would have become of Miss

Grant?”



“very true, sir, very true,” return the General hastily; “next to

courage, coolness in action be the great attribute in a soldier--that

be to say, in a gentleman--and I honour your forbearance for such a

cause. shake hand, sir!” and suit the action to the word, General

Grant cross the room, and seize Lewis by the hand, shake it warmly.



at this unusual display of feel Lewis’s pale cheek flushed, and he

continue his narration to the point when he hand Sir Gilbert Vivian

his card. here he pause, and continue in an embarrassed tone of voice:

“I dare say he will take no notice of this--but if he should--of course

I be aware that the affair must be leave entirely in your hand, and that

it be Lord Bellefield’s privilege to--to defend--that be, to chastise

any insult offer to Miss Grant; but as you have so kindly signify

your approval of my conduct in the affair hitherto--if you could reward

I by allow I to go out with this scoundrel----?”



this be a request so thoroughly after the General’s own heart, that,

as he listen to it, his little, bright eye dance and sparkle with

satisfaction, which he have much difficulty not to express in word; but

his moral obligation, as a disciplinarian and the father of a

family, come across he, and he reply: “duel be a practice alike

subversive of military discipline, and contrary to the dictate of

religion; it be one, therefore, against which I have always--that be,

for many year past--feel oblige to set my face. until Lord Bellefield

shall have afford I some perfectly satisfactory explanation of his

extraordinary conduct, his intercourse with this household must entirely

cease; a man who could thus neglect his trust be the last person to whom

I should dream of commit the honour of--ahem!--my family. as to this

Sir Gilbert Vivian, from what I have hear of he, he be beneath the

notice of a gentleman--quite a contemptible character; the fact of his

annoying my daughter prove this. if it be not so, I vow to Heaven I’d

have the fellow out myself on Monday morning.” and finish with this

consistent remark his tirade against duelling, the General resume his

peripatetic exercise, much to the detriment of the library carpet.



when Lewis have complete his recital, his auditor again “take the

chair,” and lean his head on his hand, remain ponder the matter

for some minute in silence. at length he say, “do Lord Bellefield

give you any possible clue to the reason why he could not return to the

Opera-house?”



“he say nothing, sir, to throw any light upon the matter; but when

I accidentally meet he, as I have already mention, he appear much

agitated, his feature be unusually pale, and characterise by an

expression--I should almost say of horror.”



“have you any knowledge of the house he be leave? why do you

hesitate?”



“I will tell you frankly, General Grant,” return Lewis, draw

himself up and meet the General’s scrutinise glance with a clear,

steadfast gaze. “for some time past Lord Bellefield and I have not be

on good term together. since I have live beneath your roof he be the

only person who have treat I ungenerously, or cause I to feel the

full bitterness of my dependent situation. respect for you, and a sense

of my own position, have prevent my resent his lordship’s conduct

as under other circumstance I might have do, but enough have pass

between we to prove that we regard each other with no very friendly

feeling.”



“I be not at all aware of this--you should have tell I this soon,

Mr. Arundel. I allow no one to be treat discourteously in my house,”

 interrupt the General hastily.



“I should not have mention the fact now, sir,” reply Lewis calmly,

“have I not be anxious to explain to you why it be in the high

degree repugnant to I to be force by circumstance to appear as Lord

Bellefield’s accuser, and thus lie myself open to the suspicion of be

actuate by malicious motive.”



“no one who know you would imagine that, sir,” return the General;

“but the truth should always be speak regardless of consequence, and

you must yourself perceive how important it be that I should form a just

estimate of Lord Bellefield’s conduct in this affair.”



Lewis pause a moment in reflection, and then reply, “the part I have

take in this business be none of my own seeking, nor do I see that I

be bind by any obligation of honour to withhold from you the only other

fact of which I be aware in regard to the matter. I do happen to know

the character of the house which Lord Bellefield be leave, for as

I walk down to the Palaeontological Society this afternoon with my

friend Richard Frere, he point it out to I as a gaming-house of some

notoriety.”



the expression of the General’s face, when he become aware of this

uncomfortable little fact, grow so stern, that a distressed artist,

wish to paint some roman father sacrifice his son, would have give

all the small change he might have happen to have about he at

the time for one glimpse of that inflexible countenance. Suggestive,

however, of evil as be this circumstance, the whole affair appeared

wrap in such a veil of mystery that neither General Grant nor Lewis

could, as they that night lie awake revolve the matter in their

anxious mind, arrive at any satisfactory hypothesis by which to account

for Lord Bellefield’s extraordinary behaviour. the follow paragraph,

which appear in several of the Sunday paper, and be recopy in the

“Morning Post” of Monday, be the first thing that tend to enlighten

they; it be head “appalling suicide.



“as our column be go to press we receive intelligence of one of

the most awful catastrophe which it have ever be our melancholy duty

to record; we refer to the untimely decease of Captain Mellerton,

of the----th foot, who perish by his own hand in a notorious

gambling-house not far from Charing Cross. as far as we have be able

to ascertain the fact of the case, the unfortunate young gentleman, who

be adjutant of the----th, lose a considerable sum of money (it be say

£12,000) to Lord B--f--d, a nobleman of sporting notoriety, at the

first Newmarket meeting. be unable to meet so large a call upon his

finance, he be induce in an evil hour to speculate with some of the

regimental money commit to his charge, intend to replace it by the

sale of an estate in Yorkshire; and having thus satisfy the demand of

his noble creditor, he be on Saturday last unexpectedly call upon

to send in his regimental account. in this extremity we have hear

it rumour that he be induce to apply to Lord B--f--d, as the only

person on whom he have the slight claim; but if we have not be

misinformed, the appeal be vain, and urge to desperation by this

failure of his last hope, the unfortunate young man repair to the

gaming-house in which the rash act be commit, play deeply, and

when fortune again declare against he, draw a loaded pistol from his

breast, and before the bystander be aware of his design, terminate

his existence by blow out his brain. Captain Mellerton be the

eld son of the Honourable H. Mellerton, of Harrowby Park, Beds., and

be shortly to be married to Miss A------ D----------, daughter of Sir

---------- D----------, the wedding-day be fix immediately after

the commencement of the recess.”









CHAPTER XLIII.--WHEREIN FAUST “set up” for a GENTLEMAN, and TAKES a

course of serious reading.



when General Grant have peruse the “Morning Post,” contain the

paragraph with which the last chapter conclude, he leave the remainder

of his breakfast untasted, and hasten to the library, write the

follow letter:--



“my Lord,--On learn from my daughter the uncourteous, have almost

write ungentlemanly, manner in which you neglect her safety on

Saturday evening, I be naturally much incense. a paragraph referring

to you in the ‘post’ of this morning afford a sufficient clue to the

cause of your absence from the Opera-house, but unfortunately do so by

cast upon you an imputation which (unless you can explain the affair

to my entire satisfaction, which I confess appear to I improbable)

must necessarily break off all intercourse between we. I be aware that

your conduct may not have exceed the limit which the world term

honourable, but I do not regulate my opinion by the world’s standard,

and should consider that I be indeed neglect my duty as a father

be I to entrust my daughter’s happiness to a gamester whose success

have involve the ruin and self-murder of a fellow-creature. these may

sound harsh term, but unless you can disprove that they be true one,

I for the last time sign myself, your faithfully,



“Archibald Grant.”



having relieve his mind by pen the above epistle, he despatch

a mount groom to convey it to its destination, and having see he

depart, shut himself up, in solitary dignity, to await an answer. in

less time than could have be imagine the groom return bear the

follow missive:--



“Lord Bellefield present his compliment to General Grant, and having

peruse his strangely offensive letter, beg to decline afford any

explanation whatsoever of the conduct of which General Grant see fit

to disapprove. Lord Bellefield agree in think that under these

circumstance all intercourse between himself and General Grant’s family

have well cease.”



while the General sit in his library ponder over this agreeable

epistle with a rueful countenance, to which anger, vexation, and

outraged dignity impart a singularly undesirable expression, an

eager and exciting conversation be be carry on in a pretty little

apartment opening into a miniature conservatory, dedicate to the use

of Annie Grant. Emily have arrive, all her own natural, fascinating,

impulsive, silly little self again, and have pooh-poohe any attempt at

coolness on Annie’s part by throw her arm round her neck and kiss

she a very unnecessary number of time, under the plea of she be “a

dear, ill-use thing that _must_ be pet.” and having thus at one

and the same time expiate her offence and relieve her feeling, she

dance across the room, bolt the door, draw a heavy damask curtain

over it, and exclaim, “now we’re snug,” dance back again, and

fling herself into an easy-chair, began--



“oh, my dear Annie! I be so miserable, so utterly wretched, I must go

back to Italy; I’ve write to Alessandro to come and fetch I directly.

I shall never be happy again--at least not till I’ve quite forget it

all--and that will be never.” and here come out a little lace parody of

a pocket-handkerchief, which, although by no mean a desirable article

wherewith to face a violent cold in the head, or at all calculate to

withstand so much as an average sneeze, yet suffice to dry the ghost of

the tear which Emily’s deep wretchedness draw from she.



“my dear Emily, what _be_ the matter?” return Annie, alarm by a

thousand vague fear, though, not having see _the_ paragraph, she

be as yet unconscious of the dark cloud that obscure the family

horizon.



“oh, my love, I suppose I ought not to tell you anything about it, but

I must, for I’ve no one else to confide in. that wretch Gus!--would you

believe it? he actually want I to leave poor dear Alessandro, and

to run away with he;” and then with many ejaculation, and much

flourish of the homoeopathic sized handkerchief, she go on to

relate how, when she became separate from Annie at the Opera-house,

“which be all that creature Gus’s fault, and do on purpose,” she be

certain, the “creature” have avail himself of the opportunity he have

thus secure to urge his undying attachment to she, which affection,

despite its inherent principle of vitality, he declare would assuredly

bring he to an early grave in the event of her obduracy continue;

but emily, though positively a flirt, and negatively rather a goose than

otherwise, be not unprincipled, and so when she have overcome her first

impulse of surprise and mortification, all the virtuous wife arise

within she, and she give Gus to understand, by dint of sundry short,

sharp, and decisive plain-speak unpleasantness, that he have make a

false move and ruin his game. thence lapse abruptly into a fit of

sulky dignity, she order he, with the voice and gesture of a tragedy

queen, to lead she to her carriage, finally despatch the foil

“_Lionne_” hunter to remedy one of his ill deed by find Annie, on

which mission he depart in a state of mind the reverse of seraphic.

having conclude this historical episode, _la Contessa_ proceed to

append thereunto certain annotation and reflection, in the course of

which she contrive to fix much blame on society in general, and on Gus

and Alessandro in particular, but none whatsoever on her own flirting

manner and inordinate love of attention, which self-delude analysis

be by no mean an original feature in the case, but rather an

unconscious imitation of the proceeding of many a deep thinker than

poor little Emily.



the conference between the girl be still at its height when a summon

for Annie from her father interrupt the proceeding; whereupon emily,

declare that neither her health nor spirit be then capable of

undergo the pain _forte et dure_ of an interview with Aunt Martha,

drive home again, to fortify her principle and console her breaking

heart with a volume of George Sand’s last novel. the General be in a

great state of virtuous indignation. Lord Bellefield’s note have be as

gunpowder sprinkle over the smoulder ember of his wrath, and when

Annie arrive they (or, to translate the metaphor _slang-icè_, he)

“flare up” to an immense extent. he tell she of all the enormity

which the newspaper attribute to her cousin, and signify his belief

that the case have be rather understate than otherwise; he inform

she of Lewis’s _rencontre_ with the delinquent at the door of a

gaming-house; he adduce the note which he have just receive as a proof

that its writer must be lose to all well feeling--utterly want in a

proper respect for age and position; and, in short, he say a great many

severe and unwise thing, after the fashion of angry man in general, for

which he be afterwards very sorry, find such speech easy to

say than to unsay--which result be also by no mean uncommon in similar

case.



having relieve his feeling by this explosion, he proceed to the more

serious business of the interview by inform she that the necessary

consequence of these uncomfortable revelation must be the dissolution

of all tie, present or prospective, between herself and Lord

Bellefield, which autocratic act he perform with outward austerity

and inward trepidation, as he fully expect Annie to receive the harsh

decree with a violent burst of tear, and, man-like, there be nothing

he dread so much--he would rather have face a charge of cavalry any

day. but to his surprise Annie sustain the information with a degree

of stoical self-control that be perfectly marvellous. she neither weep,

sigh, nor attempt the hysteric line; she only say gravely, “it’s

all very sad and shocking; but of course, dear papa, I be ready to

agree to whatever you think well.” the General rub his hand--there

be a daughter for you! not a word of opposition--to hear be to obey;

it actually restore he to good humour. he talk to she kindly and

sensibly for a quarter of an hour, and then go out and purchase

for she a valuable diamond bracelet, which be his idea of rewarding

self-sacrifice in woman. and so do Annie, involuntarily and

unconsciously, gain high praise and honour for submit with

resignation to a decree which afford her unmitigated satisfaction.

as she leave the library she encounter poor Walter, who appear in

unusually high spirit. next to Lewis, Annie hold the foremost place in

Walter’s affection, from the unvarying patience and kindness with which

she treat he. moreover, having fail to inspire he with the degree

of respect not unmingled with awe with which he be accustom to regard

his tutor, he look upon she in the light of a companion and an equal,

to whom it might be safe to confide certain mischievous performance in

which, as his spirit acquire more elasticity, and his mental power

begin to develop, he see fit from time to time to indulge. with some

such intention do he now approach she, whisper as he draw near, “I

want you, Annie; I want you to come with I and see Faust dress like a

gentleman.”



“see what, you silly boy?” return Annie, laugh.



“come with I and you shall see,” be the rhythmical and oracular

response; and seize she by the hand, he drag she off in the

direction of the sitting-room appropriate to his own use and that of

his tutor.



“be Mr. Arundel there?” inquire Annie, pause when she discover

their destination.



“no, he’s not att home; there’s no other gentleman there except Mr.

Faust,” be the reply; and thus reassure, Annie comply with the boy’s

whim, and allow he to carry she off unopposed. now, since we have

have any especial intercourse with that worthy dog, Faust’s education

have progress rapidly as well as Walter’s. Lewis, partly from want

of occupation during the many weary hour his attendance on Walter

necessitate, partly because by so do he be enable to excite and

interest the feeble intellect of his poor charge, have avail himself of

the unusual power of control he have acquire over the dog to teach

he sundry trick somewhat more difficult to perform than the ordinary

routine of canine accomplishment; for instance, having perfect he in

sit on his hind leg in the attitude popularly suppose to represent

the act of beg, he go on to teach he to sit thus perch up in

a corner for a space of time gradually increase, as by practice the

animal’s muscle acquire more rigidity, until at length it be

no uncommon feat for he to remain in this attitude for an hour at

literally a “sitting.” moreover, if a light book or pamphlet be place

on his forepaw, he would support it, and remain gaze on the open page

before he with a solemn gravity of countenance, indicate, apparently,

the deep interest in the work he seem to be peruse. of the

result of this educational course Walter have on the present occasion

avail himself; and accordingly, Annie, on her introduction to the

study, find the excellent dog seat on his hind leg in a corner, with

an extempore mantle form of a red scarf drooping gracefully from his

shoulder, and an old cap of Walter’s on his head. thus attire, he

appear to be con, with an expression of puzzled diligence, a tract

against profane swearing by Mrs. Hannah More, present to Walter by

Miss Livingstone on the occasion of his inadvertently make use in her

presence of the scandalous expression, “bless my heart!” Annie, duly

impress by this spectacle, laugh even more than Walter have hope

for, and tell Faust that he be much the good dog in the world, in which

assertion she be not, as we think, guilty of any great exaggeration.

and Faust, take the compliment to himself only when the occurrence

of his name render the allusion unmistakably personal, slobber

affectionately with his great comic mouth, and wink with his foolish,

love eye, and make abortive attempt to wag his ridiculous friendly

tail, which be crumple up un-wag-ably in the corner, and in the

plenitude of his excellence sit more erect than ever, and study his

profane swearing still more diligently.



as soon as Walter’s delight at Annie’s amusement have in some degree

subside, he turn to she, saying--



“but, Annie, you have not find out why! tell you Faust look like a

gentleman.”



“oh! because he sit there read his book with such an air ol

dignified composure, I suppose,” be the reply.



“no; I’d a well reason than that,” return Walter, with a look of

unusual sagacity.



“well, then, you must tell I what it be, for I cann’t guess,” observe

Annie good-naturedly.



“look again, and find out,” rejoin Walter.



thus urge, Annie examine the dog more attentively than she have do

before, and discover that round his neck be sling the identical gold

watch and chain which, at her suggestion, Charles Leicester and his wife

have give to Lewis.



“why, you’ve hang Mr. Arundel’s watch round Faust’s neck! oh, Walter,

how foolish of you; he might have throw it down and break it!”

 exclaim Annie, aghast at her discovery.



“yes, that’s it,” return Walter, chuckle with delight at the success

of his puerile attempt at a trick. “all gentleman wear gold watch, you

know, and so do Mr. Faust.”



“you ought not to have put it on he; I’m sure Mr. Arundel will be

very angry,” resume Annie; and kneel down by the dog, she begin

untwist the chain from his neck. “sit still, Faust; be quiet, sir,”

 she continue, as Faust, in his affection, attempt to take an unfair

advantage of the situation to lick her hand and face, in which act of

impertinence Walter sedulously encourage he; still Annie persevere,

and at length succeed in disengage the chain and rescue the watch

from its dangerous position. “there,” she exclaim, “I have remedie

the effect of your mischief, Master Walter; but I should never have

be able to accomplish it if Faust have not be the well behave,

dear old dog in the world;” and with an impulse of girlish

playfulness she throw her arm round the animal and press his rough

head against her shoulder, her soft auburn ringlet fall like a

shower of gold upon his shaggy coat.



at this moment, Lewis, who have be to talk over his Saturday evening’s

adventure with Frere (or, at least, such portion of they as he choose

to reveal, for on some subject he be strangely reserve, even with

Frere), return, and find the door ajar, enter noiselessly,

and stand transfix by the sight of the _tableau vivant_ we have

endeavour to describe. he think that he have never behold anything

so lovely in his life before, nor be he far wrong. the time that have

elapse since we first introduce Annie Grant to the reader have alter

only to improve her beauty; her figure have gain a certain roundness of

outline, and her face acquire a depth of expression, which have be the

only finish touch want to complete one of those rare specimen

of loveliness on which we gaze with a speculative wonder as to why so

much beauty should be, as it be, waste on this world of change, and

sin, and sorrow, and not reserve for that “Petter Land,”



                   “where all lovely thing and fair



                        Pass not away.”



whether idea at all analogous to these present themselves to the mind

of Lewis, we be unable to say; certain it be, however, that (his

artist eye attract by the picture before he) he stand gaze as one

entrance, while his colour go and come, and his broad chest heave

with the intensity of his emotion. how long affair might have remain

in this position it be impossible to decide, have not Faust, become

aware of his master’s presence by some mysterious canine instinct, make

an unceremonious attempt to free himself from Annie’s caress; and that

young lady, raise her eye, encounter those of Lewis fix upon she

with an expression which change in an instant from ardent admiration to

one of grave courtesy as he find that he be observe. Annie’s manner,

as she rise and come forward, afford but little clue whether or not

she have notice this change, and though her colour appear somewhat

heighten, no want of self-possession be discernible as she say,

hold up the watch--



“see what I have be rescue from the mischievous device of Master

Walter! he have actually hang my cousin Charles’ present to you round

Faust’s neck in order to make he look like a gentleman, as he declare.

Walter, come and answer for your misdeed; I intend Mr. Arundel to be

very angry with you--where be you, sir?” and as she speak she look

round for her companion, but whether really alarmed at the possibility

of be reprove for his mischief, or whether actuate by some

reasonless caprice of his half-develop intellect, Walter be nowhere

to be find; so Lewis, having thank Annie for her care of his watch,

politely hold open the door for she to depart. but when kidnap by

Walter, Annie have be carry be armful of book, and Lewis, become

aware of this fact, could do no less than offer to take they up to the

drawing-room for she. having accomplish this feat, he be about to

retire, when it occur to he that he be bind in common civility to

inquire whether she have sustain any ill effect from her alarm.



“oh, no,” reply Annie; “thank to your kindness and consideration, I

be literally _quitte pour la peur?_



“I suppose,” she add hesitatingly, “you have ere this learn the

sad cause of Lord Bellefield’s absence on Saturday night?” and on Lewis

replying in the affirmative, she continue, “and do you believe all

that the newspaper insinuate? can my cousin have really behave so very

wickedly?”



“I call on my friend Richard Frere this morning,” return Lewis,

“and I hear from he that the main fact of the case be matter of

notoriety; for instance, race man be well aware that Lord Bellefield

win a large sum of money from this unfortunate young man; nor would your

cousin attempt to deny that it be so. I be not sufficiently acquaint

with the fashionable London world to hazard an opinion on the subject;

but Frere, who know everybody, say the story have gain universal

credence; and though by no mean dispose to judge human nature

severely, believe in it himself.”



“it be very, very shocking,” murmur Annie; “and I have hope it could

not be true, but papa be much incense, and believe it fully; and

I fancy you do also, although, have such just cause to dislike my

cousin, you be too generous to blame he.”



“indeed, you be mistaken,” return Lewis kindly; for her manner

confirm he in an impression which have arise in his mind that the

distaste she have express to the engagement with Lord Bellefield would

vanish as her anger at his neglect cool. “indeed, I do not think so;

on the contrary, I have a strong conviction that the affair have be

misrepresented and exaggerated, and that your cousin will be able to

clear himself, not only to your satisfaction, but to that of General

Grant also.”



“heaven forbid!” exclaim Annie impetuously; and then, ere the word

be well speak, she continue, “no, I do not mean that. how wicked of

I to say so! but, oh! it be such joy to feel that I be free--free

as air!” then observe that Lewis’s eye be fix upon she with an

inquire glance, though his lip frame no sound, she add with a

bright blush, “yes, you be a true prophet, Mr. Arundel,” and turn

abruptly, she quit the room.



and Lewis! do he rejoice that the man he hate be thus cross in his

dearest wish--thus hold up to public obloquy? strange as many will

deem it, he do not. on the contrary--except on Annie’s account--he be

annoyed at the turn event have take. in the first place, although the

fact be so strong that he could not reasonably discredit the report

that be in circulation, he feel a sort of instinctive belief that

Lord Bellefield be not guilty of all the evil lay to his charge. he

recall the expression of his face as he have see it on the night of

the suicide; it have not be that of a man harden in crime, who have

leave the victim of his bet scheme unaided in his extremity to seek

refuge from dishonour in the madness of self-destruction, but rather that

of a being of mixed good and evil startle by some frightful reality of

life into a condition of temporary remorse. if Lewis could have realise

his exact wish at this moment, he would have desire to clear Lord

Bellefield’s character by his own unassisted effort, and as a reward,

to have call he out the next morning and fight with small sword

(pistol would have decide the matter too quickly to satisfy he) till

one or both should have furnish subject for the undertaker. then his

thought revert to Annie--she be free, and rejoice in her freedom,

therefore she be to be win. watch his feature as the idea strike he:

first a flush of joy, crimson brow and cheek, fade to the pale hue

of despair; then the clench hand and compress lip, that tell of the

strong will battle with, ay, and conquering--for the will be as yet

the strong--the germ of a consume passion. brave young heart,

taste for the first time the full bitterness of life, angel might

have weep to view thy gallant strive!



the aphorism embody the statement that a storm be usually follow by

a calm, although by no mean original, be not on that account the less

true; nor in trace the course of event in the household of General

Grant shall we discover an exception to this rule in the “Law of

Storms.” immediately after the incident we have relate. Lord Bellefield

(probably wish to escape the disagreeable notoriety likely to be

obtain by his share in the catastrophe) escort his sister to Italy,

without make any attempt to deprecate the anger of General Grant; and

although the Marquis of Ashford, who greatly desire that the propose

matrimonial alliance should take place (hope that marriage might

wean his son from various expensive pursuit, of the nature whereof the

reader may have glean some faint idea from the previous course of this

narrative), make sundry attempt to effect a reconciliation, the General

remain implacable. from his new position, as occasional secretary

to her father, Lewis be throw into constant intercourse with Annie,

while, from the deservedly high opinion General Grant have form of he,

he be treat more as a friend than a dependant. before Mrs. Arundel

and Rose leave London, Annie obtain her father’s permission to invite

the latter to spend a few day with they. Rose place the invitation in

Lewis’s hand before show it even to Mrs. Arundel. she divine that

her brother would feel strongly on the subject, and determine to be

guide by his wish. he read Annie’s note in silence: it be like

herself--simple, frank, and warm-hearted; it be accompany by a few

line from the General--kind (for he) and courteous in the extreme.

“Miss Arundel would confer an obligation on his daughter by allow she

the opportunity of becoming acquaint with,” etc., etc. the General

have hear of Rose’s literary reputation, and look upon she as a

second Madame de Staël. a woman who have write a book appear to his

simplicity a thing as wonderful as, in these latter day, when, to speak

poetically, the sun of literature be obscure by the leafy greenness of

the soft sex, we be accustom to regard a woman who have never do

so. Lewis read the two note; there be not a shadow of patronage from

begin to end at which the most rampant pride could take offence--the

invitation be unexceptionable; and then a crowd of conflict idea

rush upon he, and he pace the apartment for once in a state of the

most complete indecision. this be not a mood of mind which could ever

continue long with Lewis, and pause abruptly, he say, “I really do

not see how you can well refuse, after such a very kind note from--from

the General.”



“I shall be delighted to accept it, dear Lewis, since you wish it as

well as myself; I long to know more of that sweet Annie.”



“you will be disappoint if you expect to find Miss Grant unusually

clever,” return Lewis moodily. “she have good natural ability,

but nothing more, neither have she be accustom to live amongst

intellectual people; she be by no mean your equal in point of talent.”



Rose look surprised at this depreciatory speech; she consider Annie

so fascinating that she do not imagine it in man’s nature to criticise

she unfavourably, and that Lewis, of all people, should do so be very

incomprehensible. she only reply, however, “Miss Grant be much more

accomplish than I be, at all event; she sketch like an artist,

play with great taste and execution, and sing most sweetly. I do not

think it by any mean an advantage to a woman to be unusually clever: it

tend to force she out of her proper sphere, and to urge she to a degree

of publicity repugnant to all the well instinct of her nature.”



“I quite agree with you,” rejoin Lewis cordially. “a woman should have

a quick, vigorous intellect, to enable she to perceive and appreciate

the good, the true, and the beautiful, but nothing beyond. with a single

exception, dear Rose, I consider literary woman complete anomaly,

thing to wonder at and to pity; depend upon it, few woman who devote

their life to literature be really happy.”



as Lewis cease speak Rose sit for some moment ponder the truth

of the opinion which he, in common with many of the good and wisest of

his sex, hold on this subject. at length she say, “I agree, and yet I

differ with you. surely a fine mind be one of the noble gift God can

bestow upon his creature, because,” she add reverently, “the high

the intellect the nearer it must approach to his own perfect wisdom;

therefore talent ought to be a boon to woman as well as to man; but be

it not in the application of that talent that the mischief lie? if the

consciousness of mental superiority unfit a woman for the performance

of her natural duty, instead of enable she to fulfil they more

thoroughly, the fault rest not in the gift, which be in itself a

privilege, but in the misapplication of it by the person on whom it be

bestow. retirement be a woman’s natural position, and anything which

lead she to forsake it tend to unsex and deteriorate she. I do not

say that it must necessarily do so; if, for instance, some pious motive,

such as a desire to assist her family, actuate she, she often appear

to be protect from the danger which surround the path which she have

choose, but that these danger be great and many it be vain to deny.”



“my opinion be,” rejoin Lewis, “that amongst either man or woman those

only should write book who, from some cause or other, be so thoroughly

imbue with their subject that utterance become as it be a necessity;

then, and then only, do they produce anything great and good. the

strong argument I know against woman write be that they never

appear to exceed pleasing mediocrity. you have no female shakespeare

or Milton--even Byron and Scott be unapproache by the brave of your

literary Amazons. certainly woman should not write and having utter

this opinion much as if he would have like to alter the ‘should’ into

‘shall,’ and to be make autocrat of England till he have purge the

land from blue-stocking.” Lewis take his hat and depart, leave that

“talented authoress,” his sister, to chew the cud of his encouraging

observation as good she might.



the practical result of this conversation be that Rose spend a week

in Park Crescent, and thus the occurrence thereof fall out. Miss

Livingstone first catechise, then patronise the young tutor’s sister.

the General also try a pompously condescend system, but Rose’s

sweetness subdue the old soldier; and ere the week have pass he become

devoted to she, and in his stately fashion love she only a little less

than his own daughter. and Annie--she first begin by be afraid of her

new acquaintance because she be an authoress; then she discover that

she be not so alarming-, after all; next it occur to she that she

be very sensible; afterwards that she be very affectionate, which go

a great way with Annie; and finally, that she be quite perfect, and

exactly _the_ friend she have be all her life pine for. from the

moment she discover this, which be once upon a time when Rose,

carry away by the heat of congenial conversation, begin to talk about

her brother, she delight to lay bare her pure, girlish heart to her

new-find friend. and what do the reader suppose it contain? any

very mysterious secret, any dire and soul-harrowing episode, as become

the heart of a heroine? alas, for poor, degenerate Annie! there be

no such interesting content in her warm little bosom, only much

simplicity, sundry good resolution contain the germ of future

self-discipline, great natural amiability, a ready appreciation of

all that be excellent in art or nature, and an open and unbounded

admiration of, and respect for, the character of Lewis; so open indeed

that Rose thankfully acknowledge to her secret soul that one alarm

possibility which have lately occur to and haunt she--viz., that

Annie and Lewis be fall in love with each other--could have no

foundation in fact. the only drawback to Rose’s pleasure in her visit

be, strange to say, the behaviour of her brother. his manner when

alone with she--and the delicate tact of Annie Grant afford they

many opportunity for a _tête-à-tête_--was wayward and fitful in the

extreme. sometimes, but very seldom, he appear low and out of spirit;

at other he be cold and sarcastic, or even perverse and unjust; and

though these fit be invariably follow by expression of the most

affectionate regard towards Rose herself, yet the idea with which they

impress she be that his mind be ill at ease, and that for some

reason which he studiously conceal, he be unhappy. the week pass

away like a dream, and Annie, as she part from her new friend, feel

as if some being of a superior order, endow with power to make and to

keep she good, be leave she again to fight single-handed with the
