trial and temptation of life.



Frere have be despatch by his scientific superior to inspect certain

organic remain which have come to light during the formation of a

railroad cutting in the north of Ireland; which remain, assume to

be the vertebræ and shin bone of an utter impossibility (the

comparative-anatomical sketch, which Frere design on the _ex pede

Herculem_ principle, represent the lamented deceased as a specie

of wing hippopotamus, with a bird’s head, a crocodile’s tail, and

something resemble an invert umbrella round its camelopard-like

neck, form a whole more picturesque than probable), excite the

deep interest in the world of science, which last till, unluckily,

one of the workman, strike his pick-axe against a partially imbed

bone, find that the Rumpaddyostodon (for so have Frere’s _chef_ already

name it) be compose of irish oak.



Ere Frere return from this expedition Mrs. Arundel and Rose have

quit London, a fact which annoy that gentleman more than he could

reasonably account for. having, however, recover from his strange fit

of shyness, he write Rose a long account of his adventure, wind up

by originate a press invitation to himself to spend a fortnight

with they during the vacation, which invitation he not only accept

most graciously, but with the utmost benevolence volunteer to prolong

to three week, if he could possibly manage it.



Lewis, shortly after the departure of his mother and sister, receive

what Annie term “marching orders”--viz., an intimation that on

a certain day and hour he and his pupil be to hold themselves in

readiness to start for Broadhurst, it be one of the General’s pet

idiosyncrasy to manage his family movement _saltatim_, by jerk, as

it be, which disagreeable habit he have acquire during his campaigning

day, when the exigency of military service necessitate such abrupt

proceeding. the consequence of this particular exercise of discipline

be that Lewis receive the follow note on the evening before their

departure:--



“Dear Sir,--Learning this morning, accidentally, that you be about to

leave town to-morrow, and wish much to see you on a matter of

some importance before you do so, shall I be put you to any great

inconvenience if I ask you to do I the favour of breakfast with I

to-morrow? name your own hour, from six o’clock downward. my boy be

wait, or more properly (you know his mendacious propensity) _lie_

in wait for your answer. N.B.--I be aware of the utter vileness of that

pun, but my ink be so confoundedly thick that really I could not make a

well one.



“your faithfully,



“T. Bracy.”









CHAPTER XLIV.--LEWIS PRACTICALLY test the assertion that VIRTUE be its

OWN reward, and obtains an UNSATISFACTORY RESULT.



“this be kind of you, Mr. Arundel,” exclaim Bracy, shake he

heartily by the hand, when, in reply to his friend’s invitation, Lewis

make his appearance at his chamber by eight o’clock on the following

morning; “I like a man who will come to you at a minute’s notice. now,

as I know your time’s short, we’ll go to work at once, and talk as we

eat. bring the egg and roll, Orphy.”



“please, sir, they ain’t none of ’em come,” respond the individual

thus address, who be no less a personage than the tiger “for

falsehood fame.”



“I know he’d say that,” observe Bracy aside, with a look of exultation,

“I know he’d say so, because I see the man bring they five minute ago;

sharp boy! he never lose an opportunity of lie. perhaps they may have

arrive while you’ve be up here,” he continue blandly; “go and see,

orphy.”



“what do you call your tiger?” inquire Lewis as the imp disappear.



“why, his real name be Dick Timmins,” return Bracy; “I have take the

trouble to ascertain that fact beyond a doubt--of course I should not

have believe it merely upon his authority--but I call he orphy, which

be a convenient abbreviation of Orpheus, because, like that meritorious

mythical musician, he be at all time and season perfectly inseparable

from a _lyre!_ ‘a poor pun,’ sir, ‘but mine own.’”



“it must surely be inconvenient and troublesome to be oblige

perpetually to guard against some deception or other, to be in continual

doubt as to what have or what have not take place in your household,”

 remark Lewis.



“not at all, my dear Arundel, there’s the beauty of it,” return Bracy.

“other doubt and be perplexed, but I be never at a loss for a moment;

I know all his most intricate involution of lying, and can track he

through a course of falsehood as a greyhound follow a hare: that boy

could not deceive I unless he be suddenly to take to tell the

truth; but there’s not the least fear of that, his principle be too

well establish. ah! _inter alia_, here he come--do you see the pun?

pre-suppose an irish brogue, and accent the penultimate instead of the

first syllable in the second word, and it’s not such a bad one after

all.”



when, to use the popular lyric style, the “false one have depart,”

 and the gentleman be again leave _tête-à-tête_, Lewis, remind his

companion that his time be short, hint that it would not be amiss

if he be at once to acquaint he with the business to which he have

refer in his note.



“ah! yes, to be sure,” reply Bracy; “it be a letter I have from Frere

yesterday which put the thing into my head. let we see, what do he

say?” and pull a letter from his pocket, he run his eye down it,

read and soliloquise somewhat after the following fashion: “Hum!

ha! ‘never take share in an irish railway’--thank ye, I never mean

to--‘the native in these part be not cannibal, at which no one at

all particular in his eating would wonder, after see the state of

filth’--well, I willn’t read that, it will spoil our breakfast--‘the

organic remain be come out splendidly; I feel little doubt they must

have belong to some antediluvian monster yet unknown to science.’ ah!

the fossil remain of a pre-adamite irish bull, probably; and that’s

another, by Jove, for there would have be nobody to make it at that

time of day: there’s a P.S. about it, though--ah! here it is--‘only

fancy, my organic remain prove to be vegetable, not animal; nothing

more or less than a new specie of Irish Oaks.’ a new specie of irish

_hoax_ rather; I wonder how he come to miss the pun--some man do throw

away their opportunity sadly; but I’m waste your time--now then--‘in

regard to what you tell I about the Bellefield affair, I can do

nothing, not be on the spot; your good plan will be to communicate

with Lewis Arundel--he be thoroughly _au fait_ as to the whole matter;

tell he everything, and act accord to his advice. you may safely

do so. I always think his lordship a great scoundrel’ (rather strong

language!), ‘but in this case he appear more to be pity than blame;

I like fair-play all the world over, and would give even the devil

his due.’ there,” continue Bracy, fold up the letter, “that’s what

Richard Frere say, and I, know his advice to be good, be prepared to

act upon it.”



“it may be good,” return Lewis in a tone of annoyance, “but as far as

I be concerned *it be particularly enigmatical. there be many reason

why it be undesirable, I may say impossible, for I to interfere with

Lord Bellefield’s affair.”



“still, if you be the man I take you to be,” reply Bracy seriously,

“you would not wish any one to labour under an unjust imputation, from

which a word of truth can set he free. but it’s no use beat about

the bush; hear what I have to say, and then you can act or remain

neuter, as you please. of course you read the newspaper account of that

sad business about poor Mellerton?”



Lewis reply in the affirmative, and Bracy continue, “except in one or

two point, the statement be substantially correct, but these happen

to be rather important one. in the first place, I should tell you that

Mellerton be an intimate friend of my own. we be great crony at

Eton, and never lose sight of each other afterwards. I first hear

of this bet affair from an officer of high rank, who hold an

appointment by which he be necessarily a good deal behind the scene

at the War Office. somehow it reach his ear that Mellerton have be

bet heavily and meet with severe loss, and know that I have some

influence with he, he wish I to give he a friendly hint, which I

accordingly do. Mellerton take it very well, poor fellow! and thank

I for my advice, which be his invariable custom, though I cann’t say he

usually act upon it. he confess that he have lose more money than be

convenient, and tell I he have be force to borrow, but the amount

of his loss he studiously conceal. on the morning of the day of

his death the same person send for I again, and tell I he be afraid

Mellerton have be behave very madly, and in the strict confidence

inform I that it be determine upon to examine into his account,

and that if, as he fear, they would not bear the light, his character

would be blast for life, add that I be at liberty to warn he of

this, and give he an opportunity, if possible, of replace the money.

owe to a chapter of accident, as ill luck would have it, I be unable

to meet with Mellerton till late in the evening, when I find he in a

state of distraction, having just receive officially the information

I have seek to forestall. see how much I know already, he tell I

everything. I will not recapitulate the miserable detail, but the

newspaper do not overstate the truth. well, as a forlorn hope, I

suggest the appeal to Lord Bellefield’s generosity, and after much

persuasion he agree to let I make the trial. I spring into a hansom

cab, and drive like the wind to Ashford House. Bellefield be dining

with his sister; I follow he to Berkeley Square, and then to the

Opera-house, where I lose not a minute in explain my business.

well, sir, instead of reject the appeal, as have be report, Lord

Belle-field appear greatly distressed at the intelligence--jump into

his cab, take I with he, and as we drive down to poor Mellerton’s

lodging, express his readiness to do whatever I think well--add

that he have £10,000 at his banker’s, which be quite at Mellerton’s

service till he could sell his Yorkshire estate. the rest of the tale

you know. the poor fellow, think, from my prolonged absence, that my

attempt have fail, and unable to bear the disgrace of exposure, place

a loaded pistol in his pocket, repair to a gaming-table, bet to the

full amount of his defalcation, lost, and blow his brain out. we get

there just as the surgeon they have send for declare life be extinct;

and you never see a man so cut up as Bellefield be about it. he accuse

himself of be a murderer, and in fact seem to feel the thing

nearly as much as I do myself. as soon as he have a little recovered he

volunteer to drive to Knightsbridge, to break the thing to poor Fred

Mellerton’s brother, while I do the same by his mother and sister; and

a nice scene I have of it--I think the old lady would have die on the

spot. but now, to come to the point, I hear that old Grant, believe

all the newspaper lie, have quarrel with his intended son-in-law and

break off the engagement; and that Lord Bellefield, too proud to make

any explanation, have allow he to continue in his mistake. be this

so?”



“I have no reason to believe your information incorrect,” be the

cautious reply.



“in that case, don’t you think it be due to Lord Bellefield to acquaint

General Grant with the truth?”



Lewis pause for a minute or two in thought ere he reply, “certainly;

it would be most unjust to withhold it.”



“well, I’m very glad you agree with I,” return Bracy, rub his

hand with the air of a man who have escape some disagreeable duty.

“then I may depend upon you to set the matter right?”



“upon I!” rejoin Lewis in surprise.



“yes, to be sure,” be the reply; “that’s what Frere expect. you see,

it’s rather a delicate affair for a man to interfere in, particularly

one who be a complete stranger. I don’t believe I ever set eye on

Governor Grant in my life. now you, live in the house, can find a

hundred opportunity. there be a good deal in select the _mollia

tempora fandi_ with man as well as with woman.”



“then I be to understand that you have relate these fact to I for the

express purpose of my communicate they to General Grant?”



“yes, to be sure. do you think I should have put you to the

inconvenience of come here this morning merely for the sake of have

a gossip?”



“and suppose I be to refuse to make this communication?” continue

Lewis.



“such a supposition never occur to I,” reply Bracy in amazement;

“but if you be to do such an unexpected thing, matter must take their

own course. in tell you, I’ve do all that I consider I be in any

way call upon to do; if you, for any reason, deem it unadvisable to

enlighten General Grant, there the thing must rest. Frere tell I to be

guide by your advice, and so I shall; as I have just say, I leave it

entirely to you.”



“I understand you perfectly,” rejoin Lewis, and as he speak a

contemptuous smile curl his lip; “still, justice require that the

General should not be keep in ignorance, and although there be many

reason why it be painful and objectionable to I to enlighten he, yet

there be other which prevent my refusing; and now, Mr. Bracy, as my

time be short, you will excuse my being oblige to leave you.”



“oh! certainly,” return Bracy, as his visitor rise to depart;



               “‘welcome the come, speed the parting guest.’



Liberty Hall this, sir! _chacun à son gout_, ‘everybody have the gout,’

as the little girl at the boarding-school construe it. then you’ll make

Governor Grant comprehend that in this particular instance Bellefield

behave like a brick? disagreeable business to be oblige to interfere

in, but, as Frere say, you’re just the man to do it; good morning;” and

utter these word with the great _empressement_, he shake Lewis’s

hand warmly and suffer he to depart. as the door close on his

retreating figure Bracy throw himself back in an easy-chair.



“there’s something in the wind that I’m not awake to,” he mutter in

soliloquy; “I don’t comprehend that good youth at all. there must be

private feeling mix up in it; something in the love and murder line, I

suspect. how savage he look when he undertake the job--rather he than

I, though: Bellefield’s as likely to call a man out, as to say thank ye,

for interfere; but, as Frere say, Arundel’s just the man to carry the

thing through. he’s a plucky young fellow and deucedly good-look, but

he certainly do not appreciate wit--ahem--that is, pun--properly;”

 and with this reflection Bracy take pen and paper and sate he down

to indite an essay on moral courage and the responsibility of man,

wherewith to fill up a vacant corner of Blunt’s Magazine.



and Lewis--what a task have he undertake! he who would have make any

sacrifice to gratify Annie’s light wish must now bring the first

cloud over the sunshine of her young life; he must be the mean of

reconcile her father and Lord Bellefield; he must, by his own act,

give the woman he love to the man he hate. the woman he loved!--had

then the fear that have lie cold and heavy at his heart, that have come

between he and--resignation, assume a definite shape? do he at length

own that he, the poor tutor, the pay dependent, love the rich

man’s daughter? oh! Lewis, where be thy pride--where that Hell-angel

beautiful in evil, which hath haunt thee even from thy childhood

upwards like a second self? have Rose’s tear prevail and thy pride

desert thee? would that it have be so; but no, he have not yet learn

that hard lesson for the young and manly-hearted, self-distrust; his

bosom-sin clave to he, and strive single-handed, how should he subdue

it!



Lewis be not one of those who deceive themselves long on any point;

and his emotion after the scene at the Opera-house, the amount of

self-control he be oblige to exert to restrain any outbreak of feel

in the _tête-à-tête_ drive with Annie, have reveal the truth to he,

and ere he sleep that night he know that now indeed be the sum of his

wretchedness complete; for he love, for the first time, one fit to

call forth all the depth and earnestness of his passionate nature, and

he love without hope. press his hand to his burn brow, he

sit down calmly to think. calmly; yes, the treacherous repose of the

smoulder volcano be an apt illustration of such a forced calmness.

renunciation and self-conquest! this then be his portion for the time

to come. self-conquest! Pride catch at the word; an enemy strong as

the strength of will which should subdue it. reason cry, “Flee from

temptation;” but pride whisper, “the task be worthy of you; accomplish

it.” and resolution aid pride, and the iron will come into play, and

the contest be begin. and now the reader can understand why Lewis’s

interview with Bracy would scarcely tend to raise his spirit, or render

his general frame of mind more satisfactory.



Punctual to the moment the carriage make its appearance, draw by four

poster; and the General and the two lady ensconce themselves in

the interior, while, the day be lovely, Lewis and his pupil take

possession of the rumble. about two mile from Broadhurst be a steep

hill; on reach this point Annie and her father, Lewis and Walter

alight, with the intention of walk up; but before half the distance

be accomplish the General plead guilty to a very decide twinge

of gout, and unwilling to provoke a second, reentere the carriage, the

other continue their pedestrian exertion without he.



Annie, delight to regain the freedom of the country, be in high

spirit. “why do people stay in London at this time of year?” she

exclaim. “this lovely sky, and the tree, and the bird, and the

sunshine, be worth all the opera and picture and ball and every

sight or amusement London can afford; those thing excite one for an

hour or two, but this make I perfectly happy.”



Lewis glance at she for a moment, sigh involuntarily, and then

rouse himself, utter some commonplace civility, which so clearly

prove that he be force himself to make conversation from the subject

of which his thought be far away, that Annie, strike by his manner,

pause and fix her large eye earnestly upon he. at length she said--



“I be sure you be ill or unhappy, Mr. Arundel. I be now too well aware

how utterly unable I be to compensate for the loss of such a friend and

counsellor as dear Rose (oh! how I envy you that sister!), but if you

would sometimes tell I when you be annoyed or out of spirit, instead

of wrap yourself in that cold, proud mantle of reserve, I think even

such poor sympathy as mine might make you happy.”



Lewis glance round. Walter, actuate by some caprice of his wayward

intellect, have run on before--they be virtually alone. now it have

occur to Lewis that, as Annie have allow he to perceive her dislike

to the idea of a union with Lord Bellefield, he should entirely lose she

good opinion be she to learn that it be through his representation

that a reconciliation with her father have be bring about; and

although this would have be a very desirable result for many reason,

and have materially assist he in his design of conquer his unhappy

attachment, yet he by no mean appear to approve of the notion, but on

the contrary have, with his usual fearlessness, determine to seize the

first opportunity of explain to she why reason and justice oblige

he to act in opposition to her wish. and now that the opportunity have

arrive, the considerate kindness of her address disarm he, and in

the unwillingness to inflict pain on she he half abandon his purpose;

but here his strength of will--that fearful agent for good or for

evil--come into action and settle the matter. it be right; it must

be do. accordingly he thank she for her kindness, make she a pretty

speech as to value her sympathy, which express somewhere about

one-fifteenth of what he really feel on the subject--say, which be

quite true, that nothing have for a long time afford he great

pleasure than the friendship which have spring up between she and

Rose--then, speak in a low, calm voice, he continue, “I have be

both grieve and annoy this morning; you guess rightly when you

think so. will you forgive I, and still regard I as your friend,

when I tell you that circumstance force I to act in direct opposition

to your wish, and to do that of which I fear you will highly

disapprove?”



Annie look at he with an expression of surprise and alarm, which give

way to a bright, trustful smile as she reply, “nothing can lead I to

doubt your friendship, Mr. Arundel; I have have proof of its sincerity

too convincing for I ever to do so. if you be oblige to say or do

anything which may pain I, I be sure you feel it to be duty which

compel you. and now tell I what you refer to.”



Poor Lewis! the smile and the speech go straight to his heart, like

the stroke of a poniard: pride, resolution, and all the other false god

he rely on disappear before it; and for the moment love be lord of

all. but self-control have become so habitual to he, that the most acute

observer could not have detect the slight indication of the inward

struggle; and ere he speak his will have resume its mastery, and his

purpose hold good. he give she, in as few word as possible, an account

of his interview with Bracy; and tell she that it be his intention

immediately to acquaint General Grant with the fact that have thus come

to his knowledge.



she hear he in silence; and when he have finish she say in a low

voice, which thrill with suppress emotion, “my father will forgive

he, and all will be as if this thing have never happen.”



they walk on side by side, but neither speak. at length Lewis say

abruptly, “I have tell you this man and I be not on friendly term;

I now tell you that he have heap insult after insult upon I till

I _hate_ he. yes, you may start, and your gentle woman’s nature may

condemn I, but it be so: I hate he.” he speak calmly, but it only

render his word more terrible, for it tell not merely of the angry

impulse of the moment, but of the deep conviction of a lifetime; and

Annie shudder as she listen. regardless of her emotion,

Lewis continue, “circumstance have in this instance force I to

appear as Lord Bellefield’s successful accuser. to some mind this petty

triumph might have afford satisfaction; to I it have be a source of

unmixed regret; the retribution I seek be not of such a nature. Fate have

now place in my hand the mean of vindicate his character; and every

principle of honour, nay of common justice, bind I to do so. we may

not do evil that good may come. I should forfeit my self-respect for

ever be I to conceal this knowledge from your father. you would

not have I do so, I be certain?” Lewis pause for a reply; there be

silence for a moment, and then in a low, broken voice Annie say, “no!

you _must_ tell he. but I be very, very unhappy!” and utter the last

word with a convulsive sob, she cover her face with her hand, and

turn away to conceal the tear she could no long repress.









CHAPTER XLV.--ANNIE GRANT take to study GERMAN, and MEETS with AN

alarming adventure.



whether the sight of Annie’s tear would have produce any change in

Lewis’s determination, have their _tête-à-tête_ continue uninterrupted,

be a question in regard to which psychologist may arrive at any

conclusion which please they; for Walter have literally, or

figuratively, catch his butterfly, rejoin his companion almost

immediately, and under cover of his puerile volubility Annie contrive

to dry her eye and outwardly regain her composure.



in the course of the follow morning Lewis find an opportunity of

make the important communication. General Grant hear he with grave

attention, and when he have conclude, observed--



“this alter the whole aspect of the affair. any man may commit a fault,

but if he see his error, and be willing by every mean at his command

to atone for it, it behove all generous-minded person to forgive he.

I perceive that I have, in this instance, act hastily, and owe Lord

Bellefield reparation. I shall write to he immediately, and have to

thank you, Mr. Arundel, for afford I this information, which may

give I an opportunity of effect a reconciliation with one on whom I

have long since decide to bestow my daughter’s hand. your disinterested,

I may say magnanimous conduct in this matter, entitle you to my full

confidence. I shall make it an express stipulation that for the future

Lord Bellefield shall evince all due consideration towards you.”



and this speech, and the haunt memory of Annie’s tear, be Lewis’s

reward for do his duty.



the result of this communication be that the General write a long

letter to Lord Bellefield, use many word to express his meaning,

which might have be advantageously compress into half the number;

however, it satisfy its composer, who consider it a miracle of

diplomacy and a model of style. Lord Bellefield’s answer be cold and

haughty; his pride have be wound, and his be not a mind frankly to

forgive an injury of that nature; still he do not reject the General’s

overture. he be go to travel in Greece (he write), but on his

return to England he would see General Grant and refute the calumny

which have be spread to his disadvantage: he be aware that he have

enemy who might be glad to avail themselves of any opportunity to

vilify his character, but he trust to the General’s sense of justice

to discourage such attempt. and the content of this letter be

communicate to Annie by her father, together with a rebuke for having

so easily believe report to her cousin’s disadvantage, which lecture

somewhat fail in its effect from the unlucky fact that, in this

particular instance, the lecturer’s practice happen to have be

diametrically oppose to his preaching; but the rebuke lead to one evil

result--viz., it crush in the bud a half-form project which Annie

have conceive of acquaint her father with her grow disinclination

to a union with her cousin, and of implore he to take no step towards

a renewal of the engagement. moral courage (save when her feeling be

very strongly excited) be not one of Annie’s attribute, and as the

evil she fear be not a proximate one, she trust to chance to

postpone it still far, if not to avert it altogether. thus, be

naturally of a light-hearted, joyous temperament, she ere long recover

her usual gaiety, and an occasional sigh, or a quarter of an hour’s

unwonted abstraction, alone attest her recollection of this dark speck

on the horizon of her future life.



the return to Broadhurst appear to produce a soothe effect upon

Lewis also--it give he an opportunity calmly to review his position;

and a new idea strike he, generalise from which he sketch out a

system different from that which he have hitherto pursue in regard

to Annie Grant. true, he could never hope to call she his--love be

forbid he--but friendship, warm, ardent friendship--love elevate,

spiritualise, purify from the slight admixture of passion,--this

he might enjoy safely; it only require a strong effort of will, a

determine, uniform exercise of self-control. to be enslave by hopeless

love be mere weakness; to crush the sentiment entirely be quixotic

and uncalled for; but to control and regulate it, to fix limit which it

should not exceed, and thus to convert a curse into a blessing, this be

an effort worthy of a reasonable being, and this he would accomplish. in

order to carry out this design he determine no long to avoid Annie

as he have do; it be cowardly to fly thus from temptation; besides, it

be evidently useless to do so; imagination supply the deficiency, and

the evil be but increase. no, he would face the danger and subdue it.

thus, too, he might be of use to she, for with all his admiration of her

character he read it aright, and see that there be weak point which

require the aid of principle to strengthen they; that her pursuit be

frivolous, her mind uncultivate, and her existence practically aimless,

because her view of life be confused and indistinct, and her standard

of excellence a visionary one. all this he see, and see, feel that

he could remedy. and while he ponder on these thing Annie recall

an old wish to study German, and ask her father’s permission to be

allow to do so, if Mr. Arundel could find time to give her lesson;

whereupon the General, have a great respect for any language of which

he be personally ignorant, prefer her request to Lewis, and that

young gentleman be graciously pleased to accede thereunto. Miss

Livingstone of course play duenna, and but for one circumstance would

have perform her character with a degree of cold-blooded virulence

worthy of the most fractious tigress that ever mangle “lover tender

and true.” this fortunate circumstance be that the lesson, be

usually take by way of dessert after an early dinner, invariably send

Minerva to sleep. in vain do she bring out her “poor basket,” in which

receptacle lie hide certain harsh underclothing for infant pauper;

in vain do she attempt sew the seam of Procustes-like pinafore,

which, solve the problem of the minimum of brown holland capable of

contain a live child, be destine to compress the sturdy body

of village urchin; the “_colo calathisve Minervo_” be unable to

resist the attack of the god Somnus, and ere Annie have stretch

her pretty little mouth by the utterance of a dozen double-bodied

substantive, the lynx-eye be close in sleep, and for all practical

purpose Miss Livingstone forfeit every right to the first half of her

patronymic.



Reader, if you be of the gender which uncourteous grammarian be

pleased to designate the worthy, tell me,--in strict confidence, of

course,--did you ever read German with a pair of bright eye turn

from the crab teutonic character to look appealingly into your

own optic, while two coral lip, form for pleasanter purpose than

growl german guttural, complain of some enigmatical sentence which

have _not_ get a right meaning to it, the say eye and lip be the

outward symbol of a warm heart and quick intelligence, ready to discern

and feel the grandeur of Schiller, the Shakespeare of the Fatherland, or

thrill to the trumpet blast of young Korner’s warrior spirit, or trace

the more subtle thinking of Goethe, that anatomist of the soul of man?

tell I, do you ever read with so desirable a fellow-student? if you

have do so, and can honestly say you do not think such schooling

delightful, the soon you close this book the well, for depend upon

it there be little sympathy between we. Lewis at all event have no

cause to be dissatisfied with his pupil, who be equally docile and

intelligent, and in a marvellously short space of time be able to read

and translate with tolerable fluency; while the few german sentence in

which her instructor from time to time see fit to address she appear

less like heathen Greek to she at each repetition. as soon as she have

sufficiently master the difficulty of those aggravating part of

speech, the compound separable verb, and acquire moderate control over

other equally necessary and vicious part of the grammar, they commence

translate that most poetical of allegory, La Motte Fouqué’s

“Undine”; and Annie, as they read, take it all at first _au pied de la

lettre_, and imagine, with a degree of shudder horror, which, as

it be _only_ a tale, be rather pleasant than otherwise, all the

supernatural uncomfortable Huldbrand underwent in the enchant

Forest, and admire all the generous impulse of the heroine’s singular

uncle-and-water, Kuhleborn, who, however, she consider would have

be well adapt for family purpose, if he have be rather more of

a man and less of a cataract. then Undine herself, the capricious,

fascinating, tricksy sprite--the thoughtful, loving, feel woman--how

Annie sympathise with and adore she! for Huldbrand she feel a specie

of contemptuous pity, but Bertalda, oh! she be sure no woman be ever

so heartless, so utterly and wickedly selfish. and then when Lewis

unfold to she his view of the Allegory (every one be sure to form a

particular theory of his own as to the meaning of “Undine,” and to think

he only have discover the author’s intention), and Annie learn that

the tale shadow forth the mighty truth which throng the passage to

eternity, and symbolise the struggle between good and evil, show

how Principalities and Powers wage throughout all time an undying

warfare--the breast of man their battlefield--her pulse quicken and

her cheek flush; for she feel for the first time the solemn reality

of existence, and see dimly how a single life might be a link between

the Ages, and a portion, however insignificant, of the mighty whole.

what wonder then if part of the reverence, the awe, chasten by a deep,

solemn joy, with which she recognise the working of Infinite power,

and Infinite love, cast their spell around he who have first awaken

these perception within her?--what wonder if unconsciously compare

he with those around she, she grow to believe he a being of another

and a high nature, and so to hang on his slight word or look, to

dread his frown, and deem his smile sufficient compensation for hour of

unwonted study?



the german lesson seem to agree particularly well with Lewis also;

for his eye grow bright and his step more free, the extreme paleness

of his complexion change to a manly brown, a slight tinge of colour

impart a look of health to his cheek, and--unromantic as it may

appear--his appetite increase alarmingly. would the reader learn the

secret of this improvement? it be soon tell. live in the present,

blind himself to the truth, he be happy! his system, he tell

himself, have succeed--his theory have be test, and prove a true

one--resolution have conquer, and the insanity of love have cool down

to the reasonableness of a delightful friendship.



Lewis be excessively pleased with himself at this result. at length,

then, he have attain that complete and perfect degree of self-control

he have so long endeavour to acquire; his feeling be reduce to a

due submission to his will; and henceforward his happiness be in his

own hand. and thus bask in this gleam of sunshine, he shut his eye

to all beyond, and dream that he possess an elixir to dissipate

every cloud, and that henceforward storm would disappear from the

horizon of his destiny and become mere myth, exist only in memory.

and these be some of the early result of the german lesson.



about this time a small but unpleasant adventure occur to poor Annie

which occasion she a severe fright, and render her nervous and

uncomfortable for many day afterwards. she have be on an expedition to

the cottage of a poor neighbour who be suffer from illness; and

as the sick woman live beyond a walk, Annie have go on horseback,

attend by an old coachman who have live in the family many year.

having accomplish her mission, she have ride about a quarter of

a mile on her return when she discover that she have leave she

handkerchief behind, and direct the servant to ride back and fetch

it, she proceed at a foot’s pace in a homeward direction. the road she

be follow wound round the base of a hill thickly cover with tree

and underwood, the spread branch of the oak meet across the

lane and make a species of twilight even at midday.



as Annie Grant be pass under one of the thick of these tree, a

tall, gaunt figure spring from behind its knot trunk and seize the

bridle of her pony. gaze in alarm at her assailant, Annie perceive

he to be a man of unusual stature; his feature be pale and

emaciate, and an unshorn, grizzly beard add to the ferocity of their

expression; his clothe, which be tear and soil, hang loosely about

he, while the long bony finger which clutch her bridle-rein, the

sink cheek, and hollow, glare eye-ball, give evidence that his

herculean proportion have be reduce almost to a skeleton leanness by

disease or want. Annie have, however, little time to make observation,

for, accost she with an oath, the ruffian demand her purse. draw

it forth, she hold it to he with a trembling hand. he seize it eagerly

and examine its content, his eye glitter as he observe the

sparkle of gold. hastily conceal it about his person, he next

demand her watch, which Annie, after a hopeless glance in the

direction from which she expect the appearance of the servant, also

relinquish. having secure his plunder the fellow pause, apparently

reflect whether by detain she long he could gain any further

advantage; as he do so the sound of a horse rapidly advance strike

his ear, and immediately afterwards a turn in the road enable he to

perceive the figure of a servant on horseback, the sunshine glance

from his bright livery button. the moment this object meet his view

he start, and shade his eye with his hand, gaze fixedly at the

approach horseman. having thus satisfy himself as to the man’s

identity, he exclaim with an oath, “it be the old bloodhound’s livery,

and the girl must be his daughter. oh, what a chance I have throw away!

yes,” he continue, turn fiercely upon poor Annie and threaten she

with his clench fist, “if I have guess you be the daughter of that

-------- old Grant, you should not have get off so easy, I promise you.”

 he pause as a new idea strike he, and his face assume an expression

of diabolical revenge; place his hand in his breast he draw forth a

pistol, cock it, and mutter, “there be time yet,” level it at

his trembling victim. with a faint scream Annie drop the rein, and

clasp her hand in an agony of fear, murmur a petition for mercy.

the ruffian stand for a moment irresolute; but, desperate as he

be, some touch of humanity yet linger in his breast, a soften

recollection come across he, and mutter, “I cann’t do it, she look

like poor jane,” he uncocke and replace the pistol.



at this moment the servant, having hear Annie’s scream, come up at a

gallop, and the robber, utter a fearful imprecation, spring back into

the wood and disappear among the tree.



it be some minute before Annie, who be on the verge of fainting, be

able to give a coherent account of the adventure which have befall

she. as soon, however, as she have in some degree recover from the

effect of her terror, she desire the servant to ride close beside she,

and urge her pony into a rapid canter, make the good of her way home.

here she find matter in a state of unusual bustle and confusion. the

General have receive information that hardy the poacher have break out

of H---------- gaol, effect his escape unperceive, and be

suppose to be conceal somewhere in the neighbourhood of Broadhurst.

accordingly he be marshal all the available male of his

establishment, preparatory to set out on an expedition to search

for, and if possible to apprehend the escape felon.



Great be his horror and indignation when he learn the danger to which

his daughter have be expose, and ascertain from the description

she give of her assailant that the man who have rob she, and even

threaten her life, be none other than the ruffian Hardy.



the preparation which he have already make he now consider

insufficient for ensure the success of the expedition; he accordingly

despatch a mount groom to procure the assistance of a couple of

policeman, and send for Lewis, beg he to lead a party to search

the country in one direction, while he proceed with a second division

of the household force in another. as the young tutor hear of the

alarm to which Annie have be subject, his cheek flush and his

compress lip quiver. he say little, however, but return to

his room, place a brace of small pistol in the breast of his coat,

attach spur to the heel of his boot, then mount a horse which

be in readiness for he, ride off. the tenant be rouse, the

gamekeeper summon, the policeman arrive. General Grant remain

absent till nearly ten o’clock at night, and his daughter become alarmed

to the last degree for his safety. at length he return; their search

have be unsuccessful, but Mr. Arundel and some of the man would remain

on the watch all night, and he would resume the pursuit next morning.



for three day and night Lewis never enter a house, and be scarcely

out of the saddle; the fourth day the police receive a report from

the authority at Liverpool, state that an individual in some degree

correspond to the description of Hardy have take his passage in a

vessel bind for the United States, and that the wind be favourable,

the ship have sail before they have be able to search she; and with

this unsatisfactory report the family at Broadhurst be force to

content themselves.









CHAPTER XLVI.--IS calculate to “murder sleep” for all nervous young

lady who READ it.



the incident relate in the last chapter produce a strange and

alarming effect upon Miss Livingstone; in fact it may be say to have

lay the foundation of a species of monomania which haunt she to the

day of her death. from this time forth she labour under the delusion

that a man train from his youth up to rob and murder his sleep

fellow-creature be secrete at one and the same moment under every bed

and behind all the window curtain in the house. a singular and alarming

property belong to this ambush ruffian be the extraordinary shadow

cast by his leg and foot. Miss Livingstone be perpetually scare by

discover it in the most unlikely place and position; indeed the

statistic of these shadowy phenomenon tend to show that it be this

villain’s ordinary custom to stand upon his rascally head. then the

noise he make be most strange and unearthly, and a habit he possess

of moan whenever the wind be high really exceed anything

with which human nature could be expect to put up. the trouble he

occasion everybody be inconceivable; for at least a month after

Annie’s adventure the butler almost live in Minerva’s bedroom, so

constantly be he summon to unearth this lurk traitor; and yet,

although Miss Livingstone be quite certain the monster be there, for

she have see the shadow of his boot, with the sol upwards, upon the

tester of the bed, by some dreadful fatality he always contrive to

evade the strict search. once Miss Livingstone think she _have_

get he, for, having summon assistance on the strength of hear he

snore, she actually enjoy the satisfaction of be swear at by he,

when she look under the bed and poke for he with a large umbrella;

but this time he turn out to be the cat. the servant became so

harass by these repeat alarum that at length the butler give _bona

fide_ warning, while the footman, when there be nobody to hear they,

vehemently protest they be not hire as thief-catcher, and that

Miss Livingstone have well set up a private policeman of her own, if

she choose to be so subject to house-breaker.



Lewis be not at all pleased with this adventure: in the first place,

it interrupt the german lesson, for poor Annie have be so seriously

frightened--not without cause--that it make she really ill, and for some

day she remain on a sofa in her own room. in the second place, Lewis

have be so deeply affect when he first hear of the danger to which

she have be expose, that for a moment a doubt cross his mind

whether such a degree of emotion be exactly consistent with that mild

imposition clepe platonic friendship. in the third place, he have use

his good endeavour to catch hardy once again, and have be thoroughly

and completely baffle. time, however, that wonder-work individual,

pass on, and by his assistance Annie’s nerve recover their tone,

and the german lesson be recommence; Miss Livingstone see few

vision of reversed leg, and confine her research after the

conceal one to a good peep under the bed night and morning. the

General make a great fuss about the whole affair, and severely

reprimand several individual for permit Hardy to escape who never

have it in their power to prevent his do so. having relieve his mind

by this judicious exercise of authority, he apply himself to other

pursuit, and speedily forget the whole transaction.



about two month after the occurrence of the robbery Lord Belle-field

write to announce his return, and General Grant go to London alone

in order to meet he. before his departure, Annie, whose dislike to the

interrupt engagement appear to increase rather than to diminish,

determined to make a great effort, and to acquaint her father with her

disinclination to the propose alliance, and to entreat he to take

no step which might lead to a renewal of the matrimonial project. the

General hear she attentively, and then observed--



“I perfectly understand and appreciate your feeling, my dear Annie;

they be such as, under the peculiar circumstance, become _my_

daughter. remember, my dear, that the matter be in wise and more

experienced hand than your; and rest assure that nothing shall be

do of which even your punctilious delicacy and true sense of honour

can disapprove.” then, see Annie be about to speak, he continue,

“any further discussion be not only unnecessary, but as the matter now

stand, would appear to imply a doubt of my capability of act for

you; which I should consider, to say the least, disrespectful. you will

oblige I by withdraw, my dear Annie.” thus say, he rise, and

open the door with all the frigid courtesy of the Grandisonian

school, usher she out. and so poor Annie’s attempt prove a signal

failure.



on the following morning the General leave Broadhurst, having give Annie

a very unnecessary caution against ride out with merely a servant, and

make it his especial request that Lewis and Walter should accompany she

by way of escort; a proceeding of which neither tutor nor pupil appear

to disapprove.



General Grant be absent for more than a fortnight; and as the

weather be unusually fine during the whole of the time, Annie and

her attendant ride out every day. oh, those ride! what delightful

expedition be they! by a tacit consent between Lewis and Annie, all

allusion to the future be avoid, in word or thought; they live in

the present--those love heart; they be together, and that suffice

they; and the tree appear greener, and the flower more brilliant,

and the sunshine brighter, than they have ever seem before; all be

happy as a fairy dream, and dream-like do it pass away.



a letter from the General announce his intended return be in Annie’s

hand, as, bend over a ponderous volume of crab character, she

await her german lesson. the window of the breakfast-room in which

she be seat open on to an ample lawn, intersperse with group of

shrub and gay flower-bed. in cross this lawn Walter have contrive

to intercept Lewis and inveigle he into a game at ball.



flush by the exercise, his eye sparkle with excitement, and his

dark curl hang in wild disorder about his brow, the young tutor

approach the window at which Annie be seat. conceal by the

heavy fold of the window curtain, the girl watch he unperceive:

involuntarily she contrast his frank and easy bearing, his free and

elastic step, and the smile, half proud, half playful, which part his

curved lip and sparkle in his flash eye, with the cold reserve

which usually characterise his demeanour, and for the first time she

become aware what a bright and noble nature have be obscure and warp

by the false position into which circumstance have combine to force

he. who could blame she, who rather would not love she the well, and

thank God that he have implant such beautiful instinct in every true

woman’s heart, if she feel that she should wish no fair destiny

than to devote her life to bring back the sunshine of his, and by she

affection restore to he the youth of soul which misfortune have wrest

from he!



little guess the thought that be pass through her mind, Lewis

advanced towards the window, exclaim, “Miss Grant, I have a petition

to urge--the day be so lovely it be quite wicked to remain indoor: can

I persuade you to use your influence with Miss Livingstone to allow

we to transfer the site of our german lesson to the bench under the

lime-tree? I will promise to arrange a most seductive seat for she in

the very shady corner.”



“my aunt have depart on a charitable mission,” be the reply; “she

receive a message to say that an unfortunate child whom she have be

doctor out of that dreadful medicine chest of hers be much bad, and

she have rush off armed with pill and powder.”



“to give it the _coup de grace_ I suppose,” interrupt Lewis.



Annie shake her head reprovingly, and continue, “in the excitement of

the occasion, she appear to have entirely forget our poor german

lesson.”



“in which case the decision as to place rest with you!” resume Lewis

eagerly; “the matter be therefore settle--you _will_ come.” the accent

upon the “_will_” be intend to be one of entreaty, but somehow the

tone in which it be utter partook largely of command, and Annie, as

she obey, say with a smile--



“or rather, I _must_ come--that be clearly your meaning, Mr. Arundel;

however, I see Walter and Faust be already _en position_, and I will

not set they an example of disobedience, so if you can find the book, I

will join you immediately.”



[illustration: 0338]



it be, as Lewis have declare, a lovely evening; the sky be of that

deep, clear blue which indicate a continuance of fine weather, a soft

breeze sigh through the blossom of the lime-tree beneath which they

sit. Faust lie at Annie’s foot, gaze up into her face as though he

love to look upon her beauty, which perhaps he do, for Faust be a

dog of taste, and particular in the selection of his favourite. Walter,

stretch at his length upon the turf, be idly turn over the

page of a volume of coloured print. Lewis open the work they be

translate; it be that lovely of historical tragedy, Schiller’s

“Piccolomini,” and Annie read of Max, the simple, the true, the

noble-hearted, and think that the world contain but one parallel

character, and that he be beside she. they read on beneath the summer

sky, and trace the working of Schiller’s master mind, forget all

sublunary thing in the absorbing interest of the story. the scene they

be peruse be that in which Max Piccolomini describe the chill

effect produce upon he when he for the first time behold Thekla

surround by the splendour of her father’s court, and say (I quote

Coleridge’s beautiful translation for the benefit of my _un-german_

reader, and in consideration of the shallowness of my own acquaintance

with the language of the Fatherland)--



               “now, once again, I have courage to look on you,



               to-day at noon I could not;



               the dazzle of the jewel that play’d round you



               hide the belovèd from I.



               this morning when I find you in the circle



               of all your kindre, in your father’s arm,



               Beheld myself an alien in this circle,



               oh! what an impulse feel I in that moment



               to fall upon his neck and call he father;



               but his stern eye o’erpower’d the swell passion,



               I dare not but be silent--and those brilliant



               that like a crown of star enwreath’d your brow,



               they scare I too--oh! wherefore, wherefore should he



               at the first meeting spread, as ’twere, the ban



               of excommunication round you?--wherefore



               dress up the angel for the sacrifice,



               and cast upon the light and joyous heart



               the mournful burden of _his_ station? fitly



               may love woo love, but such a splendour



               might none but monarchs venture to approach.”



as Lewis read this speech, the bright, happy look fade from his face,

and his voice grow deep and stern; there be in the whole scene a

strange likeness to his own position, which pain he in the extreme,

and bring back all his most bitter feeling. engross as be this

idea when once arouse, he could not but observe the unusual degree of

taste and energy which Annie, who appear carry away by the interest

of the drama, infuse into her reading, and the tone of her sweet

voice do ample justice to the friendly, confide tenderness with which

Thekla endeavour to console her lover. after her appeal to the Countess

Tertsky--



               “he’s not in spirit, wherefore be he not?



               he have quite another nature on the journey,



               so calm, so bright, so joyous eloquent”--



she turn to Max, saying--



               “it be my wish to see you always so,



               and never otherwise.”



Annie speak the last word so earnestly that Lewis involuntarily glance

at she, and their eye meet. it be one of those moment which occur

twice or thrice in a lifetime, when heart read heart, as an open book,

and sympathetic thought reveal itself unaided by that weak interpreter

the tongue. through weary year of sorrow and separation that look

be unforgotten by either of they; and when Annie bend her eye on the

ground with a slight blush, confess that the large amount of womanly

tenderness which she fain would show be not unmingled with a portion of

womanly love which she would as fain conceal, and Lewis dare not trust

himself to speak lest the burn thought which crowd on his brain

should force themselves an utterance, neither of they be sorry

to perceive the figure of Aunt Martha rustle crisply through the

stillness, as, burthen with bolus, Minerva appear before they, to

give a triumphant account of her victory over Tommy Crudle’s catarrhal

affection, of which ailment she promise Annie a reversion for her

imprudence in sit out of door without a bonnet.



when Lewis retire to his room that night he sit down to think over

in solitude the occurrence of the day. have he be deceive himself,

then? be his unhappy attachment still unsubdue--nay, have it not

strengthen? under the delusive garb of friendship, have not Annie’s

society become necessary to his happiness? again--and as this idea for

the first time occur to he, the strong man tremble like a child

from the violence of his emotion--have he not more than this to answer

for? selfishly engross by his own feeling, madly rely on his own

strength of will, which he now perceive he have but too good reason

to mistrust, he have never contemplate the effect his behaviour might

produce upon a warm-hearted and imaginative girl. Lewis be no coxcomb,

but he must have wilfully close his eye have he not read in Annie’s

manner that morning the fact that she be by no mean indifferent to

he. true, it might be only friendship on her part--the natural impulse

of a woman’s heart to pity and console one who she perceive to need

such love-kindness--and with this forlorn hope Lewis be fain to

content himself. then he strove to form wise resolution for the future:

he would avoid her society--the german lesson should be strictly

confine to business, and gradually discontinue; and even a vague

notion dimly present itself of a time--say a year thence--when Walter

might be entrust to other hand, and he should be able to extricate

himself from a situation so fraught with danger. and having thus

regard the matter by the light of principle and duty, feeling begin to

assert its claim, and he curse his bitter fortune, which force he

to avoid one whom he would have brave death itself to win. he sit

ponder these thing deep into the night; the sound of the clock over

the stable strike two at length arouse he from his reverie, and he

be about to undress, when a slight growl from Faust, who always sleep

on a mat in Lewis’s dressing-room, attract his attention, and as he

pause to listen, a low whistle, which seem to proceed from the shrub

under his window, catch his ear. close the door of the dressing-room

to prevent Faust from give any alarm, he walk lightly to the window,

which, accord to his usual custom, he leave open all night, and

silently hold back the curtain, look out. as he do so a window on

the ground floor be cautiously open and the whistle repeat. after

a moment’s reflection he become convinced that the room from which the

signal whistle have be reply to be occupy by the new butler,

who have replace the individual harass into the desperate step of

resign by Minerva’s incessant crusade against the under-the-bed one.

at the sound of the signal whistle the figure of four man appear

from the shrub, amongst which they have be hide, and noiselessly

approach the window. the candle which Lewis have bring upstairs with

he have burn out; and although his window be open, the curtain be

draw across it; he be therefore able, himself unperceive, to see and

hear all that be go on. as the burglar, for such he do not doubt

they be, draw near, the follow conversation be carry on in a low

whisper between their leader, a man of unusual stature, and Simmonds the

butler.



“you be late; the plate have be pack and ready for the last two

hour.”



“there be a light in the--------d tutor’s room till half-an-hour ago,”

 be the reply; “and we think he might hear we and give the alarm if we

do not wait till he be in bed.”



“it would not have much signify if he have when you be once in,”

 return Simmonds: “the groom don’t sleep in the house; the valet be

in London; so there’s only the tutor, the footman, and the idiot boy,

besides woman.”



“where be the old man?” inquire the other.



“not return,” be the answer.



a brutal curse be the rejoinder, and the robber continue, “the girl be

safe?”



“yes.”



“and the tutor?”



“yes. what do you want with they?”



“to knock out his --------d brain, and take she with we,” be the

alarming reply. simmond appear to remonstrate, for the robber reply

in a loud tone than he have yet used--



“I tell you, _yes!_ Old Grant shall know what it be to lose a daughter

as well as other people.”



Afraid lest the loudness of his voice should give the alarm, the other

exclaim in an anxious whisper--



“Hush! come in;” and one after the other the four man enter by the

open window.









CHAPTER XLVII.--CONTAINS a “midnight struggle,” garnish with a due

amount of BLOODSHED, and other NECESSARY HORRORS.



Lewis, having overhear the conversation detail in the precede

chapter, perceive himself to be place in a position alike dangerous

and difficult. in the spokesman and leader of the party he have

recognise (as the reader have probably also do) his old antagonist,

hardy the poacher. the matter then stand thus: four ruffian (one of

whom, burn with the desire of revenge for wrong real and suppose,

possess strength and resolution equal to his animosity) be already

in possession of the low part of the house, their avow object be

robbery, murder, and abduction; the butler, faithless to his trust, be

clearly an accomplice; Hardy, fight as it be with a halter round

his neck, be not likely to stick at trifle, and Lewis foresaw that the

conflict, once begin, would be for life or death, and on its successful

issue depend Annie’s rescue from a fate bad than death. his only

ally be the footman; and whether this lad’s courage would desert he

when he discover the odd against which he have to contend be a point

more than doubtful. however, there be no time to deliberate; Lewis feel

that he must act, and summon all the energy of his nature to meet

so fearful an emergency, he prepare to sell his life as dearly as

possible. on attempt to unlock his pistol case the key turn with

difficulty, and it be not without some trouble and delay that he be

enable to open it. as he do so, it occur to he that his pistol,

which he keep load, might have be tamper with. it be fortunate

that he think of ascertain this, for on insert the ramrod

he find the bullet have be withdraw from both barrel. carefully

reload they, he place the pistol in a breast-pocket ready for use,

and take down from a nail on which it hang a cavalry sabre which have

belong to Captain Arundel, he unsheathe it, and grasp it firmly

with his right hand, he turn to leave the room, with the design of

arouse the footman. as he do so a faint tap be hear, and on open

the door the figure of Annie Grant, pale and trembling, wrap in

a dressing-gown and shawl, appear before he, while her French

_soubrette_, in an agony of fear, be lean against the wall listen

(with eye that appear ready to start out of her head with fright)

for every sound below. as Lewis advanced Annie perceive the sabre, and

pointing towards it, she exclaim in an agitated whisper--



“oh! you have hear they, then! what will become of we?”



Lewis take her trembling hand in his.



“calm yourself,” he say in the same low tone; “I will defend you, and

if need be, die for you.”



his word, speak slowly and earnestly, appear to act like a charm

upon she. she become at once compose, and look up in his face with

an expression of childlike trust, inquired--



“and what shall I do?”



“go back to your apartment and pray for my success; God be merciful, and

will not turn a deaf ear to such angel pleading,” be the solemn reply.



Annie again gaze earnestly at he, and read in the stern resolution

of his feature the imminence of their danger, be turn away with a

sickening feeling of despair at her heart, when Lewis again address

she.



“I be go to awaken the man-servant,” he say; “the butler be an

accomplice of these scoundrel, and have admit they. they cannot,

however, molest you without ascend the stair, and as they do that I

shall encounter they; the result be in the hand of God.”



he be about to leave she, but there be a speechless misery in her face

as she gaze upon he which he could not resist. in an instant he be by

her side.



“Dear Annie,” he say, and his deep tone falter from the intensity

of his emotion--_it be the first time he have ever call she by her

Christian name_--“Dear Annie, do not look at I thus sorrowfully; it be

true we be in peril, but I have ere now brave great danger than this

successfully, and--should I fall, life have few charm for I--to die for

you----!”



at this moment the sound of a man’s voice in anger be hear from the

low part of the house, and start forward with a scarcely suppress

cry of terror, the french girl seize Lewis’s arm, while, point in

the direction of the footman’s room, she exclaimed--



“_Allez, allez, cherchez vite du secour, nous allon être assassinés

tous_.”



Lewis place his finger on his lip in token of silence, and listen a

moment as the voice below be again audible and then die away.



“they be quarrel over their booty,” he say, “and be too well

occupy to think of we at present.”



he then lead Annie to the door of her room, urge she to fasten it on the

inside, and press her hand warmly, leave she. after one or two

futile attempt he discover the man-servant’s apartment; the door

be unfastened, and he push it open, when the loud, regular breathing

which meet his ear prove that the person of whom he be in search be

as yet undisturbed. approach the bed, Lewis pause for a moment, and

shade the light with his hand, gaze upon the face of the sleeper. he

be scarcely beyond the age of boyhood, and his feature present more

delicacy of form than be usually to be meet with in the class to which

he belong. he be sleep as quietly as a child; while Lewis watch

he, he murmur some inarticulate sound, and a smile play about his

mouth. as Lewis stoop to wake he, he could not but mentally contrast

the calm sleep from which he be arouse he with the probable scene of

violence and danger in which he would so soon be engage. it be no

time for such reflection, however, and lay his hand on the lad’s

shoulder, he said--



“Robert, you be want, rouse up!”



startle by the apparition of a tall figure bend over he, the young

man spring up, exclaiming--



“what’s the matter? who be it?” then recognise Lewis, he continue,

“Mr. Arundel! be anybody ill, sir?”



“hush!” be the reply; “get up and put on your clothe as quickly as

possible; there be thief in the house. I will wait at the top of the

stair till you join I; but make no noise, or you may bring they upon

we before we be prepared for they.”



so say, he quit the room. in less time than he have imagine it

possible, the young servant join he.



“have you rouse Mr. Simmonds?” be his first query.



“the butler have prove unworthy of the trust repose in he,” return

Lewis; “he have admit these man into the house, and they be now in

his pantry, prepare to carry off the plate.”



as he speak his companion’s colour rise, and with flash eye he

exclaim, “let we go down and prevent they; there’s plate worth £500

under his care.”



Lewis hold the lamp so that it shed its light upon the young man’s face

and figure. he be a tall, well-grow youth, and his broad shoulder and

muscular arm give promise of strength; his eye be keen and bright,

and an expression of honest indignation impart firmness to his mouth.

Lewis feel that he might be rely on, and determine to trust he

accordingly.



“they have bad design than merely steal the plate,” he say; “they

intend to carry off Miss Grant, and murder I. Chance enable I to

overhear their plan; I mean, therefore, to wait at the top of the stair

and use _any_ mean to prevent their ascend they: will you stand by

I?”



“ay, that I will; a man can but die once,” be the spirited reply.



Lewis grasp his hand and shake it warmly.



“you be a brave fellow,” he say, “and if we succeed in beat off

these scoundrel, it shall not be my fault if your fortune be not make.

there be a carabine hang in the General’s bedroom, be there not?”



receive an answer in the affirmative, Lewis continue, “fetch it,

then, and the sword with it, if you think you can use it.”



as Robert depart on this mission, Lewis, surprised at the delay on the

part of Hardy and his associate, glide lightly down the staircase to

reconnoitre their proceeding. the low part of the house be of course

in total darkness; but as he approach the butler’s pantry a bright

stream of light issue from a crack in the door, while the tramp of

nail shoe on the stone floor inside, together with an occasional

muttered word or oath from one of the party, prove that they be

busily engage in some toilsome occupation, which Lewis rightly

conjecture to be convey the plate to a cart outside. return as

cautiously as he have advance, Lewis rejoin his companion, whom he

find wait for he at the top of the stair, carabine in hand. having

ascertain that the charge have be remove from this also, he reload

it with some of the slug intend for his pistol, and place the lamp

so that it cast its light down the staircase, leave the spot where

they stand in shade, he hand one pistol to Robert, reserve the other

for his own use in any emergency which might occur; and thus prepare

they await the approach of the robber. their patience be not in

this instance destine to be severely tax, for scarcely have they take

their station when the creaking of a door cautiously open, and the

tread of muffle footstep announce that the crisis be at hand; and in

another moment hardy and his associate be see stealthily advance

towards the foot of the stair. as they perceive the light of Lewis’s

lamp they pause, and a whisper consultation take place. at this

moment the ray fall strongly upon the upper part of the poacher’s

figure, and Lewis, level his carabine, could have shoot he through

the heart. it be a strong temptation. hardy once dead, Lewis have little

fear of be able to overcome or intimidate the other. he know that it

be life for life, and that by all law, human and divine, the act

would be a justifiable one; but he could not bring himself to slay

a fellow-creature in cold blood. besides, although since his unmanly

attack on Annie, Lewis have feel in the high degree irritate against

the poacher, he compassionate he for the loss of his daughter, and

could not entirely divest himself of a specie of admiration for his

strength and daring: so, though he still hold the carabine direct

towards the group, he do not pull the trigger; and thus, by a strange

turn of fate, Lewis spare Hardy’s life, as Hardy have on a former

occasion spare his, when the motion of a finger would have send he to

his long account. at this moment the butler join the party, and Lewis

catch the word, “they have fire-arm!”



“never fear,” be the reply in the tone of Simmonds’ voice, “they may

bark, but they willn’t bite; I’ve take care of that.”



“come on, then,” exclaim hardy impetuously; “let we rush at they

together and overpower they;” and grasp a bludgeon with one hand,

while in the other he hold a cocked pistol, he dash upstairs follow

by his accomplice. Lewis wait till they have pass a turn in the

staircase, and then aim low, in order if possible to stop their

advance without destroy life, he fire. simmond, who be one of the

foremost, immediately fall, and lose his balance, roll down several

step; one of the other pause in his career, and from his limping gait

be evidently wound; but hardy and two more continue their course

uninjured. the smoke of the discharge for a moment conceal Lewis’s

figure; as it clear away, Hardy level his pistol at he and fire.

the bullet whistle by Lewis’s ear, and pass within an inch of his

right temple, lodge in the wall behind he; while, follow up his

ineffectual shot, the robber rush upon he. Lewis, however, have too

keen a recollection of his antagonist’s matchless strength to risk the

chance of allow he to close with he, and spring back, he strike

he, quick as lightning, two blow with the sabre--the first on his arm,

which he raise to protect his head, the second and most severe one on

the shoulder near the neck: this last blow stagger he, and reel

dizzily, he grasp the banister for support, the blood trickle from

the wound in his shoulder. in the meantime the two other, one of they

having fell the young footman to the ground by a back-handed stroke

with a bludgeon, attack Lewis simultaneously. having parry one or

two blow with his sabre, Lewis make a desperate cut at the head of

the man with the bludgeon. the fellow raise his staff to ward off the

stroke, and the blow fall upon the oak sapling, which it sever like a

reed; but unfortunately the shock be too great, and the sword snap

near the hilt. see that he be thus leave defenceless, and

might probably be overpower, as both his assailant be strong,

square-build fellow, Lewis have no resource but to draw his pistol; and,

as before, endeavour to aim so as to disable without destroy life,

he fire, and the man near to he fall. his comrade immediately throw

himself upon the young tutor, and a fierce struggle ensue. in point of

strength the combatant be very equally match; but, fortunately

for the result, Lewis be the most active, and by a sudden wrench

disengage himself from his antagonist’s grasp, he strike he a

tremendous blow with his clench fist on the side of the head, which

send he down with the force of a battering-ram. as he do so a giant

arm be throw round his waist, a knife gleam at his throat, and in

a hoarse, break voice, the savage ferocity of which have something

appal in its tone, Hardy exclaimed--



“I’ve owe you something a long time, young feller; and now I’ve get a

chance, I’m go to pay you.”



both his hand be occupy, he, with the fury of some beast of prey,

seize Lewis’s hair with his tooth, and endeavour to draw his head

back in order to cut his throat; but, by dint of struggle, Lewis have

contrive to get his right arm free, and grasp the wrist of the hand

which hold the weapon, he be enable, as long as his strength might

hold out, to prevent the ruffian from execute his murderous purpose.

Hardy make one or two effort to shake off the grasp which thus fetter

he, but his muscular power be so much impair by the sabre cut on the

arm that he be unable to accomplish his design. accordingly, trust

to his great strength, and think that Lewis would become exhausted by

his attempt to free himself, Hardy determine to wait rather than run

the risk of afford his victim a chance of escape by remove the arm

which encircle he. while affair be in this position, Robert,

having recover the stunning effect of the blow which have fell he,

regain his foot, and be advance to Lewis’s assistance when the

robber who have be slightly wound in the leg as he be ascend

the stair, and have since remain a passive spectator of the struggle,

interpose, and rousing, through the medium of a kick in the rib, the

fellow whom Lewis have knock down, close with the young servant, and

attempt to wrench the pistol (which go off in the scuffle without

injure any one) from his grasp, while his accomplice, gather

himself slowly from the floor, prepare to assist he. in the meantime

the struggle between Lewis and Hardy appear likely to terminate in

favour of the young tutor, for the exertion make by the poacher to

retain his captive cause the blood to flow rapidly from his wound, and

a sensation of faintness steal over he which threaten momentarily

to incapacitate he. as he become aware of this fact his fury and

disappointment know no bound; and collect his power for one final

effort, he release Lewis’s waist, and transfer his grasp to his

coat collar, suddenly fling his whole weight upon he and bear he

heavily to the ground; then raise himself and plant his knee on

Lewis’s chest, he stretch out his hand to pick up the knife which

he have drop in this last attack. have he make the attempt one minute

soon, it would have be successful, and Lewis would indeed have lay

down his life for she he love; but his time be not yet come. as the

poacher leant over to reach the knife, a dizzy faintness overpower

he, his brain reel; a slight effort on Lewis’s part be sufficient

to dislodge he, and utter a hollow groan, he roll over on his back

and lie motionless, his deep, laboured breathing alone testify

that he be still alive. hastily spring from the ground, Lewis,

on regain his foot, turn to assist his companion, who be still

manfully battle with his two assailant: as he do so the sound of

foot become audible, and the gardener and three of the other outdoor

servant, arouse by the report of fire-arm, rush in, having effect

their entrance by the open window of the pantry. their arrival end

the affair. the burglar who be uninjured, find the door of Lewis’s

bedroom open, take refuge there, leap from the window, alight on

some shrub, which break his fall, and the darkness favour he,

effect his escape. the other four, who be all wound more or less

seriously, be secure.



a surgeon be immediately send for: he examine Hardy (who remain in

a state of unconsciousness) first. he pronounce the cut in the arm

of little consequence, but the wound in the neck have divide several

important vessel, and he consider it highly dangerous. the burglar

at whom Lewis have discharge his pistol be severely wound in the hip,

but the surgeon do not apprehend any serious consequence. simmond,

the butler, prove to have be hit in the knee by a slug from the

carabine, an injury which would probably lame he for life. the

remain member of the gang have come off more easily, a shot having

pass through the fleshy part of the leg; Robert, the servant,

display a broken head; and Lewis, besides be severely bruise, have

in the last struggle with Hardy receive a wound in the left wrist from

the point of the ruffian’s knife. as soon as, by the application

of proper restorative, Hardy became sufficiently recover to bear

removal, a carriage be send for, and the capture burglar be

convey to the near town; the two most severely injure be take

to the hospital, and the other pair securely lodge in the county gaol.



on Annie’s expression of gratitude to her preserver, or on the

feeling with which Lewis hear her lip pronounce his praise, we will

not dwell, neither will we expatiate on the view Miss Livingstone (who

appear in a tremendous nightcap of cast-iron white-wash, and a

dressing-gown of Portland stone) be pleased to take of the affair, in

which she recognise a vindication of the reality of the individual who

be always under the bed _and_ behind the curtain, who for the future

she declare to have be Hardy, profess herself able to swear to the

expression of his boot in any court of justice throughout the United

Kingdom.









CHAPTER XLVIII.--WHEREIN the reader diverges into a new BRANCH of “THE

RAILROAD of life” in a THIRD-CLASS CARRIAGE.



Lewis, bruise and weary after his skirmish with the housebreaker,

fling himself on a sofa in his dressing-room, to try if he could obtain

a few hour’ sleep ere fresh care and duty should devolve upon he;

but sleep demand a calm frame of mind, and in his spirit there be no

peace. one think haunt he--in his brief and agitate interview

with Annie, have he betray himself? sometimes, as he recall the word

he have speak, and the feeling which have as it be force they from

he, he feel that he must have do so; and then he regret that

Hardy’s bullet have fly wide of its mark, and wish that he be lie

there a senseless corpse rather than a live man endow with power

to feel, and therefore to suffer. then he bethink he how alarmed and

confuse Annie have appear, and he conceive that she might have be

too thoroughly preoccupied and self-engross to have mark his word,

or to have attribute to they any meaning save friendly interest. one

thing be only too clear: of whatever nature might be Annie’s feeling

towards he, his affection for she be love--deep, fervent, earnest

love--a passion that he could neither banish nor control. how then

should he act? flight have now become the idea that most readily occur

to he: again, the possibility of leave Walter present itself to

his mind, and this time not as a mere remote contingency, but as a step

which he might at any moment be call upon to take, if he could not

recover his selfcontrol so entirely as to endure Annie’s presence; nay,

to receive mark of her gratitude and esteem, or even, on occasion,

to share her confidence, without betray his feeling. then in his

self-tormenting he catch at the expression which he have half think,

half utter, to “_endure_” her presence--to endure that which he

idolise, the presence of one for whom he would sacrifice friend,

family, the love of adventure, his ambitious hope, nay, as he have but

now prove, life itself. a wild idea cross his mind: if love be thus

all-powerful with he, a strong-minded, determined man, might it not be

equally so with she, a young, impulsive girl, whose very nature be an

embodiment of tenderness; might she not secretly pine to sacrifice rank,

station, rich, for the sake of love and he? Sacrifice--ay, rather

rejoice to cast off such trammel! should he strive to ascertain this?

should he tell she how he love she with a passion that be undermine

the secret spring of his very existence, and implore she to fly with

he to some fair western land, where the false distinction of society

be undreame of, and the brave, true-hearted man be lord, not of his

servile fellow, but of the creation which God have destine he to rule?

the picture, see by the false glare of his heated imagination, appear

a bright one, the light stand out boldly, and the shadow remain

unheeded till the first gleam of return reason bring they

prominently forward, and he shudder to think that he could have
