entertain for a moment a project so completely at variance with every

principle of honour and of duty. thus feverish alike in mind and body,

he toss restlessly on his couch, till at length, thoroughly exhaust,

he fall into a deep sleep, and dream bright dream of happy love, to

make the stern reality appear yet dark and more drear on wake.



on his return to Broadhirst, General Grant express his most

unqualified admiration at the gallant defence of his house, property,

and daughter (we quote his own “table of precedence”) by Lewis and the

man-servant. on the former he bestow a sword (present to he in

bygone day by some indian potentate) to replace the weapon break in

the struggle, together with a handsomely-bind copy of the “Wellington

Despatches”; the latter he reward by promotion to the post of butler,

_vice_ Simmonds (in a fair way to be) transport, together with a

douceur of twenty pound; which piece of good fortune so elated the

youthful Robert that he publicly declare he should like to have his

head break every night, and wish the house might be rob regularly

twice a week till further order. the wounded man recover rapidly,

with the exception of Hardy, whose case assume a very alarming

character: owe to the state of his constitution, impair by a course

of intemperance, to which, since his escape from prison, he have give

himself over, erysipelas supervene, and in a few day his life

be despair of. on receive this intelligence Lewis ride over to

H----------, and call at the hospital, request to be allow to see

the man whose life he have be the involuntary instrument of shortening.

the permission be readily accord, and he be conduct along several

passage to the room, or rather cell, for it be little else, in which,

for the purpose of security as well as to separate he from the other

inmate of the establishment, the burglar have be place. as soon

as Lewis have enter the door be close and fasten on the outside.

noiselessly approach the truckle bed on which Hardy lay, the young

tutor pause as his glance fall upon the prostrate figure of his

former antagonist. stretch at full length upon the couch, his arm and

shoulder swathe in bandage, and his muscular throat and broad, hairy

chest partially uncover, he look even more gigantic than when in

an erect posture: his face be pale as death, and an unnatural darkness

beneath the skin betoken to any one accustom to such appearance the

speedy approach of the destroyer; while a small hectic spot of colour

on the centre of each cheek give evidence of the inward fever which be

consume he. when Lewis approach the bed his eye be closed, and

his deep breathing at first lead to the belief that he be asleep; that

this be not the case, however, soon become apparent. open his

eye, he accidentally encounter those of Lewis fix upon he with an

expression of mingled pity and remorse: as their glance meet hardy give

a start of surprise, and gaze at he with a scowl which prove that his

feeling of animosity against Lewis be still unabated; while a puzzle

look evince that his mental power be so much weaken that he

doubt whether the figure he beheld be real or a creation of his

morbid fancy. advance to the bedside, Lewis break the silence by

inquire whether he suffer much pain. as he begin to speak, the

confused look disappear from the sick man’s countenance, and glare

at he with an expression of impotent rage, he exclaim in a low,

hoarse voice--



“so you’re come to look upon your handiwork, be you? I hope you like

it!”



“I be come to tell you that I be sorry the blow I strike you in

self-defence should have produce such disastrous consequence, and to

ask your forgiveness, in case the mean employ for your restoration to

health should prove ineffectual,” reply Lewis.



“restore my health!” repeat Hardy bitterly. “do you mean that you

expect these doctor can cure I? do you think these wound, that burn

like hell-fire, can be heal by their plaster and bandage? I tell you

no! you have do your work effectually this time, and I be a die man.

you want I to forgive you, do you? if my curse could wither you where

you stand, I would and _do_ curse you! if priest’ tale be true, and

there be a heaven and a hell, and by forgive you I could reach heaven,

I still would curse you, in the hope that by so do I might drag you

down to hell with I.”



the vehemence with which he utter this malediction completely

exhausted he, and fall back on the pillow he lie with closed eye,

his laboured breathing afford the only proof that he be still alive.

throw himself upon a chair by the bedside, Lewis sat wrap in

painful thought. the reflection that hatred to he for act which

circumstance have force he to commit might cause the unhappy be

before he to die impenitent, and that he might thus be instrumental to

the destruction both ot his body and soul, be distress to he in the

extreme; and yet how to bring he to a well frame of mind be not easy

to decide. at length, follow out his own train of thought, he ask

abruptly--



“Hardy, why do you hate I so bitterly?”



thus accost, the poacher unclose his eye, and fix they with a

pierce glance upon the face of his questioner, as though he would read

his very soul. apparently disappoint in his object, for Lewis meet his

gaze with the calm self-possession of conscious rectitude, he answer

surlily--



“why do you come here to torment I with foolish question? it be enough

that I hate you with just cause--and you know that it be so.

I hate you now, I shall hate you die, and I shall hate you after

death, if there be a hereafter. now go. if by stay here you think

to persuade or entrap I into say I forgive you, you only waste your

time.”



“listen to I, Hardy,” return Lewis, speak calmly and impressively.

“you be, as you truly say, a die man. in this life we shall probably

never meet again. the reality of a future life you appear to doubt: I

believe in it; and I believe that your condition there may be affect

by your die with such feeling in your heart as you have now

express. it be therefore worth while to discuss this matter, and see

whether you have such just cause to hate I as you imagine.”



as Hardy make no reply, Lewis continue: “it be true that on a former

occasion I secure your capture when perhaps I be step beyond my

regular path of duty to do so; but in this last affair I merely act

in self-defence, as I overheard from my open window your scheme for my

destruction. you discharge a pistol at I ere I attack you: have the

ball go half an inch more to the right I should have be a dead

man. whatever may be your fault, you be brave; and that quality alone

should prevent your bearing malice against one who meet you in fair, open

fight. it be a game for life and death, and it be unjust to hate I for

win it.”



“boy, you will madden I,” exclaim hardy passionately, raise himself

on his elbow as he speak, though the pain the action cause he force

a groan from his compress lip. “do you suppose I care for your paltry

blow? if they have not finish I, brandy or my own hand would soon

have do so; for life have long be a curse to I, and have become

unbearable since--may the torment I shall soon endure, if there be a

hell, fall upon you for it!--since you and the title scoundrel, your

accomplice, steal my daughter from I.”



“I!” exclaim Lewis in astonishment. “do you imagine I to have have any

share in that wickedness? why, man, I never see your daughter save on

two occasion; and on the second of these I warn she--unfortunately

without effect--against the design of the villain who betray she.”



as he speak hardy gaze eagerly at he, and when he cease, exclaimed--



“tell I when and where be it that you do this?”



“it be on the morning after the electioneering ball at Broadhurst. I

be shoot with the gamekeeper--meet your daughter by accident in the

grass field by the larch plantation--and witness she parting with

Lord Bellefield, I take the opportunity of tell she his true name and

character, and warn she against his probable design. but, unluckily,

she have observe a disagreement between we on the previous evening,

and suppose I to be actuate by malicious motive, discredit my

assertion.”



“you be not deceive I?” question hardy eagerly. “you could not,

dare not, do so now!”



“you do not know I, or you would not doubt my word. I have speak the

simple truth,” return Lewis coldly.



“here!” continue Hardy, produce from beneath the pillow a small Bible

which the chaplain have leave with he: “you tell I you believe in this

book. will you swear upon it that you be not try to deceive I?”



Lewis raise the book reverently to his lip, and kiss it, take the

require oath. Hardy watch he with a scrutinise gaze, and when he

have conclude, hold out his hand, saying--



“I have wrong you deeply, Mr. Arundel, and must ask--what! never

think again to ask at the hand of man--your forgiveness. I have seek

your life, sir, as the wild beast seek his prey; and chance, on

one occasion, and your own courage and address on other, have alone

preserve it.”



he then go on to relate how, his suspicion having be excite by

hint from the neighbour, he have learn that his daughter be in the

habit of meet some gentleman by stealth. how he watch for this

person constantly, without success, till the day after the great party

at Broadhurst, when, lie conceal in the larch plantation, he

have be attract by the sound of voice, and creep beneath the

underwood, have witness, though not near enough to overhear what

pass, the interview between Lewis and his daughter, when he naturally

conclude the young tutor to be the individual against whom he have be

caution. he then go on to relate that the opportune arrival of the

gamekeeper have alone prevent he from shoot the suppose libertine,

but that he have determine on his destruction, and that his subsequent

capture by Lewis and the General have alone hinder he from execute

his design. it be not till after his escape from H--------

gaol that he first hear Lord Bellefield’s name couple with that of his

daughter, which information complicate the affair; but still feel

convince that Lewis be guilty, either as principal or accessory, he

join in the scheme for rob Broadhurst, in order to be revenge on

the young tutor, as well as on General Grant, against whom he have

long nourished feeling of animosity, on account of his poaching

persecution.



his penitence for the wrong he have do he by his unjust suspicion

be so sincere and spontaneous, that Lewis imagine he recognise, amid

the ruin of a naturally generous disposition, that “seed of the soul”

 which remain in almost every nature, however the rank growth of evil

passion uncontrolle may have check its development. take advantage

of an expression which Hardy use, that “he thank God he have not

add to his other sin the murder of one who have seek to befriend his

child,” his companion observed--



“you say you thank God for preserve you from an additional crime:

now, do not the fact of your involuntarily make use of that form of

speech tend to convince you that the belief in a God and a future state

be natural to the mind of man?”



Hardy seem strike by the force of this remark, and Lewis, pursue

the subject, have the satisfaction of perceive that he have excite the

wound man’s interest, and ere he quit he he obtain his promise

to listen to the exhortation of the chaplain, whose advance he have

hitherto angrily repulse. please with the result of his visit, Lewis

on his way home call upon the clergyman who fulfil the duty of

chaplain to the hospital, and mention to he Hardy’s improve frame

of mind, beg he to see he again as soon as possible, to which

request the chaplain willingly accede.



three day after this interview Lewis receive a note from this

gentleman thank he for his hint, and inform he that its result

have be as satisfactory as in such a case be possible. hardy appear

sincerely penitent, willing to embrace and anxious to profit by the

truth of religion, as far as his weakened faculty enable he to

apprehend they. he add that he be sink fast, and have express

the great desire to see Lewis again before he die, as he have

some request to make to he. on the receipt of this information Lewis

immediately set out for H--------.



a great alteration have take place in Hardy’s appearance in those three

day. his cheek have become still more hollow, the unnatural brightness

of his eye be replace by a dull, leaden look, and the hectic colour

have fade to the pale, ashy hue of approach dissolution, whilst the

hoarse, deep tone of his voice be reduce almost to a whisper through

weakness. but the most remarkable change be in the expression of his

feature; the sullen scowl, which betoken a spirit at war alike with

itself and other, have give place to a look of calm resignation; there

be indeed trace of bodily pain and mental anguish about the mouth,

but the upper part of the face be in complete repose. Lewis gaze upon

he with deep interest, and the idea suggest itself that thus might

have appear the demoniac when the word of power have go forth, “hold

thy peace, and come out of he.” nor be the comparison inapt, for if

ever the mind of man be possess by an evil spirit, that of Hardy have

be so by the demon of revenge. as the die man perceive his approach

his feature light up.



“I know you would come, Mr. Arundel,” he say. “I feel that! should not

die without see you again.”



“do you suffer much pain now, Hardy?” inquire Lewis kindly.



“Scarcely any since six o’clock this morning, sir,” be the reply; “but

I know what that mean--that’s mortification come on. I’ve see man

die from sabre wound before now. I be a soldier once--at least I be

farri to a troop of cavalry, which be much the same thing; but this

be not what I want to say to you.” he pause from exhaustion, and

point to a glass contain some strengthen cordial. Lewis hold

it to his parched lip; having drink a portion of it, he appear

considerably revive.



“I be go fast,” he resume, “and must not waste the minute that

remain. you have treat I with kindness, sir--one of the few who have

ever do so; you be a bold foe and a warm-hearted friend, and that be

a character I understand and can trust. moreover, you tell I you show

poor Jane” (as he mention his daughter’s name tear stand in his eye

and his breath come short and fast) “her danger, and strove to warn she

against the villain who have wrong she, and this show you be a good

man; therefore I be go to ask you to do I a favour. when I be dead,

I want you to find out Jane and tell she whatever you may think well to

induce she to leave this man. and when she hear that I’m dead, if she

seem to feel it very deep and take on about it--which likely enough she

will, for she do care for I once--you may tell she that I forgive she

before I die. I never think to do so, for she have finish what her

mother begin; between they they’ve first make I the devil I have be,

and then--break my heart.” he pause, and when he have sufficiently

recover breath, continue, “when I marry her mother, five-and-twenty

year ago, I be a different man from whatever you’ve know I. I’d be

bring up to my father’s trade of a blacksmith, and work steadily at

it till I be able to lay by a fair sum of money, besides keep the

old man as long as he be alive. however, in the village where we live

be a farmer, well-to-do in the world, and his daughter be far the

prettiest girl in those part; she’d have a good education, and give

herself air like a lady, and look down upon a rough young fellow like

I; but I bear it patiently, for I love she, and determine I’d never

marry anybody but she. for a long time she would not look at I, but I

persevere; any man that come a-court she I pick a quarrel with and

thrash. I find many way of make myself handy to the old man, she

father, and somehow she get use to I like, and grow less scornful; and

just then a sister of my father’s, who have be housekeeper at

Broadhurst, die and leave I £300, and I’d save about £200 more, and

the old man want help to manage his farm. and the long and short of

the matter be I married Harriet Wylde, take a farm next her father’s,

and give up blacksmithing.



“for four year I be as happy as man could be; everything seem to

prosper with I. my wife have one child, a girl; a proud man be I when

she be first place in my arm, but have I know what be to be her fate

I would have smother she in her cradle! there be a young gentleman

live near we--his father be a rich baronet--I have be accustom to

break in horse for the son, and when I take the farm we use to shoot

together. he be a frank, generous-hearted man, and treat I like a

friend and equal. on our shooting expedition he would often come and

lunch at my house; on one occasion he bring his young brother with

he. this young fellow have just return from Italy, and bring foreign

manner and foreign vice with he. my wife be still very good-look,

like poor Jane, but handsomer; and this heartless villain covet her

beauty. I know not what art he use; I suspect nothing, see nothing,

but one evening on my return my home be desolate. I obtain trace

of the fugitive--he have take she to a seaport town in the south of

England, mean to embark for France--I follow they, and in the open

street I meet he; the bystander interfere between we, or I should have

slay he where he stand. he be take to an inn, where he keep his bed

for some week from the effect of the punishment I have administer to

he. I be drag off to prison; the law which suffer he to rob I

of she whom I prize more dearly than house and good punish I for

chastise the scoundrel with six month’ imprisonment. I consort with

thief, poacher, and other refuse of society; and in my madness to

obtain revenge upon the class which have injure I, I listen to their

specious argument till I become the curse to myself and other which

you, sir, have know I. well, society send I to school, and society

have have the benefit of the lesson that be teach I. I come out of

gaol a bad and well-nigh a desperate man, to learn that my wife have

return to her father’s house and die, give birth to a boy. in my

anger I refuse to acknowledge the child, but the old man take care of

it. Time pass on: the elder of the two brother quarrel with his

father and die abroad, the young one marry; but God visit he for

his sin. his wife see by accident in an old newspaper an account of my

trial for the assault; the shock bring on a premature confinement;

she also die in childbirth, and the child remain an idiot. yes! you

start, but you have guess rightly--the boy to whom you be tutor be

the son of the man who wrong I. the way of God be very wonderful:

have the boy possess his proper sense you might never have come here,

and I might not now be lie on my death-bed.”



again Hardy break off from weakness, and again Lewis administer the

cordial to he and wipe the cold dew from his brow.



“little more remain to tell,” he add after a few minute’ pause; “and

’tis well that it be so, for death come on apace. I do not fear to

die; I have long wish myself dead, life be such deep misery, yet

now I should be glad to live, that I might undo some of the evil I have

cause. since I see you last I have feel more like my former self than I

have ever do from the time my wife leave I. poor Harriet! do you think

we shall meet in the world of spirit, Mr. Arundel?”



“these be thing God alone know,” reply Lewis gravely. “he have not

see fit to reveal to live man the secret of the grave!” after a

short silence, in which Hardy appear to be collect strength to

finish his relation, he continued--



“after my release from the prison I take to drink to banish

reflection. drinking be a vice which bring all other in its train. I

soon fall into bad company, become involved in debt; and at last, in a

drunken fit, enlist in the----th dragoon, my height attract the

notice of a recruiting party from that regiment. I serve ten year, at

the end of which time my wife’s father die and leave his little property

between the two child, with the exception of a sum to purchase my

discharge if I choose to come and take care of they. the confinement and

regularity of a soldier’s life do not suit I, and I avail myself

of the opportunity thus offer, return home, and live on a certain

income set apart for the maintenance and education of the child. this

be a fresh chance for I, and have I conduct myself properly I might

have yet know some peaceful year; but a craving for excitement

haunt I. I seek out some of my old companion, join a Chartist

association, take to habit of poach--and this have be the end of

it.”



“what become of the boy who be leave to your care?” inquire Lewis.

Hardy utter a low groan.



“that be another sin I have to answer for,” he say. “I never like the

child--I doubt whether it be mine, and the sight of it recall the

memory of my wrong; accordingly, I treat the boy harshly, and

he repay I by sullen disobedience; and yet there should have be

sympathy between we. he be brave even to rashness, and copy my vice

with an aptitude which prove his power of acquire well thing. by

the time he be thirteen he could set a snare, hit a bird on the wing,

thrash any boy of his own weight, and alas! drink, game, and swear as

well as I could myself. one night I have be drink he anger I, and

in my rage I strike he. for a moment he look as if he would return

the blow; but the folly of such an attempt seem to occur to he, and

he glance towards a knife which lie on the table; then his sister throw

her arm round he, and he refrain. he wait till she have go to

bed, sit sulkily without speak. when we be alone he look up

and ask I abruptly, ‘Father, be you sorry that you strike I that

blow?’ there be something in the boy’s manner that appeal to my

well feeling, and I be half inclined to own myself wrong, but a

false shame prevent I, and I angrily reply ‘that I would repeat the

blow if he give I any more of his impertinence.’ he look sternly at

I, and mutter, ‘that you shall never do,’ quit the room. from

that day to this I have never see he. my poor Jane, who be dotingly

fond of he, be break-hearted at his loss. she tell I he often

threaten to run away when I have treat he harshly, and that his

intention be to go to sea. I have no doubt he contrive to put it into

execution. perhaps if her brother have remain with she the poor girl

might not have leave her home so readily. God help I, my sin have

bring their own punishment!”



an attack of faintness here overpower he, of so severe a character

that Lewis think it advisable to summon assistance. when Hardy have

in some degree recover, Lewis, on consult his watch, find that

he must return without further delay; he therefore prepare to depart,

bid hardy farewell, and promise to see he again on the follow

day. the die man shake his head.



“there will be no to-morrow for I in this world,” he say; then

press Lewis’s hand, he add, “God bless you, Mr. Arundel; you have

do I more good by your kind word than your sword have do I evil;

nay, even for my death I thank you; for have I live on as I be I should

only have add crime to crime. you will remember your promise about

poor jane?”



Lewis repeat his willingness to do all in his power to carry out the

die man’s wish; and Hardy add, “it may be that the poor boy I tell

you of be still alive. if he should ever return, I should like he to

know that I have often grieve for my bad conduct to he. I have leave

a letter for you with the clergyman in case I have not see you,” he

continue; “it only contain the request I have now make, and one or two

other particular of less consequence; he will give it to you when I be

go.” he again press Lewis’s hand feebly, and close his eye, lay

more dead than alive.



as Lewis quit the room the surgeon meet he and inform he that it

be not probable Hardy would survive through the night, but promise

that every attention should be bestow upon he. lewis’s thought, as

he ride back to Broadhurst, naturally run upon the history of sin and

safe course be to look upon such thing as mystery to be regard

by the twilight of a patient faith, which wait trustfully till all that

now seem dark shall be make clear in the glorious brightness of the

perfect day.









chapter XLIX.--CONTAINS a PARADOX--LEWIS, when LEAST RESIGNED, display

the VIRTUE of resignation.



on the morning after his second visit to Hardy, Lewis receive a packet

General Grant be naturally by no mean of a suspicious disposition; the

position in which he be place give he irresponsible authority

over nearly every person with whom he come in contact, have render

he pompous and arbitrary; but although not a man of enlarged mind,

or possess much delicacy of perception, he be actuate by a strong

principle of justice. this attribute impart a degree of frankness

a person shake by artful insinuation, which will require long and

patient investigation to verify or disprove. in such a state of mind

as the General utter this reproach Lewis colour, and his compress

 he continue sternly, as with a start of horrified surprise the General

“I remain here for Walter’s sake, rely on my own fortitude to

help I Heaven, be the whole and simple truth.”



as he conclude, General Grant, whose brow have gradually relax during

to have be a very great trial, and I admire and respect you for so

completely justify the favourable opinion I have form of you; I

rely upon my willingness to assist you to the uttermost. may I inquire

your future plan?”



“I have form no plan,” return Lewis hurriedly. “when I leave your

house my only prospect be to begin life anew, with every hope that

render life endurable shut out from I for ever--I be grateful for your

offer, but must decline they. Henceforward I be likely to do little

single-handed. and now, ere I leave you, let I again thank you for the

courtesy you have uniformly show I--I expect justice at your hand,

you have add kindness also: we shall probably never meet again, but

the chance of life be strange, and should it ever be in my power to

return your benefit, you will not find I forgetful.”



he raise his hat as he speak, and turn to depart. General Grant

advance as if he would detain he, but check himself, he muttered--



“you shall hear from I--I will write to you at your banker’s;” and

Lewis bow and leave he.









CHAPTER L.--SHOWS how LEWIS come to a “dogged” determination, and be

make the shuttlecock of FATE.



“Walter, I be go to leave you,” observe Lewis in a quiet, gentle

voice.



Walter, who be seat on a low stool playing with Faust, continue his

amusement, merely reply carelessly, “be you?”



Lewis know from the nature of the answer that the sound but not the

sense of his communication have reach his poor pupil’s understanding,

and yet the apparent indifference of the remark pain he; it seem

as if all he love be fall away from he. he have determine that

it would be well for Walter not to be tell at once that he be leave

never to return, but to allow the truth gradually to dawn upon he,

after he have practically test his ability to do without he; still he

be anxious in some degree to prepare the poor boy’s mind to support the

severe grief which he fear his absence would occasion he. accordingly

he return to the attack.



“look at I, Walter,” he say. having catch his eye, he continue, “you

do not understand I, dear boy; I be go away--go to leave you for

a long time.”



“ay? how long a time? a week?” inquire Walter.



“a great many week,” return Lewis gravely, “and you must be very good

all the time, and do everything as you know I should wish you to do it

if I be here: do you understand I, and will you try?”



Walter nod assent, pause, and then ask, “what will Faust do; may

he stay with I?”



Lewis do not answer. give up Faust, the only thing that he have leave to

love he! could he make this sacrifice?



“because, if he may stay, I shall feel sure you will come back some

time or other; nobody can leave Faust and not come and see he again--at

least nobody who know he and love he as well as you and I do,”

 plead Walter, throw his arm round the dog’s neck.



I be inflict injury enough on the poor boy as it be, reflect Lewis

sorrowfully; I must not deny he this thing, which he have set his heart

upon. well, it only make the sacrifice the more complete. “Walter, will

you be happy if I leave Faust with you?” he inquire gently.



“oh, yes!” be the joyful reply, “quite happy till you come again.”



“then he shall stay,” resume Lewis; “remember he be your dog, I give

he to you.”



“yes, he be my dog,” repeat Walter gleefully; “only till you come back

again though, you know,” he add, gaze wistfully at Lewis.



Poor Lewis! his heart be full, he could not trust himself to speak;

this little incident have appeal to the affectionate side of his

nature, and all but unmanned he. he approach Walter, sweep back the

soft, fair hair from his forehead, and imprint a kiss on it, pat

Faust’s shaggy head, and turn away abruptly, quit the room.

Ere nightfall he have complete the few arrangement which his sudden

departure render necessary, and take with he only a small

travel valise which he sling across his shoulder, he wait till

the shade of evening have set in, and leave direction with his ally

Robert, now invest with all the dignity and privilege of butler-hood,

in regard to his luggage, which he desire might be forward to a

certain address in London, he quit Broadhurst alone and on foot.



the town of H---------- be situate about ten mile from the park gate

of Broadhurst, and thither do Lewis direct his step. he pace along

mechanically, with a dull, heavy tread, as unlike his usual free elastic

bound step as possible; he keep his eye fix on the road before

he, neither glance to the right nor the left, and all his action

appear like those of one move in a dream. the night be dry and

warm, and when Lewis have proceed about six mile on his way the

moon come out and bathe hill and valley in a flood of silvery light.

suddenly he pause, as the ruin of a picturesque old abbey, throw

out in bold relief by a dark background of tree, become visible at a

turning of the road, and fix his eye on the time-wear structure,

gaze long and earnestly; then a new idea seem to strike he, and

spring over a gate, he ascend with vigorous stride the green

hillside on which the ruin be situate. pass beneath crumble

arch and over the fallen stone-work cover old grave of a forgotten

generation, he reach a portion of the building which seem in

somewhat well repair than the remainder. having reach the upper end

of the chancel, he pause, and lean his back against the break

shaft of a pillar which have support one of the arch, give way to

the painful recollection which the place excite. the last time he have

visit that spot, Annie Grant have stand by his side, and as he teach

she how the mystic piety of our forefather have strive to symbolise

the truth of Christianity in the cruciform cathedral, with its vault

arch and heaven-aspire pinnacle, her soft blue eye have look into

his face with an expression of the respectful love we feel towards one

whom we deem well and wise than ourselves. and now how cruel be the

contrast--how completely and painfully alone he feel. then he long

(who have not at some crisis of the inner-life?) so earnestly that he

almost fancy he possess the power to separate mind and matter, and

fly in the spirit to she he love, to learn whether she think of

he and grieve for his absence. pursue the idea, he come to speculate

on many thing. have they yet tell she he would not return? what reason

would the General assign for such an abrupt departure? would she believe

his account, or would her heart divine the true cause? and if it do,

would she pity him?--strongest proof of love--he could bear the idea of

her pity.



[illustration: 0370]



Poor Lewis! perhaps his great trial be this, that at the very moment

when he give she up for ever, a latent sense of power tell he that he

could have win she; this be indeed the “sorrow’s crown of sorrow”--the

bitterness of more than self-renunciation, for Annie, too, might

be render unhappy by his act. then the future, the blank, fearful

future--what lie in store for he there? “fresh sorrow--no” (and he

smile as man on the rack have smile when the tormentor have

outwit themselves, and the numbness of approach death have produce

insensibility to pain and rob they of their victim), “he be dead

alike to sorrow as to joy;” but at the moment, as if to prove he weak

even in the _vis inertio_ of despair, the possibility of Annie’s union

with Lord Bellefield come before he like some hideous phantom, and

he be force to own that there might be depth of misery await

he great than he have yet prove. and thus recall the past

and imagine the future, he afflict himself with grief real and

visionary, till the moonbeam grow pale and altogether flee, and

the star disappear one by one, and the red glow of the eastern sky

proclaim the come day, and the sun arise glorious in his majesty,

and his early ray pour through the broken roof and fall in a

stream of golden light upon the ruin altar; then for the first time

that night Lewis think of Rose, and of what her advice would have

be have she know of his unhappiness; and prostrate himself upon the

altar-step, he pray long and fervently.



the reflection that when our sorrow have become too heavy for we to bear

there be one mighty to save, himself in his earthly career a “man of

sorrow and acquaint with grief,” who will strengthen we to support

they, must console the deep mental anguish; and we do not believe

that any man have ever pray truly and earnestly without receive

comfort from so do. for the very act recognise a belief in the

existence and faith in the benevolence of a being, all powerful alike to

avert the evil we dread, and to bestow upon we the good we desire. and

Lewis, when he arise from his knee, do so refresh in spirit, and

well fit to do or to suffer, as he might be require by the change

and chance of that portion of the Railroad of Life over which he have

yet to pass.



he reach the town of H-------- as the inhabitant, arouse from their

slumber, be drowsily open the shop-window, and make his way to

a small, unobtrusive inn, breakfast. having ascertain at what hour

the last coach pass through for London, he leave his valise under the

care of the waiter, and pass along several dirty, narrow street, at

length reach a court, in one of the poor and most wretched quarter

of the town. here, after some trouble and a disagreeable amount of

threatening glance from sundry suspicious-look character, he

succeed in discover the abode of a certain Jerry Sullivan. this

worthy, having satisfy himself that Lewis be not a member of the

detective police, graciously accord he an interview, wherein Lewis

explain to he that, in consequence of a communication make to he by

Hardy on his death-bed, he be anxious to investigate the content of

a packet leave in possession of his (Sullivan’s) maternal ancestor. this

fact, Mr. Sullivan, whose brogue be considerably strong than his

regard for truth, immediately see fit to deny, and be proceed to

lament the death of his mother, which he aver have take place that

day fortnight, when he be interrupt by the inopportune entrance

of the lady in question, who appear by no mean dead, but in a very

lively state of virtuous indignation. she immediately silence she

mendacious offspring, and beckon Lewis into a kind of den which she

inhabit, shut the door, and then question and cross-question he

as to his connection with Hardy. having satisfied herself, by peruse

Hardy’s letter, that Lewis be no impostor, she unlock an old trunk,

whence she produce a bundle of paper and a sheet of parchment.



“there,” she say, “that’s the will he speak of, poor fellow, and they’s

the letter--and I only hope as you’ll be able to find the unfort’nate

childring, and that they will come into the money all right--it’s nigh

£100 year, I’m tell.”



“have you any idea whether Hardy have at all trace his daughter since

she leave he?” inquire Lewis.



“no; he hear nothink of she, poor chap; he be a’most brokenhearte

about she, and that’s what drive he to the course he take to. he

worn’t a reg’lar prig, bless yer; he do a little in the poarche line

wile, but only for the sake o’ the sport, same as you gent--he wor

above they thing altogether. but I know more than he do about the

gal: there be a young ’ooman here a week ago as have see she in

London, dress out and ride about in a coach like a lady; but that

wor soon arter she fust go off with the young swell, and wor a kind of

new toy like.”



“and do not the girl know anything of she since?” inquire Lewis.



“well, she know’d this much, that when the young lord go abroad with

his sister he make his valet stop behind and foller he in a few day

with Jane Hardy, arter which she in course lose sight of she; but she

think he’s leave she over in they fur part.”



“they fur part--that must mean Italy,” think Lewis; and find

the old woman have tell he all she know on the subject, he thank

she for her information, secure the paper about his person, and be

prepare to depart, when his companion stop he, and summon

Jerry, whose main, if not only, virtue appear to consist in filial

obedience, cause he to escort the “young gent” beyond the purlieus of

the miserable alley in which their abode be situate.



the visit have take long than Lewis have expect; and on his return

to the inn he find the coach would pass through in about half-an-hour.

snatch a hasty meal, he place the paper in his valise, and in a

few minute be on his road to London. the coach stop at an inn in

Holborn, and here Lewis, who, in his present state of mind, be anxious

to avoid a meeting with any of his friend, Frere himself not except,

determined for the next few day to take up his abode. accordingly, he

engage a sitting-room and bedroom, which, for the sake of privacy and

cheapness, be situate at the back of the house, at an altitude little

inferior to that of the neighbouring chimney-pot. having establish

himself in this uninviting residence, he sit down to try and arrange

some plan for the future. he feel that he ought to write to Rose and his

mother and acquaint they with his altered destiny; but to do so involve

an explanation which he shrink from attempt. he try to read, but

the only book at hand be a volume of Schiller, and with a sickening

feeling of despair he throw it from he. at length he bethink he of

Hardy’s paper, and untie the string that bind they, he spread they

on the table before he. the will, which he first examine, appear

formally draw up, sign, and attest. the testator leave property

worth, as far as Lewis could make out, about £100 a year to Jane and

Miles Hardy. lay this aside, he turn over a mass of small paper,

old game certificate, receipt for rent, and among other a note

carefully preserve, endorse in a bold free hand, “the first letter I

ever receive from Harriet.” it be an invitation, coquettishly word,

ask Hardy to join a party to the ---------- race, write by she

who have sin so deeply, and have long since go to give account of the

misery she have cause and suffer. Lewis could not look on this record

of an affection which even the great wrong woman can do to man have

be unable wholly to destroy, without the deep commiseration.



lay the note carefully aside, he take up the bundle of old letter,

and select one which be partially open, glance carelessly at its

content. why do he start and change colour as his eye fall upon the

handwriting? why press his hand to his burn brow as the momentary

doubt cross his mind whether all the mental anguish he have lately

suffer can have unsettle his brain, or whether that which he behold

be indeed reality? eagerly do he devour the content of the epistle,

eagerly do he unfold letter after letter till not one of the packet

remain unperused. again, sit late into the night, do he read and

re-read they, then fold they carefully, pace up and down the room,

chafing at the lazy hour that drag their weary length and oppose a

barrier between his wish and the come day, when he may act and

resolve doubt into certainty. for the whole of that night, the second

during which he have never close an eyelid, do he measure with

restless step the narrow limit of the apartment. leave his breakfast

untasted, he hurry, at the early business hour, to the chamber

of the family solicitor; for half the morning do they remain closet

together--together do they seek the office (clepe by Richard Frere a

den of thief) of Messrs. Jones & Levi, the lawyer who, as the reader

may remember, address a mysterious letter to Lewis soon after his

first arrival at Broadhurst. carefully do the astute man of law examine

and compare paper, and sift evidence, and draw out the crafty rogue

with whom he have to deal; and when he have gain all the information he

require, steadily and cautiously do he examine the affair in all its

bearing; nor be it till he have thoroughly make himself master of the

subject that he approach Lewis, and shake he heartily by the hand,

exclaim, “well, my dear sir, as far as one can judge in this early

stage of the proceeding, I think you have a very good case; and I beg

to congratulate you on the prospect before you.”



and what, then, be this prospect, at the mere possibility of which

Lewis’s eye sparkle and his cheek glow with the brightness of renew

hope? it be the prospect of inherit an ancient and honourable name,

of gain a position which would render he not only equal but superior

in rank to Annie Grant, and of possess an income beside which Lord

Bellefield’s fortune, impoverish by the turf and the gaming-table,

sink into comparative insignificance. one short year more for he to

prove his right before the eye of man, and then, if Annie be but true

to her own heart, he would boldly enter the list against his rival, and

in love or hate Lord Bellefield should find that he have meet his match.

well might his step be proud and his bear joyous and elated, for in

twelve hour the whole aspect of life have become change to he: such

shuttlecock be we in the hand of Fate, as unthinke man term the

mysterious ordinance of the Omnipotent.



have he know the content of a letter which be even then await he

at his banker’s, his new-find joy might have be lessen.









CHAPTER LI.--CONTAINS much SORROW, and PREPARES the way for MORE.



the letter to which reference be make at the conclusion of the

precede chapter, and which Lewis receive on the day follow that

on which he visit Mr. Coke, the family solicitor, prove to be from

General Grant, and run as follows:--



“my dear young Friend,--for in that light I must ever consider you,

after the many important service you have render me,--I be anxious

to lose no time in forward to your account, at Messrs.--, your salary

for the year begin May 18--; and as you have be compel by

honourable feel to throw up your appointment so unexpectedly, and may

not be fortunate enough to meet immediately with another suit to your

wish, you will, I feel sure, allow I, as some small testimony of the

high esteem in which I hold you, to enclose a cheque for £500 instead

of,£300. I shall feel hurt if you refuse to accept this token of my

regard.”



“two hundred pound for give up his daughter! he would scarcely have

buy I off so cheap if I have look at the matter in a pecuniary

point of view,” be Lewis’s ironical comment, as with an inward

resolution instantly to return the £200 he continue to peruse the

letter.



“you will be glad to hear that Walter bear your absence wonderfully

well; your kind consideration in leave he the dog have produce a very

good effect, as the animal serve to amuse he. we have not as yet be

able to disabuse he of the notion that you will return, although I have

impress upon my daughter, who appear to possess more influence with

he than any other member of the family, the necessity for so do. the

mention of my daughter’s name lead I for the last time to touch upon

a subject which I can conceive may be painful even to your

well-discipline mind. during an interview which I hold with she

yesterday, she express her readiness to be guide entirely by my

wish. with her full concurrence, the engagement to Lord Bellefield be

formally renew, and the marriage be to take place as soon as she come

of age. I may add that, as far as I be able to judge upon so delicate a

point, I do not doubt that her intend bridegroom possess she

entire affection. you will not think I unnecessarily communicative, or

careless of your feeling, in mention these fact; but I conceive the

knowledge of they may tend soon to restore your mind to its usually

healthy tone. should you still be in England after my daughter’s

marriage, I shall have much pleasure in see you either at Broadhurst

or in Park Crescent. convey my remembrance to Mrs. and Miss Arundel,



“and believe I to remain, your sincerely and faithfully,



“Archibald Grant.”



Lewis read the letter steadily to the end. with tremble lip and

start eyeball do he reperuse it; he could not avert his gaze, it

appear to possess a species of horrible fascination for he; he feel

as if his brain would burst, as if his reason be fail he. Annie

love Lord Bellefield, and allow the engagement to be formally

renew--oh, it be impossible! he _must_ be go mad--an evil which

his bad fear point at only as a remote possibility, when time

should have efface his image, and the influence of those around she

have conquer her linger scruple, come to pass ere the rosebud she

have give he have wither on its stem. why be it that the trial have

become too great for he to bear--that his selfcontrol have fail--be

it only the intensity of his own feeling that he fear? or be it that

he hope, yet dread to learn, that he be belove? do the sacrifice

that he have make consist only of his love for she, or do the belief

that he be relinquish the certainty of win hers in return add

a redoubled bitterness to his self-renunciation? be a thousand

remember word, look, glance, reality, or the creation of his

morbid fancy? he rise and pace the room, as be his willnot when

deeply excited. where should he seek a clue to this mystery? could he

believe--the thought flash like lightning through his brain, like

lightning, sear as it pass--could he believe that he have again be

dupe by a coquette? be all woman false and heartless alike? could

the goodness, and innocence, and purity which render beauty such as

Annie’s a link between earth and heaven be mere counterfeit, and not

the angel-instinct they appear? do good exist at all? or be this

world an initiatory hell, and the evil principle predominant? be Annie

untrue, truth itself might be but a great and specious falsehood.



from this chaos of passionate distraction a few clear, but on that

account no less painful, idea begin to evolve themselves: his newfound

dream of joy have vanish; rank and fortune, value only because they

would bring he near to Annie, would become a tie and a burden without

she--he would have none of they. for his mother and sister he would

still labour; to support they be his first duty: in work which he

_must_ perform lie his only refuge against despair, perhaps even against

madness. there be something else: some promise he make, what be it?

his brain swam, he could recollect nothing clearly; hastily remove his

neckcloth he plunge his head and face into a basin of cold water--this

precaution in all probability save he from a brain fever. having

partially dry his streaming lock and resume his walk up and down

the apartment, he remember his promise to Hardy. yes, that also be

a sacred duty; the girl must be discover, rescue from a life of

‘infamy, separate for ever from----, and here he stop abruptly as a

new idea occur to he--Lord Bellefield! the retribution he have vow

to exact from he! he be now free, in a position to demand it! for a

moment his eye flash, and the finger of his right hand involuntarily

close as if grasp a weapon, and then many conflict thought

crowd upon he, and the eye seek the ground, and the finger

insensibly relaxed. if he provoke Lord Bellefield to meet he now, at

this particular juncture, would not it appear as if he be actuate

solely by jealousy of his more fortunate rival, as if his hopeless

passion for Annie be the cause of his animosity? this idea be

especially repugnant to he for many reason. in the first place, he

have argue himself into the belief that his resentment against Lord

Bellefield be a just and reasonable feeling, and that in punish he

for the unmanly insult he have heap upon he, he be only exact a

due penalty; it be by this subtle argument alone that he could

regard the act he contemplate as at all a justifiable one. again, he

consider that it would be completely beneath he to be jealous of

Lord Bellefield. if Annie be able to love such a character, she be

unworthy _his_ affection. lastly, although he be himself scarcely aware

of the feeling, and although a personal meeting with the object of his

hatred, a contemptuous word or insolent look, would in a moment have

conquer it, he feel a natural repugnance to take any step which

might necessitate shed a fellow-mortal’s blood. to plan a duel _à

l’outrance_ as a distant possibility be one thing, to take measure

coolly and deliberately to bring about such an event immediately be

quite another affair. so catch at the only _grey_ spot among the

blackness that surround he, he console himself with the reflection

that, as Annie would not be of age till the expiration of between two

and three year, he might during that period contrive to learn how far

her heart be likely to go with her hand in the propose alliance, and

to regulate his conduct accordingly. shape his plan for the present

in accordance with this resolve, he write sundry letter (one to General

Grant respectfully decline his present) more or less coherently, and

then go to bed in the frame of mind of one who



               “dote yet doubt, suspect yet fondly love,”



must have be singularly fortunate if he enjoy a very good night’s

sleep.



we must now take a retrospective glance at Broadhurst during the short

space that have elapse since Lewis quit it, and learn how event,

which cause he such bitter grief, have be bring about.



’tis the night of Lewis’s departure, and Annie Grant sit at her open

window gaze pensively at the moon, which moon, by the way, be at

that identical moment light the old abbey and shine on her lover’s

throbbing brow, as he stood think of she beside the ruin altar.

now Annie be by no mean in a comfortable frame of mind; in the first

place, she begin more than to suspect that she be fall deeply in

love, and in the second, “the thing she love” have not exactly “die,”

 but what be quite as inconvenient and much more inexplicable, have

suddenly “convey itself away” without tell she why or wherefore.

Lewis and Walter have of late be in the habit of spend their

evening in the drawing-room, General Grant consider that it be

desirable to accustom the latter to the form and habit of society, but

on that evening they have not make their appearance as usual; Annie have

inquire of her aunt the reason of their absence. Miss Livingstone,

look like a very vicious old owl, reply “that really _she_ be the

last person likely to know. General Grant be doubtless well inform

on the subject, but he be always strangely, and as she think, most

unnecessarily reserve; she believe Mr. Arundel have be drive to

resign his situation, and she be not at all surprised; she do not know

who that could avoid it would reside in a family order about like a

regiment of dragoon; she dare say Lord Bellefield have some break-down

blackleg ready to recommend as tutor to teach Walter gambling and

horse-racing. would Annie oblige she by look under the sofa? she

think she see the shadow of a man’s foot against the chimney-piece;

she expect they should all be murder in their bed of a night, now

the only person able to defend they be drive away. would Annie oblige

she by ring the bell? she wish to ascertain whether Robert have

remember to load the percussion cap of his blunderbuss.” foil in

this quarter, Annie wait till Lord Bellefield be so obliging as to

stroll out in pursuit of a cigar, “smoke under difficulty” be one

of his most severe trial during a visit at Broadhurst. when he be go

she attack her father with a direct inquiry as to what have become of

Walter and Mr. Arundel?



“Walter be in his own study, Mr. Arundel be absent,” be the reply.



“Absent,” return Annie; “why, where be he go, papa?”



“I do not inquire Mr. Arundel’s intended route, my dear; his age and

character render he fully competent to regulate his own movement,” be

the stiff response.



Annie’s lip curl: “able to regulate his own movement!” she think

he fit to rule a universe.



“when be he come back, papa?”



“a--ahem! not at present, my dear; that is, in fact, you may consider

his absence as permanent. the reason for his departure which he

impart to I lead I to this conclusion.”



“there, I tell you so--I say he have be send away,” observe Minerva

snappishly.



“Madam, you have be misinform,” interpose the General with much

irritation; “Mr. Arundel have not be send away, he resign his

position as tutor to my ward of his own free will, for reason which I

consider good and sufficient.”



“and what be these wonderful reason, if one might make so bold as to

ask without have one’s nose snap off one’s face?” inquire Minerva,

curiosity and crossness combine overcome her habitual fear of she

august nephew-in-law.



“A--really I be not accustomed to be cross-question in this way.

A--you, madam, strangely forget our relative pos----; however, I may

as well mention once for all that I have Mr. Arundel’s authority

for state that his reason for quit Broadhurst be purely of a

personal nature. and now I beg this subject may not be allude to again;

I be the last person who should be accuse of drive he out of

the family;” and have by this time work himself up into a very

considerable passion, in which frame of mind he be, like most other of

his fellow-mortal, particularly unreasonable and incautious, he glance

furiously at poor innocent Annie and stride out of the room like an

offend autocrat, as he be.



and this agreeable little scene form the subject of Annie’s reverie,

as, with her golden hair hang like a veil around she, she watch

the moonbeam sleep on the velvet turf. why have Lewis leave they so

suddenly?--why, if her father know the reason, do he refuse to reveal

it; and still more strange, why should Lewis so scrupulously have

conceal it from she? then again, her father have appear angry with

she--could he suppose _she_ have have anything to do with the young

tutor’s departure? and then an idea strike she which even there, alone,

beneath the silent night, cause her face and neck to become suffuse

by a burn blush--could it be possible that she have betray herself,

that Lewis have discover her affection for he? and then she blush

yet more deeply at the plain word in which she have for the first time

express, even to herself, her heart’s secret yearning. the idea be

painful in the high degree to a mind of such child-like purity as

Annie’s, and yet the more she think of it the more probable do it

appear; it would account for everything that perplex she. if she

really have be so madly imprudent, so utterly deficient in maidenly

reserve, as to allow Lewis to perceive the depth of her regard for he,

his honourable feeling would instantly oblige he to leave the family,

and no doubt her father’s cross-questioning have in some degree elicit

from he the truth. oh! what deep humiliation--regard it in whatever

light she would, what bitter, endless misery! lewis’s calm manner,

his gentle, unimpassioned kindness, his late avoidance of her society,

since--distract thought--since he have begin to perceive her regard,

his stern resolve, so soon act upon, to quit the family, all prove

that her affection be not return. cruel degradation, to love a man

who be indifferent to she, and to have allow he to perceive it.

Annie possess a spice of her father’s haughty disposition, though in

general the many essentially feminine point of her character prevent

it from appear; but this be an occasion which call forth every

particle of pride in her nature. what could she do to remove the stain

(for such in her morbid self-reproach do she consider it) from she?

nothing! would to heaven she could lie down and die! her father, too,

evidently suspect the truth; Lord Bellefield would probably be the

next person to become acquainted with the disgraceful history--and with

the recollection of her cousin’s name a new idea flash across she.

yes, there be a way of escape--a method of silence every busy tongue!

but at what a sacrifice! could she bring herself to consent to marry

Lord Bellefield her object would be at once attain. no, she feel it

be impossible. but then, on the other hand, could she bear to labour

under the suspicion of loving, without return (there be the bitter

sting!), a man beneath she in station?--(she could remember this

difference now, when it would only add to her self-torment). well,

fortunately, she be not call on to decide the question at once;

she would think more upon the matter: at all event, there be the

possibility to fall back upon as a last resource. then her thought

revert to Lewis, the brave, the true, the noble-hearted! she should

never see he again; he would achieve greatness--(she feel as sure

of _that_ as if she have hold in her hand the “Gazette” announce his

acceptance of the Premiership)--and some other would share it with

he, while she should be the wife----the alternative be too hateful to

contemplate, so she substitute, in her grave. yes, she should never

see he again! and she recall his image, as on that summer day he have

approach the window to summon she to the german lesson, when, as she

read of Max Piccolomini, she have realise his appearance in the dark,

proud beauty of he who sit beside she. she remember his joyous,

animate look as he bound across the lawn, his glow cheek, his

bright, sparkle eye, the wave masse of his raven hair, and his

eager, happy smile as his glance meet her! two idea engross she: he

do not love she--she should never see he again; and forget she

pride, her woman’s dignity, even her self-upbraid, in the intensity

of her sorrow, the poor child fling herself on her bed in an agony of

tear, and pour forth the bitter desolation of a lonely, break

heart.



the next morning she plead a headache (a heart-ache would have be

nearer the truth) as an excuse for breakfast in her room, and do not

make her appearance till it be nearly luncheon time. during that meal

the General be unusually dictatorial, not to say fractious, and more

than once speak so harshly to Annie that she have some difficulty

in repress her tear. the meal be above half conclude ere Lord

Bellefield, who excuse himself by say he have have some important

letter to write, make his appearance. when at last he join they,

he do so apparently in the most amiable frame of mind; he receive

a reprimand from the General for his want of punctuality with a

good-humour smile, and introduce a carefully veil compliment into

his apology, which greatly tend to soften that gallant veteran’s

ill-temper; he interpose with skilful kindness to avert sundry crab

attack, aim by Miss Livingstone at poor Annie, and introduce

some interesting topic which draw out the elder, and give a new

and agreeable turn to the conversation; he sympathise with Annie’s

headache, for which he invent an equally opportune, plausible, and

false excuse, and, in short, he lay himself out to fascinate, and

succeed _à merveille_. Annie feel really grateful to he, for he

have come to her rescue at a moment in which kindness and sympathy be

peculiarly acceptable to she.



when luncheon be conclude the General request his daughter’s

presence in the library. Poor Annie rise to obey he. a strange, wild

idea seize she, that he might be go to refer to Lewis’s departure,

perhaps to upbraid she for her share in cause it; and she tremble so

violently that her knee almost refuse to support she: in a moment Lord

Bellefield be at her side.



“take my arm,” he say kindly; “the effect of your headache have

scarcely pass away even yet.” Annie accept his arm in grateful

silence, and in her guileless gentleness of heart accuse herself of

never before having do proper justice to her cousin’s kindly nature.

as they approach the library he detain she.



“Dear Annie,” he say, “it would be affectation on my part to pretend

ignorance of the subject on which your father be about to converse with

you. the General appear for some cause, which I be unable to divine,

especially irritable this morning: do not needlessly oppose he; and

should he chance to urge my cause urgently, remember, dear one, how

entirely my future happiness be involve in your decision.” as he cease

speak he open the library door without give she an opportunity

to reply, then, lead she in, press her hand, cast towards her an

appealing glance, and turning, quit the room. Lord Bellefield be a

good tactician: for the first time the idea cross Annie’s mind, “he

love I then,” and contrast his devotion with Lewis’s suppose

indifference, she pity he. could she have see his change of

countenance as the door close upon he she would scarcely have do so;

his look be that of some fiend who have compass the destruction of a

human soul, the personification of triumphant malevolence.



the General begin his harangue: he inform Annie that she be no long

a child, and so far he be right; but he do not add, as he might have

do, have he be as well acquainted with the working of her mind as we

happen to be, that the last twenty-four hour have perform the work of

year to effect the change from the thoughtless child to the thinking,

feel woman, for the first time cognisant of those fearful reality,

_life_ and _love!_ but if the worthy General say nothing of love, he

soon discourse at great length of (we be about thoughtlessly to add)

its usual _termination_, marriage; which institution he look upon

solely in a military point of view--viz., as a solemn alliance between

two power for their mutual benefit. having give his oratorical power

a good breathing canter around (as he attempt to depict they) the

flowery mead of matrimony, he gradually narrow his circle till he be

amble about his daughter’s propose union with Lord Bellefield, and

having by this time pretty well exhaust his eloquence, he dash at

once _in médias re_ by inquire whether she know any just cause or

impediment wherefore the engagement, break off by he on the ground of

her cousin’s falsely suppose misconduct, should not there and then be

renew, with a view, at the fitting time, i.e., as soon as she should

attain the age of twenty-one, to their become man and wife. to this

Annie reply with down-cast look and many blush, that if her father

have no objection, she have make up her mind to live and die an old

maid--she be go to add, like Aunt Martha, but, on second thought,

doubt whether the association of idea be likely to aid her cause,

she repress the simile. to this the General merely say “Pish!” and

take snuff contemptuously: so Annie try another tack.



“if ever,” she observe, “she be to marry, it must be a great many

year hence; she be such a careless, silly little thing, not at all

fit to manage a family. do not papa remember when she go with he and

Charles Leicester on their grouse-shoot expedition, and their cottage

be fifteen mile from everywhere, how she forget to take any tea or

sugar, and they be oblige to drink whisky-and-water for breakfast for

nearly a week?”



but to this papa turn a deaf ear, and show such unequivocal sign of

be about to get into a rage, that Annie in despair fall back upon she

last argument, which be that, although her cousin Bellefield be very

kind to she, and she have always look upon he and Charles Leicester

as her brother, yet she do not like he well enough to wish to marry

him,--“she be sure it be wicked to marry any one unless you love he

well--than anybody else,” she be go to say, but she change it

to, “than she love Adolphus.” thereupon the General’s anger, scarcely

hitherto control, burst forth, and he inform she, with great

volubility, that she have speak the truth when she have call herself

silly; for that her whole argument be so childish and absurd that he

be perfectly ashamed of it and of she; and that if she choose to talk

and act so childishly, she must expect to be treat as a child,

and submit to the decision of those who be old and wise than

herself--that he would give she five minute to reconsider the matter;

and if she then refuse to consent to a renewal of the engagement, he

should begin to fear that she must have have some unworthy reason for

such continued obstinacy. and as he utter the last cruel word he

fix his little sharp eye upon she as if he be try to look she

through and through. for a moment his reproach rouse somewhat of his

own spirit in his daughter; and draw herself up proudly, the girl

confront he with flash eye and heaving bosom, and then, poor

child! the consciousness of her secret attachment rush upon she, and

with stream eye she throw herself at his foot, exclaim, “I be

very foolish--very wicked. dearest papa, forgive I, and I will do

whatever you wish!”



and thus it come about that the engagement with Lord Bellefield be so

speedily renew.









CHAPTER LII.--VINDICATES the aphorism that “’TIS an ILL WIND which

blows no one any good.”



Richard Frere sit at his breakfast-table; before he stand an egg

untasted, which, having once be hot, be so no long, whilst a cup of

coffee, that have undergo the same refrigerating process, throw out its

fragrance unregarde. in his hand be an open letter: we will take the

liberty of peep over his shoulder and make our reader acquaint

with its content. they run thus:--



“Dear Frere,--I have quit Broadhurst for ever, and break off all

connection between General Grant and myself. _why_ I have do this I

cannot at present tell you; year hence, when time shall have sear

wound which now blee at the slight touch of memory, you shall

know all. I have suffer, and must suffer, much; but suffering appear

identical with existence--at least, in this present phase of be. I be

ill in mind and body; the restless spirit within be at length beginning

to tell upon even my iron constitution. the mind must have rest if I

would continue sane, the body must be brace by exertion if I wish not

to degenerate into a mere nervous hypochondriac. accordingly, when you

receive this letter I shall have quit England. my project--if such

vague idea as mine deserve the title--embrace a walk tour through

Europe, which may possibly be extend to Syria and Persia, should my

object not be previously attain. at my banker’s lie the sum of £500,

the wage (minus the little my travel expense will require) of my

two year’ slavery; before that be exhausted fresh fund will be place

at the disposal of my mother and sister, or I shall be dead; in either

case I leave my family as a sacred deposit to your care. dear old Frere,

do not judge I harshly. I be not (if I know my own motive) act

with selfish rashness in this matter. my whole being, intellectual and

physical, have receive a fearful shock, and the course I propose to

pursue appear to offer the only chance of a restoration to a healthy

frame of mind. I could not do this do I not know that in you my mother

and Rose will find a more efficient protector than the one they will

lose for a season; I could not do this do I not love you so well as to

have perfect faith in your friendship in the very high sense of the

word. enough on this head--we _know_ each other. in the unlikely event

of pecuniary difficulty arise, apply to Mr. Coke, the solicitor,

in Lincoln’s Inn. he have my direction also in case of any accident

befall I, and from time to time he will be inform of my

whereabouts, as for at least the next year I shall not write to any of

you--it be my wish to forget that such a country as England exist. I

enclose a note for poor Rose: may I ask you to deliver it in person, and

break this matter to she and my mother? as yet they be not even aware

that I have quit Broadhurst. God bless you, and good-bye for----;

but we will not pry into the future--‘sufficient for the day be the evil

thereof:’



“your ever, Lewis Arundel.”



this letter Frere read carefully through; having do so, he ejaculate

“well!” in a tone of the utmost astonishment; then push his hair back

from his forehead, as if he seek to give his intellectual power

free play, he steadily reperuse it, but apparently with little well

success, for when he have a second time arrive at the signature, he

gaze round the room with an expression of the most intense perplexity,

exclaim, “I never read such a letter, _never!_”



spread the paper before he, he carefully turn up his wristband,

seize a silver butter-knife, which in his abstraction he conceive to

be a pen, feel the point to see if it would write, dip it into the

milk by way of ink, and thus prepare, again attack the mysterious

document sentence by sentence, keep up during this third reading a

running fire of comment somewhat after the follow fashion--



“Hum! well! he’s leave Broadhurst for ever, etc., etc., and he cann’t tell

I why now, but will year hence--when he have forget all the minute

particular which would make the affair intelligible, I suppose;

sensible, very. throw away three hundred pound a year, with a mother

and sister depend upon he, and ‘no future prospect,’ as they say

in all the ‘shocking destitution’ advertisement. oh, wise young

judge! well, never mind. ‘seared wound--existence identical with

suffering--restless spirit affect iron constitution’--_cum multis

allis_, etc. now, all that clone into plain English mean that he have

get into a rampant state of mind about something, which, interfere

with the gastric juice and all the other corporeal chemical, have put

his digestion out of sort; _ergo_, in order to repair damage he

have start on a continental walking-tour: might have do bad; the

exercise will settle the dyspepsia in double-quick time; I’m doctor

enough to know that. then he leave five hundred pound to support one

mother and sister till further notice, or till I receive intelligence

of his untimely decease. in the meantime he very obligingly commit

the live stock aforesaid to my care, as a sacred deposit; thus, without

be allow as much as even a voice in the matter, I suddenly find

myself _plus_ a mother and sister--more peculiar than pleasant, eh?

well, never mind. then he assert the truism that he could not do this

without faith in my friendship, mention the unnecessary fact that we

know each other Next come a very funny idea: if the money run short,

I’m to apply to a _lawyer_ of all people in the world. now, in my

innocence I should have fancy just the reverse, and that if we have

be burden with more cash than we know what to do with, the lawyer

would have be the boy to help we through the difficulty! well, one

live and learn--what have we next? oh! my young friend wish to

forget the existence of--England! nothing more--wish to forget the

existence of his own glorious country! the boy’s as mad as a March hare.

then he very coolly hand over to I the pleasant task of break

the news of his most uncomfortable conduct to his leave-off mother and

sister; and for the prospective performance of all this toil and trouble

he benevolently bless I, and adduce a text of Scripture, which

apply much more to my case than to his own, conclude.



“well, I should just like anybody to explain to I the meaning of that

letter; for as to make out either what he have do, or what he be

go to do, from that document, I’d defy Odipus himself to accomplish

it. now, let I see what be the first article in my little list of

commission: enlighten our mother and sister, I suppose; and a very

hazy style of illumination I expect it will be, unless sister’s note.

should happen to throw some brightness on the matter. ‘poor rose!’ he

may well say poor! why, she dote on he--actually dote on he. I’d

give anything in the world to have she--that be, to have a sister love

I as that girl love he. I know she will be miserable; I’m certain of

it;” and stick the butter-knife behind his ear, a place in which he

still retain the school-boy habit of put his pen, Frere rise from

his seat, and resume his soliloquy, begin to pace the room with hasty

stride.



“what can have induce the boy to throw up his appointment in this

insane fashion I cann’t conceive. if it be any one else, I should fancy

he have misconduct himself, and that the rhapsodical letter be merely

an excuse for avoid a plain statement of a disgraceful truth; but

there’s something about Lewis Arundel which make one certain he’d never

commit a small sin or conceal a large one. if he have murder that scamp

Bellefield in a duel, he would have mention it directly. perhaps old

Grant have insult his dignity; _arcades ambo_, they’re a peppery pair;

‘high stomach be they both, and full of ire.’ the eld gentleman have

a double claim, literal and metaphorical, to the quotation, if I

remember his build rightly. Poor Lewis! I expect he be in a dreadful

state of mind; I should feel very sorry for he if I be not so angry

with he for bother Rose in this way. well, I must think about

start; no science shop for I to-day, or to-morrow either. by the

bye, I must ring for Jemima, and enlighten she as to my movement, and

she’ll be as cantankerous as a bilious crocodile, I expect. however, it

must be do, so here go;” and give the bell a very modest pull, he

drop into his reading-chair await the arrival of his acidulate

domestic with a singularly mild, not to say timid expression of

countenance.



“oh, Jemima, I ring--that be to say, the bell ring--to tell you I be

oblige to go out of town to-day, and shall not return till tomorrow

evening at the early,” begin Frere in an apologetic tone of voice,

as his ancient duenna, puff and blow from the ascent of the

staircase, enter.



as he speak, the positively cross expression of her antique feature

advance a degree, and become comparatively crosser as she reply with

a toss of the head--



“well, I’m sure! what next, I wonder!” then address her master in

a tone of wither contempt, she continue: “do you know what it be

you’re a sayin’ of, Master Richard?”



“well, I believe I do,” return frere humbly.



“I believe you don’t,” be the unceremonious rejoinder. “I believe you

go on read they foreign book in heathen Greek till you don’t know

what you’re a saying or a doing of; here you tell I one thing one day,

and something diametrically contradictious of it the next, till old

Nick his blessed self wouldn’t know how to act to please you!”



“why, what have I say contradictious, as you call it?” inquire Frere.



“what have you say?” repeat Jemima in a tone of intense disgust; “why

you’ve tell I to get ready a dinner for six this here very day, and

now you say you’re a go out of town, and willn’t be back till to-morrow

night. do you call that behave as a master of a house ought to do, let

alone a sanatory Christian?”



“a true bill, by all that’s unlucky!” mutter Frere.



“it’s a true bill that you’ll have to pay for as fine a couple of

chicken as ever be truss, which be now cast away before swine,

for as to ’em keep till the day after to-morrow, it’s a model

impossibilitude.”



“I should rather have think a physical one,” suggest Frere _sotto

voce_.



“then there’s a tongue,” continue Jemima, unheede the interruption,

“as beautiful a one as ever I set my two eye on.”



“I wonder if it’s as long as her own,” observe frere, speculatively

pursue the under-current of his private annotation.



“a tongue that with care and good carving would have last you for

breakfast for a fortnight.”



“then it would not have go by any mean as fast as a certain unruly

member with which I be acquaint,” continue the commentator.



“together with a lovely turbot, which I almost have to go down on my

bended knee to get out of the fishmonger--turbot be like pearl

of price at this time of year, with three dozen of native, which be

astonish not to be able to procure, so be force to put up with

lobster sauce instead, and a beauty it be now, though it will be _non

compo scenlis_ by the day after to-morrow, and fit only to make people

sick in the dusthole, where it’s a sin to let it go, with so many

poor starve creature a-want it, which be not the case when your

blessed mother be upon the face of the earth, in a violent satin gownd,

a set you moral copy ‘a woeful waist make wilful want,’ and ‘my

name be Norval, on the Grumpy Hills,’ which ought to have teach you

well than to have ask five gent to come here, look like fool,

and yourself the sixth, go out of town, leave I to tell ’em so,

with the house full of good thing all turn bad, and nobody but I

to eat ’em, which be a hard trial for an aged woman, that, take

you from the month, ought to be respect, if grey hair be honourable,

which they don’t seem to be nowadays, when we have get a bad lot of

wig over our head, with half of ’em nothing in ’em but crimp

horsehair, I do believe.”



here the worthy woman’s breath fail she, Frere be at length able to

get in a word or two.



“my good Jemima,” he begin blandly, “listen to I. when I invite my

friend and order a dinner, I be of course not aware that I should

be suddenly call upon to leave town; such be the case, however,

we must make the good of it. I will, therefore, despatch note to the

gentleman who be to have be my guest, put they off; and in

regard to the comestible, such as from their animal fabric require

cooking must be cook, and we must endeavour to consume they in detail

at--at our early convenience. now have I slain your Hydra, my good

Jemima?”



“I don’t understand your gibberish, Master Richard, nor don’t want

to. my poor dear mistress, which piously depart this moral life in a

mahogany coffin and silver nail, don’t use to talk so, though she’d

be bring up at boarding-school with the good of pastor and master

to honour and obey; but this I know, that the blessed dinner will go to

rack and ruin in spite of all your cook retail combustible, and

that put I in mind, what have you be do with your breakfast? why,

goodness gracious! he’s never touch a bit of it, and” (here she catch

sight of the butter-knife), “oh lor, oh lor! if he ain’t go clean

demented. what’s the matter?” she continue, as Frere, astonish at her

unusual vehemence, seek to learn the cause of her disquietude; “what’s
