the matter, indeed? look in the glass, and if you’re fit for any place

but Bedlam you’ll soon see what’s the matter.”



thus apostrophise, Frere turn his eye in the unwonted direction of

the chimney-glass, and there descry the butter-knife behind his ear,

be somewhat disconcert; and mutter that it must have get there by

accident, of its own accord, instead of a pen, he feel that his position

be quite untenable, and so, retreat ignominiously to the stronghold

of his own bedroom, he busy himself in preparation for his departure,

actually go the length of shave himself and put on a decent

suit of clothe. another half-hour see he on the road to----.



it be on the afternoon of the same day that Rose Arundel sit at the

window of their little drawing-room sketch the tower of an old

church, which peep prettily from amid a luxuriant group of giant elm.

Mrs. Arundel have go in a friend’s carriage to execute a host of minor

commission at a neighbouring town, and Rose, having write part of

her quota for the next month’s magazine, be reward her industry

by endeavour to catch a peculiar effect of sunlight on the tower

aforesaid. having work with brush and pencil for some minute, she

pause to criticise her drawing. it be a faithful copy of the landscape

before she nicely execute, but she shake her head in dissatisfaction.



“it be laboured and tame,” she say; “half-a-dozen touch from Lewis’s

pencil would have give the effect twice as well. what a strange thing

be the power of genius, the hand create at a stroke the brilliant

conception of the mind!” and then she draw out some of her brother’s

sketch in Germany: bold, free, spirited, and mark by refine,

severe taste, skilled alike to select the telling point and reject the

commonplace detail, save where such detail be require to assist in

carry out the lead idea, they all bear indisputable evidence of a

true artist-mind.



from the sketch Rose grow to think of he who have trace they. she

have not hear from Lewis for quite three week; his last letter have

indicate a mind ill at ease, and Rose have write to he to entreat

he to confide in she, if, as she fear, he be unhappy. why do he

not reply to her letter? answer her question with a sigh, she

turn again, pencil in hand, to the window, and perceive a gentleman

advance rapidly along the road lead to their cottage. for a moment

her pulse beat quickly: could it be her brother? but Lewis’s be a

figure not easily to be mistake, and a second glance convince she she

be wrong; and then she give a little start, and a bright blush make she

look so pretty that it be quite a shame nobody be there to see she;

have there be, perhaps she would not have turn to the glass, and

still blush and smile, smooth the glossy band of her rich brown

hair. why she perform this ceremony at this particular moment we leave

our female reader to discover, and having do so, of their courtesy to

enlighten we. then, like a puritanical little hypocrite as she be, she

reseat herself at her drawing-table, sketch away as zealously as

if the result of _fixature_ and _bandcline_ have be as little know to

the philosophy of the nineteenth century as be the secret of alchemy.



in another minute the full, rich tone of a man’s voice be hear,

bear down the shrill expostulation of Rachel--



“never mind about your mistress, young woman; where’s Miss Rose?”



“Upstairs, sir; but----”



“there, that’ll do, ‘but I no but;’ let I get by; which be the

door?--here we be.”



and as he utter the last word, Frere, tired and dusty, with a carpet

bag and a parcel of book in one hand, and his hat and _the_ umbrella in

the other, enter the little drawing-room. Rose advance to receive he

with a bright smile.



“this be an unexpected pleasure, Mr. Frere,” she say, extend she

hand. Frere shake it heartily, squeeze it in the process much hard

than be agreeable.



“why, how prett--a--I mean to say how well you be look,” he begin.

“country air suit you well than the pea-soup-colour atmosphere of

London.”



so unable do he appear to remove his eye from her face, that, in

spite of her good endeavour, the become blush again overspread her

feature; turn away as if for the purpose of arrange her drawing

material, she observed--



“Mamma be take a drive with a friend; I’m afraid she willn’t return just

yet.”



“so much the well,” begin Frere; then perceive the rudeness of the

remark, he continue, “what I mean be that I want to talk to you about

a letter I’ve receive from Lewis, and I can get on well with you than

with mamma, I expect. you and I understand each other, you see; now

Mrs. Arundel think I’m a bear or thereabout, and fit for nothing but

growl and bite.”



“perhaps I think the same,” remark Rose, smile at this unexpected

proof of his penetration; “but you speak of a letter from Lewis; I’m so

glad he have write to you, for it’s three week since I’ve hear from

he. you be look grave,” she add hurriedly; then become suddenly

alarm, she continue: “something have happen to he, and you have

come to break it to we--be it not so?”



Frere regard she with a good-natured smile, half laugh at, half

pity she; then hold up his finger, as if he be rebuke an

impetuous child, he said--



“how thoroughly woman-like and unreasonable, jump to a conclusion

without any sufficient datum to go upon; select the most dolorous

hypothesis imaginable, and then prepare to afflict yourself at sight

of the phantom your own fancy have conjure up; now,” he continue,

take her hand and half lead, half urge she to the sofa--“sit

down, listen quietly to what I have to tell you--think the matter over

with your usual good sense--and then we’ll consult together as to the

good course to pursue; and if anything useful and expedient can be

devised, rely upon I to execute it.”



Pale and trembling, but in every other respect collect, Rose obey.

as soon as she be seat Frere place himself by her side, and draw

out Lewis’s letter, said--



“your brother have leave Broadhurst and throw up his tutorship; his

reason for so do he have not explain to I; but as he evidently

write in a state of considerable mental agitation, that may account for

the omission. moreover, he promise to tell I all at some future time:

he send also a note for you, which may perhaps throw more light upon

the matter. here it be.”



so say, he produce the enclosure, and break the seal, hand it

to Rose. it run as follows:--



“do not fancy I unkind, dearest Rose, or insensible to the blessing

(almost the only one now leave I) of your affection, when at this

miserable crisis of my fate I deny myself the consolation of your

sympathy: I say, deny myself, for wretched as I be, tear as be my soul

by the black unbelief in the existence of human truth and goodness, I

yet know you to be good and true, and love you more entirely than I have

ever do. Frere will tell you that I be even now, as you read these

word, upon a foreign soil; the length of my self-impose exile be

as yet unfixed, but many month must elapse ere I shall again visit

England. have I come to you, I could not have withhold my confidence;

your sympathy would have utterly unmanned I; I should have lose the

little strength and self-reliance remain to I, and have totally

succumb to the blow that have fall upon I. Rose, love, at time I

fancy, when you be stay in Park Crescent, that you divine my

secret! the struggle be then go on, and I dream in my folly that

self-conquest be attainable; thus madly have I accomplish the ruin of

my happiness. I have quit Broad-hurst by my own act--flee to preserve

my honour; that and an ache heart be all that remain to I. I trust

to you and Frere to communicate this matter to my mother: of course,

should you from my broken hint divine the truth, you would never dream

of impart it to she; a thousand reason forbid it. in regard to

Frere, I leave you to judge; he be trustworthy as yourself. if he smile

at my folly in love so poor a thing as he hold woman to be, his kind

heart will sympathise with my wretchedness, even if my own weakness

have produce it. I have entrust he to pay my mother the usual yearly

allowance, and place fund at his disposal to enable he to do so.

while I live, she and you shall never know great poverty than you

endure at present. I go to regain, in foreign travel, the vigour of

mind and body which this blow have well-nigh paralyse. thank God in your

prayer that he have spare my reason, and leave I strength to make this

effort: may he watch over you both! in all difficulty apply to Richard

Frere. good-bye, dearest; forgive I the sorrow I occasion you; it seem

as though I be fate alike to suffer myself and to cause suffering to

all I love.--Yours ever affectionately, Lewis.”



Rose peruse her brother’s letter eagerly; as she proceed her bright

eye fill with tear. Frere wait until she have conclude, and then,

without speak, hand she the epistle he himself have receive. when

she have also finish this, he inquire, “well, what do you make of

it--anything?”



Rose turn away her head, and dry her eye, reply with a deep

sigh, “poor fellow, it be only too clear!”



“that’s just about the very last remark now that I should have expect

any one to utter, after having read that letter. what a thing it be to

be clever!” observe Frere.



without notice his observation, Rose place in his hand Lewis’s letter

to herself. frere read it with a gradually elongate countenance merely

pause to mutter, “much he know about my opinion of woman.”



having finish it, he refolde it carefully, and hand it back to

Rose, begin, “this enlighten we in some degree as to the matter; Lewis

have, it seem, fall in love as they call it, disastrously, with some

party unknown.”



“oh, you cannot doubt to whom he refer,” exclaim Rose earnestly. “it

be this to which my fear have point ever since I first behold she;

throw into constant communication with such a creature, one fitted----”



“why you don’t mean to say he’s fall in love with Miss Livingstone?”

 interrupt frere, look the very picture of astonishment.



“this be scarcely a subject on which it be kind to jest, Mr. Frere,”

 rejoin Rose almost sternly; “of course I refer to that gentle, lovely,

fascinating Annie Grant.”



“I do assure you I be perfectly serious,” return frere hastily. “I

wouldn’t joke about anything that make you unhappy, if my life depend

upon it; but I never dream of its be Annie Grant; why, she’s

engage to her unpleasant cousin, Lord Bellefield.”



“I think the engagement be break off,” observe Rose.



“ay, but it’s on again,” resume Frere. “I meet a man yesterday who be

one of Bellefield’s intimate, and he tell I that his lordship be

stay at Broadhurst--that he have make up his feud with the General,

and that the engagement have be, formally renew.”



“now, then, I see it all,” exclaim Rose. “Poor Lewis have be long

struggle against a deep attachment for that sweet Annie, whom none

could know without love; nourishing, perhaps half-unconsciously, a

secret hope that she be not wholly indifferent to he--a hope which to

an honourable mind like his must have bring more pain than pleasure.

and now this renewal of the engagement must have prove to he how

entirely he be mistaken; and unable to witness his rival’s triumph, he

have, as he tell I, flee the spot where each kind word from Annie, and

every haughty glance from Lord Bellefield, would have be like a dagger

to his heart. no wonder the mental conflict have nearly madden he--my

poor, poor Lewis!”



preoccupy by her sympathy for her brother’s sorrow, Rose do not

observe the effect her word have produce upon Frere; nor be it till he

speak in a low, deep voice, which tremble with suppress feeling, that

she observe his emotion.



“ay,” he say, more as if commune with his own spirit than as though

he be address she, “ay, it must be a hard thing to love with all

the depth of such a passionate nature as Lewis’s one who be indifferent

to he; but it be a more bitter thing still to see the long year

glide by, and to pass from boyhood to youth, from youth to manhood,

and to find middle age steal quickly upon you, and never to have have

any human be to love you--never to have find any heart on which you

might pour out those rich of affection which every generous nature

pant to bestow.” he pause--then, as the recollection he have excite

seem to crowd upon he, continue, “oh, the bitter tear I have shed

when, scarcely more than a child, I have weep to hear other boy tell

of happy home, and a mother’s love, and the affection of brother and

sister; then come the silent but more enduring sorrow of youth, when

tear can no long form a vent for the heart’s isolation, and the

restless spirit prey upon itself; and last, the struggle of maturer

manhood, which in its meridian strength contend against the sorrow of

its weak morning, and strive to live down the fruitless longing for

that affection which it cannot attain, and conquer all but the one

abide grief, remain to own itself still lonely-hearted, and see its

only hope of comfort in the grave. ay, this be grief which the help of

God alone can enable one to endure.”



the deep feeling, the simple, manly pathos with which he speak be more

than Rose or any true woman could hear unmove. lay her hand on his

to attract his attention, she say in a sweet, gentle voice, “indeed,

Mr. Frere, you do your friend injustice. Lewis love you as a brother;

my dear father have the warm affection for you, and often say that

if Lewis do but resemble you, if he prove as high-principle, as

kind-hearted, and as persevere, his dearest wish would be fulfil:

even I myself----” she pause, glance timidly at her companion; but

as he remain with his hand press upon his brow, apparently bury in

abstraction, she gather courage, and continued--



“even I feel that in you God have give I a second brother, and that I

should be most ungrateful, most unworthy such disintereste kindness

as you have invariably show I, do I not feel the warm esteem

and--and--gratitude----”



and here, suddenly become aware that Frere’s beautiful eye be fix

upon she with the same peculiar expression of delight which she have once

before observe in they, on the occasion of his tell she how he have

convince Rasper the irascible of the evil of duelling, poor Rose’s

eloquence fail she, and she become abruptly silent. frere pause for a

moment, and then with a forced calmness which scarcely veil the depth

of his emotion, said--



“Dear Rose, forgive I if I be about to cause you pain; but your

kindness have afford I a vision of such exquisite happiness that it

would be a source of endless self-reproach to I if through any reserve

on my part I fail to realise it. Rose, you cannot be my sister,

but you can, if you will, hold a far dearer title--you can become my

honoured wife. I have love you long, although it be my sorrow at your

departure from London which first open my eye to the nature of my

feeling. since then my sense of my own unworthiness to aspire to the

joy of possess such an angel have alone keep I silent. Rose, I know

my own presumption in thus address you; I be aware only too

painfully of my own uncouthness, my deficiency in all the polish

conventionality of life; but if the deep, tender devotion of

a heart which have pine through a lifetime for some object on which to

pour forth its treasure of love can make you happy; if you think that in

time you could in some degree return my affection----”



“oh, hush, hush!” interrupt Rose, in a break, falter voice; “I

cannot bear to hear you speak thus! if, good and noble as you be, my

love can indeed make you happier-”



she could not conclude her sentence, but Frere seem perfectly

satisfied with the fragment as it stand.



the result of the interview may well be gather from the follow

remark of Mrs. Arundel, who, return home about an hour after the

occurrence of the conversation above relate, declare that when she

come in “she find to her horror and astonishment _Ursa Major_ look

as if he would like to hug Rose, and, stranger still, Rose appear

rather flatter by the attention than otherwise.”









chapter LIII.--DEPICTS the married life of CHARLEY LEICESTER.



we must now request our reader to draw on the seven-league boot of

their imagination, and, thus accoutre, to stride remorselessly over

the space of two year. ’tis soon do; a slight mental effort, an

agile hop, skip, and jump of the fancy, and the gulf be pass--time be

annihilate. let we raise the curtain and mark the change the destroyer

have work. the world go round much the same as before; two

year make little difference in the personal appearance of the

fifty-eight-century-old planet--no lack of birth, death, and

marriage to regulate the average supply of the human race; if the

cholera create a deficiency one year, more poor curate marry, and

starve Irishmen take unto themselves wife, the next, and those

“beautiful” baby who contrive to turn out such very plain adult

multiply upon the face of the earth, and the thin rank be

replenish. and yet two year cause strange alteration when we dive

beneath the surface of society and become cognisant of the fortune

of individual: smile have give place to tear, and the grief of the

mourner have turn to joy; poor man have grow rich and rich man poor,

and the bad (with but few, very few exception to prove the rule) have

become bad, and the good advanced in righteousness; and the mass of

the half-hearted, cling yet more closely to this earth, of which they

be so enamoured, where their grave be await they, see heaven afar

off, and wish feebly, and for a short time each seventh day, that they

be good enough to reach it. thus the passenger train, with its cargo

of hope, and fear, and wish, speed along the _Railroad of Life_.



in a magnificent apartment in one of those arabian-night-like edifice,

a venetian palazzo--which, having belong to one of the great

historical family of the middle age, whose chief be, by virtue of

his position, a petty sovereign, be now let for the season to a wealthy

Englishman--lounge Charley Leicester, whose own surprise at the change

of fortune which could render such a description of he appropriate have

not even yet cease. on a sofa opposite sit his wife, on whose knee be

perch a very young gentleman, to whom we could scarcely soon have

introduce our reader, for the excellent reason that he have not make

his appearance at the Cradle Terminus of our Railroad when last we

treat of his amiable parent. the present phase of this extremely

young aristocrat be, so to speak, one of ex-babyhood; he be in the

very act of cease to be “the most beautiful creature in the world,”

 and as yet retain enough of his pristine loveliness to deserve the

epithet of a really pretty child. he exhibit in his proper person an

instance of that strange phenomenon which (why, we have either no idea,

or we hope, for the sake of morality, a _wrong_ one) always excite

such extreme astonishment in the mind of all nurse, maiden aunt,

and female acquaintance--he be decidedly like his own proper papa

and mamma. for the rest, when place on the carpet he prefer a

quadrupedal to an erect method of progression--have a strange habit of

make the rash experiment in gastronomy by put everything

wrong and dangerous into his mouth--never sit still for two minute

consecutively--would, in the same breath, laugh heartily and bewail

himself piteously, from exciting cause, which may be expect to remain

a mystery throughout all time, and confine his conversation to two

substantive and a colloquial hieroglyphic--viz., “Pap-pa,” “Mam-ma,”

 and “gib-Tarley,” which last be believe to be an infantine-english

compound of his christian name and the verb “to give,” and signify an

insatiable desire to render himself monarch of all he survey by a

process of general self-appropriation. at the moment in which we shall

introduce the reader to the party thus assemble, a servant enter

bear a packet of letter on a silver waiter, and hand they to

Leicester, withdraw.



“letter from England, by Jove!” exclaim Charles, untie the string

which encircle they.



“any for I, Charley?” inquire Laura, who in her position of wife and

mother look the prettiest little matron conceivable.



“two for I, and one for you, from Annie Grant, if I may judge by the

writing,” reply her husband, as he rise to hand it to she.



“Gib-Tarley, pap-pa! gib-Tarley,” vociferate that individual in the

prettiest of infantine treble, make insane plunge at the letter.



Laura, raise her hand above the curly pate of her acquisitive

offspring, gain possession of the interesting missive, then, hold

“Tarley” out at arm’s length, she exclaimed--



“here, take your boy, papa; he be in a troublesome humour, and I wish to

read my letter in peace.”



Leicester meekly obey, mutter as he do so, “wide-awake young

woman--know a thing or two, that mamma of your, master Tarley;” then

take the child on his knee, he continue, “now Tarley mean to be a

good boy, and sit quite still, because papa be go to be busy with the

affair of the state.”



the effect of this exhortation appear to be to excite, on the part

of the young gentleman to whom it be address, a sudden and violent

determination there and then to convert his father into an extempore

high-mettle racer, which equine transformation he strove to accomplish

by place himself astride on the paternal knee, clutch a fragile

and delicate watch-chain by way of bridle, kick the side of his

fictitious Rosinante with immense juvenile vigour, and vociferate at

the top of his small voice, “Pap-pa, gee-gee! pap-pa, gee-gee!”



[illustration: 0394]



Charley cast an appealing glance at his wife; she appear hopelessly

immerse in her letter, so resign himself to his fate, he murmur

faintly, “the thermometer stand at 750 in the shade, that’s all,” and

start at a brisk canter. the progress of the ride, however, serve

to exhilarate both horse and jockey to such a degree, that ere long a

violent game at romp be establish, which end in papa’s perch

his youthful son on his shoulder, and still influence by the equestrian

hypothesis, gallop round the room with he, and clear the sofa

at a fly leap in the course of their rapid career, to Laura’s

undisguised terror.



“there, my dear Charles, that will do; you will break the child’s neck

and your own also to a certainty if you do such wild thing. now ring

for nurse to take he, I want to talk to you about this letter.”



“Tarley,” however, by no mean approve of this arrangement, and

insist strenuously upon a prolongation of his ride, his father, who

it must be confess rather spoil he than otherwise, comply with

his demand for “Gee-gee more!” by again dash round the room with

he, and continue his headlong course till he have deposit his rider

within the august precinct of the nursery, where the precocious Ducrow,

fall under the baleful glance of an autocratic nurse, subside into a

state of infantine depression, and be hear no more.



Leicester, having return to the apartment in which he have leave his

wife, fling himself, in a state of apparent exhaustion, upon the sofa he

have lately jump over, exclaim, “that child will be the death of

I, I’m certain of it; where he can get all this dreadful energy of

character from I cann’t conceive. it must come from the Peyton side, for

I’m certain that even at his early age I have a much more clearly define

idea of the _dolce far niente_ than that unnatural little essence of

quicksilver possess. by Jove, if he should turn out as fast when he

grow up as he appear now before he have begin grow at all, it will

be an awful look out for our grey hair.”



“Nonsense, Charley, you’ve energy enough when you care to exert it; in

fact it be all your own doing, you know you delight to excite the child.

but now be sensible, and sit up and listen to I, for I really want to

consult you about this letter.”



“as to listen to you, my love, I’m only too happy to do so at all

time and season, and I’ll promise to be as sensible as be compatible

with my general mental capacity, but in regard to the sitting up, you

really must excuse I. I have a strong idea I sprain something in

jump over this sofa just now, my back or my shin, I forget the

precise spot, but I can assure you it require rest.”



“oh, you idle man,” be the laugh answer, “how incorrigible you

be!” and as Laura pronounce this condemnation she seat herself on a

footstool by her husband’s side, draw out the letter, and hand it to

he, say, “they have consent to my plan, and be come here in the

course of the next fortnight; but I do not like the tone of Annie’s

note, she must be much more really ill than I be at all aware of, and

there appear throughout a spirit of depression, which be completely

foreign to her nature--I cannot understand it.”



“I have a despatch from the General,” begin Leicester, leisurely

break the seal; “perhaps that may tend to elucidate the mystery. what

a fist the old fellow write! the letter all hold up their head as

if they be a regiment of soldier, and his signature bristle like a

stand of bayonet. oh! he ‘hope to be in Venice by Friday week, if his

daughter’s health, which have give he some little uneasiness

lately, should permit they to travel with the degree of swiftness and

punctuality which have appear to he expedient in lay out their

intend route.’ I’m very sorry dear Annie be ill; what can be the

matter with she, think you?”



“who be your other letter from?” inquire Laura, avoid his last

question.



“from Bellefield,” return Leicester, open it; “he cann’t come with

the Grants, but he’ll follow they before long. he have back the Dodona

colt for the Derby, and have get a heavy book on the race than he

like; he be hit hard at the last Newmarket meeting, and if anything

be to go wrong with the colt, and he not on the spot to hedge on the

first hint, the consequence might be more unpleasant than people in

general be aware of. well! thank heaven, with all my folly, I always

contrive to keep clear of the bet-ring. I don’t like that note of

Belle’s; he’ll get into some awful scrape if he do not take care.”



“for which I shall not pity he one bit,” rejoin Laura. “bear to a

high position, gift with a princely fortune; if he choose to disgrace

the one, and squander the other by gamble with a set of blackleg,

he deserve whatever he may meet with. I hope I have not pain you,

Charley dearest,” she continue, observe a slight shade of annoyance

on her husband’s good-humour face; “but truth be truth; I cannot like

that man; I wish he be not your brother, and oh! how I wish he be

not to be the husband of our darling Annie. I say, Charley, how come

it you never fall in love with she yourself? do you know--don’t

be conceite now--I think I be very lucky to get you under the

circumstance?”



a gay laugh answer rise to the lip of Charles Leicester, and then

the memory of the empty, heartless life he have lead before his marriage,

and the deep, true happiness he have enjoy since, come across he, and

draw his wife towards he, he imprint a kiss on her smooth forehead

as he reply, “if I be, indeed, worthy of your affection, darling, it

be you alone who have render I so, for before I know you I be a mere

conceited, idle, frivolous butterfly, spoil by the world, and with

just sense enough (like most spoilt child) to despise my spoiler,

without sufficient manliness of nature to free myself from its trammel

by any unassisted effort of my own.”



what reply Laura make to this speech, if indeed she make any, we do not

feel ourselves call upon to chronicle; suffice it to say that she

do not, by word, look, or deed evince the small symptom of having

repent of her bargain. a pause ensue, which be break by Leicester,

who exclaimed--



“by Jove! I be very nearly forget all about it--what’s o’clock?”

 then draw out a small enamel watch, one of the relic of former

day of dandyism, he continue, “half-past three; there be just time. I

have procure an order to see the picture Cardinal d’Ancona be tell

you about last week.”



“oh, the two painting from Lord Byron’s ‘Giaour,’ by the young artist

about whom no one know anything, and who be say to be a genius? I’m so

glad; when shall we go?” inquire Laura.



“why, it’s a case of Hobson’s choice,” return Leicester; “for it seem

the painter be so torment by idle people come to his studio that he

have be force to lay down a rule only to admit visitor on two day in

the week, from three till five; but the oddest part of the business

be that he choose to be absent on these occasion, leave an old

attendant to play cicerone--in fact, there appear to be some kind of

mystery about the man. however, to-day be _the_ day, so the soon

we’re off the well, more especially as I must be with the Consul by

half-past four.”



“I shall be ready in less than five minute,” rejoin Laura, “so let

we prosecute this wondrous adventure by all mean--a mystery be such a

rarity in these matter-of-fact day, that even so small a one as that

of a man who prefer avoid one’s notice instead of seek to obtrude

himself upon it, be interesting.”



“when will woman cease to be curious?” soliloquised Leicester,

elongate his body in order to reach a newspaper without the trouble of

rise. another quarter of an hour see they en route.



under Leicester’s able guidance they stop at the door of a small

house at the corner of a street turn out of the square of St. Mark’s.

on present the order an old man with grey hair come forward and

usher the visitor into a room light by a skylight, beneath which

be arrange various picture, some finish, other in a less forward

state of preparation. after examine several of the small sketch,

which display unusual talent, both Leicester and his wife pause with

one accord before a large painting. the old cicerone approach they,

“that be the picture,” he say in Italian, “about which every one be

talk; it be very grand, but the companion picture be finer: the

Signore have refuse 600 guineas for the pair. they be take from your

Lord Byron’s poem, the ‘Giaour’; here be the passage, _ecco lo!_” as he

speak he point to the follow stanzas--



                   “with sabre shiver’d to the hilt,



                   yet dripping with the blood he spill;



                   yet strain’d within the sever’d hand



                   which quiver round that faithless brand;



                   his turban far behind he roll’d,



                   and cleft in twain its firm fold;



               his flow robe by falchion tear,



               and crimson as those cloud of morn



                   that, streak’d with dusky red, portend



                   the day shall have a stormy end;



                   a stain on every bush that bear



                   a fragment of his palampore,



                   his breast with wound unnumber’d riv’n,



                   his back to earth, his face to heaven,



                   Fall’n Hassan lie--his unclosed eye



                   yet lower on his enemy,



                   as if the hour that seal’d his fate



                   surviving leave his quenchless hate;



                   and o’er he bend that foe with brow



                   as dark as his that bleed below.”



the artist have indeed well represent the fearful tragedy; the

principal light in the painting fall upon the figure, and especially the

face of the prostrate Hassan, which convulse by the death agony, yet

glance with an expression of “quenchless hate” upon his destroyer. the

feature of the Giaour, owe to the position in which he stand, with

one foot plant on the breast of his fallen enemy, be not visible,

but his figure be tall and commanding, and his attitude in the high

degree expressive of triumphant power. lean against the same easel

stand the companion picture--it contain but a single figure, but it

be one which be see, it be scarcely possible to forget, such a

live embodiment do it present of hopeless despair. the stony eye, the

sink cheek, the stern yet spiritless mouth--all speak of one who

have indeed “nothing leave to love or hate,” all realise the painful

description of “the vacant bosom’s wilderness,” that paralysis of the

soul in which



               “the keen pang the wretched find



                   be rapture to the dreary void,



               the leafless desert of the mind.”



in this painting also the feature of the Giaour be partially

conceal by the hood of a monk’s frock, which throw a deep shade across

they, and the drooping, nerveless figure serve in great degree to tell

the tale. the two picture be entitle “Revenge, and its fruit.”

 Laura and her husband gaze at they long and silently; at length

Leicester observe, with the air of a man who try to dissipate a

sentiment akin to superstitious fear, by listen to the sound of

his own voice--“’Pon my word, they be very extraordinary picture;

there’s I don’t know what about they--a kind of uncomfortable

fascination--they’re very horrible, but they’re very clever, eh?”



“oh! they be most wonderful,” return Laura in a subdued voice, as if

she almost fear to trust herself to speak, “particularly the second.

I never see anything express such utter hopelessness as that face and

attitude; one feel that active pain even would be a relief to the

monotony of that dull despair. what an uncommon person the artist must

be! the execution be good, but it be the mind in the picture that be so

extraordinary.”



Leicester, who during this speech have be attentively examine the

face of the prostrate Hassan, suddenly exclaim, “yes! of course, now

I see who it be. look here, Laura, do you perceive a likeness to anybody

you know in the face of this floor individual?”



thus accost, Laura, after a moment’s scrutiny, reply, “it be like

your brother.”



“just what strike I,” return Leicester; “what a quaint coincidence!

I’ve see some one somewhere of whom the other fellow remind I too.”



“the figure bear a shadowy resemblance to the Signore Luigi himself,

Eccellenza,” observe the old attendant; “at least, I have always

think so.”



“he must be rather an alarming, sanguinary kind of personage, at that

rate; he have not flatter himself, I must say.”



“the Signore be tall and dark, but handsome as the Belvidere Apollo--he

be not sanguinary as you say, Signore, but of a gentle kindness which

touch the heart. I be bind to love he, for he save I from ruin.”



“how be that? tell I,” ask Laura in a tone of interest.



“my dear Laura, I be grieve to prevent your hear this worthy man’s

recital, but unfortunately it only want five minute of the time at

which I promise to be with the Consul.”



“how long shall you be oblige to stay with he?” inquire his wife.



“Less than half-an-hour, perhaps twenty-five minute would suffice,”

 be the reply; “shall I leave you here and come back for you before five

o’clock?”



“there be several picture the Signora have not yet examine,” suggest

the old man. thus urge, Laura consent to remain; an idea which she

would not confess even to her husband, so wild and fanciful do she feel

it to be, have take possession of she, and her curiosity in regard to

the mysterious artist have become redouble.









CHAPTER LIV.--TREATS of a metamorphosis not describe by OVID.



“you be go to tell I some anecdote,” Laura observe as Leicester

quit the studio.



the Cicerone, who be a venerable-look old man with grey hair and a

thoroughly italian cast of feature, place a chair for the lady before

a view in Venice, at which she have not yet look, and then resumed--



“_Favorisca di sedersi la prego Signora_. I be go to relate how the

Signore whom I serve generously rescue I from ruin; but to do so I

must trouble the Eccellenza with a few particular of my own history.

I be originally educate as a painter, but although I be a correct

copyist, and possess some skill in mix colour, I have not the

_afflatus_, the inexplicable, the divine gift of genius, which cannot be

acquire. look at these picture,” he continue, warm into enthusiasm

as lie point to the painting from the “Giaour”; “in my prime I could

execute well than that, my colouring be rich and smooth, my

shade less hard and abrupt, though to acquire that skill have cost I

fifteen year’ constant study; but alas! the mind be want. I could

execute but I could not conceive--my picture would never have entrance

any one as you be entrance before those great soul-creation!” he

pause, sigh deeply, then resume: “so I grow poor, I have a wife and

child to support, and I bent my pride to become a scene-painter at

the Fenice Theatre. I work there twenty long year, and then from over

use my eyesight grow dim, and they discard I. after that I be

employ by the great painter of the day, Signore B--elli, to prepare

canvas and mix colour for the young artist whom he instruct. a year

and a half ago a pupil come to study with he--he be a stranger----”



“of what country?” inquire Laura eagerly.



“I cannot inform the Signora. he speak french, german, italian,

and very rarely english, equally well, but I do not think he be a

fellow-countryman of mine. the other young artist who frequent

B--elli’s studio would often tease I for sport, but the Signore be

always kind, and would not permit they to do so when he be present. one

day a pupil, who be finish the drapery of a Madonna and Child, of

which picture all the more important part have be paint by B--elli

himself, call to I to bring he some particular colour which he

require--in my haste I stumble and overthrow a flask of oil, which

fall upon the not yet dry painting, entirely obliterate the feature

of the Madonna. irritated at the difficulty into which I have plunge

both he and myself, the student spring up and seize I by the throat;

in a moment the Signore Luigi interfere, and compress the youth’s

arm in his powerful grasp, force he to release I.



“‘remember, Carlo,’ he say gently, ‘Antonelli be an old man.’



“‘he have ruin himself and I!’ exclaim the other, clasp his hand

in despair; ‘b--elli will discharge he without doubt, and I he will

refuse to instruct any long.’



“‘perhaps there be yet an alternative,’ urge the Signore Luigi;

‘b--elli will not return till to-morrow morning; much may be do in

eighteen hour; I will strive to restore the face.’



“he immediately set to work; fortunately he paint with as much

quickness as skill. when night draw near he dismiss we; through the

long hour of darkness he labour incessantly, pause neither for

sleep nor refreshment. with the early ray of dawn I be again at the

studio: he be paint still, calm, earnest, grave, as be his willnot,

only appear a little paler than usual; but such a work of art have

grow beneath his hand, such a marvellous creation! the Madonna herself

could not have appear more lovely than be that heavenly face. it be

complete ere B--elli arrive; when he behold it he be amazed.



“what inspire hand have trace those feature?” he demand. the

history be relate to he. he once more examine the picture, then

turn to the Signore, who stand near with fold arm, gaze on the

other’s excitement with an air of cold indifference, he exclaim in a

tone of mingle admiration and rage, “go, I can teach thee no long; it

be thou shouldst be the master.”



“the Signore take he at his word. he engage these paint room,

arrange with B--elli that I should accompany he, and be now the first

painter in Italy as to talent, and when his execution be a little more

perfect--ah! _se ne saprà qualche cosa_, we shall see how man will

talk of he!”



“and the head be very lovely, be it? what style of face be it?”

 inquire Laura.



“how can I tell you? it be perfection, _vi bisognava vederla_,” be the

enthusiastic reply. “stay,” he continue, glance at the clock, which

now only want ten minute to five; “I have an idea; there be yet time,

but you must never relate that you have behold it. here, follow I;” and

draw out a key, he unlock a door lead into a small apartment,

comfortably though simply furnish, and fit up with bookshelf

somewhat after the fashion of an english study. “_Ecco!_” resume

Antonelli, “he have again sketch the head, but the subject be

different. he will not allow I to place this picture in the studio,

though it be such a gem I could sell it for a large price.”



as he speak he draw back a curtain, and the light fall upon a small

picture paint with great care, and more elaborately finish than

any which Laura have yet see. it represent a girl of exquisite beauty

in a kneeling attitude, with her arm fling sportively around the neck

of a magnificent dog, her golden tress fall over and mingle with

the wave of his shaggy coat.



as Laura gaze her colour go and come quickly, and her eye seem to

grow to the canvas: both girl and dog be portrait do to the life,

and she recognise each of they immediately--her wild conjecture be

then the truth!--her determination be instantly take. seat herself

as if to examine the picture more nearly, she contrive by one or

two artful question to set the garrulous old man talk again, and

forgetful of the flight of moment, draw he on to relate to she how the

Signore have discover that his youngest bear, the son of his old age,

possess a talent for painting, and how the Signore be give he

lesson, and the talent be daily develop under such favourable

circumstance, until the old man have begin to hope that the boy might

succeed well than his father have do, and retrieve the shipwreck

fortune of the Antonellis.



while he be yet in the midst of his recital the clock strike five, and

almost at the same moment a quick, active footstep be hear bound up

the staircase, and the deep tone of a man’s voice exclaimed--



“_Antonelli, Antonelli, dove sei buon amico?_”



with a horror-stricken glance at his companion, the old man be about to

rush precipitately out of the room, when Laura, quietly lay her hand

upon his arm, said--



“there be nothing to be alarmed about! _bisogna ch’io gli parli_--tell

the Signore that an old friend be wait to see he.”



as she speak a tall, graceful figure appear at the door of the study,

and stop in amazement on perceive how it be tenant. in no way

embarrass by the situation in which she find herself, Laura rise from

her seat with the same degree of quiet, courteous, self-possession with

which she would have receive a guest in her own drawing-room, and

advance towards the new-comer, say, hold out her hand--



“your kindness will pardon the little stratagem by which I have seek

to verify my conjecture, that in Signore Luigi I should have the

pleasure of recognise an old friend.”



“leave we, Antonelli,” exclaim his employer sternly; then carefully

close the door, he turn towards his guest, and bow coldly,

inquire, “to what be I indebted for the honour of a visit from Mrs.

Leicester?”



“to the fact that I be vain enough to fancy the pleasure I feel in

meet an old friend might be mutual; and that Mr. Arundel would not

resent the liberty I have take in disregard the regulation of the

famous Signore Luigi: if I be so unfortunate as to have commit a

mistake, it be soon remedie,” she continue quickly, find

that Lewis--(as we have not intend any but the most transparent

mystification in regard to the identity of the painter and our hero, we

may as well call he by his proper name)--remained silent; as she speak

she rise and advance towards the door. her look and word recall

Lewis’s wander thought; he take her hand, reconducte she to her

seat, and then in a tone of deep feeling said--



“forgive I! but you do not, cannot know the train of overpower

memory your sudden appearance call up; indeed I be glad again to

look upon the face of an old friend, since you accord I the privilege

of so consider you--glad as a two year’ exile from all who ever know

or care for he can make a man.”



“be it so long since you quit England?” inquire Laura.



“it be,” be the reply. Lewis pause, and then continue: “I

leave England under circumstance which cause I great mental

suffering--suffer which time and a complete change of scene could

alone render less bitter. I travel for five month, pass through

Greece and visit Constantinople; at the expiration of that period I

wander hither, my vigour of mind and body in great measure restore.

the wonder of this country revive my enthusiasm for art; this, and the

necessity of follow some profession, lead I to the idea of adopt

the career of a painter. for a year I work ten hour daily in the

studio of Signore B--elli, at the end of that period I quit he and

commence painting on my own account; hitherto my success have surpass

my most sanguine expectation, so that I trust I have at last hit upon

my true vocation.”



“I be so delighted to hear it!” exclaim Laura warmly; “but how be it

we have see nothing of you before--do you not hear of our arrival? we

have be here more than a month!”



Lewis colour, bit his lip, and then reply, “my recollection of

England be so painful that I resolve, partly for that reason, partly

that I might keep my mind free from any anxiety which could interfere

with my devote my faculty fully and entirely to my new profession,

to avoid the society of the few English who be likely to come in my

way; indeed, my only associate have be the young artist with whom I

become acquaint in the studio of B--elli, and the family of the worthy

old man who act as my assistant.”



“but you will make we exception to the rule?” plead Laura; “Charles

will be really hurt if you refuse to come to we.” Lewis pause, his

impulse be to refuse, but there be a genuine kindness in Laura’s

manner which vouch for her sincerity; have she be a man he would have

adhere to his resolution, but it be not easy to say no to Laura.



“forgive my apparent churlishness,” he begin, “but may I ask whether you

have any of--of your english friend stay with you?”



“not at present; Charles and I be lead a quiet, humdrum Darby and

Joan life, which need not alarm even your hermit-like habit. you must

promise to dine with we to-morrow at six.”



“you be most good-natured to humour what must appear to you my absurd

caprice,” reply Lewis, touch by her thoughtful kindness.



“but you will come?” she say, hold out her hand to he.



Lewis take it in his own, and press it warmly as he reply, “nobody

could resist such gentle pleading.”



at this moment the door be fling open, and Charles Leicester burst in,

look more puzzle, excited, and angry than he have ever be know

to do in the previous course of his existence; while Antonelli,

vociferate eagerly in italian and broken English, be vainly

endeavour to detain he.









CHAPTER LV.--IS DECIDEDLY ORIGINAL, as it display MATRIMONY in a MORE

favourable light than COURTSHIP.



the Honourable Charles Leicester be, take he all in all, about as

easy-temper a fellow as ever breathe; but when old Antonelli inform

he that his young and pretty wife be closet with a mysterious

stranger, at the same time positively refuse to allow he to enter the

apartment in which they be shut up together, even he consider that

it be time to exert himself; so seize the old man by the arm and

swinge he round with a degree of energy which greatly discompose

that worthy cicerone, he throw open the door, and stare with an angry

and bewildered gaze into the dimly-light room, discover, to his

horror and disgust, Laura quietly sit with her hand clasp in that

of a handsome young Italian, for such do Lewis at first sight appear.

the period which have elapse since Leicester have last see he have

produce so mark a change in his appearance, that meet he for the

first time under circumstance so utterly disconnect with all former

association, he might well deem he be address a total stranger.

Lewis’s pale feature have regain in a great degree their look of

health, and exposure to a southern sun have convert the delicate

complexion into a manly brown, while, having allow his moustache

and even a short curly beard to grow, the low part of his face

be envelop in a mass of glossy black hair; this, and the stern,

thoughtful expression of his countenance, cause he to look at least

five year old than he really be. he rise as Leicester enter and

advance a step towards he; then, see that the other do not in the

slight degree recognise he, he pause and exchange a smile glance

with Laura as he mark Charley’s puzzle, angry expression.



Laura, enter thoroughly into the absurdity of the situation,

determine to improve it to the uttermost; return Lewis’s glance with

a look into which she contrive to throw an amount of tenderness that by

no mean soothe her husband’s irritation, she began--



“ah, Charles, let I introduce you; you will be delighted to hear that

Signore Luigi have kindly promise to dine with we to-morrow.”



“the deuce he have!” mutter Leicester to himself; “he might have wait

till I have ask he, I think;” then acknowledge the introduction by a

freeze little bow, he continue aloud--



“now, my dear Laura, we must really be go;” then cross to the

place where his wife be seat, he hold out his arm with the evident

intention of link hers with it and walk she off forthwith.



but Laura clearly disapprove of such precipitation; for without show

the slight disposition to move, she replied--



“restrain your impatience a few minute long, Mr. Leicester. having

form so agreeable an acquaintance,” she continue, glance at Lewis,

“you really must allow I time to prosecute it.”



it be not in Charles Leicester’s nature to be angry with any one for

five minute consecutively; with his wife, whom he idolise, it be

utterly impossible; so, make up his mind that Luigi be a kind of

lion, to be regard in the light of an exhibition, and stare at and

feed accordingly, and that Laura’s sudden fancy for he be only an

instance of womanly caprice--“women always go mad about celebrity,”

 he know--he make a short, penitent, civil speech, and then fling himself

lazily into a chair, with a look of half-bore, half-sulky resignation,

which, under the circumstance, be perfectly irresistible.



that his two companion find it so be evidence by their

simultaneously burst into a hearty fit of laughter, increase to

an alarming degree by the look of blank astonishment that come over

Leicester’s face at their incomprehensible conduct.



as soon as Laura could recover breath she begin, “why, Charley, you

dear, good-nature, stupid old thing! don’t you see who it be yet?”



at the same moment the Mysterious One approach he, say, “have you

quite forget the existence of Lewis Arundel?”



for a moment Charley gaze in half-sceptical astonishment, and then

seize his hand, and shake it as if he be anxious to make up for

his dulness by dislocate his friend’s shoulder, he exclaim, “my

dear fellow, I’m delighted to see you--I really be quite ashamed of

myself--but, ’pon my word, you’ve make yourself look so particularly

unlike yourself, and the whole thing altogether be so very strange and

unexpected, and more like an incident in a novel than a real _bona-fide_

transaction of every-day life, that you must hold I excuse. my dear

Laura, I begin to think you be go out of your sense, and that

I should have to procure a keeper for you. why, Arundel, then you’ve

turn out a genius after all, a second Michael Angelo, eh! I prophesy

you would, if you remember, that day when you paint the cow?”



as he speak he stoop to pick up his cane and glove, which in the

excitement of the discovery he have allow to drop; consequently he

do not perceive the effect his word have produce upon Lewis. _do_ he

remember the incident to which Leicester have allude? would to heaven

he could forget that which be brand on his memory as with a red-hot

iron, the fact that on the day in question he have for the first time

beheld Annie Grant! he turn pale--the blood seem to rush back upon

his heart, and oppress he with a feeling of suffocation--he be force

to lean against a table for support.



these sign of emotion be not lose upon Laura’s quick eye, and rise

at the moment to divert her husband’s attention, she observe, “now I

have at length succeed in enlighten your understanding, Charley

dear, I be quite at your service.”



“come along then,” be the reply; “you’ll dine with we to-morrow without

fail, Signore Luigi, _alias_ arundel, you polyglot mystery. ’pon my

word, it’s the oddest coincidence I ever know, exactly like a thing in a

play, where everybody turn out to be somebody else. come along, Laura;

I must try and conciliate your old friend the cicerone, too, for I swing

he round in my wrath most viciously; I hope I have not dislocate any

can tell you, when I fancy I have lose my love. by-by, _al piacer di

rivederla, Signore_.” thus run on, Charley Leicester tuck his wife

under his arm, and having handsomely reward Antonelli, depart.



in the course of their walk home, Laura, after her husband have again and

again express his astonishment at the denouement which have just take

place, inquire, “you never clearly make out the reason why Mr. Arundel

quit Broadhurst, do you, Charley?”



“no! Bellefield hint in his way, which give one an impression without

one’s exactly know what ground one have for take it up, that

Arundel have misconduct himself in some manner; but the General’s

letter quite contradict such an idea, and speak of he in the very

high term. I think nothing of what Bellefield say, for they never

like one another, and _entre nous_, I consider Belle behave shamefully

to he on one or two occasion.”



Laura pause for a minute in thought, and then inquire, “what do the

remark you make about sketch a cow refer to?”



“oh! do I never tell you that?” return Charles, laughing; “the

incident occur on the occasion of his first introduction to the Grant

family;” and then he proceed to give she a full, true, and particular

account of the interesting adventure, with which the reader be already

acquainted. as he conclude, Laura observed--



“in fact, then, he behold for the first time Annie Grant. now I can

guess why he turn pale when you refer to it: Charley, you must

be very careful how you say anything about the Broadhurst party before

he.”



“eh! and wherefore, oh wise little woman, endow with an unlimited

power of see into milestone?” be the banter reply.



“well, if I tell you, you must promise never to mention the idea, for it

be only an idea, to anybody till I give you leave,” return Laura.



Charley compress his lip, and go through a pantomimic

representation of sew they together.



“Nay, but I’m serious,” resume Laura; “if I tell you, you must be

careful, and not blunder it out in any of your absent fit; do you

promise?”



“I’ll do more than promise,” return her husband energetically; “I’ll

swear by all



                   the heathen god and goddess,



                   without skirt and bodice,



never to reveal to mortal ear the fatal secret--so let we have it!”



“well then, if you must know, I suspect Mr. Arundel to have have well

taste than you, and not to have escape with a whole heart from the

fascination of Annie Grant.”



“Phew--!” reply Leicester, give vent to a prolong whistle

indicative of intense surprise; “that be the state of the case, eh? then

my allusion to the cow be just about the most unlucky topic I could

have hit upon. I certainly have a genius for put my foot in it,

whenever circumstance afford an aperture for the insertion of that

extremity. I should not wonder if that idea of your, always suppose

it to be correct, might explain his sudden departure from Broadhurst,

and account for this strange freak of expatriate himself and start

as a second-hand modern Michael Angelo. I say, Laura, suppose the fancy

should happen to be mutual, Bellefield may have have more cause for

dislike Arundel than people be aware of.”



“she would never have accept your brother if she know that another

love she, and feel that she return his affection; Annie be too good

and true-hearted for that,” return Laura warmly.



“Time will show,” reply Leicester. “I only hope it may not be so;

for between Arundel and Belle I should not know how to act. Belle be my

brother, and to Arundel’s good advice I shall always consider I be in

great measure indebte for a certain plague of my life--(without whose

plague the say life wouldn’t be worth have, all the same);--the

only course I can take, if our suspicion prove true, will be to

preserve a strict neutrality.”



“and how would you wish I to act, Charley dear?” inquire Laura, take

her husband’s finger caressingly between her own soft, white little

hand. “you know I cann’t recommend Annie to marry your brother if she

do not love he.”



“follow the dictate of your own good sense and kind heart, darling, and

you will be sure to do rightly. I have the most perfect confidence in

you, and would not influence you one way or another, if I could.”



the tear rise to Laura’s eye at this fresh proof of her husband’s

affection; and as she reflect on what he have say in regard to Lewis’s

share in bring they together, she inwardly vow that if ever it lay

in her power to do he a similar good turn, she would not be slothful in

advance his interest.



true to his promise, Lewis dine with they the next day; by mutual

consent all reference to the past be avoid, and no allusion make to

any of the Broadhurst party. as soon as Lewis find this to be the case,

a certain proud embarrassment observable in his manner disappear; and

yield to the delight of again find himself in congenial

society, he unconsciously display his brilliant conversational

power--relate, with playful wit, or forcible and striking

illustration, the adventure which have befall he, and the scenery he

have behold in his late pedestrian tour, till Charles and Laura, who have

only be acquaint with he when the cloud of his dependent position

at Broadhurst hang over he and conceal his natural character beneath

a veil of proud reserve, be equally delighted and astonished; and

when, late in the evening, he take his departure, they vie with each

other in perform a duet to his praise.



“he talk so well!” exclaim Charley.



“he know so much!” cry Laura.



“he have be everywhere,” continue the former.



“and do everything,” resume the latter.



“he be so clever and epigrammatic,” urge the gentleman.



“and his description of scenery be so poetical,” put in the lady.



“his figure be so striking,” say the master.



“and his face so handsome,” rejoin the mistress.



“what a pair of eye he have!”



“and such a smile!”



“then his moustache and whisker be irreproachable.”



“and his hand whiter than mine.”



“in fact, he be a stunner!” declare the baritone.



“though I detest slang, I must confess that he be,” chime in the

soprano.



“if I be a woman I should be over head and ear in love with he,”

 suggest Charley.



“I be both the one and the other,” respond his wife, cast an arch

glance at her spouse, as much as to say, “how do you like that?” which

rebellious speech her lord and master punish by stop her mouth

with--the only remedy we believe ever to have be find effectual in

such a case.



from that time forth Lewis become a constant visitor at the Palazzo

Grassini, and at last complete his triumph over Laura’s affection by

ask, as a favour, to be allow to take a sketch of “Tarley”;

“he want a study of a child’s head so much.” then the sketch be

pronounce so successful that nothing would serve but that it must be

perpetuate in oil, and as the possibility of make “Tarley” sit still

long enough for such a purpose do not exist unless Laura sit also,

Lewis consent to paint they together, although he have hitherto

steadily refuse to take a portrait, in spite of large sum which have

be offer he to do so.



Laura receive a second epistle from Annie Grant postpone their

visit for another fortnight. her father have all along expect Miss

Livingstone would accompany they as a matter of course; but when it come

to the point that redoubtable spinster break into open revolt, assert

her independence, nail her colour to the mast, and determine upon

death or victory. so resolute be she, that after a most obstinate

engagement with sharp tongue, which follow upon two day of sulky

silence, the General be force to make term and yield his own will to

that of a woman; so Minerva remain behind to garrison Broadhurst. as,

however, the General by no mean approve of his daughter travel

without some female companion, the journey be very nearly be give

up, when, at the last moment, a lady, the wife of an austrian officer

quarter at Venice, be discover, who, seek for an escort to

enable she to join her husband, be only too happy to be allow to

accompany the Grant party. these delay, however, would necessarily

retard their arrival for at least a fortnight. day pass away; the

picture (and a very pretty one it be) of the fair young mother and her

little, rosy, merry child, advanced towards completion, and Lewis begin

to look forward with a feeling almost akin to regret, to the time when

the sitting, and the agreeable, friendly conversation to which they

give rise, would be at an end.



since he have quit England his thought and feeling have undergo

various and considerable change; at first he have strive, in the

excitement of active adventure, to banish recollection, and after a

time he succeed so far as to take a lively interest in all he see. the

revolutionary spirit, which have since produce such change in modern

Europe, be then begin to show itself, and he witness the outbreak

of a rather serious _émeute_ in one of the german States, in which he

contrive to get mix up, and by these mean he come in for a couple of

day’s hard fighting, and a week of intense fatigue and excitement.

this, paradoxical as it may appear, be of the great psychological

assistance to he; it rouse he effectually, and take he completely

out of himself. the excitement be keep up for some little time long,

for, owe to the part which his old student association have lead he

to take in the affair, he bring upon himself the suspicion of the

prussian government, and the next event of his tour be in fact a flight

to save himself from arrest. during this period he be accompany by a

young German, who, much more deeply implicate in the affair than Lewis

have be, dread that his capture might lead to his execution;

and unwilling to atone for his patriotism with his life, he and his

companion hurry from the scene of their exploit, experience

innumerable danger, difficulty, and hairbreadth escape, ere they

arrive at that sanctuary for political refugee, the city of the

Sultan. have by these mean regain his energy and vigour of mind,

Lewis apply himself heart and soul to the study of his new profession,

and in the interest of the pursuit keep his power, mental and bodily,

so fully employ as to hold memory at bay, and to require neither

society nor sympathy. but now a change have again come o’er he; he have

in great measure master the difficulty of his art, he have solve the

problem whether by his talent he could secure a competency for himself

and those belong to he; constant and indefatigable labour be no

long an obligation, and ere the Leicesters discover he he have begin

to feel, though he would scarcely acknowledge it even to himself, the

want of those social tie from which, in his first frenzy of grief, he

have voluntarily separate himself. in the society of the Leicesters

he obtain exactly the amount of relaxation which he require--Laura

appreciate and understand he, Charles, without understanding, like

he; while on his part, the lady’s society interested and soothe he,

and that of her husband afford he amusement and companionship.



as the day approach on which the Broadhurst party be expect to

arrive, Laura become considerably perplex as to how she might well

break the matter to Lewis: she have once, by way of experiment, mention

to her husband, in Lewis’s presence, the fact that she have receive

a letter from Broadhurst, and the start he give at the name, the

death-like paleness which overspread his countenance, the quivering lip,

and clench hand, tell of such deep mental suffering, that, frighten

at the effect she have produce, Laura immediately change the subject

and have never again venture to allude to it.



the last sitting for the picture chance to be fix for the very

morning before that on which the Grants be expect to arrive. Laura

consult her husband as to the affair: Charley stroke his chin,

caress his whisker, gaze vacantly at himself in the chimney-glass,

and then, put on a look of sapient self-confidence, in regard to the

“why, it seem to I, my love, that the present be exactly one of those

advise you to feel your way with great caution, very great caution, and

when by this mean you have ascertain the good method of break

and--and----”



“I think you have well undertake the business yourself, Charley dear,

as you seem to have such a clearly define idea how to set about it,”

 interrupt Laura with a roguish smile.



“not at all; by no mean, my dear,” reply Charley, speak with

unwonted energy. “a--in fact, so strongly do I feel that a _woman’s_

tact be the thing require, and that any interference of mine might

ruin the whole affair, and, in short, bring about something very

disagreeable, that I have make arrangement which will keep I from home

during the whole morning, so as to leave you a clear field.”



“oh, you dreadfully transparent old impostor! a child of five year

old could see through you,” exclaim Laura, laugh heartily at the

detect look which instantly steal over her husband’s visage. “now, if

you don’t honestly confess that you have not an idea how to get over the

difficulty,” she continue, “that you dread a scene with a true degree

of masculine horror, and yet have not the most remote notion how to

avoid one, I’ll ‘make arrangement which will take _I_ from home all

the morning,’ and leave you to flounder through the affair as good you

can.”



“there be a vixen for you,” exclaim Charley, appeal to society

at large. “poor Socrates! I always have a deep commiseration for his

domestic annoyance when I read of they at school, but I little dream

that I should live to have personal experience of the misery of

possess a Xantippe;” then throw himself into a mock-tragic

attitude, he ejaculate, “ungrateful woman! I leave you to your fate,” and

shake his fist at she, press his hand to his forehead, and rush

distractedly out of the room--in less than two minute he lounge

in again, draw on his glove. “what a bore tight glove be!” he

wife’s side, languidly hold out his hand, while with the most

helpless air imaginable he allow she to pull on the refractory

glove for he, which she do with a most amusing display of energy and

perseverance.



“_Voilà_, Monsieur!” she say; “that herculean feat be accomplish. have

you aught else to command your slave?”



Charley regard she with a look of affection as he reply, “what a

blessing it be to have a good, clever little wife to do all the horrid

thing for one! good-bye, my own! when you have do victimise Arundel

with your alarming intelligence, ask he to dine with we to-day; I want

particularly to talk to he. he know the people here well than I do;

but it strike I the politic of the place be get into a fix.”



so say, he imprint a kiss upon her brow, admire his hand in

the new, well-fitting glove, and saunter out of the apartment as

listlessly as though he be walk in his sleep.



Punctual to his appointment, Lewis arrive, look so handsome

and animate that Laura feel doubly grieve at have to make a

communication which she be persuade would tend to renew the memory of

a grief against which he appear to have struggle with some degree of

success. her task be render the more difficult from the conviction

that Lewis’s intercourse with her husband and herself have be of great

service to he, by insensibly overcome his misanthropic distaste to

society. this intercourse, she fear, the tiding she be about to

impart to he would effectually interrupt.



“where be ‘Tarley’?” inquire Lewis, after exchange salutation with

“_La Madre_.”



“in the nursery, adorn for the sacrifice of his personal freedom

during the period you may require he to remain _en position_.” answer

Laura; “shall I ring for he?”



“may I fetch he myself? I promise he a ride on my back for good

conduct at the last sitting, and he must not be disappoint,” urge

Lewis in reply.



“agree--always promise that you take great care not to tumble the

clean frock,” return Laura with a gratified smile. “who could believe

that man be the same creature who use to look so stern, and cold, and

proud?” she add mentally, as Lewis depart on his mission; “he have

as much tenderness of nature as any woman. if he really do love Annie,

and she can prefer Lord Bellefield, she deserve all the unhappiness

be good?” she continue, as that self-willed juvenile enter, seat in

triumph upon Lewis’s shoulder, and grasp a lock of his horse’s ebon

mane the well to preserve his balance.



“Tarley” having signify in the very small break English his

intention to keep the peace to the good of his little ability, the

sitting begin in good earnest, and terminate, as far as that young

gentleman be concern, in less than an hour, during which period, as

he only tear his mamma’s gown once, make a hole in the sofa-cover, and

have one violent fit of kicking, he may comparatively be consider (all

still remain to complete Laura’s portrait, and these Lewis hasten to

add. the conversation (originate in “Tarley’s” _escapade_) turn on

education.



“the theory which I hold to be the true one be simple enough,” remark

Lewis; “the first thing to inculcate be--oblige I by turn a little

more to the light--implicit obedience; that once acquire--rather more

still--you may, as the mind develop, occasionally give a reason for

your command--you see my object be to get a clear light on the left

eye-brow--thank you; don’t move.”



“but that obedience, to be of much avail, should be found on other

feeling than mere fear of punishment,” return Laura; “for that in

sturdy mind produce obstinacy, in weak one deceit and falsehood, and

in both case necessarily lose its effect as the pupil advance towards

maturity. it always appear to I that in our conduct towards child

we should strive to imitate (with reverence be it speak) God’s dealing

towards ourselves. we should teach they to love and trust we, and

obedience base on affection and faith will surely never fail for time

or for eternity. then,” she continue, as Lewis, bend over his work,

fail to reply, “I should endeavour to make their punishment appear as

much as possible the natural consequence of their fault; for instance,

I should allow they to experience to the uttermost the mental suffering

cause by pride and anger, and in their cooler moment point out to they

that it may be wise, as well as right, to suffer even injustice mildly,

rather than bear the distress of mind a contrary line of conduct be sure

to entail. I should impress upon they the evil of covet by deny

they the thing they so eagerly seek. in fact,” she add hastily,

fancy from her companion’s silence that for some reason her

conversation be distasteful to he, “I have a great many sapient,

theoretical idea in regard to education, but how they may turn out when

I come to put they in practice remain to be prove.”



Lewis, who during the conclusion of this speech have be paint away

as zealously as if his life depend upon his exertion, though a close

observer might have remark, by his downcast eye and quivering lip, the

effect Laura’s word produce on he, reply earnestly--



“would to Heaven all mother feel as truly and wisely as you do about

education; be child teach such principle of self-government as

you propose, there would be few aching heart among we.”



having utter these word and sigh deeply, he speak no more until he

have finish Laura’s portrait.



“there,” he say, “I need detain you no long; with the exception of a

few touch to the drapery, which I can do at my own room, the picture

be complete.”



Laura approach and duly admire it, declare the likeness of “Tarley”

 to be perfect, but feel quite certain Lewis have flatter she

terribly, at which little touch of woman’s nature the young artist

smile as he deny the accusation. and now the moment have arrive

when Laura must break her intelligence to he as well she might. her

straightforward, simple nature disdain all subterfuge, and she begin

accordingly.



“there be a topic which, from a fear, perhaps uncalled for, of give

you pain, Charles and I have avoid, but which I be now compel to

mention to you. you ask I at our first meeting whether we be alone;

after to-day we shall be so no long, and the guest we expect be none

other than your former pupil Walter, General Grant, and his daughter.”

 Laura have purposely place herself in such a position that she could

not see her companion’s feature as she make this communication, and the

only sign of agitation which meet her ear be the sound of his quick and

laboured breathing.



after a moment’s pause he say in a hurried, stem tone of voice, “I

cannot meet they! it be impossible, I must leave this place directly.”



“Nay, that surely be unnecessary, no one here know you but ourselves;

you have only to resume your incognito, and in Signore Luigi, the

venetian painter, no one will recognise Lewis Arundel. we will keep your

secret inviolably.”



“can I rely on the discretion of Mr. Leicester?”



“perfectly; if he know you consider the matter important, he will

remain silent as the grave.”



“be it so then,” return Lewis after a pause. having pace up and down

the room, he throw himself on a sofa, and cover his eye with his

hand, remained bury in painful thought.



Laura watch he with deep interest, till at length she could restrain

the expression of her sympathy no long.



“I _must_ speak that which be in my mind,” she say earnestly. “I know

that you be good and true-hearted, you _can_ have do no wrong that

you have cause to be ashamed of, why then do you fear to meet these

people?”



Lewis start, raise his head, and fling back his dark hair,

exclaim almost fiercely, “do you say fear? I fear no living be!

there be no man who can accuse I of evil-doing; my name be as spotless

as your own pure soul.”



“then why refuse to meet they?”



“because I fear my own heart,” be the vehement reply, “because I have

swear never to meet she again till I have learn to look upon she

with the indifference her weak fickleness deserve, and that,” he add

bitterly, “will not be till grey hair bring insensibility to woman’s

love and such-like gilded toy, or till she have crush out the last

germ of my linger madness by marry the heartless scoundrel to

whom she be engage.” he pause, and then continue more calmly, “you

ask I why I refuse to meet these people; hear the truth, and then judge

for yourself whether I can meet they; nay, judge for I also if you

will, for I be half-frenzied by the anguish I have suffer, and be as

incapable to decide for myself in this affair as a child, such puppet

be we to our love and hate;” and then in eager, hurried accent he

tell she of his love for Annie Grant, his struggle for self-conquest,

his signal failure, his fearful hope that she return his affection,

the parting, his confession to the General, the strange tiding he

have learn in London, and then the cruel paralyse blow of Annie’s

engagement, renew the very day after he have leave Broadhurst, believe

on no slight ground that she love he and he only. all the burning

sorrow, pen for two long year within his secret soul, he pour forth

before she; and Laura listen with glowing cheek and tearful eye,

and a grow resolve in her brave, pure heart to set aside all

conventionalism and every hollow form of society, and if Annie should

but prove worthy of he, to labour with all the energy of her earnest

nature to bring these young, sad, love heart together again.









CHAPTER LVI.--LEWIS ATTENDS an evening PARTY, and NARROWLY escapes being

“CUT” by an old ACQUAINTANCE.



“now listen to I, and be good, and sensible, and tractable for once in

your life,” exclaim Laura, when Lewis’s agitation have in some degree

subsided. “you appear to have act with more than sufficient self-will

and impetuosity all through this affair, and the result have not prove

so satisfactory as to justify you in refuse a friend’s advice and

assistance. excuse my plain speak,” she continue, with a frank smile

which would have thaw the moroseness of the most churlish misanthrope

who ever reverse the precept of Christianity by hate his neighbour,

“but I must either say all I think or be wholly silent. besides, it be

no kindness to hide the truth from you.”



“what would you have I do?” return Lewis sadly. “believe I, I

reproach myself for my past folly more bitterly than you could do be

you my bad enemy, instead of the gentle, zealous friend you be.”



“I would not have you at present do anything, more especially anything

rash,” return Laura, “but simply leave the matter in my hand.”



“promise me----” begin Lewis.



“I promise you I will do nothing which can in the small degree

compromise your honour, or even your pride,” return Laura, with the

slight possible degree of sarcasm in her tone; “beyond this I will

promise you nothing, and if you have not sufficient faith to trust my

friendship thus far, you be less worthy of it than I have deem you.”



Lewis glance with mingled surprise and admiration at the animate

feature of his spirited confidante. accustomed to Rose’s calm,

persuasive reasoning, and the half-earnest, half-playful, but wholly

bewitch manner of sweet Annie Grant, Laura’s keen wit and fearless

bear surprised and please, while at the same time they insensibly

influence he. “I _will_ trust you,” he say; “you have the strong

sense and bold energy of a man’s mind unite with all the gentleness and

refinement which be woman’s especial attribute. I will and do trust

you fully. but alas! dear friend,” he continue sorrowfully, “neither

you nor any one else can remove the cause of my unhappiness. I will not

attempt to deceive you or myself; despite my good endeavour to forget

she I cannot, and I be miserable. I, who deem myself so strong, be

powerless to cast this affection from I; and though I despise she

for her weak fickleness, though I scorn she for allow herself to be

contract to that man of whom I never can bear to think as the brother

of your kind-hearted, liberal-minded husband, I yet love she with the

reasonless passion of an idiot.”



“you take too gloomy a view of the affair. she may not be so much to

blame as you imagine; she may yet prove worthy of your affection,” urge

Laura.



“would to Heaven it could be so!” exclaim Lewis vehemently. “you

bid I consider the matter calmly and sensibly,” he continue, after

a pause; “by do so I perceive the hope with which you would fain
