inspire I to be unreasonable and delusive. fact speak for themselves,

and as they remain unalterable, so must my grief. either she do not

return my affection, and be attach to her intended bridegroom, or,

love I, she have with the most culpable weakness allow herself to

be persuade into an engagement with a man every way unworthy of she, to

whom she be, to say the least, indifferent; and this, not in consequence

of a lengthened persecution, but within twenty-four hour after I have

leave she, fondly deeming that have fate allow I to ask her hand she

would not have refuse it.”



“it be very strange, very unaccountable,” return Laura, musing, “so

much so, indeed, that I feel sure we do not yet know the whole

truth, and that there must be some way of explain her conduct

satisfactorily.”



Lewis shake his head mournfully.



“Farewell,” he say; “you will soon be able to judge for yourself, and

will find that the view I take of the affair, gloomy as it may appear,

be indeed the only true one.”



“you will dine with we to-day? Charles particularly wish it; you must

not refuse. remember, it will be the last time for some week that I may

have an opportunity of see you!” plead Laura.



“I do not know why I consent, except that it seem impossible to say

no to you,” return Lewis, unable to resist the influence of Laura’s

sympathetic kindness. “you will find I but a dull companion,” he

continue with a deep sigh, “for your intelligence have completely

unmanned I.”



“we will take the chance of that,” reply Laura with an incredulous

smile; and so, shake hand, they part.



the dinner pass off heavily enough. Lewis, despite his effort to the

contrary, appear out of spirit and _distrait_. Charles, having be

caution and tutor to the utmost extent of female foresight as to

what he be to say and what to avoid, grow nervous and puzzle-pat;

call Laura, Annie, and ask Lewis why he do not send for Miss Grant

(mean his, Lewis’s, sister Rose) to live with he and keep his house;

by which blunder he provoke his wife to such a degree that she could

have find in her heart to box his ear for he, without the small

compunction. the arrival of “Tarley” and the dessert produce a marked

improvement, that young gentleman be in the high possible state of

health and spirit, and influence by a strong determination to partake

of everything on the table, wine include, to ignore all establish

precedent as to eat jam by the intervention of a spoon, to consider

walnut appropriate missile to throw at the company generally, and

the cut-glass decanter in particular, to set maternal authority at

defiance, as evince by a resolution to pull off his left shoe and imbe

it in orange marmalade, and in fact to do everything which appear good

in his eye and naughty in those of his elder, and then and there to

make a night of it. these little antic, at first amusing, and secretly

patronise and foster by Charles and Lewis, soon become tiresome,

and at length unbearable, Laura assert her prerogative, and

disregard much kicking and a hysterical affection, which be neither

laugh nor cry, but a compound of the two, succeed in carry

away her unruly offspring. when the gentleman be leave to themselves,

Leicester, fill his glass and hand the bottle to Lewis, began--



“do you mix much with the young man of the place, so as to judge of

their political bias at all?”



“I be acquaint with some dozen, or more, young artist, though I do

not enter much into their pursuit, from want of inclination; although,

at first, they press I to belong to their club. I should say,

however, judge from their conversation, that democratic principle

be rife among they.”



“I fear so; indeed, from information we have receive, I should not

be surprised if some attempt be likely to be make to throw off the

Austrian yoke.”



“surely that would be great folly,” return Lewis; “with the troop and

resource the Governor, Count Palffy, have at his command, any popular

tumult might easily be quell. it be only from cowardice or inaction

on the part of the authority that any of these success in Northern

Italy have be achieve.”



“ay, but inaction be just what I fear,” rejoin Leicester; “the

Austrians will not believe in the amount of popular disaffection which

exist; they will go on ignore the danger till the moment at which it

could be most successfully combat have escape they. not that I care

very much about the matter; I be neither Trojan nor Tyrian; but I be

anxious to gain some certainty as to the chance of a popular outbreak,

that I may take measure to provide for the safety of Laura and the

child: besides, I think you be aware we have some guest come to we;

have I know this soon I should have write to they to postpone their

visit till some more favourable opportunity.”



“I will investigate the matter,” return Lewis eagerly, “and will

communicate to you any information I may obtain; woman should never be

expose to the chance of witness the horror of street warfare.”



after converse on this topic for some minute long, the gentleman,

be neither of they addict to the practice of wine-bibbing, follow

Laura to the drawing-room. Lewis appear silent and depressed, and

a gloom hang over the little party which no effort on the part of the

hostess could dispel.



soon after ten o’clock their guest rise to take leave.



“I shall send Charles to you very often; and if possible, without

attract attention, I shall occasionally come with he,” observe

Laura; “so mind you be not to freeze up again into a marble

misanthrope: I consider I have improve you vastly since you have be

under my tuition, and I by no mean desire to have labour in vain.”



“you have show I kindness which I may never be able to repay,”

 answer Lewis; “but to prove that I neither forget nor feel ungrateful

for it, I will struggle against the fault you so justly reprobate: if

I sometimes fail, you must remember that it be difficult to preserve a

cheerful, easy manner with an aching heart, and so pardon I.”



having take a cordial leave of his host and hostess, and refuse

Charles’s offer of walk home with he, partly because he know it

would be an act of self-denial in his friend to relinquish his wife’s

society, partly because he wish to be alone, Lewis quit the Palazzo

Grassini and stroll on in the direction of his own abode. as he

pass under the Piazza of St. Mark, a particularly beautiful effect of

moonlight on the opposite building strike he, and lean against one

of the column, he pause to observe it. the place where he be stand

be in deep shadow, and to any one approach from the leave his

figure be invisible, the massive column effectually hide it. having

thoroughly fix in his recollection the appearance which have attract

he, and which he propose to transfer to canvas, he be about to quit

the Piazza when a figure wrap in a dark mantle advance with a quick

yet stealthy tread.



as the new-comer approach the spot where Lewis be station a low

whistle pierce the air, and immediately a second figure, also disguise

in a dark robe, appear from behind a pillar which have hitherto

conceal he, and address the other, observed--



“you be late; I have wait for you.”



“the delay be unavoidable, Signor,” be the reply; “I be force to

wait myself for Paulo, as until I have see he I could not bring you the

password.”



“and what be it?” inquire the first speaker eagerly. the other glanced

round with a suspicious air as he reply, “I Martiri di Cosenza.” *



* the brother Bandiera, two youth of high patrician venetian descent,

be denounce to the austrian government, and shoot as conspirator at

Cosenza, June 25th, 1844.



“good!” be the rejoinder; “and the place of meeting?”



“the great Hall of the Palazzo--iani,” name one of the many ruin

palace which be to be find in Venice.



“wisely choose,” observe the first speaker, who appear of a rank

superior to that of his companion; “the time of meeting must be at

hand?”



“if Vossignoria proceed thither leisurely, the hour will strike as you

reach the appoint rendezvous.”



“’tis well,” be the reply. “now leave I; we must not be see

together.”



the person address raise his cap as a token of respect, and turning,

hurry from the spot--his confederate pause a moment as if in

deliberation, and then stroll leisurely away in the direction of the

Palazzo--iani. Lewis wait till the echo of his retreating footstep

die away in the distance, then start in the direction of his own

dwelling, he walk with rapid stride till he reach the corner of

one of the less-frequent street; having do so, he strike down it,

run at a pace which few could have keep up with till he approach

his own house, when he again moderate his speed. let himself in

with a private key, he enter his sitting-room, take a brace of small

pistol from a drawer, load they carefully, and conceal they in a

breast-pocket, fling a dark cloak over his shoulder and again quit

the room. his determination be take. accident having put he in

possession of the time and place of some secret meeting, as well as the

pass-word which he doubt not would ensure his admission, his love of

adventure occasion he instantly to resolve to be present at it. the

assembly be doubtless of a political nature, and besides gratify his

taste for excitement, he might obtain some information in regard to

the probability of a popular insurrection, and thus satisfy Leicester’s

anxiety for the safety of his wife and child--in which (though Lewis

would not own the motive even to himself) might be involve that of

Annie Grant. that the expedition he project be a dangerous one he be

well aware, but he trust to chance and to his own tact and presence

of mind to save he from discovery, and in case of these fail he, he

possess the pistol as a last resource. twenty minute’ brisk walking

bring he beneath the wall of the Palazzo.



pause under the shadow of the building, he wait till he have see

two or three person, carefully muffle up, proceed in a particular

direction. conjecture from their appearance and evident desire to

escape observation that they be bind on the same errand as himself,

he follow with a quick but noiseless step the next man who pass.

this person walk on rapidly till he reach a small archway; here

he stop and look round, as if to assure himself that he be not

follow, when, perceive Lewis, he seem embarrassed, and after a

moment’s deliberation, during which he scrutinise the young artist’s

figure narrowly, he station himself in the centre of the path, as if

to intercept Lewis’s further progress. as he approach the stranger

advance a step to meet he, observe in Italian--



“the Signor walk late, and choose a strange path; may I venture to

inquire his object in so do?”



“the same as your own,” return Lewis sternly; add in a tone of

command, “we be too late already, lead the way.”



the person thus address, in whom, from a slight peculiarity in his

accent, Lewis recognise he who have appear the inferior of the two

speaker whose conversation he have overhear in the Piazza of St. Mark,

seem for a moment undecided how to act; and then, either deceive by

Lewis’s manner, or purpose to postpone any further investigation till

he should obtain the assistance of the other conspirator, he pass

through the archway, and turn abruptly to the right hand, run up

a flight of stone step terminate by a low door closely stud with

large iron nail head. give a low whistle, some one from within

partially open the door and the stranger enter, follow by Lewis.

the moment he have do so, the door be shut and bolt behind he, and

he find himself in total darkness; at the same instant he feel his arm

pinion by a powerful grasp, while a gruff voice exclaimed--



“give the pass-word!”



“_I Martiri di Cosenza_,” reply Lewis firmly.



“proceed,” be the rejoinder, as the grasp be remove from his arm,

and the light of a dark lantern be throw along the narrow stone

passage in which Lewis now find himself. having traverse this, a

second door open at his approach, a rush of cold air stream upon

he, and he find himself in a large dimly-light chamber, in which

be assemble somewhere about thirty person, who be gather round

a long table, at the upper end of which stand a man, who, with his arm

extend, and his whole bearing indicative of strong excitement, be

address the meeting. draw the collar of his cloak more over his

face, and choose a spot where the shadow of one of the heavy column

which support the roof serve in some measure to conceal he, Lewis

join the group. as he do so, the speaker, glance with flash eye

round the assembly, exclaimed--



“we be resolve, then--the cup be full to overflow--we will bow no

long beneath the yoke of foreign tyrant. our brother in Milan have

set we a glorious example--the accursed Austrian already tremble before

their valour. Italy have shake off her lethargy;--we have only to be

true to ourselves and to the glorious cause, and liberty await our

effort.”



a subdued murmur of consent and approbation run through the assembly,

and the speaker continued--



“thus agree, then, it only remain for we to _act_, and our first duty

be to succour those who have suffer for our sake. those hero, those

martyr to the cause of the venetian people, Daniel Manin and Niccolo

Tommaseo, languish in an unjust imprisonment; we will demand their

liberation, and that with a voice that shall force the tyrant to

listen--the voice of an awakened and indignant nation.”



as the speaker cease, amidst a subdue buzz of approbation, a man in

the dress of an artisan arose, and roll his fierce blood-shoot eye

around the assembly, exclaimed--



“yes, brother, we will liberate our brave compatriot--Manin and

Tommaseo shall be set free to aid in the struggle for our liberty; but

we must do more, Venice must rise and cast out those foreign butcher. a

blow must be deal which shall strike terror into their coward heart; a

blow which shall prove to they the fate they may expect, if they dare to

oppress and withstand a people struggle for their freedom. and on

whom can it so justly fall as on the arch-tyrant, sell hand and soul

to Austria, thirst only for vengeance and for murder--the base

persecutor Marinovich?”



he pause; there be a moment’s silence, and then a low whisper go

round the assembly, “death to Marinovich!” there be again a pause, and

then man begin to communicate with one another in deep muttered tone.

after a short interval the first speaker, who have be write rapidly,

arise, and again address they, said--



“we be, then, agree; and our first act shall be the liberation

of Manin and Tommaseo. it be time that we disperse as silently and

cautiously as may be; we must creep now that we may soar hereafter.”



in order not to interrupt the thread of our narrative, we have describe

the proceeding as they occur--we must now revert to Lewis. during

the delivery of the first speech he observe that the man who have

address he as he enter, and who appear a tall, muscular young

fellow, have contrive to place himself by his side, and be regard

he from time to time with look of mistrust and suspicion. at the

proposal for the assassination of Colonel Marinovich, the commandant at

the Arsenal, a man who, though a strict disciplinarian, Lewis know by

report to be a brave and gallant officer, he have be unable to repress

some slight sign of disapprobation. as he do so he perceive a scowl

pass across the feature of his watcher, who take the opportunity of

draw yet near to he, while an accidental movement reveal the

unpleasant fact that he hold in his hand a naked stiletto. as the

president end his final address, Lewis, who have keep his eye fix

on the feature of his dangerous neighbour, feel convinced that the man

only await the termination of the business proceeding to denounce

he to his fellow-conspirator. with his usual coolness and decision

in moment of danger, Lewis see that his only chance of safety lie in

take the initiative; accordingly, catch the man’s eye, he fix on

he a pierce glance, as he say in a stern whisper--



“the first word you utter aloud you be a dead man;” at the same moment

he present the muzzle of a pistol within an inch of his ear. the man

start slightly and attempt to increase the distance between they,

but Lewis lay an iron grasp on his collar and detain he; having

stand for a moment irresolute, he say, in the same low whisper in which

Lewis have address him--



“you be an austrian spy.”



“I be not,” return Lewis; “I be an Englishman.”



the other again start, regard he fixedly, and then resumed--



“swear by all you hold sacred never to reveal that which you have

learn to-night.”



“I will swear nothing, except to blow out your brain if you attempt

to speak or move without my permission,” be the stern, uncompromising

rejoinder.



the stranger’s lip quiver and his grasp tighten on the stiletto, but

he catch the glance of Lewis’s flash eye and feel that he be in

earnest, and that his life hang upon a thread. the member of the secret

association be by this time noiselessly glide away in party of two

and three, and Lewis, fear if he remain too long he might attract

the attention of the president, who still continue write at the

table, determine to depart; accordingly, he say in a low whisper--



“now we will go--precede I; but if I observe you attempt, by word or

sign, to betray I, that moment I shoot you like a dog.”



the stranger, who seem by this time sullenly to have resign himself

to his fate, or possibly to be reserve his strength for the execution

of some scheme which he have devise for the future, obey in silence,

and leave the vault closely follow by Lewis, who still retain a firm

grasp of his collar, although the ample fold of his cloak prevent the

fact from be observe. in this manner they reach the door at

the top of the stair, and here be station two brawny-limbe,

ruffianly-look fellow, who act in the double capacity of porter

and sentry. their attention, however, appear solely direct to

prevent the intrusion of any unwelcome visitant, the advisability of

refuse egress to any one who have already pass their scrutiny never

seem to occur to they. this Lewis feel to be the decide moment

of his fate; once outside the gate he would be in comparative safety.

press the muzzle of the pistol against the back of his companion’s

neck by way of a gentle hint, he mutter, “remember!”



the young man shudder slightly as the cold iron touch he, but make

no reply. as they reach the gateway, the janitor station on the left

side, address Lewis’s companion, make some inquiry in a low voice.

Glancing round appealingly, as if to indicate that he be force, even

for their common safety, to reply, he speak a few word in a dialect

Lewis do not comprehend, when the gate-keeper respectfully hold the

wicket open and they pass out. and now once again Lewis feel that he

be a free man, and he inwardly congratulate himself on having escape

so great peril, which congratulation be, as the event prove,

somewhat premature.



having descend the step, Lewis loosen his hold on the stranger’s

collar, say carelessly, as he replace his pistol in his breast--



“there, young gentleman; thank to your prudence and my precaution of

bring a brace of pistol with I, I have draw my head out of the

lion’s mouth without have it bite off for my pain. but now I want

to have a little serious conversation with you.”



“wait till we be far from the Palazzo--iani, then,” be the reply,

in a voice that yet tremble from excitement or some other deep emotion;

“we may be overhear; keep more in the shade of the building.”



suspect no treachery, Lewis comply. Scarcely have he do so,

however, when he fancy he hear a stealthy footstep follow he, and

turn abruptly, find himself face to face with a tall, savage-look

ruffian, who, arm with a naked stiletto, be evidently meditate

mischief. confuse by his sudden motion, the fellow stand for a moment

irresolute; not so his intended victim. the path along which he have be

proceeding follow the course of one of the small _rii_ or canal by

which Venice be in so many direction intersect. avail himself of

this circumstance, Lewis roll his cloak round his arm and spring upon

his assailant, parrying, with the shield thus constitute, a hasty and

ineffectual stab which the other make at he. foil in his attempt,

the ruffian draw back to avoid Lewis’s onset, thereby approach

incautiously too near the bank of the canal. his antagonist be not

slow to perceive the opportunity thus afford he. follow up his

retreating foe so as to prevent he from turn to perceive his danger,

he wait till the man reach the brink of the canal, then stretch

out his foot, he trip he up, and parry a second stab as he have

do the former one, push he over the bank, which at that part be

somewhat steep. a heavy fall and a loud splash in the water announce

that his stratagem have succeed, but at the same moment he feel his

throat compress by a powerful grasp, a naked stiletto flash before

he, and the eye of the young conspirator, burn with hatred and

revenge, glare at he through the darkness with the ferocity of

those of some savage animal. up to this point Lewis’s courage and

self-possession have never for a moment fail he, but now a strange,

wild idea occur to he, and a horrible dread suddenly overwhelm

he: his sense reel, his limb tremble, and for the first time

in his life he experience the mental agony of fear. instinctively he

seize the uplifted wrist of his assailant, and gaze with start

eye-ball at his face, on which the cold moonlight stream. yes! there

could be no doubt: in the feature of the being with whom he be engage

in deadly conflict he recognise a dark, shadowy, but most unmistakable

resemblance to Hardy the poacher. be it incipient madness, or be

he thus horribly to be convince of the reality of tale which he have

hitherto deem the mere drivelling of superstition?--could the dead

indeed rise from their grave to seek vengeance on their slayer?



as these thought flash meteor-like through his brain, his antagonist

make a violent but ineffectual effort to free his wrist, and this action

in great measure restore Lewis’s self-possession. ghost have not thews

and sinew, and even in that moment of peril a flush of shame at his

childish terror spread over his brow, and the impulse seem to rend

redouble vigour to his frame. consequently the struggle, though

severe, be short. superior in strength to his assailant, Lewis, having

succeed in wrest the dagger from his grasp, hurl it into the

canal, leave he completely unarmed and at his mercy. the stranger be

the first to speak. fold his arm across his breast with an air of

dog resolution, he say, speak for the first time in English, and

without the slight foreign accent--



“you be wrong to throw away that weapon; it would have do your work

as effectually and more silently than the pistol.”



“you consider your life as forfeit, then?” inquire Lewis.



“I expect you to do by I as I would have do by you,” be the concise

reply.



“I be no assassin,” return Lewis coldly; “and that remind I of

your worthy associate. you engage my attention, so that I be ignorant

whether he sink or swam.”



“never fear for honest Jacopo,” be the answer; “he follow the calling

of a gondolier when his stiletto be not in requisition, and can swim

like a fish. look yonder; he have gain the shore, and be even now

watch we.”



as he speak, Lewis observe a tall figure crouch under a project

portion of the bank of the canal.



“he will not molest you far,” continue his late antagonist; “once

foil in his spring, like the tiger, he will not renew the attack. have

he slay you I should have pay he five _zwanziger_; as it be, the

poor fellow will only get his ducking for his pain.”



“why do he follow we?” ask Lewis.



“when you enter I give he a hint not to let you pass on your return;

have he attempt to stop you, however, I believe you would shoot I,

therefore, think I could obtain your death or capture without

lose my own life, I give he a glance by which he know he be not to

interrupt you. he then ask I in the thief’ patois of this place

what he be to do, and I tell he to _follow_ we, as you be a spy. you

know the rest.”



Lewis pause for a moment, and then say abruptly, “you be an

Englishman?”



“I be.”



“you will accompany I to my room,” rejoin Lewis; “I would question

you far.”



“for what purpose?”



“that you will learn at the fitting time,” return Lewis.



“what if I refuse?”



“I will summon the police, and if you attempt to escape, I will shoot

you through the head,” be the stern rejoinder.



“I will go with you,” reply the stranger; “but I warn you I will not

be arrest: my liberty be dear to I, my life I hold cheap--so cheap

that even now, unarmed as I be, and unequal to you in muscular

strength, I be tempt again to rush on you and try the chance of a

death-struggle.”



“I would advise you not to do so,” return Lewis calmly; “besides,” he

add, “I may be more disposed to befriend you than you be aware of--it

be with no hostile purpose I thus force you to accompany I, believe

I.”



“I will trust you,” be the reply. “your look and word have, I know

not why, a strange power over I--you must possess the gift of the

_Malocchio_, which these Italians believe in--it be your glance, far

more than your pistol, which keep I silent in the chamber of meeting.”



during almost the whole of this conversation they have be walk

side by side in the direction of the street in which Lewis’s studio be

situate, and in another five minute they reach it.



“have I your word of honour that you will not again attempt my life, or

seek to escape till our interview be conclude?” ask Lewis.



“you have,” be the concise reply.



“follow I, then,” continue Lewis; and draw a key from his pocket,

he unfasten the door, enter, close it again, and accompany by the

stranger, lead the way through the painting-room into his study.









CHAPTER LVII.--WALTER see a ghost.



Lewis, having light a powerful lamp by the aid of which he be

accustomed to paint at night, be enable to take a more particular

survey of his new acquaintance than circumstance have yet permit.

he be a tall, powerfully-build stripling, with a dark complexion and

handsome feature, but although he could scarcely have number twenty

year, his face wear a prematurely old expression, and there be a wild,

reckless look in his eye which tell of a spirit ill at ease. he wear a

sailor’s dress, though the material of which it be compose be of

a fine quality than ordinary; he coldly refuse the chair which Lewis

offer he, and fold his arm across his breast, wait to be

question. Lewis in the meantime take his seat at the table, place the

pistol on the desk before he, and fix his pierce glance on the

face of his captive, began--



“my knowledge of you be this: I find you an active and zealous member of

a conspiracy to overthrow the Austrian Government in this city--one of

a set of conspirator whose first act be to be the assassination of

Colonel Marinovich, commandant of the Arsenal. as far as I be concern,

you first resolve to denounce I to your associate as a spy; foil in

that attempt, you incite an accomplice to murder I, and on his failure,

use your good endeavour to stab I yourself; in the struggle I disarm

you, and you find yourself in the power of the man for whose blood you

have be thirst. even allow, for the sake of argument, that

you be justify in seek the life of one who might betray your

treasonous design, you still remain a convict conspirator, and my

natural course would be to hand you over to the police; for your threat

of never be take alive be absurd, since you lose your stiletto I

could have capture you at any moment I please. however, the fact of

your be an Englishman interest I in your behalf, and if you will

answer my question frankly and truly, I may be induce to let you off.

in the first place, tell I who you be, and enough of your former life

to enable I to understand how I find you thus plot with foreigner

with whom you can have no feeling in common, for an evil purpose.”



“I can soon satisfy you, if that be all you require,” be the reply.

“my life have from its commencement be a curse to myself and to other.

Wrong have produce wrong; I be badly bring up, and I have turn out

badly; I be not the first that have do so, nor shall I be the last.

at the age when most child be carefully train to good, I be as

sedulously instruct in evil. at twelve year old I could swear, game,

and drink, and my instructor laugh to see the boy ape the vice of

the man. my mother die in give I birth; my father, I know not why,

never love I: he use I cruelly, and I hate he for it; so I leave my

home and work for four year on board a man-of-war. at the end of

that time the ship be pay off. seek pleasure, I fall into vicious

company; squander, and be rob of my pay, and for some week I

wander a houseless beggar through London street. the chance kindness

of a stranger rescue I from that state of wretchedness”--a peculiar

expression flit across the feature of his auditor as he mention

the fact of his rescue from beggary; not observe it, he continue:

“I then enter the merchant-service, and speedily rise to the rank

of mate. the misery I have undergone render I more careful. I save

money, study my profession, and hope in time to become a captain of a

merchantman. I embark the whole of my saving in a trading speculation

which would more than have double they, when the ship contain my

property be wreck. I be pick up by a vessel bind to this port,

and be land here again a beggar; and after try in vain to procure

any well situation, I have be force to work in the arsenal as

a common labourer to save myself from starvation. but even there my

ill-fortune and the cruelty and injustice of man follow I. peculation

to a great extent be discover amongst the workman; I be examine

before Colonel Marinovich; in vain I protest my innocence. God know

I have commit sin enough; but thieve and lying be never among

they. however, I be condemn to receive forty lash. yes, sir; I,

an Englishman, innocent of the crime of which I be accuse, be beat

like a slave by the order of a tyrannical foreigner; and now, perhaps,

you can tell what take I to the meeting to-night? it be the hope of

revenge, and there be other there with the same deadly purpose. the

man who propose the assassination of Marinovich be innocent as myself,

and like I have smart beneath the tyrant’s lash. you by reveal this

plot threaten to cheat we of our just revenge, and for that reason I

would have sacrifice your life. and now you know my history, what will

you do with I?”



there be a moment’s pause ere Lewis, fix his eye on he with a

clear, penetrate glance, say slowly and impressively, “there be a

few minor particular which appear to have escape your memory;

I will try to supply the deficiency. you be bear in the village

of B--------, in H----shire. your early instructor in evil be

the worthless character who accompany your father on his poaching

expedition. you leave home because in a drunken mood your father strike

you, and would not confess afterwards that he be sorry for so do.

you would have run away soon but for your affection for your sister

Jane. the stranger who rescue you from beggary be a young man who meet

you by chance at the door of a house in -------- Street, Russell Square;

you beg of he in Italian; the merchant-ship in which you serve,

to whose commander he give you an introduction, be the ‘beauty,’ of

Southsea, Captain Singleton, and your own name be Miles Hardy. be I not

correct in these particular?”



when Lewis begin speak his companion’s attention became rivet. as

he proceed his surprise grow deep and deeply; but when he mention

his name he spring forward, and regard he with wildly gleam eye,

exclaim, “tell I, what be you? man or devil? who thus know every

secret of my life.”



“I be no devil,” return Lewis, smile, “but a mortal like yourself;

you have see I before; look well at I; do you not recognise I?”



thus appeal to, the young man carefully scan his feature, and

then, in a low, hesitate voice, rejoin, “you be, or I be much

mistaken, the gentleman who rescue I from beggary.”



“you be right,” be the reply; “we be both much change since that

night, but I know you at the moment you seize I by the throat.”



“thank God, I do not succeed in take your life!” exclaim Miles

hardy earnestly; “you be almost the only person who have ever show I

disinterested kindness; and how have I seek to repay it! oh, sir, can

you forgive I?”



“the simple fact that you do not recognise I exonerate you from the

charge of ingratitude, my poor fellow,” return Lewis kindly; “but now

sit down. Ere I can explain to you how I gain the knowledge which have

so much surprised you, you have a long tale to listen to, and one which

will cause you much sorrow. you turn pale; wait, I will get you a glass

of wine.”



“it be nothing,” be the reply; “I have fast long; it will pass away

in a moment;” but as he speak he sink heavily into a chair which stand

beside he.



Lewis produce from a cupboard food and wine, and place they before

he, induce he to partake of some refreshment, and soon have the

satisfaction of see the light return to his eye and the colour to his

cheek. Lewis then fill for himself a glass of wine, replenish that

of his companion, and seat himself, hasten to relate to Miles Hardy

the strange train of event by mean of which he have become acquaint

with so large a portion of the young man’s history. the feeling with

which Miles Hardy listen to the account of his unhappy father’s

career, and the mingled grief and anger with which he hear how the

heritage of his mother’s shame have descend to his unfortunate sister,

may easily be imagine. Lewis strove with an amount of patient kindness,

for which those who know only the fiery side of his character would

scarcely have give he credit, to soothe the passionate emotion which

his tale excited in an auditor so deeply interested in the fortune of

those to whom it relate. after long perseverance his effort be in

some degree crown with success--Miles become more calm, and agree

with Lewis that his first duty be to seek for, and endeavour to

reclaim, his sister. his share of the legacy would furnish he with

fund sufficient to enable he to live without the necessity of daily

labour, and until his right to the money should be establish, Lewis

insist on become his banker. the next question be not so easily

arrange: Lewis inform Miles that in regard to the event of the

evening he have arrive at the following determination--viz., to call on

Colonel Marinovich, make he acquaint with the plot against his life,

beg he to inform his superior that such a conspiracy be on foot, and

explain the manner in which he have become aware of its existence; but as

far as Miles be concern in the affair, he would promise to preserve a

total silence, on one condition--namely, that he, Miles, should withdraw

from the conspiracy and engage to keep the peace in regard to the

commandant of the Arsenal. to this proposition the young man demur.



“what,” he say, “give up my just revenge!--submit to undeserved

chastisement like a beat hound, and leave it to less tame and slavish

spirit to punish the tyrant for his cruelty!--allow they to meet the

danger and divide the glory while I stand by inactive! never!”



“believe I, Miles,” return Lewis earnestly, “revenge, even just

revenge, partake of the nature of sin, and bring upon he who obtain

it the curse of an upbraid conscience. but yours be not a just

revenge; you have suffer wrong, and the sense of this blind your

judgment. I know by report the character of this Marinovich; I know he

to be a just and honourable man, though a stern disciplinarian. great

abuse have exist at the Arsenal, and it be in order to reform they

that the command be bestow on he. in your individual case he have

act unjustly, but in all probability appearance be strongly against

you, and he have not sufficient personal acquaintance with you to know

that amongst such inveterate liar as be the majority of the low

order of Venetians, your word might be rely on. his only fault be,

therefore, that he commit an error in judgment, and would you on

this account take a man’s life? besides, connive at assassination be

a cowardly proceeding, unworthy any Englishman, and especially a brave

young fellow like yourself.”



it be evident that Lewis’s reasoning be not without its effect on

he whom he address, for his brow contract, his finger close and

relaxed, his mouth quiver convulsively, and his whole demeanour be

that of a person struggle against some powerful temptation. at length

he exclaim abruptly--



“I know not how it be, you sway I like a child. I have resolve not to

rest till that man be dead, but I never before see the matter in the

light in which you have now place it. I believe that his death would

be an act of justice, and consider that, in order to obtain justice,

we must take the law into our own hand--but I feel the truth of what

you say, that assassination be cowardly; I feel it when Jacopo be

dog your footstep, and but for the cause that be at stake, could

have find in my heart to warn you.”



“then you will agree to my proposal?” inquire Lewis.



“yes, I will agree to withdraw from the conspiracy, but it be at the

risk of my life that I do so; if I be find in Venice after my desertion

be know, I be a dead man. moreover, I will promise you to abstain from

secretly attempt Marinovich’s life; but if I should ever meet he

face to face and hand to hand, I will teach he to remember having

flog an Englishman.”



Lewis feel that in his new character of Mentor he ought to combat this

openly declare resolution, but he abstain from do so, partly

because he feel it would be useless, and partly because he sympathise

so completely in the sentiment, that he could not muster sufficient

hypocrisy to reprove it. accordingly he remain satisfied with the

concession he have gain, and furnish Miles with all the information

he possess in regard to his sister, which be but vague and

unsatisfactory (a rumour that she have pass some time in Rome on her

first arrival in that country be the only trace he have yet be able

to discover of her proceeding), Lewis give he an introduction to an

agent whom he have employ to gain further tiding, and force a sum of

money upon he more than sufficient to defray his expense, hasten his

departure ere the brilliant ray of an italian sun have spread the

lustre of the come day throughout the picturesque old street and

palace-crown square of Venice.



on the following morning Lewis fulfil his intention of call on

Colonel Marinovich, who hear his recital in silence, and when he have

conclude, thank he for his information, say he be aware great

disaffection exist amongst the man employ at the Arsenal, and that

energetic measure must be take to prevent its spread far,

promise to report the discovery of the secret meeting to the Governor,

take down Lewis’s address, and politely bow he out.



having despatch a note to Charles Leicester tell he he wish to

see he, Lewis debate with himself how much of the previous night’s

adventure he should reveal to he, and at length decide that it would

be more prudent to avoid mention his encounter with and recognition

of Miles Hardy, as although he have refuse to reveal to he the name of

the seducer of his sister, yet any reference to an affair in which Lord

Bellefield have so singularly misconduct himself must necessarily be

painful to Leicester. moreover, although in his dealing with Miles

Hardy Lewis have act justly, accord to the good of his judgment,

he be by no mean clear that the law might take the same view of the

matter. Charley come--listen to his friend’s account--yawn--wonder

why he have such a strange predilection for put his life in danger,

prophesy that he would do it once too often and be sorry for

it afterwards--expect there would be a shindy in Venice before

long--wish Laura and the brat be safe in England, and that the other

people be not come--vote it all an awful bore--ask Lewis whether

he like foreign tailoring, into the merit and demerit whereof he

enter at some length--yawn again, and pat he affectionately on

the back, tell he to take well care of himself for the future, and

lounge carelessly out of the studio.



a week pass away. the Grant party have arrive; Annie, although she

make a great effort to appear in her former spirit, be evidently

labour under some ailment, mental or bodily, or both combine, which

be wear away her youth, and, as it appear, change her whole

nature. Laura, who watch she closely, observe that she be unusually

silent and abstract, fall into long reverie, from which she would

awake with a start, and glance round with a half-frightened air, would

immediately begin talk in an unnaturally excited manner, as if to do

away with any suspicion to which her silence might have give rise. her

temper also, which have be remarkable for its sweetness, have now become

uncertain, and she occasionally answer even the General with a

wayward captiousness which surprise Laura only one degree less than the

preternatural meekness with which that gallant officer submit to her

caprice and indulge she every whim; but the fact be, General Grant

have sufficient acuteness to perceive that for some cause, utterly beyond

the scope of his philosophy to account for, his daughter be not

the quiet, gentle, _will-less_ creature she have be, and that if he

require she to yield to he in great matter he must allow she to rule

in small. moreover, he have lately become seriously alarmed about her

health; a London physician whom he have consult on the subject having

plainly tell he unless great caution be observe she would go into a

decline, and warn he that the seat of the disease appear to be

in the mind, and that anything like harshness or opposition must be

avoid. Walter also be much change during the two year which have

elapse. in appearance he be now a young man, tall, and slightly but

gracefully form, with well-cut, regular feature, though a want of

intellectual expression mar what might otherwise have be consider

a handsome countenance. but considerable as be the alteration in his

personal appearance, the change in his mental capacity be equally

perceivable, his power of mind have develop to a great degree than

have be anticipate, but alas! deprive of Lewis’s firm yet gentle

rule, the improvement in his disposition have by no mean keep pace with

the extension of his faculty. for some week after Lewis have quit

Broadhurst, poor Walter could not be persuade that he would not come

back again, nor be it till the arrival of a tutor, recommend by Lord

Bellefield, that he fully realise the fact of his friend having leave

he never to return. the first effect this conviction produce upon he

be a fit of deep dejection; he refuse all attempt at consolation,

could scarcely be persuade to take nourishment, and sit hour after

hour play listlessly with the wavy curl of Faust’s shaggy coat. at

length, in order to rouse he, General Grant desire the dog to be take

away from he; the remedy prove only too effectual. the new tutor, a

certain Mr. Spooner, who appear as if he have be select because he

be in every respect the exact reverse of Lewis, be the person to whom

the General entrust this commission.



absorb in his own sad thought, Walter allow he to coax the dog

from his side by the attraction of a plate of meat, but when he lay

his hand on the animal to buckle a collar and chain round his neck, he

start up, exclaiming--



“what be you go to do with Faust? he be never tie up; let he

alone.” find that his remonstrance be not attend to, he continue,

“Faust! faust! come here, sir, directly.”



the dog struggle to obey, but Mr. Spooner, having fasten the chain

round his neck, endeavour to force he out of the room, and in do

so step accidentally on Faust’s toe, who utter a shrill yelp of

pain. Walter’s eye flash.



“you be hurt he,” he cry; “how dare you!” and without wait for

a reply, he dart across the room, seize the astonish Mr. Spooner,

who, unfortunately for himself, happen to be a small, slightly-frame

man, by the throat, and shake he till his tooth chatter; then

suddenly release he, he snatch the chain from his grasp, and

lead the dog away, mutter in a threatening tone--



“never you touch Faust again; if you do, I’ll strangle you.”



the result of this scene be twofold: Walter have rebel and gain

his point, and the person whom he have thus conquer have lose all

chance of obtain that degree of ascendency over he without which his

control must become merely nominal. this produce, as might be expect,

the bad possible effect upon poor Walter’s disposition. he become

positive and wilful in the extreme, and his tutor, partly to save

himself trouble, partly to avoid any outbreak of temper, give way to

he on every occasion; unless, indeed, he have any particular personal

interest at stake, when he seek to gain his point by cajolery and

manoeuvring, and be rather an adept in those ingenious art, be

usually successful.



one new and inconvenient caprice of Walter’s be a dislike which he

appear suddenly to have take to Annie Grant, which display itself

in various way: sometimes he would avoid all intercourse with she, even

sulkily refuse to answer she when she speak to he; at other he would

seek she out and endeavour to annoy she by say what he deem sharp

thing. occasionally, however, he would fall into his old habit, and

confide in she as his playmate, from whom he be sure of sympathy

and assistance; when suddenly, perhaps, even in the midst of some

conversation with she, he would appear to recollect his new-bear

animosity, and his manner would entirely alter. one thing invariably

excite his extreme indignation, and this be any attempt on her part

to caress or notice Faust. the pain this alter demeanour cause Annie

(perhaps in consequence of some theory which she have form as to its

origin) be know but to her own heart, and could be guess at merely

by her unwearye effort to conciliate poor Walter. Laura, upon whose

quick-sightedness nothing be lose, carefully note these change, and

make her own private comment upon they. in pursuance of her design of

befriend Lewis, she make several attempt to penetrate the veil of

reserve which hang around Annie Grant; but in vain: with her lightness

of heart seem also to have depart her openness of disposition, and

Laura have too much good taste, as well as too much sympathy with her

grief, to endeavour to force her confidence. at length one day, as Laura

and Annie be sit together, Laura work zealously at some article

of juvenile finery, destine unconsciously to foster the seed of

incipient dandyism already apparent in that embryo man-about-town

“Tarley,” and Annie listlessly turn over the page of a novel, from

which her thought be far away, the eld lady suddenly break silence

by observing--



“‘Tarley’ will be two year old to-morrow; how the time slip away, it

really seem impossible!”



Annie’s only reply be a deep sigh, and Laura continued--



“why, Annie, you’ll be of age in a month--four short week more, and you

will actually have arrive at year of discretion. how wise you ought to

be!”



Finding Annie still remain silent, Laura only wait till she have

pass some interesting crisis in her stitching, and then look up. to

her alarm and surprise she behold the “big tear” silently course each

other down her friend’s pale cheek: in an instant she be by her side.



“Annie, dearest,” she say, “you be weep; what be it? have I say or

do anything to pain you?”



Annie slightly shake her head in token of dissent, and make an effort to

check her tear, which prove ineffectual, eventuate in a bitter sob.

Laura could not stand the sight of her grief; throw her arm round

she, she said--



“Annie, you be miserable; I see, I know you be; and your unhappiness

be wear you to death. why will you not confide in I? perhaps I might

help you. what be it, darling? will you not tell I?” she pause for

a reply, but obtain none, continue: “this marriage with Lord

Bellefield, it be distasteful to you, I be afraid?”



a shudder which pass through poor Annie’s frame as Laura mention the

name of her intended husband prove that on this point her suspicion

have not err. fancy she now see her way more clearly--



“Dearest,” she resume, “do not afflict yourself thus; you must not,

shall not marry he. I will speak to the General myself. Charles shall

write to his brother; you shall not be sacrifice.”



“hush! hush!” interrupt Annie, struggle to recover composure; “you

do not know what you say. I _must_ marry he; there be no alternative.”



“do not say so, Annie,” return Laura gravely; “marriage be a sacred

thing, not lightly to be enter into; and in marriage one requisite

alone be indispensable--love! taste may differ, fault of temper or

disposition may exist; yet if man and wife truly love each other, they

will be very happy; but to marry without love be a grievous sin, and it

entail its own punishment--wretchedness.”



Laura speak solemnly and with feeling, and her companion, as she

listen, tremble and turn pale. when she have conclude, however,

Annie merely shake her head, repeat hopelessly--



“it _must_ be--it _must_ be!”



“and, pray, why must it be?” ask Laura quickly, for she be becoming

slightly provoke at that which she deem Annie’s childish weakness,

the only fault, perhaps, with which her clear head, warm heart, and

earnest, zealous nature unfit she to sympathise. “why, if the thing

be wrong in itself, and be to render you miserable, _must_ it be? at

all event let we make some effort to prevent it; suffer Charles and

me----”



“Dearest Laura,” interrupt Annie mournfully, “I assure you nothing can

be do; any attempt to break off the match now would be unavaile and

only end in make I still more wretched than I be at present.”



annoy alike at her perseverance in that which Laura could not but

consider a culpable want of moral courage, and at the way in which she

still withhold her confidence, while at the same time the idea occur

to she, though she be vex with herself for admit it, that one so

feeble-minded be no fitting bride for the high-soule, brave-hearte

Lewis, the spirited little matron be about to utter a somewhat sharp

reply, when, glance at Annie’s pale, beautifully-form feature,

the expression of deep anguish she read there disarm she, and merely

say, “we take different view of this matter, Annie dear, and must

talk of it again when we be both more compose,” she rise and leave the

apartment.



Annie wait until the sound of the closing door assure she that she

be alone, and then murmur, “she too be angry with I and despise

I--nobody love I. oh, that I be dead!” »he hide her face in the sofa

cushion and give way to a passionate burst of grief.



now there be one of our _dramatis personæ_ for whom we have reason to

believe many of our reader entertain a warm regard--a regard in which

we confess ourselves fully to participate--of whom we have lately hear

but little; of course we refer to that most “meritorious individual,”

 that dog of dog, dear, honest old Faust. since Lewis have quit

Broadhurst Faust’s character, like those of his better (if mortal

_be_ well than dog), have in a degree alter. the blind,

unhesitate obedience he have be accustom to pay to his master’s

slight signal he accord to no other person; if Walter call he

he would come, it be true, but he would do so in the calm, leisurely,

dignified manner in which one gentleman would comply with the request

of another. towards the General he conduct himself with a degree of

respectful hauteur which seem to say, “we be not friend; there be no

sympathy between we, but as long as I continue to reside in a family of

which you be the head, I owe it to myself to render you the amount of

courtesy due to your position.” for Mr. Spooner, the usurper who have

dare to succeed his beloved master, he show a most unmitigated

contempt, totally ignore all his command, and resent any attempt

on his part to enforce his authority by the utterance of a low, deep

growl, accompany by a formidable display of sharp white tooth. towards

Annie alone do he evince any great affection, which he show chiefly

by attend she in her walk and take up his position under the

sofa, or close to the chair on which she be sit--demonstration of

attachment which, as we have already hint, be for some unexplained

reason a source of considerable annoyance to Walter. during the

conversation between Laura and Annie, Faust have be lie unnoticed

under the sofa, and now find his young mistress alone, and for some

cause or other unhappy (he know _that_ quite well), it occur to he

that the correct thing would be to come out and comfort she, which he

attempt to do by lay his great rough head in her lap, wag his

tail encouragingly, and lick her hand. in her loneliness of heart

even the poor dog’s sympathy (endear to she as he be by a thousand

cherish recollection) be a relief to Annie, and stoop down she

imprint a kiss on his shaggy head, whisper as she do so, “good

Faust--you have never forsake I!” at this moment the door open,

and Walter enter hastily. as his eye fall upon Annie and the dog his

cheek flushed, and he exclaim hastily--



“Annie, I wish you’d let Faust alone; how often have I tell you that I

willn’t have he meddle with?”



with a start at this sudden interruption Annie hastily raise herself,

and push the dog gently from she, said--



“Dear Walter, do not be angry; Faust come and lick my hand, you would

not have I unkind to he?”



“oh! it’s Faust’s fault, be it?” return Walter crossly. “Faust, come

here! take he to our room, Mr. Spooner, and keep he there till I come;

he shall not stay in the drawing-room if he do what I don’t like.

Faust, do you hear I, sir?”



“he will never follow I, Sir Walter; it’s no use call he,”

 remonstrate Mr. Spooner.



“he will do as I tell he, and so will you too,” return Walter

imperiously, and twist his handkerchief, he tie it round the dog’s

neck, lead he to the door, give the end of the leash thus form to Mr.

Spooner, and then fairly turn the pair of they out of the room. having

accomplish this feat, he stroll listlessly to the fireplace, and

amuse himself by pull about the ornament on the chimney-piece for

some minute. at length a new idea seem to strike he, and turn to

his companion, he said--



“do you know why I be so angry with Faust just now?”



“because I be pet he, I suppose, as you don’t seem to like I to

do so,” return Annie.



“ah! that be not all, though,” rejoin Walter; “I want he

particularly to have be with I when I be out walk to-day, very

particularly.”



“yes, and why be that?” inquire Annie, who always encourage he to

talk to she, in the hope of overcome the dislike which he have take

to she, and which, for many reason, pain she inexpressibly. Walter

remain for a minute or two silent, and then come close to she, he

ask in a low whisper--



“Annie, do you believe in ghost?”



“my dear Walter, what an odd question,” return Annie in surprise; “why

do you ask it?”



Walter glance carefully round the room, to assure himself that they

be alone ere he reply, in the same low, awe-stricken whisper,

“because, if there be such thing, I think I’ve see one.”



“silly boy,” rejoin Annie, anxious to reassure he, for she see that

he be really frightened, “you have fancy it. what be your ghost

like, pray?”



“promise you willn’t tell anybody.”



Annie, half amuse, half puzzle by the boy’s earnestness, give the

require pledge. as soon as she have do so, Walter, stoop down so as

to bring his mouth on a level with her ear, replied--



“it be the ghost of Mr. Arundel!”



overcome by so unexpected a reply, Annie be a moment or so before

she could find word to inquire, “my dear Walter, what could make you

imagine such a thing? perhaps you be asleep, and dream it--when be

it?”



“no, I be not asleep; and it be not fancy,” return Walter gravely.

“I be out walk this morning early with Mr. Spooner, and we lose

our way, and after try for some time to find it, Mr. Spooner hire a

boat, and tell the boatman to set we down near--near--well, I forget the

name, but he mean near here. when we get out, we have to go through some

narrow passage between the different street, and in one of they, which

be very dark because of the high house, we meet a figure of a man, very

tall, and wrap in a long black cloak; it draw back to let we pass,

and just as I get close to it it turn its head, and I see the face; it

be stem and dark, and wear a black beard, but the beautiful eye be

the same, and when I see they I know it be Mr. Arundel, or,” he add,

sink his voice, “his ghost!”



as his companion remain silent, he continue, “when I see who it be,

I stop, and be just go to speak, but at that moment he stare

hard at I, give a violent start, and before I could do anything to

prevent it, vanish through a dark archway.”



“oh! you must have mistake some one for he,” return Annie,

struggle for composure--“Mr. Arundel be probably in England, and

ghost be out of the question; besides, if there be such thing, which

I much doubt, they only appear after people be dead.”



Walter consider for a minute, and then meet the difficulty by

consolatorily suggest, “perhaps dear Mr. Arundel _be_ dead--perhaps

he grow so unhappy that he could not live without ever see Faust and

I, and--ah! Annie, how could you be so cruel as to send he away?”



“I send he away, Walter! what can have put such a strange notion into

your head?” exclaim Annie, astonish at the accusation.



“yes you do,” return Walter vehemently; “he go away because he

love you and you would not love he--it be very cruel of you, and

I hate you for it whenever I remember how unkind you have be,” and

overcome by his feeling, the poor boy burst into tear.



a thousand confused thought flash like lightning across Annie’s

brain. what could he mean?--was she listen to the mere folly

of idiocy, or could he indeed have any possible foundation for his

assertion? anxious to soothe he, she lay her hand caressingly

upon his, while, reply rather to her own heart than to his last

observation, she said--



“no, my poor Walter, he whom you so much regret never love I.”



“ah, but he do, though,” return Walter positively, dry his

tears--“I _know_ it.” he speak so decidedly that Annie, despite her

reason, could not but feel curious to hear more, and turn away her

head to hide her agitation, she ask in a low voice--



“how do you know?”



“if I tell you, you must never tell the General or anybody,” return

Walter--“people think I’m a fool, and I know I be not clever, and cann’t

learn like other boy, and sometimes I feel a weight just here,” and

he press his hand to his forehead, “and then all my sense go out--I

wonder where it go to, Annie--do you think it find wing and fly up

to heaven among the white angel? I think so sometimes, and then I long

to be a bird and fly with it.” too much interested to allow he to

fall into a new train of thought, Annie recall his wandering idea by

saying--



“you be talk about Mr. Arundel, Walter dear.”



“oh yes, and about you, I remember,” resume Walter. “I know, at least

I think, he be very fond of you a long time ago, but I be not quite

sure of it till one day when I dress Faust up like a gentleman, with

Mr. Arundel’s watch, and you take it off the dog’s neck, and then you

throw your arm round he and kiss he as you do just now--that be

what make I angry when I remember about the first time--well, while

you be hug Faust, Mr. Arundel come to the door and see you, though

you do not see he, and his eye dance and sparkle, and his mouth

melt into such a sweet smile; he be so glad to see how fond you be

of Faust, and then I know he love you, for if he have not, he would not

have care about it, you know. then he go away and leave I Faust, and

I think because he have leave Faust he be sure to come back, but I know

now that he leave he to comfort I, and go away himself all alone.

then that stupid Mr.. Spooner come; he’s a great friend of Lord

Bellefield’s, and one day they be talk together, and they fancy

I do not attend to they, but I do though, for I know they be talk

about Mr. Arundel. well, Mr. Spooner ask why he go away, and Lord

Bellefield reply, ‘why, if the truth must be tell, he have the audacity

(what do that mean?) to raise his eye to my cousin Annie.’ Mr.

Spooner question he far, and he inform he that Mr. Arundel have

go boldly to the General, and say he love you.”



“tell my father so!” exclaim Annie.



“yes, so he say,” resume Walter; “and the General tell he you love

Lord Bellefield instead, and mean to be his wife; and then poor Mr.

Arundel say he would go away, and so he do, but of course if you have

love he he would have stay, and we should all have be so happy

together. so you see, Annie, it _be_ you that send he away, and since

I’ve know that I’ve hate you, and try to keep Faust from love you,

only he will, and I cann’t hate you quite always; but I never mean to

tell you all this, and you must never tell Lord Bellefield, or he would

be ready to kill I.”



he pause, then regard she with a sad, regretful look, he say,



“but, Annie, be it really true that you don’t love dear Mr. Arundel?”

 Poor Annie! affect and excited as she have be by the foregoing scene,

this last speech be too much for she, and throw her arm about

the boy’s neck, and hide her burn cheek against his breast, she

whisper, “Dearest Walter, do not hate I! _you have no cause to do

so!_”









chapter LVIII.--CONTAINS MUCH plotting and COUNTERPLOTTING.



it be the evening of the Tuesday in Epsom week, the day before the

Derby. Lord Bellefield, though outwardly calm, be inwardly a prey

to the most painful mental excitement. his lordship have meet with a

continue run of ill-fortune latterly--everything he have attempt have

turn out badly: if he bet on a race, the horse he back invariably

lose; if he play, card and dice equally declare against he; he have

lavish hundred in present to a new opera dancer, and at the moment

in which he deem his suit successful she have elope with a young,

rich, and handsomer man; his tradesman begin to mistrust he and to

dun he unpleasantly; several of his intimate to whom he owe money

grow cool and eye he suspiciously; his extravagance have reach his

father’s ear, and Lord Ashford have not only venture to remonstrate

with he, but apparently bent on add insult to injury, have cite the

example of his young brother, Charles Leicester (whom from his heart

he despise), and hold he up as a pattern for his imitation, while Lord

Bellefield be force to bear this lecturing patiently; for although

the estate be entail, his father have be a careful man, and be

possess of a large personalty which he could leave to whom he please.

the only piece of good luck to set against all this “monstrous quantity”

 of vexation be the admirable promise display by the Dodona Colt.

this exemplary quadrupe, now individualise by the name of “Oracle,”

 appear to have be bear with a metaphorical silver spoon in its

delicate mouth, for from the moment in which its four black leg

(suggestive of its future fleetness, for black-leg be invariably

_fast_) put their foot into this uncomfortable world everything have

prosper with it. the breeder be astonish at it, the groom who

watch over its infancy be delighted with it; Turnbull, the trainer,

be so impressed by its merit that he never could speak of it without a

volley of the strong oath in his vocabulary, by which expletive he

be accustom (transpose a certain poetical dictum) to _strengthen_

his praise of anything which be so fortunate as to win his approval;

and by the united kind regard of all these worthy this favourite of

nature have grow in public opinion until it now hold the proud position

of first favourite for the Derby. Lord Bellefield be by this time no

new hand upon the turf; on the contrary, by dint of having be cheat,

and associate with those who have cheat he, for several year, he

have acquire, besides a sort of prescriptive diploma to _do_ as he have

be _do_, a considerable insight into the mystery of the training

stable as well as the bet ring. he be therefore habitually

cautious; but in the present instance all his acquire knowledge and

natural acuteness coincide with the opinion of his underling, to

prove to he that in the Dodona Colt he have indeed draw a rare prize;

and that if he could but ensure that which our sanguine country be

popularly suppose to expect--viz., that “every man should do his duty,”

 his horse, and none other, must be winner of the Derby. accordingly, all

the power of his intellect (which, although not enlarge, be subtle

and acute) be now direct to two point--viz., first, to take all

precaution to ensure that his horse should be fairly deal by; and

secondly, to make such a book on the event as might retrieve his

bankrupt fortune. this last feat he have succeed in accomplish even

beyond his utmost wish; and accustom as he be to hazard large sum

upon the cast of a die, he begin to grow alarmed at the magnitude of the

stake for which he be about to contend.



having dine in town at his club, he return to his luxurious bachelor

_ménage_ in ---------- Street, and desire that he might not be

disturb, draw out his bet-book, examine it carefully, go

through the calculation again and again, refer to the late

odd--and then close it with a sigh, muttered, “yes, they be all safe

man, man who will pay to the hour, and if Oracle run true, this curse

load of debt will be wipe off, and--I shall be rich enough to begin

afresh and contract a new one!--if! ay, there’s the rub--if!” he stride

up and down the room. “I be wretchedly nervous to-night,” he exclaim,

ring the bell. “bring brandy,” he continue as the servant

appear; then fill a wine-glass, he drink it off as if it have be

water--“leave it,” he say; then resume his walk, add, “it _must_

go right--there be not a horse that can come near he; Tartuffe be the

only one that have a chance, and Turnbull swear he be safe to lose;

he witness the private trial himself, and the colt win by a head,

carry 5 lbs. extra weight. that amusement cost I £50 to bribe

Austerlitz’s trainer to allow the trial to take place. true, Turnbull

may have lie--and yet why should he? he owe everything to I--though

that have nothing to do with it--gratitude, if there be such a quality,

be simply prospective--man be grateful to those only from whom they

expect favour. well, even thus, Turnbull be bind to I hand and

foot; besides, I know he have back the colt heavily himself: bar

accident, then, against which no foresight can provide, and of which

therefore it be useless to think, I stand safe to win. and yet it be

a frightful sum to hazard on the uncertainty of a horse-race. if I

should lose, I must either blow out my brain like poor Mellerton, or

quit the country, marry Annie Grant, and live abroad on her money till

my father die--and he’s as likely to last twenty year long as I be.

I scarcely know which alternative be preferable. what an infernal fool

I’ve be to bring myself into this scrape; but when a man have such

a run of ill-luck against he as I have be curse with for the last

year, what be he to do?” he pause, stretch himself wearily, and

then glance at a gilt clock on the chimney-piece, mutter, “twelve

o’clock; I must be up early to-morrow and keep a clear head--I’ll smoke

a cigar and turn in.” at this moment the house-bell ring sharply, and

Lord Bellefield start like a guilty thing. with an oath at this fresh

proof of his nervousness, he fill and drink a second glass of brandy,

then stand listen with a degree of eager anxiety which, despite his

effort, he could not restrain. door open and shut, and at length a

servant appear.



“what be it?” exclaim Lord Bellefield before the man could speak.



“a person wish particularly to see your lordship,” be the reply.



“say I be engage, and can see no one; I think I tell you I would not

be disturb,” return his master angrily; “stay,” he continue, as a

new idea strike he, “what kind of person be it?”



“he desire I to inform your lordship that his name be Turnbull,” be

the answer.



with an oath at the man’s stupidity, Lord Bellefield desire he to

admit the visitor instantly.



“well, Turnbull,” he exclaim eagerly as the trainer enter, “what be

it, man?”



thus adjure, Turnbull, a tall, stout-build fellow, with a clever but

disagreeable expression of countenance glance carefully round the

room to assure himself that they be alone, and then approach Lord

Bellefield, begin, “why, you see, my lud, I think I’d well lose no

time, for there ain’t too many hour between now and tomorrow’s race,

so I jump on to my ’ack, canter over to the rail, ’ail a

’Ansom’s cab, and ’ere I be.”



“nothing amiss, eh? nothing wrong with the colt?” ask Lord Bellefield

with an affectation of indifference, though any one who have watch he

closely might have see that he turn very pale.



“no, bless his eye, he’s as right as a trivet, and as playful and

impudent as--as a brick,” continue Mr. Turnbull, rather at a loss for

a sufficiently eulogistic simile; “it be only this morning he take up

little Bill the ’elper by the waistband of his indispentionable and

shuk he like a tarrier would a rat. it would have do your ludship’s

’art good to have see he; he’ll come out to-morrow as fresh as

paint, bless his bone.”



“well then, what be it, if Oracle be all right?” return his employer,

greatly relieve.



“why, unfortunately there’s somebody else as have get a ’orse as be

all right too, and I’m afraid we ain’t quite so sure of the race as we

fancy we be,” be the dispirit reply.



“why, I think you have satisfy yourself that there be not a horse

that could run near he. you tell I he beat Tartuffe carry 5 lbs.

extra weight.”



“ay, so I believe; but the sharp of we be do sometimes. it’s

a wicked cross-bred world to live in, and a man need be wide-awaker

than--than one o’clock, to be down to all their move.” so say, the

discomfited trainer rub his nose as if to brighten his wit, and

continue, “the truth be this, my lud--one of my groom cum to I this

morning, and say if I would stand a soveraign between he and one of

his mate, he would tell I something as I ought to know. well, see

as this race be rather a peculiar one, and as any little mistake might

turn out unpleasant----”



“what do you mean, sir?” interrupt Lord Bellefield, draw himself up

with a haughty gesture.



“nothing, my lud, nothing,” return Turnbull obsequiously, “only as our

colt stand first favourite, and as we’ve make our calcilation to win,

I think the californian farthing would not be throw away. accord

he bring up his mate, as he call he, which be the hidentical boy

as first ride the colt, and he confess that he and the boy that ride

Tartuffe have meet one day when they be out a exercising, and just for

their own amusement they give ’em a three-mile gallop. they run very

near together, but Tartuffe beat our colt by above a length; that he’d

see the trial afterwards, and that he know from the difference in

Tartufife’s run that he be not ride fair, or be overweighte, or

something. well, my lud, this information bother I, and make I feel

suspicious that some move have be try on which we be not up to, and

while I be scheme how to cipher it out, the same boy cum again,

and tell I that the lad that ride Tartuffe at the second trial be a

keep company along with his sister, and that he think she might

worm something out of he if she could be get to try. accordingly I

send for the gal, and between bribe, coax, and frightening she,

persuade she to undertake the job. she have some trouble with the young

feller, but she be a sharp, clever gal, and she never leave he till she

drag it out of he.”



“drew what out of he?” interrupt Lord Bellefield, unable to restrain

his impatience; “cann’t you come to the point at once? you’ll distract I

with your prosing.”



“well, the long and short of it be, as I see your ludship’s get in a

hurry (and, indeed, there ain’t no time to be lose), the long and short

of it be, that they’ve bin and turn the table upon we: while we put 5

lbs. extra weight on our horse, they shove 8 lbs. on theirs.”



“then Tartuffe run within a head of the colt carry 3 lbs. extra,”

 exclaim Lord Bellefield, “and of course without that disadvantage

would again have beat he.”



“I think Oracle be a well horse now than he be at the time the trial

cum off,” be the reply, “but the race ain’t the safe thing I think

it. it’s rather a ticklish chance to trust to, if your ludship’s get at

all a heavy book upon the ewent.”



as he make this uncomfortable acknowledgment the trainer leer

inquiringly with his cunning little eye at his employer.



Lord Bellefield do not immediately answer; but lean his elbow on the

chimney-piece, remained bury in thought, his pale cheek and the eager

quivering of his under lip, which from time to time he unconsciously bite

till the mark of his tooth remain in blood upon it, alone testify

the mental suffering he experience. Ruin and disgrace be before he.

nor be this all. the Duc d’Austerlitz, a young foreigner who, bite

with Anglo-mania, have purchase a racing stud and be the owner of

Tartuffe, happen to be the individual before allude to as Lord

Bellefield’s successful rival in the venial affection of the

fascinating _danseuse_. he hate he, accordingly, with an intensity

which would have secure he the approbation of the good-hater-love

Dr. Johnson. if anything, therefore, be want to render the

intelligence he have just receive doubly irritate to he, this fact

supply the deficiency. his lordship, however, possess one element of

greatness--his spirit invariably rise with difficulty, and the great

the emergency the more cool and collect do he become. having remain

silent for some minute, he observe quietly, “I suppose, Turnbull, you,

be a shrewd, clever fellow in your way, scarcely come here merely

to tell I this. you be perfectly aware that, rely upon your

information and judgment, I have make a heavy book on this race, and

can imagine that, however long my purse may be, I shall find it more

agreeable to win than to lose. you have, therefore, I be sure, some

expedient to propose. in fact, I read in your face that it be so.”



the man smile.



“your ludship I always know to have a sharp eye for a good horse or a

pretty gal,” he say, “but you _must_ be wide awake if you can read a

man’s thought in his face. it ain’t such an easy matter to say what be

good to do; if your ludship make rather too heavy a book on the race, I

should recommend a little careful hedging to-morrow morning.”



Lord Bellefield shake his head. “too late to make anything of it,” he

reply, “that be, of course, I might save myself from any very heavy

loss, but I must have money--a--in fact, I stand so fair to win largely

by this race, that hedging will be quite a _derni ressort_. but you

have some well scheme than that to propose.”



“if your ludship be at a loss how to act, it be not likely that any plan

of mine will do the trick,” be the reply.



whether or not Turnbull wish to provoke his employer, certain it

be his speech produce that effect, for with an oath Lord Bellefield

exclaimed--



“what be it you be aim at? if it be money you be stand out

for, you have only to prevent Tartuffe from start, and name your own

price.”



“why, you see, it might be as well to let he start; man have be

transport for interfere with a race ’orse to purwent his

starting--but he need not win the Derby for all that,” be the

enigmatical reply.



Lord Bellefield’s lip curl with a sardonic smile; his knowledge of

human nature have not then deceive he--Turnbull have some scheme _in

petto_, and be only wait to secure the good market for it.



“I suppose £1000 will satisfy you?” he say; and as the trainer bow

his gratitude, continue, “you be certain your plan cannot fail? what

be it you propose?”



“why, you see, my lud, ’orse be like ’uman creetur in many

respecs,” reply Turnbull sententiously; “there’s some thing as agree

with their stummick, and some as disagree with ’em; the thing

that agree with the hanimal make ’em run fast, the thing that

disagree make they run slow, or if you give it ’em too strong they

come to a standstill all together. now, if so be as Tartuffe be to

have a taste of a certain drug as I know on--that ain’t very different

from hopium--give to he afore he go to sleep to-night, he’ll come to

the start post all right and run very respectible, but if he beat

our ’orse I’ll engage to eat he, saddle and all. I cann’t speak fair

than that, I expect.”



“and who have you fix upon to execute this piece of delectable

rascality?” inquire Lord Bellefield, unable to repress a sneer at the

meanness of the villainy by which, however, he be only too glad to

profit.



“it be not a very easy matter to pitch upon the right man,” rejoin

the trainer, “but luckily I happen to remember a party that seem as

if he’d be bear a purpose for the job, and who have be so thoroughly

clean out lately that he be not likely to be particular about

trifle. I see he before I leave home, show he which way his interest

lay, put he up to my idea on the subject, and I hope when I see your

ludship to-morrow morning I shall have some good news to tell you.”



“I’ll be with you early, before people be about,” return Lord

Bellefield; “it be important that I should know the result of this

scheme as soon as possible. the great caution must be observe lest

the matter should transpire, and if anything come out, you of course

must take it upon yourself. the man should go abroad for a time. and now

I must try and get a couple of hour’ sleep, or my head will not be fit

for to-morrow’s work. I breakfast at Epsom with a set of man, but I’ll

be with you first. you’ve act with your usual zeal and cleverness,

Turnbull, and I’ll take care that you shall have no reason to repent

your honesty to your employer; only let we win to-morrow’s race and your

fortune be make. good-night.”



as he speak he ring the bell, and with many servile acknowledgment of

his master’s promise liberality the trainer depart.



while this interview be take place a far different scene have

be enact in the premise occupy by the race stud of the Due

d’Austerlitz. as the clock over the stable chime the hour after

midnight a light ladder be place against the wall of one of the outer

building, and a slightly-frame, agile man run up it, and draw it

cautiously after he lay it in a place of security, where it would

remain unnoticed till his return. he then creep with noiseless, catlike

step over roof and along parapet, find among rain-gutter and
