coping-stone a dangerous and uncertain footing, until he reach a

building nearly in the centre of the yard; here he pause, and draw

from his pocket a short iron instrument, shape like a chisel at one

end, he cautiously chip away the mortar round one of the tile which

protect an angle of the roof, and by remove the tile, expose the

end of a row of slating. quietly raise one of the slate, he,

by mean of the instrument above allude to, which be know to the

initiate by the euphonious title of a “jemmy,” snap the nail which

retain it in its place and remove it. having act in a similar

manner by two other, he produce a small cabinetmaker’s saw, and

cut through the batten, open a space sufficiently wide to admit

the passage of a man’s body. replace his tool, he creep through the

aperture thus effect, and let himself down by his hand into the

loft beneath, drop noiselessly on to some truss of hay place there

for future consumption. part of his task be now accomplish, for he

be in the loft over the loose-box in which Tartuffe be repose his

graceful limb before the come struggle; but the most difficult and

hazardous portion of his enterprise remain yet to be accomplish.

crawl on his hand and knee, he reach one of the opening by which

the hay be let down into the rack beneath, and cautiously peep

over, gaze into the interior of the stable itself, and note the

precaution take to secure the safety of the racehorse and the

difficulty which lie before he. the box in which the animal be

place be secure by a strong padlock, the key of which rest at that

moment under the pillow of Slangsby, the due d’Austerlitz’s trainer,

while in the next box, half-lie, half-sit on a truss of straw,

doze “Yorkshire Joe,” a broad-shoulder, bowlegge lad some eighteen

year of age, who have be a kind of equestrian valet to Tartuffe during

the whole “educational course” of that promising quadrupe.



these particular the intelligent eye of the tenant of the hay-loft take

in at a glance, while his quick wit decide as rapidly the exact degree

in which they be calculate to tell for or against the object he

seek to accomplish. the padlock be in his favour; for as he do not

intend to enter the horse-box by the door, it would serve to keep

Joe out without interfere with his design; but the presence of the

stable-boy present an insuperable obstacle to his further proceeding.

this difficulty have, however, be foresee and provide against.

steal on tiptoe across the loft, he select a long, stout straw, and

thrust it through the key-hole of the door by which the fodder be

take in, he suffer it to drop on the outside. Scarcely have he do

so, when a low cough announce the presence of some confederate, and

satisfied that everything be in a right train, he noiselessly return

to his post of observation. in another moment his quick ear catch the

sound of a modest tap at the stable-door. honest Joe’s sense not be

equally on the alert, the knock have to be repeat more than once ere he

become aware of it. as soon as he grow convinced that the sound be

not the creation of his sleep fancy, he rub his eye, stretch

himself, and drowsily inquire, “who’s there?”



“it be I--Mary; and I want particularly to speak to you,” reply a

woman’s voice.



“Thy want must wait till morning, lass; for I’m not a-goaing to leave

this place to-night for nothink nor nobody; so gang thee whoam agin,”

 be the uncourteous reply.



“no, but Joe, dear Joe, you must hear I to-night; it be something very

important indeed. you _must_ hear I,” plead the temptress.



“I woan’t, I tell thee; gangwhoam!” return Joe gruffly.



“well, if I’d think you’d have be so unkind, I would not have

stay out of my warm bed, trapese through Hepsom street at this time

o’night, which ain’t fit for a respecktible young woman to be out in,

and come all this way to put you up to something as may lose you

your place, and bad, if you ain’t tell of it. I don’t expect sich

unkindness--and from you, too; that I don’t;” and here a sound akin to

a sob, apparently indicate that the speaker be weep, find its

way to Joe’s ear, and go thence straight to his honest, unsuspicious

heart, overcome his prudence and conquer his resolution. rise from

his seat, he approach the door and listen; the sob still continue.



“Mary, lass, what ail thee?” he say; “I don’t mean to anger thee,

wench! but thee knoas I dare na leave t’horse; besoid t’stable-dour be

lock, and maister’s get t’key.”



“and cann’t you come to the window in the further stable, where we’ve

talk many a time before?” suggest the siren. “it’s something about

the horse I want to tell you, a dodge they’re go to try to prevent

his win to-morrow. you don’t think I’d have come out at this time

o’night for nothing, do you, stupey?” this intelligence chase away

Joe’s last linger scruple, and muttering--



“about t’horse!--why do na thee say so afore?” he light a hand-lantern at

the lamp which hang from the ceiling, and assure himself by a glance

that his charge be in safety, quit the stable by a side-door.



in the meantime, the occupant of the loft have not be idle. as soon as

Joe became engross by the forego conversation, the sound of a fine

saw at work might have be perceive by a more delicate organisation

than that of the sturdy groom; and at the moment in which he leave the

stable two of the bar of the rack be silently remove, and through

the opening thus effect a man cautiously lower himself, and rest

his foot for an instant on the manger, drop lightly into the box

occupy by Tartuffe.



[illustration: 0442]



this feat be accomplish so quietly, that the horse, which happen

not to be lie down, but be stand, try, through its muzzle, to

nibble the straw of its bed, be scarcely startle, merely raise

its head and stare at its unexpected visitant. this individual now

produce from his mysterious pocket a handful of oat, and hold they

out, allow Tartuffe to smell and nibble at they; while the animal be

thus engage, he remove the muzzle, wear for the purpose of prevent

it from eat its litter, or otherwise gain access to any food of

which the trainer might disapprove. his next proceeding be to draw out

that ingenious instrument of torture clepe a twitch, which, for the

benefit of those of our lady reader who do not happen to be gift

with “a stable mind,” or to have encourage sporting tendency, we

may describe as a short, thick stick or handle, about two foot long,

terminate by a loop of stout whipcord or leather, into which the

upper-lip, or occasionally the ear of the horse, be insert; then,

by twist the stick, the loop can be tighten so as to produce any

amount of agony the inflicter may desire: the philosophy of the matter

be, that the animal find his struggle exactly double his pain,

soon have sense enough to choose the lesser of two evil, and therefore

stand still while nasty thing be be force down his throat and

other liberty take with he, which, but for the application of the

twitch, he would actively resent. in the present instance, while the

unfortunate Tartuffe be still chew the oats by which his confidence

have be betray, the twitch be fix on his nose, tighten, and

the nauseating ball which be to impair his strength and fleetness, and

secure the victory to the Dodona Colt, and fortune to Lord Bellefield,

be already in his mouth ere he be aware that any incivility be

intend he. to give a horse a ball, however, it be not only necessary

to put it into its mouth but to thrust it back as far as, if possible,

the entrance of the gullet, and this operation, even when perform

in the most skilful manner, be by no mean easy to the operator or

agreeable to the patient. in this last particular the victimise

Tartuffe appear to be entirely of our opinion; the blood of his noble

ancestor stir within he, and toss up his head indignantly, he

become practically aware of the full virtue of the twitch; the pain,

however, only serve to increase his rage, and he attempt to rear; but

his struggle be vain; his tormentor pertinaciously clung to he, the

ball be thrust far back in the mouth, and in another moment the

desire object would have be attain, when suddenly the loop of the

twitch, unable to bear the strain upon it, snap. the first use the

racehorse make of his freedom be to shake his head violently, and at

the same time open his mouth, the stupefying ball drop from it.



we must now return to our friend, Yorkshire Joe, who, suspect no

evil, be engage in interesting colloquy with the perfidious Mary; this

seductive young lady having contrive, with a degree of ingenuity worthy

a well cause, to prolong the interview by the follow expedient.

first, she assail her admirer with coquettish reproach for his

unkindness and want of gallantry in refuse to speak to she; then she

enter into a long account of how and when and where she have discover

the pretended design against Tartuffe, which she affirm be to be put

into execution two hour from that time.



“eh! what! tie my hand behoind I, shove a gag into my mouth, and then

and there lame t’horse afore my very eye--dost thee say, lass? I’d only

like to see the man, or man either, that could do it!” exclaim Joe,

double his fist indignantly; “and thee hear this in the tap-room of

the Chequers, dost thee say?--What be that noise?”



“nothing. I drop one of my patten, that be all,” return the girl,

stoop as if to pick it up, though she be not sorry for an excuse

to hide her agitation, for her quick ear have detect the sound of a

horse’s hoof trample on straw, and she know that her accomplice be

at work. “why, you be quite startlish to-night, Joe!” she resume,

look up at he with a forced smile; “do you think it be a

ghost?--but it’s no wonder you’re nervous; it’s hard line for you, poor

fellow, sit up at night like this----”



“there it be agen!” interrupt Joe; “by ------ it’s in t’horsebox,” he

continue, listen attentively. “they ---------- thief cann’t be come

a’ready, sure!” and heedless of Mary’s assurance that it be nothing,

and her entreaty to remain only one moment long, the groom, now

thoroughly excited, leap down from the window and rush back into the

stable.



with the speed of thought the girl spring to the door at which she

have previously tap, and stoop her head to the key-hole, listen

eagerly. the first thing that meet her ear be a volley of abuse from

Joe, accompany by heavy blow strike against wood or iron; then a

noise as of a door be burst open; next, broken curse, dull, muffle

stroke, ejaculation of rage or pain, the sound of trample foot, a

crushing, heavy fall, and then total silence!



what have happen? she place her eye to the key-hole, but could see

nothing. she listen--but the throbbing of her own heart be the only

thing she could hear: for the first time the fearful idea occur

to she, that by her treacherous deal she might have occasion she

lover’s death; and regardless of consequence, she be about to start up

and summon assistance, when a man’s hand be lay on her shoulder, and a

gruff voice exclaimed--



“so this be the way my groom be tamper with! I be sure I hear

talk go on. hold up your head, you jade, and let we see what

you’re like; nay, it’s no use to struggle, I’ve get you fast enough, and

see who it be I will.”



so say, Mr. Slangsby the trainer draw the girl towards he, and

forcibly raise her head, throw the light of a bull’s-eye lantern full

on her feature. “ha! little Mary Williams,” he continue, “and what

bring you here at this time of night, you artful hussy?”



“oh! Mr. Slangsby, pray open the door, sir; I--I’m afraid they’ve be

and murder poor Joe,” be the reply, and overcome by fear and remorse,

the girl burst into tear--real one, this time.



“they, and who be they, pray? there’s some rascality go on here, I

expect; it’s lucky I get up.” as he speak, Slangsby draw a key from his

pocket, open the door, and still retain his grasp on the girl’s

wrist, enter. the first object which meet their sight be Joe, by no

mean murder, although he bear evidence of a severe struggle in a

black eye and bleeding knuckle.



“T’horse be all right, meister, but I wor only jest in time, though!”

 be his opening speech.



“in time for what?” inquire Slangsby eagerly.



“in time to stop yon villain from pizonin the bless hanimal,” return

Joe, point to something which at first sight appear to be a

large bundle, but which prove on examination to be a human being most

ingeniously tie hand and foot with hayband.



“who the deuce be you, fellow?” ask the trainer, address the

individual thus uncomfortably situate.



“it ain’t o’ no use talk to he, for a cann’t answer with a wisp o’

straw stuff atween his jaw,” observe Joe sententiously.



“take it out then, and untie his leg so that he can stand up and answer

my question.”



“well shut the dour fust then, meister, for he’s a proper slippery

customer, I can tell you,” return the groom. “he promise to gag I

and tie my hand behind I, I do hear say, but he’s find two can play

at that trick. get up, ye warmint,” he continue, apply a by no mean

gentle kick to the rib of his prostrate captive, “and show your ugly

mug.”



the person thus upcomplimentarily apostrophise rise slowly and stand

sullenly await the irainer’s scrutiny. the latter, hold the

lantern so that its light fall upon the stranger’s feature, recognise

he immediately.



“Mr. Beverley,” he say in a tone more of contemptuous pity than of

anger, “be it you, sir? I know time have be get very bad with you,

but I do not think you have come to this.”



the man’s lip quiver--the reproach touch he more than the most

virulent abuse could have do. his have be, we fear, no very unusual

fate; at all event he have only fall one step low than many who have

follow the same career as he have do. well-bear, rich, and with above

average ability, a taste for gambling and low company have cause

he to sink lower and low in the scale of society, till the depth of

misery and degradation to which he have be reduce, and the extent of

the bribe offer by Turnbull, have overcome his last feeling of honour

or honesty, and he have consent to become the agent of another’s

villainy. Slangsby eye he sternly for a moment, and then said--



“you know what you have lay yourself open to, I suppose?” the other

nod in sign of assent.



“I don’t wish to be hard upon you, sir,” the trainer continue, “so if

you will speak out and tell I _all_, we may perhaps come to some well

understanding: what say you?”



the other reflect a moment and then reply in a low voice, “I will do

as you wish, but not here.”



“Joe, you have distinguish yourself,” observe his master, put his

hand into his pocket, “here be a ten-pound note for you. do not mention

this night’s work to anybody, and I will take care your wage be

raise. now, sir,” he continue to Joe’s late adversary, “I be ready

to talk to you--by the way, about the girl; she be your accomplice, of

course?”



the stranger nod.



“your sweetheart have deceive you, Joe,” add Slangsby; “give she a

good lecturing, and then lock she up for the night in the saddle-room;

she must not be at liberty till the race be over, upon any account.”



Honest Joe scratch his head in deep perplexity--then a light dawn

upon he, and he perceive how Mary have beguile he seize she roughly

by the wrist, he drag she off, exclaim, “come along, thee cheat

jade, could’st thee foind nothing well to do than to go and deceive a

poor lad that love thee, and try to get he into trouble? if thee be

but a man, I’d wollup thee till thou could’st not stand, and as it be, a

little starving will do thee good, so cum along.”



at the same moment Slangsby and his companion quit the stable, and

adjourn to the trainer’s private apartment, hold there a long

and solemn conference; the result may be gather from the follow

speeches--



“and you feel sure Lord Bellefield be aware of the whole thing?”

 question Slangsby.



“I’ve not a doubt of it,” be the reply. “Turnbull be too ready with

the blunt to be act on his own account, he have not get the money to

do it. I be to have £500 clear for this job, and my expense pay to any

part of the continent I may select.”



“and we may trust you?”



“why, of course you may, man; by do as you propose I escape

transportation, receive £500 to start afresh with, and get send over to

Paris out of harm’s way free of expense.”



“and your conscience?” inquire Slangsby with a sarcastic smile. “curse

conscience,” be the angry reply; “I begin life with as much honourable

feeling as any man, but the villainy of the world have crush it out of

I. life be a struggle, and each one must take care of himself; while I

have money I spend it liberally, and meet my engagement honestly. now I

have none, I get it as I can. I undertake to drug your horse because I

be deeply in debt, all but starve, and Bellefield’s bribe offer I

a chance. I fail through an accident, and fall into your power; your

proposal regain I the position, and I embrace it now as I do before.

true, I deceive he: fancy your horse be poison he will double his

bet, which be very heavy already, and be ruin, as well man have

be before he, but this only serve he right for his rascality,

and--put £500 into my pocket. I have to thank you for your civility,

Mr. Slangsby, and to wish you good morning.” he turn to go, then

pausing, said--



“you have use I well in this affair, and to show you I be not all

bad, I will give you a hint. do not rely too much on the result of that

trial: Bellefield’s colt be only recover from the strangle then,

and have since improve in speed and bottom; still Tartuffe can beat

he _if he be make the most of_; everything therefore depend upon

your jockey; if he be careless or over-confident, Oracle may have it

yet--_verbum sit_.” so say, he place his hat on one side of his

head, coolly run his finger through his hair, and depart.









CHAPTER LIX.--DESCRIBES that indescribable SCENE, “the DERBY day.”



“Fair laugh the morn, and soft the zephyr play,” as Lord

Bellefield, having hold an interview with his trainer, which have serve

in great measure to set his mind at ease, canter back to the inn at

Epsom, shave the small portion of his chin which he see fit to

denude of hair, make an elaborate toilet in the good style of sporting

dandyism, and then lounge down to breakfast, of which meal he have

invite some dozen of his intimate to partake. amongst the last comer

be a tall, dark-whisker man, who might be two or three year

Lord Bellefield’s senior. point to a seat on his right hand, his

entertainer began--



“well, Philips, how be it with you this morning? you’ve be wander

about as usual, pick up the late news, I suppose? what say the

prophet?”



“there be nothing original hazard, my lord,” be the reply.



“oracle be as much in favour as ever; Phosphorus be look up slightly,

and the Tartuffe party be back their horse to a high figure; they

seem to be in earnest, and mean to win if they can.”



“ay, if they can,” return Lord Bellefield, smile ironically; “I

confess, for my own part, I do not see that animal’s good point.”



“he have wonderful power in the loin, and his deep girth give plenty

of room for the lung to play; no fear of ‘bellow to mend’ in that

quarter,” be the reply.



“very excellent point in a hunter or steeplechase horse, but misplace

in a racer, and by no mean calculate to make up for a want of

fleetness. Tartuffe, in my opinion, have not the true racehorse stride,

as Austerlitz will find to his cost, if he really be lay money on

he.”



“he may not cover so much ground in his stride as Oracle, but he be

unusually quick in his gallop, and take two stroke while another horse

be take one. still black and yellow (Lord Bellefield’s colour) will

give he the go-by, and that be all _we_ have to look to,” be the

reply.



in converse such as this, diversify by the interchange of bet of more

or less magnitude, the breakfast (if a meal consist of every delicacy

that could please the palate or pamper the appetite, include meat,

fish, etc., etc., can be legitimately so call) pass off. when

liqueur have be hand round, Lord Bellefield’s drag be announce,

and the company disperse, first to admire and criticise the turn-out,

and then to dispose of themselves on and about it. the equipage be in

perfect taste, and although not so showy as many other on which less

care have be bestow, or money expend, yet the drag, with its panel

of the darkest possible cinnamon brown, pick out with a light shade

of the same colour; the four blood bay, faultless in symmetry; the two

outrider on horse so exactly match those in harness, that any one

unaccustomed to such matter might have be puzzle to conjecture how

the groom could distinguish one from another; the harness perfectly

free from ornament of any kind, save black and yellow rosette in the

horse’ head; the two groom in dark, well-fit, pepper-and-salt

livery, and irreproachable top-boot and leather; the coronet on the

door, the cockade in the hat; every trifle down to the gold-mount

whip-handle, excellent of its kind, and in harmonious keeping with

the whole, present to the eye of a connoisseur a _tout ensemble_

calculate to excite his high admiration.



seat himself firmly on his box, and control his fiery horse with

an easy confidence which prove he a skilful whip, Lord Belle-field

drive to the Downs, apparently impassable obstacle seem to melt

before he as if by magic (one of the sure test of a good coachman),

and arrive on the course exactly at the “correct” moment. as he draw up

to take his place by the rope, a showy britska, draw by four splendid

grey, the postilion’ bright green jacket and velvet cap blaze

with gold, dash in before he. the carriage contain two person--a

singularly handsome young man with a foreign cast of feature, and a

girl with black, flash eye and a brilliant complexion, dress not

only in, but considerably beyond the height of the fashion. these

be the Duc d’Austerlitz and Mademoiselle Angélique, the fascinating_

danseuse_.



as Lord Bellefield, with curl lip, pass they to take up his station

far on, the Frenchman, catch his eye, nod carelessly, and

turn to his companion say a few word in a low tone, and they both

laugh. have Lord Bellefield been live at a period when the state of

society allow the hand to act out the feeling of the heart, he would

at that moment have spring upon the due d’Austerlitz, and seize he by

the throat, have hold on remorselessly till life become extinct. as it

be, he merely return the nod by a bow, smile, kiss the tip of his

glove to Angélique, and drive on; so that, after all, civilisation have

its advantage.



having choose his station, the bay be unharness and lead away, and a

mount groom approach, lead his master’s hack.



“I be go down to the ring, and then to the Warren, to see they

saddle,” begin Lord Bellefield, “so I must leave you to take care of

yourself; but any one dispose for luncheon will find something to

that effect go on here after the race. if I be not back, Robson

will take good care of you.” so say, he give an order to one of the

servant, who remain with the drag, then, mount his horse, canter

away.



“he carry it off boldly enough, but they say if he lose the race he

be a ruin man,” observe one of the _friend_ he have leave behind he.



“oh, Lord Ashford will clear he,” remark another; “his grandfather

be one of the lead counsel of the day, and the old boy feather

his nest well before he give up his wig and gown. he be one of the

old school of lawyer, and work in the day when a barrister’s

professional income be a great fact, whereas now it be a great

fiction.”



“come, Briefless, no grumbling; back Oracle for a cool £500, and then

you may cut chamber till the season’s over. but you be wrong about

Bellefield. Lord Ashford have pay his debt three time, and have take

an oath on the family Bible never to do so again; but I don’t believe

Bellefield’s anything like hard-up. you know he win £30,000 of poor

Mellerton before he blow his brain out. here’s Philips can tell we all

about it; eh, what do you say, man?”



“nothing,” be the cautious reply; “and I would not recommend you to

let Bellefield find out exactly all you’ve be mention, my dear

Chatterby; I’ve know he shoot a man for less.” so say, Mr. Philips

join in the laugh he have raise against the voluble Chatterby, and

then swinge himself down from the box, leave they in order to take his

place in the bet ring.



we must now change the venue to the Warren, a small but picturesque spot

of ground encircle by a wall, within which enclosure the horse for

the Derby and Oaks be saddle and mount. here jockey and gentleman,

lord, blackleg, trainer, and pickpocket, mix and jostle with

one another indiscriminately. Assuredly Epsom, on the Derby day, in

exclusive, aristocratic England, be the only true Utopia wherein those

chimera of french folly, Liberty, Fraternity, and Equality, exist

and prosper. let the reader imagine from twenty to five-and-twenty

blood-horse, each lead by its attendant groom and follow by an anxious

trainer, while the jockey who be to ride it, and on whose skill and

courage thousand of pound be depend, carefully inspect the

buckling of girth, regulate the length of stirrup-leather, and as far

as human foresight will permit, provide against any accident which

may embarrass he in the come struggle. then the horse-clothing

be remove, and the shine coat and carefully-plait mane of the

racehorse be reveal to the eye of the admire spectator; an

attendant satellite at the same moment assist the jockey to divest

himself of his greatcoat, and he emerge from his chrysalis state in

all the butterfly splendour of race dandyism. then the trainer, or the

satellite before allude to, “give he a leg up,” and with this slight

assistance he vault lightly into the saddle and become as it be

incorporate with the animal he bestride. quietly gather up the

rein, he press his cap firmly on his head, slant the point of

his whip towards the right flank, exchange a few last word with the

trainer, and then walk his horse up and down till his competitor be

all equally prepare. on this occasion the cynosure of every eye be the

first favourite, Oracle, and when his clothing be remove, and one

of the clever jockey of the day seat gracefully on his back, he

certainly do look, to quote the enthusiastic language of his trainer,

“a reg’lur pictur,” the perfection of a racehorse. turnbull’s last word

to the jockey were--



“save he as much as you safely can till the distance, and if the pace

have be anything like reasonable, it will be your own fault if the race

be not your own.”



a slight contraction of the eyelid prove that the advice be understand

and appreciate, and man and horse pass on.



“how be it Tartuffe do not show?” inquire Lord Bellefield of Turnbull

in a whisper. “the dose cann’t have be give too strong, eh?”



“no fear of that, my lud,” be the reply; “but they’ve probably

discover ere this that there be a screw loose somewhere, and they

will keep he out of sight as long as they can, lest other people should

become as wise as they be themselves.”



as he speak the object of his remark appear; his rider be already

mount, and the horse-clothing remove. Tartuffe be a complete

contrast to his rival in appearance. the Dodona Colt be a bright bay,

with black mane, tail, and leg; his head be small, almost to a fault,

and shape like that of a deer, his neck long and more arched than be

usually the case in thorough-breed horse; while his graceful, slender

limb seem to embody the very ideal of swiftness. Tartuffe be

altogether a small and more compact animal, his colour a rich, dark

chestnut, his head large in proportion, and so place on as to give he

the appearance of be slightly ewe-neck, his foreleg be short,

and the arm more muscular than those of his graceful rival; but the

sloping shoulder, the depth of the girth, the breadth and unusual

muscular development of the loin and haunch, together with a

quick, springy step and a general compactness of form, afford to the

practise eye evidence of his possess very uncommon power both of

speed and endurance.



“he look fresh and lively enough,” remark Lord Bellefield, after

observe the horse narrowly. “what do you think about it?”



“it’s all right, my lud,” be Turnbull’s confident answer; “thing

speak for themselves, the ’orse ain’t allow to show till the last

minute, and then he come out with his jockey ready mount. now the

logic of that dodge lie in a nutshell: find the hanimal sleepy and

out of sort, they keep he snug till they’re force to purduce he,

and then show he with the jockey on he, when a touch with the spur

and a pull or two at his mouth with a sharp bit make he look alive

again.” approach his lip almost to his employer’s ear, he continue,

“do you see that patch of black grease on his nose? that’s where the

twitch have cut he. Beverley be oblige to twitch he to give he the

ball--so now your ludship may bet away without any fear of Tartuffe,”

 and exchange a significant glance, this well-match pair part.



“ah! Bellefield, _mon cher!_ how lovely your colt look this morning--I

suppose he be to win; for myself I be prepare to be martyrise with a

resignation the most touching,” and as he speak Armand Duc d’Austerlitz

stroke his silky moustache and admire his glossy boot with an air of

the most innocently graceful self-satisfaction possible.



“you don’t really believe that which you say, Monsieur le Duc,” reply

Lord Bellefield; “I never see a horse in well racing condition than

Tartuffe.”



“ah! _c’est un bon petit cheval_, and I have bet, ah!--bah!--I cannot

tell you what sum of money upon he, more than half my estate in

Languedoc; positively I shall have to go through what you call the Bench

of your Queen, if I lose.”



“in that case it be useless for I to inquire whether you be dispose

to back Tartuffe against my bay colt,” insinuate Lord Bellefield.



“no, not if you have a fancy that way, _mon cher ami_,” reply Armand,

smile to show his white tooth; “what shall we say?--an even bet of

£3000 shall it be, or £5000?”



“five be the more comfortable sum of the two,” return Lord Bellefield

quickly. “I always like to bet five or ten; it simplify one’s book

amazingly, and I never have a taste for intricate arithmetic.”



“_comme il vous plaira_--say ten, if you like it well.” and as he

speak Armand draw out a miniature bet-book and a gold pencil-case

blaze with jewel. Lord Bellefield pause for a moment; certain as he

believe himself to be of the race, it be a great temptation. but, on

the other hand, if he appear too eager, might not suspicion attach to

he in the event of any clue be gain to the poisoning affair? the

idea be so alarming to he that prudence overcame avarice.



“I have unfortunately no estate in Languedoc,” he say laughingly; “and

thousand be not so entirely a matter of indifference to I as to your

grace; so we will book the bet at five.”



the wager be accordingly so enter; and with friendly smile and

courteous word upon their lip these two man part, one hate the

successful rival, the other despise the detect swindler! alas! for

the sham and deception of society! pasteboard and tinsel be more real

than its hollow-hearted seeming.



“now you see your game,” be Slangsby’s last word to the jockey who

be to ride Tartuffe. “make run early in the race, so as to render

the pace as severe as possible throughout: your horse will live to the

end, and theirs willn’t; but if he be not well blow before he get to the

distance, it will be a very close thing, and the length of his stride

may beat you.”



“I’m awake,” be the concise reply; but Slangsby be quite satisfied

therewith.



racing may be very cruel, and it may lead to gambling and various other

immorality, major and minor; and be thus prove contrary to the

precept of Christianity, good people may be quite right in use their

good effort to discourage it. nevertheless, it be a manly and exciting

sport; and although the evil to which we have allude may (and,

we fear, do) attend it, we cannot see that the amusement in itself

necessitate they. on the contrary, we conceive that they be add to

it by the proneness to evil inherent in human nature, rather than as the

natural consequence of the sport itself. however this may be, a finer

sight than the start for the Derby we cannot easily imagine. let the

reader picture to himself some twenty three-year-old colt, their proud,

expand nostril snuff the wind, and their glossy coat glisten

in the sunshine, ride by the crack jockey of England, and therefore

of the world, draw up in a line, preparatory to start; let he

reflect, in order fully to realise the earnest nature of the scene,

that on the fact of which may prove the well horse depend many

thousand--perhaps, in the aggregate, more than a million of pound

sterling; that the ruin of hundred may be involve in the event of the

race; that on the chance of that whirlwind course have be expend

the anxious thought, the careful calculation of day and week and

month; that the weighing and reduce these calculation to a theoretic

system, by which some certainty may be attain, be the business of

many man’s lives,--and he will then have some faint idea of the deep,

overpower interest that be excite by witness the start for the

Derby.



on the occasion which we be describe two false start occur. twice

as the word “go!” be pronounce by the stentorian lung of the starter

do one queer-temper animal choose pertinaciously to turn its tail

where its head should have be; and twice do the same “voice of power”

 vociferate the command “come back!” and deep, if not loud, be the

anathema breathe by those jockey who, having manoeuvre themselves

into a good position, have contrive to “get away” well. however,

“ ’tis an ill wind which blow good to nobody;” and these delay,

annoying as they be to most of the party concern, be as much

in favour of the supporter of Oracle as they be prejudicial to the

interest of those who have back Tartuffe.



Oracle, amongst other gift of fortune, chance to be bless with

a most amiable and placid temper, while Tartuffe, not possess so

philosophical a turn of mind, be apt to get excited in a crowd, and the

first false start completely unsettle he, he avail himself of the

second to bolt half-way to Tattenham Corner before his rider could pull

he in; and even when that feat be accomplish he show a decided

preference for use his hind-leg only in progression on his return

to the starting-post; by his riotous and unmanageable conduct take a

great deal more out of himself than be by any mean prudent.



once more, however, they be all in their place--the word be again

give, and they be off--Tartuffe spring away with a bound like that

of a lion, and half dislocate his rider’s arm by a furious effort

to “get his head.” as it happen that there be two or three other

“queer” temper horse besides that of the Duc d’Austerlitz which

require careful handling, the pace at first be by no mean so “good”

 as Slangsby have wish it to be; nor could the jockey ride Tartuffe

venture to improve it, for two reason: in the first place, his horse

be so excited that it require all his skill to prevent his running

away with he; in the second, his former attempt to bolt have suffice to

puff he, and he require “saving” to enable he to regain wind. in the

meantime Oracle be go sweetly and easily, keep up with his horse

in what appear scarcely beyond a canter. when past the “Corner,”

 however, Tartuffe have decidedly improve, and his rider, remember

his instruction, begin to make play. as the pace increase, the “first

flight” become considerably more select, the “tender-hearte” one

gradually drop in the rear.



up to this point Phosphorus have be lead, follow by Advance,

Whisker, the Lynx, Gossip, and Challenger; but down the next slope

Tartuffe come up, pass the other horse, and after run neck to

neck with Phosphorus for about a quarter of a mile, take the lead, and

keep it by about half a length, Oracle lie well up on the near

side. this order they preserve till near the distance, when Lynx and

Challenger put on the steam to dispute the leadership with Tartuffe, who

appear by no mean dispose to relinquish the post of honour, and

the pace grow decidedly severe, in spite of which Oracle continue

insensibly to creep up to the other.



at the distance Lynx find it “no go,” and fall back beat; Gossip

take his place, closely wait on by Phosphorus and Oracle; a few

stride more, in which Oracle improve his position, and then the final

struggle begin, whip and spur go to work in earnest--the pace be

actually terrific--Gossip shut up, Phosphorus be extinguish, Oracle

and Tartuffe run neck and neck, dust fly, handkerchief wave, the

spectator shout, when, just at the critical moment, the Frenchman’s

horse shoot forward, as if propel by some invisible power, the

favourite be beat by rather more than a head, and Tartuffe remain

winner of the Derby.









chapter LX.--CONTAINS some “novel” remarks upon the ROMANTIC CEREMONY of

MATRIMONY.



“frere, old fellow, have you prepare your wedding garment?” inquire

Bracy, meet his friend accidentally one fine day, about a week after

the occurrence of the event describe in the last chapter.



“ay, I hear that your machination have succeed,” return Frere

gruffly, “and that De Grandeville be about to marry Lady Lombard. I’ll

tell you what it be, Bracy; it strike I that in assist people to

make fool of themselves and each other, you be just waste your time

and pervert your talent: depend upon it you may very safely leave

folk to perform that operation on their own account, they be not

likely to class that amongst their sin of omission.”



“make fool of themselves!” repeat Bracy. “my dear Frere, it’s nothing

of the sort, that be an ‘_opus operatum_,’ a deed do for our

friend by beneficent nature, long before I have the pleasure of their

acquaintance. moreover, in the present case, I be seek to diminish,

rather than to increase, the standing amount of folly--man and wife

be one, you know; _ergo_, by unite Lady Lombard and the mighty De

Grandeville, the rank of the feeble-minded be one fool _minus_.”



“well, that certainly be an ingenious way of put it,” rejoin

frere, laugh in spite of himself; “and pray how have you contrive to

bring about this delectable affair; for I conclude the match _be_ your

handiwork?”



“oh! the thing be easy enough to accomplish,” reply Bracy. “I

invent pretty speech, which I declare to each that the other

have make about they; I exaggerate De Grandeville’s position to Lady

Lombard, and Lady Lombard’s wealth to De Grandeville; in short, I lie

perseveringly and judiciously until I fancy I have get the affair

thoroughly _en train_. but I soon find out there be a hitch somewhere;

it be clearly not on the lady’s side, for she be so far go as to

believe in De Grandeville to the extent of estimate he at his own

valuation, which I take to be the _ne plus ultra_ of credulity; so I set

steadily to work to investigate _he_, and if possible find out what

be the matter. I try various scheme, but none of they would act, his

reserve be impenetrable; at last, in despair, I give he a champagne

dinner at the Polysnobion, take care to ply he well with wine, and to

walk home with he afterwards. that do the business--he must have be

most transcendently drunk and no mistake, for before we reach his

lodging, having confess to I that his grandfather have be a tallow

chandler, he go on to relate that the bar to his union with Lady

Lombard be his inability to discover that she possess any pedigree.”



“well, for that matter,” interrupt frere, “having admit the tallow

chandler, I don’t see that he need have be so very particular as to

the aristocratic tendency of Lady Lombard’s ancestry.”



“De Grandeville do not think so,” resume Bracy; “he argue that no

amount of chandlery could infuse vulgarity into the blood of one of his

illustrious house; external circumstance, he declare, be powerless

to affect the innate nobility of a De Grandeville: whole year of

melt day would fail to drop a spot upon that illustrious name.

but for a man, the founder of whose family come over with the Norman

William, to marry a woman without a pedigree, one who probably never have

so much as a grandfather belong to she, be impossible: he have a

warm regard for Lady Lombard; he consider that his name and influence,

support by her wealth, would place he in one of the proud

position to which a mortal could aspire; but even for this he could not

sacrifice his lead principle, he could not ally himself to any one

without a pedigree.



“see that he be in earnest, I forbear to laugh at he; and merely

throw out hint that I have reason to believe he be in error, and

that although Lady Lombard’s father (an amiable soap-boiler, whose

virtue simmer for sixty year in the neighbourhood of Shoreditch)

have be engage in commerce (he paraphrase the tallow chandler into a

russian merchant), as well as his grandfather, still the argument which

apply to the one case would hold good in the other, and at all event

I beg he to take no rash or precipitate step in the matter till I

have apply to a friend of mine (of course invent for the occasion)

who be a genealogist, and use my good endeavour to clear up the

difficulty--for which disintereste offer he, be still more or less

inebriate, bless I reiteratedly and fervently, and so, having see

he safely home, we part. the next morning I visit Lady Lombard,

lead she on sweetly and easily to talk of her family, gain some

information, and learn where to obtain more, and in less than two

day have the satisfaction of prove her fiftieth cousin sixteen time

remove to Edward the Third. De Grandeville be introduce to my friend

in the Heralds’ Office----.”



“whom you declare a minute ago to be invent for the occasion,”

 interrupt frere.



“for which reason he be the more easily personate by Tom Edgehill

of the Fusileers,” resume the unblushing Bracy. “De Grandeville be

allow, as a great favour, to peruse the pedigree, believe in it----”



“or pretend to do so,” suggest Frere.



“to the full extent!” continue Bracy, not heed the interruption,

“and the next thing I hear be that the party be engage.”



“so he be actually go to marry a woman without an idea, properly so

call, in her head, and half as old again as he be, for the sake of

her money. well! that’s an abyss of degradation I’ll never sink to

while there be a crossing to be sweep in London,” be Frere’s disgusted

comment.



“_Chacun à son gout_--for my own part I should prefer involuntary

emigration at the expense and for the good of my country, vulgarly

denominate transportation, to be marry at all, even be the

oppose party (my hypothetical wife, I mean) the most thorough-breed

angel that ever wear a bustle,” return Bracy. “by the way,” he

continue, “I see your little friend, Miss Arundel, the other day; she

and her mother be stay with the ‘Lombardie Character,’ I find, but

of course you know all this well than I do. really that girl write

exceedingly good sense for a woman. now if I be a marry man, I

don’t know any quarter in which I’d soon throw the handkerchief.”



“you might pick it up again for your pain, for she wouldn’t have you,

I’m sure,” growl Frere.



“do you really believe so?” ask Bracy, with an incredulous smile.

“Ahem! now I flatter myself the little Arundel have well taste.”



“well sense than to do any such thing, you mean,” return frere more

crossly than before. “depend upon it, whenever Rose Arundel marrie, she

will choose a man who can respect and love she, and not a--well, I don’t

mean to insult you, my good fellow, but truth will out--a self-conceite

young puppy, whose head have be turn by foolish people, by whom his

cleverness have be overrate and his vanity foster.”



Bracy draw himself up, and for a minute pretend to look very

fierce--then burst into a hearty laugh, he pat his companion on

the back affectionately, exclaim, “poor old Frere! do I put he in

a rage? never mind, old boy, I only want to know whether there be any

truth in the report that you be engage to Miss Arundel--and now let

I congratulate you. you have no doubt good cause for think the young

lady would have show her wisdom by select a sensible man such as you

be, rather than a _vaurien_ like myself, even if I be a marrying man

and have place such a temptation before she.”



Frere look at he for a moment in utter astonishment, then mutter,

“a vaurien, indeed! I always prophesy that foot-boy of yours would

die with a rope round his neck, but I begin to think the complaint which

will necessitate such an operation run in the family, and that servant

and master be alike affect by it.”



“and what may be the name of this alarming epidemic which you consider

likely to terminate so fatally?” ask Bracy.



“a most unmitigated and virulent form of chronic impudence,” return

Frere, laugh; then shake hand most cordially, these two oddly

assorted friend part.



after having leave Bracy, Frere bend his step towards the dwelling of

Lady Lombard, with whom Mrs. Arundel and Rose be spend a few

day for the avow purpose of assist to prepare her wedding

paraphernalia, though (as the most skilful dressmaker and the most

expensive tradesman in London be at work in the good cause) their

duty be merely nominal. Mrs. Arundel having explain to her hostess

the nature of the engagement between Frere and her daughter, that

excellent bear be allow to run tame about the house. Lady Lombard,

who be at first impress by a vague sense of his awful amount of

learning, and decidedly alarmed at his snapping and growling, have become

reconcile to his presence on perceive that Rose could tame he by a

word or a smile, and commit he to her care and management, trouble

herself no far about he.



“Rose, who do you think have go to Venice?” inquire Frere, after

having disburden his pocket of a little library of heavy book, two

cold, uncomfortable fossil, and the very hard handle of a roman sword,

all highly prize and newly acquire treasure, which he have bring

that Rose might appreciate they and sympathise with he in his delight

at having obtain they.



“to Venice,” return Rose--“oh! who? do tell I.”



“why, lot of people, it seem,” reply Frere. “I call upon my uncle,

Lord Ashford, this morning, and find he in what be vulgarly term a

regular stew. Bellefield it seem have a horse which everybody fancy

be to win the Derby, but what everybody fancy do not come to pass,

for the say horse be beat, consequently his owner have lose no end of

money, for which same I for one do not pity he: I have no sympathy with

your ruined gamester--and ruin he be, by the way, horse, foot, and

artillery, as the military De Grandeville would say. well, poor

uncle Ashford show I a note he have just receive from his dutiful

first-bear, tell he that he have not a farthing of ready money in the

world, except £50 to pay for his journey; that he be quite unable to

meet his engagement, and that before the settle day for the Derby he

must put the British Channel between he and those to whom he owe sum

so large that he neither desire nor expect his father to pay they;

that he would feel oblige if his lordship would increase his yearly

allowance, and that he wish letter of credit to be forward to he

at Venice, to which place he propose immediately to follow General

Grant and his daughter, who it appear leave England only three week

ago; that it be his intention to marry the young lady forthwith, and

live abroad upon her fortune, until something to his advantage should

turn up; and he add in a postscript, that if his father should attempt

to prevent his marriage by inform General Grant of what he be please

to call his misfortune, that minute he will blow his brain out. well,

poor uncle, who be a high-minded, honourable man, though he be rather

proud and cold in his manner, could not bear the idea of his son

marry Annie Grant without inform the General of his loss of

fortune, and at length he resolve to meet the difficulty by sell

the H----shire estate, and by that mean increase Lord Bellefield’s

allowance till it would amount to £3000 a year, in which case General

Grant might be inform of the truth without the match be break off,

or Bellefield drive to desperation. Whereupon I observe innocently

enough that the success of the scheme would in great measure depend on

the tact of the person send out to manage the negotiation: Lord Ashford

agree in this most cordially, and then say how grateful he should

feel to any one who would assist he in this strait, look hard at

me----”



“and you instantly undertake the commission; I know it as well as if I

have be present and have hear all that pass,” interpose Rose with a

smile, in which, though affection predominate, a slight shade of regret

might have be trace.



“why, you see, Rose, as I be one of the family, there seem a kind of

obligation upon I to do something to help they; and poor uncle Ashford

do look so pitiful; and really if I have not undertake it, I don’t know

who could have be find to do so, for Bellefield quarrel with

their family solicitor because he refuse to allow he to make duck and

drake of some of the entail property three year ago; and I shall not

be go long. besides, I do not quite forget you, Rosey, for, do you

comprehend, I shall be able to see Lewis without his fancying that I

have be send out expressly to look after he, and perhaps I may be

able to persuade he to come home and live in England like a reasonable

being and a Christian; at all event, I shall find out how he be go

on there; and I’ve another thing to talk to Lewis about--I don’t mean to

remain for ever without a wife, Miss Rose--you need not turn your head

away--that’s sheer silliness--you know we _be_ to be marry some day,

we expect matrimony will increase our happiness, and we have sounder

ground for our expectation than most of the fool who yoke themselves

together for life; those who do so, for instance, in order to obtain

rank or rich--our next-door neighbour to wit,” and he point with

his thumb in the direction of the drawing-room, wherein be seat the

mighty De Grandeville and his lady-love. “as, therefore, my reasoning be

good, sound reasoning, and matrimony prove to be a desirable thing, why

the soon we get the ceremony over the well; so, as I say before,

don’t turn away your head like a little goose, see that you’re

nothing of the kind!”



“Poor Lewis,” murmur Rose; “he will scarcely rejoice to see you when

he learn that the object of your mission be to hasten the marriage of

Annie Grant with Lord Bellefield. oh, Richard,” she continue eagerly,

clasp her hand, “it will make he hate you--do not go!”



“well, now, I never think of that,” mutter Frere, thoroughly

perplexed. “why will people go and fall in love with one another that

don’t ought to?” he pause, rub his hair back from his forehead till

it stand on end like the crest of a cockatoo, play with Rose’s

workbox till he overturn it, and in his abstraction commit so many

gaucherie that his companion be on the point of call he to order

when he suddenly return to his sense, and take Rose’s hand in his,

begin, “now listen to I, my child: in the first place, as this matter

nearly concern Lewis, and therefore you, I will do nothing in it of

which you do not approve; premise this, I will give you my own idea

on the subject. Touching Lewis’s interest in the affair, the question

seem to hinge upon this point: do Annie Grant care for he or not?

if she do not, it cann’t signify to he who she marry; and as in that

case she be probably attach to her cousin (woman don’t _always_ love

wisely, you know), I should feel able to carry out uncle Ashford’s

wish with a clear conscience, and trust to Lewis’s good sense and kind

heart not to incur his displeasure by so do. if, on the other hand,

Annie by any chance love he, and have be bully or persuade into

this engagement, I for one will have nothing to do with promote the

match, but, on the contrary, will exert myself to the utmost to prevent

it; and now what say you?”



“that by do as you propose you will act rightly, kindly, and

judiciously, and that come what may of it, your interference must be

for good,” return Rose, gaze with look of proud affection upon the

simple-hearte, high-principle “_honest_ man” (indeed “the noble

work of God”) who sit beside she. “but,” she continue after a moment’s

thought, “there be one difficulty which I scarcely see how you will get

over--how be you to find out whom Annie Grant really love?”



“ask she myself,” be the straightforward reply. Rose look at he to

see if he be joke, but his face be earnest and resolve.



“oh, Richard, you will never be able to do that,” she remonstrate;

“remember how such a question must distress she.”



“which do you think will distress she most--to be ask abruptly to give

her confidence to a person who be anxious to befriend she, or to spend

her life with one man, when all the time she love another?” inquire

Frere almost sternly. then lay his hand on Rose’s head and stroke

her glossy hair, he continue, “no! no! Rosey, away with all such

sophistry; they be the devil’s emissary to render people first

miserable, and then reckless and wicked. marriage, properly so term,

may be make in Heaven, but depend upon it, the spurious article too

often foist upon the public under that name--alliance in which

this world’s good be everything, and the treasure of the next world

nothing--come from quite another manufactory.”



then there be a pause, and then Rose inquire when he propose to set

out.



“why, there be no good in procrastinating,” be the reply; “the soon I

start the soon I shall be back again, so to-morrow the lawyer get the

necessary paper ready; the next day good Lady Goosecap here be to be

marry, and I mean to attend the ceremony in order to learn how

to behave on such an occasion; and the day after that, if nothing

unforeseen occur to prevent I, I’m off!”



“you will write very often--every----” (Frere raise his eyebrow) “well

then, every other day, will you not?” urge Rose appealingly.



“what queer thing woman be!” soliloquise Frere. “now, if you have be

go to the North Pole,” he continue, address Rose, “it would never

have occur to I to _ask_ you to write--I should have take it

for grant that if you have discover the northwest passage, or do

anything else worth mention, you would have let one know; and why

people write if they have nothing to say I cann’t think.”



“at all event, it be a satisfaction when we be part from those who

be dear to we to be assure that they be well,” suggest Rose.



“oh, nothing ever ail I,” reply frere, quietly appropriate the

remark; “there be not a doctor in the country who have ever receive one

farthing of my money; and as to physic--throw physic to the dog, always

suppose you to have any such abomination to dispose of, and any dog

at hand to throw it to: it’s a thing I don’t know the taste of, and

where ignorance be bliss--well, never mind, I’ll write to you all the

same, if you have a weakness that way, whenever I can find pen, paper,

and a post-office; only if my letter should happen to be rather prosy,

somewhat in the much-ado-about-nothing style, small blame to I, that’s

all.” thus the expedition be agree upon, and Rose having tell Frere

some hundred thing which he be to say to and inquire of Lewis, sit

down to write a _few_ more “note and query,” wind up with a

pathetic appeal to her brother to bring his self-impose exile to a

conclusion.



so the silver-foot hour turn round the treadmill of time, till the

dewy morn appear which be to witness the celebration of the nuptial

of Lady Lombard and the mighty Marmaduke De Grande-ville. oh, the ardour

and bustle of that devoted household! as for the servant, so late do

they sit up and so early do they rise, that go to bed at all become

rather a superstitious observance than a beneficial practice. then

everybody have to dress, first themselves and then somebody else; and the

amount of white muslin concentrate in that happy family render space

crisp, and give a look of pastoral simplicity to the most iniquitously

gorgeous arrangement of modern upholstery.



the bride’s dress be wonderful--word be powerless to describe

it--happy those woman who, favour beyond all other daughter of Eve,

be permit to behold it. one very young lady, rash in her ignorance,

venture to ask how much the lace cost a yard. the french artiste,

Mademoiselle Melanie Amandine Celestine Seraphine Belledentelles,

piously invoke six authorised female saint, besides the deceased

Madame Tournour, at whose flounce she have sit to acquire her art,

and whom, on her lamented removal to Père la Chaise, she have privately

canonise for her own especial use and behoof, and thus support do

_not_ faint. the “mistress of the robe,” a black-eye, brown-cheek

grisette, turn as pale as her complexion permit she and sink upon a

chair, but be unprovide with a smell-bottle, think it advisable

not to proceed to extremity, and the mother of the culprit hurry

forward, and with great presence of mind lead she from the room--such

mystery be not for the profane.



then occur a tremendous episode--the dress be dispose in graceful

fold over the ample person of its fortunate possessor, and fit

seraphically; only the bottom hook and eye, situate in the region round

about the waist, would by no mean permit themselves to be unite, and

a lucid interval, _hiatus valde lachrymabili_, be the fearful

consequence. the grisette do she very utmost, but her strength be

inadequate to command the success her zeal deserve, and with flushed

cheek and tearful eye she glance appealingly to Mademoiselle Melanie

Amandine, etc., etc.



that ardent foreigner step forward to the rescue, all the noble

self-confidence of her nation flash in her coal-black eye, and

gallantly assume the post of danger--she be a small woman, but she

frame be compact and wiry, and Tydeus-like--



               “her little body hold a mighty mind.”



set to work with spirit, she devote all her energy to the task

before she, aged Lady Lombard wince palpably, unconsciously echo

Hamlet’s well-know aspiration--but that good lady’s melting mood be

unfortunately mental, not bodily, and in this attempt “to take she in”

 even the french dressmaker be foil; the “too solid” substance be

not compressible beyond a certain point, and with a sigh which have a

marvellous resemblance to the word _sacr-r-r-e_, Mademoiselle Melanie

Amandine, etc., etc., desist.



“_ah! qu’ils sont difficile ce agraffes!_” she exclaim, rub she

little hand with a theatrical gesture. “I have not to myself force in

_les poignet_, vot you call oncle.”



[illustration: 0462]



“wrist,” mildly suggest Mrs. Arundel, who be assist to attire the

bride--“mine be very strong, let I try”--and suit the action to the

word, she, Curtius-like, endeavour to close the yawning gulf, but in

vain.



“_ah non! c’est impossible_--you shall only hurt your hand too motch,

_chère Madame Hirondelle_,” resume Mademoiselle--“permit I to ring

zie bell, we shall make approach _le maître d’hôtel_, vot you call zie

coachman of zie chambaire, who shall have much of force, _et ce sera un

fait accompli_.”



“Stop, Madurmoysel,” exclaim Lady Lombard aghast, as the energetic

Frenchwoman lay her hand upon the bell-rope--“Stop, if you please, I

should not like--that is, it be not exactly the custom to admit the male

domestic into one’s bedroom.”



for a moment the Frenchwoman appear utterly puzzle as to the reason

of the objection, then a light break in upon she, and she begin, “_ah,

je comprend!_ it ee not etiquette; _que je suis bête!_ how I be

stupide! _mais qu’ils sont drôle ce petit scandale Anglais! Vraiment

c’est comme la comédie. a Paris nous ne remarquon pas ce petit rien;

et en Allemagne_, zie schneider, vat you call tailor, _il font toute

le robe--mais comment faire donc?_”



“why really, Madurmoysel, _je nur par_--I mean, I don’t think I could

bear it, if it be get to,” remonstrate Lady Lombard; “don’t you think

the hook and eye might be move a little? it’s unfortunate I be so

stout--_mais je nur_--can’t help it.”



“_oh, mille pardon, miladi_. your lady sheep shall not be too stout;

_après la première jeunesse l’embonpoint_ be a great beauty; but zie

hook and ee, _à est dommage; cependant, nous verron_, ve shall see vot

vill be do.” and so say, Mademoiselle Melanie etc.’s nimble finger

go to work, and a quarter of an inch be graciously accord; by which

mean the impossible become possible, and the crisis be safely get

over.



as to breakfast (not _the_ wedding-breakfast, but the breakfast before

the wedding, two very different matter), that be a regular, or rather

an irregular scramble: people eat and drank stand, like horse, but

in a general way feeling be strong than appetite, and with the

exception of one middle-aged lady, bless with a powerful intellect and

a weak digestion, who have medical authority never to allow herself to

feel hungry, breakfast three time that morning with three different

division of the party, little justice be do to the viand.



Rose make herself generally useful, help all the neglect one, and

bring comfort to the uncomfortable, until she scarcely leave herself

time to dress, and yet appear the most charming little bridesmaid of

the lot, although her five companion do not disgrace their uniform

of white muslin with pink embellishment (the white symbolise their

maiden innocence, and the pink suggest the cheerfulness with which

they would be willing to exchange it for the honourable estate of

matrimony).



then the carriage come to take up, and Mrs. Arundel and the fair

Susannah, relict of Col. Brahmin, H.E.I.C.S., have the great

difficulty in sustain the weak nerve and fluttered spirit of the

bride elect, who, as she herself express it, “bear down by two such

agitate set of recollection,” might well be overcome. however, by

the assistance of a rich male Lombard relation (whose wealth gild his

vulgarity till Mammon worshipper believe this calf a deity) she

be safely convey to the church, where De Grandeville await she,

accompany by a splendid old ancestor, who might by a very slight

stretch of imagination have be take for _the_ identical De

Grandeville who have come over with the Conqueror, and be carefully

preserve (in port wine) ever since. Bracy be there, look

preternaturally solemn, all but his eye, in which, for the time be,

the whole mischief of his nature appear concentrate, and Frere with

he, serve his apprenticeship, as Bracy phrase it.



in solemn procession they approach the altar, where the priest await

they, and open his book, read to they an account of the true nature

of the ceremony they be about to celebrate--how it be “institute by

God in the time of man’s innocency,” and be symbolical of high and holy

thing, and be ordain to assist we in fulfil the various duty

for which we be place in this world, and on the due performance of

which will greatly depend our weal or woe for everlasting, it should

not be undertake lightly or unadvisedly; then De Grandeville, having

learn the theory of the matter, proceed to afford a practical

commentary on the text by solemnly promise to love and honour Lady

Lombard till death they should part, while she, in return, pledge

herself (with less chance of perjury) to serve, obey, and _keep_ he

during the term of her natural life; then he, Marmaduke, take she,

Sarah, from the hand of the wealthy Lombard relation, and declare

that he do so “for rich, for poor,” though we much fear, if he

have foresee the small probability of the realisation of this latter

proviso, the ceremony would have be then and there interrupt,

instead of proceed as it do, sweetly and edifyingly, till it wind

up with “any amazement.” and everybody be much pleased and thoroughly

satisfied, there be, of course, a great deal of cry, though why

they cry, unless it be to see so solemn an institution thus wantonly

profane, and to hear people use word of prayer and praise, and worship

God with their lip, while in their heart they be sacrifice all the

bitter feeling of their nature before the altar of Mammon, we cannot

tell.



amongst the rest Mrs. Arundel weep most meritoriously, until catch

sight of Bracy sob aloud into a very large pocket-handkerchief, she

weeping become somewhat hysterical, and end in a sound suspiciously

like laughter. then people crowd into the vestry, which be about the

size of a good four-post bedstead, and name be sign, and fee pay,

and small joke make, and then the whole party take coach and return

to the house, where _the_ wedding-breakfast await they. the humour

of a wedding-breakfast have be describe so often and so well, that we

shall merely give a very faint outline of the lead idiosyncrasy of

the affair in question.



in the first place, people be very hungry, Nature having assert she

right and promote Appetite, _vice_ feeling sell (or rather starve)

out. even the lady with the weak digestion (which make up by increase

velocity for want of stamina) add a very substantial fourth to she

three previous breakfast. then, as mouth grow disengage, tongue

find room to wag, health be drunk, and the speechifying begin. first

uprose the De Grandeville ancestor, who be a tall, thin, not to

say shadowy old gentleman, with a hooked nose and a weak voice, who

whisper to the company that “he rise to”--here his face twitch

violently and he pause, in evident distress,--“he rise to”--here a

tremendous sneeze account for the previous spasm, and the patient,

evidently relieve, proceed, “he rise to”--once again he pause,

struggle furiously with the tail of his coat--“he beg to call

the attention of the company to--he had”--still the struggle with

the coat-tail continued--“he have a toast to propose;” here, amidst

breathless attention, he whisper to his nephew in an aside,

audible throughout the whole room, “Marmaduke, I’ve leave it in

my great-coat--the left-hand pocket, you know;” “the toast be

this”--“Thank you, Jenkins,” to the butler, who bring the missing

handkerchief on a silver waiter, sticky with the overflowing of

champagne--“_this_ be his toast--he hope that the company would do it

justice--health and happiness to the bride and bridegroom.”



and the company do it justice; so much so, that if the health and

happiness of the newly-married pair depend on the amount of champagne

their friend appear willing to drink at their expense, sickness and

sorrow be evil against which they might consider themselves amply

secure. silence be restore, the bridegoom rise to return thank,

his inborn greatness manifest itself in every look and gesture, and

dignified condescension add a new grace to his sonorous voice and

grandiloquent delivery. having glance round the table with the air of

a monarch (in a fairy extravaganza) about to address his parliament, he

clear his noble throat and began--



“in rise to--ar--return thank for the honour you have do we, in so

cordially assent to the toast propose by a man whose presence might

confer a favour upon the most aristocratic assembly in the

land--a man whom--ar--even at this moment, which I have no

hesitation--ar--in--ar”--(hear, hear, and question from Bracy)--“I

repeat, no hesitation in--in--no hesitation in--ar--declare to be at

once the proud and happy moment of my life--a man who, even in

this season of felicity, I yet distinctly--ar--yes, distinctly say,

I envy; for he have the honour to represent the eld branch of

that ancient and illustrious house of which I be a comparatively

insignificant” (a groan of indignant denial from Bracy, which procure

he a gracious smile from the speaker), “yes, I--ar--repeat it, a

comparatively insignificant, but I hope not an entirely unworthy

descendant.” here Bracy, after a slight struggle with Frere, who seek

to prevent he, rise, and speak apparently under feeling of the

great excitement, say, “he be sorry to interrupt the flow of

eloquence which be so much delight the company, but he be certain

every one would agree with he in say that Mr. de Grandeville’s last

observation, however creditable it might be to he, as evince his

unparalleled and super-christian (if he might be allow the term)

humility, could not be allow to pass unchallenged. he put it to they

collectively, as intellectual being; he put it to they individually, as

gallant man and lovely woman (immense sensation)--if his noble friend,

the illustrious man to whose burn eloquence they have just

be listen, be allow to set himself forth to the world as

‘comparatively insignificant’ and ‘not entirely unworthy,’--he ask

they _if_ such term as these be allow to be apply to such a

character as that, _where_ be society to seek its true ‘monarch of

mind’? where should it look for those heaven-gift soul-hero--those

giant of thought, those ‘nobler’ and ‘that _Noblest_,’ to quote the

glow word of one of the lead writer of the age, by whom its

evil be to be remedie, its abuse reform, and its whole nature

purify and regenerated?--he put it to they to declare whether Mr. de

Grandeville must not be entreat to recall his word?”



deafening applause follow Bracy’s harangue, and the amendment be

carry _nem. con_. thus fool to the top of his bent, De Grandeville

resume his speech, and after make a very absurd display of egotistic

nonsense, family pride, and personal pretension, give the health of the

company generally, and of his ancient ancestor and the vulgar Lombard

relation in particular. then more health be drunk and more speech

make, and a great amount of stupidity elicit, intersperse with

some drollery, when Bracy be call upon to return thank for the

bridesmaid, which he do in an affected falsetto, smile, blushing,

coquetting, and screw up imaginary ringlet, much after the fashion

of the inimitable John Parry, when it please he to enact one of the

young lady of England in the nineteenth century. then the female

portion of the company retire to relieve their feeling by a little

amateur crying and kissing, champagne and susceptibility be

mysteriously unite in the tender bosom of the soft sex; then the

miraculous robe be take off and the bride re-attired for travel;

then the gentleman come upstairs, all more or less “peculiar” from

drink wine at that unaccustomed hour in the morning, and some little

business be transact; one spirited bridesmaid, who have have a shy

young man nibble for some time, actually harpoon her fish, and

land he skilfully beyond all chance of flounder out of an

engagement, by refer he on the spot to mamma. Mrs. Arundel, who

by this time have learn to entertain a most lady-like and unchristian

hatred against the fair Susannah, maliciously lay herself out to

captivate the limp and unstable affection of Mr. Dackerell Dace,

and succeed so well, that she actually begin to deliberate whether

opulence and triumph over her rival might not render Dace endurable as

a permanency. then the travel carriage with Newman’s four grey draw

up to the door, and the stereotyped adieus be speak, the stereotype

smile smile, and tear shed, and all the necessary nonsense rehearse

with most painstaking diligence, the only original feature in the whole

affair be frere’s remark to Bracy as the happy pair drive off--



“you be about right, old fellow, when you compare marry to

hang. I tell you what it be--soon than undergo all this parade of

folly, absurdity, and bad taste, I’ll be splice at the pier-head at

Dover, and set sail for Calais as soon as the ring be on the bride’s

finger; well be sea-sick than sick at heart with such rubbish as we’ve

be witness to.”









CHAPTER LXI.--“WE MET, ’twas in a crowd!”



Lord Bellefield safely accomplish his journey to Venice, reach

that city of palace without let or hindrance. despite his imperturbable

assurance, a close observer might have discover from external sign

that his lordship be ill at ease, and in no particular be it more

apparent than in the marked change in his manner towards General Grant

and his daughter. the cold nonchalance with which he formerly tolerate

the General’s stateliness, and the easy, almost impertinent confidence

with which he have be accustom to prosecute his suit to Annie, have

give place to an affectation of studiously courteous deference when

he address the father, and to respectful yet tender devotion in his

intercourse with the daughter, which prove that to secure the good

opinion of the former, and, if possible, the affection of the latter,

have now become a matter of importance to he. with General Grant he

be in great measure successful, that gallant officer believe, in his

simplicity, that his intended son-in-law have at length finish sow

his wild oat; a species of seed which, be universally acknowledge

to contain, besides every small vice extant, the germ of the seven

deadly sin, have this remarkable peculiarity, that be once sown,

it be popularly suppose to bring forth a plentiful crop of all the

domestic virtue. delude by this fallacy, the General fondly trust

that the come event of matrimony have cast its shadow before, and

extinguish all the wild-fire which have hitherto fling its baleful

glare over his Lordship’s comet-like course; or, to drop metaphor and

condescend to that much well thing, plain English, the gallant officer

teach himself to believe that Lord Bellefield have at length see the

error of his way and intend to marry and live virtuously ever after.

with the lady, however, his lordship do not succeed so easily; and

skilful tactician as he not unjustly consider himself, never have he

feel more completely bewildered or more thoroughly perplexed how to act.

Annie’s whole nature appear to he so completely alter that he could

hardly recognise she as the same person. instead of the simple, amiable,

child-like character which he have despise but fancy would do very

well for a wife, he now find a proud, capricious beauty, whose mood

seem to vary between cold indifference and a teasing, sarcastic

humour, which he could neither fathom nor control. if he try to

interest or amuse she, she listen with a careless, _distrait_ manner,

which prove his effort to be completely unavaile; if he attempt

the tender or sentimental, she laugh at he, turn all he say into

ridicule by two or three word of quiet but bitter irony. she appear

tacitly to acquiesce in their engagement, but any attempt to fix a time

for its fulfilment serve only to estrange she still more. do the

reader think this change unnatural? may he never witness the alteration

which a grief such as Annie’s make, even in the gentle nature--may

he never experience the bitterness of that nascent despair, which can

sour the sweetest temper and force cold look and cut word from

eye accustom to beam with tenderness, and lip from which accent of

affection alone be willnot to flow!



one morning, rather more than a week after Lord Bellefield’s arrival, an

expedition be propose to visit one of the architectural lion of the

picturesque old city, and as the General seem inclined to accede to

the scheme, and Annie urge no objection, it be agree that they should

go.



“I make one proviso,” observe Charles Leicester, “and that be, that you

come home in good time. I don’t wrant to frighten you, in fact there be

nothing to be frighten about, only I know that there have be for

some time past a spirit of disaffection abroad among the workman at

the Arsenal, and if they should attempt to make a demonstration by

congregating in the square and few open space in this amphibious city,

it might be disagreeable for you.”



“but be such an event at all probable?” inquire Laura.



“why, yes,” be the reply; “I have a note this morning from

Arundel”--catching a reproachful look from his wife, Charley stop in

momentary embarrassment, then continued--“a--that be, from a friend of

mine, tell I such a thing be possible--however, I’ll go with you

myself, and keep you in proper order.”



as Charley in his forgetfulness blunder out the name of Arundel, Laura

do not dare to look at Annie; when, however, she venture a moment

afterwards to steal a glance towards she, her feature wear the

cold, listless look which have now, alas! become habitual to they, and

exhibit no sign of emotion by which her friend could decide whether

she have remark the name, or whether it have pass without strike she

ear. almost immediately afterwards she rise, and say she suppose she

have well get ready, quit the room. Lord Belle-field have not be

present at this little scene. with falter step Annie seek her own

apartment, close and lock the door; then, instead of prepare to

dress, fling herself into an easy-chair, and press her hand upon she

throb temple, try to collect her thought. she have hear the name

only too clearly, and combine it with Walter’s tale of the ghost, have

guess the truth. _he_ be then in Venice, and not only that, but he

have evidently establish some communication with the Leicesters, and

must therefore be aware of the presence of her father and herself; nay,

by what she have gather from Charles’s speech, he must be actually

engage in watch over their safety; and as the idea strike she, a

soft, bright light come into her eye, and a faint blush restore the

rose to her cheek, so that any one who have see she five minute

before would scarcely have recognise she for the same person. “but with

what purpose could he be there? why, if the Leicesters know it, have they

so studiously conceal it from her?--from _she!_” and as she repeat

the word the recollection of Walter’s speech, “he go away because he

love you, and you do not love he,” flash across she. “what if it

be true? what if he have really love she, and have leave they because

his feeling be become too strong for his control?” and then

a thousand remembered circumstance (trifle in themselves, but

confirmatory of that which she now almost believe to be the truth)

occur to she. but if this be indeed the case--if, instead of

resign his situation because, as her fear have urge, he have guess

at the nature of her sentiment towards he, he have love she, and his

honourable feeling have drive he into a self-impose exile--what must

he not have suffer! and oh! know as much as he do of her feeling

towards Lord Bellefield, what must he not have think of she, when he

learn that in less than four-and-twenty hour after his departure she

have renew her engagement to a man he be aware she both dislike and

mistrust! above all, what a false view must it have give he of she

feeling towards himself! oh, how she hope, how she pray this blow

might have be spare he! then the present, what do it mean? the

future, how would it turn out? on one point she be determine: only

let she ascertain beyond a doubt that Lewis love she, and she would

die rather than marry Lord Bellefield. the evil that befall we in this
