world be not without even their temporal benefit. two year of hopeless

sorrow have give a specie of desperate courage to a mind naturally

prone to a want of self-dependence. _anything_ be preferable to the

anguish she have go through; and Annie Grant’s decision now be very

different to the “lady’s yea” or nay she would have utter ere the

storm of passion have sweep over her maiden spirit.



the effect produce on Annie by the new light which have break in upon

she do not immediately pass away, and although her remark be chiefly

address to her cousin Charles, Lord Bellefield be equally surprised

and puzzle by the change in her manner. in order to reach the building

they be about to visit, they be force to disembark from their

gondola, and after proceed along a species of cloister, to cross one

of the foot-bridge which so constantly in Venice intersect the canal.

under the shade of an arch of this cloister stand the tall figure of a

man; as the party approach he draw back far into the shadow, and,

himself unseen, observe they attentively as they pass. the excitement

of the morning have leave its trace in the flushed cheek and sparkle

eye of Annie Grant. at the moment she quit the boat, Charley

Leicester have make her laugh by some quaint remark on the personal

appearance of a fat little individual who be one of the gondolier, but

whose figure by no mean coincide with the romantic association his

avocation recall. as, lean on Lord Bellefield’s arm, she pass the

arch behind which the stranger be conceal, her companion address to

she some observation which necessitate a reply. turn to he with

the smile Leicester’s observation have provoke still upon her lip, the

light fall strongly on her feature, reveal they fully to the eager

gaze of (for we intend no mystification as to his identity) Lewis

Arundel. he look after they with strain eyeball, till a corner of

the building hide they from his view. then dark line spread across his

forehead, the proud nostril arch, the stern mouth set, the flashing

eye grow cold and stony, and a spirit of evil seem to take possession

of he.



“so,” he mutter, “it have come to this; with my own eye have I beheld

her perfidy. it be well that it should be so, the cure will be the more

complete, and yet”--he press his hand to his throb brow--“yet

how beautiful she be! she be change; her face have acquire expression,

soul, power, all it want to render it perfect, and--to madden I.”



he pause, then appear to have collect strength, continue more

calmly, “yes, I have see it; she cling to his arm, she smile on he,

she love and will marry he. it be over; for I there must be no past;

I must sweep it from my memory. happiness I can never know; as far as

the affection be concern, the game of life be play. well, be it

so, my art still be leave I, and the dark, the unknown future.”



again he pause. Ere the arrival of the party, the sight of which have so

deeply affect he, he have be sketch an antique gable opposite. he

resume his work, and by a few hasty but graphic stroke transfer

to his sketch-book the object which have attract he to the spot.

replace his drawing material, he continue, “ ’tis strange how the

sight of that man affect I: I fancy I have teach myself the evil

and folly of nourishing sentiment of hatred against he, and yet the

moment I behold he, all the old feeling rush back upon I with

redouble vigour. I must avoid his presence, or my wise resolution will

go for nothing.” he sigh deeply. “this, then, be all the fruit of two

year of mental discipline, to find, at the end of the time, that I love

she as deeply and hate he as bitterly as I do at the beginning. oh, it

be humiliating thus to be the slave of passion!”



commune with himself after this fashion, Lewis quit the spot and

proceed in the direction of his own lodging. on reach the square

of St. Mark he find it partially occupy by an excited crowd, compose

of the very low order of the people, its number be constantly

swell by fresh party pour in from various part of the city. it

instantly occur to Lewis that in order to reach the Palazzo Grassini,

Leicester and his companion would be force to cross the square, and

consequently oblige to make their way through the crowd; and a feeling

which he do not attempt to analyse, but which be, in truth, anxiety

for Annie’s safety, determine he to remain there till he have see they

return. accordingly, turn up his coat collar, and slouch his hat

over his eye in order to conceal his feature, he mingle with the

crowd. in the meantime the Grant party, ignorant of the difficulty

that await they, be quietly examine statue and criticise

picture.



“Laura, you look tired, and Annie seem as if she be become somewhat

‘use-up,’” observe Leicester, glance from his wife towards his

cousin. “no wonder either, for we’ve be on our foot for more than two

hour, and as for my share in the matter, I tell you plainly, if you

keep I here much long, you’ll have to carry I home on your back,

Mrs. Leicester, for walk I willn’t.”



thus urge the lady confess their fatigue, and their willingness to

return; but there be still another gallery of painting unseen, which

the General evidently wish to visit. he have commission an artist to

copy two or three of they, and he require Lord Bellefield’s opinion

as to the propriety of his choice. this occasion a difficulty, which

Laura meet by propose the follow scheme--viz., that she, Annie,

and Charley should leave the General and Lord Bellefield to their own

device, and take a gondola, row to a point at which they would be

within two minute’ walk of St. Mark’s. Lord Bellefield make some slight

remonstrance, and it be clear he disapprove of the scheme, but the

General be peremptory, so he have no resource but to submit with the

good grace he be able.



“famous thing gondola be, to be sure,” observe Charley, as, place

a cushion beneath his head, he stretch himself at full length under

the awning; “they afford almost the only instance that have come under

my notice in which the intensely romantic and the very decidedly

comfortable go hand in hand--they cut out cab, and beat ’bus

into fit. now, we only want a little melody to make the thing

perfect--Laura, sing we a song!”



“sing you asleep, you mean, you incorrigible----”



“there, that will do; don’t become vituperative, you termagant,”

 interrupt her husband. “Annie, dear, gentle cousin Annie, warble forth

something romantic with your angel-voice, do, and I’ll say you’re----”



“what?” inquire Annie.



“a regular stunner!” be the reply.



“and if the epithet be at all appropriate, it clearly prove I

unqualified for the office,” return Annie, smile, “so you really

must hold I excuse.”



“then the long and short of the matter be that the duty devolve upon

I,” rejoin Charley, and slowly raise himself into a half sit,

half kneeling attitude, he place himself at his wife’s foot, after the

fashion of those very interesting cavalier who do the romantic on the

cover of sentimental song; then having play an inaudible prelude

upon a supposititious guitar, he place one hand upon his heart, and

extend the other in a theatrical attitude towards the boatman,

begun--



                        “Gondolier, row--o!”



when, having extemporarily parody the first verse of that popular

melody, he be begin the second with--



                   “ain’t this here go--



                   glorious--oh--o-”



when the prow of the gondola strike against the step where they be to

land with so sharp a jerk as to pitch the singer on his hand and knee,

and effectually check his vocalising. after discharge the boatman,

they proceed a short distance along the bank of the canal, and then

turn down a narrow lane, or alley, lead to the square of St. Mark.

in this Leicester be annoy to perceive knot of disreputable-look

man talk rapidly, or hurry along with eager gesture towards

the square. finding, as they advance, that the crowd become thick,

Leicester pause, irresolute whether or not to proceed.



“surely we have well turn back,” urge Laura. “I should not be afraid

if we be alone, for I know you could take care of I, but----,” and

she glance towards Annie, who, although she say nothing, have turn

very pale, and clung with convulsive energy to her cousin’s arm. Charles

look back, and to his utter dismay perceive that the crowd behind have

be increase by a fresh accession of number, and that their retreat

be effectually cut off.



“there be nothing remain for we but to keep on,” he say; “the stream

of people appear, fortunately, to be go our way, and all we can do

be to go with it: I dare say they be too much engross by their own

affair to trouble their head about we. whatever occur, don’t let go

my arm, either of you; it be rather disagreeable, certainly, but there

be nothing to be really afraid of, and we shall reach home in five

minute.”



hope these assertion, in regard to the truth of which he be himself

somewhat sceptical, might suffice to reassure his companion, Leicester

continue his course, occasionally annoy by the pressure of the crowd,

but not otherwise molest till they reach the square of Saint Mark.

here the sight that await they be by no means encouraging: the whole

space be fill with a dense crowd of the low rabble of Venice, who,

many of they the bad for liquor, appear in a state of considerable

excitement, and fill the air with mingled shout, cry, and curse.

to pass safely through such an assembly, with his attention divide

between his two charge, appear next to impossible, and thoroughly

perplexed, Charles Leicester pause, unable to decide whether it be

well to advance or attempt to retrace their step. as he thus

ponder a rush of people force they forward, and they find themselves

completely hem in by the crowd, while from the pressure of those

around they Laura and Annie experience the great difficulty in

retain their grasp of Charley’s arm. still no personal incivility

be offer they, and Leicester begin to hope they might gradually make

their way across the square without actual danger, when a cry from Annie

convince he of his error. the cause of her alarm be as follows:--



one of that industrious fraternity (some member of which be to be meet

with in every large city) whose principle in regard to the right of

property be reprehensibly lax, attract by the sparkling of a valuable

brooch in Annie’s shawl, conceive the opportunity too good to be lose;

accordingly, press close to she, he make a snatch at the ornament,

seize it so rudely as to tear open the shawl and partially drag it

from her shoulder. as, alarm by her cry, Charles turn to discover

its cause, a tall figure spring forward and wrest his spoil from

the robber, fling he off at the same time with such force that he

stagger and fall; then address Leicester, the stranger say in a

deep, stern voice, each accent of which thrill through Annie’s very

soul--



“make for the church step--think only of protect Mrs. Leicester. I

will be answerable for this lady’s safety.”



then Annie be conscious that her shawl be replace and carefully

wrap round she, and she feel herself half-lead, half-carry forward

by one before whose resistless strength all obstacle seem, as it

be, to melt away. how they pass through that yelling, madden crowd

she never know, but ere she have well recover from her first alarm

at the ruffian’s attack, she find herself place on the step of St.

Mark’s Church, her back lean against a column, and the tall, dark

figure of her preserver stand statue-like beside she, in such a

position as to screen she from the pressure of the crowd. involuntarily

she glance up at his feature; hide by the coat collar and slouch

hat, the only portion of his face that remain visible be the tip of a

black moustache, the proud, arch nostril, and the cold, stony gaze of

two fierce black eye, fix upon she as though they would pierce she

very soul. it be a look to haunt she to her die day, and bad than

all, _she understand it!_ in a moment the idea flash upon she. he

_have_ love she! he know she be about to marry his bitter enemy, and

now he _hate_ she. Poor Annie, if mental agony could kill, that instant

she have die. Lewis, thou art bitterly avenge!









chapter LXII.--“POINTS a MORAL,” and so it be to be HOPED “ADORNS A

TALE.”



“what be the next move?” inquire Leicester, come up with his arm

round his wife’s waist, and his hat crush into the shape of a biffin.



“wait here for a few minute,” return Lewis, “the crowd be already

disperse in the direction of the Arsenal.”



“the Arsenal, what do they want there?” inquire Leicester.



“to waylay Marinovitch as he leave the place, and murder he,” return

Lewis in a stern whisper, “but he have be warn of their design, and

will of course take measure to ensure his safety.”



“pleasant all this!” mutter Leicester, take off his injure hat and

endeavour in some degree to restore its original shape; “here’s a

case of wanton destruction--glad it be not my head all the same. now the

coast seem pretty clear, suppose we move on.”



coldly and silently Lewis resume his office of guardian: the space

intervene between St. Mark’s Church and the Palazzo Grassini be

pass in safety, and they stand within the courtyard of Leicester’s

dwelling. Charley lay his hand on Lewis’s shoulder.



“you will come in?” he say; “you be hot and tired, and require

refreshment--a glass of wine?”



Lewis shake his head.



“it be impossible,” he reply coldly; then add, “I be happy to

have be of use to--to Mrs. Leicester and yourself,” he raise his hat

slightly to Annie and turn to depart: recollect however that

he still hold in his hand the brooch which he have rescue from the

ruffian’s clutch, he pause with the intention of give it to Laura;

but Laura have catch sight of “Tarley’s” curly head peep out at she,

and actuate by a sudden impulse of maternal affection, or for some

other reason which we shall not attempt to fathom, she have trip off

in the direction of her self-willed offspring. Leicester be slowly

follow she, all his faculty apparently engross by a second

attempt to reform his outraged hat. Lewis and Annie be leave therefore

virtually alone. advance towards she with an expression of countenance

so cold and immovable that every feature might have be carve in

marble, Lewis began--



“I beg pardon, I have forget to return your brooch.”



it be the first time that morning he have personally address she, and

his do so appear to break the spell which have keep she silent; she

take the brooch from he, murmur some indistinct word of thank,

then gain courage as she proceed, she glance at he appealingly,

saying--



“strange as this meeting be, I be sure I cannot be mistake--Mr.

Arundel, have you quite forget I?”



as she utter these word a kind of spasm pass across Lewis’s

face, and for a moment he appear afraid to trust himself to speak;

recover, however, he reply in the same cold, measure tone which he

have use throughout the adventure--



“no, Miss Grant, _i_ (and he lay an emphasis on the pronoun, so light

that a casual observer would not have detect it, and yet which shoot

a pang through Annie’s heart that cause her colour to come and go, and

her limb to tremble) do not forget so quickly.”



unable to meet his glance, which she feel be fix upon she, and

scarcely conscious, in her agitation, of what she be say, Annie

falter out--



“you will give my father an opportunity of thank you, I hope;

he will, I cannot doubt--that is, we shall all be glad to renew our

intimacy with so old a friend.”



Lewis pause ere he could trust himself to reply. her evident emotion,

the earnestness of her manner, half timid, half implore, tend to

soothe his wound spirit and disarm his wrath; but the vision of the

morning, in which he have see she cling to Lord Bellefield’s arm and

smile upon he, be too fresh in his recollection, and the demon of

pride and jealousy still retain full dominion over he.



“you must pardon I,” he say, “I will reserve my visit to General Grant

till I can congratulate he on his daughter’s marriage.” then raise

his hat ceremoniously, he bow to she, and be go!



no trace of the tumultuous assembly, which have so greatly alarmed Laura

and Annie, remain when Lord Bellefield and General Grant cross the

square of St. Mark on their return from the morning’s sight-seeing.

as they draw near the Palazzo Grassini, a tall lad in squalid raiment,

lean upon crutch, and with a patch over one eye, approach and

beg of they. the General at first refuse to listen to he, but

becoming weary by his pertinacity, feel in his pocket for something to

give he.



“I have no small change about I,” he remark, after a minute’s

ineffectual search, “but you have, Bellefield; they give you a handful

of their stupid little coin at the last shop we go into. lend I two

or three, will you?”



as he mention his companion’s name the beggar fix his pierce

eye on the feature of the person address, scan they eagerly,

as though he seek to fix they indelibly in his memory. return his

glance with a haughty stare, his lordship carelessly fling he a couple

of _zwanziger_ and pass on. the beggar watch his retreating figure

till it be no long visible, then turn quickly, hobble with his

crutch out of the square, continue the same method of progression

till he reach the near canal, when, look round to assure himself

that he be not observe, he coolly pitch his supporter into the

water, remove the patch from his eye, which by no mean seem to

require such a protection, walk briskly till he reach a spot where

a small skiff be moor, spring into which he commence rowing

vigorously, and be soon hide from sight by a bend of the canal.



when Lewis return to his lodging the following note await him:--



“my search be end; I have find my sister in time to see she--die!

her seducer, heartless in his villainy, bring his victim to a foreign

land, keep she in luxury till his fancy weary of she, and then leave

she--to starve. my curse have little power, or it would have wither he

long ago; but may the curse of that God who make he and her cleave to

he until-- _I_meet he. Sir, I know not how to thank you. she have tell

I how you warn she, how you explain to she his real character.

she be infatuate, but it be not for I to judge she. we seem a doom

race, fatal alike to ourselves, to those we love, and _to those we

hate_. oh, that she could live! she be soft and gentle, and--ay! though

a scoundrel have debase she, still I say it--she be good and pure, and

she would have calm my angry spirit; she would have teach I to love

something human. but it be not to be: each hour that I sit by she I

expect to be she last. she send you her blessing; may God’s go with it.



“Miles H---------.”



Lewis could not peruse this letter without deep emotion. in the just,

though, alas! ill-govern indignation which give a rude eloquence even

to the expression of this poor youth’s outraged feeling, he trace a

likeness to his former self. “heartless in his villainy, he keep she in

luxury till his fancy weary of she, and then leave she--to starve.”



this be the man Annie Grant love and be about to marry! oh, how his

heart bleed for she! he picture to himself her future life--how she

would gradually, by slow and painful step, discover her husband’s true

character, each advance in knowledge a new and separate misfortune,

until love should become indifference, and indifference end in hatred.

even yet he might prevent it. his London agent have forward to he that

morning an english newspaper contain an unmistakable allusion to

the event of the Derby day, and openly declare Lord Bellefield a

defaulter. this show to General Grant, and his tale of Hardy’s daughter

verify by the evidence now in his possession, the old soldier would

soon see his daughter lie dead at his foot than sanction her union

with a man devoid alike of honour and of principle. but then come in

pride. have he know that Annie love he, or have General Grant never

mistrust he, Lewis would have come forward without a moment’s

hesitation; but his motive have be once doubt, his affection

betray, and his pride could neither forget nor forgive it. besides,

Lord Bellefield would attribute his interference to a feeling of petty

malice; such be not the revenge for which, despite his principle and

his reason, his soul still thirst. so pride gain the day, though,

tyrant-like, in the very midst of his triumph he make his victim

miserable.



unable to apply his mind to anything, he stroll out, trust the

evening air would allay the fever of his blood. after wander about

restlessly for some time he remember that he have eat nothing for

many hour, and turn into the near casino he call for wine and

biscuit. having finish his frugal repast, he be about to leave

the house when three person enter, and cross through the

refreshment-room, pass into a salon which he know to be devote

to play. one of the three, a short, insignificant-look man, be a

stranger to he, but the two other he recognise instantly--they be

Walter and Lord Bellefield. a sudden impulse prompt he to follow

they; at that time in the evening the salon be certain to be pretty

well fill, and Lewis trust to avoid observation by mix with

the crowd, rely on the alteration in his appearance to escape

recognition, even if he be perceive either by Walter or Lord

Bellefield. accordingly, wait his opportunity, he join a group of

Italians, who, eagerly talk over the attempt upon the life of Colonel

Marinovitch (which have be frustrate by his escaping on board the

corvette which guard the harbour), scarcely perceive this addition to

their party. enter with they, and still keep in the background, he

take up a position whence he could observe the proceeding of those

for both of whom he feel an interest equally deep, though so utterly

distinct in character.



Lord Bellefield, who appear unusually listless and indifferent,

lounge up to the table and stake a few napoleon on the chance of

the game; then, draw forward a chair he seat himself, and continue

carelessly to watch the proceeding of the other player. but despite

the presence of the man he hate, Lewis’s attention soon become wholly

absorb in observe Walter. from his entire conduct it be evident

that this be by no mean his first visit to the salon; on the contrary,

it be only too plain that a taste for gambling have be implant in

the poor boy’s feeble yet obstinate mind. that he clearly understand

the nature of the game Lewis could not believe, but that he have acquire

sufficient insight into the rule to enable he to adhere to they,

and that he be keenly alive to the result of the deal, or the throw,

elated when he win and depress when he lose, be most certain.



the third person of the party, whom Lewis rightly conjecture to be his

successor in the office of tutor, do not play himself, but appear

to take great interest in Walter’s game, look over his card and

advise he what to do. Lewis also notice that whenever Walter win he

always receive gold, but that his loss be pay in paper money, and

the truth immediately occur to he--viz., that, child-like, the poor

boy only attach value to the glitter coin, and that the worth of

the bank-note have be completely misrepresent to he, so that he

believe himself win when in fact he be lose considerable sum.

moreover, from certain glance which pass between Mr. Spooner and the

proprietor of the salon, who hold the bank, Lewis become convince

that some secret understanding exist by which the tutor share in the

profit.



that Lord Bellefield be entirely ignorant of all that be pass

before his eye Lewis could not conceive, while at the same time the

trifle nature of the stake render it most unlikely that he could

have any personal interest in the affair; the probability therefore be

that he see what be go on but feel totally indifferent as to the

matter. this view be confirm when, as Walter grow more excited, begin

play high, and at last stake ten napoleon upon one cast, Mr.

Spooner approach Lord Bellefield and whisper something in his ear,

to which his friend reply carelessly, “oh, let he have his fling

while he’s in the humour;” then in a low tone he add, “_not blind!_

but the money be, I dare say, of more use in your pocket than in his, so

you’ll be the great fool of the two if you attempt to prevent he.”



Spooner appear again to urge some difficulty, to which Lord Bellefield

rejoin with a sneer laugh, “yes, it suit _you_ charmingly to

assume the _rôle_ of the innocent! cann’t you get he to sign another

bond payable when he come of age? Tortoni will no more refuse to cash

it than he do on a former occasion;” then smile again, he add, “I

tell you I be not blind, _mon ami_, but ’tis no concern of mine; I be

not the lout’s guardian, Heaven be thank.” although from the position

in which he stand Lewis only catch a word or two here and there of

this conversation, yet his quick apprehension supply the blank with

sufficient correctness, and the whole villainy of the thing burst

upon he. here be a man engage to educate and watch over the poor,

feeble-minded being before he abuse the power thus entrust to he

to lead he to evil, and avail himself of the imbecility he be bind

to protect to swindle his helpless charge; while Lord Bellefield, whose

duty it be to denounce such practice to General Grant the instant he

suspect they, have evidently not only no intention of do so, but sat

coolly look on, smile with a fiend-like satisfaction at each fresh

development of human wickedness.



as Lewis watch Walter’s flush cheek, eager eye, and hand which

tremble as they be stretch out to receive the gold which this time

he have be allow to win; as he mark the line which excitement and

the permit indulgence of a capricious, obstinate temper have trace

upon his smooth brow and round the corner of his mouth, all his old

affection for the poor boy rush back upon he, and his just anger

grow to such a pitch that he could scarcely repress it. at this moment a

fresh deal have begin.



“I will win more,” exclaim Walter eagerly; “Mr. Spooner, tell he I

want to double my stake.”



“but that have be do already,” be the reply; “the dealer have double

every one’s stake this time.”



“then I will double that,” return Walter, carry away by the

excitement of the game; “tell he so, I say.”



Spooner appear for a moment undecided; the stake, thus quadruple,

amount to forty napoleon, and alarm at its magnitude, he glance in

irresolution towards Lord Bellefield. a look of undisguised contempt for

his pusillanimity be the only reply his lordship vouchsafe; goad on

by which, Spooner turn to comply with his pupil’s direction.



but Lewis could bear it no long; regardless of consequence, he stride

across the room and lay his hand upon Walter’s shoulder, say as he

do so, in a gentle though determine voice, “Walter, you must not play

for such high stake.”



with a cry of mingled joy and surprise Walter spring from his seat,

gaze earnestly at Lewis’s feature, then exclaim, “oh, you have come

back at last!” throw himself upon his friend’s breast with a burst

of tear. much affect, Lewis return his embrace, and lead he

carefully to a seat, wait till he should recover from his surprise

and emotion. in the meantime the game have come to a standstill, the

bystander, consist chiefly of foreigner, be as much charm by

such a scene as an Englishman would have be annoy at it. the moment

quiet be in some degree restore, the proprietor, mindful of his own

interest, resume his deal, inquire with a glance at Spooner what sum

his young friend have stake. Spooner pause, but Lord Bellefield,

who have rise and with lower brow approach the scene of action,

prompt he, and he reply, “Forty Napoleons.”



Lewis’s eye flash. “it be at your peril you do this,” he say; “my

first act on quit this place shall be to inform General Grant of the

manner in which you betray the trust he have repose in you.”



Spooner turn pale; but rely on Lord Bellefield’s support, manage

to stammer out, “and pray, sir, who the deuce may you be?”



“I will tell you, and this worshipful company also,” exclaim Lord

Bellefield, step forward. “this fellow be, or rather be, a menial

in General Grant’s household, discard for insolent behaviour, and

as such unfit for the society of gentleman, into which as he have now

venture to intrude himself, I, for one, vote he be ignominiously

expel.”



this speech cause, as might be expect, a sensation throughout the

room, and the bystander congregate round Lewis and Lord Bellefield,

glance from one to the other, to discover from their bearing and

appearance which be the true man, and which the false. up to this

moment Lewis have be wrap in a large spanish cloak; he now allow

it to glide from his shoulder, as, advance a step, he boldly

confront his adversary.



“your lordship have be pleased to speak explicitly,” he say; “be

I incline to follow your example, I might, with some shadow of truth,

denounce you as a ruin blackleg and an outlaw defaulter; but I

prefer simply declare that in the statement you have just make you

have maliciously and unequivocally--_lied!_”



as he speak he raise his head proudly, and fold his arm across

his breast, wait the effect of his word. he be not keep long in

suspense. however numerous might be Lord Bellefield’s fault, a want of

personal courage be not one of they. as Lewis refer to the cause of

his ignominious exile his face grow pale with rage, but when he give he

the lie his fury become uncontrollable. spring forward with a leap

like that of a maddened tiger, he strike Lewis a violent blow on the

cheek, which, firmly as his foot be plant, stagger he, exclaim

as he do so--



“take that, beggar!” instead of rush on his adversary, as those

amongst the spectator who know he (and there be several who do so)

expect, Lewis, recover himself, stand for an instant regard Lord

Bellefield with a smile of triumph, though to those who remark

he closely there be an expression in his eye which, in spite of

themselves, cause they to shudder, while, strange to say, he be

draw a soil _white kid glove_ on his right hand; having do so, he

advance a step, say in a stern, deep voice--



“your Lordship be too generous--the beggar return your

almsgiving--thus!”



as he speak there be a sudden movement in the crowd--a frightful blow

be strike, and Lord Bellefield lie insensible on the ground, the blood

flow from a cut on his forehead, whilst over he stand Lewis, his

mouth set, and his eye burn with the fire of hatred. several of the

bystander spring forward to assist the fallen man, but Lewis sternly

motion they back.



“wait,” he said,--his voice sound deep and hollow, and there be

something in the expression of his face which quell the stout heart

amongst those who stand around him,--drawing the glove from the hand

which have strike the blow, he dip it in the blood that still trickle

from the forehead of the fall man, mutter to himself as he do

so, “_that_, then, have come to pass--be _the rest_ to follow?” he next

examine the countenance of his prostrate foe--“He be merely stun,”

 he say; “raise he, and bring water to bathe his temple.” as he speak

he assist those who step forward to lift the injured man and

place he on a chair; having do so, he leave he to the care of the

bystander, and again fold his arm, stand coolly await the issue.



the event justify his prediction: on the first application of the

cold water Lord Bellefield revive, and in less time than could have

be expect, the bleeding, which be very slight, be arrest. as

soon as he have recover sufficiently to speak, he say, address a

young Italian of rank, with whom he be acquaint, and who have be

bathe his temple with the cold water--



“Rastelli, you may inform that scoundrel that he have succeed; rather

than allow he to escape with impunity, I will undergo the degradation

of meet he.” he speak in a low, faint voice, but the expression with

which he glance towards Lewis as he pronounce the word “scoundrel” be

one of undye hatred.



“if your Lordship intend to apply that observation to the Signore

Luigi, I shall have the felicity to explain that your Excellency labour

under a mistake; that gentleman be the son of a gallant officer, with

whom I have have the honour to serve in more than one campaign. it be no

condescension in any one under the rank of a Royal Prince to meet the

son of the brave Captain Arundel.”



the speaker be an old General Officer in the austrian service, who

possess a european reputation, and whose dictum on all point of

honour be conclusive. Lord Bellefield bite his under-lip in anger and

vexation, curse his own hastiness which have elicit this vindication

of his enemy: perceive, however, that he should only place himself

still more completely in the wrong by any attempt to impugn the old

Austrian’s statement, he merely bow haughtily in reply, then desire

to be show into a private room, he take Rastelli’s arm and quit the

salon.



Lewis stand gaze after his late opponent with a dark and troubled

countenance; it be not remorse that he experience, for be the

deed to have be do over again, he would not have shrink from its

performance; and yet the feeling which engross he partook of a

remorseful character--it seem to he as though he have now lose all

power of free will--he have take the first step, and _the rest_ must

follow; there be no long any possibility of turn back. like

one walk in his sleep, he permit himself to be lead into another

room--he hear, as in a dream, Rastelli enter and make arrangement with

a young austrian officer who have volunteer to act as his second for

his meeting Lord Bellefield at daybreak. as the person challenge, he

have the choice of weapon, but he waive his right, and allow his

opponent to select pistol. Ehrenburg (his second) whisper to he

that Lord Bellefield be report to be a dead shot, but an indifferent

swordsman.



“the more reason to allow he to choose pistol,” be Lewis’s careless

reply.



Ehrenburg still urge the madness of throw away a chance. “it will

be no boy’s play,” he say; “mark my word, Luigi, this duel will be one

for life or death.”



“do you think I do not know it?” return Lewis sternly, “ay, as well

as if I now see he lie dead before my foot,” and as he speak an

involuntary shudder pass through his powerful frame.



“may not another contingency be possible, _buon’ amico?_ especially if

you allow he to secure the advantage of pistol?” suggest Ehrenburg.



“would to Heaven it might so occur,” be Lewis’s eager reply; “I hope

no well fate than to die by his hand, believe I; but it will not

be so--I know--I feel it? Ehrenburg, that man have stand like some evil

spirit across my path; time after time he have heap insult upon I;

once, coward-like, the assassin seek my life; but till tonight I have

never oppose he. why? because it be write here” (and he touch

his forehead) “that when the final struggle shall come, my destiny be

strong than his, and he must perish. you may smile and deem my

word the mere raving of superstition, but you will see, _if_ we meet

to-morrow morning, Bellefield will never leave the ground alive, and I

shall quit it with the brand of Cain upon my brow.”



he speak so gravely and with such an evident belief in the reality of

his conviction, that for a moment Ehrenburg himself feel impressed. but

a duel be no very uncommon event with the young Austrian: he have be

principal on two occasion, when no serious result have follow, and

second on half-a-dozen more; besides, he be essentially a practical

man. so he merely shrug his shoulder, hint that Lewis’s nerve

might be excite, which would produce these little fancy, advise he

to take a cup of coffee, and then repair to the shooting-gallery and

practise steadily for an hour or so to get his hand in, promise to

be with he in good time on the follow morning, inquire whether

he could be of any further assistance, and then stroll back to the

gaming-table, relieve Lewis of his presence.



to gain his lodging and lock himself into his studio be scarcely the

work of five minute; then fling himself upon the first seat that

come in his way, he give himself up to bitter thought. two year ago he

have flee his country, have quit all who be dear to he, because his

fiery passion be beyond his control--because he have love too deeply

and hate too bitterly. he have plunge into a life of wild adventure to

dissipate his feeling; he have school his heart in solitude; he have

devote all his energy to the acquirement of an art; nay, he have

devote the first effort of the skill he have thus gain to embody a

visible representation of the danger of ill-bestow love and the curse

of gratified revenge; and _this_ be the result!



he remain for a few minute with his head rest on his hand,

apparently stun by his conflict feeling; then rouse himself by

an effort, he heave a deep sigh and draw out _the glove_. as his eye

fall upon the stain of blood, he shudder, and hastily put it from

he, begin pace up and down the apartment. an antique lamp hang by a

chain from the ceiling, throw its light strongly on the two picture

from the “Giaour.” Involuntarily Lewis pause before they, and remained

gaze from one to the other with an expression of remorse and horror.

“be I indeed about to realise these creation of my gloomy fancy?” he

murmur; “shall I become that human tiger, that stony, soulless image

of impenitent despair! revenge, how I have thirst for it, how, when

writhe under that man’s insult, I have picture to myself the day

of reckoning, and deem life itself would be a cheap sacrifice for one

hour of unlimited vengeance; and now, when this coveted boon lie within

my grasp, I see it in its true light, and own this wish-for blessing

to be a dark, consume curse. see through the distorted medium of

outraged feeling, retribution appear an act of justice. the demon wear

an angel’s form. but view in its true aspect, the sentiment be

that which lead to murder, and the deed, with its sickening detail,

revolting butchery. yet, see this clearly, know to what it will

lead, I _must_ go on: I owe _he_ satisfaction. satisfaction!” and he

smile at the mocking term. “yes,” he resume, “I _must_ go on, even if

I wish to turn back. if I _could_ forego my revenge and forgive he,

it be now too late. well, be it so; ’tis weakness to repine at the

inevitable. I will meet my fate boldly, be it what it may; and for he,

he have bring the punishment upon his own head, and must abide the

issue!” he resume his walk up and down the apartment; then a new

idea strike he. “what a strange expression her feature wear when she

venture to address I,” he say; “and in the crowd she do not

shrink from I, but trust herself to I with a gentle, child-like

confidence.” he pause, press his hand to his forehead, then

exclaim, “o God, if I have wrong she--if”--and here his voice sink

almost to a whisper--“if, Heaven help I, she should have love I after

all!”



completely overwrought by these conflict emotion, Lewis sink into

a chair, and bury his face in his hand, struggle in vain for

composure, a deep-draw, choke sob from time to time attest his

mental agony. how long he remain in this position he never know. it

might have be minute, for he take no note of thing external; it

might have be hour, for a lifetime of heartfelt desolation appear

crowded into that dark reverie. he be arouse at length by a tap at

the door, which, as at first he could scarcely collect his idea

sufficiently to attend to such sublunary matter, soon grow into a

loud and impatient knock with the handle of a stick or _umbrella_.

imagine it to be one of his artist friend, come probably to bring he

information in regard to the late disturbance, he reply in Italian

that he be particularly engage and could not see any one.



“polite and encouraging, certainly,” mutter a deep-toned voice, at

the first sound of which Lewis spring from his seat and listen with

an eager yet half incredulous expression of countenance. “a thousand and

one pardon, Signor,” continue the person on the outside, speak

in Italian, with a peculiarity of accent which prove he to be

unaccustomed to pronounce the language, or probably even to hear it

speak; “but you really must condescend to see I, even if Diabolus

himself be sup with you, and there be only macaroni enough for two.”



without a moment’s hesitation Lewis fling open the door, and there _in

proprio persono_ stand Richard Frere _and_ the cotton umbrella!



“frere, dear old fellow! be it, can it indeed be you?” exclaim Lewis

joyfully, forget for the moment everything in the surprise of

welcome such an unexpected visitant.



“yes, it’s I,” return frere, squeeze and shake his friend’s hand

as if he have a design of reduce it to a jelly. “Richard’s himself, and

no mistake. Lewis ben’t _him_self, though, it seem, but Signore

Luigi, forsooth. I have hard work to find you, I can tell you. but good

gracious! what have happen to the man?” he exclaim, catch sight

of Lewis’s bearded face and pale, haggard feature, “why, he have turn

into somebody else, bodily as well as in name. you look just like one

of these horrid italian fellow, with the proper tragic expression of

countenance which they get up by way of advertising that they be ready

and willing to cut throat at half-a-crown a windpipe, country

order punctually execute, and the business perform in a neat and

tradesman-like manner; but tell I seriously, you’re not ill?”



“not in body, nor usually in mind either,” be the reply; “but today

event have occur which have thoroughly unmanned I, still I shall

‘win through it,’ somehow; and now tell I of yourself, of Rose, of my

mother--they be well?”



“a good deal well than you seem to be,” growl Frere, who during this

speech have be attentively observe his friend’s feature; “however,

I’ll soon satisfy your curiosity--and then you shall satisfy mine,” he

add in an undertone, and remove a wonderful specie of travel

cap, he follow Lewis, who lead the way to his inner apartment, and

then listen eagerly to Frere’s account of the various event which

have take place since he have quit his native land. Rose, by Frere’s

special desire, have, in write to her brother, hitherto forbear to

allude to her engagement; the worthy bear, with a characteristic mixture

of deep-seat humility and surface vanity, fear that Lewis might not

think he a fitting match for his sister, and therefore feel anxious

that the matter should be disclose to he in the wise and most

judicious manner possible--by himself _viva voce_. thus, after having

speak of various less important matter, Frere be gradually work

his way towards the interesting disclosure with a degree of nervous

diffidence quite unusual to he, when Lewis, whose attention begin to

flag, bring he to the point by exclaim, “and about Rose, what

be she do: she tell I in her letter she still write for some

magazine; but be she look well? do she seem happy? though I

suppose,” he continue, try to hide his state of mind by fall in

with his friend’s jest mode of speech, “these be minor particular

into which it never occur to your wisdom to inquire. I know your old

habit of practically ignore the existence of woman as a sex, regard

they as a race of unscientific nonentity fit only ‘to suckle fool

and chronicle small beer.’”



Frere for a moment look rather disconcert; then veil his

discomfiture under an affectation of rough indifference, he reply,

“I can tell you one ‘minor particular,’ as you call it, and that be the

fact of the young lady in question be engage to be marry.”



“nonsense!” exclaim Lewis, start, “you be probably joke,” he

continue seriously; “but you know not, dear old friend, how deeply

such tiding might affect I at this moment; you know not--how should

you?--the mood of mind in which you find I; but tell I in a word, be

there any earnest in what you have say?”



“in a word,” mutter Frere, “hum! concise and epigrammatical that! but

I’ll try to accommodate you, so here go by way of answer. yes!”



“and she have never even hint at such a fact in her letter,” exclaim

Lewis; “out of sight, out of mind, indeed. I may have--Heaven help

me!--I _have_ neglect my trust, in my self-engrossment; but I do

not think Rose would have be the person thus to visit my sin upon my

head. who be the man?” he continue sternly. in the whole course of

his existence Frere have never feel more uncomfortable; all his old

diffidence and humility rush upon he, and for the moment he feel as

if he have be suddenly detect in an act of petty larceny; however,

his sturdiness of nature and common sense come to his rescue, and he

reply, “it be no fault of Rose’s, for _i_ make it an especial point

that she should not tell you of her engagement by letter.”



“_you_ do, and wherefore?” inquire Lewis in surprise.



“because I choose to tell you myself,” return Frere. “your sister be

not an angel, for angel live in heaven and not on earth, but she be the

most lovable, the most pure-minded, decidedly the sweetest-look woman

(though that do not so much signify) in this world, and I should have

add, the most sensible, only that she have, in her tenderness of heart,

see fit to promise to marry a rough, uncouth animal like I. Lewis, old

fellow,” he continue in a falter voice, “I know well than you can

do how unworthy I be of such a blessing, but if love she well than

my own life give I any title to possess she, Heaven know that I do

so.”



when Frere reach that point in his peroration at which he mention

Rose’s promise to marry he, his auditor start, and raise his eye,

murmur an ejaculation of fervent thankfulness. as he conclude, Lewis

clasp his hand eagerly in his own, say, “my dear old Frere,

you know not how happy you have make I; one great weight, which be

crush my soul to the dust ere you appear, be remove by your word.

of all man live you be the one I would have select for my dearest

Rose’s husband; and now, if I--that be to say, whatever befall I she

will be happy.”



“then you be not disappointed?” rejoin frere, greatly relieve; “you

know you use at one time to be just a very little bit ambitious, and I

fancy you might have be cherish some splendid scheme for marry

Rose to a duke--she’s good enough for the good of ’em, even if duke

be what they should be, instead of what they be too many of ’em.

well, I’m very glad!--but now about yourself--‘if anything befall you,’

you say; pray what be likely to befall you more than any other people?

and what do you mean by be crush by a weight, and by look so

melodramatically miserable?”



Lewis heave a deep sigh, and then reply, “you speak jestingly; but

there be many melodrama less strange than my wayward fortune: such as

it be, however, I have provoke and will go through with it. frere, you

love Rose for her own sake, be kind to and forbear with my mother for

mine--she have many fault, a giddy head, an impulsive disposition (than

which there can be no great temptation), but a warm heart--and--and I

feel I have never do a son’s duty by she. frere, you will take care of

she?”



the event of the day and evening have well-nigh exhausted even Lewis’s

untiring energy, and the sight of Frere arrive so unexpectedly have

bring back to he so many home memory, recollection of early

day, ere with the strength and freedom of manhood have come its

trial and its sin, that as he think of these old association and

remember kindness slight, affection cast away, duty neglect,

for the sake of that one master-passion, he forget for the time the

wrong he usually feel so keenly, and remorse for his selfish neglect

overwhelm he and cause his voice to falter and his eye to grow dim

with the mist of unshed tear. Frere perceive his emotion, and wait

till it have in a degree subside; then go up to he, he lay his hand

on his shoulder caressingly, say, “come, Lewis, we have know each

other from boyhood; we have long be brother in affection, and be

soon about to become so in name, associate by a still near tie--we

never use to have secret from each other, and should not do so now.

I have learn from Rose the cause you have have for sorrow, and for

two year have suffer you to try your own method of cure, without

attempt to interfere with you, but I now see that the experiment have

fail, and that you be miserable--be it not so?”



Lewis bow his head in token of assent, he could not trust himself to

speak.



“we be not place in this world to be miserable,” continue frere;

“true, this life be a state of trial, and it would not be so if we have

not many evil, temptation, and sorrow to endure; but by God’s help

the evil may be bear, the temptation overcome, and the sorrow turn

to joy, if we do not oppose our will to His; but if we do, sin lieth at

the door, we league ourselves with the enemy of mankind, and misery must

come of it. do not misunderstand I,” he add kindly; “I do not seek

to blame you, I can have no pleasure in so do, but on the contrary

deep pain; still it be evident your mind be disease, and if in

probe the wound to discover the nature of the evil I hurt you, you

must pardon I for the sake of the object I have in view. but! be

talk at random, for want of a more clear insight into the cause of

your present difficulty. come, be frank and open with I; let we face

the evil boldly, and between we devise some mean of overcome it.”



“what bring you here?” exclaim Lewis, suddenly raise his head and

fix his pierce eye full upon his friend’s countenance.



Frere smile a melancholy smile. “hot-head, petulant, and jealous of

interference yet!” he say. “my poor Lewis, I do not come to catechise

you--affair of quite another nature bring I here: I be try to

carry out an arrangement between my uncle Ashford and your _ci-devant_

foe, Lord Bellefield.” as he mention Lord Bellefield’s name Lewis

shudder, and his eye again seek the ground. “and now that I have

clear up this alarming doubt,” resume Frere, “tell I what ail you,

for that you be miserable, and that I mean to know wherefore, and do my

good to render you otherwise, be two self-evident fact.”



“’tis useless,” return Lewis in a low voice; “the die be cast, and

neither you nor any one else can help I. would to Heaven you have come a

day soon and take I away from this accursed place; as it be, my own

mad passion have hurry I on, and my fate be fix. now,” he continue,

glance at the clock, which stand at a quarter to twelve, “I must ask

you to leave I--we may meet to-morrow--or--if anything should prevent

it--and if--if I have not an opportunity of tell you all you seek

to know--my paper--that be, I will leave you a letter explain

everything--good-night.” scarcely able to control his voice in this

which Lewis feel might too probably be a last farewell, he hurry

through the speech in a strange, almost incoherent manner.



Frere regard he fixedly. “unless you condescend to explain to I what

you purpose do within the next twenty-four hour,” he say, “I’ll not

leave you till that time have expire. I tell you what it be, Lewis:

I have not live three-and-thirty year in the world without having

learn to read man’s face, and I read in yours that you be stand

on the verge of some great folly, madness, or--crime; and now, what be

it?”



Lewis pause for a moment in deep thought, and then say calmly, “sit

down, frere; you be an Englishman, and a man of highly honourable

feeling; moreover, you be my old, my most cherished friend. I be,

as you say, madden by circumstance and on the verge of a great crime;

sit down, I will tell you all, and you shall judge between God and man,

and I.”



calmly, clearly, truthfully, in the deep silence of night, do Lewis

recount to his friend the strange passage with which the reader be

already acquaint; he relate the simple fact, whether they tell for

or against he, just as they occur; without enter into unnecessary

detail he leave nothing important unsaid, till Frere have conceive a

clear idea of Lewis’s whole career from the hour he enter Broadhurst

to the moment in which he be speak.



“the upshot of all this be,” observe Lewis in conclusion, “that I be

weary of life; littleness, brutality, and oppression in man, weakness

and treachery in woman, and the tyranny of passion in oneself, render

this world an incipient hell. to-morrow must end it one way or the

other--either he will shoot I or I shall shoot he; the latter

contingency I shall not long survive; such remorse as I should feel

would be unendurable. to save myself from the guilt of suicide I

shall volunteer into some fighting regiment engage in these civil

broil--Tyrian or Trojan, Austrian or Venetian, I care little; my

sympathy side with one, my association with the other, and with

either I may obtain the only prize I covet--a soldier’s death.”



“now listen to I, Lewis,” return frere gravely. “I once at your own

request promise you that _while we both live I would never give you

up, but would stand between you and your fiery passion_, and I thank

God who in his mercy have send I here at this particular moment to

enable I to fulfil my engagement. you have suffer, and be suffer

deeply, and from my heart I pity you; but see, as I do only too

clearly, the cause of all this misery, it would be no kindness in I to

omit to point it out to you. your two lead fault of character, pride

and an overweaning degree of self-confidence, be at the bottom of it

all. Pride make Lord Bellefield your enemy--when he offer money for

the dog he never intend to insult you; your proud answer irritate his

pride, and from that time forth he seek to injure you--evil produce

evil, dislike grow to hatred, hatred begat revenge, revenge cherish

only require opportunity to become develop into assault and murder;

that opportunity have now arrive, you have be guilty of the first, you

contemplate the second. so much for pride--now for self-confidence. you

imagine nothing could tempt you to forget your dependent position in

General Grant’s family (a position which your pride lead you falsely to

consider a degradation) so far as to forfeit your self-respect by

love Annie, so you permit yourself to enjoy her society till

your affection be beyond your own control--mistake number one. then

self-confidence whisper that it would be heroic to overcome this

passion, so instead of avoid the danger, you stay to brave it till

you have sacrifice your happiness, if not hers also--mistake number two.

still untaught by experience, in your own strength you endeavour to

crush out the memory of the past; still think only of self, you flee

your country, recklessly sever tie and neglecting duty. two year’

vain struggle have prove your boasted strength to be abject weakness,

unable to save you from become the slave of your evil passion, and

I arrive here to find you contemplate the sin of--well, if I call

it murder you will deem that I exaggerate, so I will say the sin of

gambling in a lottery of manslaughter, with every chance against you.”

 Lewis again raise his eye to Frere’s face as he reply calmly, but in

a cold, hard tone of voice--



“you have describe my miserable career harshly indeed, but in the

main truly. you profess yourself my friend--in make this painful

recapitulation therefore I presume you to have some friendly object;

what be it?”



“first to exhibit to you the disease, then to point out the remedy,”

 return frere.



“and if you can do this,” exclaim Lewis--“if, remember what I be,

you can show I how I might have avoid my error in the past, how I

may do aught to retrieve they in the future, I will indeed reckon you

my friend--nay, I will bless your coming as that of an angel send from

heaven to aid a desperate, well-nigh a despairing man.”



“pray what religion do you profess?” ask Frere abruptly.



Lewis start, but recover himself, reply coldly, “the same as you

do yourself.”



“and do you believe in the truth of it?”



“why ask such a question?” return Lewis with a slight degree of

annoyance perceivable in his tone; “whatever may have be my fault, I

be no infidel.”



“I will tell you why I ask,” reply Frere; “because, though you

confess with your lip the truth of Christianity, in your life you have

practically deny they.”



Lewis make no answer, and Frere continue in an earnest, impressive

voice, his manner become every moment more animated as he grow excited

with his subject--



“if, as you say you do not doubt, Christianity be true, it amount to

this. the God who make and govern this world have be pleased to reveal

to we his will--namely, that if we believe in he and obey he, he will

save we from eternal misery and bestow upon we eternal happiness. to

enable we to fulfil the second condition, that of obedience, he have

give we a code, not so much of law as of principle of action, by

which we may become a law to ourselves. in order to demonstrate how

these abstract principle be applicable to the exigency of our

mundane career, he send his Son into the world, ‘a man subject to like

passion as ourselves, only without sin,’ _because_ he be a consistent

embodiment of the doctrine he teach. now have you take these precept,

to which you accord an unpractical and therefore an equally senseless

and useless belief, as the rule of your action, how different a result

would have follow; instead of provoke animosity by haughty look and

proud word, you would have remember that ‘a soft answer turneth away

wrath;’ instead of return evil for evil, you would have consider

the example of Him, who ‘when he be revile, revile not again,’ and

call to mind his precept, ‘resist not evil, do good to they that hate

you, and pray for they which despitefully use you and persecute you;’

instead of seek to avenge your own quarrel by deed of violence,

which outrage nature, and bring their own punishment with they even

here, in the pang of conscience, you would have think of his word

who hath say, ‘Vengeance be _mine_, I will repay,’ and leave your

cause in his hand. instead of attempt to do everything in your own

strength, and fail thus miserably, you would have recollect that

‘God’s strength be make perfect in our weakness,’ and pray to he

for support and assistance. even now, instead of having recklessly

determined to expose yourself to the chance of commit what you own

to be a crime of such frightful magnitude, that the remorse it must

entail on you would be unbearable, the question would be, not, how at

any sacrifice you must vindicate your honour in the eye of man, but

‘how then can I do this great wickedness and sin against God?”’ he

pause, then ask abruptly, “do you admit all this?”



Lewis’s feature work convulsively, as in a hollow, broken voice he

reply, “yes I do, God help I!”



“and he will help you,” return frere, “if your repentance be indeed

sincere; but that must be prove by act, not word--will you give up

your revenge, and agree not to meet Lord Bellefield tomorrow?”



“no, by Heaven!” exclaim Lewis fiercely, spring to his foot. “the

sole possession my father bequeath to I be his name and his spotless

honour, and it shall never be say that he leave they to one whom man have

a right to call coward.”



“and yet a coward you be,” return frere sternly, “not in the

particular of brute courage, share with you by the tiger and the wolf,

but in the far high attribute of moral courage, the martyr spirit

which enable the high order of mind to endure the scorn of worldly

man, rather than offend God and degrade themselves by the commission of

evil. I will ask one more question, and then I have do with you: you

say you believe in a future and eternal life--be you fit to enter

upon that life to-morrow through the dark portal of a sudden and violent

death?”



as Frere utter these awful word, in a tone of the deep solemnity,

Lewis, who have be impatiently pace the room, stop short as though

arrest in his course by a thunderstroke. place his hand to his brow,

he stagger as if about to fall, and Frere spring up to support he.

recover himself, he murmured--



“I must be alone, in half-an-hour you shall know my decision.”



then open the door, he motion to Frere to await he in the

painting-room, and close it after he, lock it. what pass in that

half-hour--how, prostrate before the Great White Throne, the proud

man wrestle with his agony, can be know but to one, the Searcher of

Hearts. when, at the expiration of the prescribed time, the door be

gently unclosed and Frere enter, he find Lewis, pale indeed and

trembling, but calm as a little child.



“bless you, dear old friend!” he say, “_truth and you have conquer_;

I place myself in your hand--do with I as you will.”









CHAPTER LXIII.--SHOWS how it fare with the LAMB which the WOLF HAD

WORRIED.



about nine o’clock in the evening mark by the occurrence of the

event narrate in the last chapter, General Grant be inform that a

young man, who refuse to give his name, request five minute’ private

conversation with he. somewhat surprised at this demand, the General

follow the servant into an apartment use by Charles Leicester as a

study, and desire that the person might be show in; in another moment

a tall, swarthy young fellow, dress in the garb usually wear by the

low class in Venice, make his appearance. as soon as the servant have

quit the room, the stranger present a note to the General, say,

“if you will read that, sir, you will perceive the object of my visit,

and learn the necessity which force I to intrude upon you at such an

untimely hour.”



the note, which be write in a delicate but somewhat illegible female

hand, run as follows:--



“a die woman implore you, sir, to visit she; not for her own sake,

for her hope rest in God and not in man, but for the sake of one who

must be dear to you in the world--your daughter. the writer have

information to impart to you which may save you and she from year of

deep misery. the bearer of this note will conduct you safely to

one who again implore you by all you hold sacred not to neglect this

summon, or delay return with the messenger, lest you should arrive

_too_ late. the writer pledge her word, the word of one about to enter

upon eternity, that you shall return safely.”



“this be a very strange note,” observe General Grant, suspiciously

eye the young man, who stand await his decision; “how be I to know

that this be not some cunningly devise scheme, dangerous to my life or

liberty?”



“I swear to you that you may safely trust I,” reply the stranger

eagerly; “adopt what precaution you will, leave your money, or aught

that be of value, at home--take pistol with you, and if you see any

sign of treachery, shoot I through the head. I _could_ tell you

that which would render you as eager to accompany I as you now appear

unwilling to do so, but I have promise to leave _her_ to explain the

affair as seem to her good--she be my sister, and die; if you delay

you will arrive too late.”



“you be an Englishman, I presume?” inquire the General, still

undecided.



“I be so,” be the reply, “and have serve my country on board a

man-of-war.”



“a sailor! what be your captain’s name, and what ship do you belong

to?” demand the General.



“‘the Prometheus’--Captain manver,” be the concise answer.



“be you in she during the year 18----?” continue his questioner, and

receive a reply in the affirmative, add, “where be you station

then?”



“we accompany a convoy of transport, take the----th and----th foot

to Madras, and then proceed to China,” be the answer.



the General nod approvingly. “quite true,” he say; “Captain Manvers

be a friend of my own, and I know his vessel to have be then employ

as you have state. I will trust you; wait five minute while I prepare

to accompany you.”



within the time he have mention General Grant return, wrap in a

military cloak, beneath which he wear a belt support a sabre and a

brace of pistol.



“if I do not return in two hour, give this note to Mr. Leicester,” he

say to the servant who attend they to the door; then motion to the

stranger to precede he, he quit the Palazzo Grassini. leave the

square of St. Mark they advance towards the Rialto; cross this, and

pass the fruit and vegetable market beyond, they reach a spot where

a gondola be moor. having step into it, the General, on a signal

from his guide, seat himself near the stern, while the young sailor

take an oar and assist his companion in propel the light vessel.

having proceed some short distance in this manner, the rower pause

at a flight of step. here the stranger signify to General Grant that

they must disembark; then resume his office of guide, he lead the way

along the bank of the canal, and through court and narrow alley,

inhabit by the low order of Venice, till he stop before a

rude door. at this he tap twice in a peculiar manner. an old crone

appear in obedience to his summon, and cautiously unclose the door,

admit they. take a lamp from her hand, the young man lead the way up

a steep flight of stair, closely follow by his companion.



“wait one minute,” he say as they reach the top; return almost

immediately, he continue in a low whisper--



“she be awake and perfectly collect, but appear sink fast; she

be anxious to see you without delay; tread as lightly as possible, and

follow I.”



advance a few step, he open the door of a bedroom, and the General,

stoop his head to avoid strike it against the top of the doorway,

enter. the apartment, though small, be clean and more comfortably

fit up than from the external appearance of the house he have be lead

to expect. on a low truckle bed, in one corner of the room, lie the form

of the die girl; at a sign from her brother, General Grant approach,

and seat himself on a chair by the bedside, wait till she should

address he. for a few minute she appear quite unable to do so,

and her visitor fear, as he gaze on her emaciated form and sink

feature, that she have indeed delay her communication till the

paralysis of come death have seal her lip, never again to unclose

in this life. in his early day General Grant have be familiar with

death in some of its most appalling shape; he have see man fall by his

side, mutilate by ghastly sabre wound, to be trample under the hoof

of madden, plunge horse; he have stand immovable when the deadly

artillery plough up the ground around he and mow down whole rank

as the scythe of the reaper prostrate the nodding corn; and when the

word of command have go forth, he have lead on the stern remnant that

be leave, till the bayonet avenge the loss they have sustain; and

when the fight be win, he have sit by the couch of some wound comrade,

and watch the strong man battle as it be with death, and yield his

last sigh in a fruitless struggle with the inexorable enemy. but he have

never before see any one wear to the brink of the grave by sorrow

and disease, and despite his utmost effort to the contrary, the sight

shock and distress he deeply. the picturesque stage of decline

have long since pass away, and in the appearance of his victim the

destroyer stood reveal in his true colour. the feature of the poor

sufferer be characterise by an expression of fatigue and distress,

that tell of long day and weary night of patient endurance; she be so

emaciate that the form of the skull and the outline of the bone of the

cheek and jaw be distinctly visible through the parchment-like skin,

give a strange, unearthly appearance to the face, while the parch

lip, the dark fever spot burn in the centre of each cheek, and at

interval the low, husky cough, which once hear can never be mistake,

evince only too surely the presence of that fell disease, which seem,

as its peculiar attribute, to select its victim amongst the young and

fair. her whole appearance be so worn and corpse-like, that when, after

a paroxysm of cough, she raise her droop eyelid and fix she

earnest, appeal glance upon her visitor, he start as though he have

see one raise from the dead by the agency of some special miracle.



“I thank God that you be come, sir,” she say in a low, sweet voice,

“that I may yet do some good before I die. I have be the cause of much

evil in my short life, and I feel it be a duty to tell you the truth of

my sad history, and do the little that be possible to save another from

endure the same misery that have bring I to the condition in which

you see I.” she pause, and the silent, inward cough--the voice of

death--again shake her fragile frame. “you do not know I,” she resume;

“I be Jane Hardy.” as she mention her name the General start, and

bend his head, drink in she every word with deep attention. “about

three year ago,” she continue, “or perhaps rather less, a gentleman

who be stay at Broadhurst be throw from his horse while hunt.

he be stun by the fall, and some of his companion bring he to

our cottage. there be no one but myself at home, and I fetch water

and bathe his temple. as soon as he begin to revive, the friend who

have bring he say laughingly that they could not leave he in well

hand, and quit we to follow the hunt. as the gentleman begin to

recover he enter into conversation with I. he be very witty and

clever, and tell I of the fine sight he have see in foreign land, and

many other beautiful and wonderful thing which I have never hear of,

and before he go away he draw I to his side and kiss I, and say

he should come again to see his kind little nurse, and I--God help I--I

be young and simple, and I believe all he say, and from that hour I

love he. well, sir, he come not only once, but often, and I listen

to his soft word and specious promise until I cease to think of or

care for anything but he. I have no mother to warn I; my poor father

have become stem and morose, and I fear he and seek only to conceal

my attachment from he. with some of the fact you, sir, be already

acquainted. my father be capture on one of his poach expedition

and send to gaol. I sit up the whole night wait for his return, and

in the early morning come, not he whom I be expect, but my tempter.

he tell I what have occur, reveal to I for the first time his real

rank, promise to obtain my father’s pardon by mean of his wealth and

influence, and, as the price of his assistance, implore I to fly with

he. he could not make I his bride in England, he say; his position

forbid it; but he vow he would carry I to some bright land in the

sunny south, and that we should be unite and live happily there. weak

fool that I be! I believe he, and consent.



“the rest of the tale be soon tell. I accompany he to London; he

be kind to I, and my dream continue. by his desire I follow he to

Rome, under the care of his valet. for a time I be treat with every

attention; servant obey I, luxury surround I; but his promise

of marriage he never fulfil. then he begin to grow tired of I, and

my punishment commence. he soon prove to I the true nature of his

disposition; his temper be fearful, at once passionate, sulky, and

vindictive, and I be a safe object on which to vent it. still I

could have bear this uncomplainingly if I could have believe that he

continue to love I. but his coldness and indifference become every

hour more apparent, till at length I awake one morning to learn that he

have desert I. I discover his direction and write to he. I forbore

reproach; I know that I have lose his love--I know, alas! too late,

that he have never really love I, and all I seek be to return to

England, beg my father’s forgiveness, and then, if it please God, to

die. but I entreat he to send I money enough to take I home again.

he leave my letter unanswered for a week, and then enclose I a cheque

for five pound, tell I that I have already cost he more than I be

worth, and that I need expect nothing far at his hand.”



“and the name of this diabolical scoundrel was---?” inquire



General Grant eagerly.



“Lord Bellefield,” be the reply, in a clear, distinct, though feeble

tone of voice.



“what proof can you give I of this?” be the cautious rejoinder.



“these letter,” return the girl, produce a small packet from

beneath her pillow.



the General take they, examine the post-mark and the seal, compare

the signature with that of a letter he take out of his pocket, read

two or three of they and then return they, mutter in a voice that

tremble with suppress rage, “they be genuine, and they be _his_.”



“the rest of my tale be soon tell,” resume Jane Hardy. “Lord Bellefield

have leave debt behind he, and when it be know he have quit Rome,

not mean to return, those to whom he owe money seize the few

valuable that I possess (chiefly dress and trinket which _he_ have

give I), and my last hope, that of return to England, be take

from I.” here a fit of coughing, prolong till it seem as though it

must annihilate her feeble frame, effectually interrupt the speaker.

her brother hold a strengthen cordial to her parch lip, and after

a lapse of some minute she be enable to resume her narration, though

her voice be so weak and husky that it be with difficulty her auditor

could catch her word. “I have little more to tell,” she say. “I

suffer much, very much misery, but, thank to the kindness of some

sister of charity (rightly be they so call), I be save from the

depth of degradation into which too many, desert as I be, have be

force.” again she pause from weakness, and with the tenderness of

a woman Miles Hardy wipe the cold dew of approach death from she

brow, and put back the rich masse of she (even yet) beautiful hair. the

General be visibly affect.



“can nothing be do to save she?” he say; “I will ascertain who be

the most skilful physician in Venice and send they to she. no money

shall be spare.”



a dark look flit across Miles’s face, but the die girl turn

towards the speaker, and a faint smile testify that she have hear and

understand he.



“tell I,” she whisper, “that my last moment have not be spend in

vain. your daughter--they say she be good and beautiful; he will take

her heart for the plaything of an hour and then crush it as he have

crush mine. you will not let she marry he?”



“Sooner would I see she stretch on her death-bed before I,” be the

stern rejoinder.



the girl smile again. “you have make I so happy,” she whisper; then

with difficulty, and pause between each word, she continue, “tell he

I forgive he and pray for he; I pray that he may repent.” again she

pause, apparently struggle for breath: “Miles, it be very dark,” she

say; “come nearer, dear!” her brother place his arm round she, and

nestle her head in his bosom, an expression of child-like happiness

spread over her feature. having lain thus for some moment she suddenly

start up, exclaim aloud, “oh God! my chest!” in a moment the severe

pain seem to pass away and the happy smile return. “may he bless

you, dearest!” she murmur; then a solemn change come over her

countenance, there be a slight struggle, and then--the jaw relaxed, the

eye glaze, and she fall back in her brother’s arm a corpse.



[illustration: 0496]



when later on that night woman come to perform the last sad office to

the dead, an English Bible be find beneath the pillow, and a leaf be

turn down at the text, “her sin, which be many, be forgive, for

she love much;” word of mercy we should do well to bear in mind, and

humbly trust they may indicate the future of many a “broken and contrite

heart.”



while General Grant be thus occupy, Annie, little dreaming of the

various event that have occur, and which so nearly concerned her

happiness, be think over the scene of the morning, and afflict

her spirit by the recollection of Lewis’s parting word. what would

she not give that he could know the truth; know _why_ she have allow

herself to be engage to a man whom Lewis have good reason to believe she

both dislike and fear; but it be impossible, situate as she be,

to enlighten he, and she must submit to bear that most bitter of all

trial, the knowledge that one we love think evil of we, and have just

and reasonable ground for such misconception. then her engagement to

Lord Bellefield, now more hateful to she than ever--what should she

do to avoid it? to whom should she turn for counsel and

assistance?--Laura?--she have great faith in her good sense, and, above

all, in her energy of character--could she, dare she, confide in she?

and she have just settle that she certainly could _not_ when a gentle

tap be hear at the door. Annie cry, “come in,” and Laura enter.



“I hope I be not disturb you, dear,” she say, “but I grow fidgety

about you, fear the alarm and fatigue of the morning might have be

too much for your strength.”



Annie smile mournfully and shake her head, at the same time make room

for her friend on the settee upon which she be recline. Laura place

herself by her side, and take Annie’s hand in her own, stroke it

caressingly.



“poor little hand,” she say; “how soft and white it be, but it’s

get sadly thin; really, dear Annie, I must lecture you. you eat

nothing, and your spirit have quite desert you--you who be such a

happy, merry little thing--it make I miserable to see you.”



she pause for a reply, and at length it come, but in a form she do not

expect, and which tend not at all to remove her anxiety.



“do you think I be very ill, Laura?” Annie ask; “so ill that I be at

all likely to die?”



“no, darling; I hope--I trust not,” return Laura earnestly; “but why

do you ask, and in so strange a tone that one could almost fancy you

wish that it might be so?”



“because I do wish it,” be the sad rejoinder; “if I live I must be very

unhappy--there be no help for it, and so I wish to die. be that wrong? I

be afraid it be.”



Laura pause ere she replied--



“I don’t think you be likely to die--grief kill very slowly. I be

sure you need not die of grief, or seek to die to escape a life of

unhappiness, if you would only be reasonable. I love you as I should

have love a sister, have I possess one; my only desire be to render

you happy; I be a woman, as yourself, and as little likely as you

would be, be our situation reverse, to do or counsel anything which

could wound your feeling or compromise your delicacy; and yet you lock

your sorrow in your own breast, and refuse to give I sufficient

insight into your heart to enable I to be of the slight comfort or

assistance to you. be this wise or even kind?”



such an appeal, come at that particular moment, be irresistible.

Annie throw her arm round her friend, hide her face on Laura’s shoulder,

and sink her voice almost to a whisper, inquired--



“what be it you wish to know?”



“you dislike Lord Bellefield, and be anxious not to marry he?”



“yes, oh, yes!” be the unmistakable answer.



“you love----”



Annie draw back, but Laura’s arm, pass round her slender waist,

detain she.



“you love Lewis Arundel?”



this time Annie do not reply, but a convulsive pressure of the hand

answer Laura’s question well than word could have do.



“then, if you love he as he deserve to be love, how could you allow

yourself to be force into an engagement with Lord Bellefield?”
