“must I, indeed, reveal to you all my folly and weakness?” murmur poor

Annie.



“really I be afraid you must, dear, if you wish my advice to be of the

small use to you,” return Laura with a kind, encourage smile;

“but perhaps the folly may prove not to have be so very foolish, and

the weakness turn out amiable one after all. come, let we hear!” thus

urge, Annie recount with smile, and tear, and word, now drop

in broken sentence, now pour forth with all the eager vehemence with

which feeling long restrain at length find vent, that portion of this

veritable history which especially relate to herself, and the rise

and progress of her unfortunate attachment; until she reach the

point whereat, overwhelm by the belief that Lewis have depart from

Broadhurst, suspect her love and not reciprocate it, she have

permit herself to be hurry into an engagement with Lord Bellefield,

sacrifice herself to guard against the possibility of any imputation

be cast upon her maidenly reserve. here Laura interrupt she by

exclaiming--



“my poor child! I see it all now; you be to be pity, not blame;

would to Heaven you have know the truth early! how much misery it

might have save you. Lewis Arundel quit Broadhurst because he love

you with all the impassioned tenderness of his fiery nature, and find

even his iron will powerless to control or even long to conceal his

feeling.”



“how do you know this?” exclaim Annie, sweep back her luxuriant

ringlet from her flushed cheek, and fix her large, eager eye upon

her friend’s countenance.



“from his own lip when he first hear that you be come here,” be

the reply. and Annie, press her hand to her eye, hide her face in

the sofa cushion and burst into tear; but this time they be tear of

joy.



then, when she have in some degree recover from her agitation, Annie

learn the history of Lewis’s wandering to cure his love, and how

signally the remedy have fail, and how he have turn painter, and be

cleverer than anybody else (a fact of which she feel convinced before

she hear it), and how Laura have discover his secret through the

medium of his sketch of Annie and Faust--(she do not mention the

“Giaour” picture, fear to alarm her friend)--and how Charles and she

have see a great deal of he and become very fond of he (oh how Annie

love she for say that!), and how at last one day she have gain his

confidence and he have tell she all, and how she have resolve never to

breathe a syllable of it to Annie unless she could clear herself in the

matter of accept Lord Bellefield, and thus prove herself not unworthy

to possess the knowledge that the priceless blessing of Lewis’s noble

and generous heart be her, and hers only. and when Laura have finish,

Annie, like a true woman, contrive by a series of “cunning-simple”

 question to make she tell her tale all over again, particularly those

portion which relate to Lewis’s nobleness of nature, and the depth,

strength, and permanent quality of his affection for herself; and when

all have be say and re-say that could by any possibility be find to

say, even on this interesting matter, Annie fix her soft, implore

eye on her friend’s countenance, and ask in a tone of the most

innocent but complete helplessness--



“and now, dear Laura, tell I what _be_ to be do?”



up to this moment Laura have consider the whole question to hinge on

one point--be Annie worthy of the love of such a man as Lewis, or not?

this satisfactorily decide, all other difficulty seem by comparison

insignificant; but now, when the monster obstacle have disappear, the

engagement to Lord Bellefield, the General’s obstinacy, Lewis’s pride,

Annie’s womanly reserve, and Charley’s indolence and dislike of say

or do anything which could by the most remote possibility irritate or

annoy any one, all flash across she and bewilder she. still she have

great faith in her own energy and in the goodness of her cause, and so

reply vaguely, but confidently--



“why, my love! it’s perfectly absurd to give way to despair as you have

be do; of course something must, and therefore can and shall

be do; but what it be to be will, I confess, require some little

consideration!”



and just when their deliberation have reach this point, Laura receive

a summon from her husband to say that he desire to speak with she; so

she imprint a kiss on Annie’s smooth brow, and they part.



“I say, Laura, read this,” exclaim Charley, look worried and

perplex, as he hand his wife the follow note:--



“Dear Charles, I have desire your servant to give you this note in case

I should not return in the course of the next two hour. I be about

to accompany a young stranger, represent himself to be an english

sailor, to visit his sister, who be say to be on her death-bed, and have

some communication to make to I. I have examine the man, and believe

his tale; but if I should not return within the time specify, it be

probably a clever fabrication, and as no lie can be frame for other

than an evil purpose you have well apply at once to the police, and

look after I in whatever way they may advise.



“your faithfully,



“Archibald Grant.”









CHAPTER LXIV.--THE FATE of the WOLF!



“pleasant that,” resume Charley, as Laura, having finish read the

note, return it with look of alarm. “Evans declare it’s more than

two hour since Governor Grant start, and there be no sign of he

yet. why people cann’t stay quietly at home when they’ve get a good house

over their head, instead of rush out to seek dangerous adventure, I

cann’t think. I should have suppose the General have arrive at a time of

life when he would have sense enough not to be gull by message

from girl, either live or die. perhaps the summon be mean for

Bellefield after all, and the bearer deliver it to the wrong man; what

a joke that would be, eh?”



“really, Charles, I don’t think it be anything to laugh at,” return

Laura anxiously; “be your brother at home?”



“no, Belle’s out too; my family be becoming shockingly dissipate.”



“have you not well apply to the police, as the note propose?” urge

Laura.



“Police, indeed!” mutter Charley: “the General cann’t remember that he

be out of London. I wonder he do not direct I to send a cab for he.

these confound sulky austrian official be rather different customer

to deal with from our blue-bottle--Messrs. Al & Co. the only thing be

to go down to the consul’s office, and that must be do, I suppose, but

it’s an awful bore.”



so say, Charley yawn, stretch himself, make Laura ring for his

boot, and have just accomplish the labour of pull they on, when

rapid footstep be hear--door open and shut, and the object of

their anxiety stand before they, his face flush with exercise, and

his whole manner bear trace of excitement and agitation. “well,

General,” begin Charley, “we be just go to commence fishing for you

in all the canals----” when his auditor interrupt he by inquire in

a quick, eager voice--



“your brother be not in the house, be he?”



“no; he have be out all the evening, and be not yet return,” be the

reply.



“leave we, Laura, there’s a good girl,” exclaim the General; “stay,”

 he continue, as Laura be quit the room, “do not say anything which

can alarm Annie.”



Laura nod her acquiescence and depart.



“I be very anxious about your brother,” resume the General. “as I be

return from this most strange and painful interview, the young man

who have summon I still act as my guide, some person follow we,

and as we be cross the Rialto approach, and tap my companion

on the shoulder, detain he. they converse in Italian, but I make

out enough of what they say to catch the following word speak by the

new-comer--



“‘I have watch he the evening through. he go from----’ (the name

of the place I could not hear) ‘to----, which he have this moment

quit. Jacopo and the other be prepare; we only await your

direction. why have you not join we soon?’



“‘it be impossible,’ be the reply; ‘but all will yet go as it should.’



“then, turn to I, my guide continue, ‘you have now only to walk

straight on to reach the Square of St. Mark; no one will interrupt you.

Farewell, sir; and remember _her_ wish.’



“this refer to his poor sister, about whom I will tell you another

time. he and his companion then quit I. mechanically I walk

forward, reflect on the interview, which have harass and distress

I greatly, till, recall the word I have just overhear, a new idea

strike I, and I turn and look back; as I do so I perceive, at

some distance off, a man carelessly advance towards I--at the moment

several other rush out upon he; there be a short struggle, then,

as it seem to I, he be overpower, a cloak be fling over his head,

and he be hurry away. instantly I run to the spot, but it be some

considerable distance from the place where I have be stand, and when

I arrive there, no trace of they be visible. the whole affair from

begin to end be over in less than a minute, but from the glimpse I

have, I feel convinced the man I see carry off be your brother.”



“nonsense,” exclaim Charles, starting, “kidnap Bellefield! why, what

possible motive could anybody have for do that?”



“one only too powerful--revenge!” be the alarming reply. “my guide be

young Hardy, whose sister Bellefield have cruelly betray and forsake.

Come, Charles, let we obtain aid to seek and save he: God grant we may

not arrive too late.”



we must now return to Lord Bellefield. after the disturbance at

the Casino, his lordship, accompany by Rastelli, repair to a

shooting-gallery, where he practise with pistol for an hour. having by

repeat success assure himself that his late fall have not shake his

nerve to a degree which could interfere with his skill as a duellist,

he turn to his companion, observe, “now, Rastelli, devise some

method of kill time for the next hour or so; I be anxious not to

return to the Palazzo Grassini till the family have retire for the

night. I have rather avoid meet any of they till this little affair be

over. what can we contrive to do with ourselves?”



“come home with I, and let we have a quiet game at _écarté_,” be the

reply; “that will amuse without excite you. I wish you to keep cool,

in order that you may punish for his temerity the insolent Luigi.” as he

speak the dark eye of the italian flash with the fire of revenge.



Lord Bellefield remark his eagerness, and smile contemptuously. “Calm

yourself, my good Rastelli,” he say, quietly light a cigar; “justice

shall be do, depend on it.”



“how cold and phlegmatic you English be!” exclaim Rastelli, irritated

at his companion’s apparent apathy; “have the brigand insult I as he

have insult you, if I have not stab he on the spot, I should have

know no peace till he lie bleed at my foot.”



Lord Bellefield place his hand on his friend’s shoulder, and

approach his lip to his ear, say in a low, impressive voice,

“listen! we englishman do not talk about these thing, we _do_ they.”

 there be a cold, grating bitterness in his tone, which tell of such

fiendish malice work at his heart that the italian’s display of

boyish passion shrink into insignificance beside it.



together they repair to Rastelli’s dwelling; card be produce,

and their game begin. with the calculate prudence of an accomplished

gamester, Lord Bellefield play cautiously and for moderate sum till

he have test his adversary’s calibre; then, confident in his own skill,

he artfully lead on the young Italian to propose high stake, until,

at the expiration of an hour and a half, he have win above a couple of

hundred pound.



“you be become excited and begin to play wildly, _amico mio_,” he

say, push back his chair; “we will pause for tonight.”



“and when will you give I my revenge?” inquire the Italian with

flush cheek and tremble lip.



“when you like--to-morrow evening, if it so please you--always

suppose our peep-of-day amusement go as it should do,” answer Lord

Bellefield carelessly.



“and what if you should be hit?” question Rastelli with a grim smile,

which involuntarily suggest to his auditor the idea that such a

catastrophe would not deeply distress he.



“to provide against such a contingency I shall make my will tonight,

and appoint you executor and residuary legatee; so that when you have

satisfy the few claim against I, the remainder of my property will

be your, to compensate for this evening’s run of ill-luck,” be the

jest reply.



Rastelli, have by this time in a degree recover his good humour,

answer in the same light tone; then having make their final

arrangement for the morrow’s meeting, they shake hand and part.



as Lord Bellefield gain the street, the conventional smile fade from

his lip, and a dark, sullen expression impart a gloomy ferocity to

his countenance. his look do not belie the nature of his thought,

which run somewhat after the follow fashion:--



“a pretty thing I’m in for--to think of that accurse arundel turning

up in such an out-of-the-way place as this! my ill-luck follow I

everywhere. that scoundrel be my evil genius. I shall be rid of he

to-morrow though, for I’ll shoot he like a dog; that’s some comfort.”

 he pause, then a new idea seem to strike he, and he mutter, “curse

he, he mean to murder I; I read it in his fiendish eye. I wonder

whether he be anything of a shot? a nice way to lose one’s life, in

a quarrel with a tutor! it’s next door to go out with one’s valet.

well, I’m in for it, and must chance it; a quick aim and a hair trigger

may pick he off, as it have do many a well fellow, before he have

time to be mischievous. I wonder whether Charles or old Grant know of

his be here? if not, the thing can be easily hush up.” a sound as

of a man’s footstep cause he to start and look round, but see no

one he resume, “assassination be say to be one of the fashion of this

place; I wish I be a little more _au fait_ as to the custom of the

native, or have long time to act in. I might get my friend quietly

dispose of without risk or trouble.” he reflect a moment on the

feasibility of such a scheme; but the spirit of revenge and hate

be strong within he, and mutter a fearful curse, he add,

“No!------------him, I’d rather shoot he with my own hand; that blow

stick by I.”



at this moment a man start out from a dark archway so suddenly

as nearly to run against Lord Bellefield, who, draw himself up

indignantly, be about to commence an angry remonstrance, when his

elbow be pinion from behind, some person trip up his heel, a

cloak be fling over his head, and despite his attempt to free himself,

he be overpower and hurry away by a party of several man. after

proceed some short distance they reach the bank of a canal; here

they pause, and still hold the cloak over the captive’s head to

prevent he from give an alarm, they bind his hand. one who seem

to possess authority over the other superintend this operation in

person.



“not so tight,” he say to an over-zealous individual who be tie the

cord as though it be never to be unfasten, “not so tight, it

will numb his arm. now,” he continue, “raise he carefully;” and in

obedience to his command Lord Bellefield feel himself lift from his

foot and place in a lie posture at the bottom of what he rightly

imagine to be a gondola.



having ascertain by listen that a portion of his captor be

engage in row the boat, Lord Bellefield make an effort to remove

the cloak from his face, at the same time slightly raise himself;

immediately a heavy hand press he down, and a deep, low voice utter

the follow caution, “there be the point of a knife within an inch of

your heart; if you again attempt to move or speak I plunge it in!”



thus warn, nothing remain but to lie still and await his captor’s

pleasure, which alternative, distract by mingled rage and fear, Lord

Bellefield be force to adopt. from the time occupy by their transit

it appear that they must have proceed some considerable distance ere

the gondola again stop. carefully guard as before, the prisoner

be take on shore, and half-lead, half-carry over some uneven, stony

ground, in traverse which his conductor be more than once force to

turn aside as if to avoid some obstacle that lie in their path; he be

then tell to ascend step; door be unbarred to afford they

ingress, and the air strike cold and damp, as from a vault. at length,

apparently, they reach their destination, and the prisoner be make to

sit down on a stone bench; a light be procure, and then the order be

give, “untie his hand, remove the cloak, and leave we.”



the person speak to obey, and in another moment Lord Bellefield be

able to look round he. the chamber in which he find himself be small,

the roof be high and vault, and the wall appear of an immense

thickness; the door be of oak, thickly stud with iron nail-head;

there be no fireplace; a ship’s lantern, hang by a cord from the

roof, dimly light the apartment, and a grated window, sink in the

thickness of the wall, seem to afford the only mean of communication

with the outward air. as Lord Bellefield become aware of these

particular the man who have release his hand and remove the cloak

quit the room, lock and bar the door on the outside; in

another moment the sound of their retreating footstep echo along the

stone passage and die away in the distance. a shudder pass over Lord

Bellefield’s frame as he find himself thus strangely leave alone with

one whose purpose he could scarcely imagine other than hostile. as

his companion--who wear one of those half-mask term a domino, which

effectually conceal his feature--do not seem inclined to address

he, Lord Bellefield have time to examine, with a beat heart, the

preparation make for his reception. the only article of furniture the

apartment contain, with the exception of the stone bench on which he

be seat, be a heavy oak table. at the end near he lie a cutlass,

the blade cross by that of a stiletto, in front of which be place a

load pistol. a similar arrangement of weapon garnish the other end

of the table, at which stand the motionless figure of the stranger. the

whole thing be so strange, and so like some fancy of a horrible dream,

that it be with difficulty Lord Bellefield could believe the evidence

of his sense. at length the silence become unendurable to such a degree

that, even at the risk of hurry on his fate, he resolve to break it.

address his captor, he ask, in a voice which tremble in spite of

his effort to appear cool and indifferent, “what place be this to which

you have bring I?”



the person address pause a moment, and then, without remove his

mask, reply, “a vault in the ruin of a convent on an island in the

lagune, a mile from Venice.”



up to this moment Lord Bellefield have be possess with a secret

belief that his captor be none other than Lewis Arundel; and having

already have a convincing proof both of his enemy’s bodily strength and

of the implacable nature of his feeling towards he, the idea that he

have kidnap he and carry he off to this desolate place in order to

force upon he a combat _à l’outrance_, with weapon in the use of which

his skill as a duellist would avail he little, be by no mean an

agreeable one. this fear his companion’s speech have dispel, for the

voice, though deep and stern, be not the voice of Lewis. Ignorant of

the existence of any other person likely to nourish deep feeling of

revenge against he, Lord Bellefield immediately conceive that he have

fall into the hand of some english ruffian connect with banditti,

in which case their object would probably be plunder; and the solitary

chamber, the naked weapon, etc., mere scenic arrangement get up for

the sake of intimidate he, and so make a well bargain. much

relieve by this view of the affair, he began--



“your object in bring I here be of course plunder, all this absurd

mummery be therefore utterly needless; you have only to name some

reasonable sum for my ransom, and as I cannot get out of the scrape

otherwise, I must pay it.”



“you will find it no mummery, and be wrong in suppose money will be

of the slight avail to you,” be the reply.



Lord Bellefield, however, still consider his idea a right one, and

accounting for this speech as he have already account for the presence

of the weapon--viz., as a means of intimidate he, in order to extort

from his fear a high ransom, continued--



“my good fellow, you have completely mistake your man; all your tragedy

nonsense be quite throw away upon I. the affair be simply a matter of

business: you require money, and know my rank, imagine I a Croesus.

I be nothing of the kind, but I can make it well worth your while to set

I free; conduct I safely to the Square of St. Mark, and I will give

you a hundred napoleon.”



“a million curse on your money!” exclaim the other furiously; “may

the bitter malediction of a desperate man cleave to the rank and rich

which have serve to add a false splendour to as mean and pitiful a

scoundrel as ever disgrace God’s earth. fool! let I undeceive you--I

be Miles Hardy” (as he speak he fling down his mask), “the brother of

Jane--your victim--I have bring you here _to die_. now do you think

your money, that money which you refuse to give to save she from a

life of infamy, or a beggar’s death, be likely to bribe I to change my

purpose?”



for a moment Lord Bellefield be utterly confound by this declaration;

he have never be aware that Jane possess a brother, and the surprise

add to his discomfiture; besides, harden as he be, he know that he

have deeply wrong the girl, and a superstitious instinct of the justice

of the retribution which have overtake he help still more effectually

to terrify and crush he: for once both his haughty spirit and his

presence of mind fail he, and mistake the character of the man with

whom he have to deal, he resolve first if possible to deceive, then to

cajole and bribe he.



“refuse money to Jane Hardy!” he begin in a tone of feigned surprise;

“you must have strangely deceive yourself: while she remain with I

I lavish hundred upon she, and when, with the caprice of her sex, she

choose to leave I for some more favoured swain, as I imagine, ignorance

of her abode alone prevent my settle a liberal allowance upon she.

even now I be ready to do so if she wish it--where be she?”



a look of contemptuous anger, which have overspread Miles Hardy’s face as

Lord Bellefield utter these falsehood, give place to an expression of

deep solemnity as he reply, “she be where you will be ere another

hour have pass, wretched liar that you be--go to answer for her sin

before her God!”



“dead!” exclaim Lord Bellefield, involuntarily shock into an

expression of feeling. Miles regard he attentively; have he discern

in he any symptom of real grief for her loss, any sign of true

penitence for the destruction he have work, there be that work in

the brother’s heart which might even yet have save he. but a doom

be upon the seducer, and a fresh display of his evil, sordid nature

hasten it. “poor girl!” he say; “ ’pon my word, Hardy, I’m quite

shocked at this sudden intelligence; I really be excessively fond of

she at one time--a--I mean to say, before she choose to run away from I.

however, you must not take the affair so deeply to heart: I can assure

you these thing be happen every day, and I always mean to make she

a liberal settlement; but as that be now unfortunately impossible, we

must see what can be do for you.” having deliver himself of this

heartless speech, which he consider a model of diplomacy, Lord

Bellefield pause to observe its effect upon his auditor. Miles stand

for a moment as if absorb in grief, murmur to himself, “my poor

Jane, and be it for such a thing as this you sacrifice your young

life? my poor, poor sister!” then suddenly raise his head, he say,

with a glance of the most wither scorn--



“your mean lie will prove of as little use to you as your money; I

loathe it, they, and you alike. I have tell you I bring you here to

die, and I have tell you true; but I be no assassin, and if you have the

courage of a man, you have one chance yet remain. on that table lie

six weapon, three for you and three for I; choose which you will, and

come on; only if the first fail we must try the second, and if that

do not end the matter there still remain the third. come, make your

choice.”



“well, but hear me----” begin Lord Bellefield, turn very pale.



“not a word,” be the angry answer, “instantly defend yourself. if you

refuse, I will shoot you where you stand;” so say he advance a step

towards the table.



Lord Bellefield, who have rise during the last speech, slowly follow

his example, cast, as he do so, a scrutinise look round the

apartment, and especially towards the window; the action do not escape

Hardy’s quick sight.



“your search be useless,” he say, smile contemptuously. “be you

here alone, with proper tool at hand, and know how to use they to

the good advantage, it would take you two hour to break out of this

place. if you call ever so loudly, there be no one to hear you--my

companion be half-way back to Venice by this time. you have nothing

left but to overcome I, or to die the dog’s death you deserve.”



“’tis false!” exclaim Lord Bellefield eagerly; “my friend have

succeed in trace I, and even now I hear the tread of soldier in

the passage--hark!”



with a gesture of surprise Hardy turn towards the door. this be

all Lord Bellefield require. spring forward, he seize the pistol

near to he, level it, and with the speed of thought, fire.

look round, Hardy perceive too late the snare that have be lay for

he. as he do so, a sharp, stinging pain, follow by a sensation like

the burn of a red-hot iron, pass round his left side. the ball, aim

at his heart, have strike against the handle of a clasp-knife which,

sailor-fashion, he wear sling round his neck by a string, and glance

off, enter the side and pass round one of the rib under the skin,

lodge among the muscular fibre of the shoulder-blade. furious at

the cowardly stratagem to which he have so nearly fall a victim, and

half-madden with the pain of his wound, Hardy seize the other pistol,

and shout, “die, you infernal, treacherous scoundrel!” snap it at

his adversary; but owe to the priming be damp, the pistol rusty, or

from some other unexplained cause, the cap explode without discharge

the weapon. fling it down with an oath, he snatch up the sword that

lie near to he, and exclaim, “come on, and be -------- to you!”

 scarcely give his antagonist time to follow his example, ere he attack

he furiously.



for a minute or two cut and thrust follow each other so rapidly that

all seem confusion. then as their first fury become expended, and

they fight more cautiously, Lord Bellefield perceive, to his extreme

satisfaction, that he be the well swordsman of the two, Hardy having

merely pick up the use of the cutlass on board a man-o’-war, while his

antagonist have learn fencing amongst the other military exercise of

a cavalry regiment in which, till within the last two or three year,

he have hold a commission. if, therefore, he could contrive to defend

himself till Hardy’s fury should have in some degree wear out

his strength, he trust either to disarm his adversary, or by a

well-direct thrust to rid himself of he for ever. nor be he

disappoint in this expectation; for have with some difficulty

parry a furious thrust, he catch Hardy’s sword with the blade of his

own weapon, and by a sudden turn of the wrist send it fly out of his

hand, leave his enemy defenceless and at his mercy. but mercy be a

quality for which his lordship be never famous, more especially when,

as in the present instance, its exercise might compromise his own

safety, he draw back a step to get room for his thrust, with the

intention of run his opponent through the body. with the speed

of lightning, Hardy perceive the only chance remain for he, and

unhesitatingly adopt it. snatch up one of the stilettos, he rush

upon Lord Bellefield, and receive his thrust through the fleshy part

of his left arm, close with he and bury the dagger in his heart.

utter a sound between a gasp sob and a groan, the young nobleman

stagger, raise his arm as if in the act to strike, and fall back a

corpse.



[illustration: 0508]



thus do the vengeance of the great God whom he have insult by a life

of selfish crime overtake this wicked man in the pride of his youth and

strength, and thus in the same night be the libertine and his victim

call to appear before the Judge of all the earth to answer for their

deed, whether they have do good, or whether they have do evil. for

the humble penitent we may indeed sorrow, yet not as without hope; but

for the impenitent sinner, cut off in the midst of crime, die with his

selfish heart untouched, his evil nature unregenerate, “there remaineth

no long any hope, but a fearful looking-for of judgment to come.”









CHAPTER LXV.--FAUST pay a morning visit.



Frere, on the principle of strike whilst the iron be hot, have no

soon obtain Lewis’s promise to place in his hand the arrangement

of the quarrel between Lord Bellefield and himself than he induce his

friend to write a carefully word apology for have in the heat of

passion assault his lordship on the previous evening. Lewis take the

pen, and without a murmur write as Frere dictate, his compress lip

and knitted brow alone tell of the martyrdom his proud spirit be

undergo; but his strength of will be as powerful for good as for

evil; he have resolve on the sacrifice, and cost what it might, he

_would_ make it.



“and now, what be your intention?” he inquire, as Frere, having

signify his approbation of its content, fold the note and deposit

it safely in his pocket-book. “suppose Bellefield should refuse to

accept this apology?”



“never fear,” be the confident reply, “he _must_ accept it; and to tell

you the truth, although he may bluster and give himself air when he

perceive you be not forthcoming, I expect he will only be too glad to

be quit of such an awkward customer. I don’t wish to be personal, but

depend upon it you be by no mean pleasant as an enemy; there be ‘a

lurk devil in your eye,’ as Byron say (and he ought to know about

devil, for, adopt the fall angel hypothesis, he be very like

one himself), that would try a man’s nerve rather when he find himself

stand opposite your loaded pistol at eighteen pace.”



Lewis smile faintly.



“the devil have be pretty well take out of I this time,” he say;

“henceforth I shall be essentially a man of peace.”



he pause, press his hand to his brow, and a slight shiver pass

through his frame. Frere regard he anxiously.



“what be you shiver about?” he inquire. “you don’t feel ill, do

you?”



“no, it be nothing,” be the reply. “I have, as you may easily imagine,

go through a good deal, both mentally and bodily, of late, and I be a

little overworn; but a couple of hour’ sleep will set I right again.”



“then the soon you take it the well,” rejoin Frere. “never mind

I; I shall ensconce myself in this arm-chair till the man of war,

your second, make his appearance, and sleep or read as the Fates may

incline. what time do you expect your accomplice?”



“he will be here at half-past four,” be the reply.



“and it be now just two; so turn in, and pleasant dream to you.” thus

say, Frere throw himself back in the chair, and draw a volume of

Dante out of his pocket, set to work to polish up his italian, as he

term it. Lewis rise to follow his friend’s advice, but a mist seem

to swim before his eye, his brain reel, his trembling knee refuse

to support he, and stagger forward he sink heavily to the ground

in a fainting fit. frere, much alarmed, raise he in his arm, and

carry he with some difficulty into the inner room, lay he on his

bed, and begin, with more energy than skill, to apply every conceivable

or inconceivable remedy to recover he, but with only partial success,

for although after the lapse of a few moment colour return to his

lip and pulsation to his heart, he neither speak nor appear to

recognise his friend’s voice, and after a few inarticulate murmur sink

into a dull, heavy sleep. frere cover he with the bedclothe as well

as he be able, then draw a chair to the bedside, seat himself

thereupon to watch his slumber. half-past four arrive, and with it

Major Ehrenburg, the austrian officer who have promise to act as Lewis’s

second. before he come a new idea have enter Frere’s head--it might not

be necessary to make use of the apology at all, Lewis’s sudden illness

would be a sufficient reason for his not meet his adversary.



“the amusement you have promise yourself in see my friend shoot or

be shoot you will be disappoint of, _Mein lieber Herr?_” he say with a

quiet smile, as the Austrian stare at he in surprise and twist his

moustache fiercely. “Lord Bellefield in his angry mood be no doubt a

very terrible fellow, but Lewis be about to wrestle with a more deadly

foe yet, or I be much mistaken.”



“excuse I, sir, I have no time for badinage,” return the other,

bow with haughty politeness; “_nothing_ can prevent this duel. I must

speak with the Signore Luigi himself immediately. permit I to pass.”



“oh, certainly,” reply Frere, hold open the door of the bedroom;

“but in regard to nothing be able to prevent the duel, ‘there be

more thing in heaven and earth, Horatio, than be dream of in your

philosophy.’ you will find my word to be true. see, his adversary have

lay he on his back already.”



the young soldier advance to the bedside; Lewis still sleep, but his

slumber be disturb and feverish. as the other bend over he he

turn uneasily and murmur some inarticulate sound. lay his hand

on his shoulder, Ehrenburg attempt to rouse he.



“Luigi,” he say, “it be late; they will be on the ground before we.”

 the only reply be again an inarticulate murmur; but on his repeat

his summon Lewis sit up and stare about he with a look of dull

unconsciousness, then a wild light come into his eye, and glare

furiously at the Austrian, he exclaim in a hoarse voice--



“villain, it be false! she love you not--she never love you!”



“do you not know I Luigi?” inquire Ehrenburg in a more soothing tone

of voice.



“know you, scoundrel, yes! on earth, or in the low hell, I should

know and hate you.” he pause, glance wildly round the room, then

exclaim in a voice scarcely audible through passion, “what I here in

my own house do you come to triumph over and to insult I? this be too

much.” and with a scream of fury he make a spring at the other’s throat,

which he would have succeed in grasping, probably to his severe

injury, have not Frere, who have watch he closely during the forego

scene, throw himself upon he, and with the assistance of the young

soldier, who at length begin to perceive the true state of the case,

contrive to hold he down, till, exhaust by the violence of his

struggle, he cease to resist any long.



“he must have expose himself to malaria, and the fever have attack

the brain--be it not so?” inquire Ehrenburg as soon as he have recover

breath enough to speak.



“so I fear,” be the reply. “Malaria, or macaroni, or some horrid

foreign thing or other, have bring on a violent fever, and, as you see,

he be now about as mad as a March hare (though perhaps a belief in

that popular zoological delusion may not extend to the Austrian

dominions)”--this last remark be make _sotto voce_. “and now, Mein

Herr, the soon you’re off the well, for Lord Bellefield, unless he

be much belie, be not particularly famous for patience. you’ll explain

to he why Lewis cann’t do himself the pleasure of shoot he this

morning; and you may add, with my compliment--Richard Frere’s, at

your service--that it’s well luck than he deserve. by the way,” he

continue, “if you could give one a hint how to come by such an article

as a doctor I should esteem it an additional favour.”



“I will call at the residence of an english physician as soon as I

leave this house,” be the reply; “fortunately, one who be reckon very

skilful reside within a few door.”



“that’s right,” return frere, “none of your foreign quack for I.

doctor be bad enough all the world over, I dare say; but an English

one be a degree well than any of your homoeopathic, mesmeric,

electro-biologic, clairvoyant humbugs--_Al piacer di revedervi_, Signore;

I mean, _Leben sie wohl, mein Herr_. a mustachioed, laced, and pad

young puppy!” he continue, as, with a haughty bow and a puzzle

expression of countenance, the young Austrian quit the apartment;

“cann’t he be content with cut throat himself without encourage

his neighbour to go and shoot at one another! I hate a fellow who will

be second in a duel as I hate a professional hangman. I’d half a mind

to let poor Lewis strangle he--a foreigner more or less be no great

matter.”



the physician’s opinion coincide with that of Richard Frere.

Overwrought both in mind and body, Lewis have be attack by fever

of the most virulent nature, and every resource that the science of

medicine afford appear powerless to subdue it. night and day Richard

Frere sit by the sick man’s bedside, listen with an aching heart to

his fevere raving. now, for the first time, do he become aware of the

depth and strength of that passion which, having destroy its victim’s

peace of mind, seem about to finish its work of devastation by sap

the very spring of life itself. in his delirium the idea appear to

have fix itself in Lewis’s imagination that the duel have take place,

and that Lord Bellefield have perish by his hand; and the agonised

self-reproach which his remorse force from he be painful to listen

to. occasionally he would appear to forget even this, and imagine

himself in the presence of she he love, would breathe forth expression

of the deep tenderness, when suddenly the recollection of his

suppose guilt would flash across he, and upbraid himself in the

bitter term, he would exclaim that a bar exist between they, and

declare himself a murderer accurse before God and man. and so the weary

day wear on, and the sufferer grow paler and weak, while still the

fire which be consume his young life burn fiercely as at first.



the day follow the night of Lord Bellefield’s death be a remarkable

one, for it witness the assassination of the unfortunate Marinovitch,

whose courage and strong sense of duty forbid he to desert his post,

even in order to preserve his life; this act of dastardly revenge

herald the revolt in Venice. the Palazzo Grassini be, as may be

suppose, the scene of much alarm and anxiety. General Grant and

Leicester have be foil in their attempt to trace the after

proceeding of the party who have kidnap Lord Bellefield, nor be any

light throw upon his mysterious disappearance until another night and

day have elapse, when, in consequence of a high reward offer by the

family to any person who could afford information in regard to the

affair, an individual in the garb of a gondolier seek an interview

with General Grant. this worthy (who be none other than Jacopo,

the bravo whose stiletto have so nearly prove fatal to Lewis) having

bargain for the promise reward and for a free pardon for his

own share in the transaction, confess that he and certain of his

associate have be engage by an Englishman name Hardy, with whom he

have be for some month acquaint, to seize and carry off a gentleman,

against whom Hardy, for some reason, appear to nourish a deep revenge;

that this gentleman have be stay at the Palazzo Grassini; and that

hardy having point he out to he, he (Jacopo) have watch he the

whole evening, and find he remain abroad so late, have arrange to

waylay he as he return home, and succeed in his design, though the

plan be nearly be frustrate by the unexpected absence of Hardy,

who however join they at the last moment. he then communicate those

detail of the enterprise with which the reader be already acquaint,

up to the time when he leave Hardy and Lord Bellefield together in the

ruin convent, beyond which he either be, or affect to be, ignorant

in regard to the affair. the clue thus gain be, however, sufficient.

lead by Jacopo to the room in which the duel have take place, the General

and Leicester soon find their bad fear realise. the body lie

cover with a cloak, on which be pin a paper, write by Hardy

before the duel, state his intention of force Lord Bellefield to a

mortal combat, add that when that paper be find either one or

both of they would have go to their long account; at the bottom be

scrawl in pencil--



“I have keep my word; he bring his fate upon his own head--no one have

any hand in his death but myself; he fall in fair fight, having wound

I severely, but, as I think, not mortally.--(Signed) Miles Hardy.”



all Leicester’s early affection for his brother be bring back by his

dreadful fate, and he weep over his corpse like a woman. the General

shudder slightly when his eye first perceive the expression of rage

and hatred stereotype on the rigid feature of the dead man’s face,

then his brow contract and his mouth grow stern as he turn to issue

direction for the murderer’s apprehension. whether, be Italians,

the police look upon manslaughter with a favour eye, or whether the

disturb state of the city facilitate his escape, certain it be

that Miles Hardy contrive to evade the search make for he; and after

offer large reward for his apprehension, and use every other mean

in his power to stimulate the exertion of the police, General Grant be

fain to rest satisfied that he have do all which the strict sense of

duty could command at his hand. perhaps, as the memory of the scene he

have witness by Jane Hardy’s death-bed recur to he, and he think

of the cruel provocation her brother have receive, even the stern old

soldier might be glad that he have not be call upon to condemn Miles

to an ignominious and painful death.



the feeling both of Laura and Annie, when they become acquainted with

this frightful catastrophe, may easily be imagine. from Laura it be

impossible to conceal it, for, unused to deep emotion of any kind, her

husband’s grief be for the time so overpower that he completely lose

all self-control, and it be only by the judicious exercise of her good

sense and tenderness that she be enable to restore he to anything

like composure. nor have she a much easy task with Annie, for a

superstitious but not unnatural fancy seize she that (her earnest

desire to avoid a union with Lord Bellefield having be thus fearfully

accomplish) she be in some degree morally guilty. but Laura, tender,

kind, judicious Laura, with her man’s head and her woman’s heart (a rare

alliance, constitute human perfection), argue and soothe and coax

and reason, until Annie’s self-upbraid horror yield to her gentle

persuading, as do of old the demon which torment Saul to the melody

of David’s harp: and indeed there be on earth no music sweet than a

love woman’s voice.



during all this time poor Walter find himself sadly neglect. after

the affair at the Casino, Mr. Spooner, ignorant of Lewis Arundel’s

illness, and fearful that he would keep his word and inform General

Grant of the shameful manner in which he have betray his trust, find

some plausible excuse for resign his situation and return to

England, at the General’s expense, before any exposure should take

place. so he write himself a letter announce the death of his mother

(at that moment drink brandy and water in the bar of a hotel in

Birmingham, whereof she be landlady), and leave three orphan sister

(invent for the occasion) solely dependent on he for _everything_;

which epistle answer his purpose nicely. after his departure Walter

be leave pretty much to his own device; and one of his chief amusement

be drill and talk to Faust, for whom all his old fondness have

revive since the interview in which he have make up his quarrel with

Annie. he be therefore especially annoyed and perplex by a habit

which the dog have lately acquire of absent himself every day for

several hour. various be the scheme Walter lay to discover what

become of the animal, but by some fatality they all fail to effect

their object; and the cause of the dog’s absence, as well as the mode

in which he contrive to effect his egress, still remain a mystery. at

length, one evening, as Walter be sit at a window of the Grassini

palace which look into a small courtyard or garden enclose by a high

wall, his attention be attract by observe something which in the

short glimpse he have of it appear like an animal’s head pop up above

the wall and disappear again. watch the spot carefully, Walter soon

witness a repetition of the phenomenon; but this time a rough,

hairy body and leg follow the head, and after a slight scramble the

delinquent Faust himself make his appearance at the top of the wall,

which be sufficiently broad to afford he a precarious footing: he then

deliberately, but with great caution, walk along the narrow causeway

thus afford, until he reach a spot where the limb of an old tree

grow so as nearly to touch the wall; upon this he get, and contrive,

by a mode of progression half-slip, half-clambering, to arrive at

a point whence he could easily jump to the ground. all these manouvre

Walter carefully note, and form his plan accordingly. the boy’s

curiosity--(we continue to use the term boy, for although in age and

appearance poor Walter be now almost a man, in mind he be still

far young than his year, in spite of those occasional flash

of intelligence so often to be observe in case of partial mental

imbecility, which render a just estimate of the individual capacity so

difficult to arrive at)--Walter’s curiosity be thoroughly arouse by

this discovery, and he determine if possible to find out the nature

and object of Faust’s clandestine expedition. that he have some definite

object Walter never for a moment doubt, for he have so completely make

a friend and companion of the dog, that he have learn to look upon

he much more as a reasonable being than as an animal guide only by an

enlighten instinct.



for the rest of that day, and from an early hour on the following

morning, Walter never lose sight of the dog, though he contrive to

effect his purpose without interfere with its liberty of action. at

length his patience be reward by see Faust enter the garden

and begin to scramble up the identical tree, by mean of which he have

effect his descent on the previous day. seize his hat, Walter lose

no time in follow he; the tree be easy to climb, and the same

branch which have afford a passage for Faust enable Walter to reach

the top of the wall in safety. on the other side the difficulty be

still less, for the ruin of some ancient building lie scatter in all

direction, and a pile of they actually come within a few foot of the

top of the wall, form a rough but efficient flight of step. by the

time Walter regain _terra firma_, however, Faust have proceed some

distance, and have he choose to run on might still have preserve his

secret inviolate. but when Walter call he, he stop and wait

till his friend approach, though neither threat nor endearment could

prevail upon he to turn back, or to allow Walter to come near enough to

lay hold of he. and so the pair proceed, Faust run on for a short

distance, wait till Walter draw near, and then resume his course.

the route the dog pursue avoid the more frequent way, and Walter

begin to think Faust be merely take a stroll for the benefit of

his constitution, when the animal suddenly turn down an archway, and

look back to see that his friend follow, proceed along a narrow

alley which lead into one of the small street, and stop at the door

of a house which project beyond some of the other. the door stand

ajar, and Faust without ceremony push it far open and walk in.

Walter pause, debate as well as his mental capacity enable he to

do, whether or not he should venture to follow. it be a knotty point

to decide. on the one hand his fear urge he to turn back and not

risk face the possible danger which might lie hide within this

mysterious mansion; curiosity, on the other hand, prompt he to

enter and discover at once and for ever the aim and end of Faust’s

incomprehensible visit. fear be very near gain the day, when,

in think over every motive, probable or improbable, which might

influence the dog, the bright idea flash across he that perhaps Faust

have discover his former master, and the hope of again meet his

“dear Mr. Arundel” outweigh every other consideration, he boldly

open the door, and encounter Faust, who have return to look for

he, follow that sagacious quadrupe up a flight of stair.



now it so happen that the particular morning in question be that of

the fourteenth day from the commencement of Lewis’s illness, and the

physician have pronounce the crisis of the disease to be at hand. he have

see his patient late on the previous evening, and administer to he

a powerful narcotic, from the effect of which he have not recover when

Walter and Faust commence their ramble. frere, who have sit up with he

all night, have go out to refresh himself with a short walk, leave

Lewis under the care of Antonelli, his old attendant. this worthy man

have in his turn be call down to see a friend, who having hear

of the Signore Luigi’s illness, have come to prescribe some outrageous

remedy, in the infallibility whereof his faith be as unshakable as his

ignorance on all medical subject be profound. Antonelli, whose grief

at his patron’s danger have be overpower, be easily interested in

his friend’s account of the wonderful specific; and with the garrulity

of age, he remain discuss its merit for a much long space of

time than he be at all aware of. thus it come about that Walter, when

he have follow Faust upstairs, and after a second fit of hesitation

enter an apartment through the partially open door of which the dog

have disappear, find himself in a room, in one corner of which stand

a small iron bedstead whereon lie some person, who from his deep,

regular breathing seem to be in a sound sleep. cautiously, and with

for a moment, could he recognise, in the pale, wear face which meet

speedily resolve when Lewis move uneasily in his sleep and mutter

two clear idea now present themselves to the boy’s mind: his friend

be asleep and must not be rouse, and from the expression of his

feature he must be either ill or unhappy. having arrive at these

conclusion, he proceed to act upon they by seat himself at the

bedside, to wait patiently till Lewis should awake, while he devote all

the power of his intellect to form some theory by which to account

for the change in his late tutor’s appearance. as he thus sit anxiously

watch, Lewis again turn restlessly, murmur something, the

meaning of which Walter could not catch, then speak more distinctly,

he said--



“she lean upon his arm; she smile on he; she love he! I see it

with my own eye.”



he say this so plainly, that Walter, fancy he must be awake,

address he, and ask if he be ill. Lewis catch the sound of the

word, and replied--



“Ill in mind, Frere, nothing more.”



Walter, still believe he to be awake, continued--



“it be I--Walter; do you not know I?”



for a moment the sleep man make no reply, then resume the

conversation which he imagine himself hold with Frere, he exclaim

eagerly--



“love one man and engage herself to another! I tell you no! Annie Grant

never love I!”



at this moment Faust (who have be lie quietly, and as if he be

quite at home, on a rug by the bedside), rouse by the sound of his

master’s voice, place his fore-paw on the bed, and find himself

unnoticed, endeavour to attract attention by lick first the sick

man’s hand and then his face. the effect of the opiate have by this time

in a great measure wear off, and rouse by Faust’s intrusive affection,

Lewis awake with a violent start, and attempt to raise himself in a

sit posture, gaze around he in surprise, then fall back upon his

pillow from weakness. after lie some moment perfectly motionless, he

again unclose his eye and ask in a low, faint voice, “be I dream

still, or do I see Walter?”



“no, you be awake now, dear Mr. Arundel; it be I, Walter. Faust find

you out, and bring I to see you.”



as Walter mention Faust, Lewis for the first time perceive his old

favourite, and stretch out his hand with the intention of pat he,

but the effort be beyond his strength, and his arm sink powerless by

his side. Faust, however, perceive the attention, and acknowledge it

by again lick his hand. Lewis turn his languid eye from Walter

to the dog, and a tear steal down his waste cheek, then his lip grow

compress, and an expression of anguish overspread his countenance.

with consciousness have also return bitter memory. in the meanwhile

Walter, delight at recover his long-lose friend, grow loquacious in

the fulness of his joy, and run on in his usual disconnected manner.



“so you haven’t forget Faust, then, Mr. Arundel? he have never

forget you either, poor fellow! all the time you have be away; but

I have take great care of he, you see; he’s nice and fat, ben’t he?

we’ve be very good friend too, only we use to quarrel sometimes when

he would follow Annie, and I do not like it because--because----”

 here he pause, have a kind of confused recollection that this be a

subject on which he want to say something particular. after wait

for a minute or two his idea grow in a degree clear, and he

continued--



“you know I take a dislike to poor Annie, because I think she make you

go away. I always think so until she tell I it be not the case, and

how fond she be of you.”



when Walter first mention Annie’s name Lewis start and make a

gesture to induce he to be silent, but the boy do not understand his

wish, and his auditor soon become too much absorb in the interest of

his disclosure to seek again to interrupt he.



“you be talk about Annie just now, you know, before you be quite

awake,” resume Walter, “and you say she do not love you. I remember I

think so too once, and that that be the reason why you go away, and

so I take a dislike to she, and would not let Faust follow she, only he

would. but we be both quite wrong, for Annie be just as fond of you

as Faust and I be, and now I’ll tell you how I come to find it out.”

 he then in his ramble way give a childish but perfectly intelligible

account of his conversation with Annie Grant, with which the reader be

already acquaint. just as he have finish his recital, Richard Frere

return from his walk in time to overhear the last few word of the

history, and to discover that Lewis have faint from intense emotion.









CHAPTER LXVI.--URSA MAJOR show his tooth.



Walter’s visit to Lewis produce a more favourable effect upon the

patient’s health than do all the pill and potion wherewith his doctor

have seek to exorcise the fever-fiend. he have _not_ then deceive

himself--Annie _have_ love he; nay, from Walter’s recital, as well as

from her manner on the occasion of his protect she through the crowd

in the square of St. Mark, be he not justify in believe that she

love he still? the idea be in itself happiness, for although the fact

of she renew her engagement with Lord Bellefield so immediately after

Lewis have quit Broadhurst still remain unaccounted for, the belief

that she love he seem to impart a new aspect to the whole affair,

and for the first time he allow himself to hope that her conduct

might admit of some satisfactory explanation. the emotion of a mind so

impulsive as Lewis’s necessarily produce marked effect upon the body;

agitation of spirit have mainly conduce to bring on the fever which

have thus prostrate, he, and the hope to which Walter’s word have give

rise seem to infuse new life into he; at all event, it be certain

that from the moment in which he become convinced that Annie have love

he he begin to amend. as soon as Frere consider he strong enough to

bear such an announcement, he inform he of the appalling fate which

have overtake his enemy. Lewis be at first strongly affected. but for

event over which he have have no control he might now have be in the

position of Miles Hardy, a wanderer on the face of the earth, bear

with he the harrowing consciousness that the blood of a fellow-creature

be upon his hand. after remain in silent thought for some minute

he suddenly raise his eye to his friend’s countenance.



“frere,” he say, “how can I ever be sufficiently grateful to God, who

choose you as his instrument to set my sin before I, and bring I to

a well frame of mind! have this dreadful fate overtake Bellefield

without my having resolve not to fight he, I should have feel morally

guilty of his death, consider that it be mere accident which have

enable Hardy to meet he soon than myself.”



“you act rightly, under circumstance which I must confess to have

afford about as severe a trial to a man of your impetuous nature as

could well be conceive,” return frere; “so it be but fair that you

should reap some advantage from your self-conquest. I pity poor young

hardy more than I blame he, for he have probably never be teach the

truth of Christianity, and nothing else could have possess sufficient

power over he to induce he to forego his revenge. ah! if such man as

Bellefield could but be make to see the mental agony their vice cause

to other, even their selfish heart would be touch, and they would

be unable to go on sin with such callous indifference; but in their

selfishness they look merely to the gratification of their own passion,

and ignore all possible result which might tend to interfere with they.

such a career as Bellefield’s be a fearful and inexplicable mystery to

reflect upon, and it be only by a high exercise of faith that we can

believe even Omnipotence able to bring good out of such consistent and

unmitigated wickedness.”



“and be such your belief?” inquire Lewis earnestly.



“most assuredly it be,” be the reply. “I be not one of those who

acknowledge God’s attribute with my tongue but in my heart practically

deny; nor can I believe that a being, the perfection of wisdom, of

justice, and of mercy, could allow evil to exist, be he not able to

overrule it to good. but if you ask I, ‘how can these thing be?’ I

tell you at once I do not know; I form no theory on the subject, for I

have no power to do so; my mind be that of a weak, fallen man, and the

secret thing of God be so immeasurably above it that to speculate upon

they be equally presumptuous and absurd. still I feel as certain of the

main fact as if each special detail of the Divine scheme lie spread out

like a map before I, because, be it not so, God would falsify his

attribute; the great be we worship would be, not a merciful Father,

but a stern, inexorable judge. depend upon it, Lewis, the real fallacy

in the religious teaching of the present day be that, practically if

not theoretically, fear rather than love be inculcate as the actuate

principle, and, as a natural consequence, man ignore and put aside

thought of futurity as they put aside any other painful and alarming

reflection.”



as Frere conclude Lewis pause in thought, then observed--



“all you have say sound wise and true, and yet there appear a

contradiction somewhere. evil must always be hateful to God, and as such

must deserve everlasting punishment. I cannot understand it.”



“nor do I wish or expect you to do so,” reply Frere; “but cannot you

wait patiently through a little space--the life of one man--trust

that when this mortal shall have put on immortality our enlarged

faculty may enable we to see clearly that which we now believe as

a matter of faith? the only difficulty arise from your attempt to

measure thing infinite with your finite intelligence; for instance, you

talk of everlasting punishment--what do you mean by the term?”



“mean, why, of course, punishment that shall endure throughout

eternity,” reply Lewis.



“and eternity, which to be eternal can no more have have a beginning

than it shall have an end, be an idea our mind cannot grasp; and

in attempt to define and realise these thing we only confuse and

mislead ourselves. take my word for it, Lewis, true religion, the

religion Christ come down from heaven to teach man, consist in a

sincere, earnest, and consistent belief in the goodness and benevolence

of the Creator, carry out practically by an unceasing endeavour to

reform our fall nature after his image.”



“and how be we to gain the knowledge and the strength requisite to

enable we to do this?” ask Lewis.



“by study God’s write word with an honest intention of do as

we be there tell to do, at the same time implore his assistance to

enable we to carry out our good intention,” be the earnest reply.



so the conversation end; but Lewis think over the idea thus

present to he, which, though not entirely new to he, or indeed to

any other reflect mind, have perhaps never before occur in a light

so clear and practical as that in which Frere have place they; and as

by slow degree his strength begin to return, and with Antonelli’s

assistance he contrive to creep for an hour at a time to his

painting-room, he arise from that couch of sickness a wise and a well

man.



as soon as Charles Leicester have recover from the first shock of his

brother’s death he determine to entrust his wife and child to the care

of General Grant, while he start for England to break the distressing

intelligence to his father. Lord Ashford be now become an old man,

and although the profligate career of his eld son have cause he the

deep anxiety and regret, he still regard he with much affection;

and Leicester have only too good reason to dread the effect which might

be produce upon he if, by any accident, he be to become aware of the

fatal event without sufficient preparation. accordingly, on the second

day after the discovery of the catastrophe, he quit Venice, and

travel day and night till he reach England; but fast as he

journey, the evil tiding journey still more swiftly: a rumour of

the truth have somehow find its way to the London club; at one to

which he belong Lord Ashford have accidentally overhear the affair

discuss, and while utter a half-frantic inquiry in regard to the

speaker’s authority, be seize with a fit, from which he recover only

to remain a heart-break man, paralysed and childish. Charles find

he in this deplorable state, be of course unable to leave he, and

write to Laura to beg that no unnecessary delay might occur to prevent

she join he as soon as possible. under these circumstance, General

Grant resolve to proceed to England at once, with the party under his

charge.



when Frere’s anxiety for Lewis’s life have end, and he feel satisfied

that he be on the road towards recovery, and might safely be entrust

to the care of Antonelli, he have make his way to the Palazzo Grassini,

and seek an interview with General Grant, have explain to he the

object which have bring he to Venice, together with the train of

event which have hitherto prevent his announce his arrival. he also

give an account of the _fraca_ between Lord Belle-field and Lewis at

the Casino, and his friend’s subsequent self-conquest, in resolve for

conscience’ sake to forego his revenge; but he say nothing of Lewis’s

attachment to Annie, feel that he have no right to betray his

confidence to the General without obtain his consent to the measure.



General Grant be much interested by this recital, and highly extoll

Lewis’s conduct throughout the whole affair, the shock of Lord

Bellefield’s death having take away any little prejudice in favour

of duelling which might have linger in the chivalrous mind of the old

soldier. he think, however, that consider the relative position of

the different party, it would be well for he not to visit Lewis so

soon after the awful catastrophe which have take place, but he send he

a kind message by Frere, say he should hope to see he on his return

to England, and thank he for his interference in Walter’s behalf.



on the morning previous to that fix for the departure of Laura and her

friend, Lewis, having over-exert himself the day before by paint

for several hour, and having pay the penalty by lie awake during

great part of the night, have fall into a deep sleep, which last so

long, that frere, having breakfast and give order that Lewis be on

no account to be disturb, go out. he have undertake, with his usual

good nature, innumerable commission for the General; these he set to

work diligently to execute, and after wander up and down the lane

and square of Venice, now trudge like an excited postman, now sit

bolt upright in the stern of a gondola, with _the_ cotton umbrella

spread like a gigantic mushroom over his head to keep off the sun, he

arrive, hot and tired, at the Palazzo Grassini. General Grant be from

home, so Frere leave a card, say he would call later in the afternoon;

then, consider on second thought that it would not be kind, as he

have be out so long, to leave Lewis again on the same day, he alter

his determination, and desire to be show into the library, send a

message to ask to be allow to speak to Mrs. Leicester, or to Miss

Grant. now the servant to whom this message be entrust, be,

like many of his better, averse to needless trouble, and chance

to encounter Annie as he be proceed from the library to the

drawing-room, see fit slightly to alter the tenor of his message, and

leave out all mention of Laura, inform Miss Grant that a gentleman

of the name of Frere, having call to visit the General, have, on

learn that he be from home, ask to be allow to see she. this

intelligence rather flurried Annie, Frere be always connect in

her mind with the idea of Lewis, and it be not without a degree of

trepidation, which mantle her cheek with a most become blush, that

she hasten to comply with his summon.



when Frere perceive who it be that his message have produce, a scheme,

which have suggest itself to he as a vague possibility, as he have sit

by Lewis’s bedside listen to the raving of his delirium, recur

to his mind, as a right and advisable step which it behove he to

take, now that chance have throw the opportunity in his way; his first

business, however, be to deliver himself of the commission entrust

to he by the General. having relieve his mind of the weight of this

responsibility, he began--



“well, Miss Grant, I’m glad to see you look well than you be. I

suppose it’s the”--(having get rid of your detestable engagement be his

original sentence, but he check himself, and substituted)--“idea of

get away from this horrid place, all puddle and palace; the man

every one of they either a tyrant or a slave, and such lazy rascal into

the bargain; the woman, not at all the style of female to talk to you

about; and without any particular beauty to account for it either, as

far as I’m a judge, though perhaps in my present position I’m a little

bit over fastidious; but then Rose Arundel be as near perfection as

anything on this earth can be--however, I’m forget you don’t know

anything of the matter, and all that I’m say must be high Dutch, or

thereabout, to you.”



and have by this time talk himself into a regular entanglement, the

worthy bear come to a sudden and unexpected standstill. Annie hasten

to relieve he.



“you have, indeed, let I into a secret, Mr. Frere,” she say, smile;

“but it be quite safe in my hand, and it be a secret, moreover, which

I be delighted to hear: there be no one in whose happiness I take deep

interest than in that of dear Rose Arundel, and I quite approve of the

step you hint at as be likely to secure it. you must allow I to

offer you my warm congratulation.”



“thank ye, thank ye,” return frere, look most comically bashful,

and route his hair about insanely in his embarrassment, “I certainly

do hope to make she happy, God bless she; though I don’t think you can

judge much about it one way or other, see that I may be a Bear in

reality (she call I one in fun, you know), mean to eat she up

bodily for aught you can tell. as to its be much of a secret, too

many people know it, too many woman in particular, to render that

possible; so, though I don’t want it announce in the _Times_ till the

event actually come off, you need not put any violent constraint

upon your natural communicativeness, for I be not so ignorant of

the idiosyncrasy of the fair sex as to forget the pain and grief

constrain silence occasion they.”



Annie make a playful rejoinder, and then, after a minute’s pause,

venture timidly to ask, “I hope Mr. Arundel continue to gain strength.

I--that be my father--and indeed all of we be so grieve to hear of

his illness!”



Frere fix his large eye upon she as he reply gruffly, “yes, he’s

get on well enough for anything I know to the contrary; but he’s as

weak as a child. it will be month before he become anything like the

man he be; he’s be unpleasantly near supply a vacancy in some

moist graveyard of this amphibious city; small thank to those who

help to bring he to such a condition.”



Annie turn very pale at this somewhat unfeele speech, but she

manage to stammer out, “I think, that is, we be tell that it be

a fever, produce by exposure to malaria, from which Mr. Arundel have

suffer.”



“a fever it be, and no mistake,” be the reply; “such a fever as I

should be very sorry to fall in the way of catch, I can tell you.”



“and yet you have nurse he through it with the most unceasing

self-devotion. you see I know you well than you be aware of, Mr.

Frere,” interrupt Annie with a beam smile.



“nurse he! why, of course I do; if I haven’t, I should have deserve

to be well kick,” return frere in a tone of intense disgust. “I’ve

know Lewis ever since he be a pretty black-eyed boy of ten year old,

and though he be a little hot-headed and impetuous sometimes, that’s no

reason why I should leave he to die of a fever in a foreign land, far

away from those that care about he. a nice sort of friend I should

be if I do, and a pretty figure I should cut the next time I come in

Rose’s way! she be not one of those who love people by half, I can

tell you; why, she actually dote on her brother.”



“oh, I be sure she do; it be that which first make I love she,”

 exclaim Annie with enthusiasm; then see all that her speech

involve, she blush “celestial rosy red” and cast down her eye in

confusion.



“Humph!” grunt Frere, “that sound all very nice and amiable, but

I prefer deed to word! I’ll tell you what it be, Miss Grant,” he

continue, turn suddenly upon Annie, “you talk about malaria be

the exciting cause of Lewis’s illness, it be no such thing--the cause

of his fever be anguish of mind--the poor boy’s be miserable for the

last two year, almost crazy with grief, as I take it, for he have be

do all sort of wild, unreasonable thing; and if the truth must be

tell, it strike I it’s more your fault than any one else’s.”



“my fault!” exclaim Annie, her face and neck flush crimson at this

unexpected charge. “oh, Mr. Frere, how can you speak such cruel word?”



“because they happen to be true one, young lady,” return Frere

sternly. “you be the daughter of a rich man, and a man in a high

station, and for that reason it’s very seldom you have the plain, honest

truth speak to you; but you shall learn it to-day from my lip, if

you never hear it before in your life, and if it be not palatable, the

fault do not rest with I. I know something of this same affair when

Lewis quit Broadhurst all in a hurry, two year ago, and I set it

down as a foolish bit of boyish romance, which a few month’ absence

would cure; but it be not till I watch by his bedside, and listen

through the solemn hour of the night to his frenzied raving, that I

become aware the passion he feel for you be root in his very heart’s

core, and see that by his deep, his overpowering love for one who I fear

be not worthy of he, he have shipwreck the happiness of a lifetime.

silence!” he continue angrily, as Annie, half rise from her seat,

seem about to interrupt he, “silence! you have voluntarily, or

involuntarily, be the cause of deep misery to the two person (for

Rose have suffer greatly on her brother’s account) for whom I care most

in the world, and you shall learn before we part the evil consequence

of your act, and tell I whether you possess either the will or the

power to repair they.”



Annie again attempt to speak, but find her accuser would not listen

to she, sink back with a gesture of despair, while Frere continued--



“very early in his residence at Broadhurst, Lewis, as I imagine, become

attach to you, though for a long time he would not acknowledge

the fact even to himself: at length, however, self-deception become

impossible; then begin the struggle between his pride and his affection;

and from that period to the hour in which he quit Broadhurst he live

in a state of mental torture. well, you could not help his fall

in love with you, you will say; and because a poor tutor be bold and

foolish enough to forget the difference of position between you (which,

by the way, he never do for one moment, though the recollection be

agony to his proud spirit), and to raise his eye to his employer’s

daughter, you be not bind to forget it also--I grant you that--but

shall I tell you what you could have help? (which I should never have

know anything about but for poor Lewis’s delirious ravings)--you could

have help say and do a hundred little nameless thing, trifle

in themselves perhaps (so be straw, but they show which way the wind

blow!), thing which give the poor fellow the idea that you return

his affection, and that have he dare to declare his feeling, he might

have obtain such a confession from you; an attempt which he be far

too honourable to make, but rather, with an aching heart, tear himself

away from Broadhurst, throw up every prospect he then have in life:

you might have help this, Miss Annie Grant, and if you have be worthy

of the love of such a noble nature you would have do so.”



as Frere, completely carry away by the excited feeling which his

recapitulation of Lewis’s wrong and suffering have arouse, pause for

breath, poor Annie, who during the latter portion of his harangue have

be utterly unable to restrain her tear, reply in a voice scarcely

audible through emotion--



“you cruelly misjudge I, Mr. Frere--most cruelly--and be make

an unkind and ungenerous use of knowledge which, if your friend have

retain his reason, would never have be in your possession.”



Frere feel the justice of this reproach, and moreover the sight of poor

Annie’s tear appeal to his kindness of heart, and serve to disarm

his wrath.



“well, that be certainly true,” he say, “and if I have indeed misjudge

you, why I can only say I be very sorry for it; at all event I need

not have speak so harshly and rudely to you; but you see, Miss Grant,

I feel very deeply about this matter, and the idea that all which Lewis

have suffer have be the consequence of your love of admiration and

idle coquetry make I angry with you.”



“indeed, indeed, I be no coquette,” murmur poor Annie.



“well, you seem to have behave like one, at all event,” return

Frere; “unless, indeed,” he continue, as a new light suddenly break in

upon him--“unless, indeed, you really do by any chance care about Lewis

as much as he care about you--of course in that event you would be more

to be pity than blame.” he pause, then after a moment’s reflection,

continue, “but no, that cannot be either; if you have really love

Lewis, you would scarcely have engage yourself to another man before

he have be out of the house four-and-twenty hour. what do you say to

that? eh, young lady!”



Poor Annie! heavily indeed do her fault press upon she; most bitterly

do she repent the weakness of character which have prevent she from

refuse to engage her hand when her heart go not with it. what could

she say? why, she could only sob like an unhappy child, and whisper in a

broken voice--



“I will send Laura to you--ask she, she know all--she can tell you.”



and so run out of the room, she throw herself upon her friend’s

neck, and beg she, incoherently and vaguely, to “go immediately to

_he_ and explain _everything_;” with which request Laura, when she

have provide the solitary pronoun with a chaperon in the shape of a

concordant noun, and restrict the transcendental “everything” to mean

the one thing needful in that particular case, hasten to comply.



the commission be rather a delicate one, and the excellent Bear do not

render the execution thereof the less difficult by choose to take

a hard-headed, moral, and common-sense view of Annie’s conduct, which

confuse Laura to such a degree, that in her desire to be particularly

lucid, she contrive to entangle the matter so thoroughly that a person

with great tact and more delicate perception than the rough and

straightforward Frere might have find the affair puzzle.



“well, I tell you what it be, Mrs. Leicester,” he at last exclaim

abruptly, “if you be to talk to I till midnight, which, see you’ve

a long journey before you to-morrow, would be equally fatigue and

injudicious, you would never be able to convince I that your young

friend act wisely. the idea of accept that unhappy man (whose

death, between ourselves, be a gain to everybody but himself, though,

of course, I shall not say so to poor Charles, who in his amiability

contrive to keep up a sort of affection for his brother); but the

notion of accept he to prevent anybody guess she be in love

with Lewis seem to I about the most feeble-minded expedient that ever

occur to the imagination, even of a woman; it’s like cut one’s

throat to cure a sore finger. I don’t admire the principle of judge

action by their result, or I should say the result of this have be

just what I should have expect--viz., to make everybody miserable.

however, though she have do a foolish thing, that be very different

from do a deliberately wicked one. well, I suppose we must not be too

hard upon she, poor little thing; I dare say Lewis, at all event, will

be magnanimous enough to overlook it, in consideration of her correct

taste in properly appreciate his good quality; however, I’ll do my

good to explain the matter to he, and put it in as favourable a light

as my conscience will allow I. and so wish you a good journey, I’ll

be off. I have a notion it willn’t be very long before Lewis and I shall

follow you; we shall not be many hour in England before we beat up your

quarter, depend upon it. Lewis will have some strange revelation to

make to Governor Grant that will cause his venerable lock to stand

on end in amazement. ah! it’s a queer world. well, good-bye, Mrs.

Leicester: I expect you and I should become good friend in time, though

you’re quite mistaken if you fancy that young woman act sensibly in

accept her scampish cousin, when all the time she be in love with

another man.”



and thus Richard Frere fairly talk himself out of the house, leave

Laura especially astonish at his _brusquerie_, and decidedly of

opinion that she have mismanage the affair and do her friend’s cause

irreparable injury.









CHAPTER LXVII.--RELATES HOW, the eclipse be over, the SUN begin to

shine again.
