in the meantime, Lewis having awake from his long sleep, and find

himself all the well and strong for his nap, have just breakfast

with much appetite when Antonelli appear and hand he a note. it be

from Laura (write before her interview with Frere), inform he of

their intend departure on the morrow, beg he to call upon she

immediately he return to England, which, as soon as his health would

permit, she advise he to do without loss of time, and wind up with

a hint that, in regard to the matter which especially interested he,

he might make himself quite easy, for that everything could be most

satisfactorily explain.



Lewis read and re-read the note. “the matter that especially interested

him!”--that could have but one meaning. oh, yes I Annie have clear

herself--she have never accept Lord Bellefield; or, if she have, she have

be cheat into do so. Annie be good and true--the Annie of his

imagination--the bright, fair, loving, gentle be his soul worship!

but he must have certainty--he must not again be the dupe of his own

wish; no, he must have certainty, and he must have it at once. wait

till his return to England? why, that might be day, week hence! and

be he all that time to suffer the torture of suspense? it be not

to be think of. he must see Laura before her departure and learn the

truth. but this would necessitate a visit to the Palazzo Grassini, in

which he must run the chance of encounter the General or Annie. and

as his thought revert to she, the idea for the first time occur

to he of the mental suffering she must have undergo if, as he now

believe, she have indeed truly love he, and be in some unaccountable

manner force by circumstance to consent to the engagement with her

cousin. then he remember the scene in the Square of St. Mark, and a

sense of the cruelty of his own conduct towards her overwhelmed he.

this decide the question. he would at all risk see Laura; and if--as

he now do not for a moment doubt--her explanation prove satisfactory,

he would entreat she to obtain Annie’s forgiveness. she _must_ forgive

he when she come to know all he have suffer--when she hear how ill

he have be: and as he think of his illness the somewhat perplexing

question occur to he how he be to reach the Palazzo Grassini in his

present weak state? never mind; where there be a will there be a way.

he _would_ do it, he be determine; and so he summon Antonelli, and

to the alarm of that worthy man, who fancy the fever have again fly

to the brain, and that his beloved master be delirious, announce

that he be go out to pay a visit, and request his assistance in

dress himself.



it be not till his toilet be complete and he attempt to walk

downstairs that he become aware how weak and helpless his illness

have leave he, and it require all his resolution to persevere in his

expedition. luckily the distance be short, and he be enable to

perform some part of it in a gondola; still, by the time he reach the

Palazzo Grassini his strength be so completely exhausted that if he

have be require to proceed a hundred yard far he would have be

unable to accomplish the task. having inquire if Mrs. Leicester be at

home and receive an answer in the affirmative, he continued--



“then show I up to her boudoir unannounced; I will hold you blameless

for do so.”



the servant, who know how intimate Lewis have be there before the

come of the Grant party, and how his visit have cease with their

arrival, naturally enough conjecture that the young painter be for

some reason desirous to avoid encounter any of the General’s family,

and comply with his request unhesitatingly. but the domestic in

question, who chance to be the same individual who have admit frere,

be not aware of the additional and, to the party concern, somewhat

important fact, that since he have perform that service Miss Grant

and his mistress have change place, and that at the moment he be

conduct Lewis to the boudoir that apartment be tenant by Annie

Grant, while Laura be engage in solemn conclave with Richard Frere

in the library. thus it fall out that when the door of the boudoir be

noiselessly open, Annie Grant, who have remain there after she have

despatch Laura on her difficult mission to Ursa Major, and _more

majorum_, from the time of Niobe downward, have indulge her feeling

with a hearty cry, be wipe her eye and try to make herself

believe that her trouble must be “work to an end,” and that dim

on the horizon of her future fate there might be discern a good time

come. Annie thus ponder, and thus engage, see a tall, bend

figure enter, in whose well-know feature, their expression soften

and spiritualise by severe illness, she need no announcement to

recognise Lewis Arundel.



the window of Laura’s boudoir be shade from the burn ray of an

italian sun by (literally) venetian blind, which keep out not only the

heat, but in great measure the light also; and Lewis, whose eye be

dizzy and his head swim from weakness, perceive a female figure

advance towards he, naturally conjecture it to be Laura, and

accost she as follows--



“you be no doubt surprised to see I here, but after peruse your note

I could not rest till I have learn the truth from your own lip, and

as you be to quit Venice to-morrow, there be no time to lose; so I

resolve, _coûte qui coûte_, to make the effort, and here I be.”



he pause for a reply, but obtain none, look up in surprise, and

perceive Annie Grant standing pale and tremble before he. completely

overcome by this unexpected encounter, he contrive to stammer out--



“I beg pardon, I believe I be address Mrs. Leicester. I must go and

seek she,” and turn to put his design into execution; but his strength

be unequal to the task, and lean against a marble slab, he remain

motionless, utterly unable to proceed. for a moment Annie pause as if

thunder-stricken, then her woman’s heart awake within she, and in an

instant she be by his side, bring a chair for he to sit down.



“oh, Mr. Arundel, how wrong, how mad of you to venture out,” she

exclaim, her anxiety for he overpower every other feeling; “you

will bring on a return of the fever. why, you be so weak that you can

scarcely stand; pray sit down.”



advance a step, Lewis take the chair from she, and lean on the back

for support, say with a faint smile--



“I have indeed somewhat miscalculate my strength, Miss Grant; I be

very, very weak,” and as he speak he sink upon the seat, while the

bright flush, which the excitement of behold Annie have call into

his cheek, fade to the most deathlike paleness: his companion become

alarmed.



“you be faint,” she say; “let I ring for assistance.”



a tray, with a decanter of water and some glass, stand upon a table

near; Lewis’s eye fall upon they.



“it be merely the unaccustomed exertion,” he say feebly, “it will pass

away in a moment.”



Annie catch the direction of his glance. “you would like a glass of

water,” she exclaim, “let I give you one;” and suit the action to

the word, she fill a glass with the sparkle liquid and hand it to

he. he take it with a slight inclination of the head, drink it eagerly,

and be about to rise, in order to put down the glass, when Annie, by

a deprecating gesture, prevent he, and take it from his trembling

finger replace it on the table. as she turn from do so, their

eye meet, and she perceive that his be fix on her feature with

a deep, earnest, scrutinise gaze, as though he strove to read in her

countenance the history of her inner life. for a moment she meet his

gaze with a bright, truthful, unshrinke look; then, unable to bear the

power of his eagle eye, she turn away with a blush and a smile, half

tender, half reproachful, for Annie be no stoic, and every feeling of

her heart reveal itself in her tell-tale countenance. Lewis could bear

it no long--speak he must.



“Miss Grant--Annie,” he say, and as he pronounce her christian name

his deep voice tremble with suppress emotion, “when I come here

to-day I have no thought of see you; but accident (if, indeed, in this

strange, complicated life anything may be so consider) have determine

it otherwise, and the opportunity shall not be lose. not very many day

since I be so grievously ill that the chance be strongly against my

rallying; it have please God to spare my life a little long; but such

an escape as this give rise to deep and solemn thought. while I lay

upon the bed of sickness, which have so nearly prove the bed of death,

I learn to read my own heart--my past life glide as it be in review

before I, and my fault and error, no long hide by the mist of

self-deceit or of passion, reveal themselves clearly in the light of

an awakened conscience: above they all stand forth in its evil beauty

the master-demon pride, and I see how it have embitter my whole

existence, and how, if ever I hope to obtain even peace of mind, much

more happiness, I must relax no effort until I should subdue it. Annie,

I have love you long; you cannot, _do_ not doubt it; but because I

deem you rich than I be, and of high rank, I be too proud to

own it to you. year of mental torture have be my punishment: I do not

complain that this should have be so--I do not impugn the justice of

the decree; on the contrary, I acknowledge it with deep contrition. I

sin, and it be fitting I should pay the penalty, however bitter;

but there be a grief I be not prepare for, and in which I could not

discern retributive justice; for whatever a slanderous world may say,

my love for you have be deep, pure, and disintereste, the true, most

earnest feeling of my inmost soul. Annie, I will be frank with you, and

even if my presumption ruin my cause, I have suffer too much from

concealment not to tell you the whole truth. when, distract by my

hopeless passion for you, and madden by the insult of one who be now

no more, I tear myself away from Broadhurst, and leave you, as I deem,

for ever, the most bitter pang proceed from a secret belief, which

even despair could not banish, that I read in your soft glance

assurance that have I dare to urge my suit, I might have learn I have

not love in vain; and in the midst of my desolation I be happy, deeply

happy, in the thought. then a ray of light break in upon the darkness--a

strange chain of event lead to the discovery that I be heir to an

ancient and honourable name and an ample fortune, and I wait but to

obtain legal evidence of the fact, ere I hasten to tell you of my

affection, in the fond hope of elicit that I be belove again: once

assure of that, I determine that _nothing_ should prevent my winning

your hand--all obstacle must yield before such a love as mine.

with these feeling burn in my breast, imagine the dismay which

overwhelm I on learn by a letter from your father that scarcely

twenty-four hour after I have quit Broadhurst, you, of your own free

will, have renew your engagement with your cousin. hear I out,” he

continue, as Annie, who with blushing cheek and tearful eye, have

remain as though spell-bind, drink in his every tone, attempt

eagerly to interrupt him--“hear I out, and then if you can explain

this mystery, the devotion of a life-time shall plead forgiveness for my

having misjudge you. how I live through the wretchedness that letter

cause I, I do not know. I believe I be go mad, for a time I _be_

mad, and rail at Heaven for having create a be so fair and false

as then I deem you. oh! the misery, the heavy, crush grief, when

the heart adore, with all its faculty of love, one whom the reason

point out as light, fickle, and all unworthy to have call forth such

true affection. for two year this black veil of doubt and mistrust hung

between your image and my spirit--I cast from I any idea of claim

the rank and rich that be my birthright, for I value they only as

they could bring I near to you; and go forth a wanderer, torment

by the consciousness, doubly humiliating to one of my proud nature,

that although I believe you unworthy of my affection, I still love you

devotedly as ever. the first person who win I from my gloomy thought,

and lead I to hope your conduct might be satisfactorily explain, be

your kind friend Laura, who in her honest singleness of heart could not

believe in the possibility of the fickleness of which I imagine you.

guilty--and I (though her argument fail to convince my reason), how I

love she for her unbelief! I could say much more--could tell you of

the agony of mind I endure, when unseen by you I watch you lean on

_his_ arm and smile upon he, and deem my bad fear realise, and

that you love he; but it be needless--Annie, I cannot look on you and

believe you false; if indeed you ever love I, I _know_ that, despite

appearance, you have be true to that affection, and that you love I

still. Annie, dearest, tell I that it be so?”



he cease, and with his hand clasp, as those of some votary adore

his saint, sit gaze on the April of smile and tear that play over

the expressive feature of she he love, until read in her tender

glance the secret her lip refuse to speak, happiness lend he

strength, and spring to her side, he draw she unresistingly towards

he, and reprove the coral lip for their silence by seal his

forgiveness upon they with a love kiss. and as Annie, albeit there be

no reason to doubt that she be an exceedingly moral and well-conduct

young lady, do not appear to discern any great impropriety in this act,

but, on the contrary, disengage herself from his embrace gently and

tenderly, the probability be, look at the matter in a correct

light, and with an artist eye (an optical delusion, popularly suppose

to fulfil one of the main duty of charity by clothing the naked), that

the view she take of the affair be a right one. and then by degree,

having declare that it be impossible she could ever tell he anything

about it, but that Laura know--would not he go and ask Laura at once?

(a proposition Lewis coolly but decidedly reject), she contrive,

she never know how, to enable he to guess the truth, which he do very

quickly and cleverly, and find so perfectly satisfactory that his anger

(such mild anger!) instantly change to the most unmitigated pity, an

emotion so nearly akin to that other Christian virtue, love, that we

fear we shall lay ourselves open to the charge of write an actual

love scene if we pursue the subject any far. and as it be a

well-ascertain fact that young person strictly bring up and never

allow to inflame their imagination and gain pervert view of life

by peruse those invention of the enemy of man- (and woman-) kind,

work of fiction, either never fall in love at all or do so accord to

parental act of parliament, pass in the year one of the reign of

good king Mammon, we (lest we incur the high displeasure of any of this

monarch’s respectable subject) will say no more about it. but when

Laura, grieve at what she consider the unsatisfactory issue of she

interview with Richard Frere, return to her boudoir to make the

good report her conscience would allow of to Annie, she be especially

surprised, and a little frightened, to discover her friend, with

heighten colour, downcast eye, and a bright smile play about the

corner of her mouth, sit on a sofa by the side of what Laura would

have take for the ghost of Lewis Arundel, only that ghost do not in

a general way look intensely happy, and be not usually addict to

hold young lady’ hand caressingly between their spectral finger.

however, the ghost soon vindicate his claim to the protection of the

Habeas Corpus Act by rise and shake Laura’s hand cordially, and

take the initiative in conversation by exclaiming--



“my dear, kind Mrs. Leicester, I owe all my happiness to you.” then

Laura begin to surmise what have happen, and in the excess of her joy

scold Lewis so vigorously for his madness in venture out, and Annie

for her folly in allow he to talk, that she be force to stop in

the midst of her harangue to declare herself a virago, and to laugh

so heartily at her own vehemence that in order to save herself from

become hysterical she be fain to betake herself to her own bedroom

and indulge in the feminine luxury of a good cry. and then Lewis and

Annie sit and look into each other’s eye; their joy be too full for

word, but such silence as theirs be far more eloquent, for as there

be a grief too deep for tear, so be there happiness which language be

powerless to express, and such happiness do they experience at that

moment. at length Lewis speak.



“Dearest,” he say, in a low, soft voice that tremble with the

tenderness which fill his soul, “I must leave you now; there be many

reason which forbid my meeting your father till we reach England and

I be prepared to _prove_ to he all that your trustful, love heart

believe because I tell you that it be so. until we meet in our own

happy country, which for the future will be as dear to I for your sake

as lately it have be for the same cause hateful, our engagement must

remain a secret from all but Laura.”



“but will that be right?” plead Annie, look up wistfully into the

face of he who would be from thenceforth her oracle.



it be a fearful responsibility when, through the affection, we gain

such a hold over a live soul that the judgment lie dormant, and

the thing which seem good in our eye appear so in theirs also; such

influence be indeed a mighty talent commit to our charge, and most

careful should we be lest we abuse the trust repose in we. Lewis feel

this strongly, and pause to reconsider his decision. his chief reason

for wish that General Grant might not be immediately inform of

his declaration be the difficult position in which it would place that

gallant officer in regard to Lord Bellefield’s relation. how could he,

for instance, expect Lord Ashford to believe that his brother-in-law have

use all possible exertion to secure the murderer of his son when Annie

Grant, that son’s destine bride, be affiance to a man who, but for

the catastrophe which have take place, would have meet Lord Bellefield in

a duel? the altercation and subsequent challenge be so completely a

matter of notoriety in Venice that it be certain that some account of

they, probably an exaggerated and distort one, would find its way

to England. but this be a reason which he could not give Annie, as he

correctly imagine that the affair at the casino have be keep from her

knowledge. thus the more he reflect the more certain he become that

his original determination be a right one. accordingly he replied--



“trust I, dear one; concealment be as foreign to my nature as to your

own. my fault (and I have only too many) do not lie in that direction;

but, to the good of my judgment, I believe that in wish your father

should, for the present, remain ignorant of our engagement, I be

consult your interest and his quite as much as my own. believe I,

love, I would sacrifice anything, even the cherished hope of one day

call you my own, rather than influence you to do aught for which your

conscience could afterwards upbraid you.”



and Annie do believe he, with the strong, unhesitate faith of

perfect love. have he advance the most incredible assertion--declare,

for instance, that he have discover perpetual motion, square the

circle, and set the Thames on fire, Annie would equally and implicitly

have believe he. have he deceive she, her only refuge from a universal

scepticism would have be to die. then come the “sweet sorrow” of a

lover’ parting--sweet in the many evidence of affection which the

occasion call forth, and sorrowful by reason of the anxious thought

to which quit those we love, even under the happy auspex,

necessarily give rise. Annie’s bright eye be dim with tear, and

Lewis’s mouth, no long sternly compress, tremble with the emotion

he in vain attempt to conceal, as, with a murmured “God bless and

protect you, my own darling!” he tear himself away.



in the meanwhile, scarcely have Richard Frere quit the Grassini Palace

than he encounter General Grant, fret and fume under the weight

of a burden of minor misery, and full of complaint of the abominable

misdemeanour of the venetian official, amongst which, by no mean the

light, be the culpable stupidity which prevent they from speak

or understand English, together with the obstinate prejudice with

which they refuse to acknowledge that by add the letter o to the

termination of word in that language they immediately become Italian--



“I say ‘_requiro uno passporto_’ to they, sir, half-a-dozen time over,

and nobody shall ever make I believe they do not know what _that_

mean!” be his indignant complaint.



of course Frere’s ready sympathy entail on he a request that if he

could spare the time to go back to the office with he, the General

would esteem it such a great favour, and of course, though his

conscience reproach he for be away from “poor, solitary. Lewis”

 for so many hour, he do what be require of he; and of course,

having say A,--B, C, and D follow as a matter of necessity, until,

before he have go through the alphabet of the General’s commission,

several hour have elapse, and Lewis, having find his way back to

his lodging, be recline in an easy-chair enjoy a feast of

happy memory and bright anticipation, when Frere, hot, tired, and

dissatisfied with his morning’s work, fling down his cotton umbrella,

and throw himself, very much unbuttoned, in a kind of dishevel

heap upon the near chair, began--



“well, confound this climate, say I, where a man cann’t get through a

morning’s business without come home more like a piece of hot boil

beef than a temperate Christian--here’s a state of dissolution for a

free-bear Briton to be in. I tell you what it be, young man, if you keep

I here much long I shall become a mere walking skeleton--flesh and

blood literally cann’t stand it, and I shall have to go home and be

married in my bone.”



“by which ceremony I suppose you hope to become possess of an

additional rib to make up for your loss of substance,” suggest

Lewis, smile at the odd, quaint way in which his friend describe his

trouble.



“yes, it’s all very well for you to sit there and laugh at a fellow,”

 return frere grumpily, “but if you have be parade about this oven

of a place for two hour, tie like a kettle to Governor Grant’s tail,

as I have be, you would find it no such laugh matter, I can tell

you. he be obstinate and wrong-headed as an elderly mule too, make a

fuss about trifle that do not signify a bit one way or the other. why

cannot he take life coolly and quietly as--as----?”



here he come to an abrupt conclusion, having discover that the

grumble tenor of the speech be somewhat at variance with the ending

he have intend to make to it--viz., “as I do.” Lewis finish it for

he.



“as a sensible man should do, I suppose you be go to observe.”



Frere detect the covert satire and shake his fist threateningly at his

friend.



“you have well be civil, you know, or I may be tempt to give you the

thrashing I have owe you so long. I could not have a well opportunity

than now, when you be so weak that you can scarcely walk across the

room alone.”



“perhaps I may be strong than you be aware of,” return Lewis; “what

do you think about my be able to go out, for instance----?”



“think,” reply frere dogmatically; “why, I think that if you attempt

it a week hence it will be too soon. Dr. Grabafee say a fortnight, but

his be scarcely an unprejudiced opinion; however, I’ll take care you

don’t set foot outside this room within a week.”



Lewis turn away to hide a smile, while frere, still suffer from

heat, and not have another available button which could be respectably

unfastened, pull off his neckcloth, and thus relieve, resumed--“Who

do you think I have be lecture this morning?”



Lewis profess his ignorance, and Frere continued--



“only a certain young lady, in whose proceeding I’ve an idea you take

particular interest--one Miss Annie Grant.”



Lewis start as Frere pronounce this name, but recover himself,

ask in an elaborately indifferent tone of voice, “pray when do this

interesting colloquy take place, and what might be the subject thereof?”



“the colloquy, as you call it, take place some four hour ago; and the

subject thereof be the young woman’s conduct towards your precious

self. now, don’t go and fly into a passion,” continue Frere, as Lewis

colour and seem about to make some hasty rejoinder; “remember, life

ought to be take easily and quietly by a sensible man, and of course

you consider yourself one: however, I take the liberty to tell Miss

General Grant a few home truth that she will be none the bad for

hearing.”



he then proceed, after his own fashion, to give an account of his

conversation with Annie, and his subsequent interview with Laura,

conclude his recital thus--



“so the upshot of the whole affair, and a very unsatisfactory one

I’m afraid you’ll think it, be this. when you have leave Broadhurst,

Ma’amselle Annie find herself in a bit of a fix, and not be a man

or Rose Arundel, she, after the fashion of her silly sex, do a weak and

injudicious thing; but as I say to the other young woman, who, by the

way, seem to have the good sense of the two, that’s very different from

do a deliberately wicked one, and therefore, perhaps, Lewis may be

induce to look over it.”



“for Heaven’s sake, my dear fellow, don’t tell I any more about it,

you will drive I frantic with your detestable common-sense platitude,”

 exclaim Lewis, spring from his chair impatiently; “at least you

would have do so,” he continue more quietly, “if I have not happen

to have see Miss Grant myself since your well-mean but somewhat

unnecessary interview with she, and learn from her own sweet lip that

she forgive I for have so hastily and ungenerously misjudge she.”



“eh! what! have the young woman be here in my absence?” return

frere, greatly scandalise. “oh! this will never do! I don’t allow such

liberty to be take with my patient; besides, I don’t consider the

proceeding by any mean a correct one; she might have find you in bed,

with your nightcap on, for aught she could tell to the contrary.”



“do you know what be report to have occur when a mountain object

to come at Mahomet’s bidding?” ask Lewis quietly.



“why, Mahomet go to the mountain, to be sure, like an arrant humbug as

he be; but what have that get to do with the case in question? why, you

don’t mean to say,” continue Frere, as a sudden light break in upon

him--“you don’t mean to say that _you’ve_ be to call upon _she?_”



“I be afraid I must confess that such be the alarming fact,” be the

cool reply.



“well! I have know many insane action in my life, certainly,”

 growl Frere, make fruitless attempt to re-unbutton his already

enfranchised garment, “but this”--here he nearly tear a wristband off

his shirt in his pursuit of coolness under difficulties--“is the very

maddest thing I ever do hear of--a man that be on the point of death

here not ten day ago to rush out of bed the moment one’s back be turn

for the sake of seeing----”



“she be look so sweetly pretty, Frere,” interrupt Lewis; “and those

eye--there never be such eye see in the world before.”



“oh, of course not,” return frere viciously. “patent double-action,

high-pressure, sky-blue revolver, make to look every way at once, see

through mill-stone, and peep round the corner into the bargain, _they_

be, no doubt; but if she could use they to no well purpose than to

lure out, at the risk of his life, a foolish boy that ought to have

have more sense--but it’s a mere waste of word talk to you,” he

continue, catch a smile on Lewis’s feature; “and here have I go

and ruin my other shirt, and this one be at the wash--psha! I mean to

say, ruin my other wash--that be, wash my other ruin--hang I if I

know what I mean to say--only if you’re not the bad for this--bother

the boy, how absurdly happy he’s look! so it’s all right between you,

eh! Lewis? well, Heaven know, you have suffer enough to deserve that

it should be so, my poor fellow, and though you must have be mad to

go out, and I ought to be very angry with you, yet, as it have end, and

always suppose it do not do you any harm, why I be heartily glad you

do it;” and so say, frere, whose feeling, and the heat together,

be decidedly too many for he, make a precipitate retreat into the

bedroom, where, for the present, we will leave he.









CHAPTER LXVIII.--LEWIS OUT-GENERALS the GENERAL, and the TRAIN stop.



Lewis’s recovery be not retard by his imprudent visit to the Palazzo

Grassini; and Frere have the satisfaction, ere many week elapse, of

perceive that he be strong enough to render their return to England

practicable. accordingly, the “Giaour” picture and the sketch of Annie

and Faust be carefully pack (Lewis having determine to retain they

as memento of the eventful portion of his career which lead to their

execution), old Antonelli receive a present of money sufficient to

enable he to carry out the darling wish of his heart--viz., to bestow

upon his son the education of a painter; and Lewis and Frere, having

wound up their affair in Venice, quit that city, which, fill with

a rabble of revolutionary demagogue and their dupe, have become no

long a desirable place of residence. the friend reach England

without any adventure worthy of record; and Rose be compensate for

many a weary hour of anxiety and suspense by her joy in welcome she

brother, and learn from his lip the unmitigated satisfaction with

which he have hear of her engagement to Richard Frere; and how that

“glorious fellow” have redouble all his former obligation to he by

his sound advice and tender and judicious nursing. if for a moment frere

could have regret the part he have play, the love smile of warm

approval with which Rose receive he would have compensate he for any

far great expenditure of time and trouble. but Lewis have much to tell,

which give rise to very different emotion in his auditor; and Rose, as

she grieve for the untimely fate of poor Jane Hardy, and shudder

at the awful retribution which have overtake her betrayer, breathe a

silent thanksgiving that her brother have be restrain from any deed

of violence, to which his impetuous disposition, keen sensibility, and

quick sense of injury might have impel he. Lewis have also something

to hear as well as to communicate.



Mrs. Arundel, in her spirit of opposition to the artless and bereaved

relict of the late Colonel Brahmin, have carry her flirtation with that

victim of literary ambition, Dackerel Dace, Esq., to such a pitch, that

when the blighted barrister determine to resign his destiny altogether

in favour of matrimony, and make she an offer of his limp hand, flabby

heart, and five thousand a year to give piquancy and flavour to the

tasteless and insipid “trifle” he _tender_ for her acceptance, that

volatile matron feel that she have commit herself too deeply to

retract, and that, set off the money against the man, the bargain

after all might not be such a bad one, and so say “yes.” Rose dislike

the match greatly, and fear Lewis would do so still more strongly,

venture upon a mild remonstrance; but when once she have take a thing

into her head, Mrs. Arundel be very determined, and Rose gain

nothing but an intimation, half earnest, half playful, that as she (Mrs.

Arundel) have not interfere with her daughter when she choose to engage

herself to Ursa Major, she expect the same for_bear_ance (and she

emphasise the vile pun most unmistakably) to be exercise towards she

and her odd fish, by which nickname she irreverently paraphrase the

ichthyological appellation of her “_future_.”



Lewis, as Rose have fear, be both hurt and annoy at this fresh and

convincing proof of his mother’s volatile and worldly nature, but there

be nothing in the connection to justify his take measure to break

off the match; Mrs. Arundel be perfectly free to do as she please, and

competent to decide her own course in life; so after one conversation

with she on the subject, the nature of which may be gather from the

result, he leave the affair to take its own course. his first step on

reach London be to seek an interview with his legal adviser; their

conference prove satisfactory, eventuate (to use an affected but

expressive word) in send for a patent cab, wherein Lewis ensconce

himself, in company with a small lawyer and a large blue bag, and the

trio drive to Park Crescent.



the feeling with which Lewis once again stand within the library of

General Grant’s mansion--that library where he have first be engage to

act as poor Walter’s tutor--the chamber into which he and Annie have be

show on the night when he have rescue she from insult in the crush-room

of the opera--the night of the unhappy Mellerton’s suicide--may well

be imagine. then he have be poor, friendless, in the situation of a

dependant, and make to feel that situation, alike by the open insult

of Lord Bellefield and the frigid courtesy of the General and Miss

Livingstone, his youth, his inexperience, sensitive disposition, and

proud, impassioned nature render all these trial doubly gall to

he; while, still more to embitter his lot, come that “sorrow’s crown

of sorrow,” his hopeless attachment to Annie. now heir to an ancient and

honourable name and an ample fortune, his affection return by she

he love, his rival sweep from his path without his have to reproach

himself with participation in the act which work his downfall, his

mind strengthen, his principle raise, and his fault diminish,

if not eradicate, by the struggle he have undergo, and above all, his

soul fortify by the recollection that, through God’s grace, he

have be enable, at the turning-point of his career, to sacrifice

everything rather than sin against his maker’s law, how different be

his position! he receive a moderately cordial welcome from General

Grant, which tepid reception be occasion by a conflict in the mind

of that noble commander, between his strong regard for Lewis, a sense of

the obligation he lie under to he, and an uncomfortable recollection

of his attachment to Annie, together with the moral impossibility of

allow his daughter to marry a man whose present income consist

of the saving of an ex-tutorship, and whose prospect embrace

the doubtful gaining of a professional artist; Lewis perceive his

embarrassment, and rightly conjecture its cause, which it be the

object of his visit to remove. but General Grant’s cold imperturbability

have cause he so much annoyance in bygone hour, that a slight spice

of what the french term _esprit malin_ actuate he, and under its

influence he begin, after a few desultory remarks--



“it may possibly not have escape your mind, General, that during a

conversation I have the honour to hold with you before I finally quit

Broadhurst, I mention to you my devoted attachment to Miss Grant.”



the General bow in token of assent, but the cloud upon his brow grow

darker. not heed this, Lewis continued--



“I remember express myself somewhat strongly against certain

conventional prejudice relate to inequality of position, which

oppose an effectual bar to the realisation of my wish. I be young

and inexperienced then--I have since become wise in the way of

the world, and be perfectly aware that, in speak as I do on that

occasion, I alike waste my word and your valuable time.”



he pause; and the General, who have be considerably puzzle during the

speech to make out what his companion might be aim at, settle, to

his own satisfaction, that the increase knowledge of human nature to

which Lewis allude have show the young man the folly of which he have

be guilty, and that this speech be intend as an apology--nothing

could be more respectful and correct. accordingly the cloud vanish, as

in his most gracious manner he reply, “Sir, your observation do

you credit. pray set your mind at rest on this subject; fortunately my

daughter never have the slight suspicion of your feeling towards

she; and for my own part, I have long ago dismiss the affair from my

recollection; and you may rest assure that in our future intercourse

the subject shall never again be broach between we.”



as the General allude to his daughter’s happy ignorance of Lewis’s

attachment a slightly ironical smile curl that young gentleman’s

handsome mouth; repress it instantly, he reply with a calm, almost

_nonchalant_ air, “I scarcely see how that can be accomplish, General

Grant, as the object of my visit here to-day be to make you a formal

proposal for your daughter’s hand!”



if Lewis have suddenly rise from his chair, and with the full power of

his return strength have hurl that article of furniture at General

Grant’s head, it might have knock he down more literally than the

foregoing speech, but, figuratively, nothing could have do so. for a

minute or two he appear utterly unable to frame a reply; then, draw

himself up to a degree suggestive of a telescopic conformation, he begin

in an awful tone of voice, “Sir, you have astonish I--nay, more than

that, sir, you have disappoint I--very greatly disappoint I. I have

hope well thing of you, sir; I have hope, from the early promise

you evince, that your judgment and good sense would, when mature and

strengthen by a little more knowledge of the world, have enable you

to conquer your strangely misplace attachment--would, in fact, have

save I from the painful situation in which you have--to which you

have--that be--you would have save yourself (you must not blame _I_,

sir, if the truth sound unpalatable) the humiliation of a refusal.”



“then be I to understand that you unhesitatingly reject my suit?”

 inquire Lewis, something of the old stern look come across his

feature.



“most unequivocally and decidedly,” be the concise reply.



“it would have be more courteous, and therefore more in accordance

with General Grant’s usual conduct towards those whom he consider

beneath he in the social scale, to inquire whether any, and if so,

_what_ amelioration might have take place in my future prospect

to have induce I to hazard so bold a step ere my proposal be thus

unmistakably decline,” observe Lewis in a marked yet respectful tone

of displeasure; “it will, however, make no difference in my intention,

as when I shall have obtain your answer to a few important question,

and explain to you my object in make they, it be possible you may

view my conduct in a different light.”



the General, who grow tall and stiffer every moment, merely

acknowledge this speech by an inclination of the head, so slight as to

be scarcely perceptible; and Lewis continued--



“the late Sir Hugh Desborough, Walter’s grandfather, be, I believe,

your intimate friend?”



“bless my soul, yes, sir; we serve together in India, be for six

year in the same regiment, and live as if we have be brother. why do

you ask such extraordinary question?” exclaim the General, startle

completely out of his dignity.



“because, in that case, you be probably well acquainted with the

circumstance of his family history, and can set I right if I state

they incorrectly,” reply Lewis, upon whom the mantle of the General’s

cast-off dignity appear suddenly to have fall. “Sir Hugh have two

son, I believe; the elder marry imprudently, quarrel with his

father, who refuse to receive the lady he have espouse, and sever

all family tie, live abroad under a feigned name, and be believe to

have die without issue. the second son be Walter’s father, and Walter

inherit the baronetcy in default of male issue of the eld son.”



he pause, and the General observe, “you be correct in your fact,

sir, but to what do all this lead?”



“that you will be well able to perceive, sir, when I inform you that

I be prepared to prove, indisputably, and to your full satisfaction,

the follow additional particular. Sir Hugh’s eld son, Captain

Desborough----”



“right; he be captain in the ----th Lancers, and throw up his

commission when he choose to live abroad. it be say he enter the

austrian army, and attain the same rank in that service,” interrupt

the General.



“he do so,” resume Lewis, who speak in the same calm, unimpassioned

voice which he have use throughout the interview, though to any one

who know he well it would have be perceivable that he do so by

the great effort; “but those who believe that he die abroad, and

without male issue, be misinform: he die in England, in the spring

of 18----, and leave (besides a daughter) one son, who be still live.”



“left a son! why he would be heir to the title and estate instead of

Walter. where be he, sir? who be he?” exclaim the General impetuously.



Lewis rise, draw himself up to his full height, advance slowly till

he stand face to face with the General, and then, fix his pierce

glance upon he, say, “he now stand before you, General Grant, and

ask you whether, when he have establish his right before the eye of

the world, you will again refuse he your daughter’s hand?”



Reader, the only little bit of mystery in our story (if indeed it have

present any mystery at all to your acuteness) be now clear up; and

the interest end, the soon the tale itself arrive at a conclusion

the well. but for the satisfaction of the unimaginative, the

strong-minded woman and practical man of the world, who cannot rest

assure that two and two make four till they have count it on their

finger, we will write a few more last word, wind up the various

thread of this veracious history.



in his interview with General Grant, Lewis have only state that which he

be fully prepared to prove; and when the lawyer and his blue bag (not

that lawyer ever do carry blue bag anywhere but in farce at the minor

theatre, or those still more “unreal mockery,” the page of modern

novel) be call in to assist at the conference, the follow fact

be elicited:--



the packet of letter which Lewis find amongst Hardy’s paper, and

which give he the first intimation that he, and not poor Walter, be

heir to the title and estate of Desborough, have be write by Captain

Arundel, or, as his name really be, Desborough, to his young brother,

Walter Desborough (the father of the poor idiot, who be in fact first

cousin to Lewis); the object with which these letter be write

be to bring about a reconciliation between Sir Hugh and his eld

son--Walter Desborough having undertake the office of mediator. in

order to do this, it be first of all necessary to disabuse Sir Hugh’s

mind of an idea that Captain Desborough’s marriage be not valid and

that the child be illegitimate; for this purpose the wedding

certificate be enclose (prove that he have be marry in his

own name and by a properly constitute authority), together with

certificate of the baptism of Rose and of Lewis. the letter also

contain an account of his having take the name of Arundel, and his

reason for so do; in fact, without go into minutia, the paper

afford complete evidence legally to establish the identity of Captain

Desborough and Captain Arundel, and to render Lewis’s claim to the

baronetcy indisputable. to account for their having be find among

Hardy’s paper, it must be bear in mind that Walter Desborough be

the scoundrel who first rouse the evil nature in that misguided man

by elope with his wife. hardy, be it remember, follow the guilty

pair, and assault the betrayer of his honour to such good effect as

to confine he to his bed for month; his companion in crime return

to her father’s house, and die shortly after give birth to the

unfortunate Miles.



when she return to her father, she bring with she a writing-case,

in which be letter she have receive from her seducer previous to

her elopement; in this desk, for convenience of travel, Walter

Desborough have place paper of his own, and amongst other, the

letter, etc., which he have shortly before receive from his brother.

long ere he recover from the effect of Hardy’s chastisement he have

forget where he have place these paper, and hardy never discover

they (he leave his home and enlist as a soldier on his release from

the imprisonment the assault entail upon he), the letter be to all

intent and purpose lose, till by a chapter of accident they fall

into the hand of Lewis. the shock which lead to Captain Arundel’s (or

Desborough, as he should rightly have be call) sudden death be

cause by read an account of his father, Sir Hugh’s demise, in the

newspaper. the clue Messrs. Jones & Levi have gain be from a shopman

in the public library in which Captain Arundel have be sit when he

first become aware of his father’s decease, who gather, from an

involuntary exclamation he make at the moment, that Sir Hugh

Desborough’s death be the subject which have so much excited he; this

shopman have be a clerk of Messrs. Jones & Levi, and learn in their

employ that knowledge be sometimes money as well as power, sell they

for a couple of sovereign the information he have acquire, give at

the same time an account of the strange death of Captain Arundel; hence

their subsequent application to Lewis.



the evidence be so clear and full, Lewis have little difficulty in

establish his claim, more especially as General Grant, convince of

its justice, do not attempt to resist it on Walter’s behalf. the poor

fellow himself could not be make to comprehend his change of fortune;

but he do comprehend, to his inexpressible delight, that for some

reason or other he be always to live with his dear Mr. Arundel,

who, when month have go by and arrangement make which he neither

understand nor heed, take he to a grand house of his own, where Faust

be wait to receive they in a great state of boisterous tail-wag

affection; and when Faust, having lick they all over, and having make

they damp, dusty, and rumple in the excess of his love, have quite do

with they and go back to a large bone on the drawing-room rug, and

Lewis place his arm round Walter’s neck, have whisper to he that he

be never to go away any more, and that he hope before very long Annie

would come and live with they, Walter feel sure he have never know

what it be to be quite happy till then, which fact he afterwards

communicate to Faust in the strict confidence.



Lewis’s assertion in regard to Annie be not base on mere conjecture;

for General Grant--albeit he feel that, in the interview we have

lately record between himself and Lewis, he have be decidedly

out-generalle--do not again reject his late tutor’s proposal for

his daughter’s hand. on the contrary, with the usual self-knowledge of

worldly elderly people (that be, of those who, nine time out of ten,

dictate the action, and influence for weal or woe the future of the

young and generous-hearted), the moment he become convinced that Lewis

be about to inherit a baronetcy and an income little short of £10,000 a

year, he contrive to persuade himself that when his first surprise have

be pass, and he have become aware how deeply his daughter’s happiness

be involve, he should certainly have allow she to unite herself

with Sir Lewis Desborough under his former phase of a precarious

portrait-painter. but if we have be Sir Lewis, we should have feel

heartily glad we have not be force to rely on such a very “forlorn

hope.”



Rose, no long Arundel, do not enjoy the name of Desborough many

week, for although she have particularly desire to be marry on the

same day as Lewis and Annie, she yet yield the point when Ursa Major,

hear that General Grant would not allow his daughter’s wedding to

take place till a year after the death of Lord Bellefield, grow so

outrageous that Rose be force to marry he out of the way, in order to

prevent he from snap and growl at every one that come near

he. but this be Richard Frere’s last bearish episode; for constant

association with Rose soften his little asperity of temper, which,

having arise solely from the unloved and unloving existence he have be

force by circumstance to lead, disappear in the sunshine of a happy

home.



Lord Ashford do not long survive the loss of his eld son, and

Charley Leicester, the portionless young brother, with “a good set

of tooth and nothing to eat,” be now a highly respectable peer of the

realm, with a rent-roll to be compute by ten of thousand. happy in

the affection of his wife and child (for “Tarley” have already have two

successor to dispute the chance of be “spoil by papa, only that

mamma willn’t let he”), Charles, Lord Ashford, have but one trouble in

life, though that unfortunately appear likely to prove an increase

one--viz., that those confound fellow, Schneider & Shears, _willn’t_

make his waistcoat to fit he as they use to do, they be all too

tight round the waist--and Schneider & Shears bear the blame meekly,

have only last week discharge an injudicious foreman, who have be

rash enough to declare that their excellent customer, Lord Ashford, be

grow stout. for a short time the Countess Portici reside with her

brother and sister-in-law, Alessandro having obligingly get himself

knock on the head in the cause of liberty, the reversion of this

popular watchword be about the only legacy he bequeath to his

young, interesting, and not particularly disconsolate widow, who, having

sow her romance, replace the handsome italian by a rich old French

nobleman, Le Marquis de Carosse-Tranquille, irreverently translate by

Bracy, who be still a bachelor and make more pun than ever, into

“my Lord Slow Coach”--a title which the mental incapacity of that

venerable foreigner render unpleasantly appropriate.



the mighty Marmaduke de Grandeville purchase with his wife’s money a

large estate in --------shire, which have belong to his family some

five hundred year before; he have since institute a set of regulation

for his tenantry, form on the model of the feudal system, and if he be

not prematurely suffocate by his own greatness, bid fair to “add a new

lustre to the noble name which--ar--ahem!” etc., etc.



Mrs. Arundel carry out her design of marry she “blighted

barrister,” and by her liveliness of disposition have do more towards

remove the mildew from his mind than could have be expect. as,

however, in accordance with her taste, they live chiefly abroad, Lewis

and Annie see but little of they.



Miss Livingstone, as she increase in year, grow harsh, stififer, and

more frozen than ever, until one bitter winter’s day, happen to catch

a slight additional cold, her temperature sink below the point at which

animal life could be maintain, and become rather stififer and cold

than usual, the first half of her patronymic cease to be any long

appropriate--her last word be a cross one.



General Grant live to a good old age, improve, under the influence of

certain bright-eyed little Desboroughs, into a very amiable grandpapa.



the fate of Miles Hardy still remain a mystery; that he do not die

of the wound receive in the death-struggle with Lord Bellefield be

ascertain; but whether he perish in the italian revolution, in which

he be know to take an active part, or, as be rumour, escape in

safety to America, the few who be interested in he have fail to

learn.



Annie and Lewis, after their stormy transit along that portion of the

_RAILROAD of life_ in which we have accompany they, be at length

happily united; their future fortune yet lie hide amid the uncut leave

of the great book of Fate; but one thing we may safely predict--viz.,

that whatever trial may be in store for they, they will find in their

mutual affection a source of constant joy and consolation, of which the

lonely-hearted be unhappily ignorant.
